Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
4 min
Author's Preface: The Purpose and Method of This Book
Editor praises Allah and introduces the book's purpose and method.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the purpose of Imam al-Sanusi's creedal work and its method of presenting clear proofs to lead students from imitation to certainty.

Core Idea
Imam al-Sanusi's book bridges imitation and certainty through gentle clarity.

This preface sets the stage for the entire book. It praises Allah, introduces the creed as a means to escape darkness and doubt, and highlights the author's commitment to clarity and gentleness in presenting proofs. Understanding this preface helps the reader appreciate the pedagogical method of the work.

Guided Path
1
Read the preface carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the traditional opening and the editor's description of the book's effect.
2
Identify the book's purpose
Find the phrases that describe what the book does: it contains important principles of religion, extracts from conjecture and imitation to certainty.
3
Note the method
The editor says Sanusi maintained gentleness in expression and clarity in presenting the proof. Why is this important?
This method is for students who may be put off by technical kalam terminology.
4
Reflect on the audience
The editor thinks this book will benefit students of guidance who are not attracted to technical terms. How does this affect your approach to the book?
You are likely part of this target audience.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الحمد لله الذي هدانا لدينِهِ القويم ، وَمَا كُنَّا لِنَهْتَدِى لَوْلَا أَنْ هَدَنَا الله [الأعراف: ٤٣] ، والصلاة والسلام على سيدنا محمد الرؤوف الرحيم ، وَمَا بِكُم مِّن نِعْمَةٍ فَمِنَ اللَّهِ ﴾ [النحل: ٥٣] ، وعلى آلِهِ الطُّهرِ ذوي النسب الصميم والحسب الكريم ، ذَالِكَ الْفَضْلُ مِنَ اللَّهِ [النساء: ٧٠] ، وعلى أصحابه أولي الفضل والتقديم ونجوم سماء الأمة وعقدها النظيم ، وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ دِينَا مِمَّنْ أَسْلَمَ وَجْهَهُ لِلَّهِ ﴾ [النساء : ١٢٥] . وبعد : فدونك هذه العقيدة الحاوية على مهمات أصول الدين ، والمخرجةَ مَنْ أتقن براهينها من ظُلُماتِ الظنِّ وضباب التقليد إلى برّ اليقين ، والمرشدة مَنْ أخذت بيده إلى سُبل السلام ، وحسبُكَ بمؤلفها دلالاً على الله جل وعزّ ، ومؤتمناً على دينه سبحانه حَرَصَ الإمام السنوسي في « شرحه » لـ « صغرى الصغرى » ألا يخرج عمَّا ألزم به نفسه في الأصل ؛ من ليونة في العبارة ، ووضوح في عرض الدليل، وهي ميزة يعرف قدرها من يتعنى بقراءة كتب الاعتقاد ولو كانت لطيفة الحجم ، ولهذا أحسب أن سيكون لهذا الكتاب شأن عند عامة طلاب الهداية في علم الاعتقاد ، ولا سيما الذين لا تروق لهم المصطلحات الكلامية ، ولا اهتمام لهم بالبحث عن شبه لم يعرضوا لها ولم تعرض لهم
Praise be to Allah who guided us to His straight religion, and we would not have been guided had Allah not guided us [Al-A'raf:43], and blessings and peace be upon our master Muhammad the kind and merciful, and whatever blessing you have is from Allah [An-Nahl:53], and upon his pure family of noble lineage and generous standing, that is the bounty from Allah [An-Nisa':70], and upon his companions of virtue and precedence, the stars of the nation's sky and its well-ordered necklace, and who is better in religion than one who submits his face to Allah [An-Nisa':125]. To proceed: Here is this creed containing the important matters of the principles of religion, which extracts whoever masters its proofs from the darkness of conjecture and the fog of imitation to the shore of certainty, and guides whoever takes hold of it to the paths of peace. Sufficient for you is its author as a guide to Allah, Mighty and Majestic, and a trustee of His religion, exalted be He. Imam al-Sanusi in his commentary on 'Sughra al-Sughra' was keen not to deviate from what he obligated himself to in the original text: of gentleness in expression and clarity in presenting the proof. This is a virtue whose value is known by whoever takes the trouble to read books of belief even if they are small in size. Thus I think that this book will have standing among general students of guidance in the science of belief, especially those who are not attracted to kalam terminology and have no interest in researching doubts that they have not encountered and that have not encountered them.
Source pages p. 7

Commentary
This passage is an editorial preface praising Allah and introducing the book. The editor begins with traditional praises (hamdalah, salawat) and then states the book's purpose: to present a concise creed ('aqidah) containing essential principles of religion (usul al-din). The book is described as extracting one from darkness of conjecture (zunun) and fog of imitation (taqlid) to certainty (yaqin) and guiding to peace. The editor highlights that Imam al-Sanusi, in his commentary on his own short text 'Sughra al-Sughra', maintained a style of gentleness ('liyuna) in expression and clarity (wuduh) in presenting the proof (dalil). The editor believes this book will benefit those not interested in technical kalam terminology, implying it bridges introductory and advanced audiences.

Key Terms
العقيدة
أصول الدين
الدليل
التقليد
اليقين
السنوسي
صغرى الصغرى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Only advanced kalam terminology and debating doubts leads to certainty.
Correction: The editor states that mastering clear proofs from this book extracts one from darkness and imitation to certainty, even for those not interested in technical terms.

Checkpoint
What does the editor say is the result of mastering the proofs in this book?
A) One becomes a scholar of kalam
B) One is extracted from darkness of conjecture and fog of imitation to certainty
C) One memorizes all Sunni beliefs
D) One can debate heretics

Key Takeaway
The goal of this book is to lead students from imitation to certainty through clear and gentle presentation of creedal proofs.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are you currently in the 'fog of imitation' when it comes to your core beliefs, going along with what you've been taught without truly understanding the proofs? What would it cost you to seek clarity?
?
The editor promises this book leads to 'paths of peace'. Is there an area of your faith where you lack inner peace because you have not moved from conjecture to certainty? Will you commit to working through this book to find that peace?
?
Imam al-Sanusi's method is gentle and clear. Do you sometimes reject truth because it comes in a form you find too simple or not technical enough? How might your pride be keeping you from the shore of certainty?

Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
5 min
Background and Features of Sharh Sughra al-Sughra
Editor introduces the background and features of Sharh Sughra al-Sughra.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the circumstances behind the writing of Sharh Sughra al-Sughra and its distinctive features as described by the editor.

Core Idea
Sanusi wrote a commentary tailored to a specific student's needs, combining clarity with prophetic glorification.

This lesson explores the background of Sharh Sughra al-Sughra, a commentary by Imam al-Sanusi on his own abbreviated creed. We learn why he wrote it, how it came about at the request of an elderly student, and what makes this commentary unique in its approach. The editor highlights several key features that set it apart.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get an overview of the historical context and features.
Notice that the passage begins by praising the original creed and then tells a story.
2
Understand the motivation behind the creed and commentary
Identify why Sanusi wrote 'Sughra al-Sughra' and later its commentary. The text states it was for the father of al-Mallali who found the Sughra too long.
This shows Sanusi's pedagogical concern for individual needs.
3
Identify the four features of the commentary
The passage lists four features of Sharh Sughra al-Sughra. Find each one and consider their significance.
The features are: ease of expression, prophetic glorification, gentleness on taqlid, and excellent editing.
4
Reflect on the uniqueness of this commentary
Consider how these features reflect Sanusi's goal of making creed accessible while preserving depth and reverence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
كلمة عن كتاب شرح صغرى الصغرى

لا شكّ أنَّ «السنوسية الصغرى» هي لب لباب «السنوسيات» المباركة، وليس لها ذاك الطولُ الذي يتعنَّى لأجله طالب علم، فما الذي ألجأ الإمام السنوسي إلى اختصارها بـ «صغرى الصغرى» التي أيدينا؟ بين

إنّ الذي يقف على سبب ذلك يعلم حرص الإمام السنوسي البالغ في تفهيم معتقد أهل السنة، وبذل الجهد منه في تنويع صياغته وحجمه لتحقيق هذه الغاية النبيلة؛ فـ «صغرى الصغرى» ألّفت لوالد العلامة المؤرخ لسيرة الإمام السنوسي الشيخ الملالي، قال في «المواهب القدسية»: (قد كان وضعها لوالدي حفظه الله تعالى من كل آفة وبلية، وأناله الدرجة العلية، وذلك أنَّ والدي لما قرأ على الشيخ رضي الله عنه «عقيدته الصغرى» وختمها عليه بالتفسير غير مرة... رأى أنه قد ثقل عليه درسها وحفظها لكبره وكثرة همومه، فطلب من الشيخ رضي الله عنه أن يجعل له عقيدة أصغر من «الصغرى» بحيث يمكنه درسها وحفظها، فعمل له هذه العقيدة، وكتبها له بخطه)

وقد نبه رضي الله عنه فيها على نكت فائقة، ودرر رائقة، لم يذكرها في «العقائد» السابقة((1))

ثم بعدما رأى الإمام السنوسي انتشار «صغرى الصغرى»، بحمد الله، وظهور بعض الاستفسارات عن عباراتها.. تصدَّى - على سنته في تأليفه - لشرحها، فجاء «شرح صغرى الصغرى» شرحاً نفيساً يستغني عنه طالب مستبحر، وقد وصفه تلميذه الملالي بقوله: ( وفيه فوائد عجيبة، ونكت غريبة ((2))

وممَّا ستراه في «شرح صغرى الصغرى»:
- سهولة العبارة، ووضوح الدليل، والابتعاد قدر الاستطاعة عن الاصطلاحات الكلامية، وهو مسلك متناغم مع أصلها «صغرى الصغرى» أو «العقيدة المختصرة»
- تعظيم الجناب النبوي الشريف بكتابة صفحات طويلة في بدايته، ففاتحته سلامٌ، وخاتمته مسك وطيب، ولا نبالغ إن قلنا: قريب من ربع الكتاب كان لتحقيق هذا المقصد الاعتقادي الذي قد يغفل عنه كثير من الكتاب والباحثين في الاعتقادات
- الترفق يسيراً في مسألة التقليد
- تحريره البديع الذي لا يستغني عنه طالب علم متخصص لمسألة

((1) انظر «المواهب القدسية» (ق ١٠٤) (2) انظر «المواهب القدسية» (ق ١٠٤))
Word about the book Sharh Sughra al-Sughra

There is no doubt that 'al-Sanusiyya al-Sughra' is the core of the blessed 'Sanusiyyat'. It does not have that length which would trouble a student of knowledge. So what compelled Imam al-Sanusi to abbreviate it with 'Sughra al-Sughra' that is in our hands? Between

Indeed, whoever stands on the reason for that knows the utmost care of Imam al-Sanusi in making the creed of Ahl al-Sunna understood, and his exertion of effort in diversifying its formulation and size to achieve this noble goal. So 'Sughra al-Sughra' was authored for the father of the historian of Imam al-Sanusi's biography, Shaykh al-Mallali. He said in 'al-Mawahib al-Qudsiyya': (He composed it for my father - may Allah Almighty preserve him from every affliction and calamity, and grant him the highest rank. And that is because my father, when he read on the Shaykh (may Allah be pleased with him) his 'Aqidat al-Sughra' and completed it with commentary more than once... he saw that studying and memorizing it had become heavy upon him due to his old age and many worries. So he asked the Shaykh (may Allah be pleased with him) to make for him a creed smaller than 'al-Sughra' such that he could study and memorize it. So he composed this creed for him and wrote it in his own handwriting.)

And he (may Allah be pleased with him) pointed out in it fine points and bright gems that he did not mention in the previous creeds.((1))

Then when Imam al-Sanusi saw the spread of 'Sughra al-Sughra', praise be to Allah, and the appearance of some inquiries about its phrases... he set about - according to his custom in authorship - to explain it. So came 'Sharh Sughra al-Sughra' a precious commentary that a student of deep knowledge would not dispense with. His student al-Mallali described it saying: (In it are wonderful benefits and strange subtleties.((2))

And among what you will see in 'Sharh Sughra al-Sughra':
- Ease of expression, clarity of evidence, and avoiding as much as possible the technical terms of kalam. This is an approach consistent with its original 'Sughra al-Sughra' or 'al-Aqida al-Mukhtasara'.
- Glorification of the noble prophetic station by writing long pages at its beginning. Its opening is peace, and its seal is musk and fragrance. And we do not exaggerate if we say that nearly a quarter of the book was for achieving this creedal objective that many writers and researchers in beliefs may neglect.
- Showing some gentleness in the issue of taqlid (imitation in faith).
- Its excellent editing that a specialized student of knowledge cannot do without...

((1) See al-Mawahib al-Qudsiyya (folio 104) (2) See al-Mawahib al-Qudsiyya (folio 104))
Source pages p. 60

Commentary
This passage is an editorial introduction that provides background on the work 'Sharh Sughra al-Sughra' and outlines its features. It begins by praising the 'Sanusiyya al-Sughra' as the core of the Sanusi creeds and notes that it is not overly long. It then explains the motivation for writing an even shorter creed: the father of al-Mallali, a student of Sanusi, found the Sughra too burdensome due to his old age and preoccupations, so he requested a shorter version. Sanusi complied by writing 'Sughra al-Sughra' and included in it fine points not present in his earlier creeds. After the short creed spread and questions arose about its phrasing, Sanusi wrote a commentary on it. The passage then lists the features of this commentary: (1) ease of expression and clarity of evidence, avoiding kalam terminology to stay consistent with the original brief creed; (2) glorification of the Prophet by dedicating roughly a quarter of the book to this topic, with the opening being salutations and the closing being praise; (3) a gentle treatment of the issue of taqlid; and (4) an excellent editing that a specialized student cannot ignore. The passage highlights Sanusi's pedagogical concern and the unique approach of this commentary.

Key Terms
السنوسية الصغرى
صغرى الصغرى
شرح صغرى الصغرى
المواهب القدسية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think Sanusi only wrote his standard creeds and did not tailor them for individuals.
Correction: This passage shows that Sanusi wrote a creed specifically for an elderly student who found the longer version difficult, and later wrote a commentary to clarify it.

Checkpoint
Why did Imam al-Sanusi write 'Sughra al-Sughra'?
A) For general teaching purposes
B) For his own son
C) For the father of al-Mallali, who found the Sughra too long
D) As a summary of his larger works

Key Takeaway
Sharh Sughra al-Sughra is a unique commentary by Sanusi, motivated by a personal request, known for its simplicity, prophetic glorification, leniency on taqlid, and masterful editing.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have learned that Imam al-Sanusi wrote a shorter creed specifically to help one elderly student. When have you made the effort to tailor your teaching or guidance to someone's specific needs? Will you commit to adapting your help to the real situations of those you assist?
?
Nearly a quarter of Sharh Sughra al-Sughra is devoted to glorifying the Prophet ﷺ. In your own study and daily life, is love and praise of the Prophet a priority? What specific action will you take this week to increase your expression of love for him?
?
The commentary shows gentleness in the issue of taqlid. Do you accept your faith based on mere imitation without striving for understanding? What step can you take today to move from blind following to informed conviction?

Introduction and Front Matter

Distinctive Features of the Commentary on Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā
Lists distinctive features and benefits of the commentary.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the distinctive features and benefits of the commentary on Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā as presented by the author.

Core Idea
The commentary combines clarity, theological nuance, literary style, moral education, and broad accessibility.

This passage highlights the unique qualities of the commentary. The author points out its concise explanation of miracles, its theological stance on modes and attributes, its literary style to ease difficulty, its moral depth, and its usefulness for both commoners and specialists. Reading this helps us appreciate the commentary's approach and its intended impact.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Let the author's own enumeration of features come through.
Notice the bullet markers and the standalone word 'القارئ'.
2
Identify each feature
List each distinctive feature mentioned in the passage. The author mentions five or six points, including the explanation of miracles, negation of modes, literary style, moral education, and the issue of superiority.
3
Understand the theological reference
The phrase 'نفي الأحوال والصفات المعنوية' refers to a theological position about modes (aḥwāl) and essential attributes. Recognize that this is a technical point.
This is not a rejection of all attributes, but a specific stance in kalām.
4
Reflect on the intended audience
The final sentences describe how the commentary benefits both the common person and the specialist. Consider why this dual benefit is important.
5
Review the translation and commentary
Read the translation and the teacher's commentary to solidify your understanding of each feature.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجه دلالة المعجزة على صدق الرسول بعبارة في غاية الوجازة والوضوح
اختياره هنا لنفي الأحوال والصفات المعنوية على القول بها التوسع في الحديث عن الصفات الواجبة للأنبياء عليهم الصلاة والسلام .
- ميله للأسلوب الأدبي في كثير من مقطعات الكتاب ؛ ليريح قارئه من عسر بعض الأبحاث
القارئ
عجن النص بالتربية الأخلاقية ، وتعميق مفهوم العبودية في نفس
•
- تحرير مسألة الأفضلية لحضرة مولانا ونبينا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام .
ويمكننا أن نقول : يصلحُ أن يكون هذا الشرح المبارك عرضاً للعقيدة الإسلامية على مسامع العامة والخاصة ؛ فينتفع العامي بعموم مباحثه ويرسخ الاعتقاد في فؤاده ، وينتعش المتخصص بنكاته وغريب تحريراته وفوائده ، نفع الله به وبسائر تصانیف مؤلفه ، آمين .
The aspect of the miracle's indication of the truthfulness of the messenger in an extremely concise and clear expression. His choice here to negate the modes and the attributes [ṣifāt maʿnawiyya] according to the view that holds them, expansion in discussing the attributes obligatory for the prophets, peace be upon them. His inclination towards the literary style in many sections of the book, to relieve his reader from the difficulty of some investigations. The reader. He kneaded the text with moral education and deepening the concept of servitude in the soul. • Resolving the issue of superiority [al-afḍaliyya] for our master and prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. And we can say: This blessed commentary is suitable as a presentation of Islamic creed to the ears of the common people and the elite; the common person benefits from the generality of its discussions and belief becomes established in his heart, and the specialist thrives on its subtleties and unusual analyses and benefits. May Allah benefit by it and by all the works of its author, Amen.
Source pages p. 62

Commentary
This passage is a list of distinctive features and benefits of the commentary on Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā. It begins by highlighting the explanation of how miracles indicate a prophet's truthfulness, done with extreme conciseness and clarity. Next, it mentions the author's choice to reject the concepts of modes (aḥwāl) and essential attributes (ṣifāt maʿnawiyya) according to the opinion that affirms them, indicating a theological position. Then it notes the author's use of literary style in many parts of the book to ease the reader's difficulty. The text then mentions incorporating moral education and deepening the concept of servitude. Another feature is settling the question of superiority (afḍaliyya) of Prophet Muhammad. Finally, it states that the commentary is suitable for both the general public and specialists: the common person benefits from its broad discussions and deepened belief, while the specialist benefits from its subtle points and unique analyses. The passage concludes with a prayer for benefit.

Key Terms
المعجزة
الأحوال
الصفات المعنوية
الأفضلية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The negation of modes and attributes means the commentary rejects all divine attributes.
Correction: The passage specifically refers to the theological concepts of aḥwāl and ṣifāt maʿnawiyya, not all attributes. It affirms obligatory attributes for prophets.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is one purpose of the author's literary style in the commentary?
A) To impress the reader with eloquence
B) To relieve the reader from the difficulty of some investigations
C) To shorten the text
D) To show off his knowledge

Key Takeaway
The commentary is designed to be both accessible to laypeople and deep enough for specialists, combining clear theological exposition with literary grace and moral teaching.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
After reading how this commentary combines theological depth with moral education, ask yourself: which aspect of your own faith do you neglect more—deep study or sincere servitude? Will you commit to balancing both starting today?
?
The author designed the book for both the common person and the specialist. Are you approaching Islamic learning with the humility of a beginner or the arrogance of someone who already knows? How can you benefit from this commentary as if it were written for you alone?
?
The text mentions resolving the issue of the Prophet's superiority. Do you truly feel in your heart that Muhammad is the most beloved to you, more than your own self and family? If not, what concrete action will you take today to increase your love for him?

Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
4 min
Author's Introduction: Purpose and Motivation for the Commentary
Sanusi explains his purpose in writing a commentary on his creedal summary.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why al-Sanusi wrote his creedal summary and commentary, and the pedagogical goal of moving from blind imitation to certain knowledge.

Core Idea
Al-Sanusi wrote to rescue believers from ignorance and blind imitation through clear rational proofs.

Al-Sanusi's introduction might seem like a formality, but it lays out his entire educational project. He tells us that he first wrote a short creedal summary, and now he is writing a commentary to remove any confusion. The goal is not just to state beliefs, but to ground them in understanding so that the believer is no longer blindly following but has certain knowledge. This sets the tone for the entire work.

Guided Path
1
Read the original Arabic
Read the Arabic passage once to get the rhythm and flow. Notice the author's name and the basmala. Then read the translation.
Pay attention to the phrase 'جوامع الكلام' which is a key concept.
2
Identify the author's stated purpose
Al-Sanusi explains he wrote a summary 'on a way that the mukallaf emerges from darkness of ignorance and blind imitation'. Then he says he intends to follow it with a commentary. Find these two statements and understand their connection.
The commentary is meant to clarify the summary, not to add new content.
3
Reflect on the pedagogical aim
Consider why al-Sanusi emphasizes 'emerging from blind imitation'. In Islamic theology, taqlid is not sufficient for essential beliefs; one must have rational proofs. This introduction signals the methodology of the entire commentary.
This connects to the broader purpose of kalam: to provide demonstrative knowledge of God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مقدمة المؤلّف
الله الرحمن الرحيم
قالَ الشيخُ الفقيه الإمامُ العالمُ ؛ أبو عبد الله محمد بن يوسف السَّنوسي الحسنيُّ ، رحمهُ الله وغفر له ولوالديه ، آمينَ :
الحمدُ للهِ الذي مَنَّ علينا بالإيمان والإسلام ، وهدانا بنبينا ومولانا محمد عليه الصلاةُ والسلامُ ، فبيَّنَ للناس معرفة مولانا العظيم على وجه التمامِ ، وبلغ لهم عنِ اللهِ تعالى الحلال والحرام وسائر الأحكامِ ، وخُص صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ في جميع ذلك بجوامع الكلام ، وتيسير المعاني للإعمال والإفهام
وبعد :
فقد وضعتُ جملةً مختصرةً فيما يجب على المكلَّف اعتقاده حقه تعالى وفي حقٌّ رسلِهِ ، على وجه يخرجُ بِهِ المكلَّفُ مِنْ ظلماتِ الجهل والتقليد ، فأردتُ أن أُتبعها بشرح مختصر يكشف عن معانيها كلَّ لَبْس وتعقيد ، والله تعالى أسألُ أن ينفع بهِ ، إِنَّهُ ولي التوفيق والتسديد .
(۱) ضُبطت في ( أ ) : ( للأعمال والأفهام ) ، وفي ( هـ ) وحدها : ( للإعلام )
بدل ( للإعمال والإفهام ) .
۱۰۳
Author's Introduction. In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. The shaykh, the jurist, the imam, the learned, Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi al-Hasani, may God have mercy upon him and forgive him and his parents, amen, said: Praise be to God who has bestowed upon us faith and Islam, and guided us through our Prophet and master Muhammad, upon him be blessings and peace, who explained to people the knowledge of our Great Master in perfect manner, and conveyed to them from God Almighty the lawful and the unlawful and all rulings, and he, may God bless him and grant him peace, was distinguished in all that by comprehensive speech, and facilitation of meanings for action and understanding. To proceed: I have written a concise summary concerning what is obligatory for the legally responsible person to believe concerning God Almighty and concerning His messengers, in a way that the legally responsible person emerges thereby from the darkness of ignorance and blind imitation. So I intended to follow it with a concise commentary that unveils their meanings from every obscurity and complexity. And I ask God Almighty to benefit through it; indeed He is the Guardian of success and guidance. (1) Recorded in (A) as 'for action and understanding' and in (H) alone as 'for notification' instead of 'for action and understanding'.
Source pages p. 102

Commentary
This passage is the author's introduction, a formal opening in which al-Sanusi states his purpose for writing this commentary. It begins with the basmala and the author's full name and lineage, followed by a prayer for himself and his parents. He then praises God for the gifts of faith and Islam, and for sending the Prophet Muhammad who conveyed religious knowledge and laws with clarity and comprehensiveness. The phrase 'جوامع الكلام' refers to the Prophet's gift of concise speech. After the 'Amma ba'd' transition, al-Sanusi reveals his project: he previously wrote a concise summary of what every mukallaf (legally accountable person) must believe about God and His messengers. That summary was designed to rescue the believer from ignorance and taqlid (blind imitation). Now, he intends to write a short commentary to clarify any obscurities or complexities in that summary. He concludes by asking God for success and benefit. The footnote records a textual variant in the phrase 'للأعمال والإفهام' (for action and understanding) which appears as 'للإعلام' (for notification) in some manuscripts.

Key Terms
المكلَّف
التقليد
الشرح المختصر
جوامع الكلام
الحمد لله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The author's introduction is merely ceremonial praise and does not contain substantive information about the work's purpose.
Correction: Al-Sanusi explicitly states his purpose: to write a summary and commentary that leads believers out of ignorance and blind imitation, establishing a rational foundation for faith.

Checkpoint
According to the introduction, what is the purpose of the concise summary that al-Sanusi wrote?
A) To compile all Islamic rulings in one volume.
B) To enable the mukallaf to emerge from ignorance and blind imitation.
C) To refute heretical sects.
D) To provide a commentary on the Quran.

Key Takeaway
Al-Sanusi wrote his commentary to clarify his creedal summary, aiming to replace blind imitation with certain knowledge for the believer.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever held a belief about God simply because your parents or teachers said so, without understanding the rational proof? What is one such belief you now realize you took on blind faith? Will you commit to learning its evidence this week?
?
Al-Sanusi wrote to rescue people from the darkness of ignorance and taqlid. In which area of your religious practice are you still merely imitating others without conviction? What is one step you can take today to replace that imitation with certain knowledge?
?
If you were asked by a skeptic why you believe in God, could you provide a clear rational argument? If not, why have you not sought to learn it? What is holding you back from acquiring the certainty that al-Sanusi considered essential?

Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
4 min
Author's Preface and Purpose
Sanusi opens with praise and states his book's aim.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the author's stated purpose and the methodological significance of his preface in combating ignorance and blind imitation.

Core Idea
This book is a tool to free the mind from ignorance and blind imitation.

This opening passage is not just a formality; it sets the entire methodology of the book. Sanusi tells us his aim: to provide a concise summary that leads the believer from ignorance and blind imitation to certain knowledge. Let's see how he frames his work and what he hopes to achieve.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic preface
Start by reading the Arabic passage from beginning to end. Notice the structure: basmala, praise to God, mention of Prophet Muhammad, then the author's purpose.
The author includes his full name and lineage.
2
Identify the author's goal
Look for the phrase 'جملة مختصرة في ما يجب على المكلف اعتقاده' and notice the verbs: 'وضعت' (I composed) and 'أتبعها بشرح مختصر' (follow it with a commentary). The goal is to 'يخرج به المكلف من ظلمات الجهل والتقليد'.
The phrase 'ظلمات الجهل والتقليد' (darknesses of ignorance and blind imitation) is key.
3
Reflect on the purpose of the commentary
The author says the commentary will 'يكشف عن معانيها كل لبس وتعقيد' (unveil its meanings from every obscurity and complexity). This shows the intended depth.
4
Answer the checkpoint
Test your understanding by answering the quiz question. This will reinforce the main purpose.
The answer is B.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم
قال الشيخ الفقيه الإمام العالم أبو عبد الله محمد بن يوسف السنوسي الحسني، رحمه الله وغفر له ولوالديه، آمين:
الحمد لله الذي من علينا بالإيمان والإسلام، وهدانا بنبينا ومولانا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام، فبين للناس معرفة مولانا العظيم على وجه التمام، وبلغ لهم عن الله تعالى الحلال والحرام وسائر الأحكام، وخص صلى الله عليه وسلم في جميع ذلك بجوامع الكلام، وتيسير المعاني للإعمال والإفهام.
وبعد:
(1)
فقد وضعت جملة مختصرة فيما يجب على المكلف اعتقاده حقه تعالى وفي حق رسله، على وجه يخرج به المكلف من ظلمات الجهل والتقليد، فأردت أن أتبعها بشرح مختصر يكشف عن معانيها كل لبس وتعقيد، والله تعالى أسأل أن ينفع به، إنه ولي التوفيق والتسديد.
In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. The shaykh, the jurist, the imam, the scholar, Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi al-Hasani, may God have mercy on him and forgive him and his parents, Amen, said: Praise be to God who bestowed upon us faith and Islam, and guided us through our Prophet and master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, who explained to people the knowledge of our Great Master completely, and conveyed to them from God Almighty the lawful and the unlawful and all rulings, and he was distinguished, may God bless him and grant him peace, in all that with comprehensive speech, and facilitation of meanings for actions and understanding. To proceed: (1) I have composed a concise summary of what is obligatory for the legally responsible person to believe concerning the right of God Almighty and concerning the rights of His messengers, in a manner by which the legally responsible person emerges from the darknesses of ignorance and blind imitation. I intended to follow it with a concise commentary that unveils its meanings from every obscurity and complexity. I ask God Almighty to benefit [people] by it; indeed, He is the Guardian of success and right guidance.
Source pages p. 103

Commentary
This passage is the author's preface. It begins with the basmala and the author's name, followed by praise to God for faith and Islam, and for sending the Prophet Muhammad who conveyed all matters. The author then states his purpose: he wrote a concise summary of required beliefs about God and His messengers, aiming to free the legally responsible person from ignorance and taqlid (blind imitation). He then intends to add a commentary to clarify its meanings. He concludes by asking God for benefit and success. This sets the methodological tone for the work.

Key Terms
المكلف
التقليد
جملة مختصرة
شرح مختصر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The preface is merely a formal introduction with no substantive content.
Correction: The preface establishes the work's purpose of combating taqlid and ignorance, which is central to the entire book's methodology.

Checkpoint
What is the primary purpose of the concise summary according to the author?
A) To detail all Islamic rulings
B) To free the mukallaf from ignorance and blind imitation
C) To refute heretical sects
D) To discuss logic and philosophy

Key Takeaway
Sanusi wrote this book to lead the mukallaf out of ignorance and blind imitation through a concise summary and commentary on essential beliefs.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you been content with blind imitation in your own beliefs, simply following what you were taught without examining the evidence? What darkness of ignorance might you still be in, and what step will you take today to seek clarity?
?
Sanusi wrote this book to free the mukallaf from ignorance. Are you truly free, or do you rely on others' opinions without understanding? What will you do today to emerge from that darkness into the light of certainty?
?
The author asks God to benefit by his book. Are you approaching this study with a sincere intention to be transformed, or just to gather information? How will you ensure that you do not merely accumulate knowledge without it changing your heart and actions?

Introduction and Front Matter
beginner
4 min
Praise of the Prophet and His Ummah
Sanusi praises the Prophet's mission and its effects on the ummah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will appreciate how Sanusi eulogizes the Prophet's mission and the unique spiritual and numerical favors bestowed upon his ummah.

Core Idea
The Prophet's mission brought divine knowledge and produced spiritual elites; his ummah fills two-thirds of Paradise.

This passage is a devotional eulogy rather than a theological argument. Sanusi celebrates the transformative impact of the Prophet's coming, from dispelling ignorance to granting treasures of divine knowledge. He then highlights the exceptional status of this ummah, which is preserved in faith and will populate the majority of Paradise. Read it as a meditation on divine grace.

Guided Path
1
Read the full passage
Read the Arabic passage once slowly, then the English translation. Let the poetic rhythm and devotional tone sink in before analyzing.
This passage is not a proof but a praise; treat it as such.
2
Identify the effects of the Prophet's mission
Notice how Sanusi describes the removal of darkness, spread of faith, and the outpouring of divine treasures. List the key blessings mentioned.
These include knowledge, wisdom, secret jewels, and spiritual elites.
3
Examine the spiritual hierarchy
Look at the terms for the spiritual elites: poles, pegs, leaders, righteous, substitutes. Reflect on their role in sustaining faith across generations.
These are standard Sufi categories but here they indicate the abundance of saints.
4
Reflect on the ummah's status
Consider the promise that the ummah will be preserved until the Day of Judgment and that its members will constitute two-thirds of Paradise. This is a great honor.
The hadith about 120 rows is cited to support this.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مولانا جل وعلا على كافَّةِ المؤمنينَ ، وتنفتح أبوابها التي لم يتجاسر أحدٌ مِنْ أهل الكمالاتِ على طلب فتحها ، وتنتشر بعنايته العظمى التي تَفضَّلَ بها المولى تبارك وتعالى على أهل الإيمانِ بهِ .. أنواع السرور وتنكشفُ عن الظواهر والبواطن أجناس الغموم وأنواع الشرورِ. وببركة مبعثِهِ الشريف وطلوع طلعتِهِ البهية السعيدة على الأرضِ انكشفت ظلماتُ الكفر والجهالات التي عمَّتْ وانتشرَتْ ، وتمكَّنَتْ غاية التمكُن في جميع الآفاقِ والقلوبِ ، وتشعشعَتْ أنوار الإيمان بالله تعالى وبرسله وكتبه وملائكتِهِ ، وانقلعَتْ بفضل الله تعالى سحائب رانِ الجهلِ وغُمَّةُ السيئاتِ والذنوبِ ، وأفاض سبحانه رحمته على الخلقِ ، وأخرجَ لهم على يد مصطفاه سيّدِنا ومولانا محمَّدٍ صلى الله عليهِ وسلَّمَ ذخائر المعارفِ الربَّانية ، ونفائس الحكم والعلوم اللدنية ، وحلاهم بجواهرِ الأسرارِ التي خَبَأَها لهم في خزائنِ الغيوبِ ، حتى كثُرَ منهم في كل جيل الأقطابُ والأوتاد والنقباء والأخيار والأبدال وعجَّتِ الأرضُ ؛ سهلها وجبلها ، برُّها وبحرها .. بتوحيد المولى تبارك وتعالى ، والتنويه بأقدارِ رسله وملائكته وكتبه ، واللَّهَج بشكرِهِ سبحانه وذكره وحمده على كل حال وبكل كمال. وانتشرَتْ أمَّةُ نبينا ومولانا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام وتطاولتْ أزمنتها إلى موافاة القيامة ، وحفظ الله سبحانَهُ عليهِمُ الإيمانَ مَعَ اختلافِ الدُّوَلِ وانتشارِ المِحَنِ وبُعْدِ العهدِ مِنْ مشاهدة أهل الحقِّ والسنن والاستقامة ، ونمَّى سبحانَهُ أنوارَهُمُ المعنوية والحسيَّةَ دنيا وأخرى ، حتى كادوا كلُّهُم مِنْ حِكَمِ قلوبهم وسطوع أنوارهم وامتدادها أن يكونوا أنبياء ، وأكثر سبحانه عددَهُم كثرةً عظيمة تخرج عن الحصر ، حتى جعلَهُم بفضلِهِ ورحمتِهِ ثلثي جميع مَنْ يدخلُ الجنَّةَ مِنَ السعداء ، وقد ورد : أنَّ صفوفَ أهل الجنَّةِ مَئَةٌ وعشرون صفاً
Our Master, may He be exalted and glorified, be upon all the believers, and open its doors which none among the people of perfections dared to request opening, and spread by His great care which the Lord, blessed and exalted, bestowed upon the people of faith in Him... types of joy, and uncover from the outer and inner aspects the species of sorrows and types of evils. And by the blessing of His noble mission and the rising of His beautiful and blessed countenance upon the earth, the darknesses of disbelief and ignorance that spread and extended were removed, and they had taken firm hold in all regions and hearts, and the lights of faith in Allah the Exalted, His messengers, His books, and His angels shone brightly, and by the grace of Allah the Exalted the clouds of the rust of ignorance and the gloom of evils and sins were uprooted, and He, glorified be He, poured forth His mercy upon creation, and brought out for them at the hand of His chosen one, our Master and Lord Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, the treasures of divine knowledge (al-maʿārif al-rabbāniyya) and the precious gems of wisdom and infused sciences (al-ʿulūm al-laduniyya), and adorned them with the jewels of the secrets that He had hidden for them in the treasuries of the unseen, until there became numerous among them in every generation the poles, the pegs, the leaders, the righteous, and the substitutes, and the earth teemed—its plains and its mountains, its land and its sea—with the tawḥīd of the Lord, blessed and exalted, and with magnifying the ranks of His messengers, angels, and books, and with devotion to thanking Him, glorified be He, remembering Him, and praising Him in every state and with every perfection. And the community of our Prophet and Lord Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, spread, and its times extended until the advent of the Resurrection, and Allah, glorified be He, preserved faith among them despite the succession of dynasties, the spread of trials, and the length of time from witnessing the people of truth, prophetic practice, and uprightness, and He, glorified be He, increased their spiritual and physical lights in this world and the next, until all of them, due to the wisdom of their hearts and the radiance and extension of their lights, nearly became prophets, and He, glorified be He, multiplied their number with a great abundance beyond enumeration, until He made them, by His grace and mercy, two-thirds of all the blessed people who enter Paradise, and it has been reported: that the rows of the people of Paradise are one hundred and twenty rows.
Source pages p. 110

Commentary
This passage is a eulogy of the Prophet Muhammad's mission and its blessings upon the believers. It begins by invoking divine favor and describes how the Prophet's advent opened doors of joy and removed sorrows. Then it details how through his mission, darkness of disbelief and ignorance was dispelled, and faith in Allah, His messengers, His books, and His angels spread. It highlights that Allah granted treasures of divine knowledge (al-maʿārif al-rabbāniyya) and infused sciences (al-ʿulūm al-laduniyya) through the Prophet, and that from each generation emerged spiritual elites such as the poles (al-aqṭāb), pegs (al-awtād), leaders (al-nuqabāʾ), righteous (al-akhyār), and substitutes (al-abdāl). The earth became filled with tawḥīd and praise of Allah's messengers, angels, and books. The passage then focuses on the Prophet's community (umma), stating that it will persist until the Day of Judgment, protected in faith despite changing dynasties and trials. It says their light—both spiritual and physical—increased to the extent that they nearly become prophets, and their numbers are so vast that they constitute two-thirds of the inhabitants of Paradise, as signified by the hadith that the rows of Paradise are 120 rows meaning the umma will occupy a large portion.

Key Terms
المعارف الربانية
العلوم اللدنية
الأقطاب والأوتاد والنقباء والأخيار والأبدال
صفوف الجنة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Students might think this passage is a theological proof or argument for a specific doctrine.
Correction: This passage is a devotional eulogy, not a logical proof. Sanusi is praising, not arguing; it should be read as an expression of gratitude and love for the Prophet and his ummah.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what did Allah bring out through the Prophet Muhammad (ﷺ) for the believers?
A) Lights of faith and tawḥīd
B) Treasures of divine knowledge and precious gems of wisdom and infused sciences
C) Abundance of wealth and power
D) The ability to perform miracles

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's mission is the source of divine knowledge and spiritual excellence, and his ummah is divinely preserved and will form the majority of Paradise's inhabitants.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage says the Prophet's mission removed the 'darknesses of disbelief and ignorance' that had taken 'firm hold in all regions and hearts.' Where in your own heart do you still allow darkness of ignorance—about Allah, His religion, or your own soul—to remain entrenched, even after hearing this truth? What concrete step will you take this week to let the Prophet's light expose that area?
?
The text mentions that the ummah will fill two-thirds of Paradise 'by His grace and mercy.' Do you live as if this is an automatic guarantee for you, or do you feel the weight of personal responsibility to be among those who truly reflect the Prophet's mission? What one habit do you need to change to align your life more closely with the purpose of praising Allah and spreading tawḥīd?
?
The spiritual elites (aqṭāb, awtād, etc.) arose 'in every generation' from this ummah. Are you content to be a passive spectator while others carry the torch of divine knowledge and spiritual excellence, or do you sense a personal calling to rise above mediocrity? What is holding you back from becoming one of 'the righteous' (الأخيار) in your own time and place?

p. 8
Module 2 · Lesson 1

Allah's Existence
beginner
6 min
Blind Imitation of the Tawhid Proof is Insufficient
Sanusi criticizes those who recite the tawhid verse without understanding its logical force.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why merely memorizing or reciting the tawhid verse without grasping its rational proof constitutes blind imitation (taqlid), and how the jurists evaluated such a person's faith.

Core Idea
Reciting a proof without understanding its logic is still blind imitation.

Sanusi moves from the general principle that taqlid is insufficient to a specific example: those who recite the Quranic proof of tawhid (21:22) but do not perceive its rational force. This passage clarifies what counts as true knowledge and why mere memorization, though it might accidentally align with truth, is not praiseworthy.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Identify the two shortcomings
Find where Sanusi says the person fails to perceive: (1) the aspect of the impossibility of multiplicity, and (2) the meaning of corruption in the verse. These are the two elements of rational comprehension that are missing.
The verse alone is not enough—understanding the logical implications is required.
3
Understand the jurists' dual ruling
Note that the jurists both rule his deliverance (najat) and his great sin. Why? Because his imitation accidentally coincided with truth, but he resembles those who blindly follow their fathers.
This shows that accidental correctness does not remove blame.
4
Reflect on the condition of certainty
The jurists require that the muqallid have certainty (jazzm) and not abandon his belief if the imitated one does. Consider why this condition is important.
Certainty here is about firm conviction, not rational proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فمَنْ ظَنَّ أنه أحاط بدليل الوحدانية بحفظه أو تلاوته لقوله عَزَّ من قائل : ﴿ لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَا آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللهُ لَفَسَدَتَا ﴾ [الأنبياء : ٢٢] دون أن يدرك وجهَ استحالة التعدد ، ومعنى الفساد المقصود من الآية . . فهو مقلدٌ، مَثَلُهُ كمثل أي مقلد لاعتقاد آخر، وإنما قرَّر الفقهاء نجاته لكون تقليده وقع اتفاقاً للحقِّ ؛ بشرط جزمه وعدم تركه لمعتقده إن تركه المُقلَّد ، وقالوا بعظيم معصيته لكونه أشبه الذين ألفوا آباءهم ضالين، فهم على آثارهم يهرعون
So whoever thinks that he has encompassed the proof of oneness by memorizing or reciting His saying, Mighty is He: 'If there had been in them gods besides Allah, they would have corrupted' [Al-Anbiya:22] without perceiving the aspect of the impossibility of multiplicity and the meaning of corruption intended by the verse, then he is a blind follower (muqallid), his example is like any follower of another belief. Indeed, the jurists ruled his deliverance because his imitation happened to coincide with the truth, on condition of his certainty and his not abandoning his belief if the imitated one abandons it, and they said his sin is great because he resembles those who found their fathers astray, so they rush in their footsteps.
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage presents a specific case of the general principle from the previous lesson: a person who merely memorizes or recites the famous Quranic proof of tawhid—'If there had been in them gods besides Allah, they would have corrupted' (21:22)—without understanding the rational aspect of the impossibility (istihalah) of multiplicity and the exact meaning of 'corruption' (fasad) in the verse, is still a blind follower (muqallid). Such a person's belief is not based on rational comprehension but on imitation. The author notes that jurists (fuqaha') have ruled that such a person is saved (najat) because his imitation coincidentally aligns with the truth, provided he has certainty (jazzm) and does not abandon his creed if the one he imitates abandons it. However, they consider his sin great because he resembles those who blindly follow their fathers, even if they are astray. Thus, mere memorization without rational insight is inadequate.

Key Terms
دليل الوحدانية
التوحيد
المقلد
الاستحالة
الفساد
التقليد
الجزم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The jurists' ruling of 'najat' (salvation) means that blind imitation is acceptable.
Correction: The jurists still call it a great sin because the person resembles those who blindly follow their fathers, even if his imitation coincidentally aligns with truth. True faith requires rational comprehension.

Checkpoint
Why did the jurists consider such a muqallid's sin great?
A) Because he memorized a verse incorrectly
B) Because he resembles those who follow their fathers even if astray
C) Because he did not pray
D) Because he rejected the Quran

Key Takeaway
Reciting a Quranic proof without understanding its rational argument is still blind imitation, and while such a person may be saved by accident, they are guilty of a great sin for resembling those who follow their fathers in error.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever recited 'If there were multiple gods, they would have corrupted' without truly grasping why multiplicity is impossible? Are you willing to confront whether your own belief is based on imitation rather than understanding? What step will you take today to move from recitation to comprehension?
?
The jurists say your imitation may save you accidentally, but you still bear great sin for resembling those who blindly follow their fathers in error. Does this description fit you? Are you prepared to examine the rational foundations of your faith, even if it means unsettling the certainty you inherited?
?
When you think about your core Islamic beliefs, how much of what you hold is because you have rationally verified it, and how much is simply what you were taught? If one of your core beliefs were challenged, would you be able to defend it with reasoned arguments, or would you only recite verses? Commit to learning one rational proof for a belief you hold.

Allah's Existence
beginner
3 min
Proof for God's Existence from the World
Sanusi states the proof for God's existence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the existence of the world is a rational proof for the existence of God.

Core Idea
The world's existence directly proves its Creator.

Sanusi begins with the most fundamental proof: the world itself. Before discussing God's attributes, we must first establish that He exists. This lesson presents that foundational proof.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the concise structure: 'The proof... is the existence of the world.'
2
Understand the implication
The proof is based on the contingency of the world. The world exists and is not self-sufficient, so it requires a cause. This cause is God.
This is a brief premise; further details are in standard kalam.
3
Relate to the lesson
Ask yourself how this proof sets the stage for the subsequent attribute proofs.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على وجودِهِ تعالى : وجود العالم . ولو لم يكن قديماً لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن باقياً لم يكن قديماً ، ولو لم يكن مخالفاً لخلقِهِ لكان مثلهم ، ولو لم يكن قائماً بنفسِهِ لاحتاج إلى محلّ أو مخصص ، ولو افتقر إلى محل لكان صفةً ، ولو احتاج إلى مخصص لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن واحداً لكان مقهوراً؛ ﴿ وَهُوَ الْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِ ﴾ . ولو لم تجب له القدرة والإرادة والعلم والحياة لما كانَ شيءٌ مِنْ خلقِهِ ، ولو لم يتصف بالسمع والبصر والكلام لكان ناقصاً ، تعالى عن ذلك علوّاً كبيراً . ولو لم يكن فعل الممكناتِ أو تركها جائزاً في حقه تعالى لانقلبَتِ الحقائق ، وقلب الحقائقِ مستحيل .
And the proof for His existence, exalted: the existence of the world. And if He were not eternal, He would be temporal. And if He were not everlasting, He would not be eternal. And if He were not different from His creation, He would be like them. And if He were not self-subsisting, He would need a locus or a specifier. And if He needed a locus, He would be an attribute. And if He needed a specifier, He would be temporal. And if He were not one, He would be overpowered; {And He is the Overpowerer above His servants} (6:18). And if power, will, knowledge, and life were not necessary for Him, nothing of His creation would exist. And if He were not described with hearing, sight, and speech, He would be deficient – exalted above that greatly. And if doing or leaving possible things were not permissible in His right, the realities would be inverted, and the inversion of realities is impossible.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage provides rational proofs for the attributes enumerated earlier. Each proof follows a conditional structure: if the opposite of an attribute were true, a contradiction would follow. The proof for existence starts from the existence of the world, which requires a Creator. Eternity is proven because if not eternal, He would be temporal (hadith), which is impossible for God. Everlastingness follows from eternity. Being different from creation (mukhalafat lil-hawadith) is proven because otherwise He would be like them, implying similarity. Self-subsistence (qiyam bi-nafsih) is proven because need for a locus would make Him an attribute, and need for a specifier would make Him temporal. Oneness is proven by the argument that if there were multiple gods, they would conflict and be overpowered, but God is al-Qahir (the Overpowerer), as cited from the Qur'an. The necessary attributes (qudra, irada, 'ilm, haya) are proven because without them creation would not exist. The attributes of hearing, sight, speech are proven because lacking them would be deficiency. Finally, the permissible attribute (doing or leaving possible acts) is proven because if it were not permissible, the very nature of possibility would be inverted, which is absurd.

Key Terms
وجود العالم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the proof depends on a specific religious text, but it is purely rational.
Correction: The proof is based on the observed existence of the world and the principle that contingent things need a cause.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the proof for God's existence?
A) The Qur'an
B) The existence of the world
C) The attributes of God
D) The angels

Key Takeaway
The existence of the world is a direct rational proof for the existence of God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that the existence of the world is sufficient proof for God. Look around you right now. Does the world you see actually convince you of His existence, or have you become numb to this proof? What would it take for you to truly see the world as the evidence it is?
?
If someone asked you why you believe in God, would you simply point to the world around you? Or do you rely on other proofs? Are you willing to let the sheer fact of existence be enough for you, or do you still demand more signs?

Proof for God's Eternity and Everlastingness
Sanusi proves God's eternity and everlastingness with conditionals.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must be eternal and everlasting, as proven by the impossibility of the opposite.

Core Idea
God cannot be temporal; He must be without beginning and end.

Having established God's existence, Sanusi now proves that He must be eternal (without beginning) and everlasting (without end). These two attributes are proven through conditionals that lead to absurdities if denied.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Notice the two parallel conditionals: one for eternity, one for everlastingness.
2
Analyze the first conditional
'If He were not eternal, He would be temporal.' A temporal being is created and needs a creator, but God is the Creator.
This is a reductio ad absurdum.
3
Analyze the second conditional
'If He were not everlasting, He would not be eternal.' Everlastingness is a consequence of eternity; if He has no end, He must have no beginning.
4
Synthesize
Together, these prove God's timelessness.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على وجودِهِ تعالى : وجود العالم . ولو لم يكن قديماً لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن باقياً لم يكن قديماً ، ولو لم يكن مخالفاً لخلقِهِ لكان مثلهم ، ولو لم يكن قائماً بنفسِهِ لاحتاج إلى محلّ أو مخصص ، ولو افتقر إلى محل لكان صفةً ، ولو احتاج إلى مخصص لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن واحداً لكان مقهوراً؛ ﴿ وَهُوَ الْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِ ﴾ . ولو لم تجب له القدرة والإرادة والعلم والحياة لما كانَ شيءٌ مِنْ خلقِهِ ، ولو لم يتصف بالسمع والبصر والكلام لكان ناقصاً ، تعالى عن ذلك علوّاً كبيراً . ولو لم يكن فعل الممكناتِ أو تركها جائزاً في حقه تعالى لانقلبَتِ الحقائق ، وقلب الحقائقِ مستحيل .
And the proof for His existence, exalted: the existence of the world. And if He were not eternal, He would be temporal. And if He were not everlasting, He would not be eternal. And if He were not different from His creation, He would be like them. And if He were not self-subsisting, He would need a locus or a specifier. And if He needed a locus, He would be an attribute. And if He needed a specifier, He would be temporal. And if He were not one, He would be overpowered; {And He is the Overpowerer above His servants} (6:18). And if power, will, knowledge, and life were not necessary for Him, nothing of His creation would exist. And if He were not described with hearing, sight, and speech, He would be deficient – exalted above that greatly. And if doing or leaving possible things were not permissible in His right, the realities would be inverted, and the inversion of realities is impossible.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage provides rational proofs for the attributes enumerated earlier. Each proof follows a conditional structure: if the opposite of an attribute were true, a contradiction would follow. The proof for existence starts from the existence of the world, which requires a Creator. Eternity is proven because if not eternal, He would be temporal (hadith), which is impossible for God. Everlastingness follows from eternity. Being different from creation (mukhalafat lil-hawadith) is proven because otherwise He would be like them, implying similarity. Self-subsistence (qiyam bi-nafsih) is proven because need for a locus would make Him an attribute, and need for a specifier would make Him temporal. Oneness is proven by the argument that if there were multiple gods, they would conflict and be overpowered, but God is al-Qahir (the Overpowerer), as cited from the Qur'an. The necessary attributes (qudra, irada, 'ilm, haya) are proven because without them creation would not exist. The attributes of hearing, sight, speech are proven because lacking them would be deficiency. Finally, the permissible attribute (doing or leaving possible acts) is proven because if it were not permissible, the very nature of possibility would be inverted, which is absurd.

Key Takeaway
God must be eternal and everlasting; otherwise, absurdities follow.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God is truly eternal—without beginning—how does that change the way you view your own life, which has a definite start and end? In what area of your life are you living as if time is irrelevant to your relationship with Him?
?
Knowing that God is everlasting means He will still be there after every single thing you love in this world has perished. What is one attachment you need to loosen today because it distracts you from seeking the One who remains forever?
?
The passage links everlastingness to eternity: if God were not everlasting, He would not be eternal. How does this truth challenge you to think of God not as a 'once upon a time' being but as the ever-present reality that demands a constant response from you right now? What will you do today to make that response real?

Proof for God's Difference from Creation
Sanusi proves God's dissimilarity from creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must be different from His creation, as proven by the impossibility of similarity.

Core Idea
God cannot be like His creation; He is absolutely unique.

Sanusi now proves the attribute of mukhalafat lil-hawadith (difference from created things). This is a key principle in Islamic theology: God is unlike anything in creation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
A single conditional: if not different, then like them.
2
Understand the implication
If God were like His creation, He would be subject to the same limitations and contingency. That would contradict His divinity.
3
Reflect on relevance
This attribute prevents anthropomorphism and ensures God's transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

?

This lesson demands that you correct that thought right now. Write down one way you will remind yourself daily that God is self-sufficient and perfect.
?
The proof is simple but profound: God must be different, or He would be like us. Are there areas in your life where you treat God as if He is distant or limited like a human? This lesson calls you to realign your heart with His absolute uniqueness. What specific step will you take today to honour His incomparability?

Proof for God's Self-Subsistence
Sanusi proves God's self-subsistence via reductio.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God must be self-subsisting (qiyam bi-nafsih), meaning He needs no locus or specifier.

Core Idea
God stands alone; He needs no place or cause.

The attribute of self-subsistence is crucial: God does not depend on anything. Sanusi proves it by showing that needing a locus or specifier would lead to absurdities.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Note the three conditionals: one for the necessity of self-subsistence and two consequences if denied.
2
Analyze the first part
If not self-subsisting, He would need a locus (mahall) or a specifier (mukhassis).
3
Follow the implications
If He needed a locus, He would be an attribute (which is dependent). If He needed a specifier, He would be temporal (created).
4
Conclude
Both imply dependence, which is impossible for God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على وجودِهِ تعالى : وجود العالم . ولو لم يكن قديماً لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن باقياً لم يكن قديماً ، ولو لم يكن مخالفاً لخلقِهِ لكان مثلهم ، ولو لم يكن قائماً بنفسِهِ لاحتاج إلى محلّ أو مخصص ، ولو افتقر إلى محل لكان صفةً ، ولو احتاج إلى مخصص لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن واحداً لكان مقهوراً؛ ﴿ وَهُوَ الْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِ ﴾ . ولو لم تجب له القدرة والإرادة والعلم والحياة لما كانَ شيءٌ مِنْ خلقِهِ ، ولو لم يتصف بالسمع والبصر والكلام لكان ناقصاً ، تعالى عن ذلك علوّاً كبيراً . ولو لم يكن فعل الممكناتِ أو تركها جائزاً في حقه تعالى لانقلبَتِ الحقائق ، وقلب الحقائقِ مستحيل .
And the proof for His existence, exalted: the existence of the world. And if He were not eternal, He would be temporal. And if He were not everlasting, He would not be eternal. And if He were not different from His creation, He would be like them. And if He were not self-subsisting, He would need a locus or a specifier. And if He needed a locus, He would be an attribute. And if He needed a specifier, He would be temporal. And if He were not one, He would be overpowered; {And He is the Overpowerer above His servants} (6:18). And if power, will, knowledge, and life were not necessary for Him, nothing of His creation would exist. And if He were not described with hearing, sight, and speech, He would be deficient – exalted above that greatly. And if doing or leaving possible things were not permissible in His right, the realities would be inverted, and the inversion of realities is impossible.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage provides rational proofs for the attributes enumerated earlier. Each proof follows a conditional structure: if the opposite of an attribute were true, a contradiction would follow. The proof for existence starts from the existence of the world, which requires a Creator. Eternity is proven because if not eternal, He would be temporal (hadith), which is impossible for God. Everlastingness follows from eternity. Being different from creation (mukhalafat lil-hawadith) is proven because otherwise He would be like them, implying similarity. Self-subsistence (qiyam bi-nafsih) is proven because need for a locus would make Him an attribute, and need for a specifier would make Him temporal. Oneness is proven by the argument that if there were multiple gods, they would conflict and be overpowered, but God is al-Qahir (the Overpowerer), as cited from the Qur'an. The necessary attributes (qudra, irada, 'ilm, haya) are proven because without them creation would not exist. The attributes of hearing, sight, speech are proven because lacking them would be deficiency. Finally, the permissible attribute (doing or leaving possible acts) is proven because if it were not permissible, the very nature of possibility would be inverted, which is absurd.

Checkpoint

A) He would be eternal
B) He would be an attribute

D) He would be like creation

Key Takeaway

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God’s self-subsistence means He needs no locus or cause, then your existence depends entirely on Him every moment. Do you live as if your existence is contingent and dependent on God, or do you act as if you are self-sufficient? What would change if you truly believed you are nothing without Him? This week, try to begin each task by saying 'Bismillah' and acknowledging your dependence.
?
The passage shows that needing a specifier would make something temporal. Your very existence is temporal and caused. How does this reality affect the way you view your life and your purpose? Take a moment to write down one area where you try to be your own 'specifier' (e.g., planning without seeking God's guidance) and commit to praying istikhara for it.
?
God is not an attribute inhering in a substance; He is the Self-Subsistent. Do you sometimes reduce God to a concept or an attribute (e.g., 'The Forgiving') and forget He is the independent Reality that all else depends on? How can you guard against making God into a mental image? This week, memorize the phrase 'Qā'im bi-nafsihi' and repeat it in your sujood to remind yourself of His absolute independence.

Proof for God's Oneness
Sanusi proves God's oneness with a conditional and Quranic support.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof that God is one, based on the impossibility of multiple gods.

Core Idea
If there were more than one god, they would conflict and be overpowered.

Sanusi now proves the oneness of God. The proof is a classic argument: if there were multiple gods, they would inevitably compete, leading to each being overpowered. But God is al-Qahir (the Overpowerer), as confirmed by the Qur'an.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Note the conditional and the Quranic proof.
2
Understand the logic
If there were two gods, they would want contrary things. One would overcome the other, so the conquered would not be divine.
3
Connect to the Quran
The verse confirms that God is the Overpowerer, implying oneness.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على وجودِهِ تعالى : وجود العالم . ولو لم يكن قديماً لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن باقياً لم يكن قديماً ، ولو لم يكن مخالفاً لخلقِهِ لكان مثلهم ، ولو لم يكن قائماً بنفسِهِ لاحتاج إلى محلّ أو مخصص ، ولو افتقر إلى محل لكان صفةً ، ولو احتاج إلى مخصص لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن واحداً لكان مقهوراً؛ ﴿ وَهُوَ الْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِ ﴾ . ولو لم تجب له القدرة والإرادة والعلم والحياة لما كانَ شيءٌ مِنْ خلقِهِ ، ولو لم يتصف بالسمع والبصر والكلام لكان ناقصاً ، تعالى عن ذلك علوّاً كبيراً . ولو لم يكن فعل الممكناتِ أو تركها جائزاً في حقه تعالى لانقلبَتِ الحقائق ، وقلب الحقائقِ مستحيل .

And if He were not different from His creation, He would be like them. And if He were not self-subsisting, He would need a locus or a specifier. And if He needed a locus, He would be an attribute. And if He needed a specifier, He would be temporal. And if He were not one, He would be overpowered; {And He is the Overpowerer above His servants} (6:18). And if power, will, knowledge, and life were not necessary for Him, nothing of His creation would exist. And if He were not described with hearing, sight, and speech, He would be deficient – exalted above that greatly. This passage provides rational proofs for the attributes enumerated earlier. Each proof follows a conditional structure: if the opposite of an attribute were true, a contradiction would follow. The proof for existence starts from the existence of the world, which requires a Creator. Eternity is proven because if not eternal, He would be temporal (hadith), which is impossible for God. Everlastingness follows from eternity. Being different from creation (mukhalafat lil-hawadith) is proven because otherwise He would be like them, implying similarity. Self-subsistence (qiyam bi-nafsih) is proven because need for a locus would make Him an attribute, and need for a specifier would make Him temporal. Oneness is proven by the argument that if there were multiple gods, they would conflict and be overpowered, but God is al-Qahir (the Overpowerer), as cited from the Qur'an. The necessary attributes (qudra, irada, 'ilm, haya) are proven because without them creation would not exist. The attributes of hearing, sight, speech are proven because lacking them would be deficiency. Finally, the permissible attribute (doing or leaving possible acts) is proven because if it were not permissible, the very nature of possibility would be inverted, which is absurd.

Key Terms
واحد
قهور

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think oneness is only known from revelation, but here it is rationally proven.
Correction: The rational proof shows that multiple gods would lead to contradiction.

Checkpoint
What would happen if God were not one according to the passage?
A) He would be temporal
B) He would be an attribute
C) He would be overpowered
D) He would need a locus

Key Takeaway
God must be one; multiple gods would lead to conflict and each being overpowered.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In what areas of your life do you act as though there is a force—money, status, or people—that can overpower Allah's will? How will you remind yourself daily that He is al-Qahir and nothing can overcome His decree?
?
When you feel 'overpowered' by trials, do you turn to other 'gods' like distraction or despair? How does knowing that Allah alone is the Overpowerer change how you face that situation? What is one concrete step you can take this week to rely only on Him?
?
The proof says if God were not one, He would be overpowered. Have you ever unconsciously placed anyone or anything in a position of co-rulership over your life—giving them the power to control your happiness or decisions? What will you do to restore His exclusive sovereignty in your heart?

Proof for God's Necessary Attributes: Power, Will, Knowledge, Life
Sanusi proves the necessity of four attributes from creation's existence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must possess power, will, knowledge, and life, as proven by the existence of creation.

Core Idea
Without these attributes, creation would not exist.

Sanusi now proves that God must have the attributes of power, will, knowledge, and life. These are necessary for Him to be the Creator. The proof is direct: if these attributes were not necessary for God, nothing of His creation would exist.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Note that four attributes are mentioned together.
2
Understand the implication
Creation exists, so its cause must have power to create, will to choose, knowledge to design, and life to act.
3
Reflect on necessity
If any of these were lacking, creation could not come about.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على وجودِهِ تعالى : وجود العالم . ولو لم يكن قديماً لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن باقياً لم يكن قديماً ، ولو لم يكن مخالفاً لخلقِهِ لكان مثلهم ، ولو لم يكن قائماً بنفسِهِ لاحتاج إلى محلّ أو مخصص ، ولو افتقر إلى محل لكان صفةً ، ولو احتاج إلى مخصص لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن واحداً لكان مقهوراً؛ ﴿ وَهُوَ الْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِ ﴾ . ولو لم تجب له القدرة والإرادة والعلم والحياة لما كانَ شيءٌ مِنْ خلقِهِ ، ولو لم يتصف بالسمع والبصر والكلام لكان ناقصاً ، تعالى عن ذلك علوّاً كبيراً . ولو لم يكن فعل الممكناتِ أو تركها جائزاً في حقه تعالى لانقلبَتِ الحقائق ، وقلب الحقائقِ مستحيل .
And the proof for His existence, exalted: the existence of the world. And if He were not eternal, He would be temporal. And if He were not everlasting, He would not be eternal. And if He were not different from His creation, He would be like them. And if He were not self-subsisting, He would need a locus or a specifier. And if He needed a locus, He would be an attribute. And if He needed a specifier, He would be temporal. And if He were not one, He would be overpowered; {And He is the Overpowerer above His servants} (6:18). And if power, will, knowledge, and life were not necessary for Him, nothing of His creation would exist. And if He were not described with hearing, sight, and speech, He would be deficient – exalted above that greatly. And if doing or leaving possible things were not permissible in His right, the realities would be inverted, and the inversion of realities is impossible.
Source pages p. 89

Commentary
This passage provides rational proofs for the attributes enumerated earlier. Each proof follows a conditional structure: if the opposite of an attribute were true, a contradiction would follow. The proof for existence starts from the existence of the world, which requires a Creator. Eternity is proven because if not eternal, He would be temporal (hadith), which is impossible for God. Everlastingness follows from eternity. Being different from creation (mukhalafat lil-hawadith) is proven because otherwise He would be like them, implying similarity. Self-subsistence (qiyam bi-nafsih) is proven because need for a locus would make Him an attribute, and need for a specifier would make Him temporal. Oneness is proven by the argument that if there were multiple gods, they would conflict and be overpowered, but God is al-Qahir (the Overpowerer), as cited from the Qur'an. The necessary attributes (qudra, irada, 'ilm, haya) are proven because without them creation would not exist. The attributes of hearing, sight, speech are proven because lacking them would be deficiency. Finally, the permissible attribute (doing or leaving possible acts) is proven because if it were not permissible, the very nature of possibility would be inverted, which is absurd.

Proof for God's Attributes of Hearing, Sight, and Speech
Sanusi proves the necessity of hearing, sight, speech via perfection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must be described with hearing, sight, and speech, as proven by the necessity of perfection.

Core Idea
Lacking these attributes would imply deficiency, but God is perfect.

After proving the necessary attributes for creation, Sanusi now proves the attributes of hearing, sight, and speech. These are attributes of perfection. If God lacked them, He would be deficient, which is impossible.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
A single conditional: if not described with these, then deficient.
2
Understand perfection
Hearing, sight, and speech are perfections. Their absence in God would be a lack.
3
Note the Quranic tone
Sanusi ends with 'exalted above that greatly', affirming God's transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Proof for the Permissible Attribute: Doing and Leaving Possible Acts
Sanusi proves the permissibility of God's action and inaction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why it is permissible for God to do or leave possible acts, based on the impossibility of inverting realities.

Core Idea
God is free to act or not; His action is not necessary in itself.

The final attribute Sanusi discusses is the permissible attribute: it is possible for God to do or leave possible acts. This is proven by the absurdity of the opposite: if it were not permissible, realities would be inverted.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Note the conditional about inversion of realities.
2
Understand the concept
Possible acts are those that are neither necessary nor impossible for God. Their occurrence or non-occurrence is up to Him.
3
Grasp the proof
If it were not permissible (i.e., either necessary or impossible), then what is possible would be otherwise, which would invert the nature of possibility.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والدليل على وجودِهِ تعالى : وجود العالم . ولو لم يكن قديماً لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن باقياً لم يكن قديماً ، ولو لم يكن مخالفاً لخلقِهِ لكان مثلهم ، ولو لم يكن قائماً بنفسِهِ لاحتاج إلى محلّ أو مخصص ، ولو افتقر إلى محل لكان صفةً ، ولو احتاج إلى مخصص لكان حادثاً ، ولو لم يكن واحداً لكان مقهوراً؛ ﴿ وَهُوَ الْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِ ﴾ . ولو لم تجب له القدرة والإرادة والعلم والحياة لما كانَ شيءٌ مِنْ خلقِهِ ، ولو لم يتصف بالسمع والبصر والكلام لكان ناقصاً ، تعالى عن ذلك علوّاً كبيراً . ولو لم يكن فعل الممكناتِ أو تركها جائزاً في حقه تعالى لانقلبَتِ الحقائق ، وقلب الحقائقِ مستحيل .

And if He were not different from His creation, He would be like them. And if He were not self-subsisting, He would need a locus or a specifier. And if He needed a locus, He would be an attribute. And if He needed a specifier, He would be temporal. And if He were not one, He would be overpowered; {And He is the Overpowerer above His servants} (6:18). And if power, will, knowledge, and life were not necessary for Him, nothing of His creation would exist. And if He were not described with hearing, sight, and speech, He would be deficient – exalted above that greatly. The proof for existence starts from the existence of the world, which requires a Creator. Eternity is proven because if not eternal, He would be temporal (hadith), which is impossible for God. The attributes of hearing, sight, speech are proven because lacking them would be deficiency. Finally, the permissible attribute (doing or leaving possible acts) is proven because if it were not permissible, the very nature of possibility would be inverted, which is absurd.

Allah's Existence
