Fifth Argument: The Proof of Mutual Hindrance (Dalil al-Tamānu')
Sanusi presents the proof of mutual hindrance against multiple gods.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the proof of mutual hindrance and why it demonstrates the impossibility of more than one god.

Core Idea
Even two agreeing gods would inevitably conflict in power and will.

This passage presents a key proof in Islamic theology for the oneness of God: the argument of mutual hindrance (dalil al-tamānu'). It shows that the existence of two gods leads to logical absurdities whether they agree or disagree. The argument relies on the nature of will, power, and the indivisibility of existence. Let's examine it closely.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the translation to get a clear sense of the argument.
2
Identify the two scenarios
The passage presents two cases: if the two gods disagree on a possible thing, and if they agree. Notice how each is treated differently.
Disagreement is said to be 'obvious', while agreement requires a deeper logical analysis.
3
Understand why agreement leads to impossibility
Focus on the reasoning for the agreement case: it leads to either division of what is indivisible or acquisition of what is already obtained, both impossible. Conclude that one god must be incapacitated.
This is the heart of the argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويلزم أيضاً : عجز المثلين في الألوهية من جهة التمانع بينَ إرادتيهما وقدرتيهما ، سواء اتفقا على ممكن واحدٍ أو اختلفا ، أمَّا إن اختلفا فظاهر . وأما إن اتفقا : فلأنَّ لكلّ ممكن وجوداً واحداً ، فيستحيل أن تَنفُذَ فيه إرادتان وقدرتانِ ، وإلا لزم انقسامُ ما لا ينقسم ، أو تحصيل الحاصل ، فلا بدَّ إذاً مِنْ عجز إحدى القدرتين وإحدى الإرادتين .
And it also follows: the incapacity of the two similars in divinity from the aspect of mutual hindrance (al-tamānu') between their wills and powers, whether they agree on one possible thing or disagree. As for if they disagree, it is obvious. And as for if they agree: because every possible thing has a single existence, so it is impossible for two wills and two powers to execute it; otherwise it would necessitate the division of what is indivisible or the acquisition of what is already obtained. Therefore, inevitably, one of the two powers and one of the two wills is incapacitated.
Source pages p. 163

Commentary
This passage presents the well-known proof of mutual hindrance (dalil al-tamānu'). It argues that if there were two gods, their wills and powers would conflict. If they disagree on what to do, the one who cannot achieve his will is weak, contradicting divinity. If they agree on a single action, that action can only have one cause; if both tried to effect it, it would either require dividing an indivisible action or achieving something already achieved, both impossible. Thus, one god would be incapacitated. Therefore, multiple gods are impossible.

Key Terms
التمانع
الإرادتان
القدرتان
انقسام ما لا ينقسم
تحصيل الحاصل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that if the gods agree on everything, there would be no conflict and multiple gods could exist peacefully.
Correction: The author shows that even if they agree, the nature of a single existent prevents two separate wills and powers from both being effective, leading to impossibility. Agreement does not resolve the logical contradiction.

Checkpoint
According to the argument of mutual hindrance, what must happen if two gods agree on a single possible thing?
A) They both effect it perfectly.
B) One of their wills and powers must be incapacitated.
C) The thing comes into existence twice.
D) They share the action harmoniously.

Key Takeaway
The proof of mutual hindrance shows that multiple gods are impossible because their wills and powers inevitably conflict, leading to incapacity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
In what areas of your life do you subtly act as if there are other 'powers' that can affect your destiny, independent of Allah? How does this proof of mutual hindrance challenge that mindset?
?
When you are in a difficult situation and you find yourself relying on your own strength or the help of others, do you ever stop to realize that any true power belongs only to Allah? How can this argument strengthen your reliance on Him alone?
?
If you truly internalize that only One God has absolute will and power, how would it change the way you pray, fear, and hope? What is one concrete step you can take today to align your heart with this truth?

The Perfection Argument Against Multiple Gods
Sanusi presents the perfection-deficiency argument against multiple gods.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's argument that multiple gods would each be deficient because they would lack perfections possessed by others.

Core Idea
Multiple gods cannot coexist because each would be deficient.

This lesson presents the first of Sanusi's two arguments against polytheism. He assumes that if there were two gods, they would differ in accidental attributes. But any accident that is a perfection in one would be absent in the other, making that other deficient. Since deficiency is impossible for God, multiple gods cannot exist.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the translation. Let the author's own words set the stage.
Note the reference to the earlier proof of God's pre-eternality.
2
Understand the premise
Sanusi builds on the idea that any accidental attribute (عَرَضى) in a god is either a perfection or a deficiency. Consider what each option implies for the other god.
The argument works even if the accident is a deficiency from the start.
3
Trace the logical chain
If one god has a perfection the other lacks, that other is deficient. But God cannot be deficient. Therefore, multiple gods are impossible.
This argument refutes the idea of multiple perfect beings.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والحدوث ينافي الألوهية ؛ لِمَا عرفت في برهان قدم الإله وبقائه . وأيضاً ذلكِ العَرَضى : إما أن يكون كمالاً فقد فات الآخر ، وفوتُ الكمال نقص ؛ فيلزمُ أنْ يكونَ كلُّ واحدٍ منهما ناقصاً في ذاته ؛ وهو وإنْ كانَ ذلكَ العَرَضِيُّ نَقْصاً .. لزمَ أيضاً اتصافُ الإله بالنقص مِنْ أولِ مَرَّةٍ ، وهو ظاهر الاستحالة . محال وأيضاً تعدُّدُ الإله : إمَّا أنْ يكونَ بعددٍ خاص مُتناه ، فيلزم افتقاره إلى مُخصص ، فيكون حادثاً ، وإما أن يكونَ بعددٍ لا نهاية له ، فيلزم دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجود ، وهو ظاهر الاستحالة
And contingency is incompatible with divinity, because of what you know from the proof of the pre-eternality and everlastingness of God. Also that accident: either it is a perfection, then the other has missed it, and missing perfection is deficiency; so it would follow that each of them is deficient in its essence; and even if that accident is a deficiency, then the description of God with deficiency would also follow from the first time, and that is clearly impossible. Impossible. And also the multiplicity of gods: either it is a specific finite number, then it would require a specifier, so it would be contingent, or it is an infinite number, then the entrance of the infinite into existence would follow, and that is clearly impossible.
Source pages p. 163

Commentary
This passage presents two arguments against the possibility of multiple gods based on contingency. The first argument: if there were two gods, each would lack any perfection present in the other, implying deficiency in each, which is impossible for a god. The second argument: the number of gods must be either finite or infinite. If finite, the specific number would require a cause to specify it, making the gods contingent. If infinite, the actual existence of an infinite number is impossible. Both lead to the conclusion that multiple gods cannot exist.

Key Terms
الحدوث (contingency)
الألوهية (divinity)
الآخر (the other)
الكمال (perfection)
النقص (deficiency)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Multiple gods could coexist if each is perfect in a different way, lacking nothing they need.
Correction: Any perfection present in one god is absent in the other, making that other deficient. Deficiency is impossible for God.

Checkpoint
According to the first argument, if there were two gods and one possessed a perfection not present in the other, what would follow?
A) Both would still be perfect
B) The one lacking would be deficient, which is impossible for a god
C) The perfection would be shared equally
D) The other god would be non-existent

Key Takeaway
Sanusi's perfection argument shows that multiple gods cannot exist because they would necessarily be deficient.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly internalised that any deficiency in your concept of God invalidates your worship, would you dare to let a single doubt about His perfection linger in your heart? What one doubt will you actively seek to remove starting today?
?
Sanusi argues that even the possibility of deficiency in a god is impossible. Have you ever treated God as if He were capable of failure, neglect, or injustice in your life? Write down one specific time you blamed God for something—then repent for that implication and reaffirm His absolute perfection in your own words.
?
The argument exposes that polytheism logically collapses into absurdity. But what about the subtle 'gods' of your own soul—your pride, your comfort, your relationships—that you act as though are independent of Allah? Which of these are you willing to dethrone today, and what step will you take to make this real?

The Finite/Infinite Number Argument Against Multiple Gods
Sanusi presents the number argument: finite or infinite, both impossible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the number of gods must be either finite or infinite, and both options lead to impossibility.

Core Idea
The very number of gods would require a cause, making them contingent.

Sanusi now considers the second impossibility: the number of gods itself. If the number is finite, it needs a specifier, implying contingency. If infinite, actual infinity is impossible. Either way, multiple gods cannot exist.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the clear dichotomy: finite or infinite number.
This argument does not depend on the first; it stands alone.
2
Analyze the finite branch
If the number of gods is finite, that specific number needs a reason (specifier). Therefore the gods would be contingent, contradicting divinity.
The specifier (mukhassis) is a cause that determines the particular number.
3
Analyze the infinite branch
If the number is infinite, then an actual infinite would exist in reality, which is impossible (according to kalam).
This argument rejects actual infinite sets in existence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والحدوث ينافي الألوهية ؛ لِمَا عرفت في برهان قدم الإله وبقائه . وأيضاً ذلكِ العَرَضى : إما أن يكون كمالاً فقد فات الآخر ، وفوتُ الكمال نقص ؛ فيلزمُ أنْ يكونَ كلُّ واحدٍ منهما ناقصاً في ذاته ؛ وهو وإنْ كانَ ذلكَ العَرَضِيُّ نَقْصاً .. لزمَ أيضاً اتصافُ الإله بالنقص مِنْ أولِ مَرَّةٍ ، وهو ظاهر الاستحالة . محال وأيضاً تعدُّدُ الإله : إمَّا أنْ يكونَ بعددٍ خاص مُتناه ، فيلزم افتقاره إلى مُخصص ، فيكون حادثاً ، وإما أن يكونَ بعددٍ لا نهاية له ، فيلزم دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجود ، وهو ظاهر الاستحالة
And contingency is incompatible with divinity, because of what you know from the proof of the pre-eternality and everlastingness of God. Also that accident: either it is a perfection, then the other has missed it, and missing perfection is deficiency; so it would follow that each of them is deficient in its essence; and even if that accident is a deficiency, then the description of God with deficiency would also follow from the first time, and that is clearly impossible. Impossible. And also the multiplicity of gods: either it is a specific finite number, then it would require a specifier, so it would be contingent, or it is an infinite number, then the entrance of the infinite into existence would follow, and that is clearly impossible.
Source pages p. 163

Commentary
This passage presents two arguments against the possibility of multiple gods based on contingency. The first argument: if there were two gods, each would lack any perfection present in the other, implying deficiency in each, which is impossible for a god. The second argument: the number of gods must be either finite or infinite. If finite, the specific number would require a cause to specify it, making the gods contingent. If infinite, the actual existence of an infinite number is impossible. Both lead to the conclusion that multiple gods cannot exist.

Key Terms
المُخصص (specifier)
الحدوث (contingency)
ما لا نهاية له (infinite)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: An infinite number of gods could exist if they are all eternal and independent.
Correction: Actual infinity in existence is considered impossible in kalam; also, a finite number would need a cause.

Checkpoint
According to the number argument, what follows if the number of gods is finite?
A) They would be self-sufficient
B) They would need a specifier and thus be contingent
C) They would be eternal
D) They would be independent

Key Takeaway
The number of gods, whether finite or infinite, leads to an impossibility, proving that only one God can exist.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever act as if there are multiple sources of control in your life, dividing your trust between Allah and something else? What would it look like to live with the logical certainty that only one God can truly exist?
?
This argument shows that any finite number of gods would need a specifier, meaning they are not self-sufficient. Where in your life have you given something or someone the status of 'ultimate decider' that is actually dependent on Allah?
?
If the very idea of more than one God is impossible, then your entire life must be oriented around that single reality. What is one area of your life where you are not treating Allah as the sole sovereign, and what will you change today?

Equality to Contingents and Mutual Impediment
Sanusi argues that two equal gods would be incapable like contingents and that their wills would mutually impede.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi argues that two equal gods would each be incapable by comparing them to contingent beings and by showing mutual impediment when they agree or differ on a single act.

Core Idea
Two equal gods logically entail mutual incapability — a single effect cannot serve two independent wills.

Sanusi now presents a second argument for God's oneness, building on the contingency argument. He shows that if there were two gods, they would be no different from contingent beings in their inability to create, and furthermore, their wills would inevitably conflict. This lesson unpacks that argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Let the flow of the argument sink in without analysis.
Notice the two main parts: first about equality to contingents, then about mutual impediment.
2
Identify the two sub-arguments
The passage presents two linked arguments. First, equality to contingents makes each god incapable. Second, mutual impediment (tamānuʻ) when two wills target the same act.
Pay attention to the logical structure: agreement leads to impossibility, disagreement is obvious.
3
Understand the logical contradiction
For a single possible act, only one existence exists. Two wills acting on it either divide the indivisible or produce the already obtained, so one will must be incapable.
This is not a physical but a logical impossibility.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأيضاً : يلزم أن يكونَ كلُّ واحدٍ منهما عاجزاً عن كل ممكن ؛ لمساواتهما في الإمكان والحدوثِ لسائر الحوادثِ التي قد عُرِفَ بالضرورة عجزها عن إيجاد الأجرام وإعدامها . ويلزم أيضاً : عجز المثلين في الألوهية من جهة التمانع بينَ إرادتيهما وقدرتيهما ، سواء اتفقا على ممكن واحدٍ أو اختلفا ، أمَّا إن اختلفا فظاهر . وأما إن اتفقا : فلأنَّ لكلّ ممكن وجوداً واحداً ، فيستحيل أن تَنفُذَ فيه إرادتان وقدرتانِ ، وإلا لزم انقسامُ ما لا ينقسم ، أو تحصيل الحاصل ، فلا بدَّ إذاً مِنْ عجز إحدى القدرتين وإحدى الإرادتين ، ويلزم منه عجز الأخرى ؛ لِمَا انعقد بينهما مِنَ المماثلة . هذا كله في المِثْلِ الحقيقي العام . وأَمَّا إذا فُرِضَ المِثْلُ خاصّاً في بعض الصفاتِ ؛ كالقدرة والإرادة مثلاً : فإنَّه يلزمُ الحدوث أيضاً لكلِّ مِنَ المثلين ؛ لأنَّ كلَّ واحدةٍ مِنَ الصفتين المتماثلتين تحتاجُ إلى مُخصص يخصصها بالمحل الذي وُجِدَتْ فيهِ ؛ لقبولِ كلَّ واحدة منهما حينئذ المحلَّينِ ، فيلزم أن تكونَ كل واحدة منهما جائزة الوجودِ حادثةً عارضةً لكلِّ مِنَ الموصوفين وكلُّ واحدٍ منهما لا يمكن أن يعرى عن هذه الصفة الحادثة أو ضدها ، ولا يكون ذلك الضدُّ إلا حادثاً ، فيلزمُ أنْ يكونَ كلٌّ مِنَ حادثاً ، وذلكَ ينافي ما ثبت للإله مِنْ وجوب الوجودِ ، ويلزم حينئذ العجز أيضاً ؛ لأجل الحدوثِ والتمانع إنْ فُرِضَ المِثلُ في القدرة والإرادة
And also: it follows that each of them is incapable of every possible thing, because they are equal in possibility and contingency to other contingents which are known by necessity to be incapable of bringing about or annihilating bodies. And also it follows: the incapability of two equals in divinity from the aspect of mutual impediment between their wills and powers, whether they agree on one possible or differ. As for if they differ, it is obvious. And as for if they agree: because every possible has one existence, so it is impossible for two wills and two powers to execute it, otherwise division of what is indivisible or obtaining the already obtained would follow, so necessarily then there is incapability of one of the powers and one of the wills, and follows from it the incapability of the other because of the likeness between them. All this in the general real likeness. And as for if the likeness is assumed specifically in some attributes, like power and will for example: then contingency also follows for each of the two likes, because each of the two like attributes needs a specifier to specify it to the subject in which it exists, because each of them then accepts both subjects; so it follows that each of them is possible of existence, contingent, accidental to each of the described, and each of them cannot be free from this contingent attribute or its opposite, and that opposite is nothing but contingent, so it follows that each of them is contingent, and that contradicts what is established for God of necessary existence, and then incapability also follows because of contingency and mutual impediment if the likeness is assumed in power and will.
Source pages p. 163

Commentary
This passage contains multiple proofs against multiple gods, focusing on mutual impediment (tamānuʻ). First, it argues that if there were two gods, each would be incapable of every possible act because they would be equal to contingent beings known to be incapable of creating bodies. Second, it presents the classic argument of mutual impediment: if two gods agreed on an act, their wills and powers would conflict, leading to either the division of an indivisible effect or a redundant effect, so one must be incapable. If they disagreed, the conflict is obvious. The argument then extends to a specific likeness in attributes like power and will: such attributes would require a specifier, making each god contingent. Thus, no plurality is possible.

Key Terms
العجز (incapability)
التمانع (mutual impediment)
المساواة (equality)
الإرادة (will)
القدرة (power)
المثل (like/equal)
الحدوث (contingency)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The mutual impediment argument is about physical conflict between two gods.
Correction: The argument is about logical impossibility: a single effect cannot originate from two equal causes without contradiction.

Checkpoint
What does the argument of mutual impediment (tamānuʻ) establish when two gods agree on the same possible act?
A) Both wills can coexist without conflict
B) One of the two powers must be incapable
C) The act is divided between them
D) The act becomes necessary

Key Takeaway
The mutual impediment argument shows that multiple gods logically cannot coexist because a single effect cannot originate from two equal causes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If a single existence cannot serve two wills, what does that say about your own life when you try to serve both Allah and your desires? Can you honestly claim to have a unified will? What one intention do you need to purify today?
?
Sanusi argues that even if two gods agreed, their partnership would lead to absurdity. Have you ever assumed you could partner your own efforts with Allah's grace without submitting entirely? Where do you need to surrender control and trust that Allah alone is sufficient?
?
The proof from equality to contingents shows that anything equal to a contingent shares its incapability. Do you ever treat created things as if they have independent power alongside Allah? Name one thing you fear or rely on that you must now reframe as utterly dependent on Allah.

Specific Likeness in Attributes Leads to Contingency
Sanusi argues that even partial likeness in attributes leads to contingency and mutual impediment.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi extends the argument to show that even if the likeness is limited to specific attributes like power and will, each god would still be contingent and thus incapable.

Core Idea
Even a partial likeness in attributes forces each god to be contingent, contradicting necessary existence.

Sanusi now considers a hypothetical scenario where the two gods are not fully equal but share only certain attributes, such as power and will. He demonstrates that even this limited likeness leads to contingency and mutual impediment, reinforcing the impossibility of multiple gods.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on specific likeness
Read the Arabic and translation, noting the shift from general to specific likeness.
This part builds on the previous argument but now focuses on partial similarity.
2
Understand the need for a specifier
Each shared attribute (e.g., power) could inhere in either god, so it needs a specifier (mukhassis) to determine its subject, indicating it is contingent.
Contingent attributes require an external cause to specify them.
3
See how this leads to contingency of the god
If a god possesses a contingent attribute, and cannot be free of it or its opposite (also contingent), then the god himself becomes contingent, contradicting necessary existence.
Thus even partial likeness undermines divinity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأيضاً : يلزم أن يكونَ كلُّ واحدٍ منهما عاجزاً عن كل ممكن ؛ لمساواتهما في الإمكان والحدوثِ لسائر الحوادثِ التي قد عُرِفَ بالضرورة عجزها عن إيجاد الأجرام وإعدامها . ويلزم أيضاً : عجز المثلين في الألوهية من جهة التمانع بينَ إرادتيهما وقدرتيهما ، سواء اتفقا على ممكن واحدٍ أو اختلفا ، أمَّا إن اختلفا فظاهر . وأما إن اتفقا : فلأنَّ لكلّ ممكن وجوداً واحداً ، فيستحيل أن تَنفُذَ فيه إرادتان وقدرتانِ ، وإلا لزم انقسامُ ما لا ينقسم ، أو تحصيل الحاصل ، فلا بدَّ إذاً مِنْ عجز إحدى القدرتين وإحدى الإرادتين ، ويلزم منه عجز الأخرى ؛ لِمَا انعقد بينهما مِنَ المماثلة . هذا كله في المِثْلِ الحقيقي العام . وأَمَّا إذا فُرِضَ المِثْلُ خاصّاً في بعض الصفاتِ ؛ كالقدرة والإرادة مثلاً : فإنَّه يلزمُ الحدوث أيضاً لكلِّ مِنَ المثلين ؛ لأنَّ كلَّ واحدةٍ مِنَ الصفتين المتماثلتين تحتاجُ إلى مُخصص يخصصها بالمحل الذي وُجِدَتْ فيهِ ؛ لقبولِ كلَّ واحدة منهما حينئذ المحلَّينِ ، فيلزم أن تكونَ كل واحدة منهما جائزة الوجودِ حادثةً عارضةً لكلِّ مِنَ الموصوفين وكلُّ واحدٍ منهما لا يمكن أن يعرى عن هذه الصفة الحادثة أو ضدها ، ولا يكون ذلك الضدُّ إلا حادثاً ، فيلزمُ أنْ يكونَ كلٌّ مِنَ حادثاً ، وذلكَ ينافي ما ثبت للإله مِنْ وجوب الوجودِ ، ويلزم حينئذ العجز أيضاً ؛ لأجل الحدوثِ والتمانع إنْ فُرِضَ المِثلُ في القدرة والإرادة
And also: it follows that each of them is incapable of every possible thing, because they are equal in possibility and contingency to other contingents which are known by necessity to be incapable of bringing about or annihilating bodies. And also it follows: the incapability of two equals in divinity from the aspect of mutual impediment between their wills and powers, whether they agree on one possible or differ. As for if they differ, it is obvious. And as for if they agree: because every possible has one existence, so it is impossible for two wills and two powers to execute it, otherwise division of what is indivisible or obtaining the already obtained would follow, so necessarily then there is incapability of one of the powers and one of the wills, and follows from it the incapability of the other because of the likeness between them. All this in the general real likeness. And as for if the likeness is assumed specifically in some attributes, like power and will for example: then contingency also follows for each of the two likes, because each of the two like attributes needs a specifier to specify it to the subject in which it exists, because each of them then accepts both subjects; so it follows that each of them is possible of existence, contingent, accidental to each of the described, and each of them cannot be free from this contingent attribute or its opposite, and that opposite is nothing but contingent, so it follows that each of them is contingent, and that contradicts what is established for God of necessary existence, and then incapability also follows because of contingency and mutual impediment if the likeness is assumed in power and will.

Commentary
This passage contains multiple proofs against multiple gods, focusing on mutual impediment (tamānuʻ). First, it argues that if there were two gods, each would be incapable of every possible act because they would be equal to contingent beings known to be incapable of creating bodies. A) Because each attribute must have a specifier for its subject

Even a limited similarity in attributes forces each supposed god to be contingent, negating necessary existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This lesson proves that even a single shared attribute would make a being contingent. Reflect: In your daily life, do you ever unconsciously attribute any of Allah's qualities—like power, knowledge, or will—to created beings in your mind? How might this logical argument help you root out subtle forms of shirk in your thinking? Commit to catching yourself when you attribute absolute qualities to anyone other than Allah.
?
If every attribute you have (intelligence, strength, wealth) needs a specific determination from Allah to exist in you, what does that tell you about your utter dependence on Him? Consider one area where you feel self-sufficient. How can you actively remind yourself of your contingency and Allah's necessity throughout your day? Write a specific reminder and place it where you will see it.

intermediate
5 min
Ibrahim's Argument: The Power of Setting over Rising
Sanusi interprets Ibrahim's statement as an interrogative or citation and explains the use of setting over rising.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi interprets the Quranic verse about Prophet Ibrahim and why he holds that setting provides a clearer proof of contingency than rising.

Core Idea
Setting reveals contingency more clearly than rising because it involves evident decrease.

In this passage, Sanusi addresses a key Quranic verse often misunderstood. Prophet Ibrahim's statement 'This is my Lord' is not a literal belief but either a rhetorical question or a citation of his people's claim. Moreover, Sanusi explains why Ibrahim chose the setting of the celestial bodies over their rising as the decisive argument for their contingency. The rising, with its dazzling beauty, can confuse the mind, whereas the setting's clear change makes the argument accessible to all.

Guided Path
1
Grasp the two interpretations of Ibrahim's statement
Read the first part of the passage where Sanusi explains that the phrase 'This is my Lord' can be understood as an omitted interrogative (Is this my Lord?) or as a citation of the opponent's claim to refute it.
This clarifies that Ibrahim never actually believed a celestial body was his Lord.
2
Understand why setting is a stronger proof than rising
Notice that Sanusi states both rising and setting indicate contingency, but setting is clearer because it involves change through decrease, which is unmistakable. Rising, though also a proof, has accompanying beauty that can mislead the foolish.
Sanusi emphasizes that the setting's evidence is obvious to both intelligent and foolish.
3
Reflect on the psychological dimension
Consider how Sanusi describes the rising as intoxicating the mind of the foolish, lustful person, preventing reflection. This shows a deep understanding of human psychology in argumentation.
The argument is not just logical but also takes into account human weaknesses.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فمعنى قوله عليه الصلاة والسلام : هَذَا رَبِّي ﴾ [الأنعام : ٧٦] : أهذا ربي ؟! على ما تزعمون ، بحذفِ حرفِ الاستفهام ، أو مِنْ بابِ ذكرِ دعوى الخصم لإقامة البرهان على إبطالها وطلوع هذه الكواكب بعد أن لم تكن هو في الاستدلال به على حدوثها كالأفول ، إلا أنَّهُ عليهِ الصلاة والسلامُ إِنَّما أَخَّرَ الاستدلال على حدوثها إلى رؤيةِ أُفولِها لِمَا في الأفولِ مِنَ التغيرِ بالنقصانِ ، فدلالته على حدوثِ تلك الكواكب وعدم صلاحيتها للربوبية واضح للذكي والغبي ، أمَّا طلوعها - وإنْ كانَ دليلاً على حدوثها مِنْ ناحية تجدُّدِهِ بعدَ أنْ لم يكن - فلأنَّهُ لمَّا كانَ فيهِ كمال لها لِمَا صاحبَهُ مِنْ تلكَ الأنوار التي تُوجَدُ لذاتِ النفس والامتدادِ إليها بالأبصار .. قد تُسكِرُ عقل الغبي الشهواني المقلَّدِ أوِ المعاندِ ، فلا يَتأَمَّلُ في وجهِ دلالتِهِ على الحدوثِ ، ولا يصغي لسماعها
The meaning of his saying, peace and blessings be upon him: 'This is my Lord' [al-An'am 76]: (Is this my Lord?! as you claim?) by omitting the interrogative particle, or it is from the category of mentioning the opponent's claim in order to establish the proof for its invalidation. And the rising of these celestial bodies after they were not is in inference upon their contingency like the setting, except that he, peace and blessings be upon him, delayed the inference upon their contingency until seeing their setting because in the setting there is change through decrease, so its indication of the contingency of those celestial bodies and their unsuitability for lordship is clear to both the intelligent and the foolish. As for their rising -- although it is a proof of their contingency from the aspect of their renewal after not being -- because since it contains perfection for them due to what accompanies it of those lights that are found for the pleasure of the soul and the extension of glances toward them, it may intoxicate the mind of the foolish, lustful, blind follower or obstinate person, so he does not reflect on the face of its indication of contingency, nor does he listen to it.
Source pages p. 229

Commentary
This passage explains the meaning of Prophet Ibrahim's statement 'This is my Lord' in the Qur'an. The author provides two possible interpretations: either it is an interrogative with the question mark omitted, meaning 'Is this my Lord?' as a challenge to his people's claim, or it is a citation of the opponent's claim in order to refute it. Then the author discusses why Ibrahim used the setting (ufūl) of the celestial bodies rather than their rising (ṭulūʿ) as an argument for their contingency. While both rising and setting indicate contingency, rising involves apparent beauty and increase that can dazzle the mind of a foolish, lustful, or obstinate person, preventing them from reflecting on the argument. Therefore, the setting is a clearer proof because it involves change through decrease, making the argument for contingency evident to everyone.

Key Terms
الاستفهام
الأفول
الطلوع
الدلالة
الحدوث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Prophet Ibrahim actually believed a celestial body was his Lord.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that Ibrahim's statement was either a rhetorical question or a citation of his people's claim to refute it, not a literal belief.

Checkpoint
Why did Prophet Ibrahim use the setting (ufūl) rather than the rising (ṭulūʿ) as an argument for the contingency of celestial bodies?
A) Because setting is more dramatic and compelling.
B) Because rising has apparent beauty that can intoxicate the mind of the foolish and prevent reflection.
C) Because setting is a direct command from Allah.
D) Because rising does not indicate contingency at all.

Key Takeaway
Prophet Ibrahim's use of setting over rising as a proof for God's existence is a masterful example of effective argumentation that takes into account human psychology and the clarity of evidence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you witness the beauty of a sunrise or a full moon, does it draw you closer to recognizing your need for Allah, or does it distract you like the 'foolish' described in this lesson? What would it take for you to see past the beauty to the contingency?
?
Ibrahim delayed his argument to the moment of setting—when what seemed powerful vanished. Have you ever experienced a 'setting' in your life—a loss, a failure, or an end of something you relied on—that made Allah's lordship undeniable? Did you respond with submission, or did you cling to what was vanishing?
?
The foolish person is 'intoxicated' by what pleases the soul. What pleasure in your life right now (a relationship, a possession, an achievement) might be keeping you from deeply reflecting on your absolute dependence on Allah? Will you deliberately set it aside to see the truth more clearly?

Refuting Belief that God Has Dimensions and Change
Sanusi refutes divine dimensions and change using Abraham's verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the author considers belief in divine dimensions and change as ignorance, and how Abraham's verse provides a logical proof for divine transcendence.

Core Idea
Change implies temporality, and temporality cannot be true of God.

In this passage, Sanusi refutes two false beliefs about God: that He has dimensions and that He changes. He invokes Abraham's famous declaration 'I do not love those that set' to construct a logical argument: anything that changes cannot be eternal, and so cannot be God. Let's examine the proof step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it, then read the translation. Let the author's own words come through before you interpret.
Notice the concise logical structure.
2
Identify the two false beliefs
The passage begins by naming two false beliefs: that God has dimensions (ab'aad) and that He is subject to change (tabaddul wa taghayyur). Identify them and note how the author labels those who hold them as ignorant.
These are common anthropomorphic conceptions.
3
Trace the logical argument
Follow the proof from the Quranic verse to the conclusion: everything that sets (afil) is changing (mutaghayyir); everything changing is not eternal (qadim); therefore it cannot be a god (ilah). Ask yourself how each step connects.
This is a syllogism: afil→mutaghayyir, mutaghayyir→la qadim, so la ilah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومَنْ ظَنّ أن الله تعالى له أبعاد تحوي ذاته جل وعزَّ ، وأنه يتّصف بصفات التبدل والتغير .. فهذا جاهل بقوله تعالى حاكياً قول خليله : لَا أُحِبُّ الْأَفِلِينَ ﴾ [الأنعام : ٧٦] ، إذ كلُّ آفل متغير ، وكلُّ متغير لا يكون قديماً ، فلا يكون إلهاً !
And whoever thinks that God the Exalted has dimensions that encompass His Essence, and that He is characterized by attributes of substitution and change... then this one is ignorant of His saying, relating the word of His friend (Abraham): 'I do not love those that set' [Al-An'am 76]. For everything that sets (afal) is changing, and everything that changes is not eternal, so it cannot be a god!
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of two false beliefs about God: that He has dimensions (ab'aad) that encompass His Essence, and that He is subject to change (tabaddul wa taghayyur). The author declares such a person ignorant (jahil) of the Quranic verse in which Abraham says, 'I do not love those that set (afilin).' The argument is then stated: everything that 'sets' (afal) is changing (mutaghayyir), and everything that changes is not eternal (qadim). Therefore, such a changing thing cannot be a god (ilah). The author uses this concise logical proof to uphold divine transcendence (tanzih) and refute anthropomorphic conceptions.

Key Terms
أبعاد (ab'aad)
آفل (afil)
متغير (mutaghayyir)
قديم (qadim)
إله (ilah)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that the verse 'I do not love those that set' applies only to celestial bodies, and does not provide a general logical proof.
Correction: The author uses the verse to derive a universal principle: setting implies change, change implies non-eternality, and non-eternality negates divinity. Thus the argument applies to anything that changes, not just stars.

Checkpoint
Why is belief that God has dimensions and change considered ignorance according to the passage?
A) Because the Quran explicitly says God has no dimensions.
B) Because Abraham's statement shows that everything that sets is changing and not eternal, so cannot be God.
C) Because the jurists have ruled it as ignorance.
D) Because it contradicts common sense.

Key Takeaway
The argument that change implies non-eternality proves that God cannot change, for any changing thing cannot be eternal and thus not divine.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever unconsciously imagine Allah as having a form, location, or even emotional changes like human moods? How does this verse from Abraham challenge that? This week, consciously remind yourself each time you think of Allah: 'I do not love those that set.'
?
When you face trials and feel that Allah's treatment of you has changed, do you assume He has changed towards you? How does the proof that change implies non-eternality correct that feeling? Commit to meditating on Allah's immutability before your next prayer.
?
The argument that 'everything that changes is not eternal' is simple yet absolute. Have you fully accepted that Allah is beyond all change, even in His attributes? Identify one area where you struggle to trust that He does not change (e.g., in His mercy or justice) and resolve to act on this truth today.

The Necessary Attributes of God
Sanusi enumerates the necessary attributes of God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the categories of necessary attributes: the essential attribute, attributes of negation, attributes of meanings, and mental attributes.

Core Idea
God's necessary attributes define His unique perfection.

Sanusi begins his classification of divine attributes by listing those that are rationally necessary for God. These attributes are not arbitrary; they are required by the fact that God is the Necessary Being. This lesson presents the first category: the attributes that must be affirmed of God, including both negative and positive descriptions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on necessary attributes
Carefully read the Arabic text and its translation to familiarize yourself with the list.
2
Identify the two main subcategories
Notice that the passage first mentions 'the essential attribute and the attributes of negation', then 'the attributes of meanings and the mental attributes'. These are two groups.
The first group describes God's essence and what He is not; the second describes His perfections.
3
Reflect on the meaning of each attribute
Consider why each attribute (e.g., necessary of existence, pre-eternality, power) is necessarily true of God.
Think about what would be missing if any of these were not affirmed.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مقدمة المؤلف
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم وصلى الله على سيدنا ومولانا محمد وآله وصحبه وسلم
قال الشيخ الإمام العالم أبو عبد الله سيدي محمد بن يوسف السنوسي الحسني رحمه الله تعالى ورضي عنه ونفعنا به :
الحمد لله ، والصلاة والسلام على رسول الله
الصفات الواجبة له سبحانه
الصفة النفسية وصفات السلوب
اعلم : أن مولانا جل وعز واجب الوجود ، والقدم والبقاء ، مخالف لخلقه ، قائم بنفسه أي غني عن المحل والمخصص ، واحد في ذاته وصفاته وأفعاله
صفات المعاني والصفات المعنوية
وتجب له تعالى : القدرة ، والإرادة ، والعلم ، والحياة ، والسمع ، والبصر ، والكلام
وكونه تعالى قادراً ، ومريداً وعالماً ، وحياً ، وسميعاً ، وبصيراً ، ومتكلماً
الصفات المستحيلة في حقه سبحانه
ويستحيل عليه جل وعز : العدم ، والحدوث ، وطروه العدم، والمماثلة للحوادث ، والافتقار إلى المحل والمخصص ، والشريك
وكذا يستحيل عليه جل وعز : العجز ، والكراهة والجهل ، والموت ، والصمم ، والعمى ، والبكم . وكونه عاجزاً ، وكارهاً ، وجاهلاً ، وميتاً ، وأصم ، وأعمى ، وأبكم
الصفات الجائزة في أفعاله سبحانه
ويجوز في حقه تعالى : فعل كل ممكن أو تركه
Introduction of the author: In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful, and may God bless our master Muhammad and his family and companions and grant them peace. The teacher, the imam, the scholar, Abu Abdullah, my master Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi al-Hasani, may God have mercy on him and be pleased with him and benefit us through him, said: Praise be to God, and prayers and peace be upon the messenger of God. The attributes necessary for Him, exalted is He: the essential attribute and the attributes of negation. Know that our Master, majestic and mighty, is necessary of existence, pre-eternality, perpetuity, dissimilar to His creation, self-subsistent, i.e., independent of substratum and specifyer, one in His essence, attributes, and acts. The attributes of meanings and the mental attributes: He, exalted, must possess: power, will, knowledge, life, hearing, sight, speech; and His being powerful, willing, knowing, living, hearing, seeing, speaking. The impossible attributes in His right, exalted is He: it is impossible for Him, majestic and mighty: nonexistence, origination, the occurrence of nonexistence, likeness to contingent things, dependence on a substratum and specifyer, and a partner. Likewise, it is impossible for Him, majestic and mighty: inability, hatred, ignorance, death, deafness, blindness, muteness; and His being incapable, hating, ignorant, dead, deaf, blind, mute. The possible attributes in His acts, exalted is He: it is permissible for Him, exalted, to do every possible thing or to leave it.
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage is a classification of divine attributes into three categories: necessary (wajib), impossible (mustahil), and possible (ja'iz). The author begins by listing the necessary attributes, which include the essential attribute (al-sifat al-nafsiyya) and attributes of negation (sifat al-sulub). God is necessary of existence, eternal, perpetual, unlike creation, self-subsistent (independent of substratum and specifier), and one in essence, attributes, and acts. Then the attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani) and mental attributes (al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya) are given: power, will, knowledge, life, hearing, sight, speech, and the corresponding mental attributes of being powerful, willing, etc. Next, the impossible attributes are listed, which are the opposites of the necessary ones: nonexistence, origination, annihilation, similarity to contingents, need for a substratum and specifier, partnership, and the opposites of the attributes of meanings: inability, hatred, ignorance, death, deafness, blindness, muteness. Finally, the possible attributes are stated: God may do or leave any possible thing. This classification is fundamental to kalam, structuring discourse on God's nature.

Key Terms
الصفة النفسية
صفات السلوب
صفات المعاني
الصفات المعنوية
واجب الوجود

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Many assume that God's attributes are distinct entities added to His essence.
Correction: Sanusi states that God is 'one in His essence, attributes, and acts', indicating that attributes are not separate but are aspects of His simple essence.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, which of the following is NOT among the attributes of meanings?
A) القدرة (power)
B) الإرادة (will)
C) الحياة (life)
D) العدم (nonexistence)

Key Takeaway
The necessary attributes of God include both negative descriptions that affirm His transcendence and positive perfections that describe His nature.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as though God is 'necessary of existence'—meaning your entire reliance is on Him alone—or do you often feel self-sufficient? How does this lesson challenge your daily dependence on God?
?
When you affirm that God is 'dissimilar to His creation' (مخالف لخلقه), does it change how you imagine Him in your prayers? Have you ever inadvertently pictured God with human limitations? What will you do to purify your conception of Him?
?
God has perfect knowledge (العلم) and power (القدرة). Is there an area of your life where you doubt His ability to know your struggle or to change your situation? Commit to one specific act of trust this week that reflects this belief.

Divine Attributes

The Impossible Attributes of God
Sanusi enumerates the impossible attributes of God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the attributes that are logically impossible for God, which are the opposites of the necessary attributes.

Core Idea
What cannot be said about God is as important as what must be said.

After establishing what God must be, Sanusi now clarifies what God cannot be. These impossible attributes serve as boundary markers, ensuring that we do not attribute to God any imperfection or limitation. This lesson covers the negations of the necessary attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on impossible attributes
Read the Arabic and translation to see the list of impossible attributes.
2
Notice the correspondence
Compare these impossible attributes to the necessary attributes from the previous lesson. Each impossible attribute is the opposite of a necessary one.
For example, 'nonexistence' opposes 'necessary of existence', 'origination' opposes 'pre-eternality', etc.
3
Understand the logical necessity
Reflect on why attributing these to God would be contradictory to His being the Necessary Being.
If God could be described by any of these, He would not be God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مقدمة المؤلف
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم وصلى الله على سيدنا ومولانا محمد وآله وصحبه وسلم
قال الشيخ الإمام العالم أبو عبد الله سيدي محمد بن يوسف السنوسي الحسني رحمه الله تعالى ورضي عنه ونفعنا به :
الحمد لله ، والصلاة والسلام على رسول الله
الصفات الواجبة له سبحانه
الصفة النفسية وصفات السلوب

The teacher, the imam, the scholar, Abu Abdullah, my master Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi al-Hasani, may God have mercy on him and be pleased with him and benefit us through him, said: Praise be to God, and prayers and peace be upon the messenger of God. The attributes necessary for Him, exalted is He: the essential attribute and the attributes of negation. Know that our Master, majestic and mighty, is necessary of existence, pre-eternality, perpetuity, dissimilar to His creation, self-subsistent, i.e., independent of substratum and specifyer, one in His essence, attributes, and acts. The attributes of meanings and the mental attributes: He, exalted, must possess: power, will, knowledge, life, hearing, sight, speech; and His being powerful, willing, knowing, living, hearing, seeing, speaking. The possible attributes in His acts, exalted is He: it is permissible for Him, exalted, to do every possible thing or to leave it.
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage is a classification of divine attributes into three categories: necessary (wajib), impossible (mustahil), and possible (ja'iz). The author begins by listing the necessary attributes, which include the essential attribute (al-sifat al-nafsiyya) and attributes of negation (sifat al-sulub). God is necessary of existence, eternal, perpetual, unlike creation, self-subsistent (independent of substratum and specifier), and one in essence, attributes, and acts. Key Terms
الصفات المستحيلة
العدم
الحدوث
المماثلة للحوادث
الافتقار

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God's inability to do certain things (like being unjust) is a limitation.
Correction: These 'inabilities' are actually logical impossibilities that preserve God's perfection. For example, God cannot be unjust because justice is necessary for Him.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is listed as an impossible attribute for God?
A) القدرة (power)
B) العجز (inability)
C) الحياة (life)
D) الكلام (speech)

Key Takeaway
The impossible attributes are the logical negations of the necessary attributes, affirming God's absolute perfection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Consider the impossible attribute 'الجهل' (ignorance). Do you ever live as if God does not know your private thoughts or struggles? When have you felt that God is unaware of your situation? How will you actively remind yourself of His all-encompassing knowledge this week?
?
The passage states that 'الشريك' (partner) is impossible for God. Yet, we often give people, status, or possessions a place in our hearts that rivals our devotion to Allah. Identify one area of your life where you have made something a 'partner' with God in your trust or reliance. What concrete step can you take to restore exclusive reliance on Him?
?
The attribute 'الكراهة' (hatred) is listed as impossible for God in the sense of an independent attribute that contradicts His perfect will. Do you sometimes imagine God's decree as stemming from anger or dislike towards you personally? How can you train your heart to see His will, even in trials, as pure wisdom and mercy?

Divine Attributes

The Possible Attributes of God's Actions
Sanusi states the possible attribute regarding God's actions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God has the freedom to do or leave any possible action, and that this does not imply arbitrariness.

Core Idea
God's actions are not compelled; He has absolute freedom.

The final category in Sanusi's classification is the possible attributes, which apply to God's actions. Unlike the necessary and impossible attributes, these are not fixed descriptions of God's essence but pertain to what He may choose to do. This lesson clarifies the scope of God's creative freedom.

Guided Path
1
Read the short passage on possible attributes
Read the Arabic and translation. Note that it says 'to do every possible thing or to leave it'.
This is brief but significant.
2
Understand the meaning of 'possible'
Here, 'possible' means logically possible, not contradictory. God can choose to bring about any logically possible state of affairs.
This distinguishes God's actions from necessary or impossible ones.
3
Reflect on the theological implications
This attribute emphasizes God's complete freedom and sovereignty. He is not compelled to act in any particular way.
It also implies that the world is not necessary but contingent on God's will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مقدمة المؤلف
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم وصلى الله على سيدنا ومولانا محمد وآله وصحبه وسلم
قال الشيخ الإمام العالم أبو عبد الله سيدي محمد بن يوسف السنوسي الحسني رحمه الله تعالى ورضي عنه ونفعنا به :
الحمد لله ، والصلاة والسلام على رسول الله
الصفات الواجبة له سبحانه
الصفة النفسية وصفات السلوب
اعلم : أن مولانا جل وعز واجب الوجود ، والقدم والبقاء ، مخالف لخلقه ، قائم بنفسه أي غني عن المحل والمخصص ، واحد في ذاته وصفاته وأفعاله
صفات المعاني والصفات المعنوية
وتجب له تعالى : القدرة ، والإرادة ، والعلم ، والحياة ، والسمع ، والبصر ، والكلام
وكونه تعالى قادراً ، ومريداً وعالماً ، وحياً ، وسميعاً ، وبصيراً ، ومتكلماً
الصفات المستحيلة في حقه سبحانه
ويستحيل عليه جل وعز : العدم ، والحدوث ، وطروه العدم، والمماثلة للحوادث ، والافتقار إلى المحل والمخصص ، والشريك
وكذا يستحيل عليه جل وعز : العجز ، والكراهة والجهل ، والموت ، والصمم ، والعمى ، والبكم . وكونه عاجزاً ، وكارهاً ، وجاهلاً ، وميتاً ، وأصم ، وأعمى ، وأبكم
الصفات الجائزة في أفعاله سبحانه
ويجوز في حقه تعالى : فعل كل ممكن أو تركه
Introduction of the author: In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful, and may God bless our master Muhammad and his family and companions and grant them peace. The teacher, the imam, the scholar, Abu Abdullah, my master Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi al-Hasani, may God have mercy on him and be pleased with him and benefit us through him, said: Praise be to God, and prayers and peace be upon the messenger of God. The attributes necessary for Him, exalted is He: the essential attribute and the attributes of negation. Know that our Master, majestic and mighty, is necessary of existence, pre-eternality, perpetuity, dissimilar to His creation, self-subsistent, i.e., independent of substratum and specifyer, one in His essence, attributes, and acts. The attributes of meanings and the mental attributes: He, exalted, must possess: power, will, knowledge, life, hearing, sight, speech; and His being powerful, willing, knowing, living, hearing, seeing, speaking. The impossible attributes in His right, exalted is He: it is impossible for Him, majestic and mighty: nonexistence, origination, the occurrence of nonexistence, likeness to contingent things, dependence on a substratum and specifyer, and a partner. Likewise, it is impossible for Him, majestic and mighty: inability, hatred, ignorance, death, deafness, blindness, muteness; and His being incapable, hating, ignorant, dead, deaf, blind, mute. The possible attributes in His acts, exalted is He: it is permissible for Him, exalted, to do every possible thing or to leave it.
Source pages p. 87

Commentary
This passage is a classification of divine attributes into three categories: necessary (wajib), impossible (mustahil), and possible (ja'iz). The author begins by listing the necessary attributes, which include the essential attribute (al-sifat al-nafsiyya) and attributes of negation (sifat al-sulub). God is necessary of existence, eternal, perpetual, unlike creation, self-subsistent (independent of substratum and specifier), and one in essence, attributes, and acts. Then the attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani) and mental attributes (al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya) are given: power, will, knowledge, life, hearing, sight, speech, and the corresponding mental attributes of being powerful, willing, etc. Next, the impossible attributes are listed, which are the opposites of the necessary ones: nonexistence, origination, annihilation, similarity to contingents, need for a substratum and specifier, partnership, and the opposites of the attributes of meanings: inability, hatred, ignorance, death, deafness, blindness, muteness. Finally, the possible attributes are stated: God may do or leave any possible thing. This classification is fundamental to kalam, structuring discourse on God's nature.

Key Terms
الصفات الجائزة
فعل كل ممكن
تركه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that 'possible' means God can do anything, including logical absurdities.
Correction: The term 'possible' in kalam refers to what is logically possible. God cannot do logically impossible things, as they are self-contradictory (e.g., creating a square circle).

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what does the possible attribute of God entail?
A) God must create the world
B) God may do every possible thing or leave it
C) God cannot do anything
D) God's actions are limited by human reason

Key Takeaway
God's possible attribute affirms His absolute freedom to act or not act, limited only by logical possibility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
God is absolutely free to do or not do anything possible. Does your prayer life reflect a humble submission to a free Master, or do you sometimes act as if God owes you something? How can you realign your heart to true servanthood?
?
If God can leave any possible act undone, then He is not compelled to give you anything—not even your next breath. Does this make you more grateful for His mercy, or does it create anxiety? What does your level of gratitude say about your belief in His freedom?
?
God's freedom means He acts according to His own wisdom, not your expectations. When your plans fail or your dua is not answered as you hoped, do you trust that His freedom is better than your demands? What would it take for you to truly surrender to that freedom?

Classes of Divine Attributes: Necessary, Impossible, Possible
Sanusi enumerates the three classes of divine attributes with their examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the threefold classification of divine attributes in Sunni kalam and recognize examples of each category.

Core Idea
Every attribute of God is either necessary, impossible, or permissible.

In this passage, Sanusi introduces the foundational framework for discussing God's attributes. He divides them into three rational classes: what God must possess, what He cannot possess, and what is possible for Him to do. This classification is the bedrock of all theological reasoning about Allah's nature.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without pausing, then read the translation. Notice how Sanusi uses distinct headings: 'واجب', 'مستحيل', 'جائز'.
The passage is dense; take your time to absorb each category.
2
Identify the three categories
Find where Sanusi lists the necessary attributes, then the impossible, then the permissible. Note that each category has its own set of examples.
Necessary attributes include 'power' and 'will'; impossible include 'non-existence' and 'temporality'.
3
Observe the sub-classification within necessary attributes
Notice that Sanusi distinguishes between 'attributes of meanings' (صفات المعاني) and 'attributes of meaning-relation' (الصفات المعنوية). The first list gives the essential attributes, the second gives their derived verbal forms.
This distinction is important in kalam to avoid multiplicity of eternal entities.
4
Reflect on the purpose of this classification
Ask yourself: why is it necessary to categorize attributes this way? This classification protects against both anthropomorphism (tashbih) and stripping God of His perfections (ta'til).
The permissible category shows God's absolute freedom in action.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفات المعاني، والصفات المعنوية ] وتجب له تعالى : القدرة ، والإرادة ، والعلم ، والحياة ، والسمع ، والبصر ، والكلام . وكونه تعالى قادراً ، ومريداً ، وعالماً ، وحياً ، وسميعاً ، وبصيراً ، ومتكلماً . الصفات المستحيلة في حقه سبحانَهُ ] ويستحيلُ عليهِ جلَّ وعزَّ : العدم ، والحدوث ، وطروء العدم ، والمماثلة للحوادثِ ، والافتقار إلى المحل والمخصص ، والشريك . وكذا يستحيلُ عليهِ جلَّ وعزّ : العجز ، والكراهة ، والجهل ، والموتُ ، والصمم ، والعمى ، والبكم . وكونه عاجزاً ، وكارهاً ، وجاهلاً ، وميتاً ، وأصم ، وأعمى ، وأبكم . الصفات الجائزة في أفعاله سبحانَهُ ] ويجوز في حقه تعالى : فعل كل ممكن أو تركه .
Attributes of meanings and attributes of meaning-relation] And necessary for Him, exalted: power, will, knowledge, life, hearing, sight, speech. And His being powerful, willing, knowing, living, hearing, seeing, speaking. The impossible attributes in His right, glorified] And impossible for Him, majestic and mighty: non-existence, temporality, occurrence of non-existence, similarity to temporal things, need for a locus and a specifier, and partner. Likewise impossible for Him, majestic and mighty: incapacity, aversion, ignorance, death, deafness, blindness, muteness. And His being incapable, averse, ignorant, dead, deaf, blind, mute. The permissible attributes in His actions, glorified] And permissible in His right, exalted: doing every possible thing or leaving it.
Source pages p. 88

Commentary
This passage is a classification of divine attributes into three categories: necessary (wajiba), impossible (mustahila), and permissible (ja'iza). The necessary attributes are those which must be affirmed of God to avoid imperfection, listed as the 'attributes of meanings' (sifat al-ma'ani): power, will, knowledge, life, hearing, sight, speech. Their derived adjectives (being powerful, etc.) are the 'attributes of meaning-relation' (sifat ma'nawiyya). The impossible attributes are those which cannot be ascribed to God, such as non-existence, temporality, similarity to creation, need for a locus or a specifier, and having a partner. Additional impossible attributes include incapacity, aversion, ignorance, death, deafness, blindness, muteness, and their corresponding adjectives. The permissible attributes concern God's actions: He may do or leave any possible act, without constraint.

Key Terms
الواجب
المستحيل
الجائز
صفات المعاني
الصفات المعنوية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
Which of the following is a necessary attribute of God according to this passage?
A) Non-existence
B) Temporality
C) Power
D) Incapacity

Key Takeaway
Every attribute of God falls into one of three rational categories: necessary to affirm, impossible to affirm, or permissible to affirm regarding His actions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin, do you truly believe that God, who has perfect knowledge of your action and perfect power to punish, still allows you to repent? How does His attribute of life and will affect your sense of accountability to Him right now?
?
If God cannot be ignorant, incapable, or dead, why do you sometimes act as if He does not see your private moments? What would change in your daily habits if you fully internalized that He is Hearing (السميع) and Seeing (البصير) of even your thoughts?
?
God's permissible actions include 'doing every possible thing or leaving it.' Does this make you feel secure in His mercy, or does it challenge you to seek His pleasure more actively? What is one specific area of your life where you need to rely on His choice rather than your own?

The Necessity of God's Existence
Sanusi proves that existence is necessary for God using the contingency argument.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why existence is necessary for God based on the contingency argument.

Core Idea
The existence of contingent things proves that God's existence is necessary.

This passage opens the discussion of the attributes necessary for God. Sanusi begins by asserting that existence is necessary for God, and he provides a concise proof based on the contingency of the world. The argument is foundational for Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing, then read the literal English translation. Get a feel for the flow.
Notice the concise style.
2
Identify the key claim
Find the statement that existence is necessary for God. This is the main thesis.
3
Understand the proof
The proof is that the existence of contingent things (al-hawadith) depends on God's existence. Trace this dependency.
4
Note the proof of origination
The proof that contingent things are originated is that they are inseparable from being in need of a specifier. This is a key step.
5
Reflect on the argument
Consider how this argument establishes God's necessary existence. How does the need for a specifier lead to a necessary being?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الصفات الواجبة له سبحانه . فإذا عرفت هذا فاعلم : أنَّه يجبُ لمولانا جل وعزَّ الوجود ؛ لتوقف وجودِ الحوادثِ على وجودِهِ تعالى ودليل حدوثها : لزومها لما يفتقر إلى المخصص
The attributes necessary for Him, glory be to Him. So if you know this, know that for our Master, may He be exalted and glorified, existence is necessary, because the existence of contingent things depends on His existence, may He be exalted, and the proof of their origination: that they are inseparable from what is in need of a specifier.
Source pages p. 94

Commentary
This passage opens the section on divine attributes. It first asserts that existence (al-wujud) is necessary for God. The proof is the contingency argument: the existence of contingent things (al-hawadith) depends on God's existence, and the proof that they are originated (al-huduth) is that they are inseparable from what is in need of a specifier (al-mukhassis). Thus, God must exist as the necessary being.

Key Terms
الصفات الواجبة
الوجود
الحوادث
الحدوث
المخصص
وجوب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The proof for God's existence requires a separate argument for the world's origination (creation ex nihilo).
Correction: Sanusi's proof is self-contained: the very nature of contingent things is that they are in need of a specifier, which implies their origination without needing an external proof.

Checkpoint
What is the proof that contingent things are originated (haditha) according to this passage?
A) They are composed of atoms
B) They depend on a specifier
C) They are temporal
D) They are multiple

Key Takeaway
Existence is necessary for God because all contingent things depend on Him as their specifier.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as if your existence is contingent on God every moment, or do you unconsciously assume self-sufficiency? What action can you take today to remember your total dependence on Him?
?
If you acknowledge that God's existence is necessary while yours is contingent, how would that shift your response to a recent success or failure? Can you attribute the outcome entirely to His specification and praise Him accordingly?
?
What would it mean for you to internalize that everything you rely on—health, wealth, relationships—is itself contingent and needs a specifier? How can you practically remind yourself of this truth while making your plans for tomorrow?

The Necessity of God's Eternity and Permanence
Sanusi proves God's eternity and permanence by showing the impossibility of His origination.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must be both eternal (no beginning) and permanent (no end) on the basis of rational proof.

Core Idea
If God were not eternal, He would be impossible, not just imperfect.

This short but powerful passage from Sanusi establishes a foundational truth about Allah: He must be eternal and permanent. The argument is twofold: first, if He were originated, He would need an agent and suffer the same incapability as other creatures; second, and more decisively, His origination would lead to circularity or infinite regress, which are impossible. This lesson unpacks the logic and shows why the attributes of qidam and baqa' are rationally necessary.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic text and its English translation. Notice the concise, logical style: the argument is presented as a conditional chain.
Pay attention to the word 'otherwise' (وإلا) which signals the counterfactual.
2
Understand the first consequence: need for an agent
Sanusi argues that if God were not eternal, He would need an agent (fāʿil) to bring Him into existence, making Him originated (ḥādith). An originated being is inherently incapable (ʿajz) like all other creatures.
This already shows that a non‑eternal God would be imperfect, but Sanusi goes further.
3
Grasp the second, stronger consequence: impossibility
Even worse, assuming God's origination leads to logical absurdities: either circularity (dawr) where existence precedes itself, or infinite regress (tasalsul) of causes, both rationally impossible. Hence His existence would be impossible altogether.
This is the core of the proof: origination is not just a flaw, it is a logical contradiction for the First Cause.
4
Connect to the attributes of qidam and baqā'
The argument establishes both pre‑eternity (qidam) and permanence (baqā'). If God had a beginning but no end, the same regress would apply to His continuation. Thus both are necessary.
Sanusi explicitly mentions both attributes in the opening sentence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجبُ له تعالى القدمُ والبقاء ، وإلا كان محتاجاً إلى الفاعل ، فيكون حادثاً ، فيجبُ له مِنَ العجزِ ما وجبَ لسائرِ الحوادثِ ، بل يكون حينئذ وجوده مستحيلاً ؛ لما يلزم على تقدير حدوثِهِ مِنَ الدور أو التسلسل المستحيلينِ
And pre-eternity and permanence are necessary for Him, exalted be He; otherwise He would be in need of an agent, so He would be originated, so there would be necessitated for Him from incapability what is necessitated for other originated things; rather, His existence would be impossible then, because of what would follow upon the assumption of His origination of circularity or infinite regress, both of which are impossible.
Source pages p. 94

Commentary
This passage proves that God must be eternal (qadim) and permanent (baqi). If He were originated (hadith), He would need an agent (fa'il), leading to the same incapability as other originated beings. Moreover, His existence would become impossible because assuming His origination leads to circularity (dawr) or infinite regress (tasalsul), both of which are impossible in Islamic theology.

Key Terms
القدم
البقاء
الفاعل
الحدوث
الدور
التسلسل
العجز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'pre-eternity' (qidam) only means God has no beginning, but He could have an end.
Correction: Sanusi explicitly states both pre-eternity and permanence (baqā') are necessary. The same argument against origination applies to both beginning and end; if God could cease to exist, He would be originated in a sense, leading to the same absurdities.

Checkpoint
What are the two impossible consequences that follow from assuming God's origination?
A) Duality and composition
B) Circularity and infinite regress
C) Incapability and need
D) Change and deficiency

Key Takeaway
God's eternity and permanence are rationally necessary because His origination would lead to circularity or infinite regress, both of which are impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God is truly eternal and permanent, and everything else is temporary, then what are you building your life on? Are you investing in what will last or in what will vanish? What one change can you make today to shift your focus from the temporary to the Eternal?
?
This passage proves that without God's eternity, He would need a creator. How does this truth affect your trust in God? Do you sometimes act as if God is not in control, as if you need to be your own agent? What would it look like to live today with full confidence in an eternal God who needs nothing?
?
Sanusi argues that if God were originated, His existence would be impossible. Does your heart truly believe that God is the only necessary being? Or do you sometimes treat other things as if they are necessary for your happiness? Identify one thing you treat as a 'must-have' and confess that only God is necessary.

Divine Attributes

God's Dissimilarity to Creation
Sanusi argues for God’s necessary dissimilarity to contingent things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must be completely unlike all created things in His essence and attributes.

Core Idea
If God resembled creation, He would be created — impossible.

Having established that God is eternal and self-sufficient, Sanusi now turns to the attributes of transcendence. The first of these is mukhalafa — that God is utterly unlike anything in creation. This short but profound statement grounds the Islamic insistence on God’s absolute uniqueness in essence and attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once. Notice it is a single sentence containing both a positive assertion and a conditional argument. Then read the literal translation.
Pay attention to the conditional phrase 'otherwise He would be originated like them.'
2
Analyze the logical structure
Break down the argument: first the assertion (God must be different in essence and attributes from all created things), then the conditional proof (if He were like them, He would be originated, which is impossible for God). So He must be different.
This is a classic kalam proof by contradiction.
3
Reflect on why this matters
Consider what belief this guards against: anthropomorphism (tashbih) — thinking God resembles creation. The doctrine of mukhalafa protects divine transcendence without denying that God has attributes.
Dissimilarity means His attributes are unlike any creature's, not that He has no attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب أن يكون تعالى مخالفاً في ذاتِهِ وصفاته لكلّ ما سواهُ مِنَ الحوادث ، وإلا كان حادثاً مثلها
And it is necessary that He, exalted be He, be different in His essence and attributes from all other contingent things, otherwise He would be originated like them.
Source pages p. 94

Commentary
This passage asserts the necessary attribute of God's complete dissimilarity (mukhalafa) to His creation. If He shared any likeness in essence or attributes, He would be like contingent things (al-hawadith), and hence would be originated (hadith) like them, which is impossible for the necessary being.

Key Terms
مخالف
الذات
الصفات
الحوادث
الحدوث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think dissimilarity means God has no attributes at all.
Correction: Mukhalafa means His attributes are unlike any creature's, not that He lacks attributes.

Checkpoint
Why must God be different from all contingent things?
A) Because He is one
B) Because if He were like them, He would be originated
C) Because He is all-powerful
D) Because He is eternal

Key Takeaway
God’s complete dissimilarity from creation is a necessary attribute; any likeness would imply originatedness.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of God's attributes like mercy or power, do you unconsciously compare them to human mercy or power? How does this lesson's demand of absolute dissimilarity challenge that habit? What will you do today to remind yourself that God's attributes are beyond your imagination?
?
Have you ever felt distant from God because you imagined He relates to you like a human would — with moods, limitations, or unpredictability? How can the truth of His dissimilarity reshape your trust in Him? Commit to one specific way you will adjust your worship this week to reflect His transcendence.
?
If God is completely unlike anything you know, how comfortable are you with not fully understanding Him? Identify one area of your life where you try to 'figure God out' or reduce Him to your expectations. Will you surrender that need for certainty and simply accept His uniqueness?

Divine Attributes
