Necessity of God's Self-Subsistence (al-Qiyam bi al-Nafs)
Sanusi proves God is self-subsisting, independent of substrate and agent.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must be self-subsisting (qiyam bi al-nafs) and how Sanusi proves this by ruling out substrate-dependence and agent-dependence.

Core Idea
God is self-subsisting: independent of substrate and agent, or He would not be God.

Sanusi now establishes a fundamental divine attribute: self-subsistence (al-qiyam bi al-nafs). This means God does not depend on anything else for His existence—neither a substrate to inhere in nor an agent to bring Him into being. The following passage presents two concise arguments showing why these are necessary for the Creator.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the two conditions: substrate and agent.
2
Understand the first argument
What happens if God were in a substrate? He would be an accident, leading to infinite regress.
The regress is impossible because it would require an endless chain of attributes.
3
Understand the second argument
What happens if God needed an agent? He would be originated, which is impossible for the eternal God.
4
Reflect on self-subsistence
Note that self-subsistence does not mean absence of attributes; God is an essence described with attributes, but independent.
This corrects a common misunderstanding.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى أنْ يكونَ قائماً بنفسِهِ ؛ أي : ذاتاً موصوفاً بالصفاتِ ، غنياً عن المحل والفاعل ؛ إذْ لو كانَ في محل لكانَ صفةً ، فيلزم ألا يتّصف بالصفاتِ الوجودية ولا لوازمها ؛ إذ لو قبلَتِ الصفة صفة وجودية .. لزم ألا تعرى عنها صفة كالذوات ، وذلك يستلزم التسلسل ، ودخول ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ، ولو كان محتاجاً للفاعل .. لكان حادثاً .، وهو محال
And it is necessary for Him, exalted be He, to be self-subsisting; meaning: an essence described with attributes, independent of substrate and agent; for if He were in a substrate, He would be an accident, and it would follow that He would not be described with existential attributes nor their concomitants; for if the attribute received an existential attribute, it would follow that no attribute could be free of it like substances, and that necessitates infinite regress and the entry of the infinite into existence; and if He were in need of an agent, He would be originated, which is impossible.
Source pages p. 94

Commentary
This passage proves God's self-subsistence (qiyam bi al-nafs). It first defines it: an essence described with attributes, independent of any substrate (mahall) or agent (fa'il). The proof: if God were in a substrate, He would be an accident (sifa), preventing Him from having existential attributes without infinite regress. Also, if He needed an agent, He would be originated. Thus, God must be self-subsisting.

Key Terms
القيام بالنفس
الذات
المحل
الفاعل
الصفة
التسلسل
ما لا نهاية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Self-subsisting means God has no attributes.
Correction: Sanusi explicitly says God is an essence described with attributes, but He does not depend on any substrate or agent.

Checkpoint
What would God be if He resided in a substrate?
A) A substance
B) An accident
C) A body
D) A spirit

Key Takeaway
God must be self-subsisting, independent of everything, or He would not be God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God were dependent on even a single thing, He would not be God. What created thing have you been treating as though your existence depends on it? How would your life change if you truly internalized that only God is self-subsisting?
?
In the argument, Sanusi shows that needing an agent implies origination. You were brought into existence by an agent. Are you living as if you are self-created, or as a being that exists at every moment by God's will? What would it look like today to act like a contingent being?
?
The impossibility of infinite regress means there must be a foundation that depends on nothing. You often build your identity, security, and happiness on things that themselves depend on other things. Would you be willing to identify one such fragile foundation and consciously rebuild it on the Self-Subsisting One?

The Necessity of God's Oneness (al-Wahdaniyya)
Sanusi proves God's oneness by definition and logical proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof for God's oneness based on the impossibility of multiple necessary beings.

Core Idea
If there were a second god, none would be necessary – both would be contingent.

Sanusi presents a concise yet powerful argument: God's oneness is not merely a doctrinal claim but a rational necessity. In this passage, he defines what oneness means and then proves that any partner would undermine God's necessity. Let's examine his reasoning carefully.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of oneness
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice that Sanusi defines oneness as having no equal in essence, attributes, or effecting.
The definition is comprehensive: it covers both essence and actions.
2
Understand the proof by contradiction
Sanusi argues that if there were a partner (likeness or effecter), God would need a specifier to distinguish Himself. That need implies contingency, which contradicts God's necessity.
This is a standard kalam argument against multiple gods.
3
Connect to divine attributes
Reflect on why oneness is necessary for God's perfection. The proof shows that any partner would introduce limitation (huduth) and inability (ajz), which are incompatible with the Necessary Being.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى الوحدانية ؛ أي : لا مِثْلَ لهُ في ذاتِهِ ، ولا في صفةٍ مِنْ صفاتِهِ ، ولا مؤثرَ معَهُ في فعل مِنَ الأفعالِ ؛ إذْ لو كانَ معَهُ مِثْلُ أو مؤثر . . لَمَا كانَ واجبَ الوجودِ ؛ لاحتياجه حينئذ إلى مَنْ يخصِّصُهُ بما يمتاز بهِ عمَّا يماثله عموماً أو خصوصاً ، وذلك يستلزم الحدوث والعجز عن كل ممكن .
And oneness is necessary for Him, exalted be He; meaning: there is no likeness to Him in His essence, nor in any of His attributes, nor any effecter with Him in any act; for if there were with Him a likeness or an effecter, He would not be necessary of existence, because He would then need someone to specify Him by that by which He is distinguished from what resembles Him generally or specifically, and that necessitates origination and incapability over every possible.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage proves divine oneness (al-wahdaniyya). It defines it as no equal (mithl) in essence or attributes, and no effecter (mu'aththir) alongside Him. The proof: if there were another being or effecter, God would need a specifier (mukhassis) to distinguish Him, making Him contingent and incapable, which is impossible for the necessary being.

Key Terms
الوحدانية
المثل
المؤثر
وجوب الوجود
المخصص
الحدوث
العجز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's oneness only means He is one in number (numerical unity).
Correction: Sanusi shows oneness also means no partner in affecting actions (no co-effector), so it includes ontological and causal uniqueness.

Checkpoint
What would happen if there were another being like God?
A) God would still be necessary
B) God would need a specifier
C) God would be all-powerful
D) God would be eternal

Key Takeaway
The oneness of God is a rational necessity: any partner would undermine His necessary existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You just read that any partner with God would make Him needy and contingent. Consider the areas of your life where you depend on your own strength, status, or relationships as if they have independent power. Will you honestly admit that you treat them as 'effecters' alongside God? What step will you take today to return sole efficacy to Him?
?
If God’s oneness means He has no specifier, how does that confront your tendency to define your own identity through your achievements or failures? Are you living as though you 'specify' yourself? How would your daily choices change if you truly believed that only God determines who you are?
?
The proof shows that even one partner would make God incapable (ajz). Have you ever felt that you can manage some part of your life without relying on God? In that area, are you not setting up an 'effecter' in your heart? What will you do to tear down that idol today?

God's Power and Will Extend to All Possibles
Sanusi proves that God's power and will necessitate coverage of all possible things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's power and will must extend to every possible thing, based on Sanusi's proof from the dependency of originated beings.

Core Idea
God's power and will cover every possibility, otherwise existence itself would be impossible.

Sanusi argues that if God's power did not cover all possibles, then there would be a logical contradiction leading to the impossibility of any created thing. This lesson unpacks that short but powerful proof, showing how every originated being depends on God's power, will, and knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once and then its translation. Notice the concise, logical structure of Sanusi's argument.
The passage is a single sentence but contains a complete proof.
2
Analyze the conditional argument
Identify the conditional: 'if incapability over some, then incapability over all.' Understand how that leads to impossibility of existence.
This step reveals the core logical move.
3
Reflect on the three dependencies
The passage lists three dependencies: existence/annihilation on power, specification on will, and being an object of will on knowledge. Consider why each is necessary.
These dependencies show why God's attributes must be all-encompassing.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى القدرة والإرادة المتعلقتان بكل ممكن ؛ إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها ، وذلكَ يستلزم استحالة وجودِها ؛ لتوقف كل حادث في وجودِهِ وإعدامه على اقتدارِ فاعله ، وفي تخصُّصِهِ على إرادتِهِ ، وفي كونه مراداً على علمه
And power and will are necessary for Him, exalted be He, both related to every possible; for incapability over some entails incapability over all, and that necessitates the impossibility of their existence; because every originated thing depends in its existence and its annihilation on the ability of its agent, and in its specification on His will, and in its being an object of will on His knowledge.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage proves that God possesses power (qudra) and will (irada) that relate to every possible (mumkin) thing. The proof: if God were incapable over some possibles, He would be incapable over all, making the existence of those things impossible. Moreover, every originated being depends on God's power for existence and annihilation, on His will for specification, and on His knowledge for being an object of will.

Key Terms

الإرادة
الممكن
العجز
الاقتدار
التخصيص
العلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's will is limited to moral or religious matters, not extending to every contingent event.
Correction: Sanusi proves that God's will must encompass every possible thing, including all contingent events, because specification of each originated being depends on His will.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, on what does the specification (takhsis) of an originated thing depend?
A) God's power
B) God's will
C) God's knowledge
D) God's existence

Key Takeaway
God's power and will must encompass every possible thing, as any limitation would undermine the very possibility of creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as though God's power covers every possibility—including the ones that scare you most? If not, what specific fear are you withholding from His authority?
?
If God's will specifies every detail of your existence, can you honestly say you are at peace with His choices for you today? What aspect of your life do you most struggle to entrust to His specification?
?
Knowing that your very being depends on God's ability, will, and knowledge at every moment, what would it take for your next prayer to reflect that total dependence rather than a mere request?

The All-Encompassing Nature of Divine Knowledge
Sanusi proves that God's knowledge encompasses all necessary, possible, and impossible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's knowledge must encompass all categories of existence—necessary, possible, and impossible—and why any restriction would imply imperfection.

Core Idea
God's knowledge is unrestricted because restriction implies need and change.

Sanusi presents a concise but powerful proof that God's knowledge cannot be limited to only some things. The argument uses the principle that any restriction in an attribute would make it dependent on an external agent, which leads to createdness. This short passage grounds the universality of divine knowledge in pure reason.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Notice the logical flow: statement, justification, consequence.
The Arabic is dense; take your time.
2
Identify the three categories
The passage states that knowledge relates to three kinds: necessary, possible, and impossible. Recognize what each category represents in Islamic theology.
Impossible includes logical contradictions, not just non-existent things.
3
Understand the proof structure
The proof is a reductio ad absurdum: if knowledge were restricted, it would need an agent (hence created), and then the subject would be created too. This is impossible for God.
Trace the chain: restriction → need → origination → impossible.
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider why this matters: it affirms that God knows everything, including what never happens, and that His knowledge is perfect and eternal.
This refutes any notion that God only knows actual things.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى العلمُ المتعلق بكل واجب وجائز ومستحيل ؛ لأَنَّ الاختصاص بالبعض يستلزم الحدوث ؛ لافتقار الصفةِ حينئذ إلى الفاعل ، وحدوثها يستلزم حدوثَ موصوفها ؛ لاستحالة تعريه عنها وعن أضدادها
And knowledge is necessary for Him, exalted be He, related to every necessary, possible, and impossible; because restriction to some necessitates origination; for the attribute would then be in need of an agent, and its origination would necessitate the origination of its subject; because it is impossible for the subject to be devoid of it and its opposites.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage proves that God's knowledge ('ilm) encompasses all things: necessary (wajib), contingent (ja'iz), and impossible (mustahil). The proof: if His knowledge were limited to some things, that restriction would mean the attribute is originated (hadith) because it would need an agent, and the origination of the attribute would entail the origination of its subject (God), which is impossible.

Key Terms
العلم
الواجب
الجائز
المستحيل
الاختصاص
الحدوث
الفاعل
الموصوف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's knowledge only pertains to what exists, not to possibilities or impossibilities.
Correction: The passage proves that God's knowledge must include the impossible as well, because restriction would imply createdness.

Checkpoint
Why would a restriction of God's knowledge to some things necessitate origination?
A) Because knowledge is eternal
B) Because the attribute would need an agent
C) Because God would be ignorant
D) Because knowledge is an accident

Key Takeaway
God's knowledge is unrestricted because any restriction would imply dependence and temporality, contradicting His perfection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God knows every impossible thought and hidden desire you have ever entertained, does that change how you view your private moments? What one thought today are you most ashamed that He knows, and will you repent from it now?
?
The proof shows that God's knowledge cannot be limited because He is perfect. Are you living as if God is unaware of the small details of your life? What area of your life are you trying to keep hidden from Him, and will you surrender it fully today?
?
God's knowledge encompasses even what will never happen—so He knows every excuse you could make. When you face Him on the Day of Judgment, will you have any excuse left? What step can you take right now to remove the gap between what you profess and how you live?

beginner
6 min
The Necessity of God's Oneness
Sanusi proves the necessity of God's oneness and defines it.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's definition of God's oneness (al-wahdaniyya) and his proof that any equal or partner would contradict God's being the Necessary Existent.

Core Idea
God's oneness means He has no peer in essence, attributes, or agency; having a peer would make Him dependent.

Sanusi begins by asserting that oneness is necessary for God. He does not merely say 'God is one' in a numerical sense but explains that oneness means absolute uniqueness: no equal in His essence, no equal in any attribute, and no partner in any act. He then provides a rational proof: if there were a like or co-agent, God would need a specifier to distinguish Himself, which would entail dependence and temporality—impossible for the Necessary Existent. This lesson unpacks that argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the literal translation. Notice the concise style and the logical structure.
The passage has a definition followed by a conditional proof.
2
Identify the three aspects of oneness
Sanusi defines oneness as negating three things: a like in essence, a like in attributes, and a co-effective agent. Write these down.
These cover God's essence, attributes, and actions.
3
Follow the proof chain
The proof is conditional: 'if there were a like or co-agent, then God would not be Necessary Existent.' The reason is that He would need a specifier to distinguish Himself from the like, which implies the need for a cause—hence temporality and incapacity.
This is a reductio ad absurdum: showing that the assumption leads to impossible consequences.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
Conclude that oneness is necessary because its opposite would negate God's necessary existence. How does this relate to the Islamic creed?
This is a foundational argument against polytheism and anthropomorphism.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى الوحدانية ؛ أي : لا مِثْلَ لهُ في ذاتِهِ ، ولا في صفةٍ مِنْ صفاتِهِ ، ولا مؤثرَ معَهُ في فعل مِنَ الأفعالِ ؛ إذْ لو كانَ معَهُ مِثْلُ أو مؤثر . . لَمَا كانَ واجبَ الوجودِ ؛ لاحتياجه حينئذ إلى مَنْ يخصِّصُهُ بما يمتاز بهِ عمَّا يماثله عموماً أو خصوصاً ، وذلك يستلزم الحدوث والعجز عن كل ممكن .
And it is necessary for Him, Exalted, oneness; meaning: there is no like unto Him in His essence, nor in any of His attributes, nor any effective agent with Him in any act; for if there were with Him a like or an effective agent, then He would not be necessary of existence; because He would then need someone to specify Him by that by which He is distinguished from what resembles Him generally or specifically, and that entails temporality and incapability over every possible thing.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage is a proof of the necessity of God's oneness (al-wahdaniyya). Sanusi first defines oneness negatively: God has no equal in His essence, nor in any attribute, nor does any other being share effective agency with Him. He then argues that if there were an equal or a sharer in agency, God would not be necessary of existence (wajib al-wujud) because He would need a specifier (mukhassis) to distinguish Him from that which resembles Him. This need for specification implies temporality (huduth) and incapacity, which are impossible for God. Thus, the proof is based on the impossibility of two necessary existents co-existing without a distinguishing factor.

Key Terms
الوحدانية
المثل
المؤثر
واجب الوجود
التخصيص
الحدوث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God's oneness simply means He is one in number, allowing for multiple gods but only one is worshipped.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that oneness (wahdaniyya) means absolute uniqueness in essence, attributes, and actions. No other entity can share any of these aspects, even in principle.

Checkpoint
Why would the existence of a 'like' (mithl) for God entail temporality (huduth)?
A) Because a like would need its own specifier
B) Because God would then need a specifier to distinguish Himself
C) Because a like would cause contradictions in attributes
D) Because existence of two necessaries is impossible by definition

Key Takeaway
God's oneness means He has no equal in essence, attributes, or actions; any such equal would require a specifier, contradicting His necessary existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever, even indirectly, attributed any power or effect to a created being, making them a co-agent with God in your heart? This lesson proves that any co-agent would undermine God's necessary existence. What will you do to purify your reliance on only Him?
?
This lesson shows that any similarity between God and creation—even in attributes—would require a specifier, contradicting His necessity. Are there aspects of God you unwittingly imagine as similar to human traits? How will you affirm His absolute uniqueness in your daily thoughts?
?
If oneness means no effective agent with Him in any act, then your every action is solely from God. Do you live with a sense of self-sufficiency or feel that your efforts are independent? What concrete change does this demand of you today?

The Necessity of God's Comprehensive Knowledge
Sanusi proves the necessity of God's universal knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi argues that God's knowledge must cover all three modal categories (necessary, possible, impossible) and how restriction would imply temporality.

Core Idea
God's knowledge must be all-encompassing, or else it would be temporal.

Sanusi now turns to the divine attribute of knowledge (al-ilm). He argues that God's knowledge is not limited to actual things but covers everything that can be conceived: the necessary, the possible, and the impossible. This passage provides a concise proof based on the impossibility of restriction leading to temporality. We will walk through the logical steps that connect limitation to contingency, and see why the divine must know all.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and its translation
First, read the Arabic text carefully, then the literal English translation. Notice the key terms: الواجب, الجائز, المستحيل.
The passage is dense; take your time.
2
Identify the claim and its scope
Sanusi states that knowledge is necessary for God and that it encompasses every wajib, ja'iz, and mustahil. Identify what each category means.
Wajib = necessary existence, Ja'iz = possible, Mustahil = impossible.
3
Trace the logical argument
Follow the reasoning: if God's knowledge were restricted to only some things, that restriction would necessitate an agent (fa'il) bringing about the attribute, implying temporality (huduth). Then the attribute's temporality would force the essence to be temporal, which is impossible.
The argument hinges on the impossibility of the essence being stripped of knowledge and its opposites.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى العلمُ المتعلق بكل واجب وجائز ومستحيل ؛ لأَنَّ الاختصاص بالبعض يستلزم الحدوث ؛ لافتقار الصفةِ حينئذ إلى الفاعل ، وحدوثها يستلزم حدوثَ موصوفها ؛ لاستحالة تعريه عنها وعن أضدادها
And it is necessary for Him, Exalted, knowledge pertaining to every necessary, possible, and impossible thing; because specification to some [of them] necessitates temporality; due to the attribute's then needing an agent, and its temporality necessitates the temporality of its described entity; because it is impossible for it to be stripped of it and its opposites.
Source pages p. 95

Commentary
This passage proves the necessity of God's knowledge (al-ilm) covering all three modal categories: necessary (wajib), possible (ja'iz), and impossible (mustahil). Sanusi argues that if God's knowledge were limited to only some things (e.g., only possibles), then that restriction would entail temporality (huduth) because the attribute would then need an agent to bring it about. If the attribute becomes temporal, then the entity described by it (the divine essence) would also be temporal, since it is impossible for the essence to be stripped of knowledge and its opposites (ignorance). Hence, God's knowledge must be all-encompassing.

Key Terms
العلم
الواجب
الجائز
المستحيل
الاختصاص
الحدوث
الموصوف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's knowledge is only of actual things (i.e., what exists).
Correction: Sanusi argues that God's knowledge includes not only actuals but also possibles (ja'iz) and impossibles (mustahil). Restriction to actuals would imply contingency.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why would restricting God's knowledge to some things lead to temporality?
A) Because knowledge cannot be partial by nature
B) Because the attribute would then need an agent to bring it about
C) Because God's essence would lack perfection
D) Because restricted knowledge implies ignorance

Key Takeaway
God's knowledge must encompass all necessary, possible, and impossible things; otherwise, the divine attribute would be temporal and thus impossible for an eternal being.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You claim to believe God knows everything, including your most hidden thoughts and future sins. But does your daily actions reflect someone who is truly aware that nothing escapes His knowledge? What is one area of your life today where you act as if God does not see? Will you commit to changing that behavior starting now?
?
If God's knowledge includes all impossibilities, then nothing—not your past, your weaknesses, or your worst failures—is outside His awareness. How does that truth confront your attempts to hide or justify sin? Will you repent and live in the light of His all-encompassing knowledge?
?
The proof shows that limited knowledge would make God temporal. Does your heart fully accept that God's knowledge is unlimited? If you doubt, what is the root of that doubt? Pray now for certainty and for the courage to live as if every thought and action is before Him.

The Necessity of God's Hearing, Sight, and Speech
Sanusi enumerates three necessary divine attributes and states their proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that hearing, sight, and speech are necessary attributes of God, known through revelation and purified from human limitations.

Core Idea
God's hearing, sight, and speech are real but unlike ours—revealed, not reasoned.

In this passage, Sanusi affirms three divine attributes that are known through the revealed law rather than rational demonstration. He carefully distinguishes God's speech from human speech, emphasizing that it is free from letters, sounds, and temporal features. This lesson will help you grasp the nature of these 'sam'iyyat' attributes and their proof.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation to get the overall meaning.
Notice that the proof (al-shar') is mentioned at the end.
2
Identify the three attributes and their descriptions
Find the three attributes: hearing (sam'), sight (basar), and speech (kalam). Note how hearing and sight pertain to all existents, while speech is described with negations.
Speech is purified from human characteristics like letters and sounds.
3
Understand the negations in God's speech
Sanusi negates from God's speech: letters, sounds, temporal order, parts, renewal, and silence. This highlights that God's speech is unlike created speech.
This safeguards against anthropomorphism.
4
Recognize the source of proof
The proof for all three attributes is the revealed law (al-shar'). This means they are known through scripture, not purely reason.
5
Reflect on the implications
Consider why these attributes are classified as 'sam'iyyat' (heard) rather than 'aqliyyat' (rational). How does this shape Islamic theology?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى السمع والبصر المتعلقانِ بجميع الموجوداتِ ، والكلام المنزَّه عن الحرف والصوتِ ، والتقديم والتأخير ، والكل والبعض ، والتجدد والسكوت ، المتعلق بما تعلَّق به العلم ، ودليل هذه الثلاثةِ الشرع .
And it is necessary for Him, Exalted, hearing and sight, pertaining to all existents, and speech, purified from letter and sound, and precedence and delay, and the whole and the part, and renewal and silence, pertaining to that which knowledge pertains to; and the proof of these three is the revealed law (al-shar').
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage asserts the necessity of three divine attributes: hearing (sam'), sight (basar), and speech (kalam). Sanusi describes them: hearing and sight pertain to all existents; speech is purified from letters and sounds (unlike human speech) and is not subject to temporal features like precedence/delay, whole/part, renewal/silence. It pertains to the same objects as knowledge. The proof for all three is the revealed law (al-shar'), i.e., scriptural evidence, not rational demonstration. This highlights that some necessary attributes are known through revelation alone.

Key Terms
السمع
البصر
الكلام
الحرف
الصوت
الشرع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's speech is like human speech, composed of letters, sounds, and subject to time.
Correction: Sanusi explicitly negates letters, sounds, precedence, delay, parts, renewal, and silence from God's speech, affirming it is an eternal, non-material attribute.

Checkpoint
What is the proof (dalil) for the necessity of God's hearing, sight, and speech?
A) Rational necessity
B) The revealed law (al-shar')
C) Consensus of scholars
D) Divine wisdom

Key Takeaway
The attributes of hearing, sight, and speech are necessary for God and known through revelation, not human reason.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
God hears every word you say, even the ones meant only for yourself. What would change in your speech today if you truly believed every syllable is heard by the One who created you? Will you commit to speaking only what pleases Him for the next hour?
?
If God's speech is not like human speech—no letters, no sounds—then how can you claim to 'hear' Him through the Qur'an? What does that demand of your heart when you recite or listen to its verses? Are you listening with the ears of your soul or just your ears?
?
Sanusi says the proof of God's hearing, sight, and speech is revelation, not reason. Does your reliance on reason over revelation sometimes make you doubt these attributes? Will you submit today to the fact that some truths are only known because God told you, not because you figured them out?

The Necessity of God's Life
Sanusi proves the necessity of God's life as a prerequisite for other attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's life is a necessary attribute that serves as the foundation for all other divine attributes.

Core Idea
God's life is necessary because the other attributes cannot exist without it.

In this brief but powerful passage, Sanusi establishes that life (al-hayah) is a necessary attribute of God, not just an optional one. He provides a simple yet compelling proof: the attributes already mentioned—power, will, knowledge, hearing, sight, and speech—cannot possibly exist without life. This lesson will unpack that reasoning and show why life is the bedrock of all divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage carefully
Read the Arabic sentence once, noting its structure: first the claim (life is necessary), then the proof (because other attributes are impossible without it).
The word 'لَمْ' (lam) in 'لِاسْتِحَالَة' indicates a causal link.
2
Identify the claim and the proof
The claim: 'ويجب له تعالى الحياةُ' (life is necessary for God). The proof: 'لاستحالة وجود الصفات السابقة بدونها' (because it is impossible for the previous attributes to exist without it).
3
Reflect on the logical connection
Ask yourself: why does the impossibility of other attributes without life prove that life itself is necessary? Think about the concept of prerequisite conditions.
If A cannot exist without B, and A is necessary, then B must also be necessary.
4
Connect to broader theology
Consider that this attribute (life) is not independent; it enables all the other attributes. In Islamic theology, God's life is eternal and perfect, underlying all His actions and qualities.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى الحياةُ ؛ لاستحالة وجود الصفاتِ السابقة بدونها .
And it is necessary for Him, Exalted, life; because of the impossibility of the existence of the previous attributes without it.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This brief passage states the necessity of God's life (al-hayah). The proof is simple: the previously mentioned attributes (power, will, knowledge, hearing, sight, speech) cannot exist without life. Since those attributes are necessary, life must also be necessary as their foundation.

Key Terms
الحياة
الصفات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think life is just another attribute among equals, not recognizing its foundational role.
Correction: Sanusi shows that life is a precondition for the other attributes, so it is logically prior.

Checkpoint
Why is God's life considered necessary?
A) Because life is intrinsically perfect
B) Because the previous attributes cannot exist without it
C) Because revelation commands it
D) Because all living beings need a creator

Key Takeaway
God's life is necessary because it is the indispensable foundation for all other divine attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God’s life is the foundation of all His other attributes, do you truly engage with Him as a living Being in your prayers, or do you recite attributes mechanically? What would change if you paused before each attribute to remind yourself that it flows from His life?
?
The proof for God’s life depends on the impossibility of other attributes without it. In your own life, are there spiritual qualities you try to cultivate—patience, gratitude, trust—without rooting them in the life of God? How can you consciously anchor them in His life?
?
Imam Sanusi’s argument shows that life is not just another attribute but the very condition for all others. Does your daily walk with God reflect the urgency of His life as the source of everything, or do you treat it as one item on a list? What practical step can you take today to live as if His life is the only foundation?

The Permissible in God's Acts: Definition and Examples
Sanusi defines and illustrates what is permissible for God to do.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what is rationally permissible for God to do, based on Sanusi's definition, proof, and examples.

Core Idea
God is bound by nothing; He may do or leave any possible act.

After establishing what is necessary and impossible for God, Sanusi now turns to the category of al-ja'iz (the permissible). This passage defines it clearly: God may do or leave any possible act, whether it appears good or not. The proof is His universal power and will. Several concrete examples are given to illustrate the scope of this permission.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage carefully, then read the literal English translation. Notice the concise style: Sanusi packs definition, proof, and examples into a few lines.
The passage begins with [الجائز في أفعاله سبحانَهُ] as a heading.
2
Analyze the definition and proof
Identify the core definition: doing or leaving any possible thing, whether good or its opposite. Then find the proof: because God's power and will cover all possible things. Ask yourself why this proof is necessary.
The proof is a brief reminder of what was established earlier (the universality of divine power and will).
3
Examine the examples
The passage lists four examples: creating vision of His essence, hearing of His eternal speech, reward in paradise, and resurrection of messengers. For each, consider why it is considered permissible and not obligatory. This will solidify your understanding.
These examples are not exhaustive; they illustrate key areas of theological debate.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ الجائز في أفعاله سبحانَهُ ] وأما الجائز في حقِّهِ تعالى : ففعل كل ممكن أو تركه ، صلاحاً كان أو ضدَّهُ ؛ لما عُرفَ قبلُ مِنْ وجوبِ عموم قدرته وإرادته تعالى لجميع الممكنات ، ويدخل في ذلكَ : جواز خلْقِ الله تعالى الرؤية لذاتِهِ العليَّة ، والسمع لكلامه القديم ، والثوابَ في دار النعيم ، والبعث لـرسـلـهِ الأكرمينَ ، صلواتُ اللهِ وسلامه عليهم أجمعينَ
[The permissible in His acts, Glory be to Him] As for the permissible with respect to Him, Exalted: doing every possible thing or leaving it, whether it is right or its opposite; because of what was known previously from the obligation of the generality of His power and will, Exalted, for all possible things; and that includes: the permissibility of God's creating vision for His lofty essence, and hearing for His eternal speech, and reward in the abode of bliss, and the resurrection for His most noble messengers, may God's prayers and peace be upon them all.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage defines al-ja'iz (the permissible) with respect to God's actions. Sanusi says it is the doing or leaving of any possible thing, whether it is good (salah) or its opposite. The proof is that God's power and will are universal, covering all possibles, so no act is compulsory for Him. He then gives examples: God may create the vision (ru'ya) of Himself (in the afterlife), hearing of His eternal speech, reward in paradise, and resurrection of the messengers. These are all possible and not obligatory for God.

Key Terms
الجائز
الممكن
الصلاح
الرؤية
الكلام القديم
الثواب
البعث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God is obligated to do what is best (al-aslah) for His creatures.
Correction: Sanusi clearly states that God may do or leave any possible thing, whether it appears good or not. He is not compelled by any external standard.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT explicitly listed as an example of the permissible for God in this passage?
A) Creating vision of His essence
B) Creating hearing of His eternal speech
C) Creating reward in paradise
D) Creating evil

Key Takeaway
God may freely do or leave any possible act; He is not obligated to choose what is good.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you believe that God owes you anything? Or do you live in the humility of knowing that every good thing in your life is a sheer gift from His free will, not a payment for your deeds? What would change if you truly accepted that God is not obligated to do anything for you?
?
When you face difficulty or when a prayer seems unanswered, do you accuse God of being unjust? This lesson teaches that God may do or leave any act, whether it appears good or not. How does that truth challenge your expectations of God? Are you willing to submit to His absolute freedom even when it hurts?
?
The passage says God may create reward in paradise, but He is not forced to. How does this truth affect your hope for the afterlife? Are you relying on God's promise (which He chose to make) or on a sense of entitlement? What would it mean to hope only in God's free favor?

Obligatory Attributes of Hearing, Sight, Speech, and Life
Sanusi enumerates four obligatory divine attributes with their proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four obligatory attributes of God and how each is proved: hearing, sight, and speech by revelation, and life by reason.

Core Idea
Three attributes come by scripture; life is the rational foundation.

Sanusi continues his list of necessary attributes. After discussing the attributes of essence and action, he now turns to four specific attributes that are obligatory for God: hearing, sight, speech, and life. He distinguishes between those proven by revelation and those proven by reason, giving a clear case for the rational necessity of life.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the concise style and the two distinct proofs.
2
Identify the four attributes and their proofs
The passage presents four attributes: hearing, sight, speech, and life. Identify which proof is given for each: revelation for the first three, rational necessity for life.
The proof for life is explicitly rational: the impossibility of the other attributes without it.
3
Understand the nature of divine speech
Sanusi specifies that God's speech is not composed of letters or sounds, and is free from temporal qualities. This is a key point in distinguishing divine speech from human speech.
4
Reflect on the logical priority of life
Ask yourself why life is necessary for the other attributes. The commentary explains that attributes like hearing and sight cannot exist in a lifeless being, so life is a foundational attribute.
This connects to the learning objective about rational proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى السمع والبصر المتعلقانِ الموجوداتِ ، والكلام المنزَّه عن الحرف والصوتِ ، والتقديم والتأخير ، والكل والبعض ، والتجدد والسكوت ، المتعلق بما تعلَّق به العلم ، ودليل هذه الثلاثةِ الشرع . ويجب له تعالى الحياةُ ؛ لاستحالة وجود الصفاتِ السابقة بدونها .
And wajib for Him, exalted, are hearing and sight which relate to existent things, and speech that is transcendent of letters and sound, and [He is transcendent of] precedence and delay, whole and part, renewal and silence, relating to what knowledge relates to; and the proof of these three is the revealed law. And wajib for Him, exalted, is life, because the preceding attributes are impossible without it.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof of four obligatory attributes of God. The author states that God must have hearing (sam') and sight (basar) that relate to all existent things. He also must have speech (kalam) which is not composed of letters or sound, and is not subject to temporal properties like precedence/delay, whole/part, renewal/silence. This speech relates to what knowledge relates to (i.e., all knowables). The proof for these three attributes (hearing, sight, speech) is the revealed law (shar'), meaning scriptural evidence. Then the author adds that life (hayat) is wajib, because the other attributes cannot exist without it; this is a rational proof.

Key Terms
سمع
بصر
كلام
حياة
شرع
واجب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that all divine attributes are proved by reason alone.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that hearing, sight, and speech are proven by revelation (shar'), while life is proven by reason.

Checkpoint
What is the proof for the attributes of hearing, sight, and speech according to this passage?
A) Rational necessity
B) The revealed law
C) Consensus
D) Personal experience

Key Takeaway
The attributes of hearing, sight, and speech are known through revelation, while life is known through reason as the basis for all other attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You believe God hears and sees everything. But does your life reflect that you are truly aware He sees every thought and hears every word you speak? Will you change one habit today to live in this awareness?
?
If God's speech is not like human speech—transcendent of letters and sound—how does that change the weight you give to the Quran? Do you approach it as the eternal speech of the One who sees and hears you right now?
?
The author says life is the foundation of the other attributes. Do you live as if your own existence depends on God's life? How would you live differently if you truly believed that without His life, nothing else exists?

Impossible Attributes Are the Negation of Necessary Ones
Sanusi defines impossibility for God as contradiction to necessary attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the impossible attributes of God are not a separate list but are derived as the opposites of His necessary attributes.

Core Idea
Impossibility for God is simply the contradiction of His necessary attributes.

Sanusi now moves from the necessary attributes to their opposite category: the impossible. Rather than listing a separate set, he defines impossibility in relation to what is wajib. This shows the logical structure of kalam: categories are defined by mutual contradiction.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic line and its translation. Note how Sanusi defines impossibility not by enumeration but by relation.
The bracket in the Arabic indicates a heading.
2
Analyze the definition
Focus on the phrase 'kull ma yunafi hadhihi al-sifat al-wajiba'. Ask: what does 'yunafi' mean? How does this connect to the previous lesson on necessary attributes?
This shows impossibility is a derivative category.
3
Compare with necessary attributes
Recall the list of necessary attributes (existence, eternity, etc.). For each, think of its opposite (non-existence, temporality, etc.). Those opposites are the impossible attributes.
4
Reflect on the implication
Why does Sanusi not list each impossible attribute separately? Because they are logically deduced from the necessary ones.
This is a core principle of kalam methodology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ المستحيلُ في حقه سبحانَهُ ] وأما المستحيلُ في حقِّهِ تعالى : فكل ما ينافي هـذهِ الصفات الواجبة .
[The impossible with respect to Him, glorified] As for what is impossible with respect to Him, exalted: everything that contradicts these obligatory attributes.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage is a concise definition of the category of impossible attributes for God (mustahil). The author states that anything that contradicts (yunafi) the wajib attributes mentioned earlier is impossible for God. Thus impossibility is not an independent list but is derived from the negation of the attributes that are wajib. This is a tawqifi (delimitative) approach.

Key Terms
مستحيل
ينافي
صفات واجبة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think impossibility is a separate set of attributes that need to be memorized independently, like 'God cannot be made of matter' or 'God cannot be unjust'.
Correction: Sanusi defines impossibility purely as the contradiction of the necessary attributes. Thus impossible attributes are not a standalone list; they are merely the opposites of the wajib attributes. Understanding the wajib attributes automatically gives the impossible ones.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what constitutes an impossible attribute for God?
A) Anything not mentioned in revelation
B) Anything that contradicts the obligatory attributes
C) Anything that is human-like
D) Anything that is not rational

Key Takeaway
The impossible attributes of God are not an independent category; they are simply the negation of His necessary attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If non-existence is impossible for God because His existence is necessary, is your heart so certain of His existence that the thought of His non-existence never crosses your mind? Where do you still waver in that certainty, and what will you do to root that wavering out?
?
You just learned that every impossible attribute is simply the negation of a necessary one. Think of an attribute of God you struggle to internalize—like His mercy or justice. Is your difficulty because you secretly entertain the possibility of its opposite? Examine your soul: what would it mean to truly believe that cruelty or injustice is impossible for Him, not just unlikely? How would that change one decision you face this week?
?
This lesson defines impossibility relationally: nothing is impossible in itself, but only because it contradicts what must be true of God. Consider one sin you are prone to justify. Does that sin contradict a necessary attribute of God (e.g., His knowledge, His power, His holiness)? If yes, then by committing it, are you not acting as if that attribute were not necessary? What concrete step will you take today to align your action with the necessity of God's perfection?

The Possible (Ja'iz) for God: Definition and Examples
Sanusi defines the possible for God and gives examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the category of 'possible' (ja'iz) in relation to God's actions, how it is defined, and what examples it includes.

Core Idea
God's actions are not constrained by human notions of good or evil.

Sanusi now turns to the third rational category: that which is possible for God. This category covers all actions that God may choose to do or not do, based on His absolute power and will. The passage clarifies that this possibility is not limited by our judgments of benefit or harm, and it gives specific examples that are often discussed in Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and find the definition
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Identify the sentence that defines what is 'possible' (ja'iz) for God.
Notice the phrase 'فعل كل ممكن أو تركه'.
2
Understand the rational basis
The passage gives a reason: because God's power and will extend to all possible things. Reflect on why this is the foundation for the category.
This refers back to earlier sections on God's attributes.
3
List the examples
The passage lists four examples: creating vision of His essence, hearing His speech, reward in paradise, and sending messengers. Write them down and see how each is a possible act.
These examples are often debated in kalam.
4
Reflect on the misconception
Consider the common misunderstanding that 'possible' might imply God could do unjust things. How does the passage show that God's actions are not bound by human concepts of good and evil?
The phrase 'صلاحاً كان أو ضدَّهُ' is key.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ الجائز في أفعاله سبحانَهُ ] وأما الجائز في حقِّهِ تعالى : ففعل كل ممكن أو تركه ، صلاحاً كان أو ضدَّهُ ؛ لما عُرفَ قبلُ مِنْ وجوبِ عموم قدرته وإرادته تعالى لجميع الممكنات ، ويدخل في ذلكَ : جواز خلْقِ الله تعالى الرؤية لذاتِهِ العليَّة ، والسمع لكلامه القديم ، والثوابَ في دار النعيم ، والبعث لـرسـلـهِ الأكرمينَ ، صلواتُ اللهِ وسلامه عليهم أجمعينَ
[The possible with respect to His actions, glorified] As for what is possible with respect to Him, exalted: doing every possible thing or refraining from it, whether it be beneficial or its opposite; because it has been previously known that His power and will must extend to all possible things. And included in that is: the permissibility of God's creating sight of His noble essence, and hearing of His eternal speech, and reward in the abode of bliss, and the sending of His most honorable messengers, may God's prayers and peace be upon all of them.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage defines the category of ja'iz (possible) for God and provides examples. Ja'iz means that God may choose to do something or not do it, regardless of whether it is considered good (salah) or bad (its opposite) by human standards. The basis is that God's power and will are universal over all possible things. Then the author lists specific examples that are often debated: God's creating the vision of His essence (ru'yat Allah), hearing His eternal kalam, giving reward in paradise, and sending messengers.

Key Terms
جائز
ممكن
قدرة
إرادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'possible' means God could be unjust according to human standards.
Correction: The passage clarifies that possibility is based on God's absolute power and will, not on human concepts of good and evil. The phrase 'whether it be beneficial or its opposite' shows that human moral judgments do not restrict God's actions.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is NOT mentioned as an example of something possible for God in this passage?
A) Creating vision of His essence
B) Hearing His eternal speech
C) Punishment in hell
D) Sending messengers

Key Takeaway
The possible (ja'iz) in God's actions means He may do or leave any possible act, unconstrained by human judgments of good or evil, as demonstrated by examples like creating vision of Himself and sending messengers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a situation that seems unjust or harmful, do you truly believe that God's actions are not bound by your sense of good and evil? How does that truth change your response to difficulty?
?
If God's power and will are universal over all possible things, what does that mean for the events in your life that you wish had turned out differently? Will you submit to His decree even when it contradicts your own judgment?
?
This lesson says God may do or refrain from any possible act without being judged. Are you willing to surrender your own moral assessments of God's actions in your life, or do you secretly hold Him to a human standard of fairness?

The Meaning of al-Hamdu Lillah: All Perfections Belong to Allah
Sanusi argues that all perfections are from Allah, explaining the meaning of al-hamdu lillah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the theological meaning of 'al-hamdu lillah' as the acknowledgement that all perfections—whether eternal or originated—ultimately belong to Allah alone.

Core Idea
All praise ultimately belongs to Allah because every perfection depends on Him.

This lesson unpacks a dense theological argument from Imam Sanusi that explains the foundational Islamic phrase 'al-hamdu lillah'. By reflecting on the nature of perfections and their dependence on Allah, Sanusi shows why all praise is due to Him. The argument also reveals a profound rhetorical elegance in the opening of Surat al-Fatiha.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Let the argument unfold in its own terms before you analyze it.
Notice that the passage begins with a conclusion and then summarizes the reasoning.
2
Distinguish two types of perfections
Identify the two categories: eternal perfections (attributes of Allah) and originated perfections (acts of Allah).
The passage explicitly states that every eternal perfection is His attribute, and every originated perfection is His act.
3
Trace the logical chain
Follow the argument: contingent worlds depend on Allah, so they testify that eternal perfections are His attributes (since they depend on Him having those perfections) and that originated perfections are His acts (due to His uniqueness).
This shows that all perfections are from Allah.
4
Connect to 'al-hamdu lillah' and Fatiha
Understand that since all perfections are from Allah, all praise is for Him—this is the meaning of 'al-hamdu lillah'. Then appreciate why the phrase 'Lord of the Worlds' follows it perfectly.
Sanusi says this conjunction is in the utmost beauty and miraculousness.
5
Reflect on the common misunderstanding
Consider that some might think praise for created things is independent. This passage shows that ultimately all praise returns to Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمستحيلات .. لزم أنَّ كلَّ حادث يدلُّ على وجوب هذه الكمالات لمولانا جل وعلا وبالجملة : فالعوالم بعد أن تقرَّرَ وجوب حدوثها وافتقارها إلى مولانا جلَّ وعلا .. شهدَتْ بأنَّ كلَّ كمال قديم فهو وصفه تعالى ؛ لتوقف حدوثِها على اتِّصافِ مولانا جل وعلا بذلك الكمال ، وشهدَتْ بأنَّ كلَّ كمال حادثٍ فهو فعلُهُ تعالى ؛ لِمَا شهدَتْ بِهِ مِنْ وجوبِ الوحدانية لمولانا تبارك وتعالى ؛ فقد شهدَتْ إذا بأنَّ المدح بكل كمال قديم أو حادثٍ إِنَّما هو لمولانا جل وعلا ، وهو معنى ( الحمد لله ) . وهذا التقريرُ يُعرِّفُكَ : أَنَّ تعقيب جملة ( الحمدُ للهِ ) في سورةِ ( الفاتحة ) بالوصف بـ ( ربّ العالمينَ ) . . هو هو في غاية الحُسْنِ والإعجاز ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
And impossibilities.. it follows that every contingent thing indicates the necessity of these perfections for our Lord, Glory be to Him. In summary: the worlds, after it has been established that they are contingent and in need of our Lord, Glory be to Him, testified that every eternal perfection is His attribute, because their occurrence depends on our Lord, Glory be to Him, being characterized by that perfection; and they testified that every originated perfection is His act, because of what they testified of the necessity of uniqueness (wahdaaniyya) for our Lord, Blessed and Exalted. Thus they testified that praise for every perfection, whether eternal or originated, is only for our Lord, Glory be to Him; and that is the meaning of 'Praise be to Allah' (al-hamdu lillah). This exposition informs you that the conjunction of the phrase 'Praise be to Allah' in Surat al-Fatiha with the description 'Lord of the Worlds' is indeed in the utmost beauty and miraculousness. And with Allah, Exalted, is success.
Source pages p. 108

Commentary
This passage is a theological argument explaining the meaning of 'al-hamdu lillah'. The author begins by stating a conclusion: every contingent thing indicates the necessity of perfections for Allah. Then he summarizes: the contingent worlds, once their contingency and need for Allah are established, testify that every eternal perfection is Allah's attribute, because their own existence depends on Him possessing that perfection. They also testify that every originated perfection is Allah's act, due to the established necessity of Allah's unity—meaning no other creator shares in His perfections. Therefore, all praise for any perfection is due to Allah alone. This is the meaning of 'al-hamdu lillah'. The author then notes the rhetorical excellence of the Fatiha's order: 'Praise be to Allah' is immediately followed by 'Lord of the Worlds', which is perfectly suited.

Key Terms
كمال قديم – eternal perfection
كمال حادث – originated perfection
الحمد لله – al-hamdu lillah
رب العالمين – Lord of the Worlds

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that praise for created things (like a beautiful sunset or a kind act) is independent and belongs to the created object itself.
Correction: The passage shows that all perfections, whether eternal (attributes) or originated (acts), are ultimately from Allah. Therefore, all praise is due to Allah alone, and any praise of created things is indirectly praise of Allah as the source.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the meaning of 'al-hamdu lillah'?
A) Thanking Allah for blessings.
B) That praise for every perfection, eternal or originated, is due to Allah.
C) That Allah is praiseworthy.
D) That the universe testifies to Allah.

Key Takeaway
The meaning of 'al-hamdu lillah' is that all perfections, eternal or originated, are from Allah alone, so all praise is due to Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'al-hamdu lillah', do you truly believe that every good thing in your life—your health, intellect, and faith—comes entirely from Allah with no share for yourself? How would your gratitude change if you lived this conviction daily?
?
If every perfection, including your own good deeds, is an act of Allah, then where does pride belong? What would it cost you to see yourself as merely a vessel through which Allah's perfections appear?
?
The worlds testify that all praise belongs to Allah. When you look at creation—a sunset, a child's smile, a moment of clarity—do you hear that testimony, or do you stop at the beauty itself? What is one thing today that you can consciously praise as a sign of Allah's perfection?

Divine Attributes
beginner
6 min
The Two Types of Divine Nurture in 'Rabb al-'Alamin'
Classifies divine nurture into general and specific types from 'Rabb al-'Alamin'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of 'Rabb al-'Alamin' in relation to the two types of divine nurture: general and specific.

Core Idea
All nurture comes from Allah, divided into general creation and specific spiritual guidance.

This passage from a classical commentary on Surah al-Fatiha unpacks the profound meaning of 'Lord of the worlds.' The author shows that Allah's nurturing care covers both the physical existence of all creatures and the spiritual guidance that leads to eternal bliss—with the latter being channeled exclusively through the prophets.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the translation. Let the author's own rhythm and terms sink in.
Notice the two-part structure: first the definition, then the division.
2
Identify the definition of tarbiyyah
Find how the author defines tarbiyyah: bringing every contingent thing to its intended perfection. This is the foundation.
3
Distinguish general and specific tarbiyyah
The passage lists two types. Note their characteristics: general is about physical life and senses; specific is about spiritual knowledge and discipline.
The specific tarbiyyah is described as 'noble' and leads to Allah's pleasure.
4
Reflect on the role of prophets
The author stresses that specific tarbiyyah is only granted through prophets, and that Muhammad received the greatest share with ease.
Quranic verses are cited to support this.
5
Connect to 'Rabb al-'Alamin'
Understand how the phrase 'Lord of the worlds' encapsulates both types: general (for all worlds) and specific (through the best of prophets).
This enriches your recitation of the Fatiha.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الخامس : أنَّ قولَهُ : ) ربِّ العالمينَ ) أشعر بأنَّ التربية كلَّها ؛ وهي إيصال كلَّ حادث إلى كمالِهِ الذي أُريدَ لَهُ . . ليسَتْ إِلا مِنَ المولى تبارك وتعالى ، وهذه التربية على قسمين : عامةٍ ، وخاصة : فالعامَّةُ : التربية بالإيجادِ والتنمية ، والإمداد بالحياة والحواس وغيرها مما هو مشترك بين عموم الأجسادِ
والخاصَّةُ : التربية الروحانية بالعلوم والمعارف العلمية والعملية ، وضبط الحركات والسكنات للجري على مقتضاهما ، وهذه التربية هي العزيزة الشريفة ، الموصلة إلى الفوز برضا مولانا جل وعزَّ ، والتمتع بما لا يُحاط بوصفِهِ مِنْ نعيمِ الجِنَانِ أبد الآبادِ ، وقد جعل الله سبحانَهُ هَذِهِ التربية الخاصة لا تحصل لأحدٍ مِنْ أَهْلِ الأَرضِ إلا على أيدي الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، وجعلَ الحاصل منها على يد نبينا ومولانا محمد عليهِ الصلاة والسلام الحظ الأوفر والنصيب الأكثر ، مع سهولة فيها وقِلَّةِ معاناة ، كما قال تعالى : ﴿ يُرِيدُ اللهُ بِكُمُ الْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ الْعُسْرَ ﴾ [البقرة : ١٨٥] ، وقال في وصف نبينا ومولانا محمَّدٍ عليه الصلاةُ والسلام : ﴿ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَالْأَغْلَلَ الَّتِي كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ﴾ [الأعراف : ١٥٧] •
وقد عرفت كثرةَ مَنْ تربَّى على يدِهِ هذهِ التربية الخاصَّةَ ، وأَنَّهُم ثلثا أهل الجنَّةِ(۱) ، فأشرنا إلى تربية مولانا جل وعلا لخلقه التربية العامة بقولنا : ( ربّ العالمينَ ) ، وأشرنا إلى تربيته الخاصة بذكرِ أفضل مَنْ أجزلَ الحظَّ منها على يدِهِ ، مقروناً ذلك بتعظيمِهِ ، والصلاة والسلام عليه
The fifth: That His saying: 'Lord of the worlds' indicates that all nurture (tarbiyyah) — which is the conveying of every contingent thing to its perfection that was intended for it — is only from the Lord, Blessed and Exalted. And this nurture is of two types: general and specific. The general: nurture by bringing into existence and development, and supplying with life, senses, and other things which are shared among all bodies. And the specific: spiritual nurture by sciences and intellectual and practical knowledges, and controlling movements and stillnesses to act in accordance with them. And this nurture is the noble, honorable one, leading to the attainment of the pleasure of our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, and the enjoyment of what cannot be encompassed by description of the bliss of Paradise forever. And Allah, glorified be He, has made this specific nurture not attainable by any of the people of the earth except through the hands of the messengers, peace and blessings be upon them. And He made the portion obtained from it through the hand of our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, the greatest share and the largest portion, with ease in it and little difficulty, as He said: 'Allah intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship' [Al-Baqarah: 185], and He said in describing our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him: 'And He removes from them their burden and the shackles which were upon them' [Al-A'raf: 157]. And you have known the multitude of those who were nurtured by his hand with this specific nurture, and that they are two-thirds of the people of Paradise. So we indicated the general nurture of our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, for His creation by our saying: 'Lord of the worlds', and we indicated His specific nurture by mentioning the best of those who received the greatest portion from it through his hand, accompanied by magnifying him and sending blessings and peace upon him.
Source pages p. 116

Commentary
This passage is a classification and explanation of the divine attribute of Rububiyyah as expressed in the phrase 'Rabb al-'Alamin' (Lord of the worlds). The author (a commentator on the Fatiha) is explaining the fifth point in his commentary. He states that the term 'Rabb' indicates that all tarbiyyah (nurture, development, lordship) comes exclusively from Allah. He then defines tarbiyyah as bringing every contingent thing to its intended perfection. The passage divides tarbiyyah into two types: general and specific.

1. General tarbiyyah (al-tarbiyyah al-'ammah): This is common to all created beings—providing existence, growth, life, senses, and other shared physical necessities.
2. Specific tarbiyyah (al-tarbiyyah al-khassah): This is spiritual nurture bestowed through knowledge (both theoretical and practical) and through disciplining one's movements and stillness to align with divine guidance. This type is described as noble and honorable because it leads to Allah's pleasure and eternal bliss in Paradise.

The author then emphasizes that this specific tarbiyyah is only granted through the prophets. Among all prophets, the Prophet Muhammad received the greatest share, and his message is characterized by ease and the removal of burdens, as supported by Quranic verses. The author also mentions that those who received this specific nurture through the Prophet comprise two-thirds of the people of Paradise. Finally, he connects the opening phrase 'Rabb al-'Alamin' to the general tarbiyyah, and the mention of the Prophet in the Fatiha (or in the context) to the specific tarbiyyah.

Key Terms
تربية عامة (tarbiyyah 'ammah)
تربية خاصة (tarbiyyah khassah)
رب العالمين (Rabb al-'Alamin)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that spiritual guidance and inner purification can be achieved through personal effort, saints, or other means without prophetic instruction.
Correction: The passage clearly states that specific spiritual tarbiyyah is not attainable by anyone except through the hands of the messengers (prophets). Prophets are the exclusive channels for this divine nurture.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, through whom does Allah exclusively grant specific spiritual tarbiyyah?
A) The angels
B) The prophets
C) The saints
D) All believers

Key Takeaway
The phrase 'Rabb al-'Alamin' points to both the general care of creation and the special prophetic guidance that leads to eternal success.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you been relying solely on Allah's general care (health, provision) while neglecting the specific nurture He sent through the Prophet? What one change can you make today to actively seek prophetic guidance in your daily life?
?
The passage emphasizes that the Prophet's message is characterized by ease. Do you feel burdened by your religious duties? Which burden might come from cultural expectations rather than the revelation itself, and how can you return to the Prophet's original lightness?
?
If specific tarbiyyah is the only path to eternal bliss, how much time do you actually spend learning and implementing the Qur'an and Sunnah compared to worldly pursuits? Are you content with a general belief, or are you hungering for the prophetic nurture that leads to Paradise?

Divine Attributes

5 min
Definition of Tarbiyah and Its Two Types
Sanusi defines tarbiyah and divides it into general and specific.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the concept of tarbiyah as defined by Sanusi and its division into general and specific types.

Core Idea
Tarbiyah is the divine nurturing that brings everything to its intended perfection.

Sanusi begins by defining tarbiyah as the conveying of every contingent being to its intended perfection. He then divides this divine nurturing into two categories: general and specific. This passage lays the foundation for understanding how Allah's nurturing relates to all creation and specifically to spiritual development.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the definition and the two divisions.
The definition is very concise: 'الإيصال إلى كماله الذي أريد له'.
2
Identify the two types
Find where Sanusi states the two types: general (عامة) and specific (خاصة). Note the characteristics of each.
General tarbiyah includes creation, sustenance, senses. Specific tarbiyah is spiritual.
3
Reflect on the distinction
Consider why this distinction matters. General tarbiyah is common to all bodies, while specific is noble and leads to Paradise.
This sets up the role of prophets in the next lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وهي إيصال كلَّ حادث إلى كمالِهِ الذي أُريدَ لَهُ . . ليسَتْ إِلا مِنَ المولى تبارك وتعالى ، وهذه التربية على قسمين : عامةٍ ، وخاصة : فالعامَّةُ : التربية بالإيجادِ والتنمية ، والإمداد بالحياة والحواس وغيرها مما هو مشترك بين عموم الأجسادِ والخاصَّةُ : التربية الروحانية بالعلوم والمعارف العلمية والعملية ، وضبط الحركات والسكنات للجري على مقتضاهما ، وهذه التربية هي العزيزة الشريفة ، الموصلة إلى الفوز برضا مولانا جل وعزَّ ، والتمتع بما لا يُحاط بوصفِهِ مِنْ نعيمِ الجِنَانِ أبد الآبادِ ، وقد جعل الله سبحانَهُ هَذِهِ التربية الخاصة لا تحصل لأحدٍ مِنْ أَهْلِ الأَرضِ إلا على أيدي الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، وجعلَ الحاصل منها على يد نبينا ومولانا محمد عليهِ الصلاة والسلام الحظ الأوفر والنصيب الأكثر ، مع سهولة فيها وقِلَّةِ معاناة ، كما قال تعالى : ﴿ يُرِيدُ اللهُ بِكُمُ الْيُسْرَ وَلَا يُرِيدُ بِكُمُ الْعُسْرَ [البقرة : ١٨٥] ، وقال في وصف نبينا ومولانا محمَّدٍ عليه الصلاةُ والسلام : وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَالْأَغْلَلَ الَّتِي كَانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ ﴾ [الأعراف : ١٥٧] وقد عرفت كثرةَ مَنْ تربَّى على يدِهِ هذهِ التربية الخاصَّةَ ، وأَنَّهُم ثلثا أهل الجنَّةِ ، فأشرنا إلى تربية مولانا جل وعلا لخلقه التربية العامة بقولنا : ( ربّ العالمينَ ) ، وأشرنا إلى تربيته الخاصة بذكرِ أفضل مَنْ أجزلَ الحظَّ منها على يدِهِ ، مقروناً ذلك بتعظيمِهِ ، والصلاة والسلام عليه
It is the conveying of every contingent being to its perfection that was intended for it... it is only from the Lord, Blessed and Exalted. This nurturing (tarbiyah) is of two types: general and specific. As for the general: nurturing by creation, development, and provision of life, senses, and other things that are common to all bodies. As for the specific: spiritual nurturing by sciences and knowledge, both theoretical and practical, and the regulation of movements and stillnesses to proceed according to them. And this nurturing is the noble and honorable one, which leads to attaining the pleasure of our Master, Glorious and Mighty, and enjoyment of the bliss of Paradise, which cannot be encompassed by description, forever and ever. And Allah, Glory be to Him, has made this specific nurturing not attainable by anyone from the people of the earth except through the hands of the Messengers, peace be upon them. And He made the portion obtained through the hands of our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, the greatest share and the most abundant portion, along with ease in it and less hardship, as He said, Exalted: 'Allah intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship' [al-Baqarah 2:185], and He said in describing our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him: 'And he relieves them of their burden and the shackles that were upon them' [al-A'raf 7:157]. And you have known the multitude of those who were nurtured by his hand with this specific nurturing, and that they are two-thirds of the people of Paradise. So we pointed to the general nurturing of our Master, Glorious and Exalted, of His creation by our saying: 'Lord of the worlds' (Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn), and we pointed to His specific nurturing by mentioning the best of the one through whom the abundant portion was obtained, accompanied by his veneration, and prayers and peace be upon him.
Source pages p. 117

Commentary
This passage defines tarbiyah (nurturing/education) as bringing every contingent thing to its intended perfection. It then divides tarbiyah into two types: general and specific. General tarbiyah is common to all bodies and includes creation, sustenance, and senses. Specific tarbiyah is spiritual and involves knowledge and actions, and leads to Paradise. The passage emphasizes that this specific tarbiyah is only obtained through the prophets, and the Prophet Muhammad has the greatest share. It also connects this to the basmala phrase 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' (Lord of the worlds), explaining that we point to general tarbiyah by saying 'Lord of the worlds' and to specific tarbiyah by mentioning the Prophet.

Key Terms
تربية
عامة
خاصة
إيصال إلى كمال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what does general tarbiyah include?
A) Spiritual sciences and knowledge
B) Creation, development, life, and senses
C) Regulation of movements
D) Attaining the pleasure of Allah

Key Takeaway
Tarbiyah from Allah encompasses both common physical nurture and special spiritual guidance leading to Paradise.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that specific tarbiyah (spiritual nurture) leads to Allah's pleasure and eternal paradise. In the last week, what concrete step have you taken to seek this spiritual tarbiyah? If none, what is the cost of ignoring it?
?
This passage says that even your life, senses, and physical development are from Allah's general tarbiyah. Do you live with the awareness that every breath and every ability is a direct gift from Him? How would your day change if you truly remembered this?
?
The specific tarbiyah requires regulating your movements and stillness according to knowledge. Is there an area of your daily routine—words, actions, even resting—where you are not submitting to what Allah has taught? What will you change starting today?

Divine Attributes

The Role of Prophets in Specific Tarbiyah
Sanusi explains that specific tarbiyah is only through prophets and highlights Muhammad's excellence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that specific spiritual tarbiyah is only attained through prophets, and that Prophet Muhammad received the greatest portion with ease.

Core Idea
Prophets are the exclusive channels for spiritual tarbiyah, and Muhammad's mission is the most merciful.

After defining the two types of tarbiyah, Sanusi emphasizes that specific tarbiyah—the spiritual nurture leading to Paradise—is only granted through the prophets. He highlights the special position of Prophet Muhammad, whose followers received the greatest share with ease, fulfilling the Quranic promise of ease.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on prophets
Read the second part of the passage. Notice the claim that specific tarbiyah is only through the hands of the messengers.
This is a key doctrine: prophets are necessary for spiritual guidance.
2
Examine the Quranic verses
Sanusi cites two verses: one about ease (al-Baqarah 2:185) and one about removal of burdens (al-A'raf 7:157). Reflect on how they support the ease of Muhammad's message.
These verses show that Muhammad's tarbiyah is easier than previous prophets.
3
Connect to the Basmala
Sanusi ends by connecting general tarbiyah to 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' and specific tarbiyah to the mention of the Prophet. Understand this link.
This shows how the basmala encompasses both types of tarbiyah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
