It is the conveying of every contingent being to its perfection that was intended for it... it is only from the Lord, Blessed and Exalted. This nurturing (tarbiyah) is of two types: general and specific. As for the general: nurturing by creation, development, and provision of life, senses, and other things that are common to all bodies. As for the specific: spiritual nurturing by sciences and knowledge, both theoretical and practical, and the regulation of movements and stillnesses to proceed according to them. And this nurturing is the noble and honorable one, which leads to attaining the pleasure of our Master, Glorious and Mighty, and enjoyment of the bliss of Paradise, which cannot be encompassed by description, forever and ever. And Allah, Glory be to Him, has made this specific nurturing not attainable by anyone from the people of the earth except through the hands of the Messengers, peace be upon them. And He made the portion obtained through the hands of our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, the greatest share and the most abundant portion, along with ease in it and less hardship, as He said, Exalted: 'Allah intends for you ease and does not intend for you hardship' [al-Baqarah 2:185], and He said in describing our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him: 'And he relieves them of their burden and the shackles that were upon them' [al-A'raf 7:157]. And you have known the multitude of those who were nurtured by his hand with this specific nurturing, and that they are two-thirds of the people of Paradise. So we pointed to the general nurturing of our Master, Glorious and Exalted, of His creation by our saying: 'Lord of the worlds' (Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn), and we pointed to His specific nurturing by mentioning the best of the one through whom the abundant portion was obtained, accompanied by his veneration, and prayers and peace be upon him.

Commentary
This passage defines tarbiyah (nurturing/education) as bringing every contingent thing to its intended perfection. It then divides tarbiyah into two types: general and specific. General tarbiyah is common to all bodies and includes creation, sustenance, and senses. Specific tarbiyah is spiritual and involves knowledge and actions, and leads to Paradise. The passage emphasizes that this specific tarbiyah is only obtained through the prophets, and the Prophet Muhammad has the greatest share. It also connects this to the basmala phrase 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' (Lord of the worlds), explaining that we point to general tarbiyah by saying 'Lord of the worlds' and to specific tarbiyah by mentioning the Prophet.

According to Sanusi, how is specific tarbiyah obtained?

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This specific tarbiyah is only through prophets. Are you actively learning the Prophet's way to nurture your soul, or have you been neglecting his sunnah? Will you commit today to take one concrete action from his guidance?
?
The Prophet came with ease, removing burdens. What burden are you still carrying—guilt, harshness, or self-made rituals—that he already lifted? Will you lay it down now and trust his teaching?
?
Two-thirds of Paradise are his followers. Do you truly count yourself among them by following him in your daily choices, or are you merely claiming it while living carelessly? What change will you make to ensure you are part of that multitude?

Definition of al-Wajib and Its Application
Sanusi defines al-wajib and explains its scope across attribute types.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of al-wajib (the Necessary Being) as that whose non-existence is inconceivable, and how it applies to both positive and negative divine attributes.

Core Idea
The Necessary Being is defined by the mind's inability to conceive its absence.

Sanusi begins by defining the foundational category of al-wajib, the Necessary Being. This definition is the starting point for all subsequent discussions about God's existence and attributes. The passage clarifies that 'non-existence' here means inconceivability in the mind, not merely external impossibility.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the original Arabic and the literal translation to get the author's own wording. Note that the passage begins with a quotation from the matn.
The phrase 'ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ' is the key definition.
2
Understand the meaning of 'inconceivability'
The author clarifies that 'non-existence is not conceived' means 'negation is not perceived by the mind'. This is a mental impossibility, not just a physical one.
This is a subtle but crucial point.
3
Apply the definition to positive and negative attributes
The passage says the definition applies whether the reality is affirmative (like God's essence) or negative (like His eternity).
This shows that both types of attributes are necessary.
4
Reflect on why this definition matters
This definition sets the standard for what we can rationally affirm about God. It establishes a criterion of necessity.
This connects to the broader kalam project.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قوله : ( ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ ) يعني : لا يُدرَكُ في العقل نفيه ، سواء كانَتْ حقيقة ذلك الواجب وجودية ؛ كذاتِ مولانا تبارك وتعالى ، أو سلبيَّةً ؛ كقِدَمِهِ جَلَّ وعلا
His saying: 'That whose non-existence is not conceived in the mind' means: its negation is not perceived in the mind, whether the reality of that necessary being is affirmative, like the essence of our Lord, blessed and exalted, or negative, like His eternity, may He be glorified and exalted.
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This passage explains the definition of al-wajib (the Necessary). The definition given is 'that whose non-existence is not conceived in the mind'. The author clarifies that this means its negation (i.e., its absence) is not perceived by the mind. He then notes that this applies to both positive (وجودية) realities, such as God's essence, and negative (سلبية) attributes, such as His eternity (which is the negation of temporal origin). So the definition covers all attributes of God that are necessary.

Key Terms
الواجب
العدم
الوجودية
السلبي
النفي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that 'non-existence' in the definition refers to something simply being impossible in external reality.
Correction: It actually means that the mind cannot conceive its absence; the impossibility is in the mind's perception, not just in external existence.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what does 'ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ' mean?
A) Its non-existence is impossible in reality
B) Its negation is not perceived by the mind
C) It is impossible to imagine
D) It exists necessarily

Key Takeaway
Al-wajib is defined as that whose non-existence is inconceivable, applying to all necessary divine attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
The definition of al-wajib encompasses both positive and negative attributes. Are you as certain of God's attributes (like His eternity) as you are of His existence? If not, what specific action will you take to strengthen your certainty in a particular attribute?

The Attribute of Being Different from Contingent Things
Sanusi defines Allah's attribute of being different from all contingent things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Allah must be completely different from all contingent things and how this attribute is derived from His pre-eternity.

Core Idea
Allah's difference from creation is not arbitrary but logically necessary.

Building on the previously established proof of Allah's pre-eternity and eternality, Sanusi now draws a necessary conclusion: Allah's essence and attributes cannot be like those of contingent things. This lesson explores the attribute of 'being different' (مخالفة) and its rational foundation. You will see how divine transcendence is not just a pious statement but a logical requirement.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its footnote
Begin by reading the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Note that the main text states the doctrine concisely, while the footnote provides additional commentary on the terminology and Quranic evidence.
The footnote by al-Baylī explains the permissibility of using the term 'different' for Allah.
2
Identify the logical proof
The passage argues that since Allah is pre-eternal and eternal (proven earlier), He cannot be of the same genus as contingent things. Identify this causal link and understand why it is decisive.
3
Reflect on the Quranic verse
The footnote discusses the verse 'Laysa kamithlihi shay’' (Q. 42:11). Consider how the negation of any likeness to Allah affirms His absolute difference while also affirming His attributes.
The verse refutes both anthropomorphism and denial of attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفة المخالفة للحوادث له سبحانه
ويجب أن يكون تعالى مخالفاً في ذاتِهِ وصفاته لكلّ ما سواهُ مِنَ الحوادثِ (۱) ، وإلا كان حادثاً مثلها.
يعني : أَنَّهُ لمَّا تَقرَّرَ بالبرهان القطعي فيما سبق وجوبُ القِدَمِ والبقاء له تعالى .. لزم أن تكونَ ذاتُهُ العليّه وصفاته المرقعة ليستا من جنس الحوادث.
(۱) قال العلامة البيلي في « تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى » ( ق ۹ ) : ( قوله : « مخالفاً » بكسر اللام ، وهذا الوصف أطلقه المصنف عليه تعالى ؛ لأنه وإن لم يرد في أسمائه المذكورة في الحديث لكن يصح اتصافه بمعناه ؛ وهو عدم المماثلة ، أو أنه على قول الغزالي ومن وافقه أن الوصف إذا لم يوهم نقصاً في حقه تعالى يجوز إطلاقه عليه وإن لم يرد إذن و معنى كونه تعالى مخالفاً لما ذكر : أنه لا يماثله شيء ؛ لقوله تعالى : ﴿ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ ﴾ [الشوري : ١١] ؛ أي : ليس شيء من الأشياء مماثلاً لذاته وصفاته وأفعاله ، فصدر الآية نفي ، وعجزها إثبات ، فهي من باب تقديم التخلية على التحلية ، ففيها ردُّ بحسب صدرها على الجهوية والمجسمة ، وردُّ بحسب عجزها على المعطلة النافين الصفات الذات فظهر لك من التقرير : أن الكاف في الآية صلة ، وليست بمعنى ( مثل » ؛ لاقتضائه أن المنفي مثل المثل ، والمقصود نفي المثل ، إلا أن يقال : المقصود من نفي مثل المثل نفي المثل ، أو أن ( مثل ( الداخلة عليه الكاف بمعنى ذات ، ويكون المعنى ليس مثل ذاته شيء )
The Attribute of Being Different from Contingent Things for Him, Exalted is He.
And it is necessary that the Exalted be different in His essence and attributes from all that is other than Him of contingent things (1), otherwise He would be contingent like them.
Meaning: Since it has been established by decisive proof in the preceding that pre-eternity and eternality are necessary for the Exalted, it follows that His exalted essence and elevated attributes are not of the genus of contingent things.
(1) Al-ʿAllāma al-Baylī said in his 'Taqyīdāt ʿalā Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā' (folio 9): 'His saying: "different" with kasra on the lām, and this attribute is applied by the author to the Exalted; because even if it does not occur among His names mentioned in the hadith, it is still correct to describe Him with its meaning, which is non-resemblance. Or, according to al-Ghazālī and those who agree with him, that a description, if it does not imply deficiency in His right, may be applied to Him even if not transmitted. And the meaning of His being different from what has been mentioned is that nothing resembles Him, due to His statement, the Exalted: "There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing" (Q. 42:11); meaning: nothing of things is similar to His essence, attributes, and actions. So the first part of the verse is a negation, and its last part is an affirmation. It is of the type of presenting purification before adornment. Thus, by its first part, it refutes the anthropomorphists and corporealists, and by its last part, it refutes the negators who deny the essential attributes. So it is clear to you from this exposition that the kāf in the verse is a particle of comparison, not meaning 'like'; because that would imply negating the like of a likeness, while the intended negation is of a likeness. Unless it is said that negating the like of a likeness is the negation of a likeness, or that 'like' (mithl) entered by the kāf means 'essence', so the meaning is 'there is nothing like His essence'.'
Source pages p. 151

Commentary
This passage defines the attribute of مخالفة (being different) for Allah. It begins by stating that Allah must be different in essence and attributes from all contingent things, otherwise He would be contingent Himself. The proof relies on a prior demonstration of Allah's pre-eternity (قدم) and eternality (بقاء); being pre-eternal and eternal necessitates being unlike originated things. The footnote by al-Baylī discusses the permissibility of using the term 'different' (مخالف) as an attribute, even though it is not among the divine names in the hadith. It explains that the meaning is valid because it indicates non-resemblance, and that such descriptions are permissible if they do not imply deficiency. The footnote then interprets the Quranic verse 'Laysa kamithlihi shay'...' (Q. 42:11) to show that the kāf is an extra particle of comparison, not meaning 'like'. The verse negates any similarity to Allah and affirms His attributes, refuting both anthropomorphism (تشبيه) and denial of attributes (تعطيل). The overall argument establishes that Allah's transcendence (تنزيه) requires His complete difference from creation.

Key Terms
مخالفة
قديم
حادث
تنزيه
تماثل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the term 'different' (مخالف) is a negative attribute implying deficiency.
Correction: Rather, it is a positive affirmation of incomparability derived from Allah's essential necessity; the attribute is permissible because its meaning does not imply any deficiency.

Checkpoint
What is the decisive proof cited for Allah being different from contingent things?
A) The Quranic verse 'Laysa kamithlihi shay'...'
B) The necessity of pre-eternity and eternality for Allah.
C) The consensus of the scholars.
D) The impossibility of two opposites coexisting.

Key Takeaway
Allah's attribute of being different from contingent things is a necessary consequence of His pre-eternity and is consistently affirmed in the Quran.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you imagine Allah during prayer, do you unconsciously picture a being with human-like qualities? How does knowing that Allah is utterly different from everything you know change how you should approach Him?
?
The attribute of difference is not just a theological concept but a truth that should shape your awe. In what area of your life have you been acting as if Allah is 'like' something else, expecting Him to behave like a human would? What will you do to correct that this week?
?
You affirm that nothing is like Allah, but does your heart feel that? When you face trials, do you trust that His response is unlike any human response? If not, what step can you take to deepen your certainty in His incomparability?

The Two Components of Self-Subsistence
Sanusi defines al-qiyam bi al-nafs and explains its two components.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two components of self-subsistence (al-qiyam bi al-nafs) and why they refute false premises about God.

Core Idea
Self-subsistence means God depends on nothing—neither a place nor a creator.

This lesson unpacks one of the most important terms in kalam: al-qiyam bi al-nafs. Sanusi defines it concisely, then explains its two parts. Understanding this definition is key to grasping how Muslim theologians affirm God's absolute independence.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Notice that Sanusi gives a concise meaning in brackets before expanding.
The bracket may be a heading in the original.
2
Identify the two components
The passage states that the meaning of self-subsistence includes two parts. Find where 'أحدهما' and 'الثاني' appear and note what each part says.
3
Understand the refutation
Sanusi says that God's self-subsistence repels two false premises. Think about how independence from a substrate and independence from an agent address specific errors.
This connects to earlier discussion about God not being an accident and not being contingent.
4
Connect to necessary existence
The second component links to the concept of wājib al-wujūd (necessary existence). Understand why self-subsistence implies necessity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ معنى القيام بالنفس : الاستغناء عن المحل وعن المخصص ]
فإذا عرفت هذا عرفت أن وجوب قيامه تعالى بنفسه يدفع هاتين المقدمتين الباطلتين؛ لأن معناه احتوى على جزأين: أحدهما: كونه تعالى غنياً عن المحل؛ أي: عن ذات يقوم بها ويكون صفة لها، فهو جل وعلا ذات موصوف بالصفات العليا، لا صفة لغيره. الثاني: كونه تعالى غنياً عن الفاعل، واجب الوجود لا جائزه.
[The meaning of self-subsistence (al-qiyam bi al-nafs): independence from a substrate (al-mahall) and from the agent (al-mukhaṣṣiṣ).]
So if you understand this, you will know that the necessity of His, exalted, being self-subsistent repels these two false premises, because its meaning includes two parts: one: that He, exalted, is independent of a substrate—i.e., of an essence in which He would subsist and be an attribute of it—for He, mighty and majestic, is an Essence qualified with sublime attributes, not an attribute for another. The second: that He, exalted, is independent of an agent, necessarily existent (wājib al-wujūd), not contingent.
Source pages p. 156

Commentary
This passage defines the key kalam term "al-qiyam bi al-nafs" (self-subsistence). The author first gives a concise meaning: independence from a substrate (mahall) and from an agent (mukhaṣṣiṣ). Then he explains that the necessity of God's self-subsistence refutes the two false premises mentioned earlier. To show this, he unpacks the definition into two components: (1) God is independent of any substrate, meaning He is not an accident or attribute subsisting in another; He is a self-subsisting Essence qualified with attributes. (2) God is independent of any agent, meaning He is necessarily existent, not contingent. This second part is crucial because contingent beings depend on an agent for their existence.

Key Terms
القيام بالنفس
الاستغناء عن المحل
الاستغناء عن المخصص
واجب الوجود
جائز الوجود

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think self-subsistence simply means independent existence, without specifying from what.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that it specifically means independence from both a substrate and an agent.

Checkpoint
What are the two components of 'al-qiyam bi al-nafs' according to Sanusi?
A) Independence from substrate and independence from agent
B) Eternity and omnipotence
C) Oneness and uniqueness
D) Self-sufficiency and perfection

Key Takeaway
Self-subsistence means God is independent of any substrate and any agent, affirming His necessary existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

Will you honestly identify that area today and make a concrete plan to shift your trust to God alone?

Commit to one action that reflects God's absolute independence in your prayer or gratitude today.

Examine a recent difficulty: did you forget that He is the ultimate agent? How will you remind yourself of this truth in your next moment of anxiety?

6 min
The Meaning of al-Mahall and the Proof that God is Not a Quality
Definition of 'al-mahall' and proof that God is a self, not a quality.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of 'al-mahall' and the logical proof that God is a self (dhāt) and cannot be a quality (ṣifa).

Core Idea
If God were a quality, an infinite regress of attributes would follow — impossible.

This lesson unpacks a key argument from Islamic theology: the distinction between God as a self (dhāt) and a quality (ṣifa). The author defines the term 'al-mahall' (locus) and then presents a proof that God cannot be a quality, because if He were, He would need to be described with qualities, leading to an infinite chain. This argument protects the absolute perfection and independence of the Divine.

Guided Path
1
Understand the term 'al-mahall'
Read the opening definition of 'al-mahall' as it relates to self-subsistence. Notice that the author emphasizes God's independence from any locus.
This sets the stage for the ontological distinction.
2
Follow the direct proof
Identify the conditional argument: if God were a quality, He could not be described with existential attributes; but He is; therefore He is not a quality.
The proof relies on the impossibility of a quality possessing qualities.
3
Grasp the infinite regress argument
Examine why assuming a quality could be described with attributes leads to an endless chain, which is rationally impossible.
This strengthens the conclusion that God is a self.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المحل ) هو تفسير للجزء الأولِ مِنَ القيام بالنفس ، وهو الذي منع كونه صفةٌ ، وزيادتنا الوصف بـ ( غنيّاً عن المحل ) بعد قولنا : ( موصوفاً بالصفاتِ ( للتأكيد ، وإلا فكلُّ ذاتِ موصوفة بالصفات هي غنية عن المحل ؛ أي : عن ذاتِ تقوم بها ، وقولنا : ( والفاعل ) هو تفسير للجزء الثاني مِنَ القيامِ بالنفس ، وهو الذي منع توهم كونِ ذاتِهِ تعالى تشبه شيئاً مِنَ الذواتِ و الصفات . أمَّا برهانُ الجزء الأول - وهو أنَّهُ تعالى ذات لا صفة - : فما أشرنا إليه في أصل العقيدة ؛ وهو أنَّه جلَّ وعلا لو كان صفةً لزمَ ألا يتصفَ بالصفاتِ الوجودية ؛ وهي صفات المعاني التي هي القدرة والإرادة والعلم والحياة ... إلى آخرِها ، ولا بلوازمها التي هي المعنوية ؛ وهي كونه تعالى قادراً ومريداً وعالماً وحياً... إلى آخرِها ، والدليلُ القطعي دلَّ على وجوبِ اتصافه تعالى بها ، فليس بصفةٍ ؛ لأنَّ الصفة لو قبلَتْ أنْ تتَّصف بالصفاتِ الوجودية . . لاستحال عُرُو كلِّ صفةٍ عنها كما في الذواتِ (١) ؛ لأنَّ القَبول نفسي ، فلا يتخلَّفُ ؛ وذلك يستلزم التسلسل ، ودخول ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ؛ لأنَّه يجب لصفة الصفة ما وجبَ للصفة الأولى مِنَ الاتصاف بالصفاتِ الوجودية ، ثمَّ هكذا إلى ما لا نهاية له ، وذلكَ لا يُعقَلُ)
(Al-mahall) is an explanation of the first part of self-subsistence, and it is that which prevents it from being a quality. And our addition of the description 'independent of a locus' after our saying 'described with attributes' is for emphasis; otherwise, every self described with attributes is independent of a locus, i.e., independent of a self in which it inheres. And our saying '(al-fāʿil)' is an explanation of the second part of self-subsistence, and it is that which prevents the misconception that His essence, exalted, resembles any of the selves and attributes. As for the proof of the first part — that He, exalted, is a self, not a quality — it is what we indicated in the original creed: that He, may He be glorified and exalted, if He were a quality, it would necessarily follow that He would not be described with existential attributes, which are the attributes of meanings, namely power, will, knowledge, life, etc., nor with their concomitants, which are the moral attributes, namely His being powerful, willing, knowing, living, etc. And the definitive proof indicates the necessity of His being described with them, so He is not a quality. Because if a quality were to accept being described with the existential attributes, it would be impossible for every quality to be devoid of them, as is the case with selves; because acceptance is essential, so it cannot be lacking. That entails an infinite regress and the entry of what is infinite into existence; because what is necessary for the attribute's attribute is what was necessary for the first attribute of being described with the existential attributes, and so on ad infinitum, and that is inconceivable.
Source pages p. 158

Commentary
This passage is a definition and a proof. The author begins by explaining the term 'al-mahall' (the locus) as part of the discussion of self-subsistence (al-qiyām bi al-nafs). The first part of self-subsistence negates that the divine essence is a quality, so the author clarifies that when they say 'described with attributes, independent of a locus', the addition of 'independent of a locus' is for emphasis. Then they define the second part of self-subsistence regarding 'al-fāʿil' (the agent). The main content is the proof that God is a self (dhāt) and not a quality (ṣifa). The proof is: God must be described with existential attributes (e.g., power, will, knowledge) as proven by definitive evidence. If He were a quality, He could not be described with such attributes. But He is described with them, so He is not a quality. The author then provides an additional argument: if a quality could be described with existential attributes, then every quality would necessarily accept such attributes (since acceptance is essential), leading to infinite regress (tasalsul) of attributes upon attributes, which is impossible. Therefore, the premise is false, and God is a self.

Key Terms
ذات
صفة
محل
اتصاف
صفات المعاني
صفات المعنوية
تسلسل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God can be called a 'quality' in a metaphorical sense without problem.
Correction: This proof shows that a quality cannot possess any attributes of its own, so even metaphorically calling God a quality would entail absurdity because God does possess attributes.

Checkpoint
What is the main reason presented in the proof that God cannot be a quality?
A) Because a quality cannot be described with existential attributes without leading to infinite regress
B) Because qualities are created and God is uncreated
C) Because a quality is dependent on a locus and God is independent
D) Because qualities are multiple and God is one

Key Takeaway
God is a self, not a quality; otherwise an infinite regress of attributes would be required.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God were dependent on a locus (mahall), He would not be truly independent. In what areas of your life are you still acting as if something other than God is your ultimate support? Are you willing to transfer that trust fully to the One who is ghaniyy 'an al-mahall?
?
The proof shows that infinite regress is irrational. Reflect on a worry or desire that seems to have no end—a chain of 'if only...' thoughts. Does knowing that God is the final, uncaused Self give you a foundation to break that chain today?
?
You affirm that God has attributes like power and knowledge. But do you ever imagine these as separate 'things' added to God? How would your worship change if you truly internalized that His attributes are not qualities added to a subject, but descriptions of His very Self—unlike anything you know?

Divine Attributes
advanced
5 min
Specification: Why the Proof Applies Only to Existential Attributes
Sanusi specifies which attributes the proof covers and why.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the proof against infinite regress applies only to existential attributes and not to essential or negative attributes.

Core Idea
Only existential attributes entail infinite regress; essential and negative attributes do not.

Sanusi has established a proof against infinite regress in attributes. Now he specifies that this proof is not universal: it applies only to existential attributes and their concomitants. He explains that essential attributes (nafsiyya) revert to the essence and do not multiply, while negative attributes (salbiyya) have no external existence. Therefore, assuming infinite regress in them does not lead to the absurdity of infinite existent entities. This distinction is essential for correctly applying the argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage until 'described with existence and eternity'. Then read the translation to grasp the distinction between attribute types.
Notice that the passage first states a general rule and then applies it to three categories.
2
Identify the three attribute types
The passage names existential attributes (ṣifāt wujūdiyya), essential attributes (ṣifāt nafsiyya), and negative attributes (ṣifāt salbiyya). Note how each is defined and why regress is possible only in the first.
Essential attributes are those that are identical to the essence (like existence for God).
3
Understand the key rationale
The proof concerns infinite regress leading to entry of infinite entities into existence. Only existential attributes have external existence; essential and negative attributes do not cause multiplication of existents. Therefore, regress in them is harmless.
This is why Sanusi says 'there is no infinite regress in them' for essential attributes.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
The passage concludes that both the self and the attributes of meanings can be described as existent and eternal, because these are shared descriptors not subject to the proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن هنا تَعرِفُ استحالة قيام الصفةِ بالصفةِ ، وأَنَّ قبول الاتصافِ بالصفاتِ الوجودية ولوازمها مِنْ خواص الذوات . . لا مشاركة بينها وبين الصفاتِ في ذلكَ تقوم بموصوفها وإِنَّما خَصَصْنا البرهانَ بالصفاتِ الوجودية ولوازمها لأنها هي التي ويلزمُ فيها دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ، أمَّا الصفاتُ النفسيةُ : فهي راجعة إلى حقيقة موصوفها ، ولا تسلسل فيها ، وأما الصفات السلبية : فلا وجود لمعانيها في الخارج (١) ، فلا يلزمُ مِنْ تقدير تسلسلها دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ، ولهذا كانَ الاتصافُ بهذين النوعين مشتركاً بين الذوات والصفاتِ ، ولهذا تُوصَفُ الذاتُ العليةُ وصفاتُ المعاني القائمة بها بالوجودِ والقِدَمِ ( وتوضيح البرهان : لو لم يكن تعالى عالماً بنفسه ؛ أي : ذاتاً موصوفة بالصفات .. لكان صفة قائمة بمحل ، ولو كان كذلك لما اتصف بصفات المعاني والمعنوية ، وعدم اتصافه بهما باطل ؛ لأنه يجب اتصافه بهما ، وإذا بطل اللازم بطل الملزوم ؛ وهو كونه صفة قائمة بمحل ، وإذا بطل ذلك ثبت المطلوب ؛ وهو كونه ذاتاً موصوفة بالصفات المذكورة . وقوله : ( إذ لو قبلت الصفة . . . » إلى آخره ؛ أي: ولو كانت الصفة قابلة إلى صفة وجودية لزم ألا تعرى صفة عن القبول كما أن الذات لا تعرى عن الصفات ، وذلك يستلزم التسلسل والدخول فيما لا نهاية له ؛ بأن تقبل الصفة صفة والصفة صفة أخرى وهكذا ، والتسلسل محال ، فما أدى إليه من قبول الصفة صفة أخرى مثله ؛ لأن الصفة لا تتصف بصفات المعاني ولا المعنوية ، وإنما المتصف بهما الذات )
From here you know the impossibility of a quality subsisting in a quality, and that the acceptance of being described with existential attributes and their concomitants is a property of selves; there is no sharing between them and qualities in that. They subsist in their described subject. And we specified the proof with existential attributes and their concomitants only because it is in them that the entry of what is infinite into existence is necessary. As for the essential attributes (al-ṣifāt al-nafsiyyah): they refer back to the essence of their described subject, and there is no infinite regress in them. And as for the negative attributes (al-ṣifāt al-salbiyyah): there is no existence of their meanings in the external world (1), so from the assumption of their regress the entry of what is infinite into existence does not necessarily follow. For this reason, being described with these two types is shared between selves and qualities. For this reason, the exalted self and the attributes of meanings subsisting in it are described with existence and eternity. (Clarification of the proof: If the Exalted were not knowing by Himself, i.e., a self described with attributes, then He would be a quality subsisting in a locus. And if that were so, He would not be described with the attributes of meanings and the moral attributes. And His not being described with them is false, because it is necessary for Him to be described with them. And when the consequent is false, the antecedent is false; that is, His being a quality subsisting in a locus. And when that is false, the required is established: that He is a self described with the mentioned attributes. And His saying: 'For if the quality accepted...' etc., meaning: If the quality were receptive to an existential quality, it would necessarily follow that no quality would be free from acceptance, just as the self is not free from attributes. That entails infinite regress and the entry into what has no end: that a quality accepts a quality, and that quality accepts another quality, and so on. And infinite regress is impossible, so what leads to it—i.e., a quality's acceptance of another quality—is likewise impossible, because a quality is not described with the attributes of meanings nor the moral attributes; rather, the one described with them is the self.)
Source pages p. 159

Commentary
This passage is a specification and clarification of the earlier proof. The author first states that from the previous argument one knows the impossibility of a quality subsisting in another quality. The acceptance of being described with existential attributes (ṣifāt wujūdiyya) and their concomitants (lawāzim) is specific to selves (dhawāt). The author explains why the proof was restricted to existential attributes and their concomitants: only these entail an infinite regress if applied to qualities. In contrast, essential attributes (nafsiyya) refer back to the essence and do not multiply; negative attributes (salbiyya) have no external existence, so assuming their regress does not entail infinite entities. Therefore, being described with these two types is shared between selves and qualities—e.g., both the self and the attributes of meanings are described as existent and eternal. Then the author provides a clarification (tawḍīḥ) of the proof: if God were not knowing by Himself, He would be a quality subsisting in a locus. But then He could not be described with the attributes of meanings or moral attributes, yet it is necessary that He be described with them. Therefore, the consequent (being a quality in a locus) is false, so the antecedent (being a quality) is false, establishing the truth that He is a self described with those attributes. The author also explicates the earlier statement about a quality accepting another quality, showing that it would lead to infinite regress; thus a quality cannot be receptive to another quality.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the proof against infinite regress applies to all types of attributes, including essential and negative ones.
Correction: The proof only applies to existential attributes because they have external existence. Essential attributes do not multiply, and negative attributes have no external reality, so regress in them does not lead to an infinite chain of existent entities.

Checkpoint
Why does the proof of impossible infinite regress apply only to existential attributes and their concomitants?
A) Because essential and negative attributes are not really attributes
B) Because essential attributes revert to the essence and negative attributes have no external existence, so regress in them does not multiply existent entities
C) Because existential attributes are the only ones that God truly possesses
D) Because essential and negative attributes are not attributed to selves

Key Takeaway
The proof against infinite regress applies only to existential attributes, because only they have external existence and can multiply; essential and negative attributes do not share this property.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever mistake essential attributes for existential ones in your own understanding of God, leading you to think of God's attributes as multiple separate entities? How does this passage clarify that distinction, and what does that mean for your worship?
?
When you reflect on God's negative attributes (like 'not unjust'), do you truly grasp that they are mere negations, or do you imagine them as positive realities? How does recognizing their nonexistence affect the intensity of your reverence?
?
The proof of infinite regress applies strictly to existential attributes. Have you ever insisted on a theological position using an argument that was appropriate for one domain but misapplied to another? How can the precision of this passage guard your own reasoning?

Divine Attributes
advanced
5 min
Clarification: The Proof That God Must Be a Self Described with Attributes
Sanusi clarifies the proof with a logical illustration and explains why a quality cannot accept another quality.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the clarified proof that if God were not knowing by Himself, He would be a quality, but qualities cannot possess the necessary attributes, so He must be a self.

Core Idea
If God were a quality, He could not be described with moral attributes, so He must be a self.

Sanusi now clarifies his proof with a logical illustration. He considers the supposition that God is not knowing by Himself—meaning He is not a self described with attributes. If that were so, He would be a quality subsisting in a locus. But then He could not be described with the attributes of meanings and moral attributes, yet such description is necessary. Therefore, the supposition is false, and we confirm that God is indeed a self described with those attributes. This clarification reinforces the central claim that God is not a quality but a self with attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the clarification passage
Read the Arabic passage starting from 'Wa-tawḍīḥ al-burhān' to the end. Then read the translation carefully.
This is a logical argument (conditional proof).
2
Identify the hypothesis and its implications
The argument begins with 'If the Exalted were not knowing by Himself' (i.e., not a self described with attributes). This leads to 'He would be a quality subsisting in a locus.' Then trace the implication that such a quality cannot be described with attributes of meanings and moral attributes.
Note the term 'صفات المعاني' (attributes of meanings) and 'صفات المعنوية' (moral attributes).
3
Understand the falsification
Since it is necessary for God to be described with those attributes, the consequent (being a quality) is false. Therefore, the antecedent (not being a self) is also false, establishing the desired conclusion: God is a self described with those attributes.
This is a modus tollens argument: if P then Q, not Q, therefore not P.
4
Examine the additional point about qualities
The passage ends with an explanation of why a quality cannot accept another quality, using the idea of infinite regress. This supports the impossibility of a quality being receptive to existential attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Checkpoint
What is the structure of the argument in the clarification?
A) It argues that if God were a quality, He would have infinite regress
B) It argues that if God were not a self, He would be a quality, but then He could not have certain necessary attributes, so He must be a self
C) It argues that qualities cannot accept other qualities, so God cannot be a quality

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God were merely a quality and not a self aware of you, would your actions matter? But He is a self described with knowledge and power—does that truth make you feel watched or cared for? How will you live today with the certainty that He knows your every thought and intention, and adjust something hidden in your heart?
?
The proof shows that only a self can possess moral attributes. You are a self, created by the Self. When you attribute goodness or power to yourself, do you remember that these are loans from the One who alone deserves them? What attribute of God will you consciously acknowledge in your actions today?
?
Infinite regress is impossible; everything must trace back to a necessary being. Reflect on your daily concerns: are you seeking ultimate security in things that are themselves contingent? Name one created thing you are relying on as if it were necessary, and commit to redirecting your trust to the Necessary Self today.

Divine Attributes
intermediate
6 min
Proof That Attributes Cannot Subsist in Attributes
Sanusi proves that attributes cannot subsist in attributes and that God is a dhāt.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof that an attribute cannot subsist in another attribute, and why this leads to the conclusion that God is an essence qualified with attributes, not an attribute itself.

Core Idea
Attributes cannot be hosts for other attributes—only essences can be qualified.

Sanusi presents a key proof in the science of divine attributes. He argues that if an attribute could accept another attribute, it would lead to an infinite regress of attributes, which is impossible. This establishes that only essences (dhawāt) are the proper subjects of qualification. The proof is carefully restricted to existential attributes, as psychical and negative attributes do not entail the same difficulty. This lesson unpacks the argument and its significance for understanding God's nature.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once carefully, then read the full translation. Notice the technical terms used.
Pay attention to the terms for different attribute types.
2
Identify the three types of attributes
The passage distinguishes existential, psychical, and negative attributes. Understand how each behaves differently in the argument.
The proof is restricted to existential attributes and their concomitants.
3
Trace the proof's logical flow
Follow the conditional arguments: if an attribute accepted another attribute, it would lead to infinite regress, which is impossible. Also follow the proof that God is a dhāt.
The proof uses reductio ad absurdum.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومن هنا تَعرِفُ استحالة قيام الصفةِ بالصفةِ ، وأَنَّ قبول الاتصافِ بالصفاتِ الوجودية ولوازمها مِنْ خواص الذوات . . لا مشاركة بينها وبين الصفاتِ في ذلكَ تقوم بموصوفها
وإِنَّما خَصَصْنا البرهانَ بالصفاتِ الوجودية ولوازمها لأنها هي التي ويلزمُ فيها دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ، أمَّا الصفاتُ النفسيةُ : فهي راجعة إلى حقيقة موصوفها ، ولا تسلسل فيها ، وأما الصفات السلبية : فلا وجود لمعانيها في الخارج (۱) ، فلا يلزمُ مِنْ تقدير تسلسلها دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ، ولهذا كانَ الاتصافُ بهذين النوعين مشتركاً بين الذوات والصفاتِ ، ولهذا تُوصَفُ الذاتُ العليةُ وصفاتُ المعاني القائمة بها بالوجودِ والقِدَمِ
( وتوضيح البرهان : لو لم يكن تعالى عالماً بنفسه ؛ أي : ذاتاً موصوفة بالصفات .. لكان صفة قائمة بمحل ، ولو كان كذلك لما اتصف بصفات المعاني والمعنوية ، وعدم اتصافه بهما باطل ؛ لأنه يجب اتصافه بهما ، وإذا بطل اللازم بطل الملزوم ؛ وهو كونه صفة قائمة بمحل ، وإذا بطل ذلك ثبت المطلوب ؛ وهو كونه ذاتاً موصوفة بالصفات المذكورة .
وقوله : ( إذ لو قبلت الصفة . . . » إلى آخره ؛ أي: ولو كانت الصفة قابلة إلى صفة وجودية لزم ألا تعرى صفة عن القبول كما أن الذات لا تعرى عن الصفات ، وذلك يستلزم التسلسل والدخول فيما لا نهاية له ؛ بأن تقبل الصفة صفة والصفة صفة أخرى وهكذا ، والتسلسل محال ، فما أدى إليه من قبول الصفة صفة أخرى مثله ؛ لأن الصفة لا تتصف بصفات المعاني ولا المعنوية ، وإنما المتصف بهما الذات ) .
(۱) وكذا لا وجود لها في الذهن ، خلافاً للنفسية والمعنوية على القول بها ؛ لأنهما من الأمور الاعتبارية
From here you know the impossibility of an attribute subsisting in an attribute, and that the acceptance of being qualified by existential attributes and their necessary concomitants is a property of essences... there is no participation between them and attributes in that; they subsist in their subject. And we only specified the proof with existential attributes and their necessary concomitants because they are the ones in which an infinite regress must enter into existence. As for the psychical attributes: they go back to the reality of their subject, and there is no infinite regress in them. As for the negative attributes: there is no existence of their meanings in the external world (1), so an infinite regress of them would not necessitate the entry of an infinite regress into existence. Therefore, being qualified by these two types is shared between essences and attributes. Hence the Supreme Essence and the attributes of meaning subsisting in it are described with existence, eternity, and non-accord with originated things, and (divine) unity. (And clarification of the proof: If He, exalted, were not knowing by Himself—i.e., an essence qualified with the attributes—then He would be an attribute subsisting in a substrate. If that were the case, He would not be qualified by the attributes of meaning and the attributes of meaning's implications. And His not being qualified by them is false because He must be qualified by them. And if the necessary consequent is false, then the antecedent is false, which is His being an attribute subsisting in a substrate. And if that is false, then the desired conclusion is proven, which is His being an essence qualified with the mentioned attributes.) And His saying: (For if the attribute accepted... etc.; i.e., if the attribute were receptive of an existential attribute, then it would necessarily not be free from acceptance, just as the essence is not free from attributes. That would entail an infinite regress and entry into what has no end—that the attribute accepts an attribute, and that attribute accepts another attribute, and so on. And infinite regress is impossible. So whatever leads to it—i.e., the attribute accepting another attribute—is likewise impossible because the attribute is not qualified by the attributes of meaning nor by the attributes of meaning's implications; rather, it is the essence that is qualified by them.) (1) And likewise, they have no existence in the mind, contrary to the psychical and the attributes of meaning, according to the position that holds them, because they are from among the mental considerations.
Source pages p. 159

Commentary
This passage provides a proof (burhan) that no attribute can subsist in another attribute, thereby establishing that God is a self (dhāt) qualified with attributes, not an attribute itself. The author begins by stating that the acceptance of being qualified by existential attributes and their necessary concomitants (lawāzim) is exclusive to essences (dhawāt), not to attributes. Then he explains why the proof is confined to existential attributes and their necessary concomitants: because these are the ones that would entail an infinite regress (tasalsul) if they were to subsist in each other. The other two types of attributes—psychical attributes (al-ṣifāt al-nafsiyya) and negative attributes (al-ṣifāt al-salbiyya)—do not have meanings that exist externally, so their hypothetical regress would not cause an actual infinite regress in existence. He then clarifies the proof: if God were not knowing by Himself—i.e., an essence qualified with attributes—He would be an attribute subsisting in a substrate. But an attribute cannot be qualified by the attributes of meaning (ṣifāt al-ma‘ānī) and the attributes of meaning's implications (al-ma‘nawiyya). Since God must be qualified by them, He cannot be an attribute; therefore He must be an essence qualified with attributes. The author then explains a statement from the text: if an attribute were receptive of an existential attribute, it would lead to an infinite chain of attributes accepting attributes, which is impossible. Thus, an attribute cannot be qualified by other attributes; only the essence is qualified. A footnote clarifies that negative attributes have no existence in the mind either, unlike psychical and meaning-based attributes which are mental considerations.

الصفات السلبية

صفة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That attributes themselves can be qualified with other attributes.
Correction: The proof shows that if an attribute could accept another attribute, it would lead to an infinite regress, which is impossible. Therefore, only essences are qualified.

Checkpoint
According to the proof, why is it impossible for an attribute to subsist in another attribute?
A) Because attributes are not real.
B) Because it would lead to an infinite regress of attributes.
C) Because God's essence is simple.
D) Because attributes are independent of essences.

Key Takeaway
An attribute cannot be qualified by another attribute, because that would entail an infinite regress; only essences are qualified.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The proof forces us to see Allah not as a collection of attributes but as a unified essence qualified by them. Is your mental picture of Allah fragmented—do you sometimes think of His mercy as separate from His justice, forgetting they are expressions of one reality? How can you train your heart to unite His attributes in worship?
?
If an attribute can never be the subject of another attribute, what does that say about your own attempts to ascribe qualities to God based on your limited understanding? Are you guilty of projecting human attribute-relationships onto Allah, like thinking His love is 'influenced by' your actions? This lesson demands you purge such mental images. Will you do it?
?
The argument eliminates infinite regress in attributes. Yet in your spiritual life, you may rationalize infinite chains of excuses: 'I will repent after this sin, then after that...' The same logic shows that chains without an end are impossible. Where in your life are you perpetuating a vicious cycle that needs a decisive break? What concrete step will you take today to end that regress?

Four Divisions of Things Regarding Locus and Agent
Al-Bayli classifies things into four categories based on need for a locus and an agent.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how al-Bayli classifies all things into four categories based on their need for a locus (mahall) and an agent (fa'il), and how this classification differentiates God's essence, His attributes, and contingent beings.

Core Idea
All things are divided into four categories based on need for a locus and an agent.

This passage presents a foundational classification in Islamic theology. Al-Bayli uses two simple criteria—whether something requires a substrate in which to exist (mahall) and whether it requires an efficient cause to bring it into being (fa'il)—to generate a complete taxonomy of all things. By understanding these four categories, we gain clarity on the unique nature of God and the dependence of all else.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the four categories are enumerated.
2
Identify the two criteria
The classification uses two criteria: need for a locus (mahall) and need for an agent (fa'il). Understand what each means.
A locus is a substrate; an agent is a cause.
3
List and label the four divisions
Write down the four categories: (1) needs neither, (2) needs both, (3) needs locus only, (4) needs agent only. Then match each with its example.
4
Reflect on the theological significance
Notice that only God's essence falls into the first category; His attributes need a locus (His essence) but not an agent. Accidents need both. This shows God's self-sufficiency.
This classification prevents mistakes like thinking God's attributes are created.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(۲) قال العلامة البيلي في " تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى » ( ق ۱۱ ) : ) واعلم : أن الأشياء بالنسبة للمحلَّ والفاعل على أربعة أقسام : أحدها : ما لا يحتاج إليهما ؛ وهي ذات الله عز وجل ، وثانيها : ما يحتاج إليهما معاً صفات الحوادث ، وثالثها : ما يحتاج إلى محل ولا يحتاج إلى فاعل ؛ كصفاته جل وعز ، ورابعها : ما يحتاج إلى فاعل دون محل ؛ كذوات وهي الحوادث ) .
(2) The scholar al-Bayli said in his annotations on Sharh Sughra al-Sughra (folio 11): (Know that things with respect to locus (mahall) and agent (fa'il) are of four divisions: The first: what does not need either of them—that is the essence (dhat) of God Almighty. The second: what needs both of them—the attributes of accidents (sifat al-hawadith). The third: what needs a locus but does not need an agent—like His attributes (sifatihi) [i.e., God's attributes]. The fourth: what needs an agent but not a locus—like essences that are accidents (dhawat al-hawadith).)
Source pages p. 160

Commentary
This passage is a classification (taqsim) by al-Bayli. He divides all things (al-ashya') with respect to two considerations: need for a locus (mahall, i.e., a substrate in which they inhere) and need for an agent (fa'il, i.e., an efficient cause). The four categories are: (1) that which needs neither a locus nor an agent—this is the essence of God, which is absolutely self-sufficient; (2) that which needs both—these are the attributes of accidents, which inhere in a locus and are brought into existence by an agent; (3) that which needs a locus but not an agent—these are God's attributes, which inhere in His essence but are not created; (4) that which needs an agent but not a locus—these are the essences of accidents, i.e., the substances that are accidents themselves (such as color), which are brought into existence but do not inhere in another accident.

Key Terms
المحل
الفاعل
أقسام
ذات الله
صفات الحوادث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: People might think that God's attributes are created or need an agent.
Correction: Al-Bayli classifies God's attributes as needing a locus (His essence) but not an agent, showing they are uncreated and eternal.

Checkpoint
Which category does the essence of God belong to in this classification?
A) Needs both locus and agent.
B) Needs only locus.
C) Needs only agent.
D) Needs neither.

Key Takeaway
The fourfold classification shows that only God's essence is absolutely independent; all else depends on a locus, an agent, or both.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This lesson shows that only God’s essence depends on nothing. Where in your life are you still trying to be independent of Him? What would it look like to truly depend on Him alone?
?
The third division teaches that God’s attributes need no agent—they are uncreated. How does this deepen your trust in His unchanging nature? What attribute of God do you most need to rely on today?
?
If every created thing depends on a locus or an agent, what does that say about your own existence? How should this affect your daily sense of humility and gratitude?

God's Oneness: Definition and Proof
Sanusi affirms absolute oneness of God, defining it and proving it by impossibility of a peer or partner.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic doctrine of God's oneness as absolute uniqueness without any peer, and the rational proof that negates any equal or partner in essence, attributes, or actions.

Core Idea
God's oneness means He has no like in essence, attributes, or action.

This lesson focuses on one of the essential attributes of God: oneness (wahdaniyyah). Sanusi defines it precisely and provides a logical proof that God must be unique without any equal or partner. The passage also clarifies what oneness does not mean, distinguishing it from types of unity that imply similarity.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic text once, then the full English translation. Notice the structure: main text by Sanusi followed by a gloss by al-Bayli.
The gloss provides important clarifications on types of unity.
2
Understand the definition of wahdaniyyah
Sanusi defines wahdaniyyah as three negations: no like in essence, no like in attributes, and no effective agent with Him in any action.
3
Trace the proof against an equal
The proof works by contradiction: if there were an equal or partner, God would need a specifier to distinguish Himself, which implies need and thus contingency (huduth) and incapacity, contradicting necessary existence.
Follow the logical steps carefully.
4
Examine the three invalid types of unity
Al-Bayli explains three types of unity that are impossible for God: unity of genus, species, and person. Understand why each implies a likeness.
These are distinguished from wahdaniyyah.
5
Reflect on theological significance
Consider why this attribute is foundational: without absolute uniqueness, God would not be the Necessary Being.
This refutes polytheism and anthropomorphism.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجبُ لهُ تعالى الوحدانية (۱) ؛ أي : لا مِثْلَ له في ذاتِهِ ، ولا في صفةٍ مِنْ صفاتِهِ ، ولا مؤثرَ معَهُ في فعلٍ مِنَ الأفعالِ ؛ إذْ لو كانَ معَهُ مِثْلُ أو مُؤثرٌ. لَمَا كانَ واجبَ الوجودِ ؛ لاحتياجهِ حِينَئِذٍ إلى مَنْ يخصِّصُهُ بما يمتاز بهِ عما يَماثِلُهُ عموماً أو خصوصاً ، وذلك يستلزم الحدوث والعجز عن كل ممكن (۱) قال العلامة البيلي في " تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى » ( ق ١٢ ) : ( قوله : « الوحدانية » نسبة للوحدة ، فالياء فيها للنسب ، والألف زائدة ، والنون للمبالغة ، والتاء للتأنيث اللفظي ، وهي في حقه تعالى تفسر بما ذكر بخلاف وحدة الجنس التي يشترك فيها الشيء مع غيره ؛ كاشتراك الإنسان وغيره من الأنواع في الحيوان ، ووحدة النوع ؛ وهي التي يشترك فيها الشيء مع غیره ؛ کاشتراك زيد وعمرو في الإنسان ، ووحدة الشخص ؛ وهي ظاهرة ، فهذه الوحدات الثلاث مستحيلة على الله تعالى ؛ لأنه لا جنس له يشترك فيه مع غيره ، ولا نوع له كذلك )
And Oneness (al-wahdaniyyah) is necessarily true of Him, the Exalted (1); meaning: there is no like (mithl) of Him in His essence, nor in any of His attributes, nor any effective agent (mu'aththir) with Him in any of the actions; for if there were with Him a like or an effective agent, He would not be Necessary Existence; because He would then need someone to specify (yukhassis) Him with what distinguishes Him from what resembles Him generally or specifically, and that entails temporality (huduth) and incapacity ('ajz) regarding every possible thing. (1) The scholar al-Bayli said in his annotations on Sharh Sughra al-Sughra (folio 12): (His saying: 'al-wahdaniyyah' is a derivation from wahdah (oneness); the ya' is for relation, the alif is extra, the nun is for intensification, and the ta' is for formal femininity. It is explained in His right, Exalted, as mentioned, unlike the unity of genus (wahdat al-jins) in which a thing shares with others—like the sharing of humans and other species in 'animal'—and the unity of species (wahdat al-naw') which is what a thing shares with others—like the sharing of Zayd and Amr in 'human'—and the unity of person (wahdat al-shakhs) which is obvious. These three unities are impossible for God Almighty, because He has no genus in which He shares with others, nor a species likewise.)
Source pages p. 161

Commentary
This passage affirms the attribute of oneness (wahdaniyyah) for God and defines it in three negations: no equal in essence, no equal in attributes, and no partner in actions. The proof is by contradiction: if there were an equal or partner, God would not be Necessary Existence, because He would require a 'specifier' (mukhassis) to differentiate Him from the other, implying need and thus contingency (huduth) and incapacity. The gloss by al-Bayli defines the term 'wahdaniyyah' linguistically and distinguishes it from three invalid types of unity: unity of genus (e.g., sharing in animality), unity of species (e.g., sharing in humanity), and unity of person (individual identity). These types entail likeness, which is impossible for God as He has no genus or species.

Key Terms
الوحدانية
المثل
المؤثر
المخصِّص
وحدة الجنس
وحدة النوع
وحدة الشخص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God's oneness is like numerical oneness, such as being a single individual of a species.
Correction: God's oneness (wahdaniyyah) is absolute uniqueness without any peer or similarity, not the unity that characterizes created individuals of a genus or species.

Checkpoint
According to the proof, what would follow if God had an equal?
A) God would still be necessary.
B) He would need a specifier, leading to contingency.
C) He would be two gods.
D) Nothing would change.

Key Takeaway
God's oneness means absolute uniqueness: no equal in essence, attributes, or actions, and any partner would render Him contingent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there any person, status, or possession in your life that you treat as an 'effective agent' alongside Allah—something you rely on as if it has independent power? What would it take to uproot that reliance from your heart?
?
When you imagine Allah, do you find yourself picturing something familiar? How does the truth that He has no like and no genus or species challenge your mental image of Him? Will you actively train your heart to affirm His absolute uniqueness even when your mind struggles?
?
If God's oneness means He alone specifies and creates every detail, where have you been acting as if you are the one in control? Will you today hand over that area to Him in humble admission of your need?

The Meaning and Proof of Divine Oneness (Wihdāniyya)
Sanusi defines wihdāniyya and proves its necessity via a conditional argument.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that wihdāniyya means God has no like in essence or attributes and no co-influencer, and the rational proof that if there were a like or influencer, God would not be necessary of existence.

Core Idea
God's oneness means He has no like or partner — any like would make Him contingent.

Sanusi begins this lesson by stating that divine oneness (wihdāniyya) is necessary for God. He then clarifies what oneness truly means: it is not merely numerical unity, but the absence of any like in essence or attributes, and the absence of any co-influencer in actions. To prove this, he offers a concise rational argument that any such like or influencer would imply contingency and limitation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Pay attention to the definition of wihdāniyya and the conditional proof that follows. Notice that Sanusi starts with a statement of necessity, then defines, then gives a proof.
2
Identify the two components of the proof
The proof has two parts: (1) if there were a like or influencer, God would need a specifier, and (2) needing a specifier implies contingency and incapability, which contradict necessary existence. Find these in the passage.
3
Reflect on the implication
Ask yourself: Why would a like or influencer require a specifier? How does this argument establish that God is unique in essence, attributes, and actions? The commentary will explain.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجبُ لهُ تعالى الوحدانية (۱) ؛ أي : لا مِثْلَ له في ذاتِهِ ، ولا في صفةٍ مِنْ صفاتِهِ ، ولا مؤثرَ معَهُ في فعلٍ مِنَ الأفعالِ ; إذْ لو كانَ معَهُ مِثْلُ أو مُؤثرٌ. لَمَا كانَ واجبَ الوجودِ ; لاحتياجهِ حِينَئِذٍ إلى مَنْ يخصِّصُهُ بما يمتاز بهِ عما يماثله عموماً أو خصوصاً ، وذلك يستلزم الحدوث والعجز عن كل ممكن
And it is necessary for Him, Exalted, oneness (wihdāniyya); meaning: there is no like (mithl) for Him in His essence, nor in any of His attributes, nor any influencer (mu'aththir) with Him in any action; for if there were with Him a like or an influencer, He would not be necessary of existence (wājib al-wujūd); because He would then need someone to specify Him (yukhaṣṣiṣuhu) with what distinguishes Him from what resembles Him generally or specifically, and that entails contingency (ḥudūth) and incapability with respect to every possible thing.
Source pages p. 161

Commentary
This passage is a definition and a proof. Sanusi states that God's oneness (wihdāniyya) is necessary, and explains it as having no like in essence, attributes, or no co-influencer in actions. He then gives a proof: if there were a like or an influencer, God would need a specifier to distinguish Him, which implies that He is contingent (ḥādith) and incapable, contradicting His being wājib al-wujūd.

Key Terms
وحدانية
مثل
مؤثر
واجب الوجود
حدوث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Oneness (wihdāniyya) means God is one in number.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that wihdāniyya means there is no like for Him in essence or attributes, and no co-influencer in actions. It is not merely numerical unity but the negation of any peer or partner.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what does wihdāniyya negate about God?
A) That He has multiple parts
B) That He has a like in essence, attributes, or a co-influencer
C) That He is one in number
D) That He is unique only in essence

Key Takeaway
Wihdāniyya means God has no like in His essence or attributes and no co-influencer in His actions, and any such other would imply God is contingent, contradicting His necessary existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that nothing in your life—no person, no possession, no ambition—deserves the worship and trust that belongs to God alone? How does the argument that any 'like' would make God contingent challenge your attachments?
?
When you face a problem, do you unconsciously seek help from created things as if they have power alongside God? How does the concept of 'no influencer with Him' expose the shirk in your daily reliance?
?
If you truly internalized that God has no like in His essence or attributes, would you still be envious of others' abilities or successes? How would you change your perspective of others' gifts?

Al-Baylī on Divine Oneness and the Three Types of Unity
Al-Baylī defines wihdāniyya and explains three types of unity impossible for God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the linguistic structure of the term 'wihdāniyya' and why three types of created unity cannot apply to Allah.

Core Idea
God's oneness is unique—no genus, no species, no numerical sharing.

Al-Baylī begins by breaking down the word 'wihdāniyya' itself, showing it is an intensive relative form. He then distinguishes Allah's oneness from three kinds of unity found in creation. This passage clarifies that divine oneness is absolute uniqueness, with no partner or equal in any category.

Guided Path
1
Examine the word 'wihdāniyya'
Read the morphological analysis: the yā' for relation, extra alif, intensifying nūn, and feminine tā'. This form emphasizes uniqueness.
Notice that the term itself is constructed to stress that Allah's oneness is not ordinary.
2
Identify the three types of unity
Find and understand the definitions: unity of genus (sharing in a broader category), unity of species (sharing in a specific kind), and unity of individual (numerical oneness).
Examples are given: genus—man and animal; species—Zayd and Amr; individual—a single person.
3
Understand why they are impossible for God
Read the conclusion: Allah has no genus or species; thus none of these unities can apply to Him. His oneness is incomparable.
This refines our understanding of tawḥīd beyond simply saying 'Allah is one'.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال العلامة البيلي في " تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى » ( ق ١٢ ) : ( قوله : « الوحدانية » نسبة للوحدة ، فالياء فيها للنسب ، والألف زائدة ، والنون للمبالغة ، والتاء للتأنيث اللفظي ، وهي في حقه تعالى تفسر بما ذكر بخلاف وحدة الجنس التي يشترك فيها الشيء مع غيره ; كاشتراك الإنسان وغيره من الأنواع في الحيوان ، ووحدة النوع ; وهي التي يشترك فيها الشيء مع غیره ; کاشتراك زيد وعمرو في الإنسان ، ووحدة الشخص ; وهي ظاهرة ، فهذه الوحدات الثلاث مستحيلة على الله تعالى ; لأنه لا جنس له يشترك فيه مع غيره ، ولا نوع له كذلك )
Al-ʿAllāma al-Baylī said in his "Taqyīdāt ʿalā Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā" (folio 12): (His saying: 'al-wihdāniyya' is a relative form to al-waḥda, so the yā' is for relation, the alif is extra, the nūn is for intensification, and the tā' is for feminine verbal form, and it is interpreted with respect to Him, Exalted, as mentioned, unlike the unity of genus (waḥdat al-jins) in which a thing shares with others, like the sharing of man and other species in animal, and the unity of species (waḥdat al-nawʿ) which is that in which a thing shares with others, like the sharing of Zayd and ʿAmr in humanity, and the unity of individual (waḥdat al-shakhṣ) which is apparent. So these three unities are impossible for Allah, the Exalted, because He has no genus in which He shares with others, nor species likewise.)
Source pages p. 161

Commentary
This passage is a lexical and theological explanation. Al-Baylī explains the word 'wihdāniyya' derived from 'al-waḥda' with an intensive form. Then he clarifies that God's oneness is not like three types of unity seen in created things: unity of genus (like sharing animality), unity of species (like sharing humanity), and unity of individual (numerical oneness of a specific person). All three are impossible for God because He has no genus or species, and His unity is absolute uniqueness without any sharing.

Key Terms
وحدانية
وحدة الجنس
وحدة النوع
وحدة الشخص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that God's oneness is like the numerical oneness of a person (waḥdat al-shakhṣ).
Correction: Al-Baylī shows that even numerical oneness involves a unique individual, but God has no genus or species, so His oneness transcends all created categories.

Checkpoint
Which type of unity does al-Baylī say is impossible for God?
A) Unity of genus only
B) Unity of species only
C) Unity of individual only
D) All three types (genus, species, individual)

Key Takeaway
Al-Baylī teaches that divine oneness is unlike any created unity, as it is absolute uniqueness without any partner or commonality.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'Allāhu Aḥad' in prayer, do you sometimes subconsciously imagine a 'big' individual being? How does al-Baylī's teaching force you to rethink that mental image? What specific change in your understanding will you commit to from now on?
?
Can you point to a moment in your daily life where you accidentally compared God's oneness to something created (like thinking 'God is one like I am one')? In light of this lesson, how will you guard your heart against such comparisons?
?
If someone asked you why God's oneness is utterly unique, would you be able to explain using al-Baylī's three types? What step will you take this week to deepen your grasp of tawḥīd so you can articulate it clearly?

Why Wihdāniyya Is Not Redundant
Al-Baylī answers an objection that wihdāniyya is redundant with God's dissimilarity to creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the attribute of wihdāniyya (oneness) is not redundant with God's dissimilarity to creation.

Core Idea
Dissimilarity does not rule out another eternal being; wihdāniyya does.

An objector might think that since God is already described as different from created things, stating that He is one is unnecessary. Al-Baylī explains why this is not so: being different from creation does not automatically mean there cannot be another eternal being. The attribute of wihdāniyya is needed to explicitly deny any co-eternal being.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection carefully
Read the objection: 'If you say: the attribute of wihdāniyya is unnecessary because He is different in His essence, attributes, and actions from contingents, so what is the benefit of stating it?' Notice the logic: dissimilarity is already affirmed, so why mention oneness?
2
Understand the answer
Read Al-Baylī's answer: 'It does not follow from His being different from contingents... that He is one, because it is possible that there is another eternal being.' Grasp the key point: dissimilarity to creation does not logically exclude another eternal being who is also dissimilar to creation.
3
Identify the logical gap
Ask yourself: What does dissimilarity actually cover? It covers difference from created things. But another eternal being would also be different from creation. So dissimilarity alone does not prevent multiple eternal beings. Wihdāniyya fills this gap by explicitly denying any 'similar eternal being'.
The phrase 'قديم مماثل' (similar eternal being) is key.
4
Test your understanding with the checkpoint
Answer the multiple-choice question to check if you see why wihdāniyya is needed.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال العلامة البيلي في « تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى » ( ق ۱۲ ) : ( فإن قلت : صفة الوحدانية يستغنى عنها بكونه مخالفاً في ذاته وصفاته وأفعاله للحوادث ، فما فائدة النص عليها ؟ فجوابه : أنه لا يلزم من كونه مخالفاً للحوادث فيما ذكر كونه واحداً ; لجواز أن يكون هناك قديم ، فالنص على الوحدانية لنفي قديم مماثل )
Al-ʿAllāma al-Baylī said in his "Taqyīdāt ʿalā Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā" (folio 12): (If you say: the attribute of wihdāniyya is unnecessary because He is different in His essence, attributes, and actions from contingents, so what is the benefit of stating it? The answer is: it does not follow from His being different from contingents in what is mentioned that He is one, because it is possible that there is another eternal being, so the stating of wihdāniyya is to negate a similar eternal being.)
Source pages p. 162

Commentary
This passage is an objection and answer. The objector says that since God is already described as dissimilar (mukhālif) to created things in essence, attributes, and actions, the attribute of wihdāniyya seems redundant. Al-Baylī answers that dissimilarity to contingents does not preclude the existence of another eternal being that is also dissimilar; hence wihdāniyya is needed to explicitly deny any co-eternal being.

Key Terms
وحدانية
مخالف للحوادث
قديم مماثل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that saying God is unique in being different from creation is enough; but this does not rule out another eternal being.
Correction: Dissimilarity to creation only shows a difference from contingent things, not that no other eternal being exists. Wihdāniyya explicitly denies any co-eternal being.

Checkpoint
Why is wihdāniyya not redundant with God's dissimilarity to creation?
A) Because dissimilarity means there is no like
B) Because dissimilarity only shows difference from creation, not that there is no other eternal being
C) Because wihdāniyya emphasizes numerical oneness
D) Because dissimilarity is an attribute of action

Key Takeaway
Wihdāniyya is necessary because dissimilarity to creation does not by itself exclude the possibility of another eternal being.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you live as if God is the only eternal being, or do you sometimes give eternal significance to worldly things? What would change if you truly internalized that nothing else shares His eternity?
?
This lesson shows that even the belief that God is different from creation does not automatically make you a true monotheist. Is your tawhid just a mental acknowledgment, or does it actively shape your worship and priorities? What practical step can you take today to affirm wihdāniyya in your heart?
