Divine Attributes
beginner
5 min
Why No Created Power Resembles God's
Sanusi proves that no contingent power can resemble God's power in origination and annihilation

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why no created power can be similar to God's power, and how the argument proceeds from hypothetical to objection to refutation via infinite regress.

Core Idea
Created powers cannot resemble God's power without leading to impossibility or infinite regress.

Sanusi now tackles a critical theological point: if any created being could bring things into existence or annihilate them by its own power, it would rival God. He examines this claim, entertains an objection, and demolishes it with a rigorous logical argument. This passage is a masterclass in using rational proof to defend divine uniqueness.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without pausing. Then read the translation to grasp the flow of the argument.
The Arabic uses dense technical terms; the translation clarifies them.
2
Identify the hypothetical and its consequence
Find where Sanusi posits a created power that could originate and annihilate. Notice the consequence: such a power would be contingent and would lead to the contingency of God's essence.
This is the first move: proving similarity is impossible because it implies God is contingent.
3
Understand the objection (what an opponent might say)
The objection claims that created powers only produce effects accidentally, by God's grant, not by their own reality. This attempts to avoid similarity.
The opponent tries to preserve God's uniqueness by denying real causation to creatures.
4
Follow the answer and the infinite regress argument
Sanusi replies that if the effect is accidental, it cannot arise from the power itself without another cause. This leads to an infinite chain of accidental attributes—an impossibility.
The regress argument is the core of the refutation; it shows the objection fails.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفة في حادث يتأتى بها الإيجادُ والإعدام .. لكانَتْ مماثلة لقدرة البارئ تبارك وتعالى ، فتكون حادثة ؛ لاحتياجها إلى مُخصص خصَّصَها بالذات العليَّةِ ، وخصَّصَها التعلُّق عن نظيرتها وخصَّصَها بعمومِ ، وحدوث الصفة يستلزم حدوث موصوفها ، وذلك يستلزم حدوث الذات العليَّة ، تعالى الله عن ذلكَ فإن قيل : تأتي الإيجادِ والإعدام على وَفْقِ إرادة القادرِ وعلمه .. هو حقيقة القدرةِ الأزلية (۱) ، ولا مثل لها في ذلك ؛ لأنَّ الإيجاد والإعدام اللذين يُدعيان لبعض القوى الحادثة.. ليسا مِنْ حقيقة تلك القوَّةِ الحادثة ، بل هو عَرَضي لها بجعل الله تعالى لها ذلكَ ، فهي تُؤثر على وَفْقِ إرادةِ اللهِ تعالى وعلمِهِ ، لا على وَفْقِ إرادةِ موصوفها وعلمه فالجواب : أن تأتي التأثير إذا كان عرضيّاً لهذه القوة الحادثة (۳).. فإنَّهُ يلزمُ ألا يَرِدَ على هذهِ القُوَّةِ على حيالِهِ ؛ لأنَّهُ حال ، والأحوال لا يمكن أن تفعل على حيالها(٤) ، فلا بدَّ مِنْ خَلْقِ صفة معنى وجودية في هذه القوة الحادثة (٥) تكونُ عِلَّةٌ لِمَا عرض لها مِنْ تأتي الإيجادِ بها والإعدام ، ويلزمُ عليهِ قيامُ العَرَضِ بالعَرَضِ والتسلسلُ ؛ لنقْلِ الكلام إلى ذلكَ العَرَضِ الثاني : هل إيجابُهُ للتأثيرِ ذاتي فلا يَتوقَّفَ بعد وجودِهِ على إرادة ، أو هو عَرَضي فيحتاج إلى عَرَض آخر موجب لهُ الإيجاب للتأثير ؟ وهلم جراً وبالجملة : فالذي يجب اعتقاده ، وقام البرهان القطعي عليه : أن لا مثل للمولى تبارك وتعالى في الذاتِ ، ولا في الصفاتِ ، ولا في الأفعال ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق (۱)
An attribute in a contingent thing by which origination and annihilation occur... it would be similar to the power of the Creator, Blessed and Exalted. Then it would be contingent due to its need for a specificator that specified it with the supreme essence, and specified its relation away from its counterpart, and specified it with generality. And the contingency of the attribute necessitates the contingency of its possessor, and that necessitates the contingency of the supreme essence. Exalted is God above that. If it is said: The occurrence of origination and annihilation according to the will and knowledge of the powerful is the reality of eternal power (1), and there is no likeness to it in that, because the origination and annihilation claimed for some contingent powers are not from the reality of that contingent power, but rather are accidental to it by God's making them so, so they effect according to God's will and knowledge, not according to the will and knowledge of their possessor. The answer: That the occurrence of effect if it is accidental to this contingent power (3)... then it necessarily follows that it does not occur upon this power by itself, because it is a state, and states cannot act by themselves (4). So there must be the creation of an existential attributive meaning in this contingent power (5) that is a cause for what occurs to it of origination and annihilation, and upon that follows the subsistence of an accident by an accident and infinite regress, because the discourse transfers to that second accident: Is its necessitation for effect essential so that it does not depend after its existence on will, or is it accidental so it needs another accident that necessitates the necessitation for effect? and so on. In summary: What must be believed, and upon which decisive proof stands, is that there is no like to the Master, Blessed and Exalted, in essence, nor in attributes, nor in actions. And by God is success (1).
Source pages p. 165

Commentary
This passage is a proof and refutation. The author begins by positing a hypothetical: if a contingent (created) power could effect origination and annihilation (bringing things into existence and destroying them) like God's power, it would be similar to God's power. But if it were similar, it would itself be contingent (haditha), because it would need a 'specificator' (mukhassis) that distinguishes it from other powers—specifying its essence, its relation, and its scope. The contingency of the attribute necessitates the contingency of the entity it qualifies (the power's possessor), which would then imply the contingency of the divine essence itself—utterly impossible. Next, an objection is raised: the origination and annihilation attributed to some created powers (like heat causing burning) are not from the power's own reality but are accidental (aradi) to it, granted by God. Thus, these powers act according to God's will and knowledge, not their own, and no similarity remains. The author answers: if the effect is accidental, it cannot occur from the power by itself because it is a 'state' (hal), and states cannot act independently. To produce the effect, another existential attribute must be created in the power to serve as a cause. But then that second attribute would itself need another cause, leading to the subsistence of an accident by an accident and infinite regress (tasalsul). The argument then questions whether this second attribute's necessitation of effect is essential (dhati) or accidental—if essential, no further cause; if accidental, the regress continues. The conclusion: definitive proof establishes that there is absolutely no likeness to God in essence, attributes, or actions.

Key Terms
قدرة
حادث
مخصص
عرض
حال
تسلسل
مماثلة
صفة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think created powers cause effects independently, disproving God's uniqueness.
Correction: The author shows such causation is merely accidental by God's grant and leads to infinite regress, so no real similarity exists.

Checkpoint
What does the author conclude about the possibility of any contingent power being similar to God's power?
A) It is possible if God wills it.
B) It is impossible because it would lead to contingency of the divine essence or infinite regress.
C) It is possible but only for prophets.
D) It is a matter of debate among theologians.

Key Takeaway
There is absolutely no likeness to God in essence, attributes, or actions; this is established by decisive rational proof.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you accomplish something difficult, do you immediately recognize that the power to do so was not your own but was given by God each moment? If not, what would it take for you to internalize the truth that no created power can act independently?
?
The proof shows that any attribution of real causal power to created things leads to absurdity. In your daily life, where do you find yourself slipping into treating people, systems, or your own skills as if they have inherent power? How can you remind yourself of this lesson in those moments?
?
Consider the infinite regress argument: if a created power's effect required another attribute, and that another, ad infinitum. How does this starkly reveal your absolute dependence on God in every instant? What practical change in your reliance on God does this demand starting today?

Necessity and Definition of Divine Power and Will
Sanusi defines divine power and will and states their necessity and first requirement.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God must have power and will that relate to all possible things, and how these attributes are defined in classical Sunni theology.

Core Idea
God's power and will are necessary for the existence and specification of every created thing.

This lesson examines two essential divine attributes—power (قدرة) and will (إرادة)—as presented by Sanusi. He argues that without these attributes, nothing contingent could exist or be specified, and he provides concise definitions that highlight their roles. The passage also introduces a list of four requisites for these attributes, of which the first is their existence.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the literal translation to grasp the overall meaning. Notice the structure: a proof of necessity, then definitions, then an enumeration of requisites.
The passage uses technical terms like 'ممكن' (possible thing) and 'حادث' (contingent event).
2
Understand the logical proof for necessity
Sanusi argues that if God lacked power over some possible thing, He would lack power over all things, making contingent existence impossible. Trace the dependency chain: existence/non-existence depends on power, specification depends on will, and being willed depends on knowledge.
This shows the interconnectedness of God's attributes.
3
Learn the definitions of power and will
Power (قدرة) is the attribute enabling creation and annihilation according to will. Will (إرادة) is the attribute that specifies one possibility over its opposite. Memorize these definitions as they are foundational.
Both attributes are eternal and relate to all possible things.
4
Note the first of the four requisites
Sanusi states that every contingent thing indicates four requisites for power and will, the first being 'their existence' (وجودها). Reflect on why existence is the foundational requisite.
The other three requisites, though not specified here, are discussed in later lessons.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفات المعاني. ويجبُ له تعالى القدرة والإرادة المتعلقتان بكل ممكن؛ إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها وذلك يستلزم استحالة وجودها؛ لتوقف كل حادث في وجوده وإعدامهِ على اقتدار فاعله، وفي تخصصه على إرادته، وفي كونه مراداً على علمه. القدرة الأزلية: صفةٌ يتأتى بها إيجاد كل ممكن وإعدامه على وَفْقِ إرادته تعالى. والإرادة: صفةٌ يتأتّى بها تخصيص كلَّ ممكن بالجائز المخصوص بدلاً عن مقابله. ولا شكّ أنَّ كلَّ حادثٍ يدلُّ على أربعة مطالب لهاتين الصفتين: الأول: وجودهما.
Attributes of meanings. It is necessary for Him, exalted, to have power and will that relate to every possible thing; because inability regarding some of them entails inability regarding all of them, and that entails the impossibility of their existence; since every contingent thing depends in its existence and non-existence on the ability of its agent, in its specification on His will, and in its being willed on His knowledge. Eternal power: an attribute by means of which the bringing into existence of every possible thing and its annihilation is accomplished in accordance with His will. And will: an attribute by means of which the specification of every possible thing to the particular permissible in place of its opposite is accomplished. And there is no doubt that every contingent thing indicates four requisites for these two attributes: The first: Their existence.
Source pages p. 167

Commentary
This passage defines and proves the necessity of two of God's essential attributes: power (قدرة) and will (إرادة). The author first argues that God must have power and will over all possible things (كل ممكن), because inability in one would imply inability in all, making contingent existence impossible. Every contingent (حادث) depends on the agent's ability for its existence and non-existence, on will for its specification, and on knowledge for being willed. Then definitions are given: power is the attribute enabling creation and annihilation according to will; will is the attribute that specifies a particular possibility over its opposite. Finally, the author states that every contingent indicates four requisites (مطالب) for these two attributes, the first being their existence (وجودها). The remaining three are presumably necessity, eternity, and perhaps other details, but only the first is given in this passage.

Key Terms
صفات المعاني
قدرة
إرادة
ممكن
حادث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that 'power and will' imply anthropomorphism, comparing God's attributes to human traits.
Correction: In Sunni theology, divine power and will are understood as necessary and eternal attributes without resemblance to human attributes. They are real but not comparable.

Checkpoint
What is the first of the four requisites that every contingent indicates for power and will?
A) Their eternity
B) Their existence
C) Their unity
D) Their knowledge

Key Takeaway
God's power and will are necessary attributes relating to all possible things, and their existence is the foundational requisite for every contingent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that every single event in your life—from the smallest to the greatest—depends entirely on God's power and will? What area of your life do you most struggle to attribute to His decree?
?
If God's power and will are necessary and relate to all possible things, then nothing happens by chance. Does your heart find peace in that complete control, or does it resist? How would your daily worries change if you lived this truth?
?
The passage says every contingent indicates four requisites. Have you ever reflected on a single event in your life as evidence of God's power, will, and knowledge? What would happen if you started seeing every 'coincidence' as a sign of these divine attributes?

Definition of Power and Will
Sanusi defines power and will and states their necessity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definitions of divine power and will and why these attributes are necessary for Allah.

Core Idea
Power and will are the basis of all contingent existence.

Sanusi begins by establishing that power and will are necessary for Allah because without them contingent events could not exist or be specified. He then gives precise definitions of these two attributes. This passage lays the foundation for understanding how every created thing depends on Allah's power and will.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the flow from necessity to definitions.
The passage starts with a general statement about attributes of meaning.
2
Understand the definitions
Focus on how Sanusi defines power (qudra) and will (irada). Power is for bringing into existence and annihilating; will is for specification.
The definitions are precise and technical.
3
Connect to contingency
Reflect on why the author says these attributes are necessary: without power, contingent events would be impossible; without will, they would lack specification.
This connects to the broader argument for divine attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفات المعاني. ويجب له تعالى القدرة والإرادة المتعلقتان بكل ممكن؛ إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها وذلك يستلزم استحالة وجودها؛ لتوقف كل حادث في وجوده وإعدامه على اقتدار فاعله، وفي تخصصه على إرادته، وفي كونه مرادا على علمه. القدرة الأزلية: صفة يتأتى بها إيجاد كل ممكن وإعدامه على وفق إرادته تعالى. والإرادة: صفة يتأتى بها تخصيص كل ممكن بالجائز المخصوص بدلا عن مقابله. ولا شك أن كل حادث يدل على أربعة مطالب لهاتين الصفتين: الأول: وجودهما، الثاني: وجوب الوجود لهما، الثالث: عموم تعلقهما بجميع الممكنات، الرابع: وحدتهما. هذه المطالب ثابتة لكل صفات المعاني، غير أن هناك تفصيلا واختلافا في التعلق، وصفة الحياة لا تعلق لها.
Attributes of meaning. And it is necessary for Him, exalted, the power and the will which are related to every possible thing; because inability regarding some of them necessitates inability regarding all of them, and that necessitates the impossibility of their existence; because every contingent event depends in its existence and its non-existence on the capability of its agent, and in its specification on His will, and in its being willed on His knowledge. The eternal power: an attribute by which the bringing into existence of every possible thing and its annihilation is accomplished in accordance with His will, exalted. And the will: an attribute by which the specification of every possible thing with the particular permissible alternative instead of its opposite is accomplished. And there is no doubt that every contingent event indicates four demands concerning these two attributes: First: their existence, Second: the necessity of existence for them, Third: the generality of their relation to all possible things, Fourth: their unity. These demands are established for every attribute of meaning, except that there is a detail and difference in relation, and the attribute of life has no relation.
Source pages p. 167

Commentary
This passage begins by stating the necessity of power and will for God, and then defines each attribute. It explains that every contingent event requires these attributes and indicates four demands (مطالب): existence (وجود), necessity of existence (وجوب الوجود), general connection to all possibles (عموم التعلق), and unity (وحدة). The passage also notes that these four demands apply to all attributes of meaning, with the exception that the attribute of life has no relation (تعلق) to possible things. The author is setting up the framework for proving these attributes from contingent events.

Key Terms
صفات المعاني
القدرة
الإرادة
ممكن
حادث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the definition of eternal power?
A) An attribute by which Allah knows all things.
B) An attribute by which the bringing into existence of every possible thing and its annihilation is accomplished.
C) An attribute by which Allah specifies every possible thing.
D) An attribute by which Allah creates from nothing.

Key Takeaway
Power and will are necessary attributes of Allah, defined respectively as the ability to bring about and annihilate possibles and the ability to specify them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If every possible thing depends on Allah's power and will for its existence and specification, how does this understanding change the way you view your own successes and failures? Will you still attribute them to your own ability, or will you recognize Allah's sole agency?
?
The passage implies that denying Allah's power over even one small event leads to denying His power over everything. Is there an area of your life where you find it difficult to believe that Allah's will is fully in control? How will you actively remind yourself of this truth when you face uncertainty?
?
Knowing that Allah's will specifies every detail of creation—from the grand to the minute—does your heart find rest in that, or does it struggle? What specific action will you take today to submit more deeply to His decree?

The Four Demands from Contingent Events
Sanusi enumerates four demands from contingent events regarding power and will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the four logical demands that every contingent event indicates regarding Allah's power and will.

Core Idea
Contingent events prove not just existence but necessity, universality, and unity of divine attributes.

After defining power and will, Sanusi now explains that every contingent event points to four demands concerning these attributes: their existence, their necessary existence, their universal relation to all possibles, and their unity. This passage shows how creatures testify to the perfection of their Creator.

Guided Path
1
Identify the four demands
Read the passage and locate the four demands (مطالب). They are listed in order.
The author uses 'الأول: ... الثاني: ... الثالث: ... الرابع: ...'
2
Understand each demand
For each demand, reflect on why it is necessary. For example, 'existence' is needed because if power/will didn't exist, contingency could not be explained.
The passage does not prove each demand here; it only states them.
3
Note the exception of life
The passage ends by noting that these four demands hold for all attributes of meaning except life, which has no relation to possible things.
This distinguishes life from power and will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفات المعاني. ويجب له تعالى القدرة والإرادة المتعلقتان بكل ممكن؛ إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها وذلك يستلزم استحالة وجودها؛ لتوقف كل حادث في وجوده وإعدامه على اقتدار فاعله، وفي تخصصه على إرادته، وفي كونه مرادا على علمه. القدرة الأزلية: صفة يتأتى بها إيجاد كل ممكن وإعدامه على وفق إرادته تعالى. والإرادة: صفة يتأتى بها تخصيص كل ممكن بالجائز المخصوص بدلا عن مقابله. ولا شك أن كل حادث يدل على أربعة مطالب لهاتين الصفتين: الأول: وجودهما، الثاني: وجوب الوجود لهما، الثالث: عموم تعلقهما بجميع الممكنات، الرابع: وحدتهما. هذه المطالب ثابتة لكل صفات المعاني، غير أن هناك تفصيلا واختلافا في التعلق، وصفة الحياة لا تعلق لها.
Attributes of meaning. And it is necessary for Him, exalted, the power and the will which are related to every possible thing; because inability regarding some of them necessitates inability regarding all of them, and that necessitates the impossibility of their existence; because every contingent event depends in its existence and its non-existence on the capability of its agent, and in its specification on His will, and in its being willed on His knowledge. The eternal power: an attribute by which the bringing into existence of every possible thing and its annihilation is accomplished in accordance with His will, exalted. And the will: an attribute by which the specification of every possible thing with the particular permissible alternative instead of its opposite is accomplished. And there is no doubt that every contingent event indicates four demands concerning these two attributes: First: their existence, Second: the necessity of existence for them, Third: the generality of their relation to all possible things, Fourth: their unity. These demands are established for every attribute of meaning, except that there is a detail and difference in relation, and the attribute of life has no relation.
Source pages p. 167

Commentary
This passage begins by stating the necessity of power and will for God, and then defines each attribute. It explains that every contingent event requires these attributes and indicates four demands (مطالب): existence (وجود), necessity of existence (وجوب الوجود), general connection to all possibles (عموم التعلق), and unity (وحدة). The passage also notes that these four demands apply to all attributes of meaning, with the exception that the attribute of life has no relation (تعلق) to possible things. The author is setting up the framework for proving these attributes from contingent events.

Key Terms
مطالب
وجوب الوجود
عموم التعلق
وحدة
صفة الحياة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the four demands are separate proofs that need to be established individually.
Correction: The passage presents them as logical implications that any proof from contingency must establish; they are not separate proofs but necessary conclusions.

Checkpoint
What is the fourth demand (مطلب) that every contingent event indicates according to Sanusi?
A) Generality of relation
B) Unity
C) Eternity
D) Knowledge

Key Takeaway
Every contingent event logically requires that divine power and will are existent, necessary, universal in relation, and one.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Sanusi says every contingent event indicates these four demands. When you look at your own existence—completely dependent and temporary—do you truly let that fact lead you to affirm not just that God exists, but that His power and will are necessary, universal, and one? Or do you stop at mere belief without letting the logic transform your certainty?
?
The attribute of life has no relation to possible things. Does your understanding of God's life—that He is alive in a way entirely unrelated to your existence—lead you to awe, or have you never even distinguished it from your own derived life?
?
The four demands are not separate proofs but logical implications. As you recite this in your study, are you merely memorizing a list, or are you personally convinced that your own contingency demands these very attributes—so much so that to deny any of them would be intellectual negligence?

Divine Attributes
intermediate
6 min
Proof of Power and Will from Contingent Events
Sanusi proves divine power and will from contingent events via impossibility of opposites.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the existence of power and will are rationally proven from the existence of contingent events using proof by contradiction.

Core Idea
Contingent events prove power and will because their absence leads to impossibility.

Sanusi presents a concise rational proof for two essential divine attributes: power and will. He argues that since contingent events exist, their absence would entail logical absurdities. This lesson unpacks that proof step by step, showing how kalam reasoning uses the impossibility of opposites to establish necessary divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the literal translation to grasp the overall argument.
Notice the structure: first a general statement, then two conditional arguments.
2
Understand the proof for power
Focus on the first conditional: if power were negated, inability would exist, preventing action. Since contingent events occur, power must exist.
3
Understand the proof for will
Now the second conditional: if will were negated, a specific possible thing would be chosen over its equal without a preponderator, which is impossible. Thus will exists.
The concept of 'preponderator' (مرجح) is key here.
4
Reflect on the method
Notice this is proof by contradiction: assume the opposite leads to absurdity. This is a standard kalam method.
Also note that the existence of contingent events is taken as a given starting point.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجه دلالة الحادثات على وجود القدرة والإرادة. أما وجه دلالة كل حادث على وجودهما: فلأنه لو انتفت القدرة لوجد ضدها وهو العجز، وذلك يستلزم عدم التمكن من الفعل، ولو انتفت الإرادة للجائز المخصوص لزم ترجحه على مقابله المساوي له بلا مرجح، وذلك محال.
The aspect of the indication of contingent events upon the existence of power and will. As for the aspect of the indication of every contingent event upon their existence: because if power were negated, its opposite would exist, which is inability, and that necessitates the lack of ability to act. And if will were negated for the particular permissible alternative, its preponderance over its equal opposite without a preponderator would be necessitated, and that is impossible.
Source pages p. 168

Commentary
This passage provides the proof that power and will must exist. It does so by considering the consequences of their absence. If power did not exist, then its opposite, inability (عجز), would exist, which would mean no action could be performed. Since contingent events do exist, power must exist. Similarly, if will did not exist, then a specific possible thing would be chosen over its equal opposite without any preponderating factor (مرجح), which is absurd. Therefore, will must exist. The proof is based on the impossibility of the opposite (دليل التمانع or proof by contradiction).

Key Terms
دلالة
وجود
قدرة
إرادة
عجز
تمكن
مرجح
مساوي
محال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the proof assumes the existence of contingent events without justification.
Correction: While the proof does take the contingency of the world as a premise, that is established earlier in kalam. The focus here is on deriving power and will from that premise.

Checkpoint
According to the proof, what would necessarily follow if power were negated?
A) Will would still suffice
B) Inability would exist, making action impossible
C) The contingent event would still occur
D) The opposite would be impossible

Key Takeaway
The existence of power and will are rationally necessary because their absence would make the occurrence of contingent events impossible or absurd.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If your very existence at this moment depended on Allah's power and will sustaining it, would you live today any differently? What one thing will you do within the next hour to acknowledge that dependence?
?
The proof shows that without divine will, the universe would be irrational. In what specific area of your life are you acting as if your own will is the ultimate decider, ignoring that Allah's will is the foundation of all order? What step will you take to realign your will with His?
?
Knowing that Allah's power is proven by the fact that things exist rather than not, how does that change the way you view the trials you are facing? Will you trust His power in that difficulty, or rely on your own ability? What concrete act of tawakkul (reliance) can you practice today?

Proving the Pre-Eternity of Power and Will
Sanusi proves that power and will are necessary via reductio ad absurdum.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi argues that God's attributes of power and will must be pre-eternal and cannot be contingent, avoiding an infinite regress.

Core Idea
If power and will were contingent, they would need another power and will, leading to an endless chain—impossible.

Sanusi builds on earlier proofs to demonstrate that power and will are necessary attributes of God, not contingent. In this passage, he presents a reductio argument: if they were contingent, they would require an agent, but that agent would need further power and will, resulting in an infinite regress. This establishes their pre-eternity and permanence.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice the tight logical structure.
The footnote at the end provides an additional reason.
2
Identify the reductio structure
Find where Sanusi assumes the opposite (power and will are contingent) and traces the absurd consequence (infinite regress).
The key phrase is 'لو كانتا جائزتين لزم حدوثهما وافتقارهما إلى الفاعل'.
3
Understand the footnote
Read the second part in parentheses. It gives another argument based on opposition to contingent events and attributes of negation.
This reinforces the necessity of pre-eternity and permanence.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
Why must the original power and will be necessary? Because otherwise we face an infinite chain of contingent attributes, which is rationally impossible.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجه دلالة الحادثات على وجوب وجود القدرة والإرادة. وأما وجه دلالة كل حادث على وجوب وجود هاتين الصفتين - ويدخل في ذلك وجوب القدم لهما والبقاء - : فلأنهما لو كانتا جائزتين لزم حدوثهما وافتقارهما إلى الفاعل، ولا فاعل إلا الله؛ لما تقدم في الوحدانية، فيلزم أن يتصف قبل فعلهما بقدرة أخرى عليهما وإرادة؛ لما عرفت في المطلب الأول من وجوب توقف كل حادث على وجودهما قبله، ثم ننقل الكلام إلى القدرة والإرادة الأخريين، فيلزمهما من الحدوث ما لزم الأوليين، فيتوقفان أيضا في إحداثهما. (وأيضا: المخالفة للحوادث، وسبق التنبيه على ذلك، أو إن شئت فقل: يجب اتصافها بصفات السلوب غير القيام بالنفس؛ لأنها قائمة بذاته جل شأنه، وإنما نبه هنا على القدم والبقاء لتوقف الاستدلال الآتي عليهما.)
The aspect of the indication of contingent events upon the necessity of the existence of power and will. And as for the aspect of the indication of every contingent event upon the necessity of the existence of these two attributes - and this includes the necessity of pre-eternity for them and permanence - : because if they were contingent, their coming into existence and their need for an agent would be necessitated, and there is no agent but God, based on what has preceded in [the proof of] Oneness. Then it would be necessitated that He be described, before His act of them, with another power over them and a will, based on what you know in the first demand about the necessity of every contingent event's dependence on their existence before it. Then we transfer the discussion to the other power and will, and what was necessitated for the first two from contingency would be necessitated for them, so they would also depend [on another] for their bringing into existence. (And also: opposition to contingent events, and the notification about that has preceded, or if you wish, say: it is necessary that they be described by attributes of negation other than subsistence in the self; because they are subsistent by His essence, exalted be His majesty. He only alerted here to pre-eternity and permanence because the following argument depends on them.)
Source pages p. 168

Commentary
This passage proves that power and will must be necessary (واجب الوجود), i.e., pre-eternal and permanent. The proof is by reductio: if they were contingent (جائزة), they would themselves be originated (حادثة) and require an agent. That agent can only be God (from the proof of oneness). But then God would need another power and will to originate these attributes, because every contingent event depends on power and will existing prior to it (from the first demand). This leads to an infinite regress of powers and wills, which is impossible. Therefore, the original power and will cannot be contingent; they must be necessary. The footnote adds another reason: these attributes must be pre-eternal and permanent because they are not like contingent things (opposition to contingents), and they are subsistent by God's essence.

Key Terms
وجوب الوجود
قدرة
إرادة
جائزة
حدوث
قدم
بقاء
فاعل
تسلسل
وحدانية
صفات السلوب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the argument proves only the necessity of power and will, but not that they are one.
Correction: The argument here focuses on necessity and pre-eternity, not unity (which was a separate demand).

Checkpoint
Why can't power and will be contingent according to this argument?
A) Because they would then need another power and will, leading to an infinite regress
B) Because contingent things cannot exist
C) Because God cannot create power and will
D) Because they are not created

Key Takeaway
Power and will must be pre-eternal to avoid an infinite regress of contingent attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that God's power and will are pre-eternal and not contingent, how would that change the way you approach your own limited power and will in your daily decisions? What specific area of your life do you need to surrender to God's pre-eternal will?
?
The argument shows that an infinite regress of contingent attributes is impossible. Reflect on a pattern in your life where you are trying to rely on your own contingent efforts without acknowledging God's pre-eternal power. What is one step you can take today to realign?
?
Sanusi uses a reductio ad absurdum to prove pre-eternity. Consider a belief you hold that might be based on a similar logical flaw—what would happen if you followed your assumption to its absurd conclusion? Are you willing to expose your thinking to such scrutiny?

The Argument for Universal Divine Power and Will
Sanusi argues by contradiction for universal divine power and will.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why divine power and will must extend to all possible things, and the three impossible consequences if they were restricted.

Core Idea
If divine power could be limited, it would be no power at all.

Sanusi now delivers a decisive proof that Allah's power and will cannot be restricted to some things but must encompass all possible existence. He uses a reductio ad absurdum: assuming restriction leads to logical impossibility, thereby affirming universality. This argument solidifies the attribute of absolute divine sovereignty.

Guided Path
1
Understand the problem
Consider what it means for divine power to be 'restricted' to only some possible things. Why would anyone think that? Sanusi assumes this for the sake of argument.
This is a hypothetical assumption, not a real belief.
2
Follow the first consequence
If power and will were restricted, then inability (ajz) would become general: the creator would be incapable of creating some things, and since all possibles are equally in need of a creator, He would be incapable of all.
Additionally, the restricted powers themselves would require a specifier, making them created.
3
Identify the second and third consequences
Second: the powers themselves would be created because they would need a creator to specify them. Third: mutual hindrance (tamānuʿ) would arise between the restricted powers and the powers that are attached to what they are not attached to.
Each consequence is impossible for the divine essence.
4
Connect to the bigger picture
Since restriction leads to impossibility, the opposite must be true: divine power and will are universal over all possible things. This preserves divine perfection and unity.
This argument is a cornerstone of Ashʿarī theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجه دلالة الحادثاتِ على عموم التعلقِ للقدرة والإرادة: وأمـا وجـه دلالـة كـل حـادث على عموم التعلقِ لهما لجميعِ الممكنات: فلأنَّهما لو اختصَّتا ببعض الممكناتِ، ووقع العجزُ عن بعضها.. لزم في ذلك أمورٌ مستحيلة - الأول: تعميم العجز في جميع الممكناتِ؛ لاستوائها في حقيقة الإمكانِ المُحوج إلى الفاعل، فإِذا تَعذَّرَ مِنَ الفاعل فعلُ بعضها.. لزمَ تعذُّرُ فعل جميعها، ويلزم أيضاً حدوثهما؛ لافتقـار عـددهـمـا المخصوص إلى مُخصص - الثاني: لزوم حدوثهما؛ لاحتياجهما حينئذ إلى الفاعل الذي خلقهما لبعض الممكناتِ وخلقَ ضدَّهما لبعضها؛ لجواز أنْ يَتعلَّقا الممكناتِ أو بالبعض الذي تَعلَّقَ بهِ العجزُ، فاختصاصهما بجميع حينئذ بما اختصَّتا بهِ يُوجِبُ افتقارهما إلى الفاعل المخصص - الثالثُ: لزوم التمانع بينهما وبينَ القدرة والإرادة اللتين تعلقنا بهما. وإلى بعض هذه اللوازم - وهو الأول منها - أشرنا بقولنا: (إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها)، فالضمير المؤنَّثُ في )بعضها) و )جميعها( يعودُ على الممكنات المفهومة من معنى قوله: (لكل ممكن).
The aspect of indication of every contingent event on the universal scope of power and will for all possible things: because if they were restricted to some possible things, and inability occurred concerning some of them... then impossible matters would follow. First: generalization of inability to all possible things because of their equality in the reality of possibility that necessitates a creator; so if from the creator the act of some is impossible, then the act of all becomes impossible. And it also necessitates their (the power and will) being created due to the specific number requiring a specifier. Second: the necessity of their being created because they would then need the creator who created them for some possible things and created their opposites for others; because it is possible that they be attached to the possible things or to those that inability attaches to; so their specification to what they are specified to necessitates their need for a specifying creator. Third: the necessity of mutual hindrance between them and the power and will that are attached to them. And to some of these consequences—the first of them—we referred by our saying: 'since inability concerning some of them entails inability concerning all of them.' The feminine pronoun in 'some of them' and 'all of them' refers to the possible things understood from the meaning of his saying: 'for every possible thing.'
Source pages p. 169

Commentary
This passage is a proof that divine power (qudra) and will (irada) must extend to all possible things (mumkinat). Sanusi argues by contradiction: assume they are restricted to some possibilities, then three impossible consequences follow. (1) Inability would become general: since all possibilities are equally in need of a creator, if the creator fails to create some, He fails all; also, the specific number of restricted powers would require a specifier, making them created. (2) The powers themselves would be created because they would require a creator who specifies them to some possibilities and their opposites to others. (3) There would be mutual hindrance (tamânu') between the restricted powers and the powers and wills attached to them. Sanusi notes that the first consequence is referenced in a previous statement. The pronouns refer back to 'possible things'.

Key Terms
تعلق (taʿalluq)
عموم (ʿumūm)
اختصاص (ikhtiṣāṣ)
عجز (ʿajz)
تمانع (tamānuʿ)
ممكن (mumkin)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Allah can voluntarily restrict His power to only some things without contradiction.
Correction: Sanusi shows that any restriction leads to impossible consequences, so it is not possible.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, if divine power and will were restricted to some possible things, what is the first impossible consequence?
A) They become necessarily existent
B) Inability becomes general to all possible things
C) They become multiple
D) They become eternal

Key Takeaway
Divine power and will must be universal over all possible things; otherwise, impossibility follows.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there any area of your life where you have mentally restricted God's power, thinking it's too small or too personal for Him to handle? How does Sanusi's argument challenge that hidden doubt?
?
If you truly believed that divine power and will cover every possible thing without exception, how would that change the way you make du'a for seemingly impossible situations?
?
When you face a situation that feels hopeless, does your heart subtly assume that God cannot intervene? What would it take for you to replace that assumption with the universal tawakkul this lesson demands?

Divine Attributes
beginner
4 min
Proving Oneness of Divine Power and Will from Contingent Events
Sanusi proves oneness of power and will via conditional argument with analogy.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi proves that divine power and will must be single by showing that multiplicity would lead to mutual hindrance and inability.

Core Idea
Multiple divine powers would conflict, making God unable to act — impossible.

Sanusi now presents a concise argument for the oneness of divine power and will, drawing on the same logic used to prove the oneness of God. He shows that just as multiple gods would conflict, multiple powers and wills within the Divine would also necessarily hinder one another. This passage is a direct application of the principle of tamânu' (mutual hindrance) to the attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic sentence aloud, then read the translation. Notice the structure: a statement of the proof's focus, then the conditional argument, then the analogy.
2
Understand the conditional argument
Identify the 'if' clause (multiplicity) and the 'then' clause (inability follows). Understand the logical connection: tamânu' (mutual hindrance) is the reason.
3
Relate to the analogy
Sanusi compares this to the argument against multiple gods. Consider how the same reasoning applies to attributes.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
Why must power and will be single? Because otherwise, even the possibility of divergence would lead to impossibility.
This shows the oneness of divine attributes is necessary.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجه دلالة الحادثاتِ على وحدة القدرة والإرادة: وأما وجه دلالةِ كلَّ حادثٍ على وحدتهما: فلأنَّهُ لو وقع التعدُّدُ فيهما لزم العجزُ؛ للزوم التمانع بينَ القدرتين والإرادتين، كما لزم في تعدد الإله
The aspect of indication of contingent events on the oneness of power and will: because if multiplicity occurred in them, inability would follow due to the necessary mutual hindrance between the two powers and the two wills, just as it followed in the multiplicity of gods.
Source pages p. 170

Commentary
This passage is a direct proof for the oneness (wahda) of divine power and will. Sanusi argues that if there were multiple powers and wills, they would inevitably conflict (tamânu'), leading to inability to act, which is impossible for God. This is parallel to the classic argument for the oneness of God: if there were multiple gods, each would want different things, creating conflict. Similarly, multiple divine powers and wills would hinder each other. Therefore, both power and will must be single, not multiple.

Key Terms
وحدة (wahda)
تعدد (ta'addud)
تمّانع (tamânu')
عجز (ajz)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Multiple divine powers could cooperate harmoniously without conflict.
Correction: Sanusi argues that even the possibility of divergence leads to mutual hindrance, making true multiplicity impossible for a perfect being.

Checkpoint
What does Sanusi say would result from multiple divine powers?
A) They would cooperate
B) Mutual hindrance and inability
C) They would be eternal
D) They would be created

Key Takeaway
Divine power and will must be single because multiplicity would necessitate mutual hindrance, leading to inability — an impossibility for Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If Allah's power were not single, you could never be certain that He could answer your prayer — how does knowing that His power is one and unmatched change the way you rely on Him today?
?
Do you ever act as if there are competing powers in your life (like money, status, or people) that can hinder Allah’s decree? What would it take for you to truly internalize that only Allah’s will is effective?
?
The argument shows that any division in divine power leads to impossibility — what practical steps can you take to ensure that you don't divide your ultimate trust between Allah and other things, even in small matters?

Divine Attributes
intermediate
6 min
Three Impossible Consequences of Multiple Divine Powers
Sanusi enumerates three impossible consequences of multiple divine powers and wills.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three logical impossibilities that follow if divine power and will are multiple.

Core Idea
Multiple divine powers lead to universal incapacity, temporal contingency, and mutual opposition.

Sanusi begins by showing that assuming multiple divine powers and wills leads to impossible conclusions. These consequences are drawn from the nature of contingency and the need for a specifier. Understanding these impossibilities strengthens the proof that power and will must be single and united.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on the three consequences
Read the Arabic text carefully, noting the enumeration of three impossible matters (أمور مستحيلة). Then read the translation to grasp the logical flow.
The first consequence is 'تعميم العجز' (generalization of incapacity).
2
Examine each consequence in detail
The passage lists three consequences: (1) generalization of incapacity, (2) temporality of powers due to need for a specifier, (3) mutual opposition between powers and wills. Understand why each is impossible.
The third consequence is similar to the argument against multiple gods.
3
Connect to the overarching proof
Notice that the author references a previous statement ('إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها') to highlight the first consequence. This shows how the creed's formulation already contains this proof.
The feminine pronoun in 'بعضها' refers to possible things (الممكنات).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لزم في ذلك أمورٌ مستحيلة الأول : تعميم العجز في جميع الممكناتِ ؛ لاستوائها في حقيقة الإمكانِ المُحوج إلى الفاعل ، فإِذا تَعذَّرَ مِنَ الفاعل فعلُ بعضها .. لزمَ تعذُّرُ فعل جميعها ، ويلزم أيضاً حدوثهما ؛ لافتقـار عـددهـمـا المخصوص إلى مُخصص الثاني : لزوم حدوثهما ؛ لاحتياجهما حينئذ إلى الفاعل الذي خلقهما لبعض الممكناتِ وخلقَ ضدَّهما لبعضها ؛ لجواز أنْ يَتعلَّقا الممكناتِ أو بالبعض الذي تَعلَّقَ بهِ العجزُ ، فاختصاصهما بجميع حينئذ بما اختصَّتا بهِ يُوجِبُ افتقارهما إلى الفاعل المخصص الثالثُ : لزوم التمانع بينهما وبينَ القدرة والإرادة اللتين تعلقنا بهما . وإلى بعض هذه اللوازم - وهو الأول منها - أشرنا بقولنا : ( إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها ) ، فالضمير المؤنَّثُ في ) بعضها ) و ( جميعها ( يعودُ على الممكنات المفهومة من معنى قوله : ( لكل ممكن ) .
وجه دلالة الحادثاتِ على وحدة القدرة والإرادة ]
وأما وجه دلالةِ كلَّ حادثٍ على وحدتهما : فلأنَّهُ لو وقع التعدُّدُ فيهما لزم العجزُ ؛ للزوم التمانع بينَ القدرتين والإرادتين ، كما لزم في تعدد الإله
فإن قيل : نفرضُ تعدُّدَ كلِّ واحدة منهما بعددِ الممكناتِ ؛ بحيثُ يكون لكل ممكن قدرة وإرادةٌ خاصتانِ بهِ ، فلا تمانع حينَئِذٍ
فالجواب : أنَّه يلزمُ عليهِ دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ؛ إذ عدد الممكنات لا نهاية له ، وأيضاً يلزم عليه الافتقار إلى المخصص ؛ لأنَّ كلَّ قدرة وإرادة حينئذ يجوز أن تتعلَّقا بغير ما تعلقتا به ، فاختصاصهما بما اختصَّتا بهِ يُوجِبُ الافتقار إلى المخصص ، وأيضاً يلزمُ مِنْ عجزهما عن التأثير في غير ما تعلَّقتا به .. العجز عن الجميع ولهذا يصح أن تأخذ مطلبين - وهما الوحدة وعموم التعلقِ - مِنْ قولنا في أصل العقيدة : ( إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها ) ، وتأخذ المطلبين الآخرين - وهما الوجود والوجوب ـ مِنْ قولنا : ( لتوقف كل حادث في وجودِهِ . . . ) إلى آخره ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
There follow from that impossible matters: First: generalization of incapacity in all possible things, because they are equal in the reality of contingency which necessitates an agent; so if the agent is unable to do some of them, then the inability to do all of them follows. And there also follows their temporality, because their specific number requires a specifier. Second: the necessity of their temporality, because their need then for the agent who created them for some possible things and created their opposites for others; because it is possible that they attach to some possible things or to those which incapacity attached to, so their specification at that time to what they were specified for necessitates their need for the specifying agent. Third: the necessity of mutual opposition between them and the power and will which attached to them. And to some of these necessary consequences — which is the first of them — we pointed by our saying: 'Indeed incapacity regarding some of them entails incapacity regarding all of them.' So the feminine pronoun in 'some of them' and 'all of them' refers to the possible things understood from the meaning of his saying: 'for every possible thing.'
[The aspect of the indication of contingent events to the unity of power and will]
And as for the aspect of the indication of every contingent event to their unity: it is that if multiplicity occurred in them, then incapacity would follow due to the necessity of mutual opposition between the two powers and the two wills, as followed in the multiplicity of deity.
If it is said: We suppose a multiplicity of each of them according to the number of possible things, such that for every possible thing there is a power and will specific to it, so then there is no mutual opposition.
The answer: That would entail the entering of what has no end into existence, because the number of possible things has no end. Also, it would entail need for a specifier, because every power and will then could attach to other than what they attached to, so their specification to what they were specified for necessitates need for a specifier. Also, from their incapacity to effect in other than what they attached to, there follows incapacity regarding all. And therefore it is valid to take two demands — which are unity and generality of attachment — from our saying in the original creed: 'Indeed incapacity regarding some of them entails incapacity regarding all of them,' and to take the other two demands — which are existence and necessity — from our saying: 'Because every contingent event depends for its existence...' etc. And with God Almighty is success.
Source pages p. 170

Commentary
This passage is a multi-part proof for the unity of divine power and will. It begins by stating that if power and will were multiple (like with multiple gods), three impossible consequences follow: 1) generalization of incapacity (if one power cannot act on some possible things, then all powers fail on all possible things), 2) temporal contingency (they would need a specifier because their number is specific), and 3) mutual opposition between the powers and wills (they would conflict). The author notes that the first consequence was already referenced in a previous statement about incapacity regarding some entailing incapacity regarding all. Then the author gives the main proof from contingent events: if there were multiple powers/wills, it would lead to incapacity due to mutual opposition, just as in the case of multiple gods. An objection follows: suppose there is a distinct power and will for each possible thing, then no mutual opposition. The answer is threefold: a) this would bring an infinite number into existence (since possible things are infinite), b) each power/will would still need a specifier to attach to its specific object (since they could have attached to others), and c) their inability to affect what they are not assigned to results in general incapacity. Finally, the author explains how from the original statement about incapacity one can extract two points: unity of power/will and their universal scope, and from another statement about contingency one extracts existence and necessity. This shows how the creed's formulations contain multiple proofs.

Key Terms
تعميم العجز
حدوثهما
التمانع
المخصص
الممكنات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: It might be thought that multiple divine powers could coexist by dividing tasks.
Correction: This passage shows that even division leads to generalization of incapacity, because each power fails for some possible things, causing universal failure.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the first impossible consequence of multiple powers and wills?
A) Mutual opposition
B) Generalization of incapacity
C) Temporality of the world
D) Necessity of a specifier

Key Takeaway
The three impossible consequences show that multiple divine powers cannot exist; power and will must be single.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you feel torn between two desires, do you ever act as if there are conflicting powers deciding your fate? How does this lesson on the impossibility of multiple divine wills reshape your trust in Allah's single will?
?
This lesson proves that any form of multiplicity in divine power leads to absurdity. Are there areas in your life where you attribute power or control to beings or systems other than Allah, as if they could independently affect your destiny? What would it take for you to uproot that association?
?
If you truly internalized that Allah's power is single and unlimited, how would your fear of created causes diminish? Can you identify one specific fear you hold today that implicitly assumes another power could overcome Allah's will?

Proof from Contingency and Refutation of an Objection
Sanusi gives the main proof for unity and refutes an objection involving infinite powers.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the main proof for the unity of power and will from contingent events, and how an objection postulating infinite separate powers is refuted.

Core Idea
Every contingent event proves unity; otherwise mutual opposition would follow, and postulating infinite powers leads to further impossibilities.

Sanusi now presents the central argument from contingent events: if power and will were multiple, mutual opposition would result, just as with multiple gods. An objector suggests that having a distinct power for each possible thing avoids conflict. The refutation shows this leads to infinite regress and continued need for a specifier.

Guided Path
1
Read the main proof and objection
Read the passage starting from 'وجه دلالة الحادثات' through the objection and answer. Notice how the proof is analogous to the argument against multiple gods.
The key is 'لو وقع التعدد فيهما لزم العجز'.
2
Analyze the objection's proposal
The objector proposes assigning a distinct power/will to each possible thing, arguing this avoids mutual opposition because each is restricted to its own object.
This is a sophisticated attempt to salvage multiplicity.
3
Examine the three refutations
The author gives three counter-arguments: (1) infinite regress (infinite number of powers entering existence), (2) need for a specifier for each power-will pair, (3) general incapacity because each power cannot affect other objects.
The third refutation mirrors the first consequence from the previous lesson.
4
Connect to the creed's formulation
The author shows how from the original statement about incapacity, one can derive both unity and universal scope of power/will, and from another statement about contingency, one derives existence and necessity.
This demonstrates the richness of the creed's phrases.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لزم في ذلك أمورٌ مستحيلة الأول : تعميم العجز في جميع الممكناتِ ؛ لاستوائها في حقيقة الإمكانِ المُحوج إلى الفاعل ، فإِذا تَعذَّرَ مِنَ الفاعل فعلُ بعضها .. لزمَ تعذُّرُ فعل جميعها ، ويلزم أيضاً حدوثهما ؛ لافتقـار عـددهـمـا المخصوص إلى مُخصص الثاني : لزوم حدوثهما ؛ لاحتياجهما حينئذ إلى الفاعل الذي خلقهما لبعض الممكناتِ وخلقَ ضدَّهما لبعضها ؛ لجواز أنْ يَتعلَّقا الممكناتِ أو بالبعض الذي تَعلَّقَ بهِ العجزُ ، فاختصاصهما بجميع حينئذ بما اختصَّتا بهِ يُوجِبُ افتقارهما إلى الفاعل المخصص الثالثُ : لزوم التمانع بينهما وبينَ القدرة والإرادة اللتين تعلقنا بهما . وإلى بعض هذه اللوازم - وهو الأول منها - أشرنا بقولنا : ( إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها ) ، فالضمير المؤنَّثُ في ) بعضها ) و ( جميعها ( يعودُ على الممكنات المفهومة من معنى قوله : ( لكل ممكن ) .
وجه دلالة الحادثاتِ على وحدة القدرة والإرادة ]
وأما وجه دلالةِ كلَّ حادثٍ على وحدتهما : فلأنَّهُ لو وقع التعدُّدُ فيهما لزم العجزُ ؛ للزوم التمانع بينَ القدرتين والإرادتين ، كما لزم في تعدد الإله
فإن قيل : نفرضُ تعدُّدَ كلِّ واحدة منهما بعددِ الممكناتِ ؛ بحيثُ يكون لكل ممكن قدرة وإرادةٌ خاصتانِ بهِ ، فلا تمانع حينَئِذٍ
فالجواب : أنَّه يلزمُ عليهِ دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ ؛ إذ عدد الممكنات لا نهاية له ، وأيضاً يلزم عليه الافتقار إلى المخصص ؛ لأنَّ كلَّ قدرة وإرادة حينئذ يجوز أن تتعلَّقا بغير ما تعلقتا به ، فاختصاصهما بما اختصَّتا بهِ يُوجِبُ الافتقار إلى المخصص ، وأيضاً يلزمُ مِنْ عجزهما عن التأثير في غير ما تعلَّقتا به .. العجز عن الجميع ولهذا يصح أن تأخذ مطلبين - وهما الوحدة وعموم التعلقِ - مِنْ قولنا في أصل العقيدة : ( إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها ) ، وتأخذ المطلبين الآخرين - وهما الوجود والوجوب ـ مِنْ قولنا : ( لتوقف كل حادث في وجودِهِ . . . ) إلى آخره ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
There follow from that impossible matters: First: generalization of incapacity in all possible things, because they are equal in the reality of contingency which necessitates an agent; so if the agent is unable to do some of them, then the inability to do all of them follows. And there also follows their temporality, because their specific number requires a specifier. Second: the necessity of their temporality, because their need then for the agent who created them for some possible things and created their opposites for others; because it is possible that they attach to some possible things or to those which incapacity attached to, so their specification at that time to what they were specified for necessitates their need for the specifying agent. Third: the necessity of mutual opposition between them and the power and will which attached to them. And to some of these necessary consequences — which is the first of them — we pointed by our saying: 'Indeed incapacity regarding some of them entails incapacity regarding all of them.' So the feminine pronoun in 'some of them' and 'all of them' refers to the possible things understood from the meaning of his saying: 'for every possible thing.'
[The aspect of the indication of contingent events to the unity of power and will]
And as for the aspect of the indication of every contingent event to their unity: it is that if multiplicity occurred in them, then incapacity would follow due to the necessity of mutual opposition between the two powers and the two wills, as followed in the multiplicity of deity.
If it is said: We suppose a multiplicity of each of them according to the number of possible things, such that for every possible thing there is a power and will specific to it, so then there is no mutual opposition.
The answer: That would entail the entering of what has no end into existence, because the number of possible things has no end. Also, it would entail need for a specifier, because every power and will then could attach to other than what they attached to, so their specification to what they were specified for necessitates need for a specifier. Also, from their incapacity to effect in other than what they attached to, there follows incapacity regarding all. And therefore it is valid to take two demands — which are unity and generality of attachment — from our saying in the original creed: 'Indeed incapacity regarding some of them entails incapacity regarding all of them,' and to take the other two demands — which are existence and necessity — from our saying: 'Because every contingent event depends for its existence...' etc. And with God Almighty is success.
Source pages p. 170

Commentary
This passage is a multi-part proof for the unity of divine power and will. It begins by stating that if power and will were multiple (like with multiple gods), three impossible consequences follow: 1) generalization of incapacity (if one power cannot act on some possible things, then all powers fail on all possible things), 2) temporal contingency (they would need a specifier because their number is specific), and 3) mutual opposition between the powers and wills (they would conflict). The author notes that the first consequence was already referenced in a previous statement about incapacity regarding some entailing incapacity regarding all. Then the author gives the main proof from contingent events: if there were multiple powers/wills, it would lead to incapacity due to mutual opposition, just as in the case of multiple gods. An objection follows: suppose there is a distinct power and will for each possible thing, then no mutual opposition. The answer is threefold: a) this would bring an infinite number into existence (since possible things are infinite), b) each power/will would still need a specifier to attach to its specific object (since they could have attached to others), and c) their inability to affect what they are not assigned to results in general incapacity. Finally, the author explains how from the original statement about incapacity one can extract two points: unity of power/will and their universal scope, and from another statement about contingency one extracts existence and necessity. This shows how the creed's formulations contain multiple proofs.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that multiple powers can coexist if each is assigned a specific domain, avoiding conflict.
Correction: The refutation shows that even such assignment leads to infinite regress, need for a specifier, and general incapacity, so it is impossible.

Checkpoint
What is one of the refutations given against the objection that each possible thing has its own power and will?

Key Takeaway

?

?
If every contingent event in your life depends on Allah's will, why do you sometimes act as if you have independent control? How does this lesson's proof of unity challenge the way you plan your future?
?
The refutation shows that claiming distinct powers for each thing leads to a need for a specifier. Where in your life are you trying to be your own specifier, deciding what is good for you without reference to Allah? What step can you take right now to realign with His specification?

Divine Attributes

Why Multiple Powers Lead to Absurdity
Sanusi refutes the objection of separate powers and wills per possible thing.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi argues that assigning a separate power and will for each possible thing leads to infinite regress and other absurd consequences, thus affirming the unity and universality of God's attributes.

Core Idea
Multiple powers and wills for each thing lead to infinite regress, not harmony.

Last lesson, Sanusi established that God's power and will must be one and attach to all things. An objector might say: why not have a separate power and will for each possible thing? That would avoid conflict. Sanusi's answer refutes this by showing three devastating consequences. This passage explains those consequences and how they prove the oneness and universality of God's attributes.

Guided Path
1
Understand the objection
Read the first part: 'For every possible thing there would be a power and a will specific to it, then there would be no conflict.' Recognize that this is a plausible objection to the unity of attributes.
The objector assumes multiplicity avoids conflict.
2
Follow the first consequence (infinite regress)
Sanusi says it would entail an infinite number of powers entering existence because possible things are infinite. Grasp why that is problematic.
Infinite actual existence of powers is impossible.
3
Examine the second consequence (need for a specifier)
Each power and will could attach to anything; why does it attach to its specific object? That requires a specifier, leading to an infinite regress of specifiers.
This defeats the purpose of avoiding multiplicity.
4
Understand the third consequence (inability regarding all)
If each power/will is limited to its own object, then collectively they are unable to affect anything else, implying inability in the essence.
This contradicts absolute power.
5
See how this yields the four attributes
Sanusi shows that from 'inability regarding some entails inability regarding all' we derive unity and universality; from dependence of contingent things we derive existence and necessity.
This ties back to the original creed.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
يكون لكل ممكن قدرة وإرادةٌ خاصتانِ بهِ ، فلا تمانع حينَئِذٍ فالجواب : أنَّه يلزمُ عليهِ دخولُ ما لا نهاية له في الوجودِ (۱) ؛ إذ عدد الممكنات لا نهاية له ، وأيضاً يلزم عليه الافتقار إلى المخصص ؛ لأنَّ كلَّ قدرة وإرادة حينئذ يجوز أن تتعلَّقا بغير ما تعلقتا به ، فاختصاصهما بما اختصَّتا بهِ يُوجِبُ الافتقار إلى المخصص ، وأيضاً يلزمُ مِنْ عجزهما عن التأثير في غير ما تعلَّقتا به .. العجز عن الجميع ولهذا(۳) يصح أن تأخذ مطلبين - وهما الوحدة وعموم التعلقِ - مِنْ قولنا في أصل العقيدة : ( إذ العجز عن بعضها مستلزم للعجز عن جميعها ) ، وتأخذ المطلبين الآخرين - وهما الوجود والوجوب ـ مِنْ قولنا : ( لتوقف كل حادث في وجودِهِ . . . ) إلى آخره ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
It is [said]: For every possible thing there would be a power and a will specific to it, then there would be no conflict. So the answer: that it entails the entry of what has no end into existence (1); because the number of possible things has no end. Also, it entails need for a specifier; because every power and will then could attach to other than what they attached to, so their specification to what they are specific to necessitates the need for a specifier. Also, from their inability to effect in other than what they attached to... there follows inability regarding all. For this reason (3) it is valid to take two requisites—namely unity and universality of attachment—from our statement in the original creed: 'since inability regarding some entails inability regarding all', and you take the other two requisites—namely existence and necessity—from our statement: 'because every contingent thing depends for its existence...' etc. And by Allah Almighty is success.
Source pages p. 171

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer proof. The objection posits that if every possible thing had its own power and will, then no conflict would occur—so perhaps multiplicity is possible. Sanusi answers by showing three consequences: (1) it would entail an infinite number of powers and wills entering existence, since possible things are infinite; (2) each power and will could have attached to something else, so their actual specification needs a specifier (thus infinite regress or circularity); (3) if each power/will cannot act beyond its specific object, then collectively they are unable to act on anything else, which is absurd. From this, Sanusi shows how two of the four required attributes (unity and universality of attachment) are derived from the creedal phrase 'inability regarding some entails inability regarding all', and the other two (existence and necessity) are derived from the dependence of every contingent thing. The passage concludes with a pious phrase.

Key Terms
قدرة
إرادة
تمانع
مخصص
الوحدة
عموم التعلق
الوجود
الوجوب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that multiple powers and wills would avoid conflict, making multiplicity plausible.
Correction: Sanusi shows that multiplicity leads to infinite regress, need for a specifier, and collective inability, which are absurd and contradict divine perfection.

Checkpoint
What is one of the consequences Sanusi mentions if every possible thing had its own power and will?
A) It would avoid all conflict.
B) It would require a specifier for each power and will.
C) It would prove the unity of God directly.
D) It would make the world eternal.

Key Takeaway
By refuting the objection of separate powers and wills, Sanusi establishes that divine power and will must be one and all-encompassing.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face competing desires or wills in your own life, how does this lesson remind you that only one divine will can ultimately bring harmony? Are you truly submitting to that single will, or are you trying to accommodate multiple masters?
?
The argument shows that inability regarding some entails inability regarding all. In your spiritual life, are there areas where you excuse small shortcomings as harmless, not realizing they reveal a deeper incapacity to submit wholly to Allah?
?
If you truly believed that Allah's power and will are one and all-encompassing, how would that change the way you approach your daily decisions and anxieties? What would you need to let go of to live that belief?

The Universality of Divine Knowledge
Sanusi presents the claim and proof for the universality of divine knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's knowledge must encompass all things—necessary, possible, and impossible—and how the proof against specification establishes this.

Core Idea
If God's knowledge were limited, it would be contingent, contradicting divine perfection.

Sanusi now turns to the attribute of divine knowledge, following power and will. He asserts that God's knowledge must cover absolutely everything that can be known—whether necessary, possible, or impossible. The proof is elegant: any restriction would imply that the knowledge attribute is contingent, which would then imply contingency in God Himself. This passage unpacks that argument.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
First read the Arabic passage and its translation to get the flow of Sanusi's argument. Notice the claim and the proof linked by 'because'.
The passage begins with the claim and immediately gives the proof.
2
Identify the proof structure
Sanusi argues that specification (اختصاص) leads to contingency (حدوث). Trace this chain: specification → need for an agent → contingency of the attribute → contingency of God (impossible).
This is a classic kalam proof from impossibility of contingency in God.
3
Connect to earlier attributes
Notice that Sanusi mentions that every contingent thing also indicates the four requisites for knowledge, just as for power and will. This shows the consistency of the proof method.
The four requisites are existence, necessity, unity, and universality.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويجب له تعالى العلم المتعلق بكل واجب وجائز ومستحيل (۱) ؛ لأنَّ الاختصاص بالبعض يستلزم الحدوث ؛ لافتقار الصفة حينئذ إلى الفاعل ، وحدوثها يستلزم حدوثَ موصوفها ؛ لاستحالة تعريه عنها وعن أضدادها • لا شكّ أنَّ كلَّ حادث يدلُّ أيضاً على الأربعة المطالب لهذه الصفة كما سبق في القدرةِ والإرادةِ ، وإِنَّما لم يُقِمْ في أصل العقيدة البرهان على وجودِ هذه الصفةِ ؛ لأنَّه قد سبق لهُ في قوله : ( وفي كونه مراداً على علمِهِ ) أي : فكما تَوقَّفَ وجودُ كلِّ حادث على الإرادةِ لزمَ أَن يَتوقَّفَ على العلم ؛ إذ القصد إلى جائز مُعيَّن مع عدم العلم به .. مستحيل ويُؤخَذُ برهان مطلبِ الوجوبِ لهذه الصفة مما ذكرنا في برهان عموم تعلقها ، وإذا كان اختصاص تعلَّقِها يُوجِبَ لها الحدوث لكونِهِ
And there must belong to Him, exalted, knowledge that encompasses every necessary, possible, and impossible thing (1); because specification to some entails contingency; for the attribute would then need an agent, and its contingency would entail the contingency of its possessor, due to the impossibility of its being devoid of it and its opposites. There is no doubt that every contingent thing also indicates the four requisites for this attribute as previously for power and will. And he only did not set up in the original creed the proof for the existence of this attribute because it had already preceded him in his statement: 'And in His being willed upon His knowledge'—i.e., just as the existence of every contingent thing depended on will, so it must depend on knowledge; for intending a specific possible thing without knowledge of it is impossible. And the proof for the requisite of necessity for this attribute is taken from what we mentioned in the proof of its universality of attachment. And if specification of its attachment necessitates contingency for it, because...
Source pages p. 172

Commentary
This passage is a proof and explanation of the attribute of divine knowledge. Sanusi first states that God's knowledge must encompass everything: necessary, possible, and impossible. The proof: if His knowledge were specific to some things only, it would be contingent, because a limited attribute would need an external agent to specify it, and if the attribute is contingent, then the one described by it (God) would also be contingent, which is impossible. Thus knowledge must be universal. Then Sanusi notes that every contingent thing also yields the same four requisites (existence, necessity, unity, universality) for knowledge as for power and will. He explains why the proof for knowledge was not given in the creed: it is implied by the proof for will, since intending a specific possible thing without knowledge is impossible. Thus the proof for the necessity of knowledge is derived from the proof for the universality of its attachment. The passage ends mid-sentence but the argument is clear.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think God's knowledge is limited to actual things or only to what will happen.
Correction: Sanusi proves that God's knowledge must cover all necessary, possible, and impossible things, because any specification would imply contingency in God.

Checkpoint
Why must God's knowledge encompass all things rather than being specific to some?
A) Because God is all-powerful.
B) Because specification would imply contingency in the attribute and hence in God.
C) Because the creed states it explicitly.
D) Because knowledge is an essential attribute.

Key Takeaway
God's knowledge must be universal; any limitation would make it contingent and thus incompatible with divinity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's knowledge covers not only your past actions but every possible path you could have taken, does that make you feel more accountable or more resigned? How should this shape your daily choices?
?
Sanusi proves that God knows even impossible things. What does it mean for you that God knows the good you could have done but didn't? How does that knowledge confront your excuses for inaction?
?
The proof relies on the impossibility of God being ignorant of anything. In what areas of your life do you act as if God does not know, and how would living in the light of His universal knowledge change that?
