Deriving the Existence of Knowledge from Its Universality
Sanusi explains the derivation of the proof for the existence of divine knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the proof for the existence of divine knowledge is derived from the proof of its universality, and why it was not separately established in the creed.

Core Idea
Intending a specific possible thing without knowledge is impossible, so will implies knowledge.

Having established that divine knowledge must be universal, Sanusi explains why he did not give a separate proof for the existence of this attribute in the original creed. He shows that the proof for the will of God inherently requires knowledge, because one cannot intend a specific possible thing without knowing it. Thus the existence of knowledge is already implied by the proof for will, and the necessity of knowledge follows from the universality proof. This short but important passage ties the attributes together.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic and translation. Note that Sanusi refers back to his earlier statement about will ('and in His being willed upon His knowledge') and explains the implication.
The passage continues directly from the previous lesson.
2
Understand the derivation from will
Sanusi says that just as every contingent thing depends on will, it also depends on knowledge, because intending a specific possible thing without knowledge is impossible. This shows that the proof for will already proves knowledge.
This is a logical implication: will requires knowledge.
3
Connect to the universality proof
Sanusi then says that the proof for the necessity (that knowledge is necessary for God) is taken from the proof of universality. If knowledge were specific, it would be contingent; hence it must be necessary and universal.
The two proofs are intertwined.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

And the proof for the requisite of necessity for this attribute is taken from what we mentioned in the proof of its universality of attachment. And if specification of its attachment necessitates contingency for it, because...

5 min
The Necessity of Universal Divine Knowledge
Sanusi asserts the necessity of divine knowledge's universality and provides a rational proof from the impossibility of specification.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why God's knowledge must encompass all things—necessary, possible, impossible—and how limitation would imply temporal origination.

Core Idea
Partial knowledge needs a cause; God's knowledge is uncaused and universal.

Sanusi begins by stating that God's knowledge must cover every category of existence because any restriction would require an external cause, leading to temporal origination which is impossible for God. This foundational argument establishes divine omniscience as a rational necessity.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation carefully. Notice the strong assertion that knowledge must encompass all three categories.
The categories are: wājib (necessary), jā'iz (possible), mustaḥīl (impossible).
2
Identify the key argument
The passage uses 'specification to some' (ikhtiṣāṣ bil-ba‘ḍ) as the problem. Why does specification entail hudūth?
Because specification requires a cause (fā‘il) to choose it, and that cause would make the attribute originated.
3
Understand the implication
If the attribute (knowledge) is originated, then its subject (God) would also be originated, which is impossible.
This is based on the principle that an attribute cannot be separated from its subject or its opposites.
4
Reflect on the conclusion
Therefore, God's knowledge must be all-encompassing, without any specification.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفته العلم له [بتخانہ وتعلق تھا؟] ويجب له تعالى العلم المتعلق بكل واجب وجائز ومستحيل (١)؛ لأنَّ الاختصاص بالبعض يستلزم الحدوث؛ لافتقار الصفة حينئذ إلى الفاعل، وحدوثها يستلزم حدوثَ موصوفها؛ لاستحالة تعريتها عنها وعن أضدادها • لا شكّ أنَّ كلَّ حادث يدلُّ أيضاً على الأربعة المطالب لهذه الصفة كما سبق في القدرةِ والإرادةِ، وإِنَّما لم يُقِمْ في أصل العقيدة البرهان على وجودِ هذه الصفةِ؛ لأنَّه قد سبق لهُ في قوله: (وفي كونه مراداً على علمِهِ) أي: فكما تَوقَّفَ وجودُ كلِّ حادث على الإرادةِ لزمَ أَن يَتوقَّفَ على العلم؛ إذ القصد إلى جائز مُعيَّن مع عدم العلم به .. مستحيل ويُؤخَذُ برهان مطلبِ الوجوبِ لهذه الصفة مما ذكرنا في برهان عموم تعلقها، وإذا كان اختصاص تعلَّقِها يُوجِبَ لها الحدوث لكونِهِ (١) قال العلامة البيلي في "تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى" (ق ١٥): (قال الكمال ابن أبي شريف في تعريف علمه تعالى: إنه صفة أزلية لها تعلق بالشيء على وجه الإحاطة على ما هو عليه دون سبق خفاء، وهو المرتضى). يستلزم جوازها .. فكيف إذا كانَتْ مِنْ أَوَّلِ مَرَّةٍ جائزة الوجودِ؟! وكذا أيضاً يُؤخَذُ نفى التعدُّدِ مِنْ هذا البرهان؛ لأنَّ التعدُّدَ يُوجِبُ الحدوث؛ لافتقار العددِ الخاص إلى مُحدِثِ وقولنا: (وحدوثها يستلزم حدوث موصوفها) يعني: ويلزمُ الدور أو التسلسل. وأيضاً: خفاء البعض يستلزم خفاء الجميع؛ إذ لا فرق. وقد سبق ذلك كلُّه في القدرة والإرادة. و وقولنا: (وعن أضدادها ( يعني: ولا تكون تلك الأضداد إلا حادثة؛ لأنَّها ضد للعلم الحادثِ؛ فإنْ جاءَ العلم بعدها فدليلُ حدوثها: طريان عدمِها، وما ثبتَ قِدَمُهُ استحال عدمه، وإِنْ جَاءَتْ بعد العلم فحدوثها ظاهر؛ إذ لا معنى للحادثِ إلا وجوده بعد عدم، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
His attribute of knowledge [with ...?] And it is necessary for Him, exalted, knowledge that pertains to every necessary, possible, and impossible thing (1); because specification to some necessitates temporal origination, due to the attribute then needing an agent, and its origination necessitates the origination of its described subject, because it is impossible for it to be stripped of it and its opposites. • There is no doubt that every originated thing also indicates the four demands for this attribute, as preceded in power and will. And he only did not establish the proof for the existence of this attribute in the basis of the creed, because he had previously mentioned in his statement: (And in His being willed upon His knowledge), i.e., just as the existence of every originated thing depends on will, it must also depend on knowledge; because intending a specific possible thing without knowledge of it is impossible. And the proof for the demand of necessity for this attribute is taken from what we mentioned in the proof of the universality of its relation. And if specification of its relation necessitates for it origination because it entails its possibility, then how about if it were from the beginning possible in existence?! (1) Al-Allamah al-Bayli said in his annotations on Sharh Sughra al-Sughra (folio 15): (Al-Kamal ibn Abi Sharif said in the definition of His knowledge, exalted: It is an eternal attribute that has a relation to a thing in the manner of encompassing it as it is, without preceding concealment, and this is the preferred view). And likewise, the negation of multiplicity is taken from this proof; because multiplicity necessitates origination, due to the specific number needing a determiner. And our statement: (and its origination necessitates the origination of its described subject) means: there follows circularity or infinite regress. Also: the hiddenness of some necessitates the hiddenness of all, since there is no difference. And all that has preceded in [the discussion of] power and will. And our statement: (and from its opposites) means: those opposites are only originated; because they are opposite to originated knowledge; if knowledge comes after them, the proof of their origination is the occurrence of their non-existence, and what has been established as eternal is impossible to cease; and if they come after knowledge, their origination is apparent, because the meaning of originated is only existence after non-existence. And with Allah, exalted, is success.
Source pages p. 172

Commentary
This passage is a multi-part argument and refutation regarding the divine attribute of knowledge. Sanusi begins by stating the necessity ('wujub') of God's knowledge encompassing all things—necessary, possible, and impossible—because specification to some would require a cause, implying temporality (huduth). He then notes that every contingent event points to the four demands (existence, necessity, possibility, etc.) as in power and will. He explains why the proof of existence of this attribute was not given in the earlier creedal text: it was already implied in the phrase 'upon His knowledge'. The proof of necessity is derived from the universality of relation: if specification causes temporality, how much more so would outright contingency? He then presents a footnote (by al-Bayli) defining knowledge as an eternal attribute of encompassing without prior concealment. Next, he derives negation of multiplicity from the same proof, as multiplicity requires a determiner. He clarifies that 'origination of attribute necessitates origination of subject' leads to circularity or infinite regress. He adds that hiddenness of some parts implies hiddenness of all. Finally, he discusses the refutation of the possibility of opposites to knowledge, arguing that they must be originated because they are opposite to originated knowledge, and if they come after knowledge their origination is clear. The passage concludes with a prayer for divine success.

علم
واجب
جائز
مستحيل
اختصاص
حدوث

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that God's knowledge is limited to only what exists or to necessary truths.
Correction: Sanusi argues that limitation requires a cause, implying temporality; thus God's knowledge must include impossibilities as well.

Checkpoint
Why does specifying God's knowledge to some things lead to temporal origination?
A) Because knowledge is intrinsically limited.
B) Because specification requires an agent to determine it.
C) Because God cannot know impossibilities.
D) Because knowledge changes.

Key Takeaway
God's knowledge must be all-encompassing because any limitation would require a cause, making it originated, which is impossible for an eternal being.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that God knows every thought, every intention, and every hidden deed — including those you have never told anyone — what would you change about what you do in private today?
?
The proof shows that if God's knowledge were limited, it would imply imperfection. In which area of your life are you still acting as if God's knowledge is limited — that He doesn't see or care? What would it take for you to live consistently with the truth of His universal knowledge?
?
This lesson teaches that God's knowledge encompasses even impossibilities. Does your awe of God reflect this vastness, or have you reduced Him to a manageable scale? How can you expand your heart to embrace His infinite knowledge today?

6 min
Proofs for the Existence and Necessity of Divine Knowledge
Sanusi presents a proof for the existence of divine knowledge from the dependency of contingent events on will, and a proof for its necessity from the universality of its relation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi proves the existence and necessity of divine knowledge through its relation to will and the principle of universality.

Core Idea
Every contingent thing depends on both will and knowledge; limitation in knowledge implies origination.

Sanusi now provides a two-fold proof: first, he shows that every originated thing points to the same four demands as power and will, establishing that knowledge is a necessary attribute. Second, he argues that the proof of necessity is derived from the universality of its relation—if specification causes origination, then outright possibility would also cause origination.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage. Notice how Sanusi connects this proof to earlier discussions on power and will.
He mentions that every originated thing indicates 'the four demands' (al-maṭālib al-arba‘a).
2
Understand the dependency on will
Sanusi says that just as existence depends on will, it also depends on knowledge, because intending a specific possible without knowledge is impossible.
This is the first proof: knowledge is necessary for any intentional act.
3
Examine the proof of necessity
The proof of necessity comes from the universality of relation: if specification (i.e., limiting knowledge to some things) would make it originated, then how much more would it be originated if it were merely possible from the beginning?
This is a fortiori argument.
4
Reflect on the implication
Thus, God's knowledge must be necessary and universal, not contingent or limited.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that God's knowledge could be a contingent (possible) attribute.
Correction: Sanusi shows that even specification implies possibility, and possibility implies origination. Thus, knowledge cannot be possible; it must be necessary.

Checkpoint
What does Sanusi mean by 'the four demands' (الأربعة المطالب)?
A) The four rational categories of knowledge.
B) The four attributes of power, will, knowledge, and life.
C) The four aspects of existence, necessity, possibility, and impossibility.
D) The four proofs of theology.

Key Takeaway
Divine knowledge is both existent and necessary, proven by its connection to will and the impossibility of limitation leading to contingency.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly live as though God's knowledge is unlimited and necessary? In what moments have you acted as if God does not see or know your secret deeds?
?
If God's will depends on His knowledge, what does that say about the precision of His plans for your life? Are you willing to trust even what you cannot understand?
?
The passage shows that even the possibility of limitation in God's knowledge would imply imperfection. How does this truth challenge your assumptions about what you think God should or should not know?

Divine Attributes

Refuting Objections: Multiplicity and Opposites in Divine Knowledge
Sanusi refutes objections that divine knowledge could be multiple or subject to opposites, using arguments from origination and logical consistency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi refutes the possibilities of multiplicity in divine knowledge and of opposites arising to it, using arguments from origination and circularity.

Core Idea
Multiplicity and opposites in divine knowledge would lead to temporal origination or logical contradictions.

Sanusi now addresses potential objections. If someone claims that divine knowledge could be multiple or that its opposites (ignorance) could exist, he shows that both are impossible because they entail hudūth (origination), which is incompatible with God's eternity. He also uses the argument from hiddenness to reinforce the universality of knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic. Sanusi draws from the same proof used for universality to negate multiplicity and other issues.
He references earlier discussions on power and will.
2
Understand the argument against multiplicity
If knowledge were multiple (e.g., many knowledges), the specific number would require a determiner, leading to origination.
He also mentions that hiddenness of some parts implies hiddenness of all, so knowledge must be perfectly manifest.
3
Understand the argument against opposites
The opposites of knowledge (ignorance) can only be originated, because if they are opposite to originated knowledge, they must be originated. Alternatively, if they come after knowledge, their origination is obvious.
Eternal things cannot cease; so if opposites were eternal, they would have to have always existed, which is impossible.
4
Conclude the refutation
Thus, divine knowledge is single, eternal, and without opposite.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفته العلم له [بتخانہ وتعلق تھا؟] ويجب له تعالى العلم المتعلق بكل واجب وجائز ومستحيل (١)؛ لأنَّ الاختصاص بالبعض يستلزم الحدوث؛ لافتقار الصفة حينئذ إلى الفاعل، وحدوثها يستلزم حدوثَ موصوفها؛ لاستحالة تعريتها عنها وعن أضدادها • لا شكّ أنَّ كلَّ حادث يدلُّ أيضاً على الأربعة المطالب لهذه الصفة كما سبق في القدرةِ والإرادةِ، وإِنَّما لم يُقِمْ في أصل العقيدة البرهان على وجودِ هذه الصفةِ؛ لأنَّه قد سبق لهُ في قوله: (وفي كونه مراداً على علمِهِ) أي: فكما تَوقَّفَ وجودُ كلِّ حادث على الإرادةِ لزمَ أَن يَتوقَّفَ على العلم؛ إذ القصد إلى جائز مُعيَّن مع عدم العلم به .. مستحيل ويُؤخَذُ برهان مطلبِ الوجوبِ لهذه الصفة مما ذكرنا في برهان عموم تعلقها، وإذا كان اختصاص تعلَّقِها يُوجِبَ لها الحدوث لكونِهِ (١) قال العلامة البيلي في "تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى" (ق ١٥): (قال الكمال ابن أبي شريف في تعريف علمه تعالى: إنه صفة أزلية لها تعلق بالشيء على وجه الإحاطة على ما هو عليه دون سبق خفاء، وهو المرتضى). يستلزم جوازها .. فكيف إذا كانَتْ مِنْ أَوَّلِ مَرَّةٍ جائزة الوجودِ؟! وكذا أيضاً يُؤخَذُ نفى التعدُّدِ مِنْ هذا البرهان؛ لأنَّ التعدُّدَ يُوجِبُ الحدوث؛ لافتقار العددِ الخاص إلى مُحدِثِ وقولنا: (وحدوثها يستلزم حدوث موصوفها) يعني: ويلزمُ الدور أو التسلسل. وأيضاً: خفاء البعض يستلزم خفاء الجميع؛ إذ لا فرق. وقد سبق ذلك كلُّه في القدرة والإرادة. و وقولنا: (وعن أضدادها ( يعني: ولا تكون تلك الأضداد إلا حادثة؛ لأنَّها ضد للعلم الحادثِ؛ فإنْ جاءَ العلم بعدها فدليلُ حدوثها: طريان عدمِها، وما ثبتَ قِدَمُهُ استحال عدمه، وإِنْ جَاءَتْ بعد العلم فحدوثها ظاهر؛ إذ لا معنى للحادثِ إلا وجوده بعد عدم، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
His attribute of knowledge [with ...?] And it is necessary for Him, exalted, knowledge that pertains to every necessary, possible, and impossible thing (1); because specification to some necessitates temporal origination, due to the attribute then needing an agent, and its origination necessitates the origination of its described subject, because it is impossible for it to be stripped of it and its opposites. • There is no doubt that every originated thing also indicates the four demands for this attribute, as preceded in power and will. And he only did not establish the proof for the existence of this attribute in the basis of the creed, because he had previously mentioned in his statement: (And in His being willed upon His knowledge), i.e., just as the existence of every originated thing depends on will, it must also depend on knowledge; because intending a specific possible thing without knowledge of it is impossible. And the proof for the demand of necessity for this attribute is taken from what we mentioned in the proof of the universality of its relation. And if specification of its relation necessitates for it origination because it entails its possibility, then how about if it were from the beginning possible in existence?! (1) Al-Allamah al-Bayli said in his annotations on Sharh Sughra al-Sughra (folio 15): (Al-Kamal ibn Abi Sharif said in the definition of His knowledge, exalted: It is an eternal attribute that has a relation to a thing in the manner of encompassing it as it is, without preceding concealment, and this is the preferred view). And likewise, the negation of multiplicity is taken from this proof; because multiplicity necessitates origination, due to the specific number needing a determiner. And our statement: (and its origination necessitates the origination of its described subject) means: there follows circularity or infinite regress. Also: the hiddenness of some necessitates the hiddenness of all, since there is no difference. And all that has preceded in [the discussion of] power and will. And our statement: (and from its opposites) means: those opposites are only originated; because they are opposite to originated knowledge; if knowledge comes after them, the proof of their origination is the occurrence of their non-existence, and what has been established as eternal is impossible to cease; and if they come after knowledge, their origination is apparent, because the meaning of originated is only existence after non-existence. And with Allah, exalted, is success.
Source pages p. 172

Commentary
This passage is a multi-part argument and refutation regarding the divine attribute of knowledge. Sanusi begins by stating the necessity ('wujub') of God's knowledge encompassing all things—necessary, possible, and impossible—because specification to some would require a cause, implying temporality (huduth). He then notes that every contingent event points to the four demands (existence, necessity, possibility, etc.) as in power and will. He explains why the proof of existence of this attribute was not given in the earlier creedal text: it was already implied in the phrase 'upon His knowledge'. The proof of necessity is derived from the universality of relation: if specification causes temporality, how much more so would outright contingency? He then presents a footnote (by al-Bayli) defining knowledge as an eternal attribute of encompassing without prior concealment. Next, he derives negation of multiplicity from the same proof, as multiplicity requires a determiner. He clarifies that 'origination of attribute necessitates origination of subject' leads to circularity or infinite regress. He adds that hiddenness of some parts implies hiddenness of all. Finally, he discusses the refutation of the possibility of opposites to knowledge, arguing that they must be originated because they are opposite to originated knowledge, and if they come after knowledge their origination is clear. The passage concludes with a prayer for divine success.

Key Terms
تعدد
أضداد
حدوث
دور
تسلسل
خفاء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that God could have multiple knowledges (e.g., separate knowledge of each thing).
Correction: Sanusi shows that multiplicity would require a cause for the specific number, implying temporality. Also, hiddenness of some would contradict omniscience.

Checkpoint
Why does multiplicity in divine knowledge lead to origination according to Sanusi?
A) Because multiplicity implies imperfection.
B) Because the specific number requires a determiner, which would be a cause.
C) Because knowledge cannot be plural.
D) Because multiplicity would conflict with unity.

Key Takeaway
Divine knowledge is single and without opposite, because multiplicity and opposites would necessitate temporal origination or logical contradictions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You claim that God's knowledge is all-encompassing and that nothing is hidden from Him. But when you sin in secret, do you truly act as though He sees you? The passage argues that hiddenness of some would necessitate hiddenness of all — meaning if even one thought could be hidden, God’s knowledge would be incomplete. How would your private life change if you fully believed that every single thought and action is fully known? What will you do this week to live in that awareness?
?
The lesson refutes the idea that opposites like ignorance could coexist with God's knowledge. Yet, in moments of tragedy or confusion, do you ever doubt that God knows what is truly best? Are there areas of your life where you act as if His knowledge is missing? The next time you feel anxious about a decision, will you consciously reaffirm that His knowledge has no opposite — no ignorance, no oversight?
?
The proof against multiplicity shows that divine knowledge must be single and perfect. Do you ever treat God’s attributes separately in your mind, imagining multiple 'knowledges' for different things? How might this fragmented view weaken your trust in His unified wisdom? Starting today, commit to one specific action that reflects your belief that His knowledge is one, complete, and ever-present.

The Divine Attributes of Hearing, Seeing, and Speech
Sanusi enumerates the attributes of hearing, seeing, speech, and states their proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the nature of Allah's attributes of hearing, seeing, and speech and why their proof is revelation.

Core Idea
God's hearing and seeing encompass all existence; His speech is free from human limitations.

Sanusi now turns to three attributes that are known primarily from revelation. While reason can affirm their possibility, their specific nature and proof come from the transmitted texts. This lesson clarifies what these attributes are not and establishes their scope.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own concise style come through before you interpret.
Notice the garbled heading in brackets—likely a marginal note.
2
Identify the two main parts
The passage first describes hearing and seeing, then speech with a series of negations, and finally states the proof. Identify each part.
3
Understand the proof
Note that Sanusi explicitly says the proof for these three attributes is revelation (al-Shar'), not rational demonstration. Ask yourself why this is significant.
This connects to the role of transmitted knowledge in theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفته السمع والبصر والكلام [ده بنخانه و تعلق تھا؟] ويجب لــه تعـالـى السمع والبصر المتعلقان بجميع الموجوداتِ، والكلام المنزَّه عن الحرف والصوتِ، والتقديم والتأخير، والكلِّ والبعض، والتجدد والسكوت، المتعلق بما تعلَّق بهِ العلم، ودليل هذه الثلاثة الشرعُ.
His attribute of hearing, seeing, and speech [with ...?] And it is necessary for Him, exalted, hearing and seeing that pertain to all existent things, and speech that is transcendent from letters and sound, from priority and posteriority, from entirety and partiality, from renewal and silence, pertaining to that which knowledge pertains to, and the proof for these three is revelation (al-Shar').
Source pages p. 173

Commentary
This passage is a brief statement on the divine attributes of hearing (sam'), seeing (basar), and speech (kalam). Sanusi first asserts that hearing and seeing are necessary for God and relate to all existent things. He then describes speech as transcendent—free from letters, sound, temporal priority/posteriority, partiality/wholeness, renewal, and silence. Speech relates to the same objects as knowledge. Finally, he declares that the proof for these three attributes is solely revelation (al-Shar'), not rational demonstration. This indicates that while reason affirms the possibility or necessity of these attributes, their specific modality and the fact that God possesses them are known through transmitted texts.

شرع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that God's speech involves letters and sounds like human speech.
Correction: Sanusi explicitly states that speech is transcendent (munazzah) from letters and sound.

Checkpoint
What is the proof for God's attributes of hearing, seeing, and speech according to Sanusi?
A) Rational demonstration.
B) Revelation (al-Shar').
C) Consensus of scholars.
D) Logical necessity.

Key Takeaway
God's attributes of hearing, seeing, and speech are known through revelation and have a unique divine nature free from human imperfections.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you speak to God in private, do you imagine Him hearing your words like a human hears? This lesson says His hearing encompasses all existent things—does that make you more cautious about what you say, even in your thoughts? How will you change your inner dialogue today to reflect that truth?
?
God's speech is not like ours: no letters, no sound, no sequence. Yet He speaks to us through revelation. Do you treat the Qur'an—His spoken word—as a human book, or as something utterly beyond your ability to fully grasp? What is one thing you need to adjust in how you listen to its verses?
?
Sanusi says these attributes are proven only by revelation. Do you ever find yourself trying to understand God's nature using only your reason, ignoring what He has revealed? Which attribute of God's—hearing, seeing, or speech—do you most struggle to accept as being beyond human likeness, and what would it cost you to submit to revelation completely?

The Attributes of Hearing, Sight, and Speech Are Proved by Revelation
Sanusi states that hearing, sight, and speech are necessary attributes proven by revelation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the attributes of hearing, sight, and speech are necessary for God and that their proof comes from revelation, not reason alone.

Core Idea
Hearing, sight, and speech are proven by revelation, not reason.

This passage from Sanusi clarifies an important distinction in Islamic theology: while some divine attributes (like power and will) are known through rational inference, others—namely hearing, sight, and speech—are known only through revelation. Sanusi defines each attribute and notes their transcendence beyond created limitations. Let's examine the text closely.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
The passage has a few unclear phrases due to OCR, but the main points are clear.
2
Identify the three attributes
Find the three attributes: hearing, sight, and speech. Notice how each is described. Hearing and sight are said to 'pertain to all existent things.' Speech is described negatively, transcending human limitations.
3
Focus on the description of speech
Sanusi describes speech as transcendent of letters, sounds, time, and other created attributes. This emphasizes that God's speech is unlike human speech.
This guard against anthropomorphism.
4
Note the proof for these attributes
The passage ends by stating that the proof for these three attributes is revelation (al-sharʿu), not reason. This distinguishes them from other attributes.
Think about why rational proof might be insufficient here.
5
Reflect on the significance
Ask yourself: Why does Sanusi make this distinction? What would be the consequence of relying on reason for these attributes?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفته السمع والبصر والكلام ده بنخانه و تعلق تھا ويجب لــه تعـالـى السمع والبصر المتعلقان بجميع الموجوداتِ ، والكلام المنزَّه عن الحرف والصوتِ ، والتقديم والتأخير ، والكلِّ والبعض ، والتجدد والسكوت ، المتعلق بما تعلَّق بهِ العلم ، ودليل هذه الثلاثة الشرعُ .
His attribute (ṣifatu) hearing, sight, and speech [؟] and it is necessary for Him, exalted, hearing and sight which pertain to all existent things, and speech which is transcendent of letters and sounds, and of precedence and delay, and of whole and part, and of renewal and silence, pertaining to that which knowledge pertains to, and the proof of these three is revelation (al-sharʿu).
Source pages p. 173

Commentary
This passage defines three essential attributes of God: hearing (samʿ), sight (baṣar), and speech (kalām). It states that these attributes are necessary for God—they are wajib (obligatory) for His essence. The hearing and sight are described as 'pertaining to all existent things,' meaning God hears and sees everything without limitation. Speech is described as being transcendent of letters, sounds, temporal precedence/delay, whole/part, and renewal/silence—all of which are accidents of created speech. This speech is related to whatever knowledge relates to, meaning it encompasses all things knowable. Crucially, the proof (dalīl) for these three attributes is revelation (al-sharʿ), not pure reason. This distinguishes them from other attributes like power and will, which are known by rational proof.

Key Terms
سمع
بصر
كلام
شرع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's hearing and sight are like human senses, and His speech is audible like human speech.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that God's hearing and sight pertain to all existent things without limitation, and His speech is transcendent of letters, sounds, and all created attributes.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the proof (dalīl) for the attributes of hearing, sight, and speech?
A) Rational inference (al-dalīl al-ʿaqlī)
B) Revelation (al-sharʿ)
C) Both reason and revelation
D) The occurrence of miracles

Key Takeaway
The attributes of hearing, sight, and speech are proven exclusively by revelation, not by rational deduction.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
God sees everything you do, even in private. Is there anything you do that you would be ashamed for Him to see? What will you do about that right now?
?
You affirm that God's speech is not like human speech, but do you treat the Quran as if it is just a human book, or do you approach it as the transcendent word of God? What needs to change in your relationship with the Quran?

Divine Attributes

The Universal Attachment of Divine Hearing and Sight
Sanusi defines the universal attachment of divine hearing and sight and gives a rational proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof for why God's hearing and sight must attach to all existents, and how this differs from created hearing and sight.

Core Idea
God's hearing and sight are universal because limitation would imply contingency.

Sanusi now turns to two specific attributes: hearing and sight. He first defines their scope and then provides a rational argument that their attachment must be universal. This lesson unpacks that argument and clarifies what it does and does not prove.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition
Sanusi states that these attributes attach to all existents. Notice how he contrasts them with created hearing and sight, which are limited to specific types of objects.
The contrast highlights the perfection of divine attributes.
2
Examine the rational proof
The argument: The only factor validating attachment is existence itself. If attachment were limited to only some existents, a particularizer would be needed, making the attributes contingent (originated). Since contingency cannot apply to God, attachment must be universal.
This is a classic kalam proof based on the principle of sufficient reason.
3
Distinguish sources
Sanusi concludes that the existence (thubūt) of these attributes is known from scripture, while the universality of their attachment is known from rational proof. Note that the same applies to the attribute of speech.
This distinguishes between revealed and rational knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقولنا في السمع والبصر : ( المُتعلقانِ بجميع الموجوداتِ ) أي : ينكشفُ لسمعِهِ تعالى وبصره جميع الموجوداتِ ؛ قديمة كانت أو حادثة ، وليس كسمع المخلوقِ الذي يختص عادةً تعلقه بالأصواتِ ، ولا كبصر المخلوقِ الذي إنَّما يَتعلَّق عادةً بالأجسام والألوانِ والأكوان . وبرهانُ عموم التعلَّقِ لسمعه تعالى وبصرِهِ : أنَّ مُصحح تعلقهما إنَّما هو الوجود ، فلو تعلقا ببعض الموجوداتِ دونَ بعض لافتقرا إلى المخصص ، فيكونان حادثين ، وقيام الحوادث بذاتِهِ تعالى مستحيل . والحاصل : أنَّ ثبوت هاتين الصفتينِ أُخِذَ مِنَ الشرع ، وتعلقهما الموجوداتِ أُخِذَ مِنْ دليل العقل ، وكذا ثبوتُ الكلام له تعالى بجميع
And our statement concerning hearing and sight: (the two attached to all existents) means: by His hearing and sight all existents are disclosed, whether ancient or originated; not like the hearing of the creature, whose attachment is usually specific to sounds, nor like the sight of the creature, which is usually attached to bodies, colors, and accidents. And the proof of the universal attachment of His hearing and sight is that the factor rendering their attachment valid is nothing other than existence; for if they were attached to some existents without others, they would need a particularizer, and hence they would be originated, and the indwelling of originated things in His Essence is impossible. The conclusion: that the affirmation of these two attributes is taken from scripture, while their attachment to existents is taken from rational proof, and similarly the affirmation of speech for Him to all
Source pages p. 175

Commentary
This passage is a definition and proof concerning the divine attributes of hearing (samʿ) and sight (baṣar). The author first states that these attributes are attached (mutaʿalliqān) to all existents (jamīʿ al-mawjūdāt). He clarifies that this means that by God's hearing and sight, all things—whether eternal (qadīma) or originated (ḥāditha)—are disclosed. This is unlike created hearing, which is specifically related to sounds, and unlike created sight, which is usually related to bodies, colors, and accidents (akwān). Then he provides a rational proof (burhān) for this universal attachment: the only factor that validates the attachment of these attributes is existence (al-wujud) itself. If they were attached to some existents but not others, they would require a particularizer (mukhaṣṣiṣ), which would make them originated (ḥādithayn), and the indwelling of originated things in God's essence (qiyām al-ḥawādith bi-dhātihi) is impossible. Thus, the attachment must be universal. The passage concludes by distinguishing the source of knowledge: the existence (thubūt) of these two attributes is taken from scripture (al-sharʿ), while the universality of their attachment is taken from rational proof (dalīl al-ʿaql). The same applies to the attribute of speech (kalām).

Key Terms
السمع
البصر
تعلق
الوجود
المخصص
حادثين
قيام الحوادث
ثبوت الصفتين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the existence of divine hearing and sight is proven by reason alone.
Correction: The passage states that the existence of these attributes is taken from scripture, while the universal attachment is proven by reason.

Checkpoint
According to the rational proof given, why must God's hearing and sight be attached to all existents?
A) Because scripture says so.
B) Because if they were limited, they would need a particularizer and be contingent.
C) Because all existents are similar.
D) Because God's essence is unlimited.

Key Takeaway
The universality of God's hearing and sight is rationally proven by the principle that limitation would require a particularizer, which is impossible for God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
God's hearing and sight are attached to every existent, including your secret thoughts and hidden actions. Right now, what is one thing you are glad God sees that others don't? And what is one thing you are ashamed of that you need to repent from right now, because He sees it clearly?
?
The proof says limitation would require a particularizer, making God's attributes contingent. Have you ever mentally limited God's hearing or sight, thinking He doesn't see or hear certain small or private matters? How does that diminish your sense of accountability? What will you change today to live as if He hears and sees everything?
?
The passage emphasizes that God's attributes are not like ours—they aren't restricted to sounds or colors. How often do you imagine God with human-like limitations? Are you willing to let go of that anthropomorphic image and submit to what scripture and reason teach about His transcendence? What is the first step you will take to train your mind to think of God as He really is?

The Universality of God's Hearing and Sight
Sanusi defines the universality of God's hearing and sight and gives the rational proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how reason alone proves that God's hearing and sight relate to all existents, and why they cannot be limited.

Core Idea
God's attributes are universal because existence itself is their basis.

Sanusi begins by defining God's hearing and sight as relating to all existents, then provides a rational proof that if they were limited, they would be temporal. This shows how reason complements scripture in establishing the divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own argument come through before you interpret.
2
Identify the definition
Notice the definition of hearing and sight: they relate to all existents, not limited like creatures' senses. Understand that the passage contrasts divine and created senses.
The key term is 'جميع الموجودات'.
3
Understand the rational proof
The proof states that the factor making relation correct is existence itself. If relation were limited, a particularizer would be needed, making the attribute originated, which is impossible for God.
Follow the conditional argument: if limited → need particularizer → originated → impossible.
4
See the conclusion
The passage concludes that the existence of these attributes is taken from scripture, but their universality from reason. The same applies to speech: existence from scripture, transcendence from reason.
Note the distinction between shar'i and aqli sources.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقولنا في السمع والبصر : ( المتعلقانِ بجميع الموجوداتِ ) أي : ينكشفُ لسمعِهِ تعالى وبصره جميع الموجوداتِ ؛ قديمة كانَتْ أو حادثة ، وليس كسمع المخلوقِ الذي يختص عادةً تعلقه بالأصواتِ ، ولا كبصر المخلوقِ الذي إنَّما يَتعلَّق عادةً بالأجسام والألوانِ والاكوان . وبرهانُ عموم التعلَّقِ لسمعه تعالى وبصرِهِ : : أنَّ مُصحح تعلقهما إنَّما هو الوجود ، فلو تعلقا ببعض الموجوداتِ دونَ بعض .. لافتقرا إلى المخصص ، فيكونان حادثين ، وقيام الحوادث بذاتِهِ تعالى مستحيل . والحاصل : أنَّ ثبوت هاتين الصفتينِ أُخِذَ مِنَ الشرع ، وتعلقهما الموجوداتِ أُخِذَ مِنْ دليل العقل ، وكذا ثبوتُ الكلام له تعالى بجميع أُخِذَ مِنَ الشرع ، وكونُهُ مُنزّهاً عن الحرف والصوت ، والتقديم والتأخير ... إلى آخرِ ما ذُكِرَ .. أُخِذَ مِنَ الدليل العقلي ، فإِنَّهُ لَوِ اتَّصَفَ كلامُهُ تعالى بشيءٍ ممَّا ذُكِرَ .. لزم أن يكون حادثاً ، وحدوث الصفة يُوجِبُ حدوث الموصوفِ
We say regarding hearing and sight: (They both relate to all things that exist) meaning: all existing things are disclosed to His hearing and sight, whether they are eternal or originated. It is not like the hearing of a created being, whose relation is usually restricted to sounds, nor like the sight of a created being, which usually relates to bodies, colors, and accidents. And the proof for the universality of relation of His hearing and sight is: that the factor that makes their relation correct is existence [per se]. So if they related to some existents without others, they would need a particularizer, and then they would be originated. And the occurrence of originated things in His essence is impossible. The conclusion is: that the affirmation of these two attributes is taken from the revealed law, and their relating to all existents is taken from rational proof. Similarly, the affirmation of speech for Him with all [???] is taken from the revealed law, and that He is transcendent beyond letters and sound, beforeness and afterness ... etc. ... is taken from rational proof. For if His speech were characterized by anything mentioned, it would necessarily be originated, and the origination of an attribute necessitates the origination of the one described.
Source pages p. 175

Commentary
This passage first defines the attribute of hearing and sight as relating to all existents, then provides a rational proof: the factor that makes their relation possible is existence itself, so if they were limited they would need a particularizer, implying temporality. It concludes that the existence of these attributes is from scripture, but their universality from reason, and similarly for speech: existence from scripture, transcendence from reason.

Key Terms
سمع
بصر
كلام
دليل عقلي
دليل شرعي
حدوث
تخصيص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the scope of God's attributes is known only from scripture.
Correction: The passage shows that reason proves the universality of God's hearing and sight, while scripture affirms their existence.

Checkpoint
What is the rational proof that God's hearing and sight relate to all existents?
A) Because scripture says so.
B) Because the corrector is existence, so limiting them would require a particularizer and imply temporality.
C) Because they are attributes of perfection.
D) Because God is omnipotent.

Key Takeaway
The universality of God's attributes is proven by reason, while their existence is known from revelation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's hearing and sight encompass every single existent, including every private thought and hidden action of yours, what is one thing you would immediately change knowing that nothing escapes His awareness?
?
The proof here shows that God's attributes cannot be limited. Does your daily life reflect a deep conviction that Allah hears and sees you always, or do you act as if He is unaware at times?
?
Since reason proves the universality of God's hearing and sight, and revelation affirms their existence, how does this double witness affect your trust in God's justice and mercy in your personal struggles?

The Necessity of Life as a Divine Attribute
Sanusi proves life as a necessary divine attribute and clarifies its relation to other attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the attribute of life is necessary for God and how it relates to other eternal attributes through a dependency of concomitance.

Core Idea
Life is the condition for all other divine attributes, yet all are equally eternal.

In this passage, Sanusi proves that life is a necessary attribute of God because the other attributes—power, will, knowledge, hearing, sight, speech—cannot exist without it. He explains why he discusses life after those attributes: the others serve as evidence for life. However, in essence, life is prior as a condition. But this priority is not temporal; all attributes are eternal and coexist in a mutual dependency.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once to get the overall argument. Then read the English translation to ensure comprehension. Notice the flow from claim to proof to clarification.
Pay attention to the footnotes; they clarify key concepts.
2
Identify the main claim and evidence
Sanusi states that life is necessary for God. His evidence is that the previous attributes (power, will, knowledge, hearing, sight, speech) cannot exist without life. Ask yourself: Why is this proof valid?
The attributes listed are those mentioned earlier in the text.
3
Understand the delay in mentioning life
Sanusi says he delayed discussing life until after the other attributes because the other attributes are evidence for life. This is a pedagogical choice: he presents the evidence first, then the indicated (life).
This is called 'delaying the indicated from the indicator'.
4
Distinguish between priority in essence and existence
Although life is a condition for the other attributes, it is not temporally prior. In essence (bi al-dhat), life is prior, but in existence, all attributes are eternal and co-exist. This is called 'dependency of concomitance'.
The footnotes discuss circular dependency and conceptual precedence.
5
Reflect on the eternality of attributes
All attributes of God are eternal (azali). None precedes another in existence. Life, as a condition, coexists with the conditioned attributes eternally. This safeguards against temporal priority.
This reinforces the doctrine of God's immutable and eternal attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفة الحياة له سبحانه ويجبُ له تعالى الحياةُ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الصفاتِ السابقة بدونها مراده بالصفات السابقة: القدرة وما ذُكِرَ بعدها إلى الكلامِ؛ فَإِنَّ كلَّ واحدةٍ مِنْ هذه الصفاتِ يستحيلُ وجودها لغيرِ الحَيَّ، ولهذا أَخَّرَ ذكرَ الحياة إلى هذا الموضع، وهو مِنْ بابِ تأخيرِ المدلول عنِ الدليل وإلا فهي مِنْ جهةِ أنَّها شرطٌ في تلك الصفاتِ.. مُقدَّمة بالذاتِ عليها؛ لتوقف وجودِ المشروطِ على وجودِ شرطِهِ، إلا أنَّ التوقف هنا توقف معيَّة، لا توقف تقدم؛ إذ صفاتُ الباري جلَّ وعلا كلُّها أزليَّةٌ يستحيل تقدُّمُ بعضها على بعض بالوجود، وبالله تعالى التوفيق. (1) عُلّق في (د): (قال المنجور: الدور المعيُّ: هو توقف وجود كل من الأمرين في الخارج أو الذهن على مصاحبته للآخر؛ كما في الجوهر والعرض، وكالأبوة والبنوة؛ الأول للخارجي، والثاني للذهني) (2) عُلقَ في (د): (إنما نبَّه على استحالة التقدُّم بالوجود؛ تحرزاً من التقدم بالتعقل؛ فإنه جائز معرفة أحدهما قبل الآخر)
The attribute of life for Him, Exalted. And life is necessary for Him, Exalted, because the previous attributes cannot exist without it. By the previous attributes he means: power and what was mentioned after it until speech. For every one of these attributes cannot exist for other than the living. For this reason he delayed the mention of life to this position, and this falls under delaying the indicated from the indicator. Otherwise, it (life) is, from the aspect that it is a condition in those attributes, prior in essence to them, because the existence of the conditioned depends on the existence of its condition, except that the dependency here is a dependency of concomitance, not a dependency of precedence; for all the attributes of the Creator, mighty and majestic, are eternal, impossible for some of them to precede others in existence. And with Allah Almighty is success. (1) A note in (D): (Al-Manjur said: Circular dependency is the dependence of the existence of each of two things, externally or mentally, on its accompaniment with the other; like substance and accident, and like fatherhood and sonship; the first is external, the second is mental.) (2) A note in (D): (He only alerted to the impossibility of precedence in existence to avoid precedence in conception; for it is permissible to know one before the other.)
Source pages p. 179

Commentary
This passage is a proof for the necessity of the attribute of life (al-hayat) for God. The author states that life is necessary (wajib) for God because the previously mentioned attributes (power, will, knowledge, hearing, sight, speech) cannot exist without life. He explains that he delayed discussing life until after those attributes because life is a condition (shart) for them, and he wanted to first establish the attributes that serve as evidence (dalil) for life. However, in terms of essence (bi al-dhat), life is prior, since a condition must exist for the conditioned to exist. But this priority is not temporal—it is a 'dependency of concomitance' (tawaqquf mu'ayya), meaning the two exist together eternally. All of God's attributes are eternal (azaliyya); none precedes another in existence. The footnotes clarify the concept of circular dependency (al-dawr al-mu'ayyi) and distinguish between priority in existence (impossible) and priority in conception (possible). Thus the author safeguards against the misunderstanding that life is temporally prior.

Key Terms
الحياة
الصفات
القدرة
الكلام
شرط
أزلي
توقف معيَّة
دور معيُّ
تقدُّم بالوجود
تقدُّم بالتعقل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the attribute of life is temporally prior to other attributes, implying that God was once living but not powerful, etc.
Correction: Life is a condition for the other attributes in essence, but all attributes are eternal and coexist. There is no temporal precedence.

Checkpoint
Why did the author delay the discussion of the attribute of life until after the other attributes like power and will?
A) Because life is less important.
B) Because life is a condition for them, so mentioning life first would be circular.
C) Because the other attributes are evidence for life, and delaying life helps explain that it is a condition.
D) Because life is not an eternal attribute.

Key Takeaway
Life is a necessary and eternal condition for all other divine attributes, coexisting with them without temporal precedence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have a life that conditions your own attributes—your knowledge, power, and speech depend on it. When you use these gifts, do you consciously remember that your life is a trust from the Living God? How should that awareness reshape your intentions today?
?
The lesson teaches that life is prior in essence but not in time. Think of something in your faith that you treat as foundational—like prayer or charity. Is it possible you prioritize it over your relationship with the Living God, as if it existed before Him? What needs to be repositioned in your heart?
?
Sanusi delayed mentioning life until after other attributes, then clarified its priority. Is there a truth about God that you have subordinated, assuming it was secondary? What would it mean for you to now see this truth as the condition for everything else you believe?

The Necessity of Life and Its Relation to Other Attributes
Sanusi explains the necessity of life and its relationship to other attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the attribute of life is necessary for Allah and how it relates to the other attributes as a condition that is co-eternal with them.

Core Idea
Life is the condition for all other attributes, but all are co-eternal.

Sanusi now turns to the attribute of life, explaining its necessity and its unique relationship to the other divine attributes. He clarifies that while life is a necessary condition for attributes like power and knowledge, it is not temporally prior; all attributes are eternal and coexist. This lesson unpacks that subtle point.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without focusing on analysis. Then read the translation to get the overall meaning.
Notice how Sanusi builds his argument step by step.
2
Identify the necessity of life
Sanusi states that life is necessary because the preceding attributes (power, will, knowledge, etc.) cannot exist without it. Reflect on why this is logically required.
3
Understand the delay in mentioning life
Life is mentioned after the other attributes even though it is a condition. Sanusi explains this as delaying the indicated (life) after the evidence (the other attributes). Grasp the pedagogical reason.
4
Grasp the type of dependence
The dependence of the other attributes on life is one of concomitance (tawaqquf mu'iyya), not temporal precedence. This preserves the eternity of all attributes.
Contrast with created things, where conditions often precede in time.
5
Reflect on co-eternality
All divine attributes are eternal and none precedes another in existence. This is a key principle of Islamic theology regarding God's attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفة الحياة له سبحانه ويجبُ لهُ تعالى الحياةُ ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الصفاتِ السابقة بدونها مراده بالصفات السابقة : القدرة وما ذُكِرَ بعدها إلى الكلامِ ؛ فَإِنَّ كلَّ واحدةٍ مِنْ هذه الصفاتِ يستحيلُ وجودها لغيرِ الحَيَّ ، ولهذا أَخَّرَ ذكرَ الحياة إلى هذا الموضع ، وهو مِنْ بابِ تأخيرِ المدلول عنِ الدليل وإلا فهي مِنْ جهةِ أنَّها شرطٌ في تلك الصفاتِ . . مُقدَّمة بالذاتِ عليها ؛ لتوقف وجودِ المشروطِ على وجودِ شرطِهِ ، إلا أنَّ التوقف هنا توقف معيَّة ) ، لا توقف تقدم ؛ إذ صفاتُ الباري جلَّ وعلا كلُّها أزليَّةٌ يستحيل تقدُّمُ بعضها على بعض بالوجودِ (۲) ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق .
The attribute of life belongs to Him, glorified be He. And life is necessary for Him, the Most High, because the existence of the preceding attributes without it is impossible. He means by 'the preceding attributes': power and what is mentioned after it up to speech. For each of these attributes, their existence for other than the Living is impossible. For this reason, he postponed the mention of life to this position. This is of the type of delaying the indicated (al-madlul) after the evidence (al-dalil). Otherwise, from the aspect that it is a condition for those attributes, it is prior by essence to them, due to the dependence of the existence of the conditioned on the existence of its condition. However, the dependence here is a dependence of concomitance (tawaqquf mu'iyya), not a dependence of precedence; since the attributes of the Creator, may He be glorified and exalted, are all eternal, and it is impossible for some of them to precede others in existence. And with Allah, the Most High, is success.
Source pages p. 179

Commentary
This passage defines the necessity of the attribute of life for God and explains its relationship to the other attributes. The author states that life is necessary because the previous attributes—power, will, knowledge, speech, etc.—cannot exist without life. He clarifies why life is mentioned after those attributes: although life is a condition for them and thus prior in essence, it is delayed in mention because it is the evidence (dalil) that indicates them. The relationship is one of mutual dependence (tawaqquf mu'iyya), not temporal precedence, because all divine attributes are co-eternal and cannot precede each other in existence. The author concludes by seeking success from Allah.

Key Terms
الحياة (al-hayat)
الصفات السابقة (al-sifaat al-saabiqah)
توقف معية (tawaqquf mu'iyya)
توقف تقدم (tawaqquf taqaddum)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think life is temporally prior to other attributes, as conditions usually precede in created beings.
Correction: In Allah, life is a condition but not temporally prior; all attributes are co-eternal.

Checkpoint
Why is life mentioned after the other attributes?
A) Because it is less important.
B) Because it is the evidence (dalil) for them.
C) Because it is temporally later.
D) Because it is not necessary.

Key Takeaway
Life is a necessary attribute of Allah that serves as a condition for all other attributes, but it is co-eternal with them, not temporally prior.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just read that Allah's life is a condition for all His other attributes, yet all are co-eternal. In what specific area of your life have you been acting as if God's power were independent of His life? Will you commit to seeing every blessing as flowing from His ever-living presence?
?
The author says life is 'prior by essence' but not temporally. Do you ever mentally rank God's attributes as if some are more fundamental? How will you adjust your thoughts about God to reflect that all attributes are equally eternal and inseparable from His life?
?
Since Allah's life is necessary and co-eternal, His promises are guaranteed by His living, unchanging nature. What is one promise of Allah that you have doubted because you forgot His life? How will you remind yourself of this truth daily?

The Impossible Attributes: Opposites of the Necessary
Sanusi enumerates the impossible opposites of the necessary divine attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi systematically derives the impossible attributes of God as the direct opposites of His necessary attributes.

Core Idea
Every necessary divine attribute has an opposite that is impossible for God.

Sanusi has established the necessary attributes of God. Now he turns to their counterparts: what cannot possibly apply to God. He shows that each necessary attribute logically excludes its opposite. This passage lists these impossibilities in a clear, paired fashion, reinforcing the rational consistency of Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice the pattern: each necessary attribute is followed by its impossible opposite.
The passage is incomplete, ending at the attribute of speech.
2
Identify the structure
The passage begins with a general rule: whatever contradicts the necessary attributes is impossible. Then it lists pairs. Identify each pair and note the logical relationship.
For example, existence is opposed by non-existence, pre-eternity by temporality, etc.
3
Reflect on the method
Ask yourself why this method is important. By showing that each impossible attribute is the direct opposite of a necessary one, Sanusi demonstrates that God's perfection is rationally coherent and not arbitrary.
This method also helps in refuting false conceptions of God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المستقبل في حقه بخانه وأما المستحيلُ في حقِّهِ تعالى : فكل ما ينافي هـذهِ الصفاتِ الواجبة . لا شكَّ أَنَّهُ لما وجبَ لهُ تعالى عقلاً الوجود وما بعدَهُ مِنَ الصفات .. استحال عقلاً عليه كلُّ ما ينافيها ؛ فينافي الوجود العدمُ ، وينافي القدم الحدوث، وينافي البقاء الفناء ، وينافي المخالفة للحوادثِ مماثلتها ، وينافي القيام بالنفس الافتقار إلى المحل أو المخصص ، وينافي الوحدانية وجود التعدُّدِ في الذاتِ أو الصفاتِ أو الأفعال ، وينافي القدرة العامَّةَ العجز العام أو الخاص ، وينافي الإرادة العامَّةَ وجود الأفعالِ أو بعضها معَ الكراهة لوجودها ، وينافي العلم العام الجهل وما في معناه بشيءٍ مِنَ المعلوماتِ ، وينافي السمعَ العام لجميع الموجوداتِ الصَّمَمُ ؛ وهو غَيبةُ شيءٍ مِنَ الموجوداتِ عن سمعه تعالى ، وينافي البصرَ العام كعموم السمع العمى ؛ وهو خَفاءُ شيءٍ مِنَ الموجوداتِ عن بصرِهِ تعالى ، وينافي كلامَهُ تعالى البَكَمُ ؛ وهو خروج شيءٍ مِنَ المعلوماتِ عن دلالة كلامِهِ جل وعلا ، وكونُ
As for what is impossible with respect to Him, the Most High: it is everything that contradicts these necessary attributes. There is no doubt that when existence and what follows it of attributes were rationally necessary for Him, the Most High, then everything that contradicts them is rationally impossible for Him. Thus, non-existence contradicts existence, temporality contradicts pre-eternity, annihilation contradicts perpetuity, similarity to contingent things contradicts otherness, need for a locus or a specifier contradicts self-subsistence, the existence of multiplicity in essence, attributes, or acts contradicts oneness, universal or particular incapacity contradicts universal power, the existence of actions or some of them with aversion to their existence contradicts universal will, ignorance and what is in its meaning regarding anything among known things contradicts universal knowledge, deafness—which is the absence of anything among existent things from His hearing, the Most High—contradicts universal hearing (which encompasses all existent things), blindness—which is the concealment of anything among existent things from His sight, the Most High—contradicts universal sight (similar to the scope of hearing), and muteness—which is the exclusion of anything among known things from the indication of His speech, may He be glorified and exalted—contradicts His speech. And that...
Source pages p. 180

Commentary
This passage enumerates what is impossible for God, i.e., the direct opposites of the necessary attributes. The author first states the general rule: whatever contradicts the necessary attributes is impossible. Then he lists each necessary attribute and its impossible opposite: non-existence for existence, temporality for pre-eternity, annihilation for perpetuity, similarity for otherness, need for self-subsistence, multiplicity for oneness, incapacity for power, aversion for will, ignorance for knowledge, deafness for hearing, blindness for sight, and muteness for speech. The enumeration is systematic, covering the major attributes. The passage cuts off at the mention of speech, likely continuing with more.

Key Terms
المستحيل (al-mustahil)
الصفات الواجبة (al-sifaat al-waajibah)
ينافي (yunaafi)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The impossible attributes are simply a list of what God cannot do, but they are not systematically derived.
Correction: Sanusi shows each impossible attribute is the direct logical opposite of a necessary attribute, forming a coherent system.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is not mentioned as an impossible opposite in this passage?
A) Non-existence
B) Temporality
C) Visibility
D) Incapacity

Key Takeaway
The impossible attributes of God are the direct logical opposites of His necessary attributes, demonstrating the rational coherence of divine perfection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a situation that seems out of control, do you ever doubt that God's power is absolutely perfect and all-encompassing? This lesson shows that any lack of power is impossible for Him. What does your doubt reveal about your understanding of God, and how can you train your heart to hold onto this truth?
?
Have you ever thought that God might not be aware of your smallest struggles or that He is 'deaf' to your pleas? The lesson calls deafness an impossible attribute. What changes in your prayer life when you truly internalize that God's hearing is universal and present?
?
If God's attributes are all necessary and perfect, then His speech (the Quran) is also perfect and complete. Are there verses you have ignored or interpreted away because they challenge your desires? How does the impossibility of muteness (lack of speech) in God compel you to submit to the Quran fully?

5 min
The Ruling on Affirming Additional Perceptions
Sanusi explains his omission of affirming additional perceptions and presents the stance of waqf.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi omits affirming additional perceptions in the creed and the methodological principle of waqf (suspension) in the face of scholarly disagreement.

Core Idea
Sometimes the most learned position is to withhold judgment.

In this passage, Sanusi addresses a subtle point about divine attributes: whether Allah has perceptions beyond sight, hearing, and knowledge. He explains that he deliberately omitted affirming these additional perceptions in his creed due to a lack of consensus among scholars. He then introduces the position of waqf—suspending judgment—as a choice of some verified imams. This lesson clarifies the reasoning behind that omission and the meaning of waqf.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic and English carefully, noting the structure: explanation of what was omitted, reason, and conclusion.
Notice that the passage begins with the heading 'الوقف عن إثبات صفة الإدراك' which sets the theme.
2
Understand the concept of waqf
Focus on the last sentence where Sanusi mentions that some imams chose waqf. Waqf here means suspending judgment, not denying.
Waqf is a methodological tool used when evidence is inconclusive.
3
Reflect on the methodological lesson
Consider why a scholar might choose to suspend judgment on a theological matter rather than affirm or deny. This shows intellectual humility and respect for scholarly disagreement.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الوقف عن إثبات صفة الإدراك وإنَّما سكتنا في العقيدة عن إثباتِ إدراكات زائدة على الصفاتِ السابقة ؛ وهي إدراك المطعوماتِ ، وإدراك المذوقاتِ ، وإدراك المشموماتِ ، وإدراك الملموساتِ ، بإدراكات زائدة على السمع والبصر والعلم ، وتكون عندَ مَنْ أثبتها عامَّةً لكلِّ موجودِ ، مِنْ غيرِ اتصال ولا تأثر بما يلازمها عادةً .. لأجل الخلاف في إثباتِ هذهِ الإدراكات ، والذي اختارَهُ بعضُ الأئمَّةِ المُحقِّقين فيها : الوقفُ .
The suspension regarding affirming the attribute of perception. Indeed, we remained silent in the creed about affirming perceptions additional to the previous attributes: namely, the perception of tasted things, the perception of smelled things, and the perception of touched things, by perceptions additional to hearing, sight, and knowledge. And these, according to those who affirm them, are universal for every existent, without connection or being affected by what is usually concomitant with them, because of the disagreement about affirming these perceptions. And what some of the verified imams chose regarding them is suspension (al-waqf).
Source pages p. 180

Commentary
This passage discusses the attribute of perception (idrak). The author explains that he omitted affirming additional perceptions beyond hearing, sight, and knowledge—specifically the perception of tasted items, smelled items, and touched items. He notes that those who affirm these perceptions consider them universal for every existent, not requiring physical connection or being affected by the object. The reason for omission is the scholarly disagreement on the matter. He then states that some verified imams chose the position of waqf (suspending judgment) regarding these perceptions.

Key Terms
إدراك
وقف
صفة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That suspending judgment (waqf) means denying the attribute or considering it impossible.
Correction: Waqf means withholding judgment due to lack of evidence or disagreement; it does not imply denial. Sanusi explicitly states the disagreement as the reason for omission, not impossibility.

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi omit affirming the five additional perceptions?
A) Because they are impossible.
B) Because of disagreement among scholars.
C) Because they are not mentioned in revelation.
D) Because they are included in knowledge.

Key Takeaway
Sanusi omits affirming additional perceptions due to scholarly disagreement and mentions that some imams adopt waqf (suspension).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a theological issue where scholars genuinely disagree, do you feel pressured to pick a side? How can the principle of waqf free you from that anxiety and allow you to remain humble before Allah's unknowable nature?
?
Think of an area in your life where you rush to affirm or deny something without enough knowledge. Are you willing to say 'I don't know' and suspend judgment, trusting that Allah does not require you to have an answer for everything?
?
The verified imams chose waqf due to disagreement. Is your faith strong enough to tolerate uncertainty on matters that Allah has not clearly revealed? Will you commit to studying without forcing conclusions?

Why Sanusi Omitted the Essential Attributes
Sanusi explains why he omitted the essential attributes and gives two reasons.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi omitted the essential attributes (al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya) and the two scholarly explanations for this omission.

Core Idea
The essential attributes are not separate; they are either entailed by or identical to the attributes of meanings.

Sanusi's concise treatment of divine attributes raises a natural question: why are some attributes not listed? In this brief but significant passage, he addresses the omission of the essential attributes (al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya) and offers two interpretations from different theological frameworks. This lesson unpacks those reasons, clarifying the relationship between essential attributes and the attributes of meanings.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the structure: Sanusi states what he omitted, then gives two reasons introduced by 'إما... وإما'.
2
Identify the two reasons
Find the two reasons Sanusi gives for omitting the essential attributes. The first relates to those who affirm 'states' (ahwal), the second is an alternative view.
These reasons represent different theological positions.
3
Understand key terms
Make sure you understand the terms: الصفات المعنوية (essential attributes), صفات المعاني (attributes of meanings), and الأحوال (states). Look up their definitions if needed.
These terms are central to the discussion.
4
Reflect on the significance
Ask yourself: why does this omission matter? How does it affect our understanding of God's attributes? This passage shows the interconnectedness of divine attribute categories.
5
Take the checkpoint quiz
Test your understanding with the quiz question below. It is based entirely on the passage.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وسكتنا أيضاً عن الصفاتِ المعنويَّةِ ؛ وهي كونه تعالى قادراً وكونه مريداً ... إلى آخره ؛ إما لأنَّها لازمةٌ لصفاتِ المعاني عندَ مَنْ أثبت الأحوال ، وإما لأنَّها عبارة عن وجودها ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق .
And we also remained silent about the essential attributes: namely, His being powerful, His being willing, etc. Either because they are necessary concomitants of the attributes of meanings according to those who affirm states (ahwal), or because they are expressions of their existence. And with God, Most High, is success.
Source pages p. 181

Commentary
This passage explains the omission of the essential attributes (sifat ma'nawiyya), such as God being powerful (qadir) and willing (murid). The author provides two possible reasons: first, for those who affirm states (ahwal), these essential attributes are necessary concomitants (lazima) of the attributes of meanings (sifat al-ma'ani). Second, alternatively, they are merely expressions (ibara) of the existence of those attributes. The author concludes with a prayer for divine success.

Key Terms
صفات معنوية
صفات معاني
أحوال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the essential attributes are separate, independent attributes distinct from the attributes of meanings.
Correction: Sanusi shows that they are either entailed by (lazima) or identical to (ibara an wujudiha) the attributes of meanings. They are not additional attributes.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what are the two possible reasons for omitting the essential attributes?
A) They are impossible or unnecessary.
B) They are either entailed by the attributes of meanings or are expressions of their existence.
C) They are not mentioned in the Quran.
D) They are the same as the five perceptual attributes.

Key Takeaway
Sanusi omitted the essential attributes because they are either necessary concomitants of the attributes of meanings or identical to their existence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever find yourself adding unnecessary descriptions to your understanding of Allah, beyond what revelation and sound reason establish? Sanusi's omission challenges us to be precise. What specific attribute descriptions might you be tempted to add that are not truly warranted? Commit to learning the proper way to describe Allah, as the scholars have done.
?
In your own spiritual life, do you sometimes confuse what is essential with what is merely a consequence? For example, do you mistake feelings or experiences for true knowledge of God? How can this lesson's distinction between essential attributes and their concomitants help you purify your understanding?
?
Sanusi ends with 'And with God is success.' Do you truly rely on Allah for success in understanding His attributes, or do you lean on your own intellect? This lesson shows that even a great scholar ends with humility. How can you incorporate such reliance into your own learning of aqeedah?
