Halting Regarding Additional Perceptions
Sanusi explains the position of halting on additional perceptions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Ash'ari school suspends judgment on affirming additional divine perceptions beyond the five senses and knowledge.

Core Idea
Silence on extra perceptions is reverent caution, not denial.

Sanusi addresses a point of methodological restraint: whether Allah possesses perceptions like tasting, smelling, and touching beyond the already affirmed hearing, sight, and knowledge. Because these are not clearly attested in revelation and scholars differ, the school chooses to halt—neither affirming nor denying. This lesson unpacks that decision and the reasoning behind it.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Identify the additional perceptions
The passage lists four types of additional perceptions: taste, flavor, smell, and touch. Note that these are beyond hearing, sight, and knowledge.
3
Understand the reason for halting
The author states that the school remains silent due to disagreement among scholars, and the chosen position of some verified imams is halting (waqf). Ask yourself why disagreement leads to silence in creed.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الوقف عن إثبات صفة الإدراك ] وإنَّما سكتنا في العقيدة عن إثباتِ إدراكات زائدة على الصفاتِ السابقة ؛ وهي إدراك المطعوماتِ ، وإدراك المذوقاتِ ، وإدراك المشموماتِ ، وإدراك الملموساتِ ، بإدراكات زائدة على السمع والبصر والعلم ، وتكون عندَ مَنْ أثبتها عامَّةً لكلِّ موجودِ ، مِنْ غيرِ اتصال ولا تأثر بما يلازمها عادةً .. لأجل الخلاف في إثباتِ هذهِ الإدراكات ، والذي اختارَهُ بعضُ الأئمَّةِ المُحقِّقين فيها : الوقفُ . الصفات المعنوية اعتبارية تؤكد ثبوت المعاني وتُلازِمُها ] وسكتنا أيضاً عن الصفاتِ المعنويَّةِ ؛ وهي كونه تعالى قادراً وكونه مريداً ... إلى آخره ؛ إما لأنَّها لازمةٌ لصفاتِ المعاني عندَ مَنْ أثبت الأحوال ، وإما لأنَّها عبارة عن وجودها ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق .
Halting regarding proving the attribute of perception. We remained silent in creed about proving additional perceptions beyond the previous attributes: perception of tastes, perception of flavors, perception of smells, perception of touch, by additional perceptions beyond hearing, sight, and knowledge. According to those who prove them, they are general for every existent, without connection or being affected by what normally accompanies them, due to disagreement about proving these perceptions. The choice of some verified scholars regarding them is halting. The moral attributes are relational, confirming the subsistence of meanings and accompanying them. We also remained silent about the moral attributes, which are His being powerful, His being willing, etc.; either because they are necessary for the attributes of meanings according to those who prove states (ahwal), or because they are an expression of their existence. And with Allah Almighty is success.
Source pages p. 181

Commentary
This passage is a statement of methodology regarding two sets of divine attributes: additional perceptions (idrakat) beyond the five senses and knowledge, and the moral attributes (sifat ma'nawiyya). The author begins by mentioning the decision to refrain from affirming extra perceptions such as taste, smell, etc., because these are not explicitly mentioned in revelation and there is disagreement among scholars. Some scholars adopt a position of 'waqf' (halting) – neither affirming nor denying them. The author then turns to the moral attributes, which are relational (i'tibariyya), like being powerful or willing. He notes that the school remains silent about affirming them as separate entities because either they are considered necessary concomitants of the essential attributes (sifat al-ma'ani) for those who accept states (ahwal), or they are simply expressions of the existence of those attributes. The passage follows the order: first present the issue, then state the position of halting, then give the reasons for silence on the moral attributes. The nuance preserved is that this silence is not denial but a methodological choice based on uncertainty or redundancy.

Key Terms
إدراكات
الوقف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That silence or halting about these attributes implies denial.
Correction: In fact, it is a reverent abstention from affirming what is not established by clear evidence.

Checkpoint
Why does the author say the school remains silent about affirming additional perceptions like tasting and smelling?
A) Because they are impossible for God
B) Because of disagreement among scholars, and some choose to halt
C) Because they are not mentioned in the Quran
D) Because they are covered by the attribute of knowledge

Key Takeaway
The Ash'ari school halts on affirming additional divine perceptions due to scholarly disagreement and lack of clear evidence, without denying them.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a theological issue that scholars disagree on, do you feel pressured to take a definitive stance? How does the principle of waqf challenge your desire for certainty and control?
?
Is there an area of your faith where you have asserted more than what is clearly known, perhaps out of pride or fear? Will you commit to halting there until clarity comes?
?
This lesson teaches that silence can be an act of reverence. In your daily life, where might speaking less about Allah's attributes actually draw you closer to Him?

Halting Regarding the Moral Attributes
Sanusi explains the position on moral attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Ash'ari school does not affirm the moral attributes (sifat ma'nawiyya) as separate entities.

Core Idea
Moral attributes are relational and depend on the essential attributes.

The second part of the passage addresses the moral attributes (sifat ma'nawiyya), such as being powerful and willing. Sanusi explains that the school remains silent about affirming them as distinct realities because either they are necessary concomitants of the essential attributes (sifat al-ma'ani) for those who accept states (ahwal), or they are merely expressions of those attributes' existence. This lesson clarifies the reasoning behind this silence.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Identify what moral attributes are
The passage defines them as relational (i'tibariyya) and gives examples: being powerful, being willing, etc. Understand that they are not independent realities.
3
Grasp the two reasons for silence
The author gives two possible reasons: either they are necessary concomitants of the attributes of meanings (for those who accept ahwal), or they are simply expressions of the existence of those attributes. Both reasons explain why they are not affirmed as separate.
This connects to the learning objective.

The Permissible in God's Right: Actions Not Essence
Sanusi defines permissible for God and distinguishes it from necessary essence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what 'permissible' means with respect to God and why it applies only to His actions, not to His essence or essential attributes.

Core Idea
For God, 'permissible' only applies to His actions, not His essence.

After establishing that God's power and will encompass all possibilities, Sanusi now defines what 'permissible' means in God's right. He carefully distinguishes this from any suggestion of change or contingency in God's essence. This passage will clarify a key concept in divine attributes: the difference between attributes of action and attributes of essence.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage slowly, noting its structure. Then read the English translation to grasp the overall meaning.
Pay attention to the opening definition and the examples listed.
2
Identify the definition of 'permissible'
Find the core definition: 'it is the doing of every possible thing or leaving it'. Reflect on why both doing and leaving are included.
This is the first part of the passage.
3
Note the examples given
Sanusi mentions permissibility of creating vision, hearing, reward, and sending messengers. These illustrate the scope of permissible actions.
These examples help ground the abstract definition.
4
Understand the distinction from essence
Read the part where Sanusi says permissibility does not apply to the essence or essential attributes because they are necessary. Grasp the logical necessity behind this.
This is the crucial point: the permissible is from attributes of action, not essence.
5
Trace the argument from premises
Notice how he concludes 'every possible thing is permissible' based on the previously established universality of God's power and will and His oneness.
This argument ties back to earlier lessons about God's attributes.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الجائز في حقه تعالى وأما الجائز في حقِّهِ تعالى : ففعل كل ممكن أو تركه ، صلاحاً كان أو ضدَّهُ ؛ لِمَا عُرِفَ قبلُ مِنْ وجوب عموم قدرته وإرادته تعالى لجميع الممكناتِ ، ويدخل في ذلكَ : جواز خلْقِ الله تعالى الرؤية لذاتِهِ العليَّةِ ، والسمع لكلامِهِ القديم ، والثوابَ في دار النعيم ، والبعثَ لرسله الأكرمين ، صلواتُ اللهِ وسلامه عليهم أجمعينَ الجائزُ مِنْ صفاتِ الأفعالِ ، لا مِنْ صفاتِ الذاتِ لا شكّ أنَّ الجواز لا يَتطرَّقُ للذاتِ العليَّةِ ، ولا لشيءٍ مِنْ صفاتها المرفّعةِ ؛ لوجوب الوجودِ لجميع ذلك ، وإِنَّما مرجع الجواز للتعلُّقِ التنجيزي لقدرته تعالى وإرادته ، وهذا التعلُّقُ ليس بقديم ، ومرجعُهُ إلى صدور الكائناتِ عن قدرتِهِ تعالى وإرادتِهِ ، ولما عرفت فيما سبق عموم تعلَّقِ قدرتِهِ تعالى وإرادتِهِ لجميع الممكنات ، وعرفت وجوب وحدانيته تبارك وتعالى .. عرفت أنَّ كلَّ ممكن فهو جائز أنْ يكونَ بقدرة الله تعالى وإرادته .
The permissible in His right, the Exalted. As for the permissible in His right, the Exalted: it is the doing of every possible thing or leaving it, whether it is good or its opposite, because of what was known previously about the necessity of the generality of His power and will, the Exalted, over all possible things. And included in that is the permissibility of God creating vision for His supreme essence, hearing for His eternal speech, reward in the abode of bliss, and sending messengers, the most honored, may the blessings and peace of God be upon them all. The permissible is from the attributes of actions, not from the attributes of essence. There is no doubt that permissibility does not apply to the supreme essence nor to any of its exalted attributes, because of the necessity of existence for all of that. Rather, the reference of permissibility is to the effective relation of His power and will, and this relation is not eternal, and its reference is to the emanation of existents from His power and will. And since you have known previously the generality of the relation of His power and will to all possible things, and you have known the necessity of His oneness, the Blessed and Exalted, you have known that every possible thing is permissible to be by the power and will of God.
Source pages p. 182

Commentary
This passage is a definition and explanation of what is permissible (ja'iz) with respect to God. The author first states the definition: the permissible is for God to do or leave any possible thing, whether good or evil, because His power and will encompass all possibilities. Examples include creating vision of His essence, enabling hearing of His eternal speech, granting reward, and sending messengers. Then the author clarifies a crucial distinction: permissibility pertains to actions (attributes of action) not to the essence or essential attributes (attributes of essence), because the latter are necessary. Consequently, permissibility is about the temporal relation of God's power and will to creation, not about God Himself. The argument relies on previously established premises: the universality of God's power and will over all possibilities, and the necessity of His oneness. From these, the conclusion follows that every possible thing is permissible by God's power and will. The passage structures the argument: definition and scope, distinction, reasoning, and concluding affirmation.

Key Terms
الجائز
صفات الأفعال
صفات الذات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Thinking that the permissible in God's right implies potential change or contingency in His essence.
Correction: The passage clarifies that permissibility only pertains to actions, not essence, because essence and essential attributes are necessary.

Checkpoint
Why does the author say permissibility does not apply to God's essence or essential attributes?
A) Because they are eternal and necessary
B) Because they are not subject to human understanding
C) Because only actions are possible
D) Because the essence is not an attribute

Key Takeaway
The permissible in God's right refers to His ability to do or leave any possible action, and it concerns His attributes of action, not His essence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's essence is absolutely necessary and unchanging, does that make you feel secure knowing that His nature cannot change or be affected by your actions? Or does it feel distant?
?
You believe God can do any possible action, including creating vision for Himself or sending messengers. Does this belief shape your daily trust in His power over your life, or is it merely a theological concept?
?
The author says the permissible includes 'good or its opposite' from our perspective. Are you willing to accept that God's actions may not always align with your human sense of fairness? How does that challenge your heart's submission?

Refutation of the Obligation of the Best for God
Sanusi refutes the obligation of God to do the best for creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi argues that it is not obligatory for God to create what is most beneficial, and how this refutes the Mu'tazilite position.

Core Idea
The permissible cannot become obligatory without destroying its nature.

Sanusi addresses a crucial theological dispute: is God obligated to create the best possible world for His creatures? Some thinkers, notably the Mu'tazila, argued yes. Sanusi counters that this would overturn the very concept of permissibility and contradict observable reality. In this passage, he presents a crisp logical refutation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then read the literal translation. Try to grasp the overall argument before diving into details.
Notice the footnote marker (1) and its content.
2
Identify key terms
Locate the terms 'al-ṣalāḥ wa al-aṣlaḥ', 'wājib', 'jā'iz', 'imkān', and 'fasād'. Understand their meanings and roles.
These terms are central to the argument.
3
Analyze the logical consequences
Sanusi says that if what is permissible were made obligatory, its nature would be overturned and evil could not exist. Trace this reasoning.
Ask yourself: Why does obligating the best prevent evil?
4
Reflect on the footnote
The footnote reinforces that the permissible is defined by possibility. Consider how this supports the main argument.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ لا يجب على الله تعالى مراعاة الصلاح والأصلح ]
وليس فيه ما هو واجب عقلاً (۱) ؛ كالصلاح والأصلح كما قاله بعضُ مَنْ ضَلَّ ؛ لأنَّه يلزمُ عليهِ قلب حقيقة الصلاح والأصلح الجائزة بأن ترجع واجبةً ، وذلك يمنع وقوع ضدّها ؛ وهو الفساد ، كيف وهو موجود بالمشاهدة ؟!
(۱) فالجائز لا ينفكُ في حقيقته عن الإمكان ، فكيف يصير واجباً بذاته ؟!
[It is not obligatory upon God, Exalted be He, to observe what is beneficial and most beneficial.] And there is not in it what is rationally obligatory (1); like the beneficial and most beneficial as said by some of those who went astray; because it would require overturning the nature of the beneficial and most beneficial which are permissible, making them obligatory, and that prevents the occurrence of their opposite, which is corruption/evil, while it exists by observation! (1) For the permissible is never free in its reality from possibility, so how can it become obligatory by itself?
Source pages p. 183

Commentary
This passage refutes the doctrine that God is obligated to create what is best (al-ṣalāḥ wa al-aṣlaḥ) for creatures. The author states that there is nothing in the category of the permissible that is rationally obligatory, such as the good and the best, as claimed by some misguided groups (likely the Mu'tazila). The argument is that if we consider what is beneficial and most beneficial as obligatory upon God, that would reverse the very nature of these things: they are intrinsically permissible (jā'iz), and being obligatory contradicts their essence. Moreover, if God were required to always do the best, then evil or corruption (fasād) could never occur, but it is plainly observed in the world. The footnote reinforces the point: the permissible always possesses possibility (imkān) in its reality; it cannot become intrinsically obligatory. Thus, the claim is false.

Key Terms
الصلاح والأصلح
الواجب عقلاً
الجائز
الإمكان
الفساد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God's goodness obligates Him to always do what is best for His creatures.
Correction: This confuses moral obligation with the nature of permissibility; the permissible cannot become obligatory without contradiction.

Checkpoint
What reason does Sanusi give to refute that God must create what is most beneficial?
A) Because God is not obligated to do anything
B) Because it would make the permissible obligatory and prevent the existence of evil, which is observed
C) Because the most beneficial is impossible for God to create
D) Because God's will is not general

Key Takeaway
Sanusi refutes the obligation of the best by showing it would negate the essential possibility of the permissible and contradict the observed existence of evil.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face suffering or evil in your life, do you ever blame God or feel He owes you better? How does this lesson challenge that assumption and call you to trust His absolute wisdom?
?
If God is not obligated to do the best for you, does that shake your trust in Him? Or does it deepen your gratitude that every good He gives is a pure gift, not a debt?
?
The permissible actions of God are free, not forced. How does this truth change the way you pray—are you begging a Master who owes you nothing, or delighting in a Generous One who chooses to give?

Refutation of Divine Corporeality and Change
Sanusi refutes corporeality and change in Allah using Ibrahim's verse.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi refutes the attribution of dimensions and change to Allah using a logical argument derived from the Quranic verse of Ibrahim.

Core Idea
Change implies temporality; what is temporal cannot be God.

Sanusi presents a decisive refutation of those who ascribe physical dimensions or change to Allah. He draws on the Quranic account of Prophet Ibrahim, who rejects the worship of celestial bodies because they set—and whatever sets is changeable, hence not eternal. This short argument encapsulates a foundational principle of Islamic theology: Allah is beyond all change and imperfection.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once and then its translation. Notice the strong refutation tone and the logical flow.
The passage builds from a false belief to a Quranic proof to a syllogism.
2
Identify the two false beliefs
The passage refutes two ideas: that Allah has dimensions (ab'ad) and that He undergoes change (tabaddul/taghayyur). Note how they are linked.
Both imply limitation and temporality.
3
Analyze the Quranic citation
The verse from Ibrahim (AS) 'I do not love those that set' is used as the starting point. What does 'setting' imply about change?
Setting is a type of change—a celestial body moves from visibility to invisibility.
4
Trace the logical syllogism
The argument: Everything that sets (aafil) is changeable (mutaghayyir). Everything changeable is not eternal (qadim). Therefore, nothing that sets can be God. Apply this to Allah: He does not set or change.
This is a classic reductio ad absurdum against corporeal conceptions of God.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومَنْ ظَنّ أن الله تعالى له أبعاد تحوي ذاته جل وعزَّ ، وأنه يتّصف بصفات التبدل والتغير .. فهذا جاهل بقوله تعالى حاكياً قول خليله : ﴿ لَا أُحِبُّ الْأَفِلِينَ ﴾ [الأنعام : ٧٦] ، إذ كلُّ آفل متغير ، وكلُّ متغير لا يكون قديماً ، فلا يكون إلهاً !
And whoever supposes that Allah, exalted, has dimensions that encompass His essence, Mighty and Majestic, and that He is described with attributes of change and alteration... then he is ignorant of His saying, exalted, narrating the saying of His intimate friend [Ibrahim]: 'I do not like those that set' [Al-An'am:76]. For everything that sets is changeable, and everything changeable cannot be eternal, so it cannot be God!
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of those who attribute physical dimensions (ab'ad) or attributes of change (tabaddul, taghayyur) to Allah. The author argues that such a person is ignorant of the Quranic verse where Prophet Ibrahim says 'I do not like those that set' (Al-An'am:76), referring to the setting of celestial bodies. The logical argument is: everything that sets (aafil) is changeable (mutaghayyir). Everything changeable cannot be eternal (qadim). Therefore, it cannot be God (ilah). The argument uses a syllogism: major premise—everything changeable is not eternal; minor premise—everything that sets is changeable; conclusion—the setting celestial bodies are not eternal and hence not God. Applied to God: one cannot attribute change to Him, as change implies contingency and thus being created, contradicting His eternality.

Key Terms
الأبعاد
التبدل
التغير
الآفل
المتغير
القديم
الإله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that Allah can be described with physical attributes like dimensions or change.
Correction: The passage shows that change implies temporality, which contradicts Allah's eternal nature. The Quranic example of celestial bodies setting demonstrates that anything subject to change cannot be divine.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why can something that sets (aafil) not be God?
A) Because it is far away
B) Because it is changeable and therefore not eternal
C) Because it is made of fire
D) Because it is too small

Key Takeaway
Attributing change to Allah is inconsistent with His eternality, as shown by the example of setting celestial bodies.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about Allah's actions in your life, do you ever imagine that He 'changes His mind' or goes through emotional shifts? How does Ibrahim's statement that 'I do not like those that set' challenge that assumption? What must you actively remind yourself of when your emotions fluctuate?
?
If you truly believe that Allah is not subject to change, how does that affect your response to trials and changes in your own life? Does your reliance on 'the Unchanging' remain steady, or like the setting celestial bodies, does your faith rise and fall? What one practical change will you make tonight in your supplication to reflect this truth?
?
Consider a time you felt that Allah was 'absent' or 'hidden' from you. Did you, even for a moment, attribute change to Him? How does knowing that change implies temporality give you certainty that He is always present, not subject to the changes of time? What will you do the next time you feel that way?

The Impossible with Respect to God
Sanusi defines al-mustahil as contradiction of necessary attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what Sanusi defines as impossible for God and how it relates to His necessary attributes.

Core Idea
The impossible for God is anything that contradicts His necessary attributes.

Sanusi has established the necessary attributes that belong to God. Now he turns to what cannot be attributed to Him. In this short but foundational passage, he defines divine impossibility in a way that connects directly to those necessary attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the concise definition: 'everything that contradicts these necessary attributes.'
2
Connect to previous attributes
Recall the necessary attributes from the previous lesson (e.g., knowledge, power). The impossible is whatever negates them. For example, ignorance contradicts knowledge, so ignorance is impossible for God.
This shows the logical relationship between wajib and mustahil.
3
Address the common misunderstanding
Some might think 'impossible for God' means logical impossibilities like a square circle. Sanusi's definition is more specific: it is only what contradicts His attributes.
This guards against speculative theology divorced from revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ المستحيلُ في حقه سبحانَهُ ] وأما المستحيلُ في حقِّهِ تعالى : فكل ما ينافي هذه الصفات الواجبة .
[The impossible with respect to Him, Glory be to Him] As for the impossible with respect to Him, Exalted: everything that contradicts these necessary attributes.
Source pages p. 96

Commentary
This passage defines al-mustahil (the impossible) with respect to God. Sanusi states that the impossible is anything that contradicts the necessary attributes (al-sifat al-wajiba) that have been established. For example, ignorance contradicts knowledge, weakness contradicts power, etc. The definition is simple and directly links to the previous section on wajib attributes.

Key Terms
المستحيل
الحق
الصفات الواجبة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Thinking that 'impossible' for God refers to logical impossibilities like a square circle, independent of His attributes.
Correction: Sanusi defines it specifically as contradiction of God's necessary attributes, not just any logical contradiction.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is meant by 'impossible' with respect to God?
A) Whatever is logically contradictory
B) Whatever contradicts His necessary attributes
C) Whatever He does not do
D) Whatever is physically impossible

Key Takeaway
The impossible for God is everything that contradicts His necessary attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever acted as if God could be ignorant of your circumstances? How does Sanusi's definition of the impossible challenge you to trust in God's perfect knowledge, even when you don't see His wisdom? What one situation will you surrender to His knowledge today?
?
Consider a time you doubted God's power. According to this lesson, weakness is impossible for God. Will you repent of that doubt and commit to affirming His absolute power in every challenge you face starting now?
?
Does your life reflect belief that ignorance and weakness are impossible for God? Or do you worry as if He might not know or might not be able? What specific change will you make today to align your heart with this truth?

Divine Transcendence

Refuting Eternal or Causal Origin – The Necessity of a Free Creator
Sanusi refutes the possibility of an eternal or causally necessitated world.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the world cannot be eternal or produced by a necessary cause, and how its observed variation and temporal sequence prove it must be created by a free agent.

Core Idea
A single necessary cause cannot produce variety and delay – only free choice can.

This lesson addresses a critical objection: what if the world is eternal or produced by a necessary cause rather than God's free will? Sanusi presents a powerful argument that such views are contradicted by the obvious diversity and temporal sequence we observe in the world. Let's see how he uses this simple observation to refute both ideas.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection carefully
Start by reading the objection in Arabic and English. Notice the two possibilities it raises: the world could be eternal (no creator) or caused by nature (a necessary cause rather than free choice).
The objector is questioning why free will is necessary.
2
Identify Sanusi's key reasoning
Sanusi argues that if the world were eternal or produced by a single necessary cause, its quantities and attributes would be uniform and simultaneous with the cause. This is a logical principle: one cause produces one uniform effect.
3
Apply the reasoning to observed reality
Now consider the world as we see it: are its quantities and attributes uniform? No – they differ infinitely. Are they all simultaneous? No – many are temporally delayed. This contradicts what would be expected from a single necessary cause.
Think of examples: different shapes, sizes, times of appearance.
4
Draw the conclusion
Since the world shows diversity and temporal delay, it cannot be eternal or from a single necessary cause. Therefore, it must be created by a free agent who chooses among possibilities.
This is a classic kalam argument for God's free will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن قلت : ما المانعُ مِنْ أن يكون ما سوى الله تعالى قديماً ، أو يكون الترجيح لوجودِ مقاديره وصفاته بطريق التعليل أو الطبع لا بطريق الاختيار ؟
فالجواب : أنَّهُ لو كانَ كذلكَ لَمَا اختلفَتْ مقاديره وصفاته ، ولَمَا تأخَّرَ منها شيءٌ عنِ الأَزلِ ؛ لأنَّ العِلَّةَ الواحدة والطبيعة الواحدة يستحيل اختلافُ آثارهما وتأخُرُ شيءٍ منها عن وجودهما ، والمشاهدة الضرورية تقضي بخلافِ ذلكَ ؛ فإنَّ اختلافها في مقاديرها وصفاتها كثير لا حصر لهُ ، وتأخُرَ جميعها عن الأزلِ معلوم على القطع ؛ لمشاهدة التأخُرِ في كثيرٍ مِنَ الأجرام وصفاتها اللازمة لها ، فوجبَ أَنْ يكون جميعها كذلك ؛ لوجوب استوائها في صفة الافتقار إلى الفاعل.
If you say: What prevents that which is other than God from being eternal, or that the preponderance of its quantities and attributes is by way of cause or nature, not by choice?
The answer: That if it were so, its quantities and attributes would not differ, and none of them would be delayed from eternity; because one cause and one nature cannot have differing effects or have any of them delayed from their existence, while necessary observation judges the contrary; for their difference in quantities and attributes is countless, and their delay from eternity is decisively known due to observed delay in many bodies and their necessary attributes, so all of them must be likewise due to their equality in the attribute of need for the agent.
Source pages p. 144

Commentary
This passage presents an objection and its refutation. The objector asks: what prevents the world (other than God) from being eternal, or its existence being necessitated by a natural or causal process rather than free choice? Sanusi answers: if the world were eternal or causally necessitated by a single cause or nature, its quantities and attributes would be uniform and simultaneous with the cause, never changing or being delayed. Yet we observe obvious variation in the world's quantities and attributes (different sizes, shapes, times of appearance) and we see that all originated things are temporally subsequent to eternity. Thus, the world cannot be eternal or produced by a necessary cause; it must be created by a free agent. The key reasoning is that a single necessary cause produces a single uniform effect, but the world shows multiplicity and temporal sequence, so it requires a choice-based agent.

Key Terms
قديم
تعليل
طبع
اختيار
علة واحدة
طبيعة واحدة
آثار
تأخر
مشاهدة ضرورية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Causal necessity can produce variety and delay because of different conditions or intermediate causes.
Correction: Sanusi's argument assumes a single, necessary cause that is unconstrained. If the cause is truly necessary and single, it cannot produce varying effects or temporal delay. Variety and delay require a will that chooses among possibilities.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why cannot the world be eternal or produced by a single cause or nature?
A) Because the world's quantities and attributes are uniform and simultaneous
B) Because the world shows variation and temporal delay, which is impossible from a single necessary cause
C) Because the world is made of atoms that cannot be eternal
D) Because free will is the only way to explain complexity

Key Takeaway
The world's observable variety and temporal sequence prove it is created by a free agent, not a necessary cause.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You rely on causes and natural processes every day. This lesson argues that the world's very existence cannot be explained by cause or nature alone. If you truly believed the world is a product of free choice, how would that change your reliance on causes today? What would you need to surrender?
?
The argument says that a necessary cause cannot produce variety. Look at your own life: do you ever treat God's actions as if they were necessary outcomes, forgetting that He could have created you differently? What does that reveal about your view of God's freedom, and how should you adjust your trust in Him?
?
You observe the timing of events in your life—delays, opportunities. This lesson says that delay from eternity proves creation by a free agent. When you face a delay, do you see it as the result of a free Creator's choice, or as mere accident? What would it mean for you to respond to that delay as a deliberate act of a free God?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
6 min
The Attribute of Being Unlike Created Things (Mukhalafat al-Hawadith)
Sanusi defines and proves the attribute of Allah being unlike all contingent things.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational proof for why Allah must be unlike all contingent things in essence and attributes.

Core Idea
If Allah were like contingent things, He would be contingent — impossible for the Eternal.

Having established that Allah is eternal and everlasting, Sanusi now turns to a necessary consequence: His incomparability. This passage shows that any resemblance to creation would force contingency, which is impossible. The proof proceeds by analyzing what would follow if Allah were similar to contingent things, both in essence and attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read and translate the passage
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the formal structure: a statement of obligation, a conditional (otherwise), an explanation, and a detailed proof.
2
Identify the proof structure
The proof is a conditional argument: if Allah were similar to contingent things, He would be contingent. But He is eternal, so similarity is impossible. Trace how Sanusi splits this into two parts: essence and attributes.
3
Study the list of impossible attributes
Sanusi lists corporeality, accidentality, dimensions, directions, etc. Reflect on why each of these entails contingency or temporality.
These are necessary concomitants of contingent things.
4
Understand the attribute-based proof
If Allah's attributes resembled contingent attributes, they would be contingent. Since His essence cannot be without attributes, the essence would then be contingent as well. This is a key subtlety.
The principle: whatever necessarily accompanies a contingent thing is itself contingent.
5
Grasp the conclusion
The premise of similarity leads to an impossibility (contingency for the Eternal). Therefore, the premise is false, and the opposite — dissimilarity — is established.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
صفة المخالفة للحوادث له سبحانه ويجب أن يكون تعالى مخالفاً في ذاتِهِ وصفاته لكلّ ما سواهُ مِنَ الحوادثِ (۱) ، وإلا كان حادثاً مثلها يعني : أَنَّهُ لمَّا تَقرَّرَ بالبرهان القطعي فيما سبق وجوبُ القِدَمِ والبقاء له تعالى .. لزم أن تكونَ ذاتُهُ العليّه وصفاته المرقعة ليستا من جنس الحوادثِ ، فيستحيلُ على ذاتِهِ وصفاتِهِ الجِرْمِيَّةُ والعَرَضيَّةُ وكلُّ لازم من لوازمهما المقتضية للحدوثِ ؛ كالمقادير ، والجهات ، والأزمنة ، والقربِ والبعدِ بالمسافة ، والصَّغَرِ والكِبَرِ ، والمماسة ، والحركة والسكون ؛ إذ لوِ اتَّصفَتْ ذاتُهُ العليَّة أو صفاته المرفّعةُ بمماثلة الحوادثِ .. لزم أن يكون حادثاً أمَّا لزوم حدوثِهِ في مماثلة ذاتِهِ للحوادث : فظاهر وأما لزوم حدوثِهِ في مماثلة صفاتِهِ للحوادثِ : فلأنَّهُ لمَّا لزمَ حينئذ أن تكونَ صفاته حادثة ، والذاتُ يستحيلُ عُرُوها عَنِ الصفاتِ .. لزمَ أن تكونَ الذاتُ حادثةً مثلَ صفاتها ؛ لأنَّ ما لازم الحادث حادث ضرورةً ، وهذا معنى قولنا : ( وإلا كان حادثاً مثلها ) (۱) ؛ أي : إن لم يكن مخالفاً بل كان مماثلاً ، وإذا كان مماثلاً لها كان حادثاً مثلها ؛ لأن ما ثبت لأحد المثلين ثبت للآخر ، وحدوثه مستحيل ؛ لما سبق من وجوب قدمه وبقائه ، فبطل ملزومه ؛ وهو كونه مماثلاً ، وثبت المطلوب ؛ وهو كونه مخالفاً للحوادث
The attribute of being unlike created things belongs to Him, Glorified be He. And it is necessary that the Exalted be different in His essence and His attributes from everything other than Him of contingent things, otherwise He would be contingent like them. Meaning: When it was established by decisive proof previously the necessity of eternality and everlastingness for the Exalted, it followed that His exalted essence and His elevated attributes are not of the genus of contingent things. Therefore, corporeality, accidentality, and every necessary concomitant of them that entails contingency are impossible for His essence and His attributes, such as dimensions, directions, times, proximity and distance by space, smallness and bigness, contact, motion, and rest. For if His exalted essence or His elevated attributes were characterized by resemblance to contingent things, it would follow that He is contingent. As for the necessity of His contingency in the resemblance of His essence to contingent things, it is apparent. And as for the necessity of His contingency in the resemblance of His attributes to contingent things: because when it then follows that His attributes would be contingent, and the essence cannot be devoid of attributes, it follows that the essence would be contingent like its attributes; because whatever necessarily accompanies a contingent thing is necessarily contingent. This is the meaning of our saying: 'otherwise He would be contingent like them'; i.e., if He were not different but rather similar, and if He were similar to them, He would be contingent like them; because whatever is established for one of two similars is established for the other, and His contingency is impossible due to what preceded of the necessity of His eternality and everlastingness. Thus, its necessary condition is invalidated—namely, His being similar—and the desired conclusion is established—namely, His being unlike contingent things.
Source pages p. 151

Commentary
This passage defines and proves the attribute of mukhalafat al-hawadith (being unlike created things). It begins by stating the obligation: Allah must be different in His essence and attributes from all contingent things. The reason given is that if He were similar, He would be contingent like them. The explanation (يعني) expands: since it has been decisively proven that Allah is eternal and everlasting, His essence and attributes cannot be of the same genus as contingent things. Therefore, it is impossible for Him to possess corporeality, accidentality, or any necessary concomitants that entail contingency, such as dimensions, directions, times, spatial proximity/distance, smallness/largeness, contact, motion, and rest. If His essence or attributes were to resemble contingent things, He would be contingent. The proof is then split into two parts: (1) The contingency resulting from similitude of essence is obvious. (2) The contingency resulting from similitude of attributes: if His attributes were contingent (because they resemble contingent attributes), then His essence would also necessarily be contingent because the essence cannot be devoid of attributes, and whatever is necessarily concomitant with a contingent thing is itself contingent. This is the meaning of the statement 'otherwise He would be contingent like them.' The conclusion is that if He were not different but similar, He would be contingent, which is impossible given His necessary existence. Therefore, the premise (that He is similar) is false, and the desired conclusion (that He is unlike contingent things) is established.

Key Terms
مخالفة الحوادث
المماثلة
الحادث
القديم
الجوهر
العرض
اللوازم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Being 'unlike' means Allah has a positive attribute of opposition to creation.
Correction: It actually means negation of similarity — that He is not like anything. It is a negative attribute.

Checkpoint
According to the proof, why does similitude in attributes entail contingency of the essence?
A) Because the essence is necessarily devoid of attributes.
B) Because attributes are contingent, and whatever necessarily accompanies a contingent thing is itself contingent.
C) Because both essence and attributes must be eternal.
D) Because similitude only applies to essence, not attributes.

Key Takeaway
Allah's eternality necessitates that He is completely unlike all contingent things in both essence and attributes.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever catch yourself imagining Allah in a form or with attributes that resemble something you know? What does this lesson demand you to reject in your own mental image of Allah, and what concrete change will you make in your thoughts about Him starting today?
?
If you truly believed that Allah is unlike anything you have ever seen or conceived, how would that transform the way you approach Him in prayer and supplication? What specific habit will you adopt to remind yourself of His incomparability?
?
This passage proves that any similarity to creation would make Allah contingent, which is impossible. Is there any area of your belief or speech where you have unconsciously compared Allah to created beings (e.g., saying 'Allah is like a father' or imagining Him in a direction)? What steps will you take tonight to purify your 'aqeedah from such subtle anthropomorphism?

Two False Conclusions from Corrupt Analogies: Anthropomorphism and Atheism
Sanusi identifies two false conclusions from corrupt analogies.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how a single flawed analogy can lead to two opposite theological errors: anthropomorphism and atheism.

Core Idea
A faulty analogy can lead to both extreme anthropomorphism and outright atheism.

This passage from Sanusi warns against drawing conclusions about Allah based on imperfect analogies with created things. He shows that the same corrupt reasoning can lead to two opposite errors: some end up attributing a body to God, while others deny His existence altogether. Understanding this helps us appreciate the importance of sound analogical reasoning in theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation to understand the two errors.
Notice the common premise about agents in the contingent world.
2
Identify the common premise
Both errors start from the observation that agents in the created world are bodies.
3
Trace the two conclusions
See how one group concludes God is a body, the other denies God because a body cannot create.
These are opposite conclusions from the same premise.
4
Reflect on the warning
Understand why using a valid commonality (jami') is crucial in analogy.
Without it, reasoning becomes corrupt.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ما تقرر في الحوادث، والقياس عليها بغير جامع، فاعتقد بهذا النظر الفاسد أن الذات العلية جسم؛ لما قام البرهان القطعي أنه تعالى ذات موصوف بالصفات، لا صفة.
وقد قال أيضاً بمقتضى هذا الوهم الفاسد في هذه القضية المجسمة؛ كالحشوية واليهود ومن تبعهم على ذلك، ومنهم من اعتقد هذه المقدمة الباطلة، وقادته إلى التعطيل ونفي وجود الإله أصلاً، وأن العوالم وجدت وجوداً اتفاقياً بغير فاعل؛ لأنه لما استقر في الحوادث أن الفاعل منها لا يكون إلا جسماً، فقاس من غير جامع وقال: لو كان للعوالم فاعل لوجب أن يكون جسماً، لكن الجسم يستحيل منه إيجاد الأجرام وكثير من الصفات، فتعين أن أجسام العوالم وجدت بلا فاعل.
What was established regarding contingent things, and analogizing [God] to them without a valid commonality, led one to believe through this corrupt reasoning that the sublime Essence is a body, even though definitive proof has established that He, exalted, is an Essence qualified with attributes, not an attribute.
And [some] also said, in accordance with this corrupt illusion in this anthropomorphist claim—like the Hashwiyya, the Jews, and those who follow them in that—and among them are those who believed this false premise, and it led them to ta'til (denial of attributes) and the denial of the existence of God altogether, and that the worlds came into existence accidentally without an agent. Because when it was established among contingent things that the agent among them is only a body, he analogized without a valid commonality and said: If the worlds had an agent, it would have to be a body. But it is impossible for a body to create other bodies and many attributes. Therefore, it necessarily follows that the bodies of the worlds came into existence without an agent.
Source pages p. 156

Commentary
This passage identifies two false conclusions arising from a corrupt analogy. The author explains that some people, by analogizing God to contingent things without a valid commonality (jami'), fell into two opposite errors. First, the anthropomorphists (mujassima) concluded that God is a body because every agent in the contingent world is a body. Second, another group, based on the same premise, concluded that no agent exists because if God were a body He could not create, so they denied God's existence altogether (ta'til). These are two extremes resulting from the same flawed reasoning.

Key Terms
قياس فاسد
تجسيم
تعطيل
جامع
الذات العلية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: False analogies only lead to one kind of error, like anthropomorphism.
Correction: This passage shows that the same flawed reasoning can lead to two opposite conclusions: anthropomorphism and atheism.

Checkpoint
What are the two false conclusions mentioned in this passage?
A) Tajsim (anthropomorphism) and ta'til (denial of God)
B) Pantheism and polytheism
C) Skepticism and agnosticism
D) Determinism and libertarianism

Key Takeaway
A single flawed analogy can produce contradictory theological errors, underscoring the need for sound reasoning (qiyas sahih) with a valid commonality.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The corrupt analogy led some to deny God's existence entirely. Where in your life have you encountered an argument that, if followed to its end, could erode your faith? What was the faulty step?
?
This passage shows that a wrong method can produce opposite errors. What personal assumptions about God might you be holding without realizing they come from a flawed comparison with creation?
?
If you truly believed that God is an agent without being a body, how would that change the way you think about your own actions and their relationship to divine power?

Refuting the Premise That Non-Corporeal Equals Attribute
Sanusi refutes the first false premise of the illusion.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi refutes the false premise that anything non-corporeal must be an attribute of another.

Core Idea
Not being a body does not make something an attribute.

Sanusi now dismantles the first false premise that the human illusion accepts: that whatever is not a body must be an attribute existing in something else. He shows this is wrong because God, who is not a body, is a self-subsistent essence with attributes, not an attribute itself. Let's see how he does it.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Note the key terms: 'jirm' (body) and 'sifa' (attribute).
The passage is short; read it slowly.
2
Identify the false premise
The passage mentions 'al-wahm' (illusion) and the false premise: 'everything not a body is an attribute of another.' Why is this an illusion?
The illusion leads to thinking God must be either a body or an attribute.
3
See how God disproves the premise
Sanusi uses God as the perfect counterexample: God is not a body, yet He is not an attribute; He is a self-subsistent essence qualified with attributes. This refutes the premise.
This connects to God's attribute of self-subsistence (al-qiyam bi al-nafs).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فالجزء الأول أبطل المقدمة الأولى؛ وهي اعتقاد الوهم: أن كل ما ليس بجرم فهو صفة لغيره؛ فإن مولانا جل وعز ليس بجرم، وهو مع ذلك ذات موصوف بالصفات، ويستحيل أن يكون صفة لغيره.
Thus the first part refuted the first premise—which is the illusion's belief that everything that is not a body is an attribute of another. For our Lord, mighty and majestic, is not a body, yet He is, with that, an Essence qualified with attributes, and it is impossible that He be an attribute of another.
Source pages p. 157

Commentary
This passage shows how the first component of self-subsistence—independence from a substrate—refutes the first false premise. The false premise claimed that whatever is not a body must be an attribute (accident) existing in another substance. But God is not a body, yet He is not an attribute; He is a self-subsisting Essence qualified with attributes. Therefore, the premise is false.

Key Terms
الجزء الأول
المقدمة الأولى
جرم
صفة
ذات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That if God is not a body, He must be an attribute (accident) existing in another.
Correction: God is not a body, but He is a self-subsistent essence with attributes, not an attribute of another. Self-subsistence excludes both being a body and being an accident.

Checkpoint
What false belief does the first part of al-qiyam bi al-nafs refute?
A) That everything non-corporeal is an attribute of another
B) That God is a body
C) That God has no attributes
D) That the universe is eternal

Key Takeaway
Not being a body does not force something to be an attribute; God is the ultimate proof that a non-corporeal being can be a self-subsistent essence.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

?
If God were an attribute of something else, He would be dependent and limited. What does it mean for your life that He is completely self-sufficient, having no need of anything?
?
The illusion's premise seems plausible at first glance. What false assumptions about God's nature might you still hold without realizing it? Ask Allah to purify your belief and guide you to certainty.

The Two Parts of Self-Subsistence (al-Qiyam bi al-Nafs)
Sanusi enumerates the two parts of al-qiyam bi al-nafs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two components of self-subsistence and how each one distinguishes Allah from attributes and from contingent essences.

Core Idea
Self-subsistence means that Allah is neither an attribute nor a body.

In this passage, Sanusi breaks down the meaning of al-qiyam bi al-nafs (self-subsistence) into two distinct parts. The first part ensures that Allah is not an attribute subsisting in another. The second part ensures that He is not a body or similar to any contingent being. Understanding these parts is crucial for preserving His absolute uniqueness.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Identify the two parts
The passage presents two parts of self-subsistence. Find them and notice how each part is argued.
Look for the phrases that begin the first and second parts.
3
Understand the first part
The first part uses the premise that anything not a body is an attribute of another. Since Allah is not a body but is an essence with attributes, He cannot be an attribute.
4
Understand the second part
The second part refutes the false belief that every essence with attributes must be a body. Allah is with attributes yet not a body, and contingent essences, though self-subsistent, need an Agent.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ما ليسَ بجِرْمِ فهو صفةٌ لغيرِهِ ؛ فإنَّ مولانا جل وعزَّ ليسَ بجِرْمِ ، وهو مع ذلكَ ذات موصوف بالصفاتِ ، ويستحيل أن يكون صفة لغيره . وعلا هو ذات والجزء الثاني أبطل المقدمة الثانية ؛ وهي اعتقاد الوهم : أَنَّ كلَّ ذات موصوفة بالصفاتِ فهي جِرْمٌ ؛ فإن مولانا جلَّ وعلا موصوف بالصفاتِ ، وهو مع ذلك يستحيل أن يكونَ جِرْماً أو مماثلاً لشيء مِنَ الحوادثِ ، فهو تعالى ذات حقيقةٌ ، ولا مثلَ لهُ مِنَ الذواتِ ، وبهذا الجزء باينَتْ ذاتُهُ تعالى سائر الذواتِ الحادثة ؛ فإنَّها وإنْ كانَتْ غنيَّةً عن المحل - أي : لا تكون صفةً قائمةً بغيرها ـ فهي مُفتقرةٌ إلى الفاعل افتقاراً لازماً لا يمكن انفكاكها عنه ضرورةً؛ لوجوبِ حدوثها ، وافتقارها إلى المولى الكريم جل وعلا ابتداءً ودواماً (١) والحاصل : أنَّ بالجزء الأولِ مِنْ معنى القيام بالنفس باين جلَّ وعلا الصفاتِ ؛ فليسَ مِنْ جنسِها ، وبالجزء الثاني باين تبارك وتعالى سائر الذواتِ ؛ فلا شبيه له منها ، ولا يشاركها في أجناسها ولا فصولها ولا خواصها .
(١) في هامش (د) : ( قوله : ( وافتقارها ( مرفوع مبتدأ ، وخبره قوله : « إلى المولى الكريم » ، والجملة مستأنفة استئنافاً بيانياً ؛ كأن قائلاً قال : « من الفاعلُ المفتقرة إليه ؟ » ، فأجابه بقوله : ( وافتقارها إلى المولى الكريم » ، ولا يصح جرُّهُ بالعطف على قوله : ( حدوثها » ؛ لأنه حينئذ يصير الافتقار علة لنفسه ، والشيء لا يكون علة لنفسه ) .
Whatever is not a body is an attribute of another; for our Lord, Exalted and Majestic, is not a body, and He is with that an essence described by attributes, and it is impossible that He be an attribute of another. And He is exalted, an essence. And the second part invalidated the second premise, which is the estimation's belief that every essence described by attributes is a body; for our Lord, Exalted and Majestic, is described by attributes, and with that it is impossible that He be a body or similar to any of the contingent things. So He, Exalted, is a true essence, and there is no likeness to Him among essences. And by this part, His essence (Exalted) distinguished itself from all other contingent essences; for they, even if they are independent of a locus (i.e., they are not an attribute subsisting in another), they are in need of the Agent with a necessary need that cannot be separated from Him necessarily; due to the necessity of their origination, and their need of the Generous Lord, Exalted and Majestic, from the beginning and perpetually. (1) And the result: that by the first part of the meaning of self-subsistence (al-qiyam bi al-nafs), the Lord (Exalted and Majestic) distinguished Himself from attributes; so He is not of their genus, and by the second part He (Blessed and Exalted) distinguished Himself from all other essences; so there is no resemblance to Him from them, and He does not share with them in their genera, nor their differentiae, nor their properties. (1) In the margin of (d): (His saying: 'and their need' is raised as a subject, and its predicate is his saying: 'to the Generous Lord', and the sentence is a resumptive explicative; as if a speaker said: 'Who is the Agent on which they depend?' so he answered with his saying: 'and their need of the Generous Lord'. And it is not correct to make it genitive by conjunction with his saying: 'their origination'; because then the need would become the cause of itself, and a thing is not a cause of itself.)
Source pages p. 157

Commentary
This passage defines the two parts of self-subsistence (al-qiyam bi al-nafs). The first part: that God is not an attribute. The second part: that God is not a body. The text begins by stating that whatever is not a body is an attribute of another. Then it asserts that God is not a body, yet He is an essence described by attributes, so He cannot be an attribute. Then it explains the second part: it invalidates the false belief that every essence described by attributes must be a body. God is described by attributes but cannot be a body or similar to anything contingent. Thus His essence is unique. The contingent essences, even if independent of a locus (i.e., not attributes), are still in need of an Agent. The result: the first part distinguishes God from attributes; the second distinguishes Him from contingent essences. The footnote clarifies the grammar of 'and their need' in the preceding sentence.

Key Terms
ذات
صفة
القيام بالنفس
جِرْم
الفاعل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That being described by attributes necessarily makes it a body or an attribute.
Correction: Sanusi shows that Allah is described by attributes yet is neither a body nor an attribute. Being described by attributes does not entail being a body; likewise, not being a body does not entail being an attribute.

Checkpoint
What are the two parts of al-qiyam bi al-nafs according to this passage?
A) that God is eternal and that He is powerful
B) that God is not an attribute and that God is not a body
C) that God is self-sufficient and that He is the Creator
D) that God is one and that He is unique

Key Takeaway
The two parts of self-subsistence safeguard Allah's transcendence: He is neither an attribute subsisting in another nor a body comparable to contingent beings.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever catch yourself thinking of Allah as if He were like something you know—a powerful king, a father, a force? How does this lesson’s insistence that He is neither an attribute nor a body, nor like any essence, strike your heart? What will you do today to repent of any subtle imaginative likeness you have harbored?
?
You depend on Allah for every single moment of your existence. When was the last time you truly felt that dependence—not as a concept, but as a gut-wrenching need? If you live as though you are self-sufficient, how does this lesson expose that as a lie? What will you change in your daily routine to acknowledge your need of the Self-Subsistent?
?
The passage says Allah’s essence is utterly unique, sharing nothing with created essences in genus, differentia, or properties. Does your worship reflect that uniqueness? Or do you serve Him as if He needs your praise? Commit now to one specific act of worship today that consciously affirms He is beyond your imagination and need.

The Impossibility of Partial Likeness in Divine Attributes
Sanusi argues against partial likeness in divine attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why even a partial similarity between God and any being in attributes like power and will is rationally impossible.

Core Idea
Even one shared attribute with God makes a being temporal and dependent.

Sanusi completes his proof of divine oneness by addressing a potential loophole: what if two essences share only some attributes, like power? He argues that even this limited likeness leads to the same contradictions — temporality and dependence. This lesson unpacks that argument and shows why any resemblance to God is impossible.

Guided Path
1
Read the opening conclusion on full likeness
Start by reading the first sentence which concludes that a full universal likeness leads to impotence. This sets the stage for the more specific case.
2
Understand the argument for partial likeness
Now read the second paragraph. Notice the assumption: two beings share only some attributes (like power and will). Sanusi argues that each such attribute needs a specificer to assign it to its subject, because the attribute could potentially inhere in either.
The need for a 'mukhaṣṣiṣ' is crucial.
3
Trace the chain to temporality
Follow the reasoning: since each attribute is contingent and needs a specificer, it is temporal (ḥādith). Moreover, neither subject can be devoid of that attribute or its opposite, which is also temporal. Hence both subjects are temporal.
4
Connect to divine necessity and impotence
Temporality contradicts God's necessary existence. Additionally, if the attributes are power and will, mutual obstruction (tamānuʻ) would lead to impotence. This closes the argument.
5
Examine the final explanatory note
Read the last part where Sanusi explains the phrase 'no effecter with Him'. He shows that positing an effecter alongside God reduces to positing a likeness in attributes, which has been proven impossible.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ويلزم منه عجز الأخرى؛ لما انعقد بينهما من المماثلة.
هذا كله في المثل الحقيقي العام.
وأما إذا فرض المثل خاصا في بعض الصفات؛ كالقدرة والإرادة مثلاً: فإنه يلزم الحدوث أيضا لكل من المثلين؛ لأن كل واحدة من الصفتين المتماثلتين تحتاج إلى مخصص يخصصها بالمحل الذي وجدت فيه؛ لقبول كل واحدة منهما حينئذ المحلين، فيلزم أن تكون كل واحدة منهما جائزة الوجود حادثة عارضة لكل من الموصوفين وكل واحد منهما لا يمكن أن يعرى عن هذه الصفة الحادثة أو ضدها، ولا يكون ذلك الضد إلا حادثا، فيلزم أن يكون كل من حادثا، وذلك ينافي ما ثبت للإله من وجوب الوجود، ويلزم حينئذ العجز أيضا؛ لأجل الحدوث والتمانع إن فرض المثل في القدرة والإرادة.
فقولنا : ( ولا مؤشر معه في فعل من الأفعال ) هو من باب عطف الخاص على العام؛ لأن وجود المؤثر معه تعالى يرجع إلى وجود المثل له تعالى في بعض صفاته؛ وهي القدرة والإرادة، فلو وجدت صفة في حادث يتأتى بها الإيجاد والإعدام .. لكانت مماثلة لقدرة البارئ تبارك وتعالى، فتكون حادثة؛ لاحتياجها إلى مخصص خصصها بالذات العلية، وخصصها التعلق عن نظيرتها وخصصها بعموم [؟]، وحدوث الصفة يستلزم حدوث موصوفها، وذلك يستلزم حدوث الذات العلية، تعالى الله عن ذلك.
And from it necessarily follows the impotence of the other; due to the likeness established between them.
All this concerns the real universal likeness.
And as for if the likeness is assumed to be specific to some attributes, such as power and will for example: then temporality also necessarily follows for each of the two likes; because every one of the two similar attributes needs a specificer to specify it to the locus in which it is found; due to the potentiality of each of them then for the two loci, so it necessarily follows that each one of them is contingent in existence, temporal, accidental to each of the two described, and each one of them cannot be devoid of this temporal attribute or its opposite, and that opposite is nothing but temporal, so it necessarily follows that each one is temporal, and that contradicts what has been established for the deity of necessity of existence, and then impotence also necessarily follows; due to temporality and mutual obstruction if the likeness is assumed in power and will.
So our statement: (And no effecter with Him in any of the actions) is of the type of conjunction of the specific to the general; because the existence of an effecter with Him, exalted, returns to the existence of a likeness to Him, exalted, in some of His attributes; namely power and will. So if an attribute existed in a temporal being by which bringing into existence and causing non-existence are effected... it would be similar to the power of the Creator, blessed and exalted, and thus it would be temporal; because it needs a specificer that specified it to the supreme essence, and specified it in relation from its counterpart, and specified it in generality [؟], and the temporality of the attribute necessitates the temporality of its possessor, and that necessitates the temporality of the supreme essence, exalted is God above that.
Source pages p. 164

Commentary
This passage is a continuation of the proof of divine oneness by showing the impossibility of any likeness to God. The author first concludes the argument for a perfect universal likeness (al-mithl al-ḥaqīqī al-ʻāmm), stating that it leads to impotence due to the established similarity. Then he considers a more specific case: if the likeness is assumed only in some attributes, like power and will. He argues that even this partial similarity leads to temporality (ḥudūth) for both supposed likes. The reasoning is that each of the two similar attributes requires a specificer to assign it to its locus (the essence it qualifies), because each attribute could potentially inhere in either subject. Since neither subject can be devoid of the attribute or its opposite, and the opposite is also temporal, both subjects become temporal. This contradicts the divine attribute of necessary existence. Furthermore, if the likeness is specifically in power and will, it also leads to impotence due to temporality and mutual obstruction (tamānuʻ). The author then explains the phrase 'no effecter with Him in any action' as an instance of conjoining the specific to the general, because positing an effecter alongside God reduces to positing a likeness in the attributes of power and will. He then elaborates that if such a temporal attribute could effect bringing into existence and causing non-existence, it would resemble God's power, but it would be temporal because it needs a specificer for its essence, its relation, and its generality. The temporality of the attribute necessitates the temporality of its possessor, leading to the impossible consequence of the divine essence being temporal.

Key Terms
مثل (mithl)
حدوث (ḥudūth)
تمانع (tamānuʻ)
مخصص (mukhaṣṣiṣ)
عارضة (ʻāriḍa)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Partial similarity in only some attributes (like power) does not threaten divinity.
Correction: Sanusi shows that even partial likeness leads to temporality, which contradicts God's necessary existence, making it impossible.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, even if we assume a likeness in only some attributes (like power), what necessarily follows?
A) The two likes are both eternal.
B) The two likes are both temporal.
C) Only the created one is temporal.
D) The divine essence becomes composite.

Key Takeaway
Even a single shared attribute would make God contingent, which is impossible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever attributed ultimate control over any aspect of your life to a person, system, or circumstance? How does Sanusi's argument that even a single shared attribute would make God temporal expose the irrationality of that belief? Will you consciously affirm that only God has true power, starting today?
?
Think of a fear that grips you—perhaps of failure, loss, or another person's opinion. Does that fear reveal an implicit belief that something besides God holds decisive power over you? How can the lesson's proof that no being can share even one attribute with God reshape your trust and calm your heart?
?
Consider a time you relied on your own strength or planning as if it were independent of God. How does the necessity of a specificer for every attribute remind you that even your abilities are contingent? Will you repent from this subtle shirk and depend wholly on God?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
8 min
Refutation of Accidental Effecting by Created Powers
Sanusi refutes the claim that created powers can effect without a real attribute.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi argues that even if a created power's effecting is accidental by God's grant, it still leads to the impossibility of an accident inhering in an accident.

Core Idea
Accidental effecting still requires a real attribute, leading to the absurdity of an accident in an accident.

In this passage, Sanusi addresses a subtle objection to the proof of divine uniqueness. The objector claims that created powers can effect changes accidentally, by God's grant, and therefore do not resemble God's eternal power. However, Sanusi demonstrates that this very claim leads to an even greater problem: the need for a real attribute in the creature that would result in an accident inhering in an accident, which is impossible. This lesson unpacks the objection and Sanusi's incisive refutation.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection
Read the opening lines of the passage where the objector claims that temporal powers effect by God's grant, not by their own will. Notice the objector's attempt to avoid likeness to God's power.
2
Identify the claim of accidentality
Focus on the phrase 'عرضي لها بجعل الله تعالى لها ذلك'. Understand that the objector says the effecting is accidental (ʻaraḍī) to the created power.
3
Follow Sanusi's answer
Read Sanusi's response. He argues that if the effecting is accidental, it cannot subsist by itself—it must be caused by an existential attribute. That attribute would be an accident inhering in the created power, which is itself an accident, leading to the absurdity of 'accident in accident' (قيام العرض بالعرض).
4
Connect to divine transcendence
Reflect on how this argument upholds the uniqueness of God's power: no creature can truly effect without a real attribute, and such an attribute would entail an impossibility, thus proving that only God's power is truly effective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن قيل: تأتي الإيجاد والإعدام على وفق إرادة القادر وعلمه .. هو حقيقة القدرة الأزلية، ولا مثل لها في ذلك؛ لأن الإيجاد والإعدام اللذين يدعيان لبعض القوى الحادثة.. ليسا من حقيقة تلك القوة الحادثة، بل هو عرضي لها بجعل الله تعالى لها ذلك، فهي تؤثر على وفق إرادة الله تعالى وعلمه، لا على وفق إرادة موصوفها وعلمه.
فالجواب: أن تأتي التأثير إذا كان عرضيا لهذه القوة الحادثة.. فإنه يلزم ألا يرد على هذه القوة على حيالها؛ لأنه حال، والأحوال لا يمكن أن تفعل على حيالها، فلا بد من خلق صفة معنى وجودية في هذه القوة الحادثة تكون علة لما عرض لها من تأتي الإيجاد بها والإعدام، ويلزم عليه قيام العرض بالعرض.
For if it is said: The effecting of bringing into existence and causing non-existence in accordance with the will and knowledge of the powerful is the reality of eternal power, and there is no likeness to it in that; because the bringing into existence and causing non-existence that are claimed for some temporal powers... are not of the reality of that temporal power, rather it is accidental to it by God, exalted, making that for it. Thus it effects in accordance with the will and knowledge of God, exalted, not in accordance with the will and knowledge of its possessor.
The answer is: If the effecting of influence is accidental to this temporal power... then it necessarily follows that it does not occur to this power by itself; because it is a state, and states cannot act by themselves. So there must be the creation of a real existential attribute in this temporal power which is the cause of what accidentally arises in it of effecting bringing into existence and causing non-existence, and there necessarily follows from this the inherence of an accident in an accident.
Source pages p. 165

Commentary
This passage presents an objection and its answer in the context of proving divine oneness. The objector claims that when a created power (like fire's burning) produces effects, it does so by God's grant, not by its own essence. Thus the effecting (taʼtī) is accidental to the created power, and the power itself is not a likeness to God's eternal power. The objector argues that this accidental effecting is in accordance with God's will and knowledge, not the created power's own will. The answer refutes this by analyzing the nature of the accidental effecting. If it is truly accidental (ʻaraḍī) to the temporal power, then it is a state (ḥāl) that cannot subsist by itself; it requires an ontological ground. To be a cause of bringing into existence and causing non-existence, a real existential attribute (ṣifat maʻnā wujūdiyya) must be created in that temporal power. But then this attribute itself is an accident (ʻaraḍ) inhering in the temporal power, which is also an accident (since created powers are accidents). This leads to the impossibility of an accident inhering in another accident (qiyām al-ʻaraḍ bi al-ʻaraḍ), a well-known absurdity in kalām. Thus the objection fails, and the original proof of divine uniqueness stands.

Key Terms
تأتي (taʼtī)
قوة حادثة (quwwa ḥāditha)
عرضي (ʻaraḍī)
صفة معنى (ṣifat maʻnā)
قيام العرض بالعرض (qiyām al-ʻaraḍ bi al-ʻaraḍ)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that accidental effecting (by God's grant) avoids likeness to God's power without any further problem.
Correction: Sanusi shows that accidental effecting still requires a real attribute in the creature, leading to the impossibility of an accident inhering in an accident.

Checkpoint
What does Sanusi say must be created in the temporal power if its effecting is accidental?
A) A new essence.
B) A real existential attribute.
C) A separate will.
D) Nothing additional.

Key Takeaway
Accidental effecting by God's grant does not escape the need for a real attribute; it leads to the impossibility of an accident inhering in an accident.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you witness a cause in the world—like fire burning or medicine healing—do you instinctively feel the creature has its own power, or do you truly see God as the only agent? What would it take to shift your default perception to match this lesson?
?
This lesson argues that even a 'granted' accidental effect requires a real attribute in the creature, leading to an absurd infinite regress of accidents. How does this logical rigor challenge any lingering sense of independence you attribute to created causes? Can you think of a moment today where you implicitly relied on a creature's power?
?
If the impossibility of an accident inhering in another accident is so foundational, what does that say about the uniqueness of God's self-subsistence? How might this truth change the way you pray or seek help from created means?
