God's Absolute Authority and the Nature of Excuse
Sanusi argues that God may excuse or punish as He wills, being free from injustice.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that God's forgiveness or punishment is entirely according to His will, and that any excuse granted to a servant is pure grace, not an obligation.

Core Idea
God owes no one an excuse; His mercy is pure grace.

Sanusi presents a foundational statement about divine sovereignty and gratuitous mercy. He insists that God is not bound to excuse anyone, and if He does, it is a pure favor. This lesson unpacks the implications for understanding God's justice and freedom.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice the assertive tone: Sanusi makes several interconnected claims about God's authority.
The passage is dense; read it twice.
2
Identify the key claims
List the distinct statements: (1) God is not obligated to excuse forgetfulness or misinterpretation. (2) The proof always belongs to God. (3) Being declared excused is pure grace. (4) God may punish or forgive whom He wills. (5) God is praiseworthy and free from deficiency and injustice.
Each claim reinforces God's absolute sovereignty.
3
Reflect on divine justice
Ask yourself: If God punishes whom He wills, does that make Him unjust? Sanusi answers by affirming God is 'free from injustice' by definition. His actions are not subject to external standards.
4
Connect to broader theology
Consider how this teaching underlies the Ash'ari view that good and evil are defined by divine command, not independent reason. It also guards against thinking God 'owes' us mercy.
This connects to the topic of divine transcendence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
على المولى العظيم أن يَعذِرَهُ بنسيان أو تأويل ، بلِ الحُجَّةُ للمولى تبارك وتعالى على كلِّ حالٍ ، وحكمه على عبدِهِ بأَنَّهُ معذور في بعض الأحوال.. محض فضل منه جلَّ وعلا ، ولهُ أنْ يُعذِّبَ مَنْ يَشَاءُ ويرحم مَنْ يَشَاءُ ، وهو المحمودُ المُنزَّهُ عَنِ النقص والظُّلْمِ على كلِّ حالٍ
Upon the great Lord to excuse him due to forgetfulness or interpretation, but the proof is for the Lord, blessed and exalted, in every case. His judgment upon His servant that he is excused in some situations is pure grace from Him, majestic and exalted, and He has the right to punish whom He wills and have mercy on whom He wills, and He is the Praiseworthy, free from deficiency and injustice in every case.
Source pages p. 218

Commentary
This passage is a theological statement about God's absolute sovereignty and justice. It begins by stating that God is not obligated to excuse a person who commits a sin out of forgetfulness or mistaken interpretation. The proof (hujja) always belongs to God; He is the judge. If He declares a servant excused, that is pure grace (mahd fadl) from Him, not an obligation. God may punish or forgive as He wills, and He is always praiseworthy, free from any deficiency or injustice. This sets the stage for discussing prophetic stories where apparent sins are reinterpreted.

Key Terms
المولى العظيم
الحجة
محض فضل
المحمود المنزه عن الظلم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think God is obliged to excuse certain sins, e.g., sins committed out of ignorance.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that God is not obliged to excuse anyone; if He does, it is pure grace.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, when God declares a servant excused, it is because:
A) God is obligated to excuse him
B) It is pure grace from God
C) The servant deserved it through his actions
D) The servant's sin was not really a sin

Key Takeaway
God's mercy is never owed; it is His gift.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you sin out of ignorance or forgetfulness, do you assume God is obligated to forgive you? How does this passage challenge your view of God's mercy as a gift rather than a right?
?
If God's judgment of your excuse is 'pure grace,' how should that affect the way you approach Him in repentance—are you truly humble or do you still feel entitled?
?
This passage says God has the right to punish whom He wills and have mercy on whom He wills. Does that comfort you or disturb you? Why? And how does that reveal what you truly believe about His justice?

The Infallibility of Angels and Refutation of Harut and Marut
Sanusi states the obligation to interpret apparent deficiencies in angels and refutes the story of Harut and Marut.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the principle of reinterpretation (ta'wil) when texts suggest angelic deficiency and why the story of Harut and Marut must be rejected.

Core Idea
Angels are infallible; any story implying otherwise is false or requires reinterpretation.

In this passage, Sanusi addresses a critical point of Islamic theology: the absolute protection of angels from sin and deficiency. He introduces the principle of ta'wil (interpretation) to handle any text that seems to contradict this. The famous story of Harut and Marut serves as a test case, showing how false reports must be discarded entirely in favor of clear Qur'anic descriptions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Observe the assertive tone: Sanusi declares the story of Harut and Marut as kadhb wa zur (lie and falsehood).
2
Identify the principle of ta'wil
Notice that Sanusi begins by stating the obligation (wujub) of interpreting any apparent deficiency in angels. This is a rule: the apparent meaning must be reinterpreted to preserve angelic perfection.
Ta'wil is not guessing; it's a method to reconcile conflicting evidence.
3
Analyze the example of Harut and Marut
Find where Sanusi mentions the two angels teaching sorcery. Note that he distinguishes between the core story (which itself needs interpretation) and the false additions by lying historians (which are outright rejected).
4
Understand the refutation
Sanusi declares that believing or even listening to such falsehood is not permissible. This is a strong warning: not all historical reports are acceptable.
5
Confirm with Qur'anic verses
The passage ends with three descriptions of angels from the Qur'an: honored servants, obedient, and constantly worshiping. These verses form the foundation of correct belief about angels.
Memorize these attributes: they are the standard against which all claims about angels are measured.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجوبُ تأويل ما يوهم نقصاً في حقٌّ الملائكة الكرام عليهم السلام وبمثل هذه التأويلاتِ يجبُ أنْ يُتأَوَّلَ مَا يُوهِمُ ظاهرُهُ نقصاً في حق الملائكة عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام ؛ كقضيَّة هاروت وماروت ، وجعلهما مَلَكين يُعلِّمانِ الناسَ السحرَ ، ويزيدُ فيها كَذَبَةُ المؤرِّخينَ مِنْ أَنَّهما عُوقبا ومسخا ، وذلك كلُّهُ كذبٌ وزُورٌ ، لا يَحِلُّ اعتقاده ولا سماعُهُ بل الذي يجب اعتقاده في حقّ جميع الملائكة : ما وصفهم به المولى العظيم تبارك وتعالى : أنَّهم عِبادُ مُكْرَمُونَ ﴾ [الأنبياء : ٢٦] ، يعْصُونَ اللهَ مَا أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ ﴾ [التحريم: 7]، وأنهم لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ، وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ * يُسَبِّحُونَ الَّيْلَ وَالنَّهَارَ لَا يَفْتُرُونَ) [الأنبياء : ۱۹ - ۲۰] .
The obligation of interpretation of what gives the impression of deficiency regarding the noble angels, peace be upon them. And with such interpretations must be interpreted what its apparent meaning implies deficiency regarding the angels, peace be upon them, like the case of Harut and Marut, and their being made two angels teaching people sorcery, and the lying historians add to it that they were punished and disfigured. All of that is falsehood and fabrication; it is not permissible to believe it nor to listen to it. Rather, what must be believed regarding all angels is what the Mighty Lord, blessed and exalted, described them with: that they are honored servants [al-Anbiya 26], they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and they do what they are commanded [al-Tahrim 6], and they are not arrogant about His worship, nor do they tire; they glorify Him night and day without slackening [al-Anbiya 19-20].
Source pages p. 230

Commentary
This passage establishes the theological principle that any text or report that appears to attribute deficiency to angels must be reinterpreted (ta'wil) to remove that implication. The author explicitly applies this to the story of Harut and Marut, which some sources claim involved two angels teaching sorcery and being punished. The author declares such additions by lying historians to be false and forbidden to believe or even listen to. Instead, the correct belief about all angels is based on clear Qur'anic verses: they are honored servants who never disobey Allah, they do exactly what they are commanded, they are never arrogant or weary, and they constantly worship without pause. This refutes any story implying angelic disobedience or deficiency.

Key Terms
التأويل
هاروت وماروت
السحر
الكذب
العباد المكرمون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some believe that angels can sin or be punished, as in the story of Harut and Marut teaching sorcery and being disfigured.
Correction: Angels are infallible honored servants who never disobey Allah; any report suggesting otherwise is false and must be rejected or reinterpreted.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the correct belief about angels regarding their obedience?
A) Angels sometimes disobey Allah but are forgiven.
B) Angels never disobey Allah and always do what they are commanded.
C) Angels can teach sorcery as a test for humans.
D) Angels may be punished for their mistakes.

Key Takeaway
Angels are absolutely obedient and flawless; any narrative contradicting this must be rejected as false or reinterpreted to align with the Qur'an.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?
The angels never tire of worship, day and night. When you consider your own prayer and worship, how far are you from that standard? What one change will you make this week to move closer to the angelic quality of constant devotion?
?
Sanusi says it is forbidden to listen to reports that imply deficiency in angels. Is there a lecture, video, or conversation you have listened to that subtly lowers your awe of Allah's majesty? Will you commit to cutting it off today?

Obligation to Allegorically Interpret Apparent Deficiencies in Angels
Sanusi states the obligation to interpret apparent deficiencies and refutes false stories.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that any apparent deficiency in angels must be interpreted allegorically, and that false historical reports like the story of Harut and Marut are to be rejected.

Core Idea
Angels are flawless; apparent flaws in scripture require interpretation.

Sanusi teaches that angels are honored servants of Allah, completely obedient and sinless. Any passage that suggests otherwise requires allegorical interpretation. He then refutes the fabricated story of Harut and Marut as an example.

Guided Path
1
Read the principle of interpretation
Read the first part of the passage where Sanusi states the obligation (wujub) to interpret (ta'wil) any scriptural passage that implies deficiency in angels.
Notice the emphatic language: 'وجوب' (obligation).
2
Examine the example of Harut and Marut
Read the example and the refutation of false historians' additions. Understand why these stories are rejected.
The text declares the additions as 'كذب وزور' (falsehood and lies).
3
Learn the correct belief from the Quran
Read the positive belief about angels as described in the Quranic verses cited: they are honored, obedient, and constantly glorify Allah.
These verses form the definitive doctrine on angelic nature.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجوبُ تأويل ما يوهم نقصاً في حقّ الملائكة الكرام عليهم السلام وبمثل هذه التأويلاتِ يجبُ أنْ يُتأَوَّلَ مَا يُوهِمُ ظاهرُهُ نقصاً في حق الملائكة عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام؛ كقضيَّة هاروت وماروت، وجعلهما مَلَكين يُعلِّمانِ الناسَ السحرَ، ويزيدُ فيها كَذَبَةُ المؤرِّخينَ مِنْ أَنَّهما عُوقبا ومسخا، وذلك كلُّهُ كذبٌ وزُورٌ، لا يَحِلُّ اعتقاده ولا سماعُهُ بل الذي يجب اعتقاده في حقّ جميع الملائكة: ما وصفهم به المولى العظيم تبارك وتعالى: أنَّهم عِبادُ مُكْرَمُونَ ﴾ [الأنبياء: ٢٦]، يعْصُونَ اللهَ مَا أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ ﴾ [التحريم: 7]، وأنهم لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ، وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ * يُسَبِّحُونَ الَّيْلَ وَالنَّهَارَ لَا يَفْتُرُونَ) [الأنبياء: ١٩ - ٢٠].
The obligation to interpret [allegorically] what implies deficiency in the honored angels, peace be upon them. And by such interpretations must be interpreted what its apparent meaning implies deficiency regarding the angels, peace be upon them; like the case of Harut and Marut, and making them two angels teaching people sorcery, and the lying historians add to it that they were punished and transformed, and all of that is falsehood and lies; it is not permissible to believe it nor to hear it. Rather, what must be believed regarding all the angels is what the Mighty Lord, Blessed and Exalted, described them with: that they are honored servants [Quran 21:26], they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and they do what they are commanded [Quran 66:6], and that they are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they tire; they glorify [Him] night and day, never slackening [Quran 21:19-20].
Source pages p. 230

Commentary
This passage is a doctrinal ruling about the correct belief regarding angels. The author begins by stating the obligation (وجوب) to allegorically interpret (تأويل) any scriptural passage that appears to imply a deficiency (نقص) in the angels. He then gives a concrete example: the story of Harut and Marut, where some sources claim they were two angels who taught sorcery and were later punished and transformed. The author declares these additions by lying historians as pure falsehood (كذب وزور) and prohibits both believing and listening to such stories. He then states the positive belief required about all angels: they are exactly as Allah describes them in the Quran — honored servants, obedient, never tired, and constantly glorifying Allah. The passage thus establishes a principle of hermeneutics (tanzih) for angelology: any apparent imperfection must be explained away, and the definitive attributes of angels are their complete submission and purity.

Key Terms
تأويل
نقص
ملائكة
هاروت وماروت
عباد مكرمون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The story that Harut and Marut were punished and transformed is historically true.
Correction: The text explicitly states that such additions by historians are falsehood and lies, and it is not permissible to believe them.

Checkpoint
What is the ruling on the story that Harut and Marut were punished and transformed?
A) It is a true story that must be believed.
B) It is a lie and it is not permissible to believe or listen to it.
C) It is permissible to believe it but not to narrate it.
D) It is a matter of opinion.

Key Takeaway
The correct belief about angels is that they are honored, obedient, and never tire in worship; any contrary report must be interpreted or rejected.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever casually listened to a story about angels that seemed to lower their status? How did you reconcile it in your mind? After reading Sanusi's ruling, what will you do differently when you encounter such stories?
?
The passage says it is not even permissible to listen to false reports about angels. What popular Islamic tales or cultural narratives have you accepted without verifying them against the Quran? Commit to one specific source you will check before accepting any claim about the unseen.
?
Is there any area of your beliefs where you prioritize historical anecdotes or weak narrations over the clear verses of the Quran? Write down one practical step you will take to ensure your aqeedah is built solely on authentic revelation.

Harut and Marut: Teaching Sorcery as a Warning
Sanusi explains that if Harut and Marut were angels, their teaching sorcery was to warn people, not to practice it.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's defense of angelic perfection regarding the story of Harut and Marut: if they were angels, their teaching sorcery was solely to warn people, not to practice it.

Core Idea
Angels teach evil only to warn, never to practice.

Some readers of the Quran are troubled by the story of Harut and Marut, where angels are described as teaching sorcery. Did they condone evil? Sanusi clarifies that either they were not angels, or if they were, their teaching was a necessary warning, much like teaching about sin to avoid it. In this passage, he explains the logic and supports it with a hadith.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on Harut and Marut
Read the Arabic passage and translation provided below. Notice how Sanusi structures his argument: first stating the correct belief, then explaining the purpose of teaching, and finally giving an analogy.
The hadith of Hudhayfa reinforces the idea that knowledge of evil helps avoid it.
2
Analyze the core argument
Identify the two possibilities Sanusi presents: either Harut and Marut were not angels (no issue) or they were angels (teaching was for warning). Understand why teaching sorcery is like teaching other sins: to define and prevent.
The Quranic verse 'We are only a trial' shows their teaching tested people's response.
3
Reflect on the analogy and hadith
Consider how teaching about adultery or usury helps people avoid them. Then read Hudhayfa's statement: asking about evil to avoid it. This shows that knowledge of evil is not endorsement.
The author uses this to prove that angels teaching sorcery does not imply they practiced it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنَّما الذي يجبُ اعتقادُهُ في قضية هاروت وماروت: أنَّهما إِنْ لم يكونا مَلكين فواضح، وإن كانا مِنَ الملائكةِ فتعليمهما للسحرِ لم يكن لأجلِ العملِ بهِ، بل للتحرُّزِ منه؛ بتعريف حقيقتِه، وبيان شره وعقوبته، ولهذا أخبر الله تعالى عنهما أنَّهما قالا: ﴿ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تكفر ﴾ [البقرة: ١٠٢]، وهذا كتعليم حقيقـة الـزنا وأنـواع الـربـا والمحرَّماتِ ليَتحرَّزَ المكلَّفُ منها؛ لأنَّ التحرُّزَ مِنَ الشرّ موقوفٌ على معرفتِه، ولهذا قال حذيفة رضي الله عنه: ( كانَ الناسُ يسألونَ النبيَّ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ عن الخير، وكنتُ أسأله عن الشرِّ؛ مخافة أن أقعَ فيه ). وأمَّا قولُ الملائكة عليهم الصلاة والسلام خطاباً لمولانا جل وعلا حينَ أخبرهم أَنَّهُ جاعل في الأرض خليفةٌ: أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ﴾ [البقرة: ٣٠] : فهو استفهام منهم لمجرد الاستعلام، لا للإنكار والاعتراض الموجبين لكفرِ مَنْ صدرا منه، ولهذا أتوا عليهم الصلاة والسلام بجملة ﴿ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ﴾ احتراساً عمَّا يُوهِمُهُ الاستفهام مِنَ الإنكار أو الاعتراض، فقالوا عليهم السلامُ ما معناه: لم نسأل إنكاراً ولا اعتراضاً، ونحنُ نُسَبِّحُ؛ أي: نتزه يا مولانا ذاتَكَ وصفاتِكَ عن النقص والتمثيل، ونُنَزِّهُ أفعالَكَ كيفما تَصرَّفَتْ، وأحكامك كيفما
Rather, what must be believed regarding the case of Harut and Marut is: if they were not angels, then it is obvious; and if they were angels, then their teaching of sorcery was not for the purpose of practicing it, but for [people] to guard against it, by defining its reality and explaining its evil and punishment. For this reason Allah informed about them that they said: 'We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve' [Quran 2:102]. This is like teaching the reality of adultery, types of usury, and forbidden things so that the obligated person may guard against them, because guarding against evil depends on knowing it. For this reason Hudhayfa, may Allah be pleased with him, said: 'The people used to ask the Prophet, peace be upon him, about good, and I used to ask him about evil, for fear that I might fall into it.' As for the words of the angels, peace be upon them, addressing our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, when He informed them that He would place a vicegerent on earth: 'Will You place therein one who will cause corruption therein and shed blood, while we glorify Your praise and sanctify You?' [Quran 2:30]: it is a question from them merely for inquiry, not for rejection or objection which would necessitate disbelief of the one from whom it issues. For this reason they, peace be upon them, added the clause 'while we glorify Your praise and sanctify You' as a precaution against what the question might imply of rejection or objection. So they, peace be upon them, said the meaning of which is: we do not ask in rejection or objection, and we glorify; that is, we declare, O our Lord, Your Essence and Attributes free from deficiency and likeness, and we deem Your acts transcendent however they may be, and Your judgments however...
Source pages p. 231

Commentary
This passage is a detailed interpretation of two specific issues regarding angelic perfection. First, the story of Harut and Marut: The author states that the correct belief is either they were not angels (so no issue) or if they were angels, their teaching of sorcery was not to practice it but to warn people by defining its reality and consequences. They explicitly said 'We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve,' indicating their teaching was a test, not an endorsement. The author analogizes this to teaching about sins like adultery and usury to help people avoid them, supported by the hadith of Hudhayfa who asked about evil to avoid it. Second, the angels' question in Quran 2:30 ('Will You place...') is interpreted as a mere inquiry (استعلام) for understanding, not as rejection or objection (إنكار أو اعتراض), which would be disbelief. The angels safeguarded their question by immediately adding 'while we glorify Your praise and sanctify You' to show their submission and to preempt any misunderstanding. Thus, both cases are defended against implying any deficiency in angelic obedience and veneration of Allah.

Key Terms
هاروت وماروت
فتنة
تحرز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Angels who teach sorcery must have practiced it themselves or condone it.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that they taught only for warning and prevention, not for practice, just as teaching about other sins is for avoidance.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what was the purpose of Harut and Marut's teaching of sorcery?
A) To practice it themselves.
B) To test people and lead them astray.
C) To warn people against it by explaining its reality and consequences.
D) To demonstrate their power over humans.

Key Takeaway
Sanusi defends angelic perfection by showing that any teaching of evil by angels is solely for warning, not participation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are you deliberately learning about the sins and weaknesses that tempt you, or do you avoid such knowledge out of fear? What is one specific evil you need to understand better in order to guard against it, and what step will you take today to learn about it?
?
The angels taught sorcery as a trial. When Allah exposes you to the reality of a sin—whether through a book, sermon, or personal experience—do you feel drawn to it or more cautious? Honestly, where does your heart lean, and what does that reveal about your faith?
?
Hudhayfa asked the Prophet about evil because he feared falling into it. What evil are you currently ignoring because it is uncomfortable to confront? Ask yourself: what would it cost you to learn the full reality of that evil, and are you willing to pay that price to protect your soul?

The Angels' Question: Inquiry Without Objection
Sanusi interprets the angels' question as mere inquiry, not objection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's interpretation of the angels' question in Quran 2:30 as a mere inquiry for knowledge, not as an objection or rejection of Allah's decree.

Core Idea
Angels question only to learn, never to object.

The angels' question to Allah in Surah Al-Baqarah—'Will You place therein one who will cause corruption...?'—might seem like a challenge to divine wisdom. Sanusi clarifies that it was a simple inquiry for understanding, not rejection. The angels' immediate addition of praise shows their submission and guards against misreading their intent.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on the angels' question
Read the Arabic and translation. Note how Sanusi first states the type of question (استعلام) then explains why the angels added the clause of glorification.
The verse is from Quran 2:30, just after Allah announced He would place a khalifah.
2
Distinguish between استعلام and إنكار
Understand that استعلام is a question for information, while إنكار is objection. Sanusi insists their question was the former, and that the latter would be disbelief.
The angels are impeccable, so they could not have intended objection.
3
Examine the safeguard of تسبيح
The angels added 'while we glorify Your praise and sanctify You' to preempt any misunderstanding. This shows their question was from a position of submission, not criticism.
This clause reframes their question as a humble request for wisdom.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنَّما الذي يجبُ اعتقادُهُ في قضية هاروت وماروت: أنَّهما إِنْ لم يكونا مَلكين فواضح، وإن كانا مِنَ الملائكةِ فتعليمهما للسحرِ لم يكن لأجلِ العملِ بهِ، بل للتحرُّزِ منه؛ بتعريف حقيقتِه، وبيان شره وعقوبته، ولهذا أخبر الله تعالى عنهما أنَّهما قالا: ﴿ إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تكفر ﴾ [البقرة: ١٠٢]، وهذا كتعليم حقيقـة الـزنا وأنـواع الـربـا والمحرَّماتِ ليَتحرَّزَ المكلَّفُ منها؛ لأنَّ التحرُّزَ مِنَ الشرّ موقوفٌ على معرفتِه، ولهذا قال حذيفة رضي الله عنه: ( كانَ الناسُ يسألونَ النبيَّ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ عن الخير، وكنتُ أسأله عن الشرِّ؛ مخافة أن أقعَ فيه ). وأمَّا قولُ الملائكة عليهم الصلاة والسلام خطاباً لمولانا جل وعلا حينَ أخبرهم أَنَّهُ جاعل في الأرض خليفةٌ: أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ﴾ [البقرة: ٣٠] : فهو استفهام منهم لمجرد الاستعلام، لا للإنكار والاعتراض الموجبين لكفرِ مَنْ صدرا منه، ولهذا أتوا عليهم الصلاة والسلام بجملة ﴿ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ﴾ احتراساً عمَّا يُوهِمُهُ الاستفهام مِنَ الإنكار أو الاعتراض، فقالوا عليهم السلامُ ما معناه: لم نسأل إنكاراً ولا اعتراضاً، ونحنُ نُسَبِّحُ؛ أي: نتزه يا مولانا ذاتَكَ وصفاتِكَ عن النقص والتمثيل، ونُنَزِّهُ أفعالَكَ كيفما تَصرَّفَتْ، وأحكامك كيفما
Rather, what must be believed regarding the case of Harut and Marut is: if they were not angels, then it is obvious; and if they were angels, then their teaching of sorcery was not for the purpose of practicing it, but for [people] to guard against it, by defining its reality and explaining its evil and punishment. For this reason Allah informed about them that they said: 'We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve' [Quran 2:102]. This is like teaching the reality of adultery, types of usury, and forbidden things so that the obligated person may guard against them, because guarding against evil depends on knowing it. For this reason Hudhayfa, may Allah be pleased with him, said: 'The people used to ask the Prophet, peace be upon him, about good, and I used to ask him about evil, for fear that I might fall into it.' As for the words of the angels, peace be upon them, addressing our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, when He informed them that He would place a vicegerent on earth: 'Will You place therein one who will cause corruption therein and shed blood, while we glorify Your praise and sanctify You?' [Quran 2:30]: it is a question from them merely for inquiry, not for rejection or objection which would necessitate disbelief of the one from whom it issues. For this reason they, peace be upon them, added the clause 'while we glorify Your praise and sanctify You' as a precaution against what the question might imply of rejection or objection. So they, peace be upon them, said the meaning of which is: we do not ask in rejection or objection, and we glorify; that is, we declare, O our Lord, Your Essence and Attributes free from deficiency and likeness, and we deem Your acts transcendent however they may be, and Your judgments however...
Source pages p. 231

Commentary
This passage is a detailed interpretation of two specific issues regarding angelic perfection. First, the story of Harut and Marut: The author states that the correct belief is either they were not angels (so no issue) or if they were angels, their teaching of sorcery was not to practice it but to warn people by defining its reality and consequences. They explicitly said 'We are only a trial, so do not disbelieve,' indicating their teaching was a test, not an endorsement. The author analogizes this to teaching about sins like adultery and usury to help people avoid them, supported by the hadith of Hudhayfa who asked about evil to avoid it. Second, the angels' question in Quran 2:30 ('Will You place...') is interpreted as a mere inquiry (استعلام) for understanding, not as rejection or objection (إنكار أو اعتراض), which would be disbelief. The angels safeguarded their question by immediately adding 'while we glorify Your praise and sanctify You' to show their submission and to preempt any misunderstanding. Thus, both cases are defended against implying any deficiency in angelic obedience and veneration of Allah.

Key Terms
استعلام
إنكار
اعتراض
تسبيح
تقديس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The angels' question indicates they doubted Allah's wisdom or objected to His decree.
Correction: Sanusi states it was merely an inquiry (استعلام), not objection (إنكار), and their addition of glorification proves their submission.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what did the angels intend by their question in Quran 2:30?
A) To object to Allah's decision.
B) To express doubt about the wisdom of creating humans.
C) To seek knowledge and understanding.
D) To test Allah's response.

Key Takeaway
The angels' question demonstrates perfect submission; any inquiry from them is for knowledge, not criticism.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face trials or events you don't understand, do your questions carry the spirit of the angels' inquiry – seeking knowledge while affirming Allah's perfection – or do they reveal hidden objection? How can you train your heart to add 'tasbih' before your 'why'?
?
The angels immediately declared Allah's transcendence even as they asked. Reflect: In your prayers or moments of confusion, do you rush to complain before glorifying? What would change if you made tasbih the preface to every question you bring to Allah?
?
Imagine you are the angels: you know Allah's absolute perfection, yet you ask a question that might be misunderstood. How does their precautionary clause challenge your own boldness in addressing the Creator? Are you willing to submit your questions to the same humility?

Divine Transcendence
intermediate
6 min
Three Meanings of 'Biḥamdika' and Two for 'Nuqaddisu'
Sanusi interprets the angels' tasbeeh and taqdees with multiple meanings.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the multiple interpretations of the angels' declaration of praise and sanctification and how they illustrate key theological principles about divine transcendence and free will.

Core Idea
The angels' words reveal layers of meaning about divine transcendence and free will.

This passage from Sanusi's commentary unpacks the rich theological content behind the angels' familiar words from Surah Al-Baqarah. It shows that even seemingly simple phrases can carry multiple layers of meaning, each reinforcing core Islamic beliefs about Allah's perfection and the nature of His actions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing, then read the translation. Let the author's rhythm come through before interpreting.
The passage is dense with possibilities—focus on the flow.
2
Identify the three meanings of biḥamdika
Sanusi gives three possibilities for the bā' in 'biḥamdika': accompaniment, causality, and instrumentality. Notice how each changes the meaning of the angels' praise.
Each interpretation highlights a different aspect of Allah's transcendence.
3
Examine the interpretations of wa nuqaddisu laka
Two meanings are offered: either the angels sanctify themselves (purify from evil thoughts) for Allah's sake, or Allah purifies their hearts for His service. Both emphasize sincere devotion.
4
Understand Allah's response
Allah's reply 'I know what you do not know' is explained in terms of divine wisdom occurring by pure choice, not necessity. This counters the angels' implied objection.
This reinforces the doctrine of free will in divine actions.
5
Reflect on the theological point
All the interpretations collectively emphasize Allah's absolute transcendence and that His actions are by pure will, not compulsion.
The passage guards against reducing divine actions to necessity.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقولهم : ﴿ بِحَمْدِكَ يعنون : نُنَزِّهُ في حال كوننا حامدينَ لكَ ؛ أي : مادحينَ لكَ بكلِّ كمال على كلِّ حالٍ ؛ فتكون الباء للمصاحبة ، أو تنزه بسبب نعمة توفيقِكَ الذي يُوجِبُ حمدَكَ وشكرَكَ ، لا بحول منا ولا قُوَّةٍ ؛ فالباء على هذا سببيةٌ ، ويكونُ مِنْ بابِ التعبير بالمسبَّبِ عنِ السبب ؛ لأنَّ الحمدَ بمعنى الشكرِ مُسبَّبٌ عنِ النِّعَمِ ، ويحتمل أَنْ يكون المعنى : نُنَزِّهُ بنفس حمدِكَ ؛ أي : مدحِكَ بكل كمال ؛ لأنَّ المدح بالكمال تنزيه عن ضدّهِ ؛ فتكونُ الباء باءَ الآلة ، والله تعالى أعلم . وقولهم : ﴿ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ﴾ يعنون والله تعالى أعلمُ : نُقدِّسُ أنفسنا ؛ أي : نُطْهَّرُها مِنْ كل خاطر رديء لكَ ؛ أي : لأجل رضاك والغنية بكَ عن كل ما سواك ، ويحتمل أن يكون المعنى : تُطهر قلوبنا لأجل خدمتِكَ وعبادتِكَ ؛ إذ لا تصلحُ الخدمة والعبادة إلا مع قلب نقي مِنْ جميع الأدران وأما جوابه جلَّ وعلا لهم بقوله : ( إنّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ ) : فمعناه والله تعالى أعلمُ : إنّي وإن جعلتُ في الأرض مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فيها ويسفك الدماء فإني أعلمُ في ذلكَ مِنَ الحِكَم والمصالح التي تقع بمحض الاختيار ، لا باللزوم والإيجاب.. ما لا تقدرون على الإحاطة بعلمه . وبقيَّةُ ما في الآيةِ مِنَ المعاني محله التفسير ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق.
And their saying: 'With Your praise' they mean: we declare You free from imperfection while being praisers of You; i.e., praising You with every perfection in every state; so the bā' is for accompaniment. Or we declare freedom from imperfection because of the grace of Your success that necessitates Your praise and thanks, not through any power from us; so the bā' here is causal, and it is an expression of the effect instead of the cause, because praise (al-ḥamd) meaning gratitude is an effect of blessings. And it is possible that the meaning is: we declare freedom from imperfection by virtue of Your praise itself; i.e., praising You with every perfection, because praising with perfection is a declaration of freedom from its opposite; so the bā' is instrumental, and Allah Almighty knows best. And their saying: 'And we sanctify for You' they mean, and Allah Almighty knows best: we sanctify ourselves; i.e., purify them from every evil thought for You; i.e., for Your pleasure and independence from everything besides You. And it is possible that the meaning is: You purify our hearts for Your service and worship; because service and worship are only valid with a heart pure from all impurities. As for His answer, glorified and exalted, to them with His saying: 'Indeed, I know what you do not know': its meaning, and Allah Almighty knows best: I, even if I place on earth one who will cause corruption and shed blood, yet I know in that of wisdoms and interests that occur by pure choice, not by necessity and obligation, that which you cannot encompass with knowledge. And the remainder of the meanings in the verse belong to exegesis, and with Allah Almighty is success.
Source pages p. 232

Commentary
This passage is a detailed commentary on the angels' statement 'We declare Your praise' (biḥamdika) and 'We sanctify for You' (wa nuqaddisu laka) from Surah Al-Baqarah, followed by the meaning of Allah's response. The author first analyzes the phrase 'biḥamdika'. He gives three potential interpretations for the bā': 1) accompaniment (li-l-muṣāḥabah): they declare God's transcendence while praising Him in every state; 2) causality (sababiyyah): they declare transcendence because of the grace of God's tawfīq which necessitates praise; 3) instrumentality (ālah): they declare transcendence by the very act of praising Him, since praising with perfection itself negates imperfection. For 'wa nuqaddisu laka', he gives two readings: a) they sanctify themselves (purify from evil thoughts) for God's sake; b) God purifies their hearts for His service. Finally, he explains Allah's reply 'I know what you do not know' as indicating that divine wisdom and interests occur through pure choice (ikhtiyār), not necessitation (luzūm), and that human knowledge cannot encompass them. The author then defers further meanings to tafsīr.

Key Terms
الباء للمصاحبة
الباء سببية
الباء الآلة
التنزيه
التقديس
مَحْض الاختيار
اللزوم والإيجاب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the angels' words are merely liturgical and have no deeper theological implications.
Correction: Sanusi shows that their words contain principles about divine transcendence, the nature of praise, and the role of free will in divine actions.

Checkpoint
How many possible meanings does the author give for the bā' in 'biḥamdika'?
A) Two
B) Three
C) Four
D) One

Key Takeaway
Careful analysis of scriptural language reveals multiple layers of meaning that reinforce core Islamic beliefs about Allah's transcendence and free will.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you praise Allah, do you rely solely on His tawfīq, or do you assume it is from your own ability? How can you cultivate a deeper awareness that your very praise is a gift from Him?
?
The angels acknowledged that even their sanctification is 'for You' and by 'Your pleasure'. Examine one area of your worship: is it truly for Allah alone, or have you mixed it with seeking people's approval or your own satisfaction? What step will you take this week to purify your intention?
?
Allah's wisdom includes allowing free choice, even when it leads to evil. When you face a situation that seems unjust or painful, do you trust that His wisdom operates through pure choice, not necessity? How can you respond to that trial with submission instead of complaint?

The Necessity of Rational Proof for Creedal Matters
Sanusi argues that rational proof is indispensable for some creedal matters.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that some creedal matters cannot be properly believed without rational proof, and that one must grasp the three rational judgments (wujub, istihalah, jawaz) through active reflection.

Core Idea
Rational proof is necessary for creed; mere hearing of revelation is not enough.

In this passage, Imam Sanusi addresses the student directly, warning that passive acceptance of revelation does not constitute proper belief in certain creedal matters. He introduces the three fundamental rational judgments—obligation, impossibility, and possibility—that one must actively perceive. The passage then grounds this need in Quranic injunctions to reflect and reason.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation, noting the direct address 'O my brother.' Let the author's warning sink in before analyzing.
2
Identify the key claim
Find the sentence where Sanusi states that some creedal matters cannot do without rational proof. What does he mean by 'cannot do without'?
This claim is radical: rational proof is not optional.
3
Understand the three judgments
The text mentions three judgments: wujub, istihalah, jawaz. Consider how these apply to creedal statements (e.g., Allah exists is wajib; Allah is created is mustahil).
These are the building blocks of kalam reasoning.
4
Reflect on the Quranic evidence
Read the two verses cited. How do they support the need for rational reflection? The first rebukes lack of tadabbur, the second condemns lack of 'aql.
The Quran itself commands reflection.
5
Consider the implication
This does not deny revelation; it asserts that revelation must be understood rationally. Discuss how this balances transmitted knowledge with reasoned conviction.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم يا أخي : أنَّ من العقائد ما لا غنية فيه عن الدليل العقلي ؛ فلا بدَّ من إدراك وجه الوجوب أو الاستحالة أو الجواز فيه من قبل المخاطب، حتى لو سمع كلام الله وصحيح سنته .. لا يغنيه ذلك بمجرد السماع، بل لا بدَّ له من التفكر في وجه الدليل، وإلا كان ممَّن ذمَّه المولى تعالى بمثل قوله جل شأنه : ﴿ أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْءَانَ أَمْ عَلَى قُلُوبِ أَقْفَالُهَا ﴾ [محمد : ٢٤] ، وقوله تعالى : ﴿ إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَابِّ عِندَ اللَّهِ الصُّمُّ البُكُمُ الَّذِينَ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ ﴾ [الأنفال : ۲۲]
Know, O my brother, that among the creedal matters are those that cannot do without rational proof. So one must perceive the aspect of obligation, impossibility, or possibility regarding it before the addressee. Even if he hears the speech of Allah and the authentic Sunnah, that does not suffice him by mere hearing. Rather, he must reflect on the aspect of the proof; otherwise he would be among those whom the Lord, exalted, has condemned with statements like His saying, Mighty is His mention: 'Do they not then reflect on the Quran, or are there locks upon their hearts?' [Muhammad:24], and His saying, exalted: 'Indeed, the worst of living creatures in the sight of Allah are the deaf and dumb who do not use reason' [Al-Anfal:22].
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is a direct admonition to the reader about the necessity of rational proof ('dalil 'aqli) in certain creedal matters. The author states that some beliefs cannot be correctly held without understanding the rational basis—specifically, grasping the judgment of obligation (wujub), impossibility (istihalah), or possibility (jawaz) concerning the matter. Mere passive hearing of divine revelation or authentic hadith is insufficient; one must actively reflect (tafakkur) on the proof. The author supports this with two Quranic verses: one condemning those who do not reflect on the Quran, and another comparing those who do not use reason to the worst of creatures. Thus, rational engagement is mandatory.

Key Terms
الدليل العقلي
الوجوب
الاستحالة
الجواز
التفكر
العقل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think this passage denies the authority of revelation or makes reason independent of revelation.
Correction: It asserts that revelation alone without rational understanding does not count as proper belief; reason and revelation are complementary.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what three rational judgments must one perceive regarding creedal matters?
A) Wajib, haram, mubah
B) Wujub, istihalah, jawaz
C) Haqq, batil, mushkil
D) Qudrah, iradah, 'ilm

Key Takeaway
Rational proof is necessary for creedal matters; one must actively perceive the three rational judgments and reflect, beyond passive hearing.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear a verse of the Quran, do you stop to reflect on its rational proof, or do you let it pass as mere sound? What locks are on your heart that prevent deep thought?
?
If rational proof is necessary for creedal matters, how many of your beliefs have you actually reasoned through? Are you among those who just follow without thinking? What will you do about it today?
?
The worst creatures are those who do not use reason. Does that description make you uncomfortable? What specific belief in your heart have you never examined with reason? Submit yourself to the proof now.

Method of Learning

The Quran's Rational Appeal
Sanusi explains why non-Muslims accepted Islam and the necessity of kalam scholars.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Quran addresses the intellect and why the mutakallimun are essential guides in matters of creed.

Core Idea
The Quran speaks to the mind, not just the heart.

This lesson explores a powerful observation by Sanusi: non-Muslims were drawn to Islam because the Quran directly addressed their intellect. He explains how clear proofs combined with sound reason lead to faith, and warns that without proper guidance, even believers can be shaken by doubts. The passage sets the stage for the role of kalam scholars as navigators in the wilderness of confusion.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on Quran's appeal
Read the Arabic passage from 'وإنما عظُمَ شأن القرآن' to 'مشاعل الهداية إلى طرق الرشاد'. Then read the translation to grasp the flow.
Notice how Sanusi describes the Quran as 'speaking to the ears of their minds' – a metaphor for rational appeal.
2
Identify the two lights
Find the phrase 'نوري العقل السليم والدين القويم' (the two lights of sound reason and upright religion). Understand that Sanusi sees both as complementary.
This is a key principle: faith and reason are allies.
3
Understand the role of mutakallimun
Sanusi says the mutakallimun are guides in the wilderness of doubts. Ask yourself: why do people need such guides?
The passage implies that without kalam, people can easily fall into deviation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنما عظُمَ شأن القرآن في عيون غير أهل الإسلام ؛ لكونهم وجدوه يصافح آذان عقولهم، وتعيهِ من غير ما عناء ، حتى إذا استقرت براهينه الوضاءة وسمح التوفيق ولاحت معالمه . . رأيتهم يهرولون إلى الإيمان فرحين برحمة الله تعالى بهم ؛ أن أنقذهم ممَّا كانوا يكابدونه من تهافت الاعتقاد الذي كانوا عليه ولا يعرفون له برهاناً ، وإلحاحِ الفطرة بوجدان المعبود المستحق للعبادة ، فاطمأنت نفوسهم بما جمع الله لهم من نوري العقل السليم والدين القويم . وكم من أناس يحسبون أنهم من دينهم على قوة يقين ، فما تلبث عواصفُ الشُّبه أن تقلب أحوالهم ، فيتيهوا في بيدائها ، وما المتكلمون وعلماء العقائد إلا الهداة في هذه المهامه ؛ يفسدون على أهل الزيغ مقاصدهم ، ويضيئون في وجه الظلام مشاعل الهداية إلى طرق الرشاد • وما زلنا نسمعُ جَمْجَمةٌ رخيصة تصدُّ الناظرين عن النظر ؛ ينعت أصحابها أنفسَهم بأهل الحديث والأثر ، يستبدلون بعلم العقيدة كلام أهل التشبيه والضلال ، ويرون في علوم أعلام الأمة انحرافاً عن النَّهْج السديد ، ويتلون أمام هؤلاء الأعلام جملة من الآيات الكريمة ، ويسردون كمّاً من الأحاديث الشريفة ! وهؤلاء السادة هم أجمع لهذه الآيات والآثار منهم ؛ يعرفونها وغيرها ، وقد ألموا علماً بها ، فليس الشأن في سردها وحفظها ، بل في الفقه بها معنى جليّاً وإشارة خفية ، فما أشبههم بطفل في الكتاب ؛ يسمع من معلّمِهِ تلاوةً وقراءةً
And the status of the Quran is great in the eyes of non-Muslims because they found it speaking to the ears of their minds, and they comprehended it without difficulty. When its bright proofs settled, and success permitted, and its features appeared, you would see them hastening to faith, rejoicing in God's mercy upon them for saving them from the incoherence of the belief they were upon, for which they knew no proof, and the insistence of innate nature (fitra) to find the deity worthy of worship. So their souls were at ease with what God gathered for them from the two lights of sound reason and the upright religion. How many people think they are strong in certainty in their religion, but no sooner do the storms of doubts overturn their states, that they wander in its desert! And the mutakallimun and scholars of beliefs are but guides in these wildernesses; they spoil the aims of the people of deviation, and light the lamps of guidance to the paths of rectitude in the face of darkness. And we still hear a cheap murmuring that prevents viewers from looking; its proponents describe themselves as Ahl al-Hadith and Athar, exchanging the science of creed for the speech of the people of anthropomorphism and misguidance, and see in the sciences of the leading figures of the nation a deviation from the right path. They recite before these leading figures a quantity of noble verses and recount a quantity of noble hadiths! But these masters (the mutakallimun) are more comprehensive of these verses and hadiths than them; they know them and others, and have thoroughly understood them. So the matter is not in their recitation and memorization, but in the jurisprudence (fiqh) of them – a clear meaning and a subtle indication. They resemble a child in the school who hears from his teacher recitation and reading.
Source pages p. 9

Commentary
This passage combines praise for the Quran's rational appeal with a sharp criticism of those who reject kalam under the banner of Ahl al-Hadith. First, the author explains that non-Muslims were attracted to the Quran because it addresses the intellect ('uqul); they found clear proofs and were guided to faith by the combination of sound reason and true religion. He then warns that many who think they have strong certainty are quickly swayed by doubts. The role of mutakallimun and creed scholars is to guide such people and refute deviations. Second, he criticizes a group that calls itself Ahl al-Hadith and Athar but actually replaces the science of creed with anthropomorphism (tashbih) and misguidance. They accuse the great scholars (a'lam al-umma) of deviation, yet these scholars have a more comprehensive knowledge of the revealed texts than they do. The author emphasizes that the real issue is not recitation or memorization but deep understanding (fiqh) of the meanings and subtle indications of the texts. He likens these superficial adherents to a child who merely repeats what the teacher recites, lacking true comprehension. The passage thus defends the science of kalam and its practitioners as the true heirs of prophetic understanding.

Key Terms
متكلمون (mutakallimun)
فطرة (fitra)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what two 'lights' did God gather for those who accepted Islam?
A) Light of the sun and moon
B) Light of sound reason and upright religion
C) Light of knowledge and wealth
D) Light of the Quran and hadith

Key Takeaway
The Quran appeals to reason, and kalam scholars are essential to protect faith from doubts.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read the Quran, do you engage your mind (the 'ears of your mind') or do you read passively? What would change if you actively sought its rational proofs? This week, choose one verse and reflect on its logical appeal.
?
Sanusi says even those who think they are strong can be overturned by doubts. Have you ever experienced a doubt that shook your faith? Did you seek guidance from a scholar (a 'guide in the wilderness') or did you try to handle it alone? Commit to finding a trusted scholar if doubts arise.
?
The passage describes non-Muslims hastening to faith when they saw the Quran's coherence. Is your own faith coherent? Are there beliefs you hold without proof? Will you examine them today and strengthen them with evidence from the Quran and reason?

Critique of Pseudo-Ahl al-Hadith
Sanusi criticizes those who claim to follow hadith but reject kalam.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's criticism of those who reject kalam while claiming to be Ahl al-Hadith, emphasizing the importance of deep understanding (fiqh) over mere memorization.

Core Idea
Recitation without understanding is like a child's parrot.

In this passage, Sanusi turns a critical eye on a group that calls itself Ahl al-Hadith but actually rejects the science of kalam, accusing its scholars of deviation. He argues that true scholarship lies not in memorizing verses and hadiths but in grasping their deep meanings and subtle indications. The lesson defends the mutakallimun as the true inheritors of prophetic understanding.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on pseudo-Ahl al-Hadith
Read from 'وما زلنا نسمعُ جَمْجَمةٌ رخيصة' to the end. Compare the Arabic with the translation.
Sanusi uses vivid language: 'cheap murmuring' and 'child in the school'.
2
Identify the accusation
Sanusi says this group 'exchanges the science of creed for the speech of the people of anthropomorphism and misguidance.' What does he mean?
They have abandoned true kalam for a distorted literalist approach.
3
Understand the criterion of true knowledge
Sanusi emphasizes that the mutakallimun know the texts more comprehensively ('أجمع'). The real issue is fiqh, not sarda (recitation).
This is a key distinction: deep understanding vs. surface-level repetition.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنما عظُمَ شأن القرآن في عيون غير أهل الإسلام ؛ لكونهم وجدوه يصافح آذان عقولهم، وتعيهِ من غير ما عناء ، حتى إذا استقرت براهينه الوضاءة وسمح التوفيق ولاحت معالمه . . رأيتهم يهرولون إلى الإيمان فرحين برحمة الله تعالى بهم ؛ أن أنقذهم ممَّا كانوا يكابدونه من تهافت الاعتقاد الذي كانوا عليه ولا يعرفون له برهاناً ، وإلحاحِ الفطرة بوجدان المعبود المستحق للعبادة ، فاطمأنت نفوسهم بما جمع الله لهم من نوري العقل السليم والدين القويم . وكم من أناس يحسبون أنهم من دينهم على قوة يقين ، فما تلبث عواصفُ الشُّبه أن تقلب أحوالهم ، فيتيهوا في بيدائها ، وما المتكلمون وعلماء العقائد إلا الهداة في هذه المهامه ؛ يفسدون على أهل الزيغ مقاصدهم ، ويضيئون في وجه الظلام مشاعل الهداية إلى طرق الرشاد • وما زلنا نسمعُ جَمْجَمةٌ رخيصة تصدُّ الناظرين عن النظر ؛ ينعت أصحابها أنفسَهم بأهل الحديث والأثر ، يستبدلون بعلم العقيدة كلام أهل التشبيه والضلال ، ويرون في علوم أعلام الأمة انحرافاً عن النَّهْج السديد ، ويتلون أمام هؤلاء الأعلام جملة من الآيات الكريمة ، ويسردون كمّاً من الأحاديث الشريفة ! وهؤلاء السادة هم أجمع لهذه الآيات والآثار منهم ؛ يعرفونها وغيرها ، وقد ألموا علماً بها ، فليس الشأن في سردها وحفظها ، بل في الفقه بها معنى جليّاً وإشارة خفية ، فما أشبههم بطفل في الكتاب ؛ يسمع من معلّمِهِ تلاوةً وقراءةً
And the status of the Quran is great in the eyes of non-Muslims because they found it speaking to the ears of their minds, and they comprehended it without difficulty. When its bright proofs settled, and success permitted, and its features appeared, you would see them hastening to faith, rejoicing in God's mercy upon them for saving them from the incoherence of the belief they were upon, for which they knew no proof, and the insistence of innate nature (fitra) to find the deity worthy of worship. So their souls were at ease with what God gathered for them from the two lights of sound reason and the upright religion. How many people think they are strong in certainty in their religion, but no sooner do the storms of doubts overturn their states, that they wander in its desert! And the mutakallimun and scholars of beliefs are but guides in these wildernesses; they spoil the aims of the people of deviation, and light the lamps of guidance to the paths of rectitude in the face of darkness. And we still hear a cheap murmuring that prevents viewers from looking; its proponents describe themselves as Ahl al-Hadith and Athar, exchanging the science of creed for the speech of the people of anthropomorphism and misguidance, and see in the sciences of the leading figures of the nation a deviation from the right path. They recite before these leading figures a quantity of noble verses and recount a quantity of noble hadiths! But these masters (the mutakallimun) are more comprehensive of these verses and hadiths than them; they know them and others, and have thoroughly understood them. So the matter is not in their recitation and memorization, but in the jurisprudence (fiqh) of them – a clear meaning and a subtle indication. They resemble a child in the school who hears from his teacher recitation and reading.

Commentary
This passage combines praise for the Quran's rational appeal with a sharp criticism of those who reject kalam under the banner of Ahl al-Hadith. First, the author explains that non-Muslims were attracted to the Quran because it addresses the intellect ('uqul); they found clear proofs and were guided to faith by the combination of sound reason and true religion. He then warns that many who think they have strong certainty are quickly swayed by doubts. The role of mutakallimun and creed scholars is to guide such people and refute deviations. Second, he criticizes a group that calls itself Ahl al-Hadith and Athar but actually replaces the science of creed with anthropomorphism (tashbih) and misguidance. They accuse the great scholars (a'lam al-umma) of deviation, yet these scholars have a more comprehensive knowledge of the revealed texts than they do. The author emphasizes that the real issue is not recitation or memorization but deep understanding (fiqh) of the meanings and subtle indications of the texts. He likens these superficial adherents to a child who merely repeats what the teacher recites, lacking true comprehension. The passage thus defends the science of kalam and its practitioners as the true heirs of prophetic understanding.

?

?

?
The child hears recitation but does not understand. Are there truths you have heard many times—about God, yourself, or the Hereafter—that have never truly penetrated your heart? What will you do today to move from hearing to understanding?

Editorial Warning: Interpreting Revelation with Discipline
Editorial passage warns against capricious interpretation and introduces the Sanusiyyat series.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the importance of disciplined interpretation of the Quran and Sunnah guided by reason, language, and usul, and the recognition of the Sanusiyyat series as an accepted scholarly work.

Core Idea
Interpreting revelation requires disciplined tools—reason, language, and usul—not caprice or fanaticism.

This editorial passage from Dar al-Taqwa sets the stage for the Sanusiyyat series by warning against common misinterpretations of the Quran and Sunnah. It critiques those who rely on caprice, fanaticism, or a few deviant scholars, and instead emphasizes the need for sound methodology. The passage also introduces the Sanusiyyat series as a trusted resource for learning Islamic creed.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the full Arabic passage and its literal English translation to grasp the author's tone and key points before analysis.
Notice the author's use of prayer and direct warning.
2
Identify the scenario and warning
The passage begins with a scenario: a student with limited knowledge arrogantly corrects his teacher. Understand why this is criticized as combining error with bad manners.
The ignorant observer wrongly sides with the student.
3
List the prohibited approaches
The passage lists several errors: interpreting by caprice, ignoring reason/language/usul, fanaticism, analogizing eternal to temporal, restricting religion to deviant scholars, and disparaging Ahl al-Sunnah. Note each one.
These errors violate sound methodology in usul al-din.
4
Reflect on the positive guidance and endorsement
The author prays for understanding and reverence, and then praises the Sanusiyyat series as a work accepted by scholars for teaching creed. Recognize the contrast.
The Sanusiyyat is presented as a reliable source free from the criticized errors.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لا يألفها ، فيستطيلُ عليه بما تعلّم مع قصر باعه وقلة اطلاعه ويوجهه لقراءة أستاذه أعلمُ بها منه ، فيحسب الجاهل أنَّ الأستاذ قد أخطأ وأنَّ الفُتَيَّ قد أصاب ، وما يدري أنه قد جمع إلى خطئه سوء الأدب واستحق التأديب ، وعى الرغم من هذا لا يسعنا إلا أن نسأل لإخواننا هؤلاء سعة الصدر ، ونور الفهم ، وتعظيم جلال الله تعالى في قرآنه ، وتعظيم الجناب النبوي في أخباره ؛ أن تفسَّرَ نصوصهما بالهوى بعيداً عن رقابة العقل واللغة ، وعلم الأصول الذي هو مفخرة علوم الإسلام ، وبنزعة العصبية وغلبة الوهم ، وقياس القديم الباقي على المحدَثِ الفاني وحصر فهم الدين بشيخ أو شيخين شذا في أقوالهما ـ ولا تعويل على شذوذ العلماء ، بل لا تقليد في أصول الدين - وذم عامة علماء أهل السنة والطعن في عقائدهم لكونهم خالفوهم فيما ذهبوا إليه ! والله في خلقه شؤون ، واليومَ تُعمِّمُ ) دارُ التقوى ( كتب العقائد بهذه النشرة العلمية الرصينة لسلسلة « السنوسيات » التي انعقدت خناصر العلماء على تلقيها بالقبول ، وإدراجها في سلاسل تدريس العقيدة الإسلامية ، والتعويل على مباحثها ومسائلها وتحريراتها ، ضمن عناية فذة علمية ، ونقلة في تقديم الأنفع والمفيد في حلة بهية ، سائلين المولى تعالى رضاه ابتداءً وانتهاءً
He does not become familiar with them, so he presumes over him with what he learned despite his short reach and little knowledge, and directs him to reading his teacher who is more knowledgeable of it than him. The ignorant one thinks that the teacher erred and the young student was correct, and he does not know that he has combined to his error bad manners and deserved discipline. Despite this, we cannot but ask for our brothers these: broadness of chest, light of understanding, magnification of the majesty of Allah Almighty in His Quran, and magnification of the Prophetic station in his reports; that its texts be interpreted with caprice far from the oversight of reason and language, and the science of fundamentals (usul) which is the pride of the sciences of Islam, and with the tendency of fanaticism and the predominance of illusion, and analogizing the eternal ancient to the temporal passing, and restricting understanding of the religion to one or two shaykhs who deviated in their statements—and no reliance on the deviant opinions of scholars, rather no taqlid in the fundamentals of religion—and disparaging the generality of the scholars of Ahl al-Sunnah and criticizing their beliefs because they differed from them in what they held! And Allah has concerns in His creation. Today, Dar al-Taqwa publishes books of creed with this sound scientific edition of the 'Sanusiyyat' series, upon which the fingers of scholars have agreed to accept it, and to include it in the chains of teaching Islamic creed, and to rely on its investigations, issues, and precis, with an outstanding scientific care, and a shift in presenting the most beneficial and useful in a beautiful form, asking the Lord Almighty for His pleasure from beginning to end.
Source pages p. 10

Commentary
This is an editorial passage that combines a warning against improper interpretation of the Quran and Sunnah with an endorsement of the Sanusiyyat series. The author describes a situation where a student who has learned a little presumes to correct his teacher, and the ignorant observer thinks the student is right. Such behavior combines error with bad manners. The passage then prays for fellow Muslims to have open hearts, understanding, and reverence for Allah and the Prophet, and warns against interpreting scriptures by caprice, ignoring reason, language, and the science of usul (principles). It criticizes fanaticism, illusion, comparing the eternal to the temporal, and restricting religion to a few deviant scholars. It rejects taqlid in matters of usul al-din (fundamentals of religion) and condemns disparaging Ahl al-Sunnah scholars. Finally, it praises the publication of the Sanusiyyat series by Dar al-Taqwa as a work that scholars have accepted and rely upon for teaching Islamic creed.

Key Terms
العقل
اللغة
علم الأصول
أصول الدين
تقليد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that questioning a teacher is always prohibited.
Correction: The passage warns against arrogant presumption and ignorance, not against legitimate, respectful inquiry. It criticizes the student's bad manners and the observer's ignorance, not the act of questioning per se.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does the ignorant observer mistakenly think when a student corrects his teacher?
A) The teacher is correct and the student is wrong
B) The student is correct and the teacher is wrong
C) Both are wrong
D) Both are correct

Key Takeaway
The Quran and Sunnah must be interpreted with reason, language, and usul, not caprice or fanaticism, and trusted scholarly works like the Sanusiyyat should be relied upon.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever interpreted a verse of the Quran or a hadith in a way that suited your own desires or convenience, ignoring the checks of reason, language, and usul? What will you do today to ensure you never do that again?
?
Are you blindly loyal to a particular scholar or teacher in matters of aqeedah, even when their views contradict the broader Ahl al-Sunnah consensus? Will you commit to studying the fundamentals of religion with evidence, not allegiance?
?
The author prays for 'broadness of chest' and 'light of understanding'. Is your heart open to being corrected when you are wrong about Islam? What step will you take this week to humble yourself before the texts and the scholarly tradition?

Method of Learning
beginner

Sanusi's Graded Method: A Novel Kalam Curriculum
Description of Sanusi's graded educational method and its novelty.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the graded structure of Sanusi's educational method and how his inspired foresight distinguished it from earlier scholars' approaches.

Core Idea
Sanusi's graded series was an inspired pedagogical innovation for all levels.

This passage introduces the Sanusiyyat as a carefully structured series of kalam texts, progressing from concise summaries to extensive commentaries. The author compares Sanusi's arrangement to that of al-Ghazali and al-Taftazani, but emphasizes that Sanusi's unique foresight—anticipating the needs of students from weak to bright—made his series truly revolutionary. Here, we see the vision of a scholar who built a comprehensive pedagogical system, not just a set of books.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the English translation. Let the author's description of the graded series sink in. Notice the terms for each level.
The passage ends mid-sentence; this is the full page content.
2
Identify the four stages of the series
The passage lists four levels: mu'tasar al-mukhtasar, al-mukhtasar, al-wasiṭ, and al-basīṭ. Find these in the Arabic and understand their progression from most concise to most expansive.
These correspond to increasing detail and depth.
3
Compare Sanusi's novelty to his predecessors
The passage mentions al-Ghazali and al-Taftazani also wrote graded works. Note that Sanusi's novelty lay not in the idea of gradation, but in his inspired foresight that his series would dominate future teaching.
The author highlights Sanusi's 'badīʿ' as his life-giving discovery.
4
Reflect on Sanusi's pedagogical insight
The passage describes Sanusi's consideration of all student types: weak, moderate, bright, and successful. Reflect on how this insight shaped his curriculum to be inclusive and effective.
This foresight was realized even during his lifetime.
5
Consider the inclusive scope of the school
The passage ends by noting that Sanusi's school began with children in kuttab and common people, even women. Appreciate the radical inclusivity of his vision.
This aligns with the goal of teaching creed to all.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
سلسلة التعليم المنهجي لعلم الكلام ؛ بما تميزت عن غيرها من كتب الاعتقاد بالابتداء بمعتصر المختصر الذي تجده في وجيز متونها ، إلى المختصر الذي تراه في كبير المتون ووجيز الشروح ، إلى الوسيط ، ثم إلى سعة البسيط
هذا الترتيب البديع الذي انتهجه إمامنا السنوسي لم يكن بدعاً ؛ فقد سبق إليه من  ِقبَلِ أعلام علم الكلام ، فلحجة الإسلام الغزالي مثله ؛ في «قواعد العقائد » و « الرسالة القدسية » و « الاقتصاد في الاعتقاد » ، وللعلامة المحقق سعد الدين التفتازاني شِبْهُهُ ؛ في تهذيب المنطق والكلام » و « المقاصد » و « شرح المقاصد » ، وكلاهما من أقصى المشرق الإسلامي ، ولكن البديع في سلسلة الإمام السنوسي هو استحياؤه وكشفه الإلهامي عن تربع هذه السلسلة المباركة مناحي النظر والتدريس في علم العقائد في المستقبل القريب ، بل إنّ تأليفه هذه عانقتها أنظار العلماء وأقلامهم في حياته قبل موته ، كلُّ هذا كان مدعاة له أن يتفرّس وجوه طلبة المستقبل ، مُلِمّاً بضعف الضعيف وبلادة ذهنه ، وشرود النَّصف النصيف في عقله ، وكدورة وهم الذكي على حدته ونباهته ، واكتفاء الموفق بلمحته وإشارته .
كل هذا هو بعضُ ما أدركه الإمام السنوسي لتنفذ همَّتُهُ في إنشاء مدرسة كلامية لها منهاجها وأسلوبها ، تبدأ حدودها بصبيان الكتاب وعامة أهل الصنائع والحِرَف الذين لا شغل لهم بالعلم ، بل بالنسوة
The series of graded education for kalam, distinguished from other books of creed by beginning with the concise of the abridgment which you find in the brief of its texts, to the abridgment which you see in the large texts and synopses of commentaries, to the intermediate, then to the extent of the expanded. This wonderful arrangement that our Imam Sanusi adopted was not unprecedented; it was preceded by the luminaries of kalam: for example, the Proof of Islam al-Ghazali has something similar in his 'Qawa'id al-'Aqa'id', 'al-Risala al-Qudsiyya', and 'al-Iqtisad fi al-I'tiqad'; and the verifying scholar Sa'd al-Din al-Taftazani has its parallel in 'Tahdhib al-Mantiq wa al-Kalam', 'al-Maqasid', and 'Sharh al-Maqasid' — both from the far Islamic East. But the wonderful thing in Imam Sanusi's series is his life-giving and inspired discovery that this blessed series would occupy the fields of reflection and teaching in the science of creeds in the near future. Indeed, his authorship of these was embraced by the eyes and pens of scholars during his lifetime before his death. All this was a reason for him to foresee the faces of future students, taking into account the weakness of the weak and the dullness of his mind, the distraction of the moderate in his intellect, the turbidity of the bright one's illusion despite his sharpness and cleverness, and the sufficiency of the successful one with his glance and hint. All this is part of what Imam Sanusi realized to carry out his aspiration in establishing a kalam school with its curriculum and style, whose boundaries begin with the children of the kuttab and the common people of crafts and trades who have no occupation with learning, but rather with women.
Source pages p. 53

Commentary
This passage begins by describing the Sanusiyyat series as a graded educational curriculum for kalam, distinguished from other creedal books by its progression: starting with the most concise summary (mu'tasar al-mukhtasar) in the brief texts, then moving to the abridgment (mukhtasar) found in the larger texts and synopses of commentaries, then to the intermediate (wasiṭ), and finally to the extensive (basīṭ). The author then notes that this wonderful arrangement is not unprecedented; it was preceded by major figures such as al-Ghazali, who employed a similar gradation in his works, and al-Taftazani, who had parallels in his logical and kalam writings. However, the truly novel aspect in Sanusi's series is his inspired foresight that this blessed series would come to occupy the fields of theological reflection and teaching in the near future. Indeed, the works were embraced by scholars even during his lifetime. Sanusi thus anticipated the needs of future students, accommodating all levels: the weak student with dullness of mind, the average student with distraction, the bright student with his sharpness but potential pitfalls, and the successful student who catches on with just a hint. All of this reflects Sanusi's high aspiration to establish a kalam school with its own curriculum and style, starting from the youngest students in the kuttab and common people whose lives are not dedicated to learning, including even women.

Key Terms
السنوسيات
معتصر المختصر
المختصر
الوسيط
البسيط
الترتيب البديع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Sanusi's graded series was simply a convenient arrangement copied from earlier scholars without original insight.
Correction: The passage shows that while Sanusi adopted the method of gradation, his novelty was in his inspired foresight that his series would become the standard curriculum, and his deep consideration for all levels of students, making his approach a true pedagogical innovation.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what makes Sanusi's arrangement 'badīʿ' (novel) compared to predecessors?
A) He included logic and kalam together
B) His intuitive foresight that the series would dominate future teaching
C) He wrote only for beginners
D) He omitted commentaries

Key Takeaway
Sanusi's graded series was an inspired pedagogical innovation, tailored to all learner levels and aimed at making kalam education accessible to everyone.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you learn or teach Islamic knowledge, do you consider the intellectual capacity of each person as Sanusi did, or do you assume a one-size-fits-all approach? How can you adapt your learning from this lesson to actually meet people where they are?
?
Sanusi anticipated the weaknesses of students—the weak, the distracted, the bright but confused. What weakness in your own approach to learning kalam do you tend to ignore, and how will you address it starting today?
?
This passage reveals that Sanusi's works were embraced by scholars even during his lifetime. Are you actively engaging with the traditional scholarly methods of graded learning, or are you satisfied with superficial knowledge? What concrete step will you take this week to deepen your study using a graded approach?
