Sanusi's Methodological Features and His Praise of al-Jaza'iri's Poem
Describes Sanusi's works and his praise of al-Jaza'iri's method.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the distinctive features of Imam al-Sanusi's creedal works, including his attention to diverse audiences and his integration of rational proofs with spiritual elements.

Core Idea
Sanusi's works blend clear rational proofs with spiritual sweetness for both elite and common.

Imam al-Sanusi's numerous creedal works are not mere repetitions; each offers unique contributions in style and content. This passage highlights how he carefully addressed different audiences, from scholars to laypeople, using clear language. Moreover, he deliberately included Sufi allusions, supplications, and poetry to make the texts engaging and spiritually uplifting. The passage also quotes Sanusi's own praise of al-Jaza'iri's poem for combining demonstrative proofs with Sufi discourses, a method he traced back to the Quran and Sunnah.

Guided Path
1
Read the full passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation to get an overview of Sanusi's methodological features as described.
Notice the transition from describing Sanusi's general approach to quoting his specific praise.
2
Identify the main features
List the features mentioned: new content in each work, diversity of audience, clear language, inclusion of Sufi allusions, supplications, and poetry.
These are the key methodological traits.
3
Understand the purpose of diversity
Reflect on why Sanusi addressed different audiences—commoners, specialists, men, women, children—and how that influenced his style.
He aimed to rescue muqallids (blind followers) and make creed accessible.
4
Analyze the quoted passage from Sanusi
Read Sanusi's praise of al-Jaza'iri's poem. Note how he says it combines 'hudā al-khawāṣṣ wa al-ʿawāmm' (guidance of elite and commoners) through both rational proofs and Sufi discourses.
Sanusi sees this as following the prophetic method.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
المتراكب الذي لا بد فيه من جديد مفيد ؛ فالمتون على تفاوت حجمها ترى فيها تكراراً في معالمها العامة ، ولكن مع جديد في أسلوب العرض ، وتنويع في الدليل والمثال ، وزيادة تنفرد بها كل « سنوسية » لا توجد في غيرها ، وكم ترى للعلماء نقولاً وتحريرات عن إمامنا السنوسي يختمونها بقولهم : كما في « شرح العقيدة الكبرى » ، أو شرح العقيدة الوسطى » ، أو « شرح العقيدة الصغرى » ، ولا يمكنهم الاكتفاء بـ ( شرح العقيدة الكبرى » مثلاً عن غيرها وكل ناظر يعلم تنوع الخطاب الذي روعي فيه تنوع المخاطب ؛ فقد كان للسنوسي نية حسنة في استنقاذ المقلدين من جميع الشرائح ؛ فتراه يخاطب العامي والمختص ، والصغير والكبير ، والأمة والصبي ، بلغة حريصة على التفهيم والبيان ، دون اشتغال بتنميق عبارة ، ومع هذا كله جاءت سنوسياته ( حلوة الكلمات ، رائقة العبارات . ثم من الملامح البديعة في هذه السلسلة الموفقة : المزج الهادف والمتعمد لبعض الإشارات المعرفية على طريقة السادة الصوفية ، ولبعض الأدعية الرقيقة التي يختم بها فصوله على سبيل الإيناس والتذكير وشحذ الهمم واستلهام التوفيق ، ولبعض الأشعار والحكم والأقوال التي ترطب جفاف النص وتطرّي من خشونته ، وكم لهذا الملمح من أثر طيب ، وطمأنينة تسري في النفوس ، فلله دره من عالم تحرير موفّق ! وقد لوّح الإمام السنوسي بوفاء هذه الطريقة في عرض الاعتقاد بالمقصود ، وبركتها وسلامتها من كدر الخوض فيما لا يعني ؛ فقد قال في طالعة كتابه ( المنهج السديد ( المعروف بـ ( شرح الجزائرية ) وهو يتحدث عن ( منظومة الإمام أبي العباس الجزائري » : ( إذ هو منظوم مشتمل على طريقي هداية الخواص والعوام ؛ لأنه قد ضم فيه إلى حلاوة النظم المستميلة للطباع تقرير الأدلة البرهانية للعقائد على التمام ، ثم وشّحها بخطابات تصوفية تهزُّ النفوس النائمة لتعظيم جناب الحق ويدخل بها الضعيف مع القوي في سلك الانتظام ، وتلك سنة الله تعالى في تقرير الأدلة في كتابه العزيز ، ثم سنة مصطفاه الرسول صلى الله عليه وسلم . وما أبركها من طريقة ، وأنصحها من دلالة ! لتضمنها الهداية العامة ، وإنالة البغية لكل موفق يروم إلى الحق الوصول ).
The layered [compilation] which inevitably contains new useful [material]; for the texts, despite their varying sizes, you see repetition in their general features, but with newness in the style of presentation, diversification in the proof and example, and an increase unique to each 'Sanusiyya' that is not found in others. How often you see scholars quoting and editing from our Imam al-Sanusi, concluding with their saying: 'as in Sharh al-ʿAqida al-Kubra,' or 'Sharh al-ʿAqida al-Wusta,' or 'Sharh al-ʿAqida al-Sughra,' and they cannot suffice with (Sharh al-ʿAqida al-Kubra) for example over others. And every observer knows the diversity of discourse in which diversity of the addressee was taken into account; for al-Sanusi had a good intention in rescuing the muqallids (blind followers) from all strata; so you see him addressing the commoner and the specialist, the young and the old, the woman and the boy, in language keen on making understand and clarifying, without preoccupation with embellishing expression. And despite all this, his Sanusiyyat (works) came sweet in words, lucid in expressions. Then among the wonderful features in this well-ordered series: the purposeful and deliberate blending of some gnostic allusions in the manner of the Sufi masters, and some delicate supplications with which he concludes his chapters by way of cheering, reminding, sharpening determination, and seeking inspiration for success, and some poems, wisdoms, and sayings that moisten the dryness of the text and soften its roughness. How great an effect this feature has, and what tranquility it spreads in souls! So blessed be he, a writer, a successful scholar! And Imam al-Sanusi hinted at the faithfulness of this method in presenting creed to the intended [purpose], its blessedness, and its safety from the trouble of delving into what does not concern; for he said in the preface of his book al-Minhaj al-Sadid (known as Sharh al-Jaza'iriyya) while speaking about 'the poem of Imam Abu al-ʿAbbas al-Jaza'iri': (For it is a poem comprising the two ways of guidance of the elite and the commoners; because he included in it, along with the sweetness of poetry that attracts natures, the establishment of demonstrative proofs for beliefs in full, then he adorned it with Sufi discourses that shake the sleeping souls to glorify the court of Truth, and by it he brings the weak along with the strong into the order of organization. And that is the way of Allah the Exalted in presenting proofs in His Mighty Book, then the way of His Chosen Messenger, peace and blessings be upon him. How blessed is this method, and how sincere as guidance! For it includes general guidance and the attainment of the wish for every successful one who intends to reach the truth.)
Source pages p. 55

Commentary
This passage is a descriptive and commentary section that highlights the distinctive features of Imam al-Sanusi's series of creedal works (the Sanusiyyat). The author begins by noting that although Sanusi's texts share common content, each work brings newness in style, proof, and examples. He then stresses the wide range of audiences Sanusi addressed—from scholars to laymen, young to old, men to women—using clear language without rhetorical embellishment. The passage then turns to a key feature: the deliberate inclusion of Sufi references, gentle supplications, and poetic wisdom to enliven the text and console the soul. To support this, the author quotes Sanusi's own preface to his commentary on al-Jaza'iri's poem, where Sanusi praises the poem for combining rational proofs with spiritual discourse, making it suitable for both the elite and the common people. Sanusi states that this method follows the divine and prophetic practice of presenting guidance. The passage concludes by extolling this approach as blessed and universally guiding.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think Sanusi's works are merely popularized summaries without depth.
Correction: They are carefully composed with both rational proofs and spiritual elements, designed for varied audiences.

B) Sufi allusions, supplications, and poetry alongside clear language

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Sanusi's method combines clear rational demonstrations with spiritual nourishment to guide both elite and common people.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you seek knowledge of ʿaqīda, do you intentionally look for both clear proofs and spiritual sweetness, or do you lean toward one side? What would it take for you to cultivate a balanced approach like Sanusi's?
?
Sanusi wrote for the commoner and the specialist alike. In your own sharing of Islamic knowledge, do you adapt your language to your audience, or do you assume everyone understands the same way? How can you step out of your comfort zone to reach those who are different from you?
?
Sanusi included supplications and poetry to 'moisten the dryness of the text' and 'shake the sleeping souls'. When you study theology, is your heart stirred, or are you merely collecting information? What will you change today to let the truths you learn penetrate your soul?

Method of Learning

The Danger of Over-Reliance on Sanusi's Works
Editorial commentary on over-reliance on Sanusi's texts

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the significance of al-‘Attar's warning against treating any single text, even Sanusi's, as the sole authority in Islamic theology.

Core Idea
Exclusive reliance on one text blinds us to the broader scholarly tradition.

In this lesson, we examine a passage from the editor of Sanusi's commentary, who quotes the renowned Azhari scholar Hasan al-‘Attar. Al-‘Attar laments the tendency of his contemporaries to treat Sanusi's al-Sughra and its commentaries as the sole authority, dismissing other works. The editor then highlights how this problem has worsened in later times, with students and teachers prioritizing shortcuts and partisan works over deep, nuanced study. This lesson invites us to reflect on proper scholarly methodology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the English translation. Let the author's own words come through before you interpret.
Notice the editor's use of al-‘Attar's quote as a springboard for his own commentary.
2
Identify al-‘Attar's core criticism
Find what exactly al-‘Attar is criticizing. Is he attacking Sanusi's works themselves? Look at the phrasing: 'لا من باب الاعتراض على ما حوته من علم جم'.
The criticism is about the attitude of scholars, not the content of Sanusi's works.
3
Understand the editor's defense of Sanusi
After quoting al-‘Attar, the editor immediately clarifies that this is not an attack on Sanusi. Note the phrase 'فحاشا أن يفهم منه الطعن في السنوسيات'.
The editor distinguishes between the text and the improper use of it.
4
Reflect on the contemporary problems described
The editor lists several ills: superficial 'quick academic meals', harmful abridgments, flattering authors, and bigoted teachers. Consider how these might appear today.
The passage warns against both blind partisanship and negligence of proper scholarship.
5
Connect to your own approach to knowledge
Ask yourself: Do I rely on a single source or teacher? Do I dismiss other works without consideration? How can I emulate the verifiers (المحققون) who engage deeply with multiple sources?
This lesson is not just historical; it is a call to balance reverence with critical engagement.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد بلغ شأن « السنوسيات » عموماً وشرح العقيدة الصغرى » خصوصاً شأواً جعل بعض محققي العلماء يتضجر من ذلك ، لا من باب الاعتراض على ما حوته من علم جم ، بل أن يُخطَّأ ما سواها لمجرَّد مخالفتها ، حتى قال العلامة الشيخ حسن العطار في ( حاشيته على شرح المحلي لجمع الجوامع » : ( ليت هذا القائل عاش حتى الآن ؛ ليرى ما يقوله المدرسون في دروسهم ، بل ما ينقله المؤلفون في عصرنا ممن يتعلّق بعلم الكلام ؛ فإنهم اتخذوا « الصغرى » وما كتب عليها من الحواشي والشروح عمدة وإماماً ، ولم تطمح نفوسهم بما قرره محققو هاذا الفن في كتبهم ، حتى إنه لو أُتي لواحد منهم بنقل ساطع أو برهان قاطع .. لم يعدل عمَّا استقر في ذهنه مما يخالف الصواب ، وقال : لا أعدل عمَّا رأيته في ذلك الكتاب )(۱) وهذه الكلمة من شيخ الأزهر العلامة العطار يقدر قدرها العالم والموفق ، فحاشا أن يفهم منه الطعن في السنوسيات » ، بل هو تنبيه على ألا تجعل - لا سيما ( الصغرى » و « شروحها » ـ كل شيء في هذا العلم المترامي الأطراف ؛ فقد ترى تحقيقاً في مسألة أو أكثر لا تجده أو تجد خلافه فيها ولو أنَّ العلامة العطار عاش حتى الآن ، ورأى حال طلبة علم العقائد والكلام ، وما هم عليه من اهتمام بالوجبات العلمية السريعة ، والاختصارات المحدثة المخلَّة وغير المحققة ، والتأليفات الهشة التي داهن فيها مؤلفوها أهل البدع وجانبوا المداراة ، وأخرى تعصّب فيها أصحابها دون غيرهم فضيقوا واسعاً ، وجعلوا الجنةَ وَقْفاً على أهل السنة ورأى إلى ذلك أحوال مدرّسي هاذا العلم العظيم ، والذين عامتهم تلقلقُ ألسنتهم بمصطلحات هذا العلم دون دراية ، وبعضُهم قد أضمر الخصومة له وهو يدرسه محتسباً بزعمه ليصحح الأخطاء ويردَّ البدع التي انتشرت فيه ! ولا يعلم أنه قد ضلَّ بفعله وأضل (١) . . لرجا المولى تعالى أن تعود تلك العصبيَّة لهذه المدرسة العريقة ، وأن يؤول الأمر إلى ما كان عليه ، والشكوى إلى المولى العظيم
And the status of the 'Sanusiyyat' in general and the commentary on 'al-Aqida al-Sughra' in particular reached such a level that it annoyed some of the verifying scholars, not due to objection to what they contained of abundant knowledge, but because whatever differed from them was deemed mistaken merely for contradicting them. Until the scholar Sheikh Hasan al-‘Attar said in his gloss on al-Mahalli’s commentary on Jam‘ al-Jawami‘: 'Would that this speaker were alive now to see what the teachers say in their lessons, rather what the authors of our time transmit from those concerned with kalam; for they have taken 'al-Sughra' and the glosses and commentaries written upon it as the primary reference and imam, and their aspirations did not reach what the verifiers of this art established in their books, so that even if a clear transmission or conclusive proof were brought to one of them, he would not turn from what had settled in his mind that contradicts correctness, and he would say: I will not turn from what I saw in that book.' (1) And this statement from the Sheikh of al-Azhar, the scholar al-‘Attar – the knowledgeable and successful person measures its worth. Far be it that one understand from it an attack on the 'Sanusiyyat'; rather it is a warning not to make – especially 'al-Sughra' and its commentaries – everything in this vast science. For you may find a verification on a certain issue or more that you do not find [elsewhere] or find its contrary in it. And if the scholar al-‘Attar were alive now and saw the condition of the students of creed and kalam, and their preoccupation with quick academic meals, and the harmful and unverified new abridgments, and the flimsy compositions whose authors flattered the people of innovation and avoided conciliation, and others in which their partisans were bigoted without others, narrowing what was broad, making Paradise a monopoly for Ahl al-Sunna, and he also saw the conditions of the teachers of this great science, most of whom babble the terminology of this science without knowledge, and some of them have hidden hostility towards it while studying it, reckoning themselves [to be doing] good to correct errors and refute the innovations that have spread in it, not knowing that he has gone astray and led others astray (1) – he would hope from Allah Almighty that that partisanship would return to this venerable school, and that the matter would return to what it was. And the complaint is to Allah Almighty.
Source pages p. 58

Commentary
This passage is an editorial commentary on the elevated status of Sanusi’s works, particularly 'al-Aqida al-Sughra' and its commentaries, to the point that some scholars became annoyed. The annoyance is not with the knowledge itself, but with the practice of dismissing anything that contradicts these texts merely for being different. The editor then quotes Hasan al-‘Attar, a prominent Azhari scholar, who expresses regret that earlier critics of such over-reliance did not witness the current situation where teachers and authors treat al-Sughra and its glosses as the sole authority, ignoring the works of the verifiers of the art. Even a clear proof would not turn them from their entrenched positions. Al-‘Attar’s statement is then defended as a warning, not an attack on Sanusi’s works. The editor then imagines al-‘Attar’s reaction if he saw the current state: students focusing on superficial summaries, flawed abridgments, flattering authors, and bigoted teachers who misuse terminology without understanding, even harboring hostility while pretending to correct errors. The editor hopes for a return to true attachment to this school and ends with complaint to Allah.

Key Terms
السنوسيات
الصغرى
العطار
علم الكلام
المحققون

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think al-‘Attar is criticizing Sanusi's works themselves, implying they are flawed or should be abandoned.
Correction: Al-‘Attar's criticism is directed at the practice of making Sanusi's works the exclusive authority, not at their content. He warns against dismissing all other scholarship.

Checkpoint
Why did al-‘Attar lament the state of his time according to the passage?
A) Because Sanusi's works were being ignored
B) Because students and teachers relied exclusively on al-Sughra and its commentaries, disregarding other works
C) Because the scholars were writing too many books
D) Because the science of kalam was dead

Key Takeaway
Al-‘Attar's criticism is not of Sanusi's works but of the exclusive reliance on them to the exclusion of other scholarly contributions.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you study 'aqida, do you find yourself limiting your sources to one book or teacher, dismissing others without investigation? What would change today if you committed to seeking verification from multiple authoritative works?
?
Al-‘Attar says even a clear proof would not turn some from what is in their book. Is there a belief you hold that you refuse to re-examine, despite evidence? Are you willing to test your own certainty against the broader tradition?
?
The editor warns against making Paradise a monopoly for Ahl al-Sunna. Do you ever feel a sense of superiority or exclusion towards other Muslims? How can you cultivate humility and sincerity in seeking truth, not just confirming your group?

Al-Ghazali's Three Conditions for Teaching Creed
Al-Ghazali enumerates three qualities for teachers of tawhid.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three characteristics al-Ghazali requires for a teacher of tawhid and why each is essential.

Core Idea
Teaching sacred creed requires devotion, intelligence, and piety.

Al-Ghazali, one of Islam's greatest scholars, outlined three essential qualities for anyone who would teach the science of tawhid and creed. These are not just desirable but necessary to ensure that the teacher can properly convey the faith and protect students from doubt and error. Let's examine each condition as he presents it in his work.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Familiarize yourself with al-Ghazali's words and the editor's context. Notice the three conditions are introduced with 'First', 'Second', 'Third'.
2
Identify the three conditions
List each condition and note the reasoning al-Ghazali gives for each. Write them down in your own words.
3
Reflect on why each condition matters
Consider what could go wrong if a teacher lacks devotion, or intelligence, or piety. How does each condition protect the student and the religion?
Think about the specific harms al-Ghazali mentions.
4
Note the editor's addition
The editor points out that the third condition also applies to someone corrupt in belief (i.e., a heretic). Reflect on why correct belief itself might be necessary for teaching creed.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإليك هذه الذكرى في صفات المتعلم بل والمتصدر لتدريس علم التوحيد والعقائد ، وهي صفات ذكرها حجة الإسلام إمامنا الغزالي في كتابه ( قواعد العقائد ( من كتب » إحياء علوم الدين ) ( ١ / ٣٦٠ ) حيث قال : : ( العالم به ينبغي أن يخصص بتعليم هذا العلم مَنْ فيه ثلاث خصال : إحداها : التجرد للعلم والحرص عليه ؛ فإن المحترف يمنعه الشغل عن الاستتمام وإزالة الشكوك إذا عرضت • والثانية : الذكاء والفطنة والفصاحة ؛ فإنَّ البليد لا ينتفع بفهمه ، والفدم لا ينتفع بحجاجه ، فيخاف عليه من ضرر الكلام ، ولا يرجى فيه نفعه والثالثة : أن يكون في طبعه الصلاح والديانة والتقوى ، ولا تكون الشهوات غالبة عليه ؛ فإن الفاسق بأدنى شبهة ينخلع عن الدين ؛ فإن ذلك يحلُّ عنه الحجر ويرفع بينه وبين الملاذ ) ، وهذه الثالثة يدخل فيها الفاسق بالاعتقاد كما لا يخفى
And here is this reminder concerning the qualities of the student, rather the one who takes up teaching the science of tawhid and creeds—qualities mentioned by the Proof of Islam, our Imam al-Ghazali, in his book 'Qawa‘id al-‘Aqa‘id' from the books of 'Ihya’ ‘Ulum al-Din' (1/360) where he said: 'The scholar of it should dedicate to teaching this science one in whom there are three characteristics: First: devotion to knowledge and eagerness for it, for the professional is prevented by occupation from completing [study] and removing doubts when they arise. Second: intelligence, acumen, and eloquence, for the dull do not benefit from his understanding, and the inarticulate do not benefit from his argumentation; so harm from kalam is feared for him, and no benefit from him is hoped for. Third: that in his nature there be righteousness, religiosity, and piety, and that desires not be dominant over him; for the immoral person renounces religion at the slightest doubt; for that removes the restraint and lifts the barrier between him and pleasures.' And this third includes the one who is immoral in belief, as is not hidden.
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage is a quotation from al-Ghazali’s 'Qawa‘id al-‘Aqa‘id' regarding the qualifications needed for someone to teach the science of tawhid and creeds. Al-Ghazali states that the teacher must possess three characteristics. First, devotion to knowledge and eagerness for it, because a person who is preoccupied by a profession cannot complete the study and remove doubts. Second, intelligence, acumen, and eloquence; a dull person cannot understand correctly, and an inarticulate person cannot argue effectively, so teaching such a person is harmful. Third, righteousness, religiosity, and piety, with desires not dominant; an immoral person abandons religion at the slightest doubt because his restraint is removed. The editor then notes that this third condition also includes someone who is morally corrupt in belief (i.e., heretical).

Key Terms
الغزالي
قواعد العقائد
إحياء علوم الدين
التجرد
الذكاء
الفصاحة
الصلاح
الديانة
التقوى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that the third condition (righteousness) only applies to moral behavior, not to correct belief.
Correction: The editor explicitly states that the third condition includes the one who is immoral in belief (fasiq bil i'tiqad), meaning heretics are also excluded from teaching.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ghazali, why should a teacher not be a professional (i.e., having another occupation)?
A) Because professionals are not intelligent enough
B) Because occupation prevents him from completing study and removing doubts
C) Because professionals are usually immoral
D) Because kalam should not be taught at all

Key Takeaway
Al-Ghazali insists that teachers of creed must be devout, intelligent, and righteous to prevent harm and ensure benefit.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to teach someone about Allah today, which of these three qualities are you lacking most? What will you do to address that before you open your mouth?
?
Al-Ghazali says the immoral renounce religion at the slightest doubt. Are there any sins you are attached to that make you vulnerable to abandoning faith when tested? What step will you take to remove that restraint?
?
This lesson is about teachers, but what about you as a student? Which of these three characteristics do you need to cultivate in yourself to truly benefit from learning tawhid? What will you change starting today?

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
Al-Ghazali's Three Qualities for a Teacher of Tawheed
Al-Ghazali enumerates the three necessary qualities for a teacher/student of tawheed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three essential qualities that al-Ghazali prescribes for anyone teaching or studying the science of tawheed (divine unity).

Core Idea
Teaching tawheed requires devotion, intelligence, and piety.

Al-Ghazali, the great Islamic scholar, lays out three non-negotiable qualities for those who would teach or deeply study the science of divine unity. These are not merely recommended; they are prerequisites to ensure that the sacred knowledge is conveyed safely and effectively. In this lesson, we will explore each quality and its underlying rationale.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Begin by reading al-Ghazali's original Arabic passage and its English translation. Let the words of the Imam settle before moving to analysis.
Notice the careful structure: he introduces the three qualities, then explains each one with a brief reason.
2
Identify the first quality: Devotion to Knowledge
The first quality is 'al-tajarrud lil-ilm' — single-minded devotion to learning. Al-Ghazali warns that worldly occupations prevent a person from completing study and resolving doubts.
3
Identify the second quality: Intelligence and Eloquence
The second quality combines intelligence, acumen, and eloquence. Without these, a person cannot understand or argue effectively, and may even cause harm through kalam.
The word 'fadm' means coarse or dull; such a person is a danger when engaging in theological discussion.
4
Identify the third quality: Righteousness and Piety
The third quality is inner righteousness and dominance of piety over desires. Al-Ghazali explains that an immoral person abandons religion at the slightest doubt because they lack inner restraint.
He adds that this includes those corrupt in belief (heretics), showing the broad danger of lacking piety.
5
Reflect on the interconnection
Consider how each quality supports the others. Devotion ensures time; intelligence ensures understanding; piety ensures steadfastness. All three are needed for safe and effective transmission of aqeedah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإليك هذه الذكرى في صفات المتعلم بل والمتصدر لتدريس علم التوحيد والعقائد، وهي صفات ذكرها حجة الإسلام إمامنا الغزالي في كتابه (قواعد العقائد) من كتب «إحياء علوم الدين» (1/360) حيث قال: (العالم به ينبغي أن يخصص بتعليم هذا العلم مَنْ فيه ثلاث خصال: إحداها: التجرد للعلم والحرص عليه؛ فإن المحترف يمنعه الشغل عن الاستتمام وإزالة الشكوك إذا عرضت. والثانية: الذكاء والفطنة والفصاحة؛ فإن البليد لا ينتفع بفهمه، والفدم لا ينتفع بحجاجه، فيخاف عليه من ضرر الكلام، ولا يرجى فيه نفعه. والثالثة: أن يكون في طبعه الصلاح والديانة والتقوى، ولا تكون الشهوات غالبة عليه؛ فإن الفاسق بأدنى شبهة ينخلع عن الدين؛ فإن ذلك يحلُّ عنه السد الحجر ويرفع بينه وبين الملاذ). وهذه الثالثة يدخل فيها الفاسق بالاعتقاد كما لا يخفى.
And this is a reminder regarding the qualities of the learner, nay, the one who takes up teaching the science of tawheed (divine unity) and the creeds. These are qualities mentioned by the Proof of Islam, our Imam al-Ghazali, in his book (The Rules of the Creeds) from the books of the Revival of the Religious Sciences (1/360) where he said: 'The scholar of it should designate for teaching this science one who possesses three characteristics: The first: devotion to knowledge and eagerness for it; for the professional is prevented by occupation from completing [the study] and removing doubts when they arise. The second: intelligence, acumen, and eloquence; for the dull-witted does not benefit from his understanding, and the coarse does not benefit from his argumentation, so he is feared for harm from kalam (dialectical theology), and no benefit is hoped from him. The third: that he have in his nature righteousness, religiosity, and piety, and that desires not dominate him; for the immoral person is stripped of religion at the slightest doubt, for that loosens the barrier and lifts [the restraint] between him and pleasures.' And this third category includes the immoral in belief, as is not hidden.
Source pages p. 59

Commentary
This passage is a classification and definition. It lists three prerequisites for someone who teaches or studies 'ilm al-tawhid (the science of divine unity). The first characteristic is single-minded devotion to learning, free from worldly distractions. The second is intellectual ability and clarity of speech to ensure proper understanding and effective argument. The third is moral integrity and piety, as a corrupt person will easily abandon faith when confronted with doubts. Al-Ghazali adds that the third includes those corrupt in belief (i.e., heretical), thus emphasizing the danger of lacking piety.

Key Terms
التجرد للعلم
الذكاء والفطنة والفصاحة
الصلاح والديانة والتقوى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that anyone can teach aqeedah without special qualifications.
Correction: Al-Ghazali emphasizes strict criteria: devotion, intelligence, and piety are prerequisites to prevent harm and ensure benefit.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ghazali, what is the second quality required for a teacher of tawheed?
A) Devotion to knowledge and eagerness for it
B) Intelligence, acumen, and eloquence
C) Righteousness, religiosity, and piety
D) Being free from desires

Key Takeaway
Al-Ghazali teaches that teaching tawheed requires devotion, intelligence, and piety to protect the student and preserve the integrity of the sacred knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to honestly assess yourself against these three qualities, which one would be your weakest? What specific action will you take this week to strengthen it?
?
Al-Ghazali says the immoral person is 'stripped of religion at the slightest doubt.' Can you think of a time when a doubt shook you because your desires were dominant? How can you build the piety that holds fast?
?
This lesson sets a high standard for teachers of tawheed. Do you see yourself as someone who will teach others? If so, are you more focused on accumulating knowledge or on becoming the kind of person Al-Ghazali describes?

Distinctive Features: Clarity, Veneration, and Taqlid
Editor introduces the commentary and lists its first three features.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the first three distinctive features of Sanusi's commentary: its clarity and avoidance of jargon, its deep veneration of the Prophet, and its gentle treatment of taqlid.

Core Idea
Sanusi's commentary combines doctrinal clarity with deep reverence for the Prophet.

The editor introduces Sanusi's commentary by praising its unique qualities and then enumerates specific features. In this lesson, we examine the opening remarks and the first three features, which highlight the commentary's accessible style, its emphasis on love for the Prophet, and its careful handling of the issue of blind following in belief. These features set the tone for the entire work.

Guided Path
1
Read the introductory remarks
Read the passage from the beginning through the description by al-Mallali. Notice how the editor sets the stage for the commentary's importance.
This is an editor's preface, not from al-Sanusi himself.
2
Identify the first feature
Look for the first bullet point: ease of expression and clarity of proof, avoiding technical jargon. Reflect on why this would benefit students.
3
Examine the second feature
The second bullet discusses veneration of the Prophet through lengthy introductory pages. Note that nearly a quarter of the book is devoted to this.
4
Consider the third feature
The third bullet covers gentleness in the matter of taqlid. Think about why taqlid is a sensitive issue in theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد نبه رضي الله عنه فيها على نكت فائقة، ودرر رائقة، لم يذكرها في العقائد السابقة. ثم بعدما رأى الإمام السنوسي انتشار « صغرى الصغرى »، وظهور بعض الاستفسارات عن عباراتها .. تصدَّى - على سنته في تأليفه - لشرحها، فجاء شرح صغرى الصغرى شرحاً نفيساً يستغني عنه طالب مستبحر، وقد وصفه تلميذه الملالي بقوله: (وفيه فوائد عجيبة، ونكت غريبة). وممَّا ستراه في ( شرح صغرى الصغرى » :
- سهولة العبارة، ووضوح الدليل، والابتعاد قدر الاستطاعة عن الاصطلاحات الكلامية، وهو مسلك متناغم مع أصلها ( صغرى الصغرى ) أو ( العقيدة المختصرة ).
- تعظيم الجناب النبوي الشريف بكتابة صفحات طويلة في بدايته ففاتحته سلامٌ، وخاتمته مسك وطيب، ولا نبالغ إن قلنا : قريب من ربع الكتاب كان لتحقيق هذا المقصد الاعتقادي الذي قد يغفل عنه كثير من الكتاب والباحثين في الاعتقادات.
- الترفق يسيراً في مسألة التقليد.
- تحريره البديع الذي لا يستغني عنه طالب علم متخصص لمسألة وجه دلالة المعجزة على صدق الرسول بعبارة في غاية الوجازة والوضوح.
- اختياره هنا لنفي الأحوال والصفات المعنوية على القول بها التوسع في الحديث عن الصفات الواجبة للأنبياء عليهم الصلاة والسلام.
- ميله للأسلوب الأدبي في كثير من مقطعات الكتاب؛ ليريح قارئه من عسر بعض الأبحاث.
- عجن النص بالتربية الأخلاقية، وتعميق مفهوم العبودية في نفس القارئ.
- تحرير مسألة الأفضلية لحضرة مولانا ونبينا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام.
ويمكننا أن نقول : يصلحُ أن يكون هذا الشرح المبارك عرضاً للعقيدة الإسلامية على مسامع العامة والخاصة؛ فينتفع العامي بعموم مباحثه ويرسخ الاعتقاد في فؤاده، وينتعش المتخصص بنكاته وغريب تحريراته وفوائده، نفع الله به وبسائر تصانیف مؤلفه، آمين.
And he (may Allah be pleased with him) pointed out in it subtle points and splendid pearls that he did not mention in the previous creeds. Then, after Imam al-Sanusi saw the spread of 'al-Sughra al-Sughra' and the appearance of some inquiries about its phrases, he undertook – according to his habit in authorship – to comment on it, so the commentary on al-Sughra al-Sughra came as a precious commentary that even a profound student does not need [another], and his student al-Mallali described it saying: 'It contains marvelous benefits and strange subtle points.' Among what you will see in the commentary on al-Sughra al-Sughra: ease of expression, clarity of proof, avoiding as much as possible the technical terms of kalam – an approach harmonious with its original (al-Sughra al-Sughra) or (al-Mukhtasara al-Mukhtasara); veneration of the noble prophetic station by writing long pages at its beginning – its opening is peace, its seal is musk and perfume, and we do not exaggerate if we say close to a quarter of the book was for the realization of this doctrinal objective that many writers and researchers in beliefs may neglect; being gentle in the matter of taqlid; his excellent analysis that a specialized student of knowledge cannot do without on the issue of the manner of indication of the miracle to the truthfulness of the Messenger in extremely concise and clear wording; his choice here to negate the modes (ahwal) and the attributive attributes (al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya) according to those who affirm them, expanding the discussion on the necessary attributes of the prophets, peace be upon them; his inclination to the literary style in many sections of the book to relieve his reader from the difficulty of some researches; kneading the text with moral education and deepening the concept of servitude in the reader's soul; and his analysis of the issue of precedence (afdaliyya) for our master and prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. And we can say: This blessed commentary is suitable to be a presentation of the Islamic creed to the ears of the common and the elite; the common person benefits from its general discussions and belief becomes rooted in his heart, and the specialist revives from its subtle points and rare analyses and benefits. May Allah benefit [people] through it and through the rest of its author's compositions. Amen.
Source pages p. 61

Commentary
This passage is an editorial preface describing the distinctive features of Imam al-Sanusi's commentary on his own work 'al-Sughra al-Sughra' (also called 'al-Mukhtasara al-Mukhtasara'). The editor begins by noting that al-Sanusi included in this commentary subtle points and precious pearls not found in his earlier creedal works. After the widespread acceptance of 'al-Sughra al-Sughra' and inquiries about its expressions, al-Sanusi followed his customary method and wrote a commentary. The editor then cites a student of al-Sanusi, al-Mallali, who praised the commentary as containing 'marvelous benefits and strange subtle points.' The editor proceeds to enumerate eight specific features of the commentary:

1. Ease of expression and clarity of proof, avoiding technical kalam terminology as much as possible, consistent with the original abridged work.
2. Veneration of the Prophet through lengthy introductory pages, with a focus on love and respect, occupying nearly a quarter of the book – a matter often neglected by writers on creed.
3. Gentle treatment of the issue of taqlid (blind following) in belief.
4. Excellent analysis of how a miracle indicates the truthfulness of a prophet, expressed with extreme conciseness and clarity.
5. Rejection of the notions of 'ahwal' (states) and 'al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya' (attributive attributes) according to those who affirm them, while expanding discussion of the necessary attributes of prophets.
6. Occasional use of literary style to relieve the reader from difficult topics.
7. Integration of moral education and deepening the concept of servitude to Allah.
8. Analysis of the issue of superiority (afdaliyya) of Prophet Muhammad over all other prophets.

Finally, the editor concludes that this commentary serves both the common person and the specialist: the common person benefits from its general discussions to strengthen belief, while the specialist gains from its subtle points and unique insights.

Key Terms
صغرى الصغرى
شرح صغرى الصغرى
السنوسي
التقليد
الملالي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think this passage is by al-Sanusi himself, but it is actually the editor's preface.
Correction: This is the editor's description of the commentary, not al-Sanusi's own words.

Checkpoint
According to the editor, what proportion of the commentary is dedicated to venerating the Prophet?
A) One quarter
B) One third
C) One half
D) One tenth

Key Takeaway
Sanusi's commentary is distinguished by its clarity, deep veneration of the Prophet, and a gentle approach to the issue of taqlid.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you study the attributes of Allah or matters of creed, do you seek clarity and simple proofs, or do you prefer complex academic language? Which approach would bring you closer to certainty? Commit to seeking clear explanations first.
?
The commentator devoted nearly a quarter of his book to veneration of the Prophet. How much space does the Prophet occupy in your daily reflections on faith? What specific act will you do today to honor him in your heart and actions?
?
Are you comfortable with blind following (taqlid) in matters of belief, or have you sought to understand the evidence yourself? What is one belief you hold that you cannot prove from scripture? Will you seek its proof before tonight?

Miracle Indication and Negation of Ahwal
Editor lists two features regarding miracle proof and theological positions on attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand two advanced features of Sanusi's commentary: his concise analysis of how miracles prove prophethood, and his rejection of the concepts of 'ahwal' and 'attributive attributes'.

Core Idea
Sanusi provides a brilliant, concise explanation of miracle-based proof and takes a clear stance on theological subtleties.

The editor continues listing distinctive features, now turning to more specialized theological topics. Sanusi's commentary includes a masterful analysis of how miracles indicate the truthfulness of a prophet, expressed with extreme conciseness and clarity. Additionally, he chooses to reject certain concepts (ahwal and attributive attributes) while expanding on the necessary attributes of prophets. These features show the commentary's depth for advanced students.

Guided Path
1
Read the fourth feature
Locate the bullet point about 'tahrir al-badi'' regarding the miracle's indication. Understand what is being analyzed: how a miracle proves the messenger's truthfulness.
2
Read the fifth feature
The next point concerns the negation of ahwal and al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya. Note that this is a specific theological position.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider why these two features are particularly valuable for a specialized student. They address complex issues with clarity and decisiveness.

?

?

Literary Style, Moral Education, and Superiority of the Prophet
Editor lists the last three features and gives a final assessment of the commentary's value.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the remaining features of Sanusi's commentary: its literary style, integration of moral education, and analysis of the Prophet's superiority, as well as the editor's overall assessment.

Core Idea
Sanusi's commentary is not just theological; it is also literary, ethical, and deeply reverent of the Prophet.

The editor concludes the list of features with three more qualities that make this commentary unique. Sanusi occasionally adopts a literary style to ease difficult topics, weaves moral education and the concept of servitude throughout, and provides a thorough analysis of the superiority (afdaliyya) of Prophet Muhammad over all other prophets. The editor then sums up that this commentary serves both the common person and the specialist.

Guided Path
1
Read the sixth feature
Look for the point about 'inclination to literary style' and understand its purpose: to relieve the reader from difficult research.
2
Read the seventh feature
The seventh bullet mentions 'kneading the text with moral education' and deepening the concept of servitude. Reflect on the pedagogical value.
3
Read the eighth feature
The eighth point is about the analysis of the superiority (afdaliyya) of Prophet Muhammad. Consider why this is an important doctrinal issue.
4
Read the concluding remarks
The editor states that the commentary benefits both the common person and the specialist. Note the inclusive nature of the work.

⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد نبه رضي الله عنه فيها على نكت فائقة، ودرر رائقة، لم يذكرها في العقائد السابقة. ثم بعدما رأى الإمام السنوسي انتشار « صغرى الصغرى »، وظهور بعض الاستفسارات عن عباراتها .. تصدَّى - على سنته في تأليفه - لشرحها، فجاء شرح صغرى الصغرى شرحاً نفيساً يستغني عنه طالب مستبحر، وقد وصفه تلميذه الملالي بقوله: (وفيه فوائد عجيبة، ونكت غريبة). وممَّا ستراه في ( شرح صغرى الصغرى » :
- سهولة العبارة، ووضوح الدليل، والابتعاد قدر الاستطاعة عن الاصطلاحات الكلامية، وهو مسلك متناغم مع أصلها ( صغرى الصغرى ) أو ( العقيدة المختصرة ).
- تعظيم الجناب النبوي الشريف بكتابة صفحات طويلة في بدايته ففاتحته سلامٌ، وخاتمته مسك وطيب، ولا نبالغ إن قلنا : قريب من ربع الكتاب كان لتحقيق هذا المقصد الاعتقادي الذي قد يغفل عنه كثير من الكتاب والباحثين في الاعتقادات.
- الترفق يسيراً في مسألة التقليد.
- تحريره البديع الذي لا يستغني عنه طالب علم متخصص لمسألة وجه دلالة المعجزة على صدق الرسول بعبارة في غاية الوجازة والوضوح.
- اختياره هنا لنفي الأحوال والصفات المعنوية على القول بها التوسع في الحديث عن الصفات الواجبة للأنبياء عليهم الصلاة والسلام.
- ميله للأسلوب الأدبي في كثير من مقطعات الكتاب؛ ليريح قارئه من عسر بعض الأبحاث.
- عجن النص بالتربية الأخلاقية، وتعميق مفهوم العبودية في نفس القارئ.
- تحرير مسألة الأفضلية لحضرة مولانا ونبينا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام.
ويمكننا أن نقول : يصلحُ أن يكون هذا الشرح المبارك عرضاً للعقيدة الإسلامية على مسامع العامة والخاصة؛ فينتفع العامي بعموم مباحثه ويرسخ الاعتقاد في فؤاده، وينتعش المتخصص بنكاته وغريب تحريراته وفوائده، نفع الله به وبسائر تصانیف مؤلفه، آمين.
And he (may Allah be pleased with him) pointed out in it subtle points and splendid pearls that he did not mention in the previous creeds. Then, after Imam al-Sanusi saw the spread of 'al-Sughra al-Sughra' and the appearance of some inquiries about its phrases, he undertook – according to his habit in authorship – to comment on it, so the commentary on al-Sughra al-Sughra came as a precious commentary that even a profound student does not need [another], and his student al-Mallali described it saying: 'It contains marvelous benefits and strange subtle points.' Among what you will see in the commentary on al-Sughra al-Sughra: ease of expression, clarity of proof, avoiding as much as possible the technical terms of kalam – an approach harmonious with its original (al-Sughra al-Sughra) or (al-Mukhtasara al-Mukhtasara); veneration of the noble prophetic station by writing long pages at its beginning – its opening is peace, its seal is musk and perfume, and we do not exaggerate if we say close to a quarter of the book was for the realization of this doctrinal objective that many writers and researchers in beliefs may neglect; being gentle in the matter of taqlid; his excellent analysis that a specialized student of knowledge cannot do without on the issue of the manner of indication of the miracle to the truthfulness of the Messenger in extremely concise and clear wording; his choice here to negate the modes (ahwal) and the attributive attributes (al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya) according to those who affirm them, expanding the discussion on the necessary attributes of the prophets, peace be upon them; his inclination to the literary style in many sections of the book to relieve his reader from the difficulty of some researches; kneading the text with moral education and deepening the concept of servitude in the reader's soul; and his analysis of the issue of precedence (afdaliyya) for our master and prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. And we can say: This blessed commentary is suitable to be a presentation of the Islamic creed to the ears of the common and the elite; the common person benefits from its general discussions and belief becomes rooted in his heart, and the specialist revives from its subtle points and rare analyses and benefits. May Allah benefit [people] through it and through the rest of its author's compositions. Amen.
Source pages p. 61

Commentary
This passage is an editorial preface describing the distinctive features of Imam al-Sanusi's commentary on his own work 'al-Sughra al-Sughra' (also called 'al-Mukhtasara al-Mukhtasara'). The editor begins by noting that al-Sanusi included in this commentary subtle points and precious pearls not found in his earlier creedal works. After the widespread acceptance of 'al-Sughra al-Sughra' and inquiries about its expressions, al-Sanusi followed his customary method and wrote a commentary. The editor then cites a student of al-Sanusi, al-Mallali, who praised the commentary as containing 'marvelous benefits and strange subtle points.' The editor proceeds to enumerate eight specific features of the commentary:

1. Ease of expression and clarity of proof, avoiding technical kalam terminology as much as possible, consistent with the original abridged work.
2. Veneration of the Prophet through lengthy introductory pages, with a focus on love and respect, occupying nearly a quarter of the book – a matter often neglected by writers on creed.
3. Gentle treatment of the issue of taqlid (blind following) in belief.
4. Excellent analysis of how a miracle indicates the truthfulness of a prophet, expressed with extreme conciseness and clarity.
5. Rejection of the notions of 'ahwal' (states) and 'al-sifat al-ma'nawiyya' (attributive attributes) according to those who affirm them, while expanding discussion of the necessary attributes of prophets.
6. Occasional use of literary style to relieve the reader from difficult topics.
7. Integration of moral education and deepening the concept of servitude to Allah.
8. Analysis of the issue of superiority (afdaliyya) of Prophet Muhammad over all other prophets.

Finally, the editor concludes that this commentary serves both the common person and the specialist: the common person benefits from its general discussions to strengthen belief, while the specialist gains from its subtle points and unique insights.

Key Terms
الأفضلية
العبودية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is one purpose of Sanusi's use of literary style?
A) To show off his writing ability
B) To relieve the reader from difficult research
C) To make the book longer
D) To confuse the common reader

Key Takeaway
Sanusi's commentary combines literary style, moral education, and analysis of the Prophet's superiority, making it beneficial for both common and specialized readers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read Islamic texts, do you seek only dry facts, or do you allow yourself to be moved by the literary beauty and moral lessons? How can you cultivate a deeper sense of servitude in your heart from the texts you read?
?
The commentary aims to benefit both the common person and the specialist. Which category do you fall into, and what specific step will you take today to move from being a passive reader to an active learner who lets belief become rooted in your heart?
?
If you truly believed that Prophet Muhammad is superior to all creation, how would that change the way you follow his example in your daily decisions? What one habit would you adjust starting today?

Editorial Methodology of Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā
Explains the editorial method: manuscript sources, collation, annotations.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the methodology used in editing this commentary, including the number of manuscripts, collation process, and additional annotations.

Core Idea
The edition of Sanusi's works is built on six manuscript copies and enriched with scholarly glosses.

This lesson explains how the editors prepared the text you are reading. It is important to know the care taken to ensure accuracy and clarity. The passage from the editor's introduction details the sources and methods used.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the editor's description of the widespread fame of Sanusi's works.
2
Identify the editorial goals
Look for the editor's stated goals: a well-dressed edition, solid text, no ambiguity, flexible style, titled paragraphs.
These goals guide the entire editorial process.
3
Note manuscript selection and collation
The passage mentions using six manuscript copies for Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā, taking one as base, collating with others, and recording variants.
This ensures textual accuracy.
4
Recognize the additional annotations
The edition includes scholarly annotations, especially glosses by Aḥmad al-Baylī. Consider the value of these additions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
منهج العمل في الكتاب
عمومُ كُتُب الإمام السنوسي كُتِبَ لها الذيوع والانتشار كما لا يخفى ، و « سنوسياته » من الكتب العقدية التي لا تكاد تخلو منها مكتبة باحث في الأصول ، وقد كان الجهد مبذولاً لإحياء هذه السلسلة المباركة ضمن طبعة قشيبة الزيّ متينة النص ، لا غمغمة فيها ولا لَبْسَ ، طريَّةٍ مفاصلها ، معنونة فقراتها ، تعين طالب العلم عند النظر فيها على فهمها
ولهذه المقاصد عمدنا إلى جمع ما توافر من النسخ الخطية القريبة العهد من مؤلفها ، الحسنة الضبط على الجملة ، فاعتمدنا لـ « شرح صغرى الصغرى » ست نسخ خطية ، اتخذت أولاها أصلاً ، وعورضت بباقي النسخ المعتمدة ، وأثبتت أبرز الفروق والمغايرات التي قد يكون فيها اعتبار معنى للناظر فيها
وقد أُثري هذا الشرح المبارك بجملة من التعليقات العلمية ؛ من جملتها بعض التقييدات التي كتبها العلامة أحمد البيلي على صغرى الصغرى ، المتوفى سنة ( ١٢١٤هـ ) ، مع بعض
Methodology of work in the book. The books of Imam al-Sanusi are generally widespread and well-known, as is not hidden, and his 'Sanusiyyat' are among the creedal books that a researcher in fundamentals can hardly do without. Effort has been made to revive this blessed series in a well-dressed edition, with solid text, no ambiguity nor confusion, flexible [in style], with its paragraphs titled, helping the student of knowledge when examining it to understand it. For these purposes, we undertook to collect what was available of manuscript copies close in time to its author, generally well-corrected. So we relied for 'Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā' on six manuscript copies, taking the first of them as a base, and collated with the other relied-upon copies, and recorded the most prominent differences and variants that may contain a consideration of meaning for the one examining them. And this blessed commentary has been enriched with a number of scholarly annotations; among them some glosses written by the scholar Aḥmad al-Baylī on Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā, who died in the year 1214 AH, with some...
Source pages p. 63

Commentary
This passage is a statement of editorial methodology. It begins by noting the widespread fame of al-Sanusi's works, especially his 'Sanusiyyat' as indispensable creedal books. The editor then explains the goals of the edition: to present a well-designed edition with clear text, no ambiguity, flexible structure, titled paragraphs, aiding students. To achieve this, the editor collected manuscript copies close in time to the author and generally well-corrected. Specifically, for the commentary 'Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā', six manuscripts were used: the first as base, collated against others, and notable variants were recorded. Additionally, the commentary was enriched with scholarly annotations, including glosses by the scholar Ahmad al-Bayli (d. 1214 AH). The passage cuts off mid-sentence, but the described methodology is complete.

Key Terms

النسخ الخطية
شرح صغرى الصغرى
التعليقات العلمية
أحمد البيلي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The phrase 'طريَّةٍ مفاصلها' refers to the physical flexibility of the book's binding.
Correction: It actually describes the style as smooth and flexible in the sense of clarity and ease, not the physical book.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

?
The goal was a text with 'no ambiguity nor confusion'. Are there areas in your own faith where you have allowed ambiguity to persist? What is one specific belief or practice you will clarify today, and whom will you ask for guidance?

Method of Learning

6 min
The Meaning and Ruling of Al-Hamdu Lillah
Sanusi explains why he begins with praise, its meaning, and its ruling.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of 'Al-hamdu lillah', why Sanusi begins with it, and its ruling of obligation once in a lifetime.

Core Idea
Praising Allah is both a method and a duty.

Sanusi opens his creed with the phrase 'Al-hamdu lillah' (Praise be to God). In this passage, he explains why he does so: following the Quran and the Prophet's instruction, and he defines what praise truly means and its ruling. This lesson unpacks the significance of beginning with praise and the theological implications of attributing all perfection to Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once to get the flow. Then read the English translation to understand the content. Note how Sanusi moves from methodology to definition to ruling.
The passage ends abruptly; the ruling is incomplete.
2
Understand the reason for beginning with praise
Sanusi states he begins with praise following the Quran and the hadith 'every matter of importance that does not begin with praise is cut off.' Reflect on the hadith variants and what they imply about completeness.
The hadith highlights that praise is essential for any important undertaking.
3
Grasp the meaning of al-hamdu lillah
Sanusi defines 'Al-hamdu lillah' as praising God with every perfection. He divides perfection into eternal (divine attribute) and temporal (divine act), concluding that all belongs to God, so He alone deserves genuine praise.
4
Recognize the ruling
The ruling is that this praise is obligatory once in a lifetime, analogous to Hajj and the two shahadas. Note that 'once in a lifetime' does not mean it is not recommended often.
This ruling is a key point often misunderstood.
5
Connect to broader aqeedah
Consider why Sanusi starts his entire creed with this: it establishes the foundation that all perfection is Allah's, which underlies all subsequent theological discussions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الكلام على الحمدلة وما يتعلق بها
الحمد لله.
بدأ بالحمد اقتداء بالكتاب العزيز، وامتثالاً لما رغب فيه المصطفى صلى الله عليه وسلم حيث قال: «كل أمر ذي بال لا يبدأ فيه بـ (الحمد لله)... فهو أبتر»، ويروى: «أجذم»، ويروى: «أقطع»، وكلها على طريق التشبيه البليغ بالأبتر والأجذم والأقطع؛ في العيب المنفر وعدم التمام.
ومعنى (الحمد لله): المدح بكل كمال لله؛ لأن الكمال: إما قديم فهو وصفه، وإما حادث فهو فعله، فالكل إذاً له، فلا يستحق المدح على الحقيقة سواه.
وحكم هذا الحمد: الوجوب مرة في العمر؛ كالحج، وكلمتي
Discussion of the Hamdalah and what pertains to it. Praise be to God. He began with praise following the Noble Book, and in compliance with what the Chosen One, may God bless him and grant him peace, encouraged, where he said: 'Every matter of importance that does not begin with praise be to God... is cut off.' And it is narrated: 'stump-tailed,' and narrated: 'cut-off,' and all of them are on the way of eloquent comparison to the tailless, the stump-tailed, and the cut-off; in repulsive defect and lack of completeness. And the meaning of 'Praise be to God': praise with every perfection to God; because perfection: either eternal so it is His attribute, or temporal so it is His act. Thus all belongs to Him, so none deserves praise in reality except Him. And the ruling of this praise: obligation once in a lifetime; like the Hajj, and the two words
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage is a commentary on the phrase 'الحمد لله' (Praise be to God). It first explains the reason for beginning with praise: following the Quran and the prophetic hadith, which states that any important matter not beginning with praise is 'abtar' (cut off), with variant readings 'ajdham' and 'aqta'', all conveying deficiency. Then it defines the meaning: praise with every perfection to God, because perfection is either eternal (His attribute) or temporal (His act), so all perfection belongs to Him alone, making Him the only one truly deserving praise. Finally, it gives the ruling: this praise is obligatory once in a lifetime, like the Hajj and the two testimonies of faith (the phrase 'and the two words' is incomplete, likely referring to the shahadatayn).

Key Terms
الحمدلة
الحمد
الكمال
الوجوب
الأبتر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The obligation of saying 'Al-hamdu lillah' is for every instance or it is only recommended.
Correction: According to this passage, the ruling is that it is obligatory once in a lifetime, like Hajj, but it is also highly recommended to be said frequently.

Checkpoint
According to the author, why does God alone deserve praise in reality?
A) Because He created everything
B) Because all perfection is either His eternal attribute or His temporal act, so all belongs to Him
C) Because He commanded us to praise Him
D) Because the Prophet said so

Key Takeaway
Al-hamdu lillah acknowledges that all perfection belongs to Allah alone, and it is obligatory to say it at least once in a lifetime.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that every good thing in your life—your health, intellect, wealth—originates from Allah alone? If so, why does your heart often attribute success to your own effort without conscious praise to Him? Commit today to a specific time when you will sincerely say 'Al-hamdu lillah' with full awareness that you owe everything to Him.
?
The Prophet ﷺ said that any important matter not begun with praise is 'cut off'—meaning deficient and incomplete. Think of the last major decision you made (a career move, a relationship choice, a financial commitment). Did you begin it with conscious praise of Allah? If not, do you fear that it might be 'cut off' from blessing? What will you do differently today to ensure every important matter starts with hamd?
?
Sanusi says praise is obligatory once in a lifetime. But have you ever actually fulfilled that obligation with understanding? Or have you only repeated the phrase mechanically? Imagine standing before Allah and being asked: 'Did you ever truly praise Me, recognizing that all perfection is Mine alone?' Answer that question now, in this moment, with a sincere 'Al-hamdu lillah' from the depths of your heart, and commit to never let this praise be just a habit again.

The Meaning and Ruling of Al-Hamd
Sanusi explains the meaning and ruling of al-hamd.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of 'al-hamdu lillah' as praising Allah for all perfection, and the ruling that it is obligatory once in a lifetime.

Core Idea
Al-hamd is not just thanks; it is praise for Allah's eternal and originated perfection.

Sanusi begins his work with the praise of Allah, following the Quran and the Prophet's command. In this passage, he defines what 'al-hamdu lillah' truly means and explains its ruling. Understanding this sets the foundation for the entire study of theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the hadith and its variants.
2
Identify the three parts
The passage has three clear sections: why Sanusi begins with hamd, the definition of hamd, and its ruling. Identify each one and notice how they connect.
The ruling is compared to Hajj and shahada.
3
Reflect on the core idea
Ask yourself: Why does Sanusi emphasize that perfection is either eternal (attribute) or originated (act)? How does this lead to the conclusion that only Allah deserves praise?
This is the heart of the lesson.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الكلام على الحمدلة وما يتعلق بها ] الحمد لله . بدأ بالحمدِ اقتداءً بالكتاب العزيز ، وامتثالاً لِمَا رغَبَ فيه المصطفى صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ حيثُ قال : « كُلُّ أَمْرٍ ذِي بَالٍ لا يُبْدَأُ فِيهِ بِـ ( الْحَمْدُ اللهِ ) . . فَهُوَ أَبْتَرُ » (١) ، ويُروى : « أَجْذَمُ (۲) ، ويُروى : أَقْطَعُ (۳) ، وكلُّها على طريق التشبيه البليغ بالأبتر والأجذمِ والأقطع ؛ في العيب المنفّر وعدم التمام . ومعنى ( الحمدُ للهِ ) : المدح بكل كمال للهِ ؛ لأنَّ الكمال : إمَّا قديم فهو وصفه ، وإمَّا حادث فهو فعلهُ ، فالكل إذاً له ، فلا يستحقُ المدح على الحقيقة سواه . وحكم هذا الحمد : الوجوب مرَّةً في العمرِ ؛ كالحج ، وكلمتي الشهادة ، والصلاة على سيدنا ومولانا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ تسليماً كثيراً كثيراً كثيراً .
Discourse on the Hamdalah and what relates to it. Praise be to God. He began with praise following the Mighty Book, and in compliance with what the Chosen One, God bless him and grant him peace, encouraged, where he said: 'Every matter of importance that is not begun with "Praise be to God" is severed.' And it is narrated: 'maimed' and it is narrated: 'cut off,' and all of them are in the way of eloquent simile with the severed, the maimed, and the cut off, regarding the repulsive defect and lack of completion. The meaning of 'Praise be to God' is: praise with every perfection is for God; because perfection: either it is eternal, and that is His attribute, or it is originated, and that is His act, so all of it belongs to Him. Therefore, no one deserves praise in reality other than Him. And the ruling of this praise is: obligation once in a lifetime, like Hajj, and the two words of testimony, and sending blessings upon our master and lord Muhammad, God bless him and grant him peace, abundantly, abundantly, abundantly.
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage is an explanation of the Hamdalah (the phrase 'al-hamdu lillah'). The author begins by stating that he starts with praise in emulation of the Quran and following the Prophet's hadith. The hadith states that any important matter not begun with 'al-hamdu lillah' is 'abtar' (severed), with variant narrations using 'ajdham' (maimed) and 'aqta' (cut off), all metaphorical for deficiency. Then Sanusi defines 'al-hamdu lillah' as praising God with every perfection, whether eternal (His essential attributes) or originated (His acts). Thus, He alone truly deserves praise. Finally, the ruling of this praise is that it is obligatory once in a lifetime, analogous to Hajj, the two testimonies, and sending blessings on the Prophet. This emphasizes the importance of beginning with praise.

Key Terms
الحمد
الحمدلة
أبتر
وجوب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'al-hamdu lillah' is simply thanking God.
Correction: It means praising God with every perfection, not just thanking.

Checkpoint
What is the ruling on saying 'al-hamdu lillah' according to Sanusi?
A) Sunna (recommended)
B) Obligatory once in a lifetime
C) Obligatory every day
D) Forbidden

Key Takeaway
Al-hamdu lillah is praise for Allah's perfection, and it is obligatory to utter it at least once in a lifetime.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'Alhamdulillah', do you truly mean it as praise for Allah's perfection in both His attributes and His actions? Or do you merely utter it as a habit, unaware of its profound meaning?
?
The hadith warns that any important matter not begun with praise is 'severed, maimed, cut off.' Think of your most important tasks today. Did you start them with genuine al-hamd? If not, what does that reveal about your reliance on Allah?
?
Sanusi states that al-hamd is obligatory once in a lifetime, like Hajj and shahada. Have you ever fulfilled this obligation consciously? Or have you never really 'said' it with this intended meaning? What will you change?

4 min
The Proof After the Claim in Sūrat al-Fātiḥa
Sanusi explains the placement of 'Rabb al-ʿālamīn' as a proof after the claim in 'Al-Ḥamdu li-llāh'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the phrase 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' functions as a rhetorical and theological proof for the claim that all perfection belongs to Allah alone.

Core Idea
The sequence of the Basmala is not just praise—it is proof.

In the Quran, Sūrat al-Fātiḥa begins with 'Al-Ḥamdu li-llāh' (Praise be to Allah) and immediately follows with 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' (Lord of the worlds). Imam Sanusi shows that this is not merely a sequence of titles; it is a rhetorical argument. The first phrase makes a claim about exclusive perfection, and the second provides the evidence. This lesson unpacks that subtle theological reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Identify the claim and the proof
The passage states that the second phrase (Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn) is like a proof after the claim (Al-Ḥamdu li-llāh). Find the claim and see how it is supported.
The claim is that all perfection belongs to Allah alone.
3
Understand the two types of perfection
Sanusi divides perfection into eternal (qadīm) and originated (ḥādith). Notice how he assigns each to Allah: eternal as His attribute, originated as His act.
This is key to the proof.
4
Examine the evidence from the worlds
The worlds (al-ʿālamīn) serve as evidence. Their change (taghayyur) and need for a specific (mukhaṣṣiṣ) prove their origination, which points to Allah as the Creator.
The word 'rabb' implies nurturing, which indicates change.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وذِكْرُ هذا الوصف ؛ وهو ( ربُّ العالمينَ ) بعد ( الحمدُ للهِ ) شبه البرهان بعد الدعوى ؛ لأنَّهُ لمَّا ادَّعى في الجملة الأولى أنَّ كلَّ كمال فهو الله تعالى وحدَهُ ، لا يُمدَحُ عليه في الحقيقة سواه ، وقد عرفتَ أنَّ الكمالَ إمَّا قديمٌ وإِمَّا حادث .. أتى بما يدلُّ على أنَّ كلا الكمالين لهُ تعالى ؛ بمعنى : أنَّ الأول وصفه ، والثاني فعله ، والدليل على ذلكَ : العوالم ؛ لأنَّه قد قام البرهان القطعي على حدوثها من جهة تغيُّرها الذي آذنَتْ بهِ التربية المأخوذةُ مِنْ لفظ ( رَبِّ ) ، ومِنْ جهة احتياجها إلى المُخصِّص في اختصاصها ببعض ما تقبلُهُ مِنْ مقدار.
And the mention of this description—and it is 'Lord of the worlds'—after 'Praise be to Allah' is like a proof after a claim; because when He claimed in the first sentence that every perfection belongs to Allah alone, and none other is truly praised for it—and you have known that perfection is either eternal or originated—He brought what indicates that both kinds of perfection belong to Him: meaning that the first is His attribute (waṣf) and the second is His act (fiʿl). And the evidence for that is the worlds; because a decisive proof has been established for their origination (ḥudūth) by virtue of their change (taghayyur) that the tarbiya (upbringing) taken from the word 'rabb' announces, and by virtue of their need for a specific (mukhaṣṣiṣ) in their specification (ikhtiṣāṣ) with some of what they accept of measure.
Source pages p. 106

Commentary
This passage explains the rhetorical and theological significance of the sequence 'Al-Ḥamdu li-llāh' (Praise be to Allah) followed by 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' (Lord of the worlds). Sanusi states that mentioning the description 'Lord of the worlds' after 'Praise be to Allah' is like a proof (burhān) after a claim (daʿwā). The claim in the first part is that all perfection belongs exclusively to Allah; no one else is truly praised for any perfection. Sanusi then notes that perfection is either eternal (qadīm) or originated (ḥādith). The phrase 'Lord of the worlds' provides proof that both kinds of perfection belong to Allah: eternal perfection is His attribute (waṣf), and originated perfection is His act (fiʿl). The evidence (dalīl) for this is the worlds (al-ʿawālim) themselves, because a decisive proof (burhān qaṭʿī) has been established for their origination (ḥudūth). This origination is evident from two angles: (1) their change (taghayyur), which is indicated by the meaning of 'tarbiya' (upbringing) derived from the word 'rabb'—the world is constantly being nurtured and changed; (2) their need for a specific (mukhaṣṣiṣ) to assign them specific measures and properties, since they could have been otherwise. Therefore, the worlds are originated and thus are acts of an Eternal Creator. This argument reinforces that the Creator possesses both eternal perfection and the act of creation, which is originated perfection. Hence, the second phrase serves as a proof vindicating the claim of the first phrase.

Key Terms
رب العالمين
الحمد لله
برهان
دعوى
كمال
قديم
حادث
تربية
مخصص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may see 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' as merely a title of praise, without recognizing its logical function.
Correction: Sanusi shows it is a logical proof for God's sole perfection and creative power.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why is the phrase 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' placed after 'Al-Ḥamdu li-llāh'?
A) To provide a proof for the claim that all perfection belongs to Allah alone
B) To explain the meaning of tawḥīd linguistically
C) To introduce the subject of the world's creation
D) To refute the polytheists who deny creation

Key Takeaway
The phrase 'Rabb al-ʿĀlamīn' is not just praise but a logical proof that Allah alone possesses all perfection, both eternal attributes and originated acts.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'Al-Ḥamdu li-llāh' in your prayers, do you truly mean that all perfection belongs to Allah alone, or do you find yourself attributing good qualities to others? What specific steps can you take today to redirect your praise solely to Him?
?
This passage shows that the world's constant change and need for a specifier point directly to Allah's lordship. Are you living with that awareness in your daily life, or do you act as if the world is self-sufficient? Pick one routine activity and decide now to see it as a sign of Allah's nurturing.
?
The proof in Sūrat al-Fātiḥa is meant to strengthen your certainty. Are you satisfied with your current level of certainty about Allah's exclusive perfection? What is one doubt you struggle with, and how will you use this logical structure to confront it starting today?

The Spiritual Power of Salawat for the Seeker
Sanusi presents the virtue of salawat and its role for the teacherless seeker.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how sending salawat upon the Prophet can serve as a spiritual substitute for a living teacher and the promise of relief from worry.

Core Idea
Salawat can replace a missing teacher and relieve the seeker's burden.

This passage from Sanusi addresses a critical situation: the sincere seeker who cannot find a qualified spiritual teacher. He presents a powerful solution rooted in the sunna: increasing salawat on the Prophet. The lesson explores the basis for this practice and its profound effect on the soul.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the flow from general virtue to specific recommendation to hadith proof.
The passage is short but dense.
2
Identify the problem and solution
The passage addresses someone who lacks teachers of spiritual training. Find the solution offered and the evidence given.
3
Reflect on the hadith
Consider the hadith about Abu Hurayra. What promise is made? How does it apply to the seeker's worry about purifying the soul?
This connects the practice to inner purification.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فضلها العظيم ما ألَّفَ أئمَّتُنا فيهِ على الانفراد تأليف عديدة ؟! وقد رأيتُ لبعض أئمةِ التصرُّفِ : أَنَّ مَنْ فقد شيوخ التربية فليكثر مِنَ الصلاةِ على النبيِّ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ؛ فَإِنَّهُ يصلُ بِهِ إِلَى مقصوده ، ولعلَّهُ أخذَ ذلكَ مِنْ قولِهِ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ لأبي هريرةَ رضيَ الله عنه عندما التزم أن يجعل جميع صلاتِهِ للنبيِّ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : إِذا تُكْفَى هَمَّكَ ، وَيُغْفَرَ ذَنْبُكَ. ولا شكّ أنَّ المريد الطالب على مشايخ التربية قد اهتم بتنقية نفسه وشفائها مِنْ علائق سواه تبارك وتعالى ، فإذا أكثرَ مِنَ الصلاةِ على سيدنا ومولانا ونبينا محمَّدٍ عليه الصلاة والسلام . . كُفِي هذا الهم الذي اهتم به، والله تعالى أعلم
Its great virtue: our imams authored many works on it individually. And I have seen from some of the imams of spiritual authority: that whoever lacks teachers of spiritual training should increase in sending prayers upon the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, for it will lead him to his goal. And perhaps he took that from his statement, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, to Abu Huraira, may Allah be pleased with him, when he committed to making all his prayer for the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace: 'Then your worry will be taken care of, and your sin will be forgiven.' And there is no doubt that the desiring seeker who relies on the teachers of spiritual training has concerned himself with purifying his soul and healing it from the attachments of other than Him, blessed and exalted. So if he increases in prayer upon our master, our patron, and our Prophet Muhammad, peace and prayer be upon him, he will be relieved of this worry that he was concerned with. And Allah knows best.
Source pages p. 113

Commentary
This passage discusses the great virtue of sending prayers (ṣalāt) upon the Prophet Muhammad and its practical benefit for the spiritual seeker. It begins by noting that many imams wrote treatises on this topic. The author then quotes from some 'imams of spiritual authority' (a'immat al-taṣarruf) that whoever lacks a spiritual teacher (shuyūkh al-tarbiya) should increase in reciting ṣalāt upon the Prophet, because it will lead him to his goal. This is based on the ḥadīth where the Prophet told Abū Hurayra that if he devotes all his prayer to ṣalāt, his worries will be taken care of and his sins forgiven. The passage then explains: the true seeker (murīd) who relies on spiritual teachers is concerned with purifying his soul from worldly attachments. By increasing in ṣalāt, he is relieved of that concern. The passage ends with 'Allah knows best.'

Key Terms
الصلاة على النبي
شيوخ التربية
المريد
التصرُّف
الهم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Salawat is only for honoring the Prophet, not for spiritual training or solving personal problems.
Correction: This passage shows that salawat can function as a substitute for a spiritual teacher and directly relieves the seeker's worry, indicating its transformative power beyond mere honor.

Checkpoint
What did the Prophet ﷺ promise Abū Hurayra if he made all his prayer ṣalāt upon the Prophet?
A) He would enter Paradise.
B) His worry would be taken care of and his sin forgiven.
C) He would become a saint (walī).
D) He would be given wealth.

Key Takeaway
Sending abundant salawat on the Prophet can substitute for a missing teacher and bring relief from the seeker's spiritual worries.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were to lose access to all spiritual teachers today, would you have enough reliance on the Prophet's salawat to carry you through? What keeps you from making salawat your daily lifeline?
?
What is the one 'worry' in your spiritual life that you have been trying to solve through your own effort rather than through the Prophet's intercession? Will you commit right now to send one hundred salawat daily for a week with that intention?
?
The Prophet promised Abu Huraira that his worry would be taken care of and his sin forgiven if he devoted all his prayer to salawat. Do you truly believe that this promise applies to you? If yes, why have you not acted on it today?
