Two Reasons for Salawat After Hamd in the Creed
Sanusi gives two reasons for placing salawat after hamd in the creed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two reasons Sanusi gives for including salawat upon the Prophet immediately after praising Allah in the creed.

Core Idea
Salawat after hamd is both specific praise and a form of thankfulness to the Prophet.

Sanusi begins by explaining why it is appropriate to mention prayer and peace upon the Prophet right after praising Allah. He gives two interconnected reasons rooted in the structure of gratitude and praise. This passage shows the theological depth behind what might seem like a mere convention.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage carefully, then the literal translation. Notice the structure: it states a general point, then gives two reasons introduced by 'الأولُ' and 'الثاني'.
The passage begins with 'فذكرنا في هذه العقيدة...' indicating the placement of salawat after hamd.
2
Identify the two reasons
The first reason: salawat is like specific praise after general praise. The second reason: it is an act of thanking the Prophet, based on the hadith 'Whoever does not thank people does not thank Allah.'
Each reason is a complete argument in itself.
3
Reflect on the theological significance
Consider why the blessing of the Prophet's mission is called 'the greatest, most comprehensive, and most enduring.' This shows that the placement of salawat is not arbitrary but rooted in the order of gratitude.
The passage emphasizes that thanking the Prophet is part of thanking Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فذكرنا في هذه العقيدة بعدَ حمدِ اللهِ تعالى الصلاة والسلام على نبيِّهِ وأشرف خلقِهِ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ . . مناسب مِنْ أوجُهِ : الأولُ : أَنَّهُ شبْهُ حمدِ خاص بعدَ حمدِ عام ؛ لأنَّهُ لمَّا حمدَ المولى تبارك وتعالى حمداً مطلقاً على جميع الفضائل والفواضل ، وإن شئت قلت : على كمالِهِ وتكميلِهِ . . حمدَهُ بعد ذلك حمداً خاصاً ؛ وهو أمره سبحانَهُ فيما أمرَ بهِ مِنَ الصلاةِ والتسليم على نبيه محمَّدٍ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ على نعمةٍ خاصةٍ ؛ وهي نعمةُ بَعْثِ الله سبحانه نبينا ومولانا محمداً عليه الصلاة والتسليم ، ورحمتِهِ سبحانَهُ بهِ الخَلْقَ دنيا وأخرى ، وخَصَّ هذه النعمة بالذكر لأنَّها أكبرُ النعم وأعمُّها وأدومها الثاني : أنَّهُ لمَّا حمدَ المولى تبارك وتعالى وشكرَهُ على جميع نعمه التي تَفضَّلَ بها سبحانه وأوجدها وحدَه . . شكرَ بعد ذلكَ مَنْ أظهرَ سبحانه على يدِهِ تلكَ النعم وأفاضَها ببركته على الخلق دنيا وأخرى ؛ وهو نبينا ومولانا محمد صلّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ؛ لقولِهِ عليهِ الصلاة والسلامُ : مَنْ لَمْ يَشْكُرِ النَّاسَ . . لَمْ يَشْكُرِ الله
We mentioned in this creed, after praising Allah, prayer and peace upon His Prophet and the noblest of His creation, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, appropriate from several aspects: The first: that it is like a specific praise after a general praise; because when he praised the Master, blessed and exalted, with an absolute praise for all virtues and bounties – or if you wish, say: for His perfection and His making perfect – he then praised Him with a specific praise; and that is His command, glorified be He, regarding what He commanded of prayer and peace upon His Prophet Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, for a specific blessing; and that is the blessing of Allah sending our Prophet and patron Muhammad, peace and prayer be upon him, and His mercy, glorified be He, to creation in this world and the next, and He singled out this blessing for mention because it is the greatest, most comprehensive, and most enduring of blessings. The second: that when he praised the Master, blessed and exalted, and thanked Him for all His blessings which He, glorified be He, bestowed and created alone, he then thanked the one through whom, glorified be He, manifested those blessings and poured them forth by his blessing upon creation in this world and the next; and that is our Prophet and patron Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, because of his statement, peace and prayer be upon him: 'Whoever does not thank people does not thank Allah.'
Source pages p. 114

Commentary
This passage explains two reasons (awjuh) why it is appropriate to mention salawāt upon the Prophet after praising Allah (ḥamd) in a creed (ʿaqīda). The first reason: after offering an absolute praise (ḥamd ʿāmm) to Allah for all perfections, one then praises Him with a specific praise (ḥamd khāṣṣ) – that is, the command to send prayers upon the Prophet as a thanks for the specific blessing of sending the Prophet as a mercy to creation. This blessing is singled out because it is the greatest, most comprehensive, and most enduring. The second reason: after thanking Allah for all His blessings, one thanks the Prophet, through whose hands those blessings were manifested, based on the ḥadīth: 'Whoever does not thank people does not thank Allah.' Thus, mentioning salawāt after ḥamd is both a specific praise and an act of thankfulness.

Key Terms
مناسبة
حمد خاص
حمد عام
شكر الناس
نعمة البعثة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think salawat after hamd is merely a conventional or optional addition.
Correction: Sanusi shows it has a theological basis: it is a form of specific praise and a required act of gratitude toward the Prophet, which itself fulfills thanking Allah.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is one reason for mentioning salawāt after praising Allah?
A) It completes the number of obligatory deeds.
B) It is like a specific praise after a general praise.
C) It is a sunna of the Prophet.
D) It is a way to increase blessings.

Key Takeaway
Placing salawat after hamd in the creed is both a specific praise for the greatest blessing (the Prophet) and an act of thanksgiving that fulfills the command to thank both Allah and His creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you send salawat upon the Prophet, do you consciously do it as an act of specific praise for the greatest blessing—his very mission? Or is it just a habit? What would change if you internalized this meaning?
?
Do you truly thank people for the blessings they bring into your life? Or do you assume it's between you and Allah alone? This lesson links thankfulness to the Prophet with thankfulness to Allah—examine one relationship you have where you've neglected to thank the person. How can you rectify that starting today?
?
If the blessing of the Prophet's mission is the greatest, most comprehensive, and most enduring blessing, how does your daily life reflect gratitude for it beyond ritual? What concrete actions are you taking to show that you consider this blessing superior to all others?

6 min
Why Bless the Prophet After Praising God?
Sanusi enumerates four reasons for invoking blessings on the Prophet after praising God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi invokes blessings on the Prophet after praising God, including the four reasons: specific praise, gratitude, teaching monotheism, and the incomplete fourth.

Core Idea
Blessings on the Prophet are a specific praise for the greatest favor.

Sanusi begins his creed with praise of God, then immediately invokes blessings on the Prophet. He knows this sequence might seem arbitrary, so he provides a reasoned justification. This passage explains four aspects of the appropriateness (munāsaba) of this placement, grounding it in theology and gratitude.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very structured enumeration here.
2
Identify the four reasons
The passage presents four reasons. Find each one and note how Sanusi introduces them with 'الأول', 'الثاني', 'الثالث', 'الرابع'. The fourth is incomplete.
Each reason has its own internal logic.
3
Examine the logic of each reason
For each reason, ask: what is the key connection being made? Reason 1: general vs specific praise. Reason 2: gratitude to the intermediary. Reason 3: Prophet's role in teaching tawhid. The commentary will help you.
Consider how each reason links the Prophet to the praise of God.
4
Reflect on the incomplete fourth reason
The fourth reason is cut off. Think about how it might have continued based on the pattern of the first three.
This teaches us that even incomplete passages can be studied for their structure.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
١ مناسبة ذكرِ الصلاة والسلام على النبي بعد حمدِ اللهِ تعالى. فذكرنا في هذه العقيدة بعدَ حمدِ اللهِ تعالى الصلاة والسلام على نبيَّهِ وأشرف خلقِهِ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ. مناسب مِنْ أوجُهِ: الأولُ: أَنَّهُ شبْهُ حمدِ خاص بعدَ حمدِ عام؛ لأنَّهُ لمَّا حمدَ المولى تبارك وتعالى حمداً مطلقاً على جميع الفضائل والفواضل، وإن شئت قلت: على كمالِهِ وتكميلِهِ، حمدَهُ بعد ذلك حمداً خاصاً؛ وهو أمره سبحانَهُ فيما أمرَ بهِ مِنَ الصلاةِ والتسليم على نبيه محمَّدٍ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ على نعمةٍ خاصةٍ؛ وهي نعمةُ بَعْثِ الله سبحانه نبينا ومولانا محمداً عليه الصلاة والتسليم، ورحمتِهِ سبحانَهُ بهِ الخَلْقَ دنيا وأخرى، وخَصَّ هذه النعمة بالذكر لأنَّها أكبرُ النعم وأعمُّها وأدومها. الثاني: أنَّهُ لمَّا حمدَ المولى تبارك وتعالى وشكرَهُ على جميع نعمه التي تَفضَّلَ بها سبحانه وأوجدها وحدَه، شكرَ بعد ذلكَ مَنْ أظهرَ سبحانه على يدِهِ تلكَ النعم وأفاضَها ببركته على الخلق دنيا وأخرى؛ وهو نبينا ومولانا محمد صلّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ؛ لقولِهِ عليهِ الصلاة والسلامُ: (مَنْ لَمْ يَشْكُرِ النَّاسَ لَمْ يَشْكُرِ الله). ولما كنا عاجزينَ عن مكافأتِهِ عليهِ الصلاةُ والسلامُ مِنْ قِبَلِ أَنفسنا، وجبَ أن نَرجِعَ في ذلك إلى مولانا الكريم القادرِ الذي بيدِهِ خزائنُ النعم، فنطلب منه أن يصلي على هذا النبي الشريف؛ أي: يُنعِمَ عليه بنَعَم يصحبها تكريم وتعظيم على ما يليقُ بمنزلة هذا السيد عندَه، وأن يُسلَّمَ عليه؛ أي: يُعظُمَهُ؛ بأن يُسمِعَهُ مِنْ كَلامِهِ الذي لا مثلَ لهُ ما تقرُّ به عينه، وتبتهج به نفسُه، ويَتَسعُ بِهِ جَاهُهُ. الثالثُ: أنَّه لما صدر منهُ (الحمدُ للهِ ربِّ العالمينَ)، وكانَ ذلكَ مقتضياً لمعرفة توحيد مولانا جل وعلا، ومعرفة ما يليقُ بهِ مِنْ أوصاف الألوهيَّةِ على حسَبِ ما مضى تقريره، شكر بعدَهُ مَنْ أوصلَ سبحانَهُ على يده هذه النعمة العظيمة؛ إذ الناسُ قبل بعثه كانوا يمدحونَ غير الله تعالى؛ مِنَ الأصنام وغيرها، ويُضيفون على سبيل الحقيقة في زعمِهِم نِعَمَهُ تبارك وتعالى وأنواع تربيتِهِ إلى غيرِهِ مِنَ الأسبابِ العادية وغيرها. فلمَّا بُعِثَ نبينا ومولانا محمَّدٌ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ، عرَّفَهُم أَنَّ الحمدَ لا يستحقُه على الحقيقة إلا الله تعالى؛ إذْ لا كمال قديماً ولا حادثاً إلا له، وأنه هو ربُّ العالمين وحده، وبلغهم قوله تعالى: {يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ} [فاطر: ٣] ونحو ذلكَ ممَّا كثُرَ في القرآنِ، وقدِ اختُصِرَ ذلكَ كلُّه في (الفاتحة)، ولهذا كانَتْ أُمَّ القرآن. الرابع: أَنَّ حمدَ اللهِ تعالى وشكرَهُ الذي دخل تحت عمومِهِ. . .
1. The appropriateness of mentioning ṣalāh and salām upon the Prophet after praising God, may He be exalted. So we mentioned in this creed after praising God, may He be exalted, the ṣalāh and salām upon His Prophet and the most noble of His creation, may God bless him and grant him peace. It is appropriate from several aspects: The first: that it is like a specific praise after a general praise; because when he praised the Master, blessed and exalted, with an absolute praise for all virtues and excellences—and if you wish you may say: for His perfection and His perfecting—he praised Him after that with a specific praise; and that is His command, glory be to Him, regarding what He commanded of ṣalāh and salām upon His Prophet Muḥammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, for a specific favor; and that is the favor of God, glory be to Him, sending our Prophet and master Muḥammad, upon him ṣalāh and salām, and His mercy, glory be to Him, through him to creation in this world and the next, and He singled out this favor for mention because it is the greatest of favors, the most comprehensive, and the most lasting. The second: that when he praised the Master, blessed and exalted, and thanked Him for all His favors which He, glory be to Him, bestowed and brought into existence alone, he thanked after that the one through whose hands He, glory be to Him, manifested those favors and poured them forth by his blessing upon creation in this world and the next; and that is our Prophet and master Muḥammad, may God bless him and grant him peace; due to his saying, upon him ṣalāh and salām: 'Whoever does not thank the people has not thanked God.' And since we are incapable of rewarding him, upon him ṣalāh and salām, from ourselves, it was necessary that we refer in that to our generous, capable Master, in whose hand are the treasuries of favors, so we ask Him to send ṣalāh upon this noble Prophet; that is, to bestow upon him favors accompanied by honor and veneration in a manner befitting the station of this master with Him, and to send salām upon him; that is, to magnify him; by making him hear from His speech, which has no likeness, that which delights his eye, rejoices his soul, and increases his rank. The third: that when he uttered 'Praise be to God, Lord of the worlds,' and that necessitated knowledge of the tawḥīd of our Master, may He be glorified and exalted, and knowledge of what befits Him of the attributes of divinity according to what has previously been established, he thanked after him the one through whose hands He, glory be to Him, conveyed this great favor; for people before his mission used to praise other than God, may He be exalted—idols and others—and would ascribe, as a reality in their claim, His favors, blessed and exalted, and the types of His nurturing to other than Him from customary causes and others. Then when our Prophet and master Muḥammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, was sent, he made them know that praise is not deserved in reality except by God, may He be exalted; since there is no perfection, ancient or contingent, except from Him, and that He alone is the Lord of the worlds, and he conveyed to them His statement, may He be exalted: 'O mankind, remember the favor of God upon you. Is there any creator other than God?' [Sūrat Fāṭir: 3] and similar to that which is abundant in the Qurʾān, and all of that was summarized in al-Fātiḥa, and for this reason it is the Mother of the Qurʾān. The fourth: that the praise of God, may He be exalted, and gratitude to Him which is included in its generality...
Source pages p. 114

Commentary
This passage provides the author's justification (munāsaba) for including the invocation of blessings and peace upon the Prophet immediately after the praise of God (ḥamd) at the beginning of his creed. He enumerates three complete reasons and begins a fourth that is cut off. The first reason (al-awwal) characterizes the ṣalāh and salām as a specific praise (ḥamd khāṣṣ) following the general praise (ḥamd ʿāmm). The general praise is the opening 'Praise be to God' for all perfections, while the specific praise is God's own command to bless the Prophet for the particular favor of his mission—the greatest and most comprehensive favor. The second reason (al-thānī) emphasizes gratitude (shukr) for the means through whom favors are conveyed: after thanking God for all favors, we thank the Prophet as the intermediary, because the Prophet commanded thanking people, and since we cannot adequately reward him ourselves, we ask God to reward him by sending ṣalāh (as a form of honor) and salām (as magnification, including hearing God's speech). The third reason (al-thālith) connects the ḥamd to the Prophet's role in teaching true monotheism: before his mission, people misdirected praise to idols and false causes; the Prophet taught that all praise belongs solely to God, and this teaching is encapsulated in Sūrat al-Fātiḥa, which is called the Mother of the Qurʾān. The fourth reason is only begun and appears incomplete.

Key Terms
مناسبة
حمد
صلاة
تسليم
شكر
نعمة
أم القرآن

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that sending ṣalāh upon the Prophet is merely asking for forgiveness or mercy.
Correction: Ṣalāh is an act of honoring him, acknowledging his unique role as the conduit of divine favors, and is a specific praise from God.

Checkpoint
According to the second reason given by the author, why do we ask God to send ṣalāh upon the Prophet?
A) Because the Prophet commanded us to do so.
B) Because we are incapable of rewarding him ourselves and thus refer to God.
C) Because the Prophet asked for it in his life.
D) Because it is a general praise after a specific praise.

Key Takeaway
Sanusi's four reasons demonstrate that invoking blessings on the Prophet after praising God is not incidental but logically grounded in the specific favor of prophethood, gratitude, and the Prophet's role in teaching monotheism.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite 'Alhamdulillahi Rabbil 'Alamin' in prayer, do you truly feel that this praise encompasses all of God's favors? How does that awareness change the way you then send blessings upon the Prophet?
?
Sanusi says we are incapable of rewarding the Prophet ourselves, so we ask God to reward him. Is there someone in your life whom you have never properly thanked? How can you turn to God to express that gratitude today?
?
Before the Prophet, people misdirected praise to idols. What modern 'idols'—status, wealth, self—do you unconsciously praise? How will you actively redirect that praise to God alone, as the Prophet taught?

Method of Learning

Necessary Knowledge: The Foundation of Certainty
Sanusi defines necessary knowledge and gives examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the concept of necessary knowledge (darura) as defined by Sanusi: a compelled certainty that cannot be repelled, with examples.

Core Idea
Necessary knowledge is certainty you cannot refuse, like knowing you exist.

Sanusi begins by laying the epistemological groundwork. Before we can talk about proof, we need to understand what it means to know something with absolute certainty. He defines necessary knowledge as a certainty that the soul is compelled to accept without reflection, and that it cannot shake off. Let's read his definition and simple examples.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of 'necessary knowledge'
Read the Arabic sentence defining darura and its literal translation. Notice the key phrase 'بلا تأمل' (without reflection) and the inability to repel.
The definition is very precise; each word matters.
2
Examine the examples
Look at the two examples: knowledge of one's own existence and that one is half of two. Reflect on why these are considered 'necessary'—you cannot doubt them.
These are self-evident truths.
3
Contrast with non-necessary knowledge
Think about things we know through reasoning or testimony. Are they also necessary? The next lesson will show the difference.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومعنى ( الضرورة ) : إلجاء المولى سبحانه النفس لأن تجزم بأمرٍ جزماً مطابقاً بلا تأمل ، بحيثُ لو حاولت أن تدفع عن نفسها ذلكَ الجزم بتشكيك أو نحوه .. لم تقدر .
ومثالُهُ : جزمنا بوجودِ أنفسِنا ، وبأنَّ الواحد مثلاً نصفُ الاثنينِ ونحو ذلك ممَّا هو كثير .
ومعنى ( البرهان ( : الدليلُ المركَّبُ مِنْ مُقدِّماتٍ قطعيَّةٍ ضروريَّةٍ
في نفسها ، أو منتهية في الاستدلال عليها إلى علوم ضروريَّة
ومثال ذلك : إذا قيلَ لنا : فلان اشترى هذه السلعة بربع عُشْرِ أربعين درهماً ؛ فجزمُنا بأنَّهُ اشتراها بدرهم واحدٍ ليس بضروري لنا ندركُه بلا تأمل (۱) ، بل لا يحصلُ لنا الجزم العرفاني بذلكَ مِنْ غيرِ تقليد لأحدٍ حتى نختبر لأنفسنا فنقول : أقل عددٍ لهُ رُبع : أربعة ،
المقدّمة
وربعها : واحدٌ ، وهذهِ مُقدّمةٌ واحدةٌ ضرورية لا تفتقر إلى تأمل أعني : كونَ الواحدِ رُبِّعَ الأربعة ، لكن لا تكفينا هذهِ في معرفة ما اشترى به الإنسان تلك السلعة حتى نعرف معرفةً قطعيَّةً أن الأربعةَ عُشْرُ الأربعينَ ، وهذه المعرفة بهذه المقدمة ليسَتْ ضروريةً ، إلا أنَّها تنتهي إلى الضرورةِ ؛ فإِنَّكَ إِذا قسَّمتَ أربعينَ على عشرة أنصباءً متساوية .. خرج في كلِّ نصيب أربعة ، وكذا لو عددتَ في أصابعك أربعةً ثم أربعة وتجمعُ إلى أن تفرغ مِنْ أصابعك العشرة ،
(۱) في (و) : ( لأنا لا ندركه بلا تأمل ) .
أو تضعُ في لوح أربعة وفوقها أربعةً عَشْرَ مرَّاتٍ ، وتَجمعَ .. لكانَ مجموع ذلك أربعينَ ، فقد حصلَ لكَ علمٌ ضروري لا تَقدِرُ أن تدفعَهُ بأنَّ الأربعةَ عُشْرُ الأربعينَ ، لكن لم يحصل لك هذا العلم الضروري أولاً ، بل بعد رؤيتِكَ حِسّاً انقسام الأربعينَ إلى عشرة أجزاء متساوية ، كلُّ جزء منها أربعةٌ ، فإذا ضممت هذه المقدمة الضرورية انتهاء ؛ أن رُبُعَ الأربعةِ رُبُعُ عُشْرِ الأربعين ، إلى المقدمة الضرورية ابتداءً ؛ وهي أَنَّ الواحدَ رُبُعُ الأربعة.. حصلَ لكَ عنهما أنَّ الذي اشتُرِيَتْ بهِ تلك السلعة درهم واحدٌ
وهي
وو
فتقولُ في نظم البرهان : يجب أن يكون المشترى به درهماً واحداً ؛ لأنَّ الدرهم الواحدَ رُبُعُ الأربعة ، ورُبعَ الأربعةِ رُبُعُ عُشْرِ

الأربعين المشترى بهِ ؛ يُنتَجُ : الدرهم الواحدُ رُبُعُ عُشْرِ الأَربعينَ
المشترى به .
فالجزم بهذه النتيجةِ يُسمَّى معرفةً وعلماً ؛ لأنَّهُ جزم مطابق لِمَا في نفس الأمر ، حاصل عن برهان ، وهو دليل قطعي ؛ لتركُبِهِ مِنْ
مقدمتين ؛ الأولى منهُما ضرورية ابتداءً ، والثانية ضرورية انتهاءً . ولو جزمت بهذه النتيجة تقليداً لِمَنْ  َتثقُ به ممَّنْ يعرفُ الحساب ، ولم تستعمل أنتَ فكرَكَ في ذلكَ .. لسُمِّيَ جزمُكَ اعتقاداً صحيحاً ولا يُسمى معرفة ولا علماً . ولو لم تثق بمَنْ أخبرَكَ بهذه النتيجة ، بل تَرجَّحَ عندَكَ صدقه ، واحتمل احتمالاً مرجوحاً عندك أن يكون مخطئاً .. لكان إدراككَ
The meaning of (necessity): the subjection by the Lord, exalted, of the soul to be certain of a matter with a certainty conforming [to reality] without reflection, such that if it tried to repel that certainty from itself by doubt or the like, it could not. Its example: our certainty of the existence of ourselves, and that one is half of two, and the like of that which is abundant. The meaning of (demonstration): a proof composed of premises that are certain and necessary in themselves, or [that] in reasoning upon them terminate in necessary sciences. An example of that: if it is said to us: so-and-so bought this commodity for a quarter of a tenth of forty dirhams; then our certainty that he bought it for one dirham is not necessary for us, we perceive it without reflection, rather we do not obtain intellectual certainty of that without taqlid of anyone until we test for ourselves and say: the smallest number that has a quarter is four; its quarter is one. And this is a single necessary premise that does not require reflection, namely that one is a quarter of four. But this does not suffice us in knowing what the man bought that commodity for until we know with certainty that four is a tenth of forty. And this knowledge of this premise is not necessary, except that it terminates in necessity; for if you divide forty into ten equal shares, each share is four; likewise if you count on your fingers four and then four and combine until you finish your ten fingers, or you place on a slate four and above it four ten times and add, the sum of that would be forty. So there came to you a necessary knowledge that you cannot repel, that four is a tenth of forty. But this necessary knowledge did not come to you first, but after your sensory observation of the division of forty into ten equal parts, each part being four. So when you join this premise that is necessary by termination, that a quarter of four is a quarter of a tenth of forty, to the premise necessary initially, which is that one is a quarter of four, you obtain from them two that what was bought with that commodity is one dirham. So you say in the formulation of the demonstration: it must be that what was bought with is one dirham; because the one dirham is a quarter of four, and a quarter of four is a quarter of a tenth of forty which was bought with; thus it follows: one dirham is a quarter of a tenth of forty which was bought with. The certainty of this result is called knowledge and science; because it is a certainty conforming to what is in the matter itself, obtained from a demonstration, and it is a certain proof because it is composed of two premises: the first of them is necessary initially, and the second is necessary by termination. If you were certain of this result by taqlid of someone you trust among those who know arithmetic, and you did not use your own intellect in that, your certainty would be called sound belief and would not be called knowledge or science. And if you did not trust the one who told you this result, but his truthfulness was preponderant to you, and you considered a remote possibility that he might be mistaken, then your perception would be...
Source pages p. 127

Commentary
This passage defines two key epistemological concepts: necessary knowledge (darura) and demonstrative proof (burhan). The author begins by stating that necessary knowledge is a compelled certainty in the soul, conforming to reality, achieved without reflection, such that the soul cannot repel it even by doubt. Examples include self-awareness and basic mathematical truths like 'one is half of two'. The author then defines burhan as a proof composed of premises that are either self-evidently necessary or that ultimately reduce to necessary knowledge. To illustrate, he gives a detailed example: someone buys a commodity for a quarter of a tenth of forty dirhams. One might initially not know that this equals one dirham. Through reasoning, one uses two premises: (1) one is a quarter of four (immediately necessary), and (2) four is a tenth of forty (necessary after sensory demonstration of dividing forty into ten parts). Combining these yields the necessary conclusion that the price is one dirham. The author emphasizes that this conclusion, when reached through burhan, is called ma'rifa and 'ilm. However, if reached by blind taqlid of a trusted calculator, it is called correct 'i'tiqad but not ilm. If the person only has a preponderant belief without certainty, that is even lower. The passage thus carefully distinguishes between levels of certainty and the role of demonstration.

Key Terms
ضرورة
جزم
علم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the key feature of necessary knowledge?
A) It is based on logical reasoning.
B) It is achieved after careful thought.
C) It cannot be repelled even by doubt.
D) It is only about mathematical truths.

Key Takeaway
Necessary knowledge is a certainty that the mind cannot reject, achieved without any reflection or reasoning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Can you point to one certainty in your life that you could not shake no matter how hard you tried? Now ask yourself: is your belief in Allah’s existence that kind of certainty? If not, what would it take for you to reach that level of undeniable conviction?
?
Sanusi says necessary knowledge comes without reflection. When you think about your own existence, you simply know it—you don't need to prove it. Have you ever applied this same immediate awareness to your faith? If you were to stop all intellectual arguments, would you still find yourself compelled to believe in Allah?
?
The text says Allah forces the soul to accept certain truths. If you have ever doubted something about your faith, was it because you relied on reflection rather than the compulsion He places in your heart? What change can you make today to stop trying to 'prove' everything and instead submit to the certainties He has already given you?

Method of Learning
intermediate
7 min
Demonstrative Proof and the Degrees of Certainty
Sanusi defines demonstrative proof and uses an example to classify types of certainty.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how demonstrative proof (burhan) produces certain knowledge from necessary premises, and how that differs from blind taqlid and mere probability.

Core Idea
Real knowledge comes from proof; believing a fact on authority is not knowledge.

The first lesson gave us necessary knowledge. But many truths require reasoning. Sanusi now introduces demonstrative proof (burhan) as a compound of certain premises. He uses a detailed arithmetic example to show how we can reach certain knowledge through demonstration, and he distinguishes that from accepting a result on trust (taqlid) or having only a probable opinion. Let's follow his careful analysis.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of burhan
Read the definition: a proof composed of certain necessary premises, either immediately or ultimately. This is the core idea.
2
Walk through the arithmetic example
Follow the example of the commodity bought for 'a quarter of a tenth of forty dirhams'. Notice how two premises are used: one immediate (one is a quarter of four) and one that becomes necessary after verification (four is a tenth of forty). See how the conclusion is reached.
This illustrates the structure of a demonstration.
3
Understand the three epistemic categories
Sanusi then distinguishes three outcomes: (1) certain knowledge from demonstration (called 'ilm/ma'rifa), (2) certain belief from trusting an authority (called 'i'tiqad sahih), and (3) a preponderant belief without certainty (just probability).
Only the first qualifies as true knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومعنى ( الضرورة ) : إلجاء المولى سبحانه النفس لأن تجزم بأمرٍ جزماً مطابقاً بلا تأمل ، بحيثُ لو حاولت أن تدفع عن نفسها ذلكَ الجزم بتشكيك أو نحوه .. لم تقدر .
ومثالُهُ : جزمنا بوجودِ أنفسِنا ، وبأنَّ الواحد مثلاً نصفُ الاثنينِ ونحو ذلك ممَّا هو كثير .
ومعنى ( البرهان ( : الدليلُ المركَّبُ مِنْ مُقدِّماتٍ قطعيَّةٍ ضروريَّةٍ
في نفسها ، أو منتهية في الاستدلال عليها إلى علوم ضروريَّة
ومثال ذلك : إذا قيلَ لنا : فلان اشترى هذه السلعة بربع عُشْرِ أربعين درهماً ؛ فجزمُنا بأنَّهُ اشتراها بدرهم واحدٍ ليس بضروري لنا ندركُه بلا تأمل (۱) ، بل لا يحصلُ لنا الجزم العرفاني بذلكَ مِنْ غيرِ تقليد لأحدٍ حتى نختبر لأنفسنا فنقول : أقل عددٍ لهُ رُبع : أربعة ،
المقدّمة
وربعها : واحدٌ ، وهذهِ مُقدّمةٌ واحدةٌ ضرورية لا تفتقر إلى تأمل أعني : كونَ الواحدِ رُبِّعَ الأربعة ، لكن لا تكفينا هذهِ في معرفة ما اشترى به الإنسان تلك السلعة حتى نعرف معرفةً قطعيَّةً أن الأربعةَ عُشْرُ الأربعينَ ، وهذه المعرفة بهذه المقدمة ليسَتْ ضروريةً ، إلا أنَّها تنتهي إلى الضرورةِ ؛ فإِنَّكَ إِذا قسَّمتَ أربعينَ على عشرة أنصباءً متساوية .. خرج في كلِّ نصيب أربعة ، وكذا لو عددتَ في أصابعك أربعةً ثم أربعة وتجمعُ إلى أن تفرغ مِنْ أصابعك العشرة ،
(۱) في (و) : ( لأنا لا ندركه بلا تأمل ) .
أو تضعُ في لوح أربعة وفوقها أربعةً عَشْرَ مرَّاتٍ ، وتَجمعَ .. لكانَ مجموع ذلك أربعينَ ، فقد حصلَ لكَ علمٌ ضروري لا تَقدِرُ أن تدفعَهُ بأنَّ الأربعةَ عُشْرُ الأربعينَ ، لكن لم يحصل لك هذا العلم الضروري أولاً ، بل بعد رؤيتِكَ حِسّاً انقسام الأربعينَ إلى عشرة أجزاء متساوية ، كلُّ جزء منها أربعةٌ ، فإذا ضممت هذه المقدمة الضرورية انتهاء ؛ أن رُبُعَ الأربعةِ رُبُعُ عُشْرِ الأربعين ، إلى المقدمة الضرورية ابتداءً ؛ وهي أَنَّ الواحدَ رُبُعُ الأربعة.. حصلَ لكَ عنهما أنَّ الذي اشتُرِيَتْ بهِ تلك السلعة درهم واحدٌ
وهي
وو
فتقولُ في نظم البرهان : يجب أن يكون المشترى به درهماً واحداً ؛ لأنَّ الدرهم الواحدَ رُبُعُ الأربعة ، ورُبعَ الأربعةِ رُبُعُ عُشْرِ
وو
الأربعين المشترى بهِ ؛ يُنتَجُ : الدرهم الواحدُ رُبُعُ عُشْرِ الأَربعينَ
المشترى به .
فالجزم بهذه النتيجةِ يُسمَّى معرفةً وعلماً ؛ لأنَّهُ جزم مطابق لِمَا في نفس الأمر ، حاصل عن برهان ، وهو دليل قطعي ؛ لتركُبِهِ مِنْ
مقدمتين ؛ الأولى منهُما ضرورية ابتداءً ، والثانية ضرورية انتهاءً . ولو جزمت بهذه النتيجة تقليداً لِمَنْ  َتثقُ به ممَّنْ يعرفُ الحساب ، ولم تستعمل أنتَ فكرَكَ في ذلكَ .. لسُمِّيَ جزمُكَ اعتقاداً صحيحاً ولا يُسمى معرفة ولا علماً . ولو لم تثق بمَنْ أخبرَكَ بهذه النتيجة ، بل تَرجَّحَ عندَكَ صدقه ، واحتمل احتمالاً مرجوحاً عندك أن يكون مخطئاً .. لكان إدراككَ
The meaning of (necessity): the subjection by the Lord, exalted, of the soul to be certain of a matter with a certainty conforming [to reality] without reflection, such that if it tried to repel that certainty from itself by doubt or the like, it could not. Its example: our certainty of the existence of ourselves, and that one is half of two, and the like of that which is abundant. The meaning of (demonstration): a proof composed of premises that are certain and necessary in themselves, or [that] in reasoning upon them terminate in necessary sciences. An example of that: if it is said to us: so-and-so bought this commodity for a quarter of a tenth of forty dirhams; then our certainty that he bought it for one dirham is not necessary for us, we perceive it without reflection, rather we do not obtain intellectual certainty of that without taqlid of anyone until we test for ourselves and say: the smallest number that has a quarter is four; its quarter is one. And this is a single necessary premise that does not require reflection, namely that one is a quarter of four. But this does not suffice us in knowing what the man bought that commodity for until we know with certainty that four is a tenth of forty. And this knowledge of this premise is not necessary, except that it terminates in necessity; for if you divide forty into ten equal shares, each share is four; likewise if you count on your fingers four and then four and combine until you finish your ten fingers, or you place on a slate four and above it four ten times and add, the sum of that would be forty. So there came to you a necessary knowledge that you cannot repel, that four is a tenth of forty. But this necessary knowledge did not come to you first, but after your sensory observation of the division of forty into ten equal parts, each part being four. So when you join this premise that is necessary by termination, that a quarter of four is a quarter of a tenth of forty, to the premise necessary initially, which is that one is a quarter of four, you obtain from them two that what was bought with that commodity is one dirham. So you say in the formulation of the demonstration: it must be that what was bought with is one dirham; because the one dirham is a quarter of four, and a quarter of four is a quarter of a tenth of forty which was bought with; thus it follows: one dirham is a quarter of a tenth of forty which was bought with. The certainty of this result is called knowledge and science; because it is a certainty conforming to what is in the matter itself, obtained from a demonstration, and it is a certain proof because it is composed of two premises: the first of them is necessary initially, and the second is necessary by termination. If you were certain of this result by taqlid of someone you trust among those who know arithmetic, and you did not use your own intellect in that, your certainty would be called sound belief and would not be called knowledge or science. And if you did not trust the one who told you this result, but his truthfulness was preponderant to you, and you considered a remote possibility that he might be mistaken, then your perception would be...
Source pages p. 127

Commentary
This passage defines two key epistemological concepts: necessary knowledge (darura) and demonstrative proof (burhan). The author begins by stating that necessary knowledge is a compelled certainty in the soul, conforming to reality, achieved without reflection, such that the soul cannot repel it even by doubt. Examples include self-awareness and basic mathematical truths like 'one is half of two'. The author then defines burhan as a proof composed of premises that are either self-evidently necessary or that ultimately reduce to necessary knowledge. To illustrate, he gives a detailed example: someone buys a commodity for a quarter of a tenth of forty dirhams. One might initially not know that this equals one dirham. Through reasoning, one uses two premises: (1) one is a quarter of four (immediately necessary), and (2) four is a tenth of forty (necessary after sensory demonstration of dividing forty into ten parts). Combining these yields the necessary conclusion that the price is one dirham. The author emphasizes that this conclusion, when reached through burhan, is called ma'rifa and 'ilm. However, if reached by blind taqlid of a trusted calculator, it is called correct 'i'tiqad but not ilm. If the person only has a preponderant belief without certainty, that is even lower. The passage thus carefully distinguishes between levels of certainty and the role of demonstration.

Key Terms
برهان
مقدمة
علم
معرفة
اعتقاد
تقليد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that accepting a mathematical truth from a calculator is knowledge.
Correction: Sanusi says such acceptance is only correct belief (i'tiqad), not knowledge ('ilm), because it lacks personal demonstration.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what do we call certainty that comes from demonstration?
A) Necessary knowledge
B) Correct belief
C) Knowledge ('ilm)
D) Preponderant belief

Key Takeaway
Demonstrative proof (burhan) yields certain knowledge by combining necessary premises, whereas belief from taqlid is not knowledge even if correct.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the Shahada, do you truly know it through demonstrative proof, or have you accepted it on authority (taqlid)? What evidence could you personally marshal to transform your belief into certain knowledge?
?
Sanusi says that even a correct belief from taqlid is not knowledge. If you were to stand before Allah with a belief that is true but merely inherited, would that be sufficient? What is one belief you currently hold on authority that you must now verify through proof?
?
In your daily life, how often do you settle for 'correct belief'—accepting what others say without thinking—when Allah demands knowledge from you? Will you commit this week to choose one matter of faith and investigate it until you reach certainty?

Obligation of Certain Knowledge about God and His Messengers
Sanusi defines valid theological knowledge as certain, true, and proof-based.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the conditions for valid theological knowledge: it must be certain, conform to reality, and be obtained through necessary knowledge or proof, and why lesser epistemic states are insufficient.

Core Idea
Valid knowledge of God requires certainty, truth, and proof — not mere opinion or doubt.

Having established the categories of knowledge in the previous lesson, Sanusi now applies these distinctions to the fundamental obligation of knowing God and His messengers. He clarifies what it truly means to 'know' in a theological sense, distinguishing genuine certainty from mere psychological conviction. This passage sets the standard for what every Muslim must attain in their faith.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing it, then read the translation. Notice the formal style and key terms.
The passage refers back to earlier examples of knowledge and its opposites.
2
Identify the key conditions
Sanusi lists three conditions for valid knowledge: certainty (jism), conformity to reality (mutabaqa), and derivation from necessity or proof.
3
Note the rejection of lesser states
The passage explicitly states that opinion (zann), doubt (shakk), and illusion (wahm) are insufficient by consensus (ijma').
4
Reflect on the role of proof
Sanusi notes that necessary knowledge (darura) is not customary regarding these matters, so proof (burhan) is the required path.
This highlights the importance of rational inquiry in theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فاعتبر من هذا الذي ذكرناه مثال المعرفة وأمثلة أضدادها مِنْ فإذا عرفت هذه المقدّمة عرفت حينئذٍ معنى قولنا : ( يجب على كلِّ مُكلَّف أن يعرف ... ( إلى آخره ؛ أي : يجب شرعاً على كلّ ) مُكلَّف أن يجزمَ بهذهِ الثلاثةِ في حقِّهِ تعالى ، وفي حقٌّ رُسُلِهِ عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، جزماً مطابقاً لِمَا في نفس الأمرِ ، حاصلاً ذلكَ الجزمُ عن ضرورة أو برهان ، إلا أنَّ الضرورة لم يُجْرِ الله تعالى بها العادةَ ، فتعيَّن طلبها بالبرهان
فلو لم يحصل للمُكلَّف الجزم بهذهِ الثلاثة في حق الله تعالى وفي حق رسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، بل إنّما حصل له الظن أو الشك أو الوهم . . لم يكفِهِ ذلكَ بإجماع .
ولو حصل له الجزمُ ، إلا أنَّهُ غير مطابقِ لِمَا في نفس الأمرِ ؛ كجزم
So consider from this that we have mentioned an example of knowledge (ma'rifa) and examples of its opposites. Then when you understand this introduction, you will then understand the meaning of our saying: 'It is obligatory upon every mukallaf (legally responsible person) to know...' etc.; that is: it is legally obligatory upon every mukallaf to be certain of these three things regarding Him, Exalted is He, and regarding His messengers, peace and blessings be upon them, with a certainty conforming to what is in reality, that certainty being obtained from necessity or proof, except that necessity has not been established by God as a custom, so it is determined to seek it by proof. And if the mukallaf does not obtain certainty regarding these three things about God and His messengers, but rather only obtains opinion (zann) or doubt (shakk) or illusion (wahm), that would not suffice him by consensus. And if he does obtain certainty, but it does not conform to what is in reality, like certainty...
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage applies the previously established epistemological categories to the theological domain. The author first instructs the reader to reflect on the earlier example of knowledge and its opposites. Then he explains the meaning of the statement 'It is obligatory upon every mukallaf to know...' i.e., it is a religious obligation (shar'an) for every accountable person (mukallaf) to have certain knowledge (jism) about the three essential matters concerning God and His messengers, with the criteria that this certainty must conform to reality (mutabiq li-ma fi nafs al-amr) and be obtained either through necessary knowledge (darura) or through proof (burhan). However, the author notes that necessary knowledge regarding these matters is not customary (lam yujri Allah al-'ada biha), meaning God does not ordinarily impart it directly, so it must be sought through proof. The passage then states that if the mukallaf attains only opinion, doubt, or illusion regarding these matters, this is insufficient by scholarly consensus (ijma'). Finally, it mentions that even if one attains certainty, but it does not conform to reality—such as being certain of a falsehood—that too is unacceptable. The passage ends abruptly, but the implication is clear: valid theological knowledge requires true, certain, and proof-based conviction.

Key Terms
مكلف
جزم
ضرورة
برهان
ظن
شك
وهم
إجماع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any strong belief about God is acceptable as long as it is firm.
Correction: This passage clarifies that valid belief must be certain, true, and based on proof; opinion, doubt, illusion, and even false certainty are insufficient.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what is the minimum requirement for a mukallaf's belief about God and His messengers to be valid?
A) Strong opinion or plausible conjecture.
B) Certainty (jism) that conforms to reality and is obtained by proof or necessity.
C) Mere acceptance of what one's parents believed.
D) Having no doubts, regardless of whether the belief is correct.

Key Takeaway
True knowledge of God and His messengers requires certain, reality-conforming conviction obtained through proof, not mere opinion or blind acceptance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are you certain of your belief in God and His messengers? Where does that certainty come from — is it based on real proof or just what you've been taught? The lesson says that mere opinion or doubt is not enough. If your faith were tested by someone demanding proof, would you crumble or stand firm? What will you do today to strengthen one belief with actual evidence?
?
The lesson requires that your certainty conforms to reality. Have you ever held a sincere belief about God that later turned out to be incorrect? How do you know your current beliefs about God's attributes match what He truly is? Are you willing to examine them critically, even if it means changing something you've held for years?
?
You are legally responsible before God to know Him and His messengers with certainty and proof. If your life ended tonight, would your faith meet that standard? Don't assume it does—check honestly. What is the one doubt or weak belief about Allah that you have avoided confronting? Will you face it today with a scholar or a book, rather than letting it slide?

The Obligation of Certainty from Demonstration
Sanusi explains the obligation and the source of certainty.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the religious obligation to know Allah and His messengers requires certain knowledge that must be based on demonstration (burhan) rather than unexamined necessity.

Core Idea
Religious certainty must be grounded in demonstration.

Sanusi now applies the earlier definitions to the central obligation of kalam. He unpacks the well-known phrase 'it is obligatory upon every legally responsible person to know...' and explains that this obligation demands certainty (jazzm) that matches reality. This certainty must come from either necessity (darura) or demonstration (burhan). However, since God has not made it customary to attain such certainty through mere necessity, demonstration becomes the only viable path. This lesson establishes that rational proof is indispensable for fulfilling one's religious duty.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without pausing. Then read the English translation. Let Sanusi's concise style sink in before you analyze.
Notice the phrase 'إلى آخره' which indicates the author is referring to a well-known statement.
2
Understand what 'jazzm' means here
The passage says certainty must be 'conforming to what is actually the case.' This is not mere subjective conviction; it is objective certainty that matches reality.
3
Identify the two sources of certainty
The passage names 'necessity' (darura) and 'demonstration' (burhan). Ask yourself: What is the difference? Which one is available?
Necessity is immediate knowledge that cannot be doubted, like sensory perception. Demonstration is rational proof.
4
Grasp why demonstration is necessary
Sanusi states that God did not make it customary for necessity to occur (in these matters). Therefore, we must seek certainty through demonstration. This is the key conclusion.
This answers a potential objection: 'Why can't we rely on simple faith or innate knowledge?'

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإذا عرفت هذه المقدّمة عرفت حينئذٍ معنى قولنا : ( يجب على كلِّ مُكلَّف أن يعرف ... ( إلى آخره ؛ أي : يجب شرعاً على كلّ ) مُكلَّف أن يجزمَ بهذهِ الثلاثةِ في حقِّهِ تعالى ، وفي حقٌّ رُسُلِهِ عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، جزماً مطابقاً لِمَا في نفس الأمرِ ، حاصلاً ذلكَ الجزمُ عن ضرورة أو برهان ، إلا أنَّ الضرورة لم يُجْرِ الله تعالى بها العادةَ ، فتعيَّن طلبها بالبرهان .
So if you have understood this preamble, you will then understand the meaning of our saying: 'It is obligatory upon every legally responsible person to know ... etc.' That is: it is legally obligatory upon every legally responsible person to hold certain judgment (jazzm) concerning these three matters regarding God Most High and regarding His messengers, peace and blessings be upon them—a judgment conforming to what is actually the case, such judgment being obtained from necessity (darura) or demonstration (burhan). However, God Most High has not made it customary for necessity to occur, so seeking it through demonstration is determined (as the only means).
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage applies the earlier definitions to the central obligation of kalam. The author explains that the well-known statement 'It is obligatory upon every legally responsible person to know...' means that one must have certainty (jazzm) about God and His messengers, and this certainty must match reality. It must arise from either necessary knowledge (darura) or demonstration (burhan). However, since God has not made it customary to attain such certainty through mere necessity, the only remaining method is demonstration (burhan). Thus, the passage establishes that rational proof is the necessary means to fulfill the religious obligation of knowing God and His messengers.

Key Terms
مكلّف
جزم
ضرورة
برهان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that blind faith or emotional conviction is sufficient for the obligation to know God.
Correction: The passage insists on certainty (jazzm) that conforms to reality, derived from necessity or demonstration. Since necessity is not available, demonstration is required—mere blind faith is insufficient.

Checkpoint
Why is demonstration (burhan) necessary for the obligatory knowledge?
A) Because necessary knowledge is impossible
B) Because God did not make it customary for necessity to occur
C) Because demonstration is easier
D) Because the prophets commanded it

Key Takeaway
Certainty in matters of faith must be based on demonstration, not on unexamined necessity or blind acceptance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever examined the basis of your certainty about God? If it is not from demonstration, is it truly certain conforming to reality?
?
Would you be able to provide a rational proof for God's existence if asked? If not, what does that say about the strength of your conviction?
?
Are you content to rely on inherited belief or authority, knowing that this passage says such certainty is insufficient? What will you do to seek demonstration?

The Obligation of the Muqallid to Seek Proof
Sanusi gives the ruling that a muqallid must search for demonstrative proof for his beliefs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why a person who has correct beliefs through imitation is still required to seek rational proof according to Sanusi.

Core Idea
Correct belief without proof is not enough—seekers must strive for certainty.

Sanusi now addresses a common scenario: a person who holds true beliefs about God and the prophets, but only because they heard it from others, not because they reasoned it out. If such a person has the ability to understand arguments, what is their obligation? The passage gives a clear reply.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice how Sanusi describes the situation of a muqallid and then presents the strongest scholarly view.
The passage includes a footnote marker (1) in the original.
2
Identify the ruling and condition
Find the phrase 'the most correct of them is that it is obligatory upon him to search for demonstration'. Note the condition: 'as long as there is in him capacity to understand that'.
3
Reflect on the rationale
Ask yourself: why is imitation not sufficient even for correct beliefs? The commentary explains that certainty must be based on evidence, not blind acceptance.
This connects to the core idea that faith requires rational grounding.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولو حصل منهُم جزمٌ مطابق لِمَا في نفس الأمر ، إلا أنه لم يكن عن ضرورة ولا برهان ، بل إنَّما كان عن تقليد . . ففي ذلك طرق وأقوال ، أصحها : أَنَّهُ يجب عليهِ البحثُ عن البرهان حتى تحصلَ لهُ المعرفة عنه (١) ، مهما كانَتْ فيهِ قابليةٌ لفهم ذلكَ
And if a certainty conforming to what is actually the case is obtained by them, but it was not from necessity nor demonstration, but rather from imitation (taqlid)—there are ways and sayings regarding that; the most correct of them is that it is obligatory upon him to search for demonstration until knowledge of it is obtained by him, as long as there is in him capacity to understand that.
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage addresses the case of a person (a muqallid) who holds correct beliefs about God and His messengers through imitation (taqlid) without proof. The author notes that there are multiple scholarly opinions on this matter, but the strongest view (asahhuha) is that such a person must actively seek demonstrative proof until he attains knowledge (ma'rifa) through it, provided he has the intellectual capacity to understand it. This ruling stresses that mere acceptance of correct beliefs through blind imitation is not sufficient; one must engage in rational inquiry to meet the obligation of knowing God.

Key Terms
تقليد
البرهان
المعرفة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If one holds correct beliefs, imitation is enough; no further effort is required.
Correction: According to Sanusi, the most correct view is that a muqallid who is capable of understanding proof must actively seek demonstrative certainty.

Checkpoint
What is the most correct ruling according to Sanusi regarding a muqallid?
A) His imitation is sufficient
B) He must search for demonstration
C) He is excused due to ignorance
D) He must follow his imam only

Key Takeaway
A muqallid who can understand proof must seek it; mere imitation is insufficient.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are you comfortable with a faith that you inherited from your family without ever actively seeking proof? What would you risk if you actually tried to rationally understand why God exists?
?
The passage says you must search for demonstration if you have the capacity. Do you truly believe you lack capacity, or are you avoiding the effort? What small step can you take today to seek certainty?
?
Imagine standing before Allah and He asks why you believed in Him. Would your answer be 'because my parents told me' or 'because I sought and found evidence'? What will you do from today to ensure your answer is the latter?

The Customary Necessity of Knowledge: Scholarly Dispute
Sanusi reports a scholarly disagreement on the customary necessity of knowledge

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the nature of the dispute among Ash'ari imams regarding whether God's creation of knowledge of the conclusion after knowledge of the evidence is necessary by custom.

Core Idea
Knowledge of evidence does not cause knowledge of the conclusion; God habitually creates it.

This lesson examines a point of internal Ash'ari debate: is the connection between knowing a proof and knowing its conclusion a matter of divine custom or logical necessity? Sanusi notes that the imams differed on whether God customarily creates knowledge of the thing evidenced after knowledge of the evidence, absent impediments. This distinction is key to understanding Ash'ari occasionalism.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic and translation
Read the Arabic passage once to get its rhythm. Then read the literal translation. Notice the key terms: 'madlul', 'dalil', 'āfa khāṣṣa', 'āfa 'āmma', and 'lāzim 'ādatan'.
The ellipsis indicates a pause; the phrase 'lāzim 'ādatan' completes the question.
2
Understand the technical terms
Identify the 'evidence' (dalil) and the 'thing evidenced' (madlul). The 'impediments' (āfa) are either specific (e.g., mental deficiency) or general (e.g., death). The absence of these allows the customary sequence to operate.
3
Ask: Why 'by custom' and not 'by necessity'?
If the connection were necessary by reason, God would be compelled. But Ash'aris hold that God is free; the regular sequence is a habit. This dispute explores whether that habit is absolute.
This connects to the broader principle of occasionalism in Ash'ari theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واختلف أئمَّتُنا : هل خَلْقُ اللهِ تعالى معرفة المدلولِ عقب خلقِهِ معرفة الدليل مِنْ غيرِ عُروض آفةٍ خاصَّةٍ ولا عامة . . لازم عادةً
And our imams differed: whether God Most High's creation of knowledge of the thing evidenced (madlul) subsequent to His creation of knowledge of the evidence (dalil), without the occurrence of a specific impediment nor a general one, is necessary by custom (lazim 'ādatan).
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage presents a point of scholarly disagreement among Ash'ari imams. The question is: when God creates knowledge of the evidence (dalil) in a person, and no specific impediment (like mental deficiency) or general impediment (like death) prevents it, does God customarily (bi'l-‘āda) create knowledge of the thing evidenced (madlul) as a necessary consequence? The phrase 'lazim 'ādatan' means 'necessarily by custom'—implying that this sequence is not ontologically necessary but is the usual pattern established by God. The author does not resolve the dispute here, but simply notes that it is a point of difference. This reflects the Ash'ari view that causal relations are not necessary but habitual.

Key Terms
المدلول
الدليل
الآفة الخاصة
الآفة العامة
لازم عادةً

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What is the subject of the difference among the imams?
A) Whether the evidence itself causes knowledge
B) Whether God creates knowledge of the conclusion after knowledge of the evidence by custom
C) Whether knowledge is possible without proof
D) Whether impediments affect knowledge

Key Takeaway
This passage highlights that for Ash'ari scholars, the connection between evidence and conclusion is a matter of divine custom, not logical necessity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you study proofs about Allah, do you genuinely rely on Him to create understanding in your heart, or do you secretly trust your own intellect? What would change if you truly believed that every moment of insight is a direct gift from Allah?
?
Think of a time you were certain about a religious truth after studying evidence. Did you thank Allah for creating that certainty in you, or did you attribute it to your own reasoning? How can you make your study of knowledge an act of dependence on Allah?
?
If the connection between evidence and conclusion is merely customary—not necessary—then your understanding could fail at any moment if Allah wills. Does that thought humble you? How does it affect the way you approach learning about your faith?

The Necessary: Intuitive and Speculative Knowledge
Sanusi defines al-wajib and divides it into daruri and nazari.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of al-wajib (the necessary) and its two categories: intuitive (daruri) and speculative (nazari).

Core Idea
The necessary is that whose non-existence is inconceivable; it may be known immediately or through reflection.

In this passage, Sanusi lays the groundwork for all theological reasoning by defining what it means for something to be rationally necessary. He then divides the necessary into two types based on how we arrive at that certainty. This distinction is crucial for understanding subsequent discussions of what must be affirmed of Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it. Then read the literal translation to understand the flow.
Notice that the passage starts with a definition, then a division, then a justification.
2
Identify the two types of the necessary
Find where Sanusi distinguishes between the intuitive (daruri) and the speculative (nazari). Note how he defines each and the examples given.
The key words are 'بلا تأمل' (without reflection) and 'بعد التأمّل' (after reflection).
3
Reflect on why this distinction matters
Sanusi explains that the mukallaf must know rational judgments about Allah and His messengers. Understanding the types of necessary is the first step to knowing what is necessary, impossible, and possible for them.
This connects to the broader purpose of kalam: defending theology with reason.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
تعريف الواجب الضروري والنظري
وحقيقة الواجب : ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ ؛ إما بلا تأمل ويُسمَّى الضروري ؛ ككون الواحد نصف الاثنين مثلاً ، وإمَّا بعد التأمُّلِ ويُسمَّى النظري ؛ ككون الواحد نصف سدس الاثني عشر مثلاً .
ولما قدم الحكم بوجوب معرفة المكلف شرعاً لما يجب عقلاً وما يستحيل عقلاً وما يجوز عقلاً في حق الله تعالى ، وفي حق رسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، وكان الحكم على شيء أو بشيء موقوفاً على تصور معناهما .. تعين على كل مكلف أن يعرف معنى الحكم العقلي وأقسامه ومعانيها ؛ ليعرف بذلك معنى وجوب ما يجب من الكمالات لمولانا تبارك وتعالى ، ومعنى استحالة ما ينزه عنه تعالى ، ومعنى جواز ما يجوز في حقه تعالى ، ويعرف بذلك ما تتعلق به الصفات من أقسام الحكم العقلي ، وما لا تتعلق به منها ، وبفهم ذلك يتأنى له فهم البراهين ، وفهم لزوم المعارف لها ، ورد الشبه والجهالات التي صاحبتها ، وبذلك يعرف أيضاً ما يجب في حق الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام أو يستحيل أو يجوز .
Definition of the necessary, the intuitive and the speculative. And the reality of the necessary: that whose non-existence is not conceivable in the mind; either without reflection and it is called the intuitive, such as one being half of two, for example; or after reflection and it is called the speculative, such as one being half of one-sixth of twelve, for example. And since he had previously stated the ruling that the legally accountable person is obligated by divine law to know what is necessarily true, what is impossible, and what is possible by reason concerning Allah, and concerning His messengers, peace be upon them, and the ruling about something or by something is dependent on conceiving their meanings, it is incumbent upon every legally accountable person to know the meaning of rational judgment, its divisions, and their meanings; so that by that he may know the meaning of the necessity of the perfections necessary for our Master, Blessed and Exalted is He, and the meaning of the impossibility of that which He is transcendent above, and the meaning of the possibility of that which is possible in His right, and by that he knows which attributes are related to the divisions of rational judgment and which are not related to them, and by understanding that he may achieve the understanding of proofs, and the understanding of the necessary connection of the cognitions to them, and the refutation of doubts and ignorances that accompany them, and by that he also knows what is necessary, impossible, or possible concerning the messengers, peace be upon them.
Source pages p. 132

Commentary
This passage defines the reality of the necessary (al-wajib) and divides it into two categories: intuitive (daruri) and speculative (nazari). It begins by stating that the necessary is that whose non-existence is inconceivable in the mind. Then it explains that if this inconceivability occurs without any mental reflection, it is called intuitive (daruri), giving the example of knowing that one is half of two. If it requires reflection, it is called speculative (nazari), exemplified by knowing that one is half of one-sixth of twelve. The author then explains why these definitions are important: because the mukallaf is obligated by shari'ah to know what is rationally necessary, impossible, and possible concerning Allah and His messengers. Since any judgment about something depends on first conceiving the meanings of the subject and predicate, it is necessary for every mukallaf to understand the meaning of rational judgment (al-hukm al-'aqli) and its divisions. Once this is understood, one can grasp the meanings of the necessary perfections of Allah, His transcendence, and what is possible for Him. Furthermore, one can understand which of the divine attributes fall under which division of rational judgment, and this understanding enables the comprehension of proofs, the necessary connection between knowledge and proofs, and the refutation of doubts and ignorances. Finally, it helps in knowing what is necessary, impossible, and possible concerning the messengers.

Key Terms
الواجب
الضروري
النظري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the necessary (al-wajib) only refers to what is known without proof (intuitive), excluding what is known through reflection.
Correction: Sanusi clearly states that the necessary includes both intuitive (daruri) and speculative (nazari) types. The speculative is also necessary, but it requires reflection to be known.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the definition of al-wajib (the necessary)?
A) What exists in the mind.
B) That whose non-existence is inconceivable in the mind.
C) What is known without proof.
D) What is known after reflection.

Key Takeaway
The necessary is that whose non-existence is inconceivable, and it is either intuitive (known without reflection) or speculative (known after reflection).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have just learned that some necessary truths require reflection. Are you willing to engage in that reflection to know your Lord, or do you rely only on what comes easily? What are you actually doing about it?
?
Consider that knowing Allah is dependent on understanding rational categories. When you have doubts, do you seek to understand the proofs, or do you avoid them? What will you change starting today?
?
The passage warns about ignorance and doubts. What specific doubt have you entertained about Allah that you have not resolved? How will you use this lesson to address it?

Definition of Rational Judgment and Its Distinction
Sanusi defines rational judgment and distinguishes it from customary and legal judgments.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the precise definition of rational judgment (al-hukm al-'aqli) and how it differs from customary and legal judgments.

Core Idea
Rational judgment is what we know without relying on custom or divine law.

In this passage, Sanusi lays the foundation for all of kalam by defining the very tool we use: rational judgment. He gives a precise definition and then distinguishes it from two other types of judgment that might be confused with it. This clarity is essential before we can discuss what reason can know about Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition carefully
Read the opening sentence that defines rational judgment. Notice the two negations: 'without dependence on repetition' and 'nor on the stipulation of a stipulator'.
These two negations are the key to the definition.
2
Understand the first exclusion
The first qualification excludes customary judgments based on repeated experience. The example of food warming the body illustrates this.
3
Understand the second exclusion
The second qualification excludes legal judgments based on divine legislation. The example of bartering dates and wheat illustrates this.
4
Relate to the bigger picture
Ask yourself why it matters to isolate rational judgment. Rational judgments are the bedrock of theological proofs, independent of custom and law.
This connects to the learning objective.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أما معنى الحكم العقلي : فهو إثبات أمر أو نفيه من غير توقف على تكرر ولا وضع واضع .
فقولنا : ( من غير توقف على تكرر ) احتراز من الحكم العادي ؛ أي : الذي عرف من العادة ؛ فإن الإثبات فيه والنفي إنما عرفا وحكم بهما بواسطة التكرر والتجربة ؛ كقولنا : أكل هذا الطعام يسخن البدن ، وأكل هذا لا يسخنه .
وقولنا : ( ولا وضع واضع ) احتراز من الحكم الشرعي ؛ أي : الذي عرف من الشرع ؛ فإن الإثبات أيضاً فيه والنفي إنما عرفا وحكم بهما بواسطة وضع الشرع لذلك ؛ كقولنا : البر بالتمر يجوز فيه التفاضل ، والبر بالبر لا يجوز فيه التفاضل .
ومثال الإثبات في الحكم العقلي : قولنا مثلاً : كل موجود فهو :
As for the meaning of rational judgment: it is affirming something or negating it without dependence on repetition nor on the stipulation of a stipulator. So our statement: 'without dependence on repetition' is a qualification excluding customary judgment; that is, that which is known from custom; for affirmation and negation in it are only known and judged by means of repetition and experience; such as our statement: 'Eating this food warms the body, and eating this does not warm it.' And our statement: 'nor on the stipulation of a stipulator' is a qualification excluding legal judgment; that is, that which is known from the Law; for affirmation and negation in it are only known and judged by means of the Law's stipulation for that; such as our statement: 'Dates for wheat, inequality in it is permissible, and wheat for wheat, inequality in it is not permissible.' And an example of affirmation in rational judgment: our statement, for example: 'Every existent is ...'
Source pages p. 133

Commentary
This passage defines rational judgment (al-hukm al-'aqli) and distinguishes it from two other types of judgment: customary and legal. It begins by stating that rational judgment is the affirmation or negation of a matter without dependence on repetition (custom) nor on the stipulation of a stipulator (divine law). It then explains the first qualification: 'without dependence on repetition' excludes customary judgment (al-hukm al-'adi), which is based on repeated experience, giving the example of eating certain food warming the body. Then it explains the second qualification: 'nor on the stipulation of a stipulator' excludes legal judgment (al-hukm al-shar'i), which is based on divine legislation, giving the example of permissible inequality in bartering dates for wheat but not wheat for wheat. It then begins to give an example of a rational judgment, stating 'Every existent is...' but the text breaks off. Nevertheless, the definition and key distinctions are clear.

Key Terms
الحكم العقلي
الحكم العادي
الحكم الشرعي
إثبات
نفي
تكرر
وضع واضع

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That rational judgment is only based on reason in isolation, without any reference to experience or revelation.
Correction: Rational judgment is defined by not depending on custom or law, but it may still involve reasoning about empirical or revealed premises; the key is that the judgment itself is derived rationally, not by repetition or stipulation.

Checkpoint
What distinguishes rational judgment (al-hukm al-'aqli) from customary judgment (al-hukm al-'adi)?
A) Rational judgment does not depend on repetition or custom.
B) Rational judgment is based on divine revelation.
C) Rational judgment is based on experience.
D) Rational judgment is limited to necessary truths.

Key Takeaway
Rational judgment is the affirmation or negation of something based purely on reason, not on repeated experience or divine legislation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a moral dilemma, do you tend to rely more on what people commonly do (custom) or on what reason dictates? How might this affect the strength of your conviction?
?
Is there a belief you hold about God that you have never examined to see if it is based on custom, law, or pure reason? What would it take for you to check its foundation?
?
If rational judgment is independent of experience and divine command, what does that mean for your personal responsibility to use your intellect in matters of faith? Are you willing to exercise it today?

Why the Derived Forms Are Used in Kalam
Sanusi gives two reasons for using derived forms over abstract nouns.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two reasons Sanusi gives for using the derived adjectives (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) instead of their abstract noun sources.

Core Idea
Derived forms are more specific and practical; knowing them implies knowing the general.

Sanusi explains his choice of terminology in the creed. He opts for the active participles over the abstract nouns based on logical and practical considerations. Let's examine his reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation carefully. Pay attention to the logical flow.
2
Identify the first reason
Notice that the first reason is based on specificity: the derivative is more specific than its source, and knowing the specific implies the general.
This is a logical principle: knowledge of the particular entails knowledge of the universal.
3
Identify the second reason
The second reason is practical consistency: earlier it was stated that one must know the specific attributes (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) regarding Allah and His messengers, so explaining these derivatives aligns with that obligation.
This shows Sanusi's attention to coherence in his presentation.
4
Reflect on the overall argument
Consider why both reasons together justify the use of derived forms over abstract nouns. This sets the stage for understanding the three categories.
Think about how terminology affects the clarity of theological concepts.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنَّما تَعرَّضُنا في أصل العقيدة لشرح الواجب والمستحيل والجائز دون الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز لاستلزام تصورها تصور مصادرها لأن المشتق أخص من مصدره الذي اشتق منه ومعرفة الأخص تستلزم معرفة الأعم بخلاف العكس. وأيضا لما ذكرنا أنه يجب على المكلف أن يعرف الواجب في حقه تعالى والمستحيل والجائز وكذا في حق رسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام ولم نقل يجب عليه أن يعرف في حق الله تعالى وفي حق رسله الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز كان الأنسب في مطابقة ما سبق أن نتعرَّض لشرح المشتقات وهي أسماء الفاعلين لا المشتق منها وهي المصادر. وإنما بدأنا بشرح الواجب لوجهين أحدهما أنه أشرف إذ هو الذي يتصف به مولانا جل وعلا والثاني أنه إذا عرف عرف منه المستحيل والجائز في حقه تعالى.
And we only addressed in the principle of the creed the explanation of the Wajib, the Mustahil, and the Ja'iz, instead of necessity, impossibility, and possibility, because their conceptualization necessitates the conceptualization of their sources, since the derivative is more specific than its source from which it is derived, and knowledge of the more specific necessitates knowledge of the more general, unlike the reverse. And also because we mentioned that it is obligatory upon the responsible person to know the Wajib in His right Most High, and the Mustahil and the Ja'iz, and likewise regarding His messengers, peace and blessings be upon them, and we did not say that he must know in the right of Allah Most High and the right of His messengers necessity, impossibility, and possibility. So it was more appropriate in conformity with what preceded that we address the explanation of the derivatives, which are active participles, not the source from which they are derived, which are infinitives. And we only began with the explanation of the Wajib for two reasons: one is that it is nobler, since it is what our Master, Majestic and Exalted, is described with; and the second is that when it is known, the Mustahil and the Ja'iz regarding Him Most High are known from it.
Source pages p. 135

Commentary
This passage explains the author's choice to use the derived forms (al-wājib, al-mustaḥīl, al-jā'iz) rather than the abstract nouns (al-wujūb, al-istiḥālah, al-jawāz). The first reason is that a derivative (mushtaqq) is more specific than its source (masdar), and knowing the specific implies knowing the general (but not vice versa). The second reason is that earlier it was stated that one must know the wājib, mustaḥīl, and jā'iz regarding Allah and His messengers, so it is appropriate to explain those specific terms. Then he gives two reasons for starting with al-wājib: it is nobler (because it describes Allah) and knowing it leads to knowledge of the impossible and possible regarding Him.

Key Terms
واجب
مستحيل
جائز
مشتق
مصدر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think abstract nouns are more fundamental and should be explained first.
Correction: Sanusi argues derivatives are more specific and practical; they are the terms actually used in obligations, so they are the appropriate starting point.

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi use the derived forms (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) instead of the abstract nouns (wujud, istihalah, jawaz)?
A) Because the derived forms are easier to understand
B) Because the derived forms are more specific and knowing them implies knowing the general, and they align with the earlier obligation
C) Because the derived forms are mentioned in the Quran
D) Because the derived forms are shorter

Key Takeaway
Sanusi prefers derived forms because they are more specific and align with the practical obligation to know these attributes of Allah and His messengers.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you learn about Allah, do you settle for abstract concepts like 'His mercy is vast' without asking what that means for your specific actions today? What one specific attribute of Allah (e.g., al-Wājib) will you turn into a concrete act of obedience before you sleep?
?
Sanusi prioritized derivatives because they are more specific. In your own study of Islam, have you avoided diving into the detailed obligations (wājib) because you felt the general principle was enough? What is one specific ruling you have neglected that you will now look up and act upon?
?
If knowing the specific implies knowledge of the general, why do you often skip the specifics of worship and hope your vague intentions suffice? Choose one action you plan to do tomorrow and decide to do it with full awareness that Allah is Wājib al-Wujūd—the Necessarily Existent—and let that awe reshape your execution.
