Method of Learning
beginner
3 min
Why Sanusi Begins with al-Wajib
Sanusi gives two reasons for starting with al-wajib.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two reasons Sanusi gives for starting the explanation of the three categories with al-wajib.

Core Idea
Al-wajib is nobler and knowing it reveals the other categories.

After explaining why he uses derived forms, Sanusi now explains why he begins with al-wajib. He offers two compelling reasons rooted in theology and logic. Let's explore them.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the short Arabic passage and its translation. Notice that it is a direct explanation with two reasons.
2
Identify the first reason: nobility
The first reason is that al-wajib is nobler because it is an attribute of Allah Himself. Therefore, it deserves priority.
This reflects the theological principle that what relates directly to Allah is most honorable.
3
Identify the second reason: epistemological priority
Knowing al-wajib leads to knowledge of al-mustahil and al-ja'iz. They are defined in relation to the necessary.
For example, if something is necessary for Allah, its opposite is impossible, and what is neither is possible.
4
Reflect on the logic
Consider how the reasons work together: nobility gives it honor, and epistemological priority makes it the key to understanding the other two categories.
This shows Sanusi's pedagogical method: start with the most important and foundational.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنَّما تَعرَّضُنا في أصل العقيدة لشرح الواجب والمستحيل والجائز دون الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز لاستلزام تصورها تصور مصادرها لأن المشتق أخص من مصدره الذي اشتق منه ومعرفة الأخص تستلزم معرفة الأعم بخلاف العكس. وأيضا لما ذكرنا أنه يجب على المكلف أن يعرف الواجب في حقه تعالى والمستحيل والجائز وكذا في حق رسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام ولم نقل يجب عليه أن يعرف في حق الله تعالى وفي حق رسله الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز كان الأنسب في مطابقة ما سبق أن نتعرَّض لشرح المشتقات وهي أسماء الفاعلين لا المشتق منها وهي المصادر. وإنما بدأنا بشرح الواجب لوجهين أحدهما أنه أشرف إذ هو الذي يتصف به مولانا جل وعلا والثاني أنه إذا عرف عرف منه المستحيل والجائز في حقه تعالى.
And we only addressed in the principle of the creed the explanation of the Wajib, the Mustahil, and the Ja'iz, instead of necessity, impossibility, and possibility, because their conceptualization necessitates the conceptualization of their sources, since the derivative is more specific than its source from which it is derived, and knowledge of the more specific necessitates knowledge of the more general, unlike the reverse. And also because we mentioned that it is obligatory upon the responsible person to know the Wajib in His right Most High, and the Mustahil and the Ja'iz, and likewise regarding His messengers, peace and blessings be upon them, and we did not say that he must know in the right of Allah Most High and the right of His messengers necessity, impossibility, and possibility. So it was more appropriate in conformity with what preceded that we address the explanation of the derivatives, which are active participles, not the source from which they are derived, which are infinitives. And we only began with the explanation of the Wajib for two reasons: one is that it is nobler, since it is what our Master, Majestic and Exalted, is described with; and the second is that when it is known, the Mustahil and the Ja'iz regarding Him Most High are known from it.
Source pages p. 135

Commentary
This passage explains the author's choice to use the derived forms (al-wājib, al-mustaḥīl, al-jā'iz) rather than the abstract nouns (al-wujūb, al-istiḥālah, al-jawāz). The first reason is that a derivative (mushtaqq) is more specific than its source (masdar), and knowing the specific implies knowing the general (but not vice versa). The second reason is that earlier it was stated that one must know the wājib, mustaḥīl, and jā'iz regarding Allah and His messengers, so it is appropriate to explain those specific terms. Then he gives two reasons for starting with al-wājib: it is nobler (because it describes Allah) and knowing it leads to knowledge of the impossible and possible regarding Him.

Key Terms
واجب
مستحيل
جائز
أشرف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think the order of explaining the three categories is arbitrary or based on ease.
Correction: Sanusi deliberately begins with al-wajib because it is the noblest and knowing it reveals the other two categories.

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi begin with the explanation of al-wajib rather than al-mustahil or al-ja'iz?
A) Because it is the easiest to understand
B) Because it is the most common term
C) Because it is nobler and knowing it reveals the other two
D) Because it has the longest definition

Key Takeaway
Sanusi begins with al-wajib because it is the noblest attribute and serves as the key to understanding the other two categories.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about Allah, do you first focus on what is necessary for Him—like His perfection and majesty—or do you start from your own assumptions? How would your worship change if you prioritized the noble attributes as Sanusi did?
?
Sanusi says that knowing al-wajib reveals what is impossible and possible. In your daily life, do you seek to understand Allah's necessary attributes before forming opinions about what He can or cannot do? What mistaken beliefs might you avoid by doing so?
?
The author chose to begin with the noblest topic. In your study of Islam, do you deliberately start with the most important and honorable subjects, or do you let convenience dictate? Commit to reordering one area of your learning to reflect this priority.

Method of Learning
beginner
4 min
Why Derived Terms Are Used Instead of Sources
Sanusi explains his methodological choice to use derived terms over source nouns.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the methodological reasoning behind using derived terms (الواجب، المستحيل، الجائز) rather than their source nouns (الوجوب، الاستحالة، الجواز) in kalam texts.

Core Idea
Derived terms are more specific; knowing the specific gives knowledge of the general.

Sanusi gives a brief but important methodological reason for his choice of terminology. He explains that the derived terms (al-wājib, etc.) are more specific (أخص) than the source nouns (al-wujūb, etc.). Since knowing the specific entails knowing the general, using the derived terms is more efficient and instructive.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage once through, then read the English translation. Notice the two sets of terms: the derived terms (الواجب, المستحيل, الجائز) and the source nouns (الوجوب, الاستحالة, الجواز).
This passage is short but dense. Pay attention to the logical connective 'لأنَّ' (because).
2
Identify the logical relationship
Sanusi claims that the derived term is 'more specific' (أخص) than its source. Reflect on what that means: the derived term (e.g., الواجب) has a specific subject (something that must exist), while the source (الوجوب) is the abstract concept of necessity.
In Arabic grammar, the derived term (المشتق) is often an اسم فاعل or اسم مفعول, which implies a relation.
3
Understand the implication of specificity
Knowing the specific (المشتق) gives you knowledge of the general (المصدر), but not vice versa. This is the key methodological point: by explaining the derived terms, Sanusi ensures that the source concepts are also understood.
Consider examples: if you understand what 'necessary' (واجب) means, you also understand 'necessity' (وجوب).
4
Reflect on why this matters for learning aqidah
Ask yourself: why would a teacher choose the more specific terms? The answer is efficiency and clarity. Starting with the specific examples (i.e., things that are necessary, impossible, possible) makes the abstract concepts easier to grasp.
This methodological choice is a common feature in Islamic pedagogy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإِنَّما تَعرَّضُنا في أصل العقيدة لشرح الواجب والمستحيل والجائز ، دون الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز ؛ لاستلزام تصورها تصور مصادرها ؛ لأنَّ المشتق أخصُّ مِنْ مصدرِهِ الذي اشتق منه ومعرفة الأخص تستلزم معرفة الأعم ، بخلافِ العكس
We only addressed in the foundation of the creed the explanation of the Necessary (al-wājib), the Impossible (al-mustaḥīl), and the Possible (al-jāʾiz), without [the sources] obligation (al-wujūb), impossibility (al-istiḥālah), and possibility (al-jawāz), because their conception necessitates the conception of their sources; for the derived term is more specific than its source from which it is derived, and knowledge of the more specific necessitates knowledge of the more general, unlike the reverse.
Source pages p. 135

Commentary
This passage gives the reason why the author chooses to discuss the derived terms (أسماء الفاعلين: الواجب, المستحيل, الجائز) rather than the verbal nouns (المصادر: الوجوب, الاستحالة, الجواز). The reason is that the derived term is more specific (أخص) than its source. Knowing the specific entails knowing the general, but not vice versa. Therefore, by explaining the derived terms, one automatically gains understanding of the source terms as well, which is more efficient.

Key Terms
المشتق
المصدر
الأخص
الأعم
الوجوب
الاستحالة
الجواز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the derived term and source are interchangeable and that the choice is arbitrary.
Correction: The author explains that the derived term is more specific (أخص) and knowing it yields knowledge of the more general source, so the choice is methodologically deliberate and efficient.

Checkpoint
Why does the author use the derived terms (الواجب, etc.) instead of the source terms (الوجوب, etc.)?
A) Because the derived term is more general
B) Because the derived term is more specific, and knowing the specific implies knowing the general
C) Because the source terms are harder to understand
D) Because the derived terms are more poetic

Key Takeaway
Sanusi uses derived terms because they are more specific and knowing them gives knowledge of the more general source terms.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you learn about Allah's attributes, do you tend to memorize abstract definitions or do you actively connect them to concrete realities in your worship and daily life? Be honest: which approach has deepened your knowledge more?
?
Sanusi chose to teach specific terms because knowing them gives you the general. Is there an area in your life where you content yourself with vague generalities about Islam? What would change if you dove into the specific details of that topic starting today?
?
The principle 'knowledge of the specific gives knowledge of the general' applies beyond this lesson. Think of a belief you hold about Allah. Can you trace it back to a specific attribute (like al-Wājib) rather than a general concept? If not, what steps will you take to ground your faith in concrete realities?

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
The Reasoning Behind the Order: Wajib, Muhal, Ja'iz
Sanusi justifies the order of exposition for the three categories.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two reasons Sanusi gives for beginning with al-wajib and the two reasons for placing al-mustahil before al-ja'iz.

Core Idea
The order of exposition reflects nobility and logical priority of simple over compound.

Before diving into the specific attributes of God and His messengers, Sanusi explains his methodological choice for the order of presentation. He first justifies using derived terms (active participles) rather than abstract nouns, then provides two reasons for beginning with the Necessary, and finally two reasons for placing the Impossible before the Possible. This lesson unpacks that reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice that Sanusi gives two sets of reasons: why start with wajib, and why put mustahil before ja'iz.
Also note the justification for using derived terms.
2
Understand the reason for using derived terms
Sanusi says that since we earlier stated the mukallaf must know the three attributes as derived terms (wajib, muhal, ja'iz), not the abstract nouns, it is appropriate to explain the derived terms.
This connects the method to earlier statements.
3
Examine the two reasons for starting with al-wajib
Reason 1: Wajib is nobler because it describes God. Reason 2: Knowing wajib allows knowing the other two.
Nobility and epistemic priority.
4
Examine the two reasons for placing al-mustahil before al-ja'iz
Reason 1: Mustahil is opposite of wajib, thus closer. Reason 2: Ja'iz is a compound of affirmation from wajib and negation from mustahil, while wajib and mustahil are simple; the simple is prior to the compound.
Logical priority.
5
Reflect on the overall methodology
Consider why such ordering matters in theology. The order is not arbitrary but based on inherent relationships.
This shows Sanusi's systematic approach.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأيضاً : لما ذكرنا أنَّه يجب على المكلَّف أن يعرف الواجب في حَقِّهِ تعالى والمستحيل والجائز ، وكذا في حقّ رسله عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام ، ولم نقل : يجب عليه أن يعرف في حق الله تعالى وفي حقّ رسله الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز . . كان الأنسب في مطابقة ما سبق أن نتعرَّضَ لشرح المشتقاتِ ؛ وهي أسماء الفاعلين ، لا المشتق منها ؛ وهي المصادر .
وإنَّما بدأنا بشرح الواجب لوجهين : أحدُهُما : أنَّهُ أشرفُ ؛ إذ هو الذي يتصف به مولانا جل وعلا . والثاني : أنَّهُ إذا عُرِفَ .. عُرِفَ منهُ المستحيلُ والجائز فى حقه تعالى .
وقدمنا المستحيل على الجائز : لأنَّهُ أقرب إلى الواجب ؛ إذ هو مقابله ، وأيضاً : فالجائز شبهُ مُركَّب ممَّا ثبت للواجب مِنَ الثبوتِ ، وما ثبت للمستحيل مِنَ النفي ، والواجب والمستحيل شبه بسيطين ، إذ لم يثبت لكلِّ واحدٍ منهما إلا أحد الأمرين ، ولا شك أن رتبة البسيط أحقُّ أن تكون قبل المركَّبِ (۱) .
And also: since we mentioned that the legally responsible person must know the Necessary (al-wājib) with respect to Him, may He be exalted, and the Impossible (al-mustaḥīl) and the Possible (al-jāʾiz), and likewise with respect to His messengers, peace be upon them, and we did not say: he must know with respect to God and His messengers obligation (al-wujūb), impossibility (al-istiḥālah), and possibility (al-jawāz)... it was more appropriate to conform to what preceded that we address the explanation of the derived terms – which are the active participles – not the terms derived from them – which are the sources. And we only began with the explanation of the Necessary for two reasons: one of them is that it is nobler, since it is that which our Master, may He be glorified and exalted, is described with; and the second is that when it is known, the Impossible and the Possible with respect to Him, may He be exalted, are known from it. And we placed the Impossible before the Possible because it is closer to the Necessary, as it is its opposite, and also because the Possible is like a compound of what is established for the Necessary in terms of affirmation and what is established for the Impossible in terms of negation, while the Necessary and the Impossible are like simples, since for each of them only one of the two matters is established, and there is no doubt that the rank of the simple is more entitled to be before the compound.
Source pages p. 135

Commentary
This passage provides a second reason for using derived terms, then gives two reasons for starting with al-wajib, and then explains the order of al-mustahil before al-ja'iz. The second reason for using derived terms is that the author had already stated that the mukallaf must know al-wajib, al-mustahil, and al-ja'iz (the derived terms) regarding God and His messengers, so it is consistent to explain those. The two reasons for starting with al-wajib are: (1) it is nobler because it describes God, (2) knowing al-wajib leads to knowing the others. Then, the reason for placing al-mustahil before al-ja'iz is twofold: (1) al-mustahil is the opposite of al-wajib and thus closer, (2) al-ja'iz is a compound of affirmation from al-wajib and negation from al-mustahil, while al-wajib and al-mustahil are each simple (only one aspect), and the simple is logically prior to the compound.

Key Terms
الواجب
المستحيل
الجائز
بسيط
مركب
أشرف
المشتقات
أسماء الفاعلين

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the order of exposition is arbitrary or based only on convenience.
Correction: Sanusi shows the order is based on nobility (wajib first) and logical priority of the simple over the compound (mustahil before ja'iz).

Checkpoint
Which of the following is a reason given for placing al-mustahil before al-ja'iz?
A) Al-mustahil is easier to understand
B) Al-mustahil is the opposite of al-wajib and thus closer, and al-ja'iz is a compound of the two
C) Al-mustahil is more important
D) Al-ja'iz requires prior knowledge of al-mustahil

Key Takeaway
The order of exposition in kalam is deliberate, based on nobility and logical priority of the simple over the compound.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If knowing the Necessary is the noblest starting point, what does that reveal about your own spiritual priorities? Do you begin your reflection on God with what He necessarily is, or do you start with what you hope or fear He might do? Commit today to study the attributes of God that are necessary for His perfection.
?
Sanusi insists the simple precedes the compound. In your faith journey, are you building on clear, simple fundamentals (like the oneness of God), or have you jumped into complex debates before solidifying the basics? Identify one core belief you need to strengthen before moving forward.
?
The Possible is a compound from the Necessary and the Impossible. How does this shape your understanding of God's actions? Do you live in the tension that God can act or not act as He wills, without being forced by anything? How can this truth increase your trust in His wisdom and power?

Method of Learning
beginner
4 min
Al-Bali's Four Questions on Divine Transcendence
Al-Bali records four mnemonic questions affirming tawhid and tanzih.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how four simple questions about God's attributes encapsulate the core Islamic beliefs of tawhid and tanzih.

Core Idea
Four questions, four answers: the essence of Islamic monotheism.

Al-Bali presents a mnemonic device: four questions that test one's understanding of God's transcendence. These questions address common misconceptions about God's being, how He is, where He is, and when He is. The answers, drawn from Quran and reason, succinctly affirm that God is utterly unique and beyond human categories.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read Al-Bali's Arabic text and its translation. Notice the four questions and answers as a coherent unit.
2
Identify the four questions
List the four questions: كم, كيف, أين, متى. Understand what each question challenges about God.
These questions target common human assumptions about number, quality, location, and time.
3
Analyze each answer
Examine each answer's theological implication. Note the Quranic reference in the answer to كيف (Quran 42:11) and the negation of spatial and temporal limitations.
The answer to كيف includes 'ليس كمثله شيء' which is a direct Quranic quote.
4
Reflect on 'واحد لا من قلة'
This phrase clarifies that God's oneness is not numerical but unique. Consider why this clarification is needed to avoid misunderstanding.
Without this, one might think God is 'one' in a countable sense.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قال العلامة البيلي في «تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى» (ق 12): (إذا ترك الشخص السؤال بأربع كلمات كملَ إيمانه؛ فإذا قال لك قائل: كم الله؟ الله أحد، وإذا قال لك: كيف الله؟ فقل له: واحد لا من قلة، ليس كمثله شيء وهو السميع البصير، وإذا قال لك: أين الله؟ فقل: لا يحويه مكان، ولا يمرُّ عليه زمان، وإذا قال لك: متى الله؟ فقل: أول بلا ابتداء، وآخر بلا انتهاء؛ أي: لا مثلَ له في ذاته).
Al-Bali said in his annotations on Sharh al-Sughra al-Sughra (folio 12): 'If a person abandons questioning with four words, his faith is complete. So if a questioner says to you: "How many is God?" Say: "God is One." If he says: "How is God?" Say to him: "One, not from paucity; there is nothing like Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing." If he says: "Where is God?" Say: "No place contains Him, nor does time pass over Him." If he says: "When is God?" Say: "First without beginning, Last without end." That is, there is no likeness to Him in His essence.'
Source pages p. 166

Commentary
This passage is a traditional pedagogical note from al-Bali, providing concise answers to four common questions about God. The questions address God's quantity (كم), quality (كيف), location (أين), and time (متى). The answers affirm the core Islamic beliefs of tawhid (oneness), tanzih (transcendence), and eternalness. The phrase 'واحد لا من قلة' clarifies that God's oneness is not numerical but unique. The answer to 'كيف' quotes Quran 42:11. The answer to 'أين' negates spatial containment and temporality. The answer to 'متى' affirms pre-eternity and post-eternity, concluding that God is unlike anything in His essence.

Key Terms
توقيت
مكان
مثل
واحد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The phrase 'واحد لا من قلة' might be misinterpreted as implying God is one in a quantitative sense, i.e., numerically one among many.
Correction: The phrase actually negates any numerical composition; God's oneness is unique and not a category of number.

Checkpoint
According to al-Bali, what is the answer to 'How is God?'
A) God is One.
B) There is nothing like Him, and He is the All-Hearing, the All-Seeing.
C) No place contains Him.
D) First without beginning.

Key Takeaway
The four questions encapsulate the core of Islamic theology: God is one, unique, beyond space and time, and eternal.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'Allah is One,' do you truly mean He is unique and incomparable, or do you sometimes reduce Him to a numerical concept? Reflect on the phrase 'لا من قلة'—how can you make your tawhid more conscious?
?
Do you ever catch yourself imagining God as somehow located in a direction, like above the heavens? How does al-Bali's answer to 'أين الله?' challenge that imagination, and what will you do to purify your mental image of God?
?
The question 'متى الله?' is answered with 'أول بلا ابتداء، وآخر بلا انتهاء'. How does this eternal perspective affect your attitude towards time, your life, and your priorities? Will you live today with the awareness that your time is finite but your Lord is not?

Method of Learning
beginner
4 min
The First Division: Articles Requiring Rational Proof
Sanusi defines the first division and explains the circularity argument.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why certain foundational articles of faith—like God's existence and attributes—can only be known through rational proof, not scripture.

Core Idea
Some truths must be known before we can trust any messenger.

Sanusi begins his classification by identifying the foundational beliefs that make prophecy possible. If these were proven by scripture, a vicious circle would result. This lesson explores that crucial first division and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice how Sanusi defines the first division and gives examples before presenting the circularity argument.
The circularity is key: scripture depends on miracle, miracle depends on God's existence, so scripture cannot prove God's existence.
2
Identify the examples
List the attributes Sanusi gives as examples: existence, power, will, knowledge, life. Ask: why are these essential for a miracle to be possible?
These are necessary attributes of God that a miracle presupposes.
3
Understand the circularity
Trace the argument: if we tried to prove God's existence using scripture, we would need to verify the miracle that attests the messenger, but the miracle already requires God's existence. This is a logical circle.
The Arabic term 'دور' (dawr) means circular reasoning.
4
Reflect on the principle
Ask yourself: Why does Sanusi start with this division? What does it tell us about the role of reason in theology?
It establishes that reason is independent and necessary for grounding revelation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اعلم : أنَّ عقائد الإيمانِ تنقسم إلى ثلاثة أقسام : الأول : ما لا يصح أن يُعلَمَ إلا بالدليل العقلي ؛ وهو كلُّ ما تتوقَّفُ عليه دلالة المعجزة ؛ كوجودِهِ تعالى وقدرته وإرادته وعلمه وحياته ؛ فإِنَّهُ لَوِ اسْتُدِلَّ على هذا القسم بالدليل الشرعي وهو مُتوقفٌ على صدقِ الرسولِ المُتوقِّفِ على دلالة المعجزة . . لزم الدور كلُّ القسم الثاني : ما يصح أن يُستدل عليه بالدليل الشرعي ؛ وهو ما لا تتوقَّفُ عليهِ دلالة المعجزة ؛ كالسمع والبصر والكلام ، والبعث وأحوال الآخرة جملة وتفصيلاً . الثالث : ما اختُلِفَ فيهِ بالتردُّدِ فيهِ : هل هو مِنَ القسم الأولِ ، أو مِنَ القسم الثاني ؛ كالوحدانية ؛ فإنَّهُ اختُلِفَ فيها (۱) : هل يكفي فيها الدليل السمعي ؛ بناءً على عدم توقفِ دلالة المعجزة عليها في علم الناظرِ وإِنْ تَوقَّفَ وجودُ المعجزة عليها في نفس الأمر ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الفعل مع وجودِ الشريكِ ، أو لا بدَّ فيها مِنَ الدليل العقلي ؛ نظراً إلى توقف دلالةِ المعجزة على صحة وجودِ المعجزة المتوقفة على (في هامش ( و ) : ( حاصلُ الخلاف : أنه هل يكفي استناد هذه الصفة إلى السمع وحده أو لا ؟ ذهب الإمامان إمام الحرمين والفخر إلى الأول ، وذهب بعضُ المحققين ومنهم العلامة شرف الدين التلمساني إلى الثاني ، وهو ما عليه المصنف في الكبرى ( ص ٤٢٨] ، فراجعه )
Know that the articles of faith (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) are divided into three divisions: The first: what is not valid to be known except by rational evidence (al-dalīl al-ʿaqlī); it is everything upon which the indication of a miracle depends; like His existence, His power, His will, His knowledge, and His life; for if this division were inferred by scriptural evidence (al-dalīl al-sharʿī) which is dependent upon the veracity of the messenger which is dependent upon the indication of a miracle, circularity (al-dawr) would necessarily follow. The second division: what is valid to be inferred by scriptural evidence; it is what the indication of a miracle does not depend upon; like hearing, sight, and speech, and the resurrection and the states of the hereafter in general and in detail. The third: what is disputed with hesitation concerning it: whether it is of the first division or of the second; like unity (al-waḥdāniyya); for there is disagreement concerning it: is scriptural evidence sufficient for it, based on the fact that the indication of a miracle does not depend upon it in the knowledge of the investigator, even if the existence of a miracle depends upon it in reality, because of the impossibility of an act existing with the existence of a partner, or is rational evidence necessarily required in it, considering that the indication of a miracle depends upon the validity of the existence of the miracle which depends upon (Footnote: The gist of the disagreement: is it sufficient to base this attribute on revelation alone or not? The two imams, Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Fakhr [al-Rāzī], held the first view, and some investigators, among them the scholar Sharaf al-Dīn al-Tilimsānī, held the second, and this is what the author [held] in al-Kubrā, so refer to it.)
Source pages p. 174

Commentary
This passage is a classification of the articles of faith (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) according to the method of proof. Sanusi divides them into three categories. First: those that can only be known by rational evidence (al-dalīl al-ʿaqlī). These are doctrines upon which the very validity of a miracle depends—specifically, God's existence, power, will, knowledge, and life. If one tried to prove these by scriptural evidence, which depends on the veracity of the messenger, which in turn depends on a miracle, a vicious circle (al-dawr) would result. Thus rational proof is indispensable. Second: those that can be validly inferred by scriptural evidence (al-dalīl al-sharʿī). These are doctrines that do not affect the validity of a miracle—like the attributes of hearing, sight, and speech, and the resurrection and afterlife details. These are known from revelation. Third: those about which there is disagreement, such as the oneness of God (al-waḥdāniyya). The dispute is whether it falls into the first or second category. One argument says scriptural evidence is sufficient because the investigator's knowledge of the miracle does not depend on knowing God's oneness, even though in reality a miracle cannot occur if there were a partner with God. The other argument requires rational evidence because the miracle's validity itself depends on the impossibility of a partner. A footnote adds that Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Fakhr al-Rāzī held the first opinion (scripture suffices), while some investigators like Sharaf al-Dīn al-Tilimsānī and the author in his larger work al-Kubrā held the second (rational proof is needed).

Key Terms
دليل عقلي
دور
وجود
قدرة
إرادة
علم
حياة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All Islamic beliefs can be proven by Qur'an and Hadith alone.
Correction: Some foundational truths about God must be rationally established before scripture can be accepted as authoritative.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why can't the first division be proven by scriptural evidence?
A) Because scripture is unreliable
B) Because it would lead to circular reasoning
C) Because the first division is about unseen matters
D) Because scripture only covers legal rulings

Key Takeaway
Rational proof is required for beliefs that underpin the validity of revelation itself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you try to explain to someone why you believe in God, do you find yourself quoting verses before establishing rational foundations? How does this passage expose the weakness of that approach, and what concrete step will you take this week to learn a rational proof for God's existence?
?
Is your personal certainty about God's attributes rooted in intellectual conviction or mere upbringing? If it is the latter, how can you build a rational foundation that would withstand doubt? Will you dedicate time this month to study the kalam arguments for God's power and knowledge?
?
The passage shows that even the miracle of the Qur'an presupposes rational belief in God. Have you ever realized that your acceptance of the Qur'an as revelation depends on a prior rational acknowledgment of God? How does this shift your understanding of faith, and what will you do to ensure your faith is intellectually grounded?

The Second Division: Articles Provable by Scriptural Evidence
Sanusi defines the second division and gives examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand which articles of faith can be known through scriptural evidence alone and why they do not require rational proof.

Core Idea
Some truths are known from revelation, not reason.

After establishing the rationally necessary beliefs, Sanusi turns to the second division: articles that can be validly inferred from scripture. These are doctrines that do not affect the possibility of miracles. This lesson examines those teachings and their reliance on revelation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice that this division includes attributes like hearing, sight, and speech, as well as eschatological details.
These are attributes that are not necessary for the concept of a miracle.
2
Compare with the first division
Contrast the examples: hearing, sight, speech versus existence, power, will. Why are these not required for a miracle to be recognized?
A miracle can be recognized without knowing that God hears or speaks in detail.
3
Understand the logical freedom
Since the indication of a miracle does not depend on these attributes, there is no circularity in proving them from scripture.
Scripture can be accepted on the basis of the miracle, and then it informs us about these attributes.
4
Reflect on the scope
Sanusi also includes the resurrection and all details of the afterlife. This shows that revelation is the primary source for eschatology.
These are matters that reason alone cannot determine.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اعلم : أنَّ عقائد الإيمانِ تنقسم إلى ثلاثة أقسام : الأول : ما لا يصح أن يُعلَمَ إلا بالدليل العقلي ؛ وهو كلُّ ما تتوقَّفُ عليه دلالة المعجزة ؛ كوجودِهِ تعالى وقدرته وإرادته وعلمه وحياته ؛ فإِنَّهُ لَوِ اسْتُدِلَّ على هذا القسم بالدليل الشرعي وهو مُتوقفٌ على صدقِ الرسولِ المُتوقِّفِ على دلالة المعجزة . . لزم الدور كلُّ القسم الثاني : ما يصح أن يُستدل عليه بالدليل الشرعي ؛ وهو ما لا تتوقَّفُ عليهِ دلالة المعجزة ؛ كالسمع والبصر والكلام ، والبعث وأحوال الآخرة جملة وتفصيلاً . الثالث : ما اختُلِفَ فيهِ بالتردُّدِ فيهِ : هل هو مِنَ القسم الأولِ ، أو مِنَ القسم الثاني ؛ كالوحدانية ؛ فإنَّهُ اختُلِفَ فيها (۱) : هل يكفي فيها الدليل السمعي ؛ بناءً على عدم توقفِ دلالة المعجزة عليها في علم الناظرِ وإِنْ تَوقَّفَ وجودُ المعجزة عليها في نفس الأمر ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الفعل مع وجودِ الشريكِ ، أو لا بدَّ فيها مِنَ الدليل العقلي ؛ نظراً إلى توقف دلالةِ المعجزة على صحة وجودِ المعجزة المتوقفة على (في هامش ( و ) : ( حاصلُ الخلاف : أنه هل يكفي استناد هذه الصفة إلى السمع وحده أو لا ؟ ذهب الإمامان إمام الحرمين والفخر إلى الأول ، وذهب بعضُ المحققين ومنهم العلامة شرف الدين التلمساني إلى الثاني ، وهو ما عليه المصنف في الكبرى ( ص ٤٢٨] ، فراجعه )
Know that the articles of faith (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) are divided into three divisions: The first: what is not valid to be known except by rational evidence (al-dalīl al-ʿaqlī); it is everything upon which the indication of a miracle depends; like His existence, His power, His will, His knowledge, and His life; for if this division were inferred by scriptural evidence (al-dalīl al-sharʿī) which is dependent upon the veracity of the messenger which is dependent upon the indication of a miracle, circularity (al-dawr) would necessarily follow. The second division: what is valid to be inferred by scriptural evidence; it is what the indication of a miracle does not depend upon; like hearing, sight, and speech, and the resurrection and the states of the hereafter in general and in detail. The third: what is disputed with hesitation concerning it: whether it is of the first division or of the second; like unity (al-waḥdāniyya); for there is disagreement concerning it: is scriptural evidence sufficient for it, based on the fact that the indication of a miracle does not depend upon it in the knowledge of the investigator, even if the existence of a miracle depends upon it in reality, because of the impossibility of an act existing with the existence of a partner, or is rational evidence necessarily required in it, considering that the indication of a miracle depends upon the validity of the existence of the miracle which depends upon (Footnote: The gist of the disagreement: is it sufficient to base this attribute on revelation alone or not? The two imams, Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Fakhr [al-Rāzī], held the first view, and some investigators, among them the scholar Sharaf al-Dīn al-Tilimsānī, held the second, and this is what the author [held] in al-Kubrā, so refer to it.)
Source pages p. 174

Commentary
This passage is a classification of the articles of faith (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) according to the method of proof. Sanusi divides them into three categories. First: those that can only be known by rational evidence (al-dalīl al-ʿaqlī). These are doctrines upon which the very validity of a miracle depends—specifically, God's existence, power, will, knowledge, and life. If one tried to prove these by scriptural evidence, which depends on the veracity of the messenger, which in turn depends on a miracle, a vicious circle (al-dawr) would result. Thus rational proof is indispensable. Second: those that can be validly inferred by scriptural evidence (al-dalīl al-sharʿī). These are doctrines that do not affect the validity of a miracle—like the attributes of hearing, sight, and speech, and the resurrection and afterlife details. These are known from revelation. Third: those about which there is disagreement, such as the oneness of God (al-waḥdāniyya). The dispute is whether it falls into the first or second category. One argument says scriptural evidence is sufficient because the investigator's knowledge of the miracle does not depend on knowing God's oneness, even though in reality a miracle cannot occur if there were a partner with God. The other argument requires rational evidence because the miracle's validity itself depends on the impossibility of a partner. A footnote adds that Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Fakhr al-Rāzī held the first opinion (scripture suffices), while some investigators like Sharaf al-Dīn al-Tilimsānī and the author in his larger work al-Kubrā held the second (rational proof is needed).

Key Terms
دليل شرعي
السمع
البصر
الكلام
البعث
أحوال الآخرة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
Which of the following is an example of the second division according to Sanusi?
A) God's existence
B) God's power
C) God's speech
D) God's will

Key Takeaway
Some articles of faith, like God's attributes of hearing, sight, and speech, and eschatological details, are known through revelation alone.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly submit to the details of the afterlife that have been revealed, or do you secretly wish for a more 'rational' version? What does that reveal about your trust in Allah's message?
?
The resurrection and states of the hereafter are not rationally provable—they are accepted on faith. Are you living today as if the resurrection is a certainty, or as a distant possibility? What would change if you truly believed it is nearer than your next heartbeat?
?
Sanusi teaches that God's hearing, sight, and speech are known through scripture. When you call upon Allah, do you genuinely believe He hears every word? How would your du'a change if you were utterly convinced of that?

intermediate
5 min
The Third Division: The Disputed Case of God's Unity
Sanusi presents the dispute over whether God's unity requires rational or scriptural proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the disagreement among scholars about whether God's unity (tawḥīd) requires rational proof or scriptural evidence is sufficient.

Core Idea
Even necessary truths can be disputed in their method of proof.

The third division presents a case where scholars disagreed: the oneness of God (al-waḥdāniyya). Some argued that scriptural evidence suffices, while others insisted on rational proof. Sanusi presents both sides and the reasoning behind each. This lesson explores that debate.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice that the dispute is about whether God's unity falls into the first or second division.
The footnote mentions the positions of key scholars.
2
Identify the two positions
First position: scriptural evidence is sufficient, because the investigator's knowledge of the miracle does not depend on knowing unity. Second position: rational proof is needed, because the miracle's existence in reality depends on unity.
The first position is held by Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Rāzī; the second by al-Tilimsānī and the author in al-Kubrā.
3
Understand the reasoning
The first view focuses on epistemology (knowledge of the miracle). The second view focuses on ontology (actual existence of the miracle). Both are coherent.
This shows that subtle distinctions in starting assumptions lead to different conclusions.
4
Reflect on the implication
Why does this matter? It shows that even within orthodox theology, there can be legitimate disagreement about the method of proof for certain doctrines.
This is not a dispute about the doctrine itself, but about how to prove it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اعلم : أنَّ عقائد الإيمانِ تنقسم إلى ثلاثة أقسام : الأول : ما لا يصح أن يُعلَمَ إلا بالدليل العقلي ؛ وهو كلُّ ما تتوقَّفُ عليه دلالة المعجزة ؛ كوجودِهِ تعالى وقدرته وإرادته وعلمه وحياته ؛ فإِنَّهُ لَوِ اسْتُدِلَّ على هذا القسم بالدليل الشرعي وهو مُتوقفٌ على صدقِ الرسولِ المُتوقِّفِ على دلالة المعجزة . . لزم الدور كلُّ القسم الثاني : ما يصح أن يُستدل عليه بالدليل الشرعي ؛ وهو ما لا تتوقَّفُ عليهِ دلالة المعجزة ؛ كالسمع والبصر والكلام ، والبعث وأحوال الآخرة جملة وتفصيلاً . الثالث : ما اختُلِفَ فيهِ بالتردُّدِ فيهِ : هل هو مِنَ القسم الأولِ ، أو مِنَ القسم الثاني ؛ كالوحدانية ؛ فإنَّهُ اختُلِفَ فيها (۱) : هل يكفي فيها الدليل السمعي ؛ بناءً على عدم توقفِ دلالة المعجزة عليها في علم الناظرِ وإِنْ تَوقَّفَ وجودُ المعجزة عليها في نفس الأمر ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الفعل مع وجودِ الشريكِ ، أو لا بدَّ فيها مِنَ الدليل العقلي ؛ نظراً إلى توقف دلالةِ المعجزة على صحة وجودِ المعجزة المتوقفة على (في هامش ( و ) : ( حاصلُ الخلاف : أنه هل يكفي استناد هذه الصفة إلى السمع وحده أو لا ؟ ذهب الإمامان إمام الحرمين والفخر إلى الأول ، وذهب بعضُ المحققين ومنهم العلامة شرف الدين التلمساني إلى الثاني ، وهو ما عليه المصنف في الكبرى ( ص ٤٢٨] ، فراجعه )
Know that the articles of faith (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) are divided into three divisions: The first: what is not valid to be known except by rational evidence (al-dalīl al-ʿaqlī); it is everything upon which the indication of a miracle depends; like His existence, His power, His will, His knowledge, and His life; for if this division were inferred by scriptural evidence (al-dalīl al-sharʿī) which is dependent upon the veracity of the messenger which is dependent upon the indication of a miracle, circularity (al-dawr) would necessarily follow. The second division: what is valid to be inferred by scriptural evidence; it is what the indication of a miracle does not depend upon; like hearing, sight, and speech, and the resurrection and the states of the hereafter in general and in detail. The third: what is disputed with hesitation concerning it: whether it is of the first division or of the second; like unity (al-waḥdāniyya); for there is disagreement concerning it: is scriptural evidence sufficient for it, based on the fact that the indication of a miracle does not depend upon it in the knowledge of the investigator, even if the existence of a miracle depends upon it in reality, because of the impossibility of an act existing with the existence of a partner, or is rational evidence necessarily required in it, considering that the indication of a miracle depends upon the validity of the existence of the miracle which depends upon (Footnote: The gist of the disagreement: is it sufficient to base this attribute on revelation alone or not? The two imams, Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Fakhr [al-Rāzī], held the first view, and some investigators, among them the scholar Sharaf al-Dīn al-Tilimsānī, held the second, and this is what the author [held] in al-Kubrā, so refer to it.)
Source pages p. 174

Commentary
This passage is a classification of the articles of faith (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) according to the method of proof. Sanusi divides them into three categories. First: those that can only be known by rational evidence (al-dalīl al-ʿaqlī). These are doctrines upon which the very validity of a miracle depends—specifically, God's existence, power, will, knowledge, and life. If one tried to prove these by scriptural evidence, which depends on the veracity of the messenger, which in turn depends on a miracle, a vicious circle (al-dawr) would result. Thus rational proof is indispensable. Second: those that can be validly inferred by scriptural evidence (al-dalīl al-sharʿī). These are doctrines that do not affect the validity of a miracle—like the attributes of hearing, sight, and speech, and the resurrection and afterlife details. These are known from revelation. Third: those about which there is disagreement, such as the oneness of God (al-waḥdāniyya). The dispute is whether it falls into the first or second category. One argument says scriptural evidence is sufficient because the investigator's knowledge of the miracle does not depend on knowing God's oneness, even though in reality a miracle cannot occur if there were a partner with God. The other argument requires rational evidence because the miracle's validity itself depends on the impossibility of a partner. A footnote adds that Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Fakhr al-Rāzī held the first opinion (scripture suffices), while some investigators like Sharaf al-Dīn al-Tilimsānī and the author in his larger work al-Kubrā held the second (rational proof is needed).

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All Islamic scholars agree on the method of proof for God's unity.
Correction: There is a legitimate scholarly disagreement about whether rational proof is necessary or scriptural evidence suffices, as seen in the positions of major theologians.

Checkpoint
According to the footnote, which scholars held that scriptural evidence is sufficient for proving God's unity?
A) al-Tilimsānī and the author
B) Imām al-Ḥaramayn and al-Rāzī
C) All scholars agree
D) Only the author

Key Takeaway
The method of proving God's unity is disputed among theologians, reflecting nuanced views on the relationship between reason and revelation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the method of proving God's unity is disputed among great scholars, what does that say about the depth of your own conviction? Do you rest your belief on borrowed certainty, or have you wrestled with the proof yourself?
?
When you recite 'Lā ilāha illallāh', do you feel the weight of its rational necessity, or is it merely a familiar phrase? What would it take for you to own that truth with both mind and heart?
?
The scholars debated whether a miracle can be known independently of God's unity. In your life, how often do you recognise divine signs without connecting them to the One who sent them? What will you do today to make that connection real?

6 min
Types of Indicatory Evidence: Conventional and Habitual
A footnote explaining the nature of conventional and habitual indication.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how conventional indication (dalāla wadʿiyya) in miracles is based on pure reason, not on divine-human agreements, and how it differs from habitual indication.

Core Idea
Miracles are not based on agreements; reason itself recognizes their indication.

This footnote clarifies a subtle but crucial point in kalam epistemology. Before discussing miracles' evidential force, one must understand the nature of the indication they carry. Sanusi here distinguishes three layers: conventional indication (which he argues is rational), habitual indication (firm but not necessary), and pure rational indication. The passage prepares the ground for understanding why miracles are convincing proofs.

Guided Path
1
Read the footnote carefully
Read the Arabic passage once. Then read the literal translation. Note the technical terms: دلالة وضعية, دلالة عادية, دلالة عقلية.
The passage is from a commentary; it assumes familiarity with the main text.
2
Identify the three types of indication
The passage contrasts conventional (وضعية), habitual (عادية), and intellectual (عقلية) indication. Notice how each is defined and distinguished.
3
Understand the rational grounding of conventional indication
The passage argues that conventional indication here does not require agreement (اتفاق) but is based on pure reason. Contrast this with the traffic light example.
This is the key insight: miracles are rationally indicative.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(1) وترجع الدلالة الوضعية إلى العقل أيضاً ؛ إذ المواضعة هنا ليست باتفاق بين تعالى وعباده، فهي ليست كاللون الأحمر الموضوع في إشارة المرور علامة على وجوب الوقوف للمَرْكبة مثلاً فيما بين البشر، بل مواضعة مجرَّد العقل يدلُّ عليها، فلا تتوقف على اتفاق كما قد يتوهم أما من رأى أن دلالتها دلالة عادية فلا يعني بإمكان الانفكاك إلا التجويز العقلي، وإلا فالدلالة العادية عند الأشاعرة متينة، لا يُنصرفُ عنها إلا بدليل وإلا للزمنا التشكيك في كلّ أمرين بينهما ارتباط عادي، فمن سمع بقطع رأس زيد فتوقف في موته لكون الرابط بين الأمرين عادي .. فهو مهوس، ولكن تبقى بين دلالة العقل والوضع ودلالة العادة مفارقة؛ إذ ليست حمرة الخجل في دلالتها على الخَجَل كقيام الملك المرار الثلاث في المثال المشهور والآتي ذكر شبيهه، وانظر ( شرح الإرشاد ) للعلامة المقترح ( ص ٥٠٠ - ٥٠١ )
(1) And the conventional indication also goes back to reason, because the convention here is not an agreement between the Exalted and His servants. It is not like the red light in a traffic sign as a sign for the obligation to stop for a vehicle, for example, among humans. Rather, it is a convention that pure reason indicates, so it does not depend on agreement as might be supposed. As for whoever holds that its indication is habitual, that does not mean anything other than intellectual permissibility of separation. Otherwise, the habitual indication among the Ash'aris is firm; it is not to be deviated from except with evidence, otherwise we would be forced to doubt every two matters between which there is habitual connection. So whoever hears of the cutting off of Zayd's head and then hesitates regarding his death because the connection between the two matters is habitual – he is insane. But there remains a distinction between intellectual and conventional indication on one hand and habitual indication on the other: for the redness of shame in its indication of shame is not like the possession of property for three years in the well-known example, and similar to be mentioned. And see (Sharh al-Irshād) by al-ʿAllāma al-Muqriḥ (pp. 500-501).
Source pages p. 176

Commentary
This passage is a methodological note distinguishing types of indication. It explains that the conventional indication (dalāla wadʿiyya) used for miracles is actually based on reason ('aql), not on an agreement (ittiqāq) between God and creatures. It gives the example of a red traffic light, which is based on human agreement, unlike the miracle's indication which reason itself perceives. It then addresses an alternative view that the indication might be habitual (ʿādī). For Ashʿaris, habitual indications are firm and should not be disregarded without evidence, otherwise it would lead to skepticism about all regular correlations, like hesitation about death after decapitation. Finally, it notes a distinction between intellectual/conventional indications and habitual indications, citing a famous example from fiqh (possession for three years as evidence of ownership) and referencing Sharh al-Irshād.

Key Terms
دلالة وضعية
دلالة عادية
دلالة عقلية
مواضعة
اتفاق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That conventional indication requires explicit agreement between God and humans.
Correction: The passage explains that the convention here is not an agreement but is based on pure reason; reason itself indicates the sign.

Checkpoint
According to the footnote, how does the conventional indication (dalāla wadʿiyya) of a miracle differ from a human traffic light?
A) It is based on agreement between God and His servants.
B) It is based on pure reason, not on agreement.
C) It is the same because both are conventional.
D) It is based on habitual experience.

Key Takeaway
Conventional indication in miracles is not an arbitrary convention but is grounded in reason, making it a firm proof that does not rely on prior agreement.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you witness clear signs of Allah's power in creation (like the regularity of sunrise or the miracle of life), do you ever treat them as mere 'habits' and hesitate to see the Hand of Allah behind them? What would it take for you to see them as firm indications rather than just natural coincidences? Make a commitment this week to consciously acknowledge Allah's direct involvement in a habitual event.
?
The redness of someone's face is a conventional indicator of shame that reason grasps without agreement. Are there spiritual indicators in your own life—like a lack of peace or a heavy heart—that you ignore because you think they're 'just psychological'? How would your life change if you treated those as direct, rational signs from Allah calling you to repentance? Choose one such sign and act on it today.
?
The passage calls someone who doubts the link between decapitation and death 'insane'. Are there truths about Allah's promises or warnings that you know are habitual (e.g., ingratitude leads to loss) yet you still hesitate to act on them? What is the 'beheading' equivalent in your faith that you're refusing to accept? Resolve this week to treat one of Allah's habitual laws as absolutely certain and adjust your behavior accordingly.

6 min
Refuting Circularity in Proving Allah's Speech
Sanusi refutes the claim of circularity in proving Allah's speech via transmitted evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi refutes the claim that proving Allah's speech through transmitted evidence leads to circularity in reasoning.

Core Idea
The miracle's indication is like a gesture, not speech itself — so no circularity.

Sanusi tackles a sophisticated objection: if we try to prove that Allah speaks using the Qur'an or prophetic miracles, aren't we going in circles? The objection claims that transmitted evidence depends on miracles, but miracles themselves depend on speech. Sanusi's answer is elegant: the miracle's conventional indication is like a gesture — it points to truthfulness without being speech itself. This short passage frees the student from a common logical trap.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection to circularity
Identify the opponent's claim: proving kalam via transmitted evidence leads to circularity because transmitted evidence depends on miracles, and miracles depend on speech.
The objection is that the chain loops back on itself.
2
Examine Sanusi's answer
Understand the key distinction: the miracle's conventional indication does not make it identical to speech; it merely indicates the prophet's truthfulness, like a gesture indicates meaning.
The answer breaks the loop by showing the miracle does not require speech itself.
3
Consider the distinction between conventional and habitual indication
The passage also clarifies that even though the miracle's indication is conventional (not based on agreement with God), it is still firm like habitual indication but can be broken by evidence.
This strengthens the answer against alternative objections.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
1 ثبوت الكلام بدليل الشرع لا يلزم منه الدورُ ] فإن قلت : إثباتهم الكلامَ بالدليل الشرعيّ يلزم منه الدورُ ؛ لأنَّ الدليل الشرعيَّ موقوف على دلالةِ المعجزة ، وهي مُتوقفةٌ على الكلام ، بناءً على الصحيح مِنْ أنَّ دلالتها وضعية (۱) وترجع الدلالة الوضعية إلى العقل أيضاً ؛ إذ المواضعة هنا ليست باتفاق بين تعالى وعباده ، فهي ليست كاللون الأحمر الموضوع في إشارة المرور علامة على وجوب الوقوف للمَرْكبة مثلاً فيما بين البشر ، بل مواضعة مجرَّد العقل يدلُّ عليها ، فلا تتوقف على اتفاق كما قد يتوهم أما من رأى أن دلالتها دلالة عادية فلا يعني بإمكان الانفكاك إلا التجويز العقلي ، وإلا فالدلالة العادية عند الأشاعرة متينة ، لا يُنصرفُ عنها إلا بدليل وإلا للزمنا التشكيك في كلّ أمرين بينهما ارتباط عادي ، فمن سمع بقطع رأس زيد فتوقف في موته لكون الرابط بين الأمرين عادي .. فهو مهوس ، ولكن تبقى بين دلالة العقل والوضع ودلالة العادة مفارقة ؛ إذ ليست حمرة الخجل في دلالتها على الخَجَل كقيام الملك المرار الثلاث في المثال المشهور والآتي ذكر شبيهه ، وانظر ( شرح الإرشاد ) للعلامة المقترح ( ص ٥٠٠ - ٥٠١ ) ) أي : تَتَنزَّلُ منزلة تصديقِ اللهِ تعالى لِمَنْ ظهرَتْ على يَدِهِ بالقولِ فالجواب : أن تنزلَها منزلة التصديق بالقول إنَّما معناه : أنَّها تدلُّ على ما يدلُّ عليه القولُ مِنْ صدقِ الآتي بها ، لا أنَّ معناهُ : أَنَّ فاعلها الله تكلَّمَ بتصديقِ مَنْ ظهرَتْ على يدِهِ ؛ وذلك كما تقولُ : الإشارة تدلُّ وضعاً على ما يدلُّ عليه القولُ ، وهل المشير متكلم أو أبكم ؟ محتمل ، ليس في الإشارة ما يدلُّ على شيءٍ مِنْ ذلك ، وهي في نفسها الكلام بلا فرق ، سواء كان المشير مُتكلّماً أو أبكم (1) دلالة تدل بالوضع ( الكلام عند سماعه سماعَ إدراك يدلُّ على معنى يقع في قلب السامع ، ودلالة المعجزة من حيث الوضع دالة على معنى يقع أيضاً في قلب المشاهد لها ، فهي من حيث الدلالة مشاركة لصفة الكلام ، لا أنها في نفسها كلام ، وعليه : فلا أصل للدور إذ لا تلازم بين المعجزة من حيث الدلالة وبين الكلام ، بل هما مشتركان في مفهوم الدلالة كاشتراك الكلام مع الإشارة ، ولا يعرف قيمة هنا وحرَّره وبيَّنه بوجازةٍ أحسن بيان . . إلا من طول النظر في مبسوطات كتب الكلام ) وهذه غايةُ التحقيقِ في جواب السؤالِ ، وإِنْ كانَ قدِ استهولَهُ وعظَمَهُ كَثيرٌ مِنَ الأئِمَّةِ (۲) ( قال ابن التلمساني : وهذا سؤال صعب ) وهذا الجواب القصيرُ المُحقَّقُ لم يترك (۳) عليه غباراً ، والله تعالى أعلم (٣) ، وبه التوفيق .
1 The establishment of speech by transmitted evidence does not necessitate circularity. For if you say: Their establishing speech by transmitted evidence necessitates circularity, because transmitted evidence depends on the indication of the miracle, and the miracle depends on speech, based on the correct view that its indication is conventional (wad'iyya); meaning that it takes the station of Allah's attestation for the one on whose hand it appears by speech. The answer: That its taking the station of attestation by speech only means that it indicates what speech indicates of the truthfulness of the one who brings it, not that its meaning is that its agent, Allah, spoke attesting the one on whose hand it appeared. This is like your saying: The gesture indicates conventionally what speech indicates, but whether the gesturer is a speaker or mute is possible; there is nothing in the gesture indicating anything of that, and it is in itself not speech, without difference, whether the gesturer is speaker or mute. (1) A conventional indication. And this is the ultimate verification in answering the question, even though many of the imams considered it difficult and great; and this short verified answer left no dust upon it. And Allah knows best, and He is the grantor of success. (1) Speech when heard with a hearing of perception indicates a meaning that occurs in the heart of the hearer, and the indication of the miracle from the aspect of convention indicates a meaning that also occurs in the heart of the beholder; thus from the aspect of indication it shares with the attribute of speech, not that it is in itself speech. And accordingly: there is no basis for circularity, because there is no necessary connection between the miracle from the aspect of indication and speech; rather they share in the concept of indication like the sharing of speech with gesture. And its value is not known except by one who has long contemplated the detailed books of kalam.
Source pages p. 176

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer on the topic of proving Allah's attribute of speech (kalam) through transmitted evidence (dalil shar'i). The objection claims circularity (dawr): transmitted evidence depends on the miracle (mu'jiza), but the miracle depends on speech (kalam) because its indication is conventional (wad'iyya), meaning it takes the place of Allah's verbal attestation. The answer clarifies that the miracle's conventional indication does not imply that the miracle itself is speech; rather it indicates the truthfulness of the prophet in the same way a gesture indicates meaning without the gesturer necessarily being a speaker. A footnote elaborates on the nature of conventional indication (al-dalalah al-wad'iyya), explaining that it is not based on agreement between God and His servants but on a purely rational convention, unlike human traffic signs. It distinguishes between conventional and habitual ('adiyya) indication, noting that habitual indication, though firm, can be broken by evidence. The answer concludes that there is no necessary connection between the miracle and speech itself, thus no circularity. The passage ends with praise for the concise answer and a note that Ibn al-Tilmisani considered the question difficult.

Key Terms
دور
دليل شرعي
دلالة وضعية
دلالة عادية
معجزة
كلام
إشارة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Proving kalam through transmitted evidence is circular because the miracle depends on kalam for its indication.
Correction: The miracle's conventional indication does not make it identical to speech; it indicates truthfulness like a gesture, so the circle is broken.

Checkpoint
Why does the conventional indication of the miracle not lead to circularity in proving Allah's speech?
A) Because the miracle is itself speech.
B) Because the miracle's indication is like a gesture, indicating truthfulness without requiring the miracle to be speech.
C) Because transmitted evidence does not actually depend on the miracle.
D) Because the miracle is a direct act of God without any indication.

Key Takeaway
The miracle's conventional indication is like a gesture — it indicates truthfulness without being speech itself, thus breaking the alleged circularity.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a proof for a belief you hold dear, do you ever stop to check if it might be circular? How would you feel if the strongest pillar of your conviction turned out to be a logical loop? Will you examine your own reasoning this week?
?
The miracle is only an indication of truth, not the truth itself. In your daily life, do you mistake secondary signs (like feelings, circumstances, or opinions of others) for the ultimate reality of Allah's guidance? What sign are you currently treating as if it were the real thing?
?
Sanusi's answer is concise yet profound. If such a brief response can dismantle a difficult objection, how many layers of intellectual laziness are you hiding behind? Will you commit to studying the detailed books of kalam instead of settling for shallow summaries?

Circular Dependence and Precedence in Attributes
Marginal notes define circular dependence and clarify precedence types.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of circular dependence (al-dawr al-mu'iyy) and the distinction between precedence in existence and precedence in intellection as applied to the divine attributes.

Core Idea
Circular dependence is mutual reliance; precedence can be in existence or in thought.

In studying the relationship among the divine attributes, medieval scholars encountered a potential logical problem: if attributes are co-eternal, how can we even speak of one preceding another? The two marginal notes in this lesson clarify exactly what kind of precedence is impossible and what kind is permissible. They also define the technical term 'circular dependence' (al-dawr al-mu'iyy) to prevent misunderstandings.

Guided Path
1
Read the two marginal notes
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice that the first note defines circular dependence, while the second draws a distinction between two types of precedence.
The notes come from al-Manjur and the author's own caution.
2
Understand circular dependence
Focus on the definition: each of two things depends on the other for its existence or intelligibility. Examples: substance & accident (external), fatherhood & sonship (mental).
This concept helps explain how attributes can be mutually necessary.
3
Distinguish the two precedences
Notice that precedence in existence is impossible among co-eternal attributes, but precedence in intellection (knowing one before the other) is allowed. This prevents a false inference.
The author's warning is to avoid confusing logical priority with temporal priority.
4
Connect to divine attributes
Reflect on how these clarifications apply to the attributes of Allah: they are co-eternal, so no real precedence in existence, but we can understand one attribute before another in our minds.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
(۱) عُلّق في (د) : ( قال المنجور : الدور المعيُّ : هو توقف وجود كل من الأمرين في الخارج أو الذهن على مصاحبته للآخر ؛ كما في الجوهر والعرض ، وكالأبوة والبنوة ؛ الأول للخارجي ، والثاني للذهني ) (۲) عُلقَ في ( د ) : ( إنما نبَّه على استحالة التقدُّم بالوجود ؛ تحرزاً من التقدم بالتعقل ؛ فإنه جائز معرفة أحدهما قبل الآخر )
(1) Annotated in (D): al-Manjur said: 'Circular dependence (al-dawr al-mu'iyy) is the dependence of the existence of each of two things, in the external world or the mind, on its accompaniment by the other; as in substance and accident, and as in fatherhood and sonship; the first for the external world, the second for the mind.' (2) Annotated in (D): 'He only warned about the impossibility of precedence in existence; as a precaution against precedence in intellection; for it is permissible to know one of them before the other.'
Source pages p. 179

Commentary
This passage contains two marginal notes that clarify key concepts in the discussion of divine attributes. The first note, by al-Manjur, defines 'circular dependence' (al-dawr al-mu'iyy) as the mutual dependence of two things on each other, such as substance and accident in the external world, or fatherhood and sonship in the mind. The second note explains that the author's caution about the impossibility of precedence in existence is to avoid confusion with precedence in intellection, which is permissible. Together, these notes help the reader understand the exact nature of the relationship among the attributes: they are mutually dependent but co-eternal, so there is no real temporal priority.

Key Terms
الدور المعي (al-dawr al-mu'iyy)
تقدم بالوجود (taqaddum bil-wujud)
تقدم بالتعقل (taqaddum bil-ta'aqqul)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that because attributes are co-eternal, we cannot even think of one before another.
Correction: The second note clarifies that precedence in intellection is permissible; we can know one attribute before another in order of understanding.

Checkpoint
According to the first marginal note, what is an example of circular dependence in the external world?
A) Fatherhood and sonship
B) Substance and accident
C) Power and will
D) Existence and essence

Key Takeaway
Circular dependence describes mutual necessity among co-eternal attributes, while precedence in existence is impossible but precedence in thought is allowed.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you mentally arrange Allah's attributes in a certain order (e.g., power before mercy), do you ever slip into thinking that order reflects a hierarchy in reality? How can you train yourself to hold the co-eternality of attributes firmly in your mind?
?
Reflect on a time you assumed that because you understood one concept first, it must be more fundamental or prior. How does this lesson's distinction between precedence in existence and intellection challenge that assumption?
?
If you truly internalized that circular dependence among attributes does not imply temporal priority, what would change in the way you think about Allah's perfection and unity? What specific step will you take this week to guard against misordering His attributes?

The Danger of Literalist Interpretation in Theology
Sanusi warns against relying on apparent meanings, citing anthropomorphists.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why relying solely on apparent meanings of scripture, without proper training in rational and transmitted sciences, can lead to unbelief.

Core Idea
Mere literalism in theology is a root of unbelief.

Sanusi warns that the same error that leads to disrespecting prophets—clinging to apparent meanings—also leads to anthropomorphism and other heresies. He identifies the root cause: lack of deep knowledge and divine illumination. This passage establishes that proper theological method requires integrating reason and revelation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and note the analogy
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice how Sanusi draws an analogy between the deception of those who disrespect prophets and the deception of anthropomorphists and others.
2
Identify the groups mentioned
Find the names of the groups: anthropomorphists (mujassima), those who affirm direction (jiha), those who attribute effect to created powers, etc. Ask why each is an example of literalism gone wrong.
3
Understand the root cause
Sanusi says they lacked deep immersion in rational and transmitted sciences and lost divine lights. Reflect on why both types of knowledge are necessary.
4
Reflect on the maxim
The passage ends with a strong statement: clinging to mere apparent meanings is a root of unbelief. Consider the implications for how we read scripture.
This is a serious warning from a major Sunni theologian.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ فتنةُ ظواهر النصوص بضعفاء العقولِ ] ونظيرُ الاغترار بهذهِ الظواهر : اغترارُ المجسمة ، والقائلين بالجهة ، وبتأثير القوى الحادثة ، وتعليل الأفعال والأحكام ، ونحوِ ذلك .. بظواهرَ مِنَ الكتاب والسنَّةِ تُوهِمُ ذلك ، ولم يُحيطوا بعلمها ؛ لعدم تضلُّعهم مِنَ العقليَّاتِ والنقليات ، وفقدهم الأنوار الربانية والعصمة الإلهية ، ولهذا قيلَ : إِنَّ التمسك في معرفة الله تعالى ومعرفة رُسُلِهِ عليهِمُ الصلاة والسلامُ بمُجرَّدِ ظواهر الكتاب والسنَّةِ . . أصلٌ مِنْ أصول الكفر
[The temptation of textual apparent meanings for the weak-minded] And similar to being deceived by these apparent meanings is the deception of the anthropomorphists (mujassima), and those who affirm direction (jiha), and the effect of created powers, and the rationalization of actions and rulings, and the like — by [citing] apparent meanings from the Book and the Sunnah that give the illusion of that, while they have not encompassed their knowledge due to their lack of deep immersion in intellectual and transmitted sciences, and their loss of divine lights and divine protection. Hence it is said: 'Indeed, clinging in knowledge of Allah the Exalted and knowledge of His messengers — peace and blessings be upon them — to mere apparent meanings of the Book and the Sunnah is one of the roots of unbelief.'
Source pages p. 206

Commentary
This passage is a warning against literalist interpretation. Sanusi draws an analogy: just as some are deceived by apparent meanings to disrespect prophets, others are deceived into anthropomorphism (tajsim), affirming direction (jiha), attributing effect to created powers, etc. The root cause is lack of deep knowledge in both rational and transmitted sciences, and lack of divine illumination. He then cites a maxim: relying on mere apparent meanings of scripture in theology is a root of unbelief.

Key Terms
فتنة ظواهر النصوص
المجسمة
القائلون بالجهة
تأثير القوى الحادثة
تعليل الأفعال والأحكام
العقليّات والنقليّات
الأنوار الربانية
العصمة الإلهية
أصل من أصول الكفر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any literal reading of scripture is valid and sufficient for theology.
Correction: Sanusi shows that without proper training in rational and transmitted sciences, literalism leads to heresy and is a root of unbelief.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is a root of unbelief?
A) Following the majority
B) Relying on mere apparent meanings of scripture in theology
C) Using reason
D) Studying transmitted sciences

Key Takeaway
Clinging to mere apparent meanings of scripture without proper knowledge is a root of unbelief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read Quranic verses that describe Allah in human-like terms, do you ever feel a pull to take them literally? What does Sanusi's warning reveal about the danger of that inclination? Are you willing to seek deeper knowledge beyond your initial understanding?
?
Sanusi says that lack of deep knowledge in both rational and transmitted sciences leads to error. In your own pursuit of Islamic knowledge, which of these two have you neglected? How will you restore balance?
?
The passage calls reliance on mere apparent meanings a root of unbelief. Examine your own heart: Do you ever assume that a simple reading of a text is enough, without consulting scholars or studying principles? How does this realization challenge your intellectual humility?

4 min
The Danger of Literalist Interpretation
Sanusi warns against reliance on apparent meanings, citing anthropomorphists and others.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why relying solely on apparent meanings of scripture can lead to disbelief, as demonstrated by Sanusi's warning about groups like the anthropomorphists.

Core Idea
Mere literalism in scripture is a root of disbelief.

Sanusi begins by warning against a dangerous pitfall: taking the apparent meanings of scripture at face value without deeper knowledge. He points to groups like the anthropomorphists who fell into this trap. This lesson unpacks his warning, showing why proper interpretation requires both rational and transmitted sciences.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its heading
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice the heading: 'The trial of apparent meanings for weak intellects.' This sets the tone for the warning.
2
Identify the deluded groups
Sanusi lists several groups: anthropomorphists, those affirming direction, etc. How are they deluded? They took apparent meanings that seemed to support their views.
These groups are examples of the danger of literalism.
3
Understand the cause of their delusion
The passage states they lack proficiency in rational and transmitted sciences and divine lights. This is why they fell into error.
This shows that both reason and revelation are needed.
4
Grasp the consequence
The passage concludes that clinging to mere apparent meanings is a root of disbelief. Reflect on why this is a serious warning.
This is the key takeaway.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ فتنةُ ظواهر النصوص بضعفاء العقولِ ]
ونظير الاغترار بهذهِ الظواهر : اغترارُ المجسمة ، والقائلين بالجهة ، وبتأثير القوى الحادثة ، وتعليل الأفعال والأحكام ، ونحوِ ذلك .. بظواهرَ مِنَ الكتاب والسنَّةِ تُوهِمُ ذلك ، ولم يُحيطوا بعلمها ؛ لعدم تضلُّعهم مِنَ العقليَّاتِ والنقليات ، وفقدهم الأنوار الربانية والعصمة الإلهية ، ولهذا قيلَ : إِنَّ التمسك في معرفة الله تعالى ومعرفة رُسُلِهِ عليهِمُ الصلاة والسلامُ بمُجرَّدِ ظواهر الكتاب والسنَّةِ . . أصلٌ مِنْ أصول الكفر (٢)
[The trial of apparent meanings of texts for weak intellects]
And similar to being deluded by these apparent meanings is the delusion of the anthropomorphists (al-mujassimah), and those who affirm direction (al-jihah), and the influence of created powers, and the causation of actions and rulings, and the like... by apparent meanings from the Book and the Sunnah that give the impression of that, and they did not encompass their knowledge due to their lack of proficiency in rational sciences (al-ʿaqliyyāt) and transmitted sciences (al-naqliyyāt), and their loss of divine lights and infallibility. For this reason it was said: 'Indeed, clinging in knowing Allah Most High and knowing His messengers, peace be upon them, to mere apparent meanings of the Book and the Sunnah is an origin of disbelief (aṣl min uṣūl al-kufr).'
Source pages p. 206

Commentary
This passage is a warning against the danger of relying on the literal, apparent meanings (ẓawāhir) of scripture without proper rational and transmitted knowledge. The author begins with the heading 'The trial of apparent meanings for weak intellects' and then says that the delusion of those who take apparent meanings superficially is similar to the delusion of the anthropomorphists (mujassimah) who attribute a body to Allah, and those who affirm direction (jihah) for Allah, and those who believe in the influence of created powers (like natural causes independent of Allah), and those who explain actions and rulings by mere secondary causes. All these are deluded because they cling to apparent meanings of the Quran and Sunnah that suggest such ideas, but they fail to understand the true knowledge behind them. This is because they lack proficiency in both rational sciences (ʿaqliyyāt) and transmitted sciences (naqliyyāt), and they are deprived of divine lights and infallibility (ʿiṣmah). Therefore, it is said that relying solely on the apparent meanings of scripture in knowing Allah and His messengers is a root of disbelief (aṣl min uṣūl al-kufr). This highlights the necessity of proper interpretation (taʾwīl) and deeper knowledge.

Key Terms
ظواهر (apparent meanings)
المجسمة (anthropomorphists)
الجهة (direction)
العقليات (rational sciences)
النقليات (transmitted sciences)
أصل الكفر (origin of disbelief)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That mere literal reading of scripture is sufficient for correct belief.
Correction: Sanusi shows it can lead to disbelief; proper interpretation requires both rational and transmitted sciences.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the consequence of relying solely on apparent meanings of scripture in knowing Allah and His messengers?
A) It leads to proper understanding.
B) It is a root of disbelief.
C) It is acceptable if combined with reason.
D) It is the method of the Salaf.

Key Takeaway
Relying solely on apparent meanings of scripture without rational and transmitted knowledge is a root of disbelief.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever interpreted a Quranic verse or Hadith literally without checking the deeper meaning or consulting a scholar? What was the effect on your belief about Allah?
?
Do you rely more on your own reading or on the teachings of qualified scholars when learning about Allah's attributes? How might the 'trial of apparent meanings' be a danger in your own life?
?
What specific area of your understanding of Allah or His messengers might be built on surface-level knowledge rather than deep study? Will you commit to seeking out reliable sources and scholars to correct it?
