The Danger of Superficial Learning in Sacred Knowledge
Sanusi warns against relying on books or superficial teachers for sacred knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi warns against learning kalam solely from books or from teachers who lack proper verification (taḥqīq).

Core Idea
Sacred knowledge cannot be acquired from books alone or from superficial teachers.

Sanusi concludes his introduction with a pointed warning. He has already established the importance of knowing the principles of kalam. Now he cautions the student about the method of acquiring that knowledge: mere reading or studying with unqualified teachers is not enough. This lesson highlights the necessity of rigorous verification (taḥqīq) and qualified instruction.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then read the translation to understand the warning.
Notice the author's use of terms like 'ṣaḥafiyyīn' and 'mutafaqqihīn'.
2
Understand the key terms
Identify the two types of problematic learners: al-ṣaḥafiyyīn (paper-scholars) and al-mutafaqqihīn (pretended jurists). Understand why learning from them without verification is dangerous.
The footnote in the source explains that ṣaḥafiyyūn take knowledge from papers rather than from teachers.
3
Reflect on the author's dua
Notice that Sanusi ends with a prayer for safety and divine support. Ask yourself why he feels the need to pray for protection in the context of seeking knowledge.
This shows the gravity of the method: proper transmission is a matter of spiritual safety.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قلتُ : وكذلك تلقّي هذا العلم مِنْ مُجرَّدِ الكُتُبِ والمشايخ الصَّحَفِّيِّينَ والمُتفقِّهِينَ بلا تحقق (۱) ، نسأل الله تعالى السلامةَ مِنْ فتنة المحيا والمماتِ ، والتأييد بالتوفيق والعصمة من جميع الآفات (۲) ؛ بجاه أشرف الخلقِ سيدنا ونبينا ومولانا محمَّدٍ صَلَّى اللهُ علَيهِ وسلَّمَ .
I say: And similarly, receiving this knowledge merely from books and from paper-scholars (al-ṣaḥafiyyīn) and jurists (al-mutafaqqihīn) without verification (taḥqīq). We ask Allah Most High for safety from the trial of life and death, and for support through success and protection from all calamities, by the rank of the noblest of creation, our master, our prophet, and our patron Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
Source pages p. 207

Commentary
This passage is a methodological warning from the author. He says: 'I say: Similarly, receiving this knowledge (i.e., the science of kalam or sacred knowledge) merely from books and from teachers who are ṣaḥafiyyūn (people who learn from papers without actually studying with verified scholars) and from mutafaqqihūn (those who pretend to be jurists but are not thorough) without true verification (taḥqīq) is also a dangerous practice.' The author then prays to Allah for safety from trials and for divine support. This passage emphasizes the importance of proper transmission and direct study with qualified scholars, as opposed to self-study from books or from those who are not truly grounded in the sciences.

Key Terms
الصحفيون
المتفقهون
التحقق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That reading books alone is sufficient for attaining knowledge.
Correction: Sanusi stresses the need for teachers and verification (taḥqīq) explicitly rejecting reliance on mere books or scholars who themselves lack proper training.

Checkpoint
What does Sanusi mean by 'al-shuyūkh al-ṣaḥafiyyīn'?
A) Teachers who are very old.
B) Teachers who learn from books without actual study with masters.
C) Teachers who are ascetics.
D) Teachers who focus on sufism.

Key Takeaway
Sanusi warns that learning sacred knowledge from books or from teachers who lack verification is perilous, and we should seek divine guidance in our pursuit.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you study a religious text, do you rush through it to get it done, or do you pause to verify each concept with a teacher? How many times have you assumed you understood something from reading alone, only to later discover you were mistaken? Will you commit today to finding a qualified teacher for the next topic you learn?
?
Think of the 'paper-scholars' Sanusi warns about—those who rely on books without deep verification. Are there areas of your own life where you are content with surface-level knowledge, perhaps from videos or quick articles, without truly verifying it? What step can you take this week to go deeper in your learning?

Method of Learning
beginner
5 min
Warning Against Learning from Books Alone
Sanusi warns against book-only learning and defines 'paper-scholars'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the traditional scholarly emphasis on oral transmission and the dangers of relying solely on written texts in studying kalam.

Core Idea
Kalam requires living teachers, not just books.

In this passage, Sanusi issues a stern warning that resonates throughout traditional Islamic learning: the science of kalam cannot be mastered from books alone. He distinguishes proper study under qualified shaykhs from the shallow learning of 'paper-scholars' who rely on written pages. This lesson unpacks that warning and its implications.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage and its literal translation. Notice the prayer-like tone at the end.
2
Understand the term 'ṣaḥafī'
Focus on the footnote defining ṣaḥafī. How does the author distinguish it from a true scholar?
The footnote cites Ibn Jamā‘ah and al-Shāfi‘ī.
3
Reflect on the methodology
Consider why oral transmission is so important in Islamic sciences. What dangers come from learning only from books?
Relate to the concept of 'taḥqīq' (verification).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قلتُ : وكذلك تلقّي هذا العلم مِنْ مُجرَّدِ الكُتُبِ والمشايخ الصَّحَفِّيِّينَ والمُتفقِّهِينَ بلا تحقق (۱) ، نسأل الله تعالى السلامةَ مِنْ فتنة المحيا والمماتِ ، والتأييد بالتوفيق والعصمة من جميع الآفات (۲) ؛ بجاه أشرف الخلقِ سيّدنا ونبينا ومولانا محمَّدٍ صَلَّى اللهُ علَيهِ وسلَّمَ .
(۱) الصَّحَفيُّون : جمع صَحَفي بفتحتين ، وهو الذي يأخذ العلم من الصحيفة دون المشايخ ، وهذا النسب كالنسب إلى حنيفة وبجيلة : حَنَفَي وبَجَلي وما أشبه ذلك ، وقد قال العلامة الإمام بدر الدين بن جماعة في ( تذكرة السامع » (ص ۹۷ ) : ( وليجتهد على أن يكون الشيخ ممن له على العلوم الشرعية تمام اطلاع ، وله مع من يُوثق به من مشايخ عصره كثرة بحث وطول اجتماع ممن أخذ لا عن بطون الأوراق ولم يُعرف بصحبة المشايخ الحذاق ، قال الشافعي رضي الله عنه : من تفقه من بطون الكتب ضيَّعَ الأحكام ، وكان بعضهم يقول : من أعظم البلية تمشيخ الصحفية ؛ أي : الذين تعلموا من الصُّحُف ) .
(۲) عُلِّقَ في (د) على كلمة ( العصمة ) هنا : ( أي : الحفظ ؛ لأن العصمة لا يجوز معها الوقوع ، فهي خاصَّةُ بالأنبياء ، بخلاف الحفظ ؛ فإنه يجوز معه الوقوع ) .
I said: Likewise, receiving this knowledge from mere books and from the "paper-scholars" (al-ṣaḥafiyyīn) and the "jurists" (al-mutafaqqihīn) without verification [1] – we ask Allah Almighty for safety from the trials of life and death, and for assistance with success and protection from all afflictions [2]; by the rank of the most noble of creation, our master, our Prophet, and our protector Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace.
[1] Al-ṣaḥafiyyūn: plural of ṣaḥafī, with two fathahs; he is the one who takes knowledge from the page (al-ṣaḥīfa) without [taking from] the shaykhs. This relative attribution is like the attribution to Ḥanīfa and Bajīla: Ḥanafī and Bajalī, and what resembles that. The distinguished scholar Imam Badr al-Dīn ibn Jamā‘ah said in “Tadhkirat al-Sāmi‘” (p. 97): “He should strive that the shaykh be one who has complete knowledge of the religious sciences, and who has prolonged discussion and association with reliable shaykhs of his time, one who acquired [knowledge] not from the pages of books and who is not known for keeping company with the expert shaykhs.” Al-Shāfi‘ī, may Allah be pleased with him, said: “Whoever acquires fiqh from the pages of books will miss the rulings.” One of them used to say: “One of the greatest calamities is the ‘shaykhification’ of the paper-scholars,” i.e., those who learned from the pages.
[2] It is appended in [D] to the word “al-‘iṣma” here: i.e., protection; because ‘iṣma (infallibility) does not permit falling into error, so it is specific to the prophets, unlike protection (ḥifẓ), for falling into error is possible with it.
Source pages p. 207

Commentary
This passage is a methodological warning. The author begins by cautioning against acquiring the science of kalam from mere books or from teachers who are themselves ‘paper-scholars’ (ṣaḥafiyyūn) – those who have not studied under qualified shaykhs but rely on written texts. He emphasizes the need for verification (taḥqīq) and prays for divine protection from trials. The footnote expands on the meaning of ṣaḥafī, citing Ibn Jamā‘ah and al-Shāfi‘ī to stress the importance of learning from living teachers rather than from books alone. The note on ‘iṣma clarifies the difference between prophetic infallibility and ordinary protection.

Key Terms
صحفي (ṣaḥafī)
تحقق (taḥqīq)
عصمة ('iṣma)
حفظ (ḥifẓ)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One can master kalam by reading books on their own.
Correction: Kalam requires teachers for proper understanding and verification; books alone lead to error.

Checkpoint
According to the footnote, what is a 'ṣaḥafī'?
A) One who teaches from books
B) One who takes knowledge from the page without shaykhs
C) One who writes many books
D) One who memorizes Quran

Key Takeaway
Learning kalam requires living teachers, not just books, because oral transmission ensures proper understanding and preservation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you read Islamic books or articles, do you secretly assume that your understanding is sufficient without submitting what you learn to a qualified teacher? How can you break this pattern today?
?
Think of a specific belief you hold about Allah or the Prophet. Can you trace it through a chain of living teachers back to the sources, or did you pick it up from a webpage? If the latter, what are you willing to do to verify it?
?
The prayer in this passage asks for safety from trials of life and death. If you were to die today, would your knowledge of aqeedah be based on oral transmission from reliable scholars or on your own reading? Make a concrete plan to connect with a teacher this week.

Rational Judgments
beginner
10 min
The Necessity of Rational Proof and Condemnation of Blind Imitation
The author asserts the need for rational proof in beliefs and warns against blind imitation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why rational proof is necessary for foundational beliefs and the ruling on blind imitation (taqlid) in matters of faith.

Core Idea
Blind imitation in faith is blameworthy; rational proof is essential for true belief.

This passage from Imam Sanusi's creed establishes a fundamental principle: certain beliefs cannot be known without rational demonstration. The author warns that simply hearing revelation or reciting verses does not constitute proof; one must reflect and grasp the rational aspect. He then condemns those who imitate blindly, even if their belief happens to be correct, and explains the juristic ruling on such imitation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once and its English translation to get a sense of the author's argument.
Notice the direct address 'my brother' — this is a personal appeal.
2
Identify the core claim
Identify the author's claim that some beliefs require rational proof and that mere hearing of revelation is insufficient.
Look for the phrase 'لا غنية فيه عن الدليل العقلي'.
3
Analyze the Quranic evidence
Note the two Quranic verses condemning those who do not reflect or reason.
The verses support the need for reflection.
4
Examine the example of tawhid
Read the example about the verse 'لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَا...' and understand why memorization without comprehension constitutes taqlid.
The example illustrates the principle.
5
Understand the ruling on muqallid
Understand the juristic ruling: salvation conditional on coinciding with truth and certainty, but still a grave sin.
This shows the seriousness of taqlid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واعلم يا أخي : أنَّ من العقائد ما لا غنية فيه عن الدليل العقلي ؛ فلا بدَّ من إدراك وجه الوجوب أو الاستحالة أو الجواز فيه من قبل المخاطب ، حتى لو سمع كلام الله وصحيح سنته .. لا يغنيه ذلك بمجرد السماع ، بل لا بدَّ له من التفكر في وجه الدليل ، وإلا كان ممَّن ذمَّه المولى تعالى بمثل قوله جل شأنه : أَفَلَا يَتَدَبَّرُونَ الْقُرْءَانَ أَمْ عَلَى قُلُوبِ أَقْفَالُهَا ﴾ [محمد : ٢٤] ، وقوله تعالى : ﴿ إِنَّ شَرَّ الدَّوَا عِندَ اللَّهِ الصُّمُّ البُكُمُ الَّذِينَ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ ﴾ [الأنفال : ۲۲] فمَنْ ظَنَّ أنه أحاط بدليل الوحدانية بحفظه أو تلاوته لقوله عَزَّ من قائل : ﴿ لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَا الهَهُ إِلَّا اللهُ لَفَسَدَنَا ﴾ [الأنبياء : ٢٢] دون أن يدرك وجهَ استحالة التعدد ، ومعنى الفساد المقصود من الآية . . فهو مقلدٌ ، مَثَلُهُ كمثل أي مقلد لاعتقاد آخر ، وإنما قرَّر الفقهاء نجاته لكون تقليده وقع اتفاقاً للحقِّ ؛ بشرط جزمه وعدم تركه لمعتقده إن تركه المُقلَّد ، وقالوا بعظيم معصيته لكونه أشبه الذين ألفوا آباءهم ضالين ، فهم على آثارهم يهرعون
And know, my brother, that among beliefs there are those that cannot do without rational proof; so the person addressed must perceive the aspect of obligation, impossibility, or possibility in it, even if he hears the speech of God and His authentic Sunnah... that does not suffice him by mere hearing, rather he must reflect on the aspect of the proof, otherwise he would be among those whom the Exalted Majesty has condemned with such as His saying: 'Then do they not reflect upon the Quran, or are there locks upon their hearts?' [Muhammad 24], and His saying: 'Indeed, the worst of creatures in the sight of God are the deaf and dumb who do not reason' [Al-Anfal 22]. So whoever thinks that he has encompassed the proof of oneness by memorizing or reciting His words 'If there were other gods in them besides God, they would have corrupted' [Al-Anbiya 22] without perceiving the aspect of the impossibility of multiplicity and the meaning of the corruption intended by the verse... he is a blind imitator (muqallid), his example is like any other blind imitator of another belief. And the jurists have ruled his salvation because his imitation coincidentally corresponded to the truth, on condition of his certainty and his not abandoning his belief if his imitated one abandons it, and they have said that his sin is great because he resembles those who found their fathers astray, so they follow in their footsteps, hastening.
Source pages p. 8

Commentary
This passage is an argument for the necessity of rational proof in certain beliefs. The author begins by stating that some beliefs cannot be known without rational proof; the person must understand the aspect of wujub (obligation), istihala (impossibility), or jawaz (possibility) in that belief. Mere hearing of revelation is insufficient—one must reflect (tafakkur) on the proof. He cites two Quranic verses condemning those who do not reflect or use reason. He then gives an example: someone who thinks that memorizing the verse 'If there were other gods...' suffices for the proof of tawhid, without grasping the rational impossibility of multiple gods, is a muqallid (blind imitator). Such a person is compared to any imitator of false beliefs. The jurists (fuqaha) rule that such a person is saved only if his imitation happens to coincide with the truth and he is certain and consistent, but he commits a grave sin (ma'siya) because he resembles those who blindly follow their ancestors (as in Quranic condemnation). The passage thus establishes that taqlid in foundational beliefs is blameworthy and that rational proof is essential.

Key Terms
دليل عقلي
مقلد
تقليد
وجوب
استحالة
جواز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Reciting or memorizing a Quranic verse without understanding the rational proof is sufficient for correct belief.
Correction: The author states that one must perceive the aspect of wujub/istihala/jawaz and that mere hearing does not suffice; otherwise one is a muqallid.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the ruling on someone who believes in tawhid simply by memorizing the verse 'لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَا...' without understanding the rational proof?
A) He is a true believer (mu'min).
B) He is a muqallid with a grave sin.
C) He is a disbeliever (kafir).
D) He is excused because he followed the text.

Key Takeaway
Rational proof is indispensable for foundational beliefs; blind imitation, even if correct, involves grave sin and is condemned.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever held a belief about Allah simply because you were taught it, without personally understanding the rational proof? Will you commit today to seeking that understanding, even if it takes effort?
?
When you recite or hear a verse like 'If there were other gods... they would have corrupted,' do you stop to grasp why multiplicity is impossible, or do you let the words pass as mere recitation? What will you change about your recitation habits starting now?
?
The author warns that a blind imitator resembles those who 'found their fathers astray and followed them.' Is there any belief you hold simply because it is familiar or traditional, rather than because you have examined it with your God-given reason? Will you dare to question it honestly before Allah?

Rational Judgments
beginner
4 min
Definition of Wajib and Mustahil in Rational Judgments
Sanusi defines wajib and mustahil with their subdivisions and states the obligation to know them.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation to know the three rational judgments (wajib, mustahil, ja'iz) regarding Allah and the messengers, and the definition of wajib and mustahil with their subdivisions into self-evident and theoretical.

Core Idea
The rational judgments are defined by what the mind can or cannot conceive.

This lesson begins Sanusi's core subject: what every Muslim must know about Allah and the prophets. He first states the obligation to know the three rational judgments—necessary, impossible, and permissible—then defines the first two. Understanding these definitions is essential because they form the foundation for all subsequent theological arguments.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the obligation
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice that Sanusi first states that every accountable person must know three things about Allah and the prophets: the necessary, the impossible, and the permissible.
2
Understand the definition of wajib
Sanusi defines wajib as that whose non-existence cannot be conceived. Look at his two subtypes: dharuri (self-evident, no reflection needed) and nadhari (theoretical, requires reflection). The examples help clarify.
3
Understand the definition of mustahil
Mustahil is defined as that whose existence cannot be conceived. It also divides into dharuri and nadhari. The passage gives the first example and begins the second.
Note the parallel structure with wajib.
4
Reflect on the importance of these definitions
These definitions are the building blocks of kalam. They provide a clear rational framework for discussing what can and cannot be true about Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم وصلى الله على مولانا محمد وآله الحمدُ للهِ ربِّ العالمينَ ، والصلاة والسلام على سيدنا ومولانا محمد خاتم النبيِّينَ وإمامِ المرسلين . اعلم : ما يجب معرفته على كل مكلف : أنه يجب على كل مكلف أن يعرف ما يجب في حق مولانا جل وعزَّ ، وما يستحيل ، وما يجوزُ ، وكذا يجب عليه أن يعرف مثل ذلك في حق الرسل عليهم الصلاة والسلام . [ حد الواجب والجائز والمستحيل ] وحقيقة الواجب : ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ ؛ إما بلا تأمل ويُسمَّى الضروري ؛ ككون الواحد نصف الاثنين مثلاً ، وإمَّا بعد التأمُّلِ ويُسمَّى النظري ؛ ككون الواحد نصف سدس الاثني عشر مثلاً . والمستحيلُ : ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل ثبوتُه ؛ إما بلا تأمل أيضاً ؛ ككونِ الواحدِ نصفِ الأربعةِ ، وإمَّا بعد التأمل
In the name of God, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. And may God bless our master Muhammad and his family. Praise be to God, Lord of the worlds, and prayers and peace be upon our master and lord Muhammad, the seal of the prophets and the imam of the messengers. Know: what must be known by every legally responsible person is that it is obligatory for every legally responsible person to know what is necessary (wajib) in respect of our Lord, Glorified and Exalted, and what is impossible (mustahil), and what is permissible (ja'iz), and likewise it is obligatory for him to know the like of that in respect of the messengers, peace be upon them. [Section on the necessary, the permissible, and the impossible] The reality of the necessary (wajib): that whose non-existence is not conceivable in the mind; either without reflection, and it is called the self-evident (dharuri), like the fact that one is half of two, for example; or after reflection, and it is called the theoretical (nadhari), like the fact that one is half of a sixth of twelve, for example. And the impossible (mustahil): that whose existence is not conceivable in the mind; either without reflection as well, like the fact that one is half of four; or after reflection...
Source pages p. 92

Commentary
This passage begins with the author's customary praise and prayer, then immediately states the foundational obligation: every legally responsible person (mukallaf) must know what is wajib, mustahil, and ja'iz regarding God and regarding the prophets. This establishes the subject matter of the entire treatise. The author then provides a section heading for the definitions of the three rational judgments. He defines al-wajib (the necessary) as that whose non-existence cannot be conceived by the mind. He divides it into two types: dharuri (self-evident), which requires no reflection, such as the truth that one is half of two; and nadhari (theoretical), which requires reflection, such as the truth that one is half of a sixth of twelve. Note that the example for nadhari requires a simple calculation, but is not immediately obvious. He then begins the definition of al-mustahil (the impossible) as that whose existence cannot be conceived by the mind. He similarly divides it into dharuri and nadhari, but the text cuts off at 'or after reflection' on this page. The definition is structurally parallel to that of wajib: just as wajib is inconceivable non-existence, mustahil is inconceivable existence.

Key Terms
واجب
المستحيل
الجائز
الضروري
النظري
مكلف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think self-evident (dharuri) and theoretical (nadhari) are equally obvious.
Correction: Sanusi distinguishes them: dharuri requires no reflection, nadhari requires reflection.

Checkpoint
How does Sanusi define 'al-wajib' (the necessary) in this passage?
A) That whose existence is impossible in the mind
B) That whose non-existence is not conceivable in the mind
C) That whose existence is not conceivable in the mind
D) That which requires no reflection to understand

Key Takeaway
The necessary (wajib) is what the mind cannot conceive as non-existent, while the impossible (mustahil) is what the mind cannot conceive as existent, and both have self-evident and theoretical forms.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly believe that understanding what is necessary and impossible for God is an obligation upon you personally, or have you relegated theological reflection to scholars while you go through life without ever contemplating these truths? If it is obligatory, what would a day in your life look like if you took this duty seriously?
?
The author says that some truths are self-evident, like that one is half of two, requiring no reflection. Consider: Is your conviction that God exists and that He is one self-evident to you, or have you never paused to examine whether it is? If it is not self-evident, are you willing to undertake the reflection (nadhari) to make it so?
?
The definition of impossible (mustahil) is that whose existence cannot be conceived. What are some things you have allowed yourself to imagine about God that are actually impossible according to the rational judgments taught here? Have you ever thought of God in ways that contradict His necessary attributes, and what will you do to rectify those thoughts?

Rational Judgments
beginner
5 min
The Three Rational Judgments: Obligatory, Impossible, and Possible
Sanusi enumerates the three rational judgments and defines each with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three rational judgments (obligatory, impossible, possible) and their subdivision into necessary and theoretical knowledge.

Core Idea
The three rational judgments are the grammar of all theological reasoning.

Sanusi begins by stating the foundational knowledge every Muslim must acquire: understanding what is obligatory, impossible, and possible for Allah and the prophets. He then provides clear definitions and examples, showing that some truths are self-evident while others require reflection.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Start by reading the Arabic text and then the English translation. Notice the structure: an introductory statement about obligatory knowledge, followed by definitions of each term.
2
Identify the three main judgments
Find where Sanusi defines 'obligatory' (wājib), 'impossible' (mustaḥīl), and 'possible' (jā'iz). Write down each definition.
The definitions are given in the second half of the passage.
3
Note the two subcategories
Each judgment is divided into necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). Understand that necessary knowledge is immediate, while theoretical knowledge requires reflection.
The examples for each judgment differ, showing the contrast.
4
Reflect on the examples
Consider the arithmetic examples: one is half of two (obligatory, necessary), one is half of four (impossible, necessary), etc. These show how the definitions apply even to simple truths.
5
Connect to theology
Remember that these judgments are applied to Allah and the prophets. This passage lays the groundwork for discussing divine attributes and prophetic miracles.
The introduction mentions this application.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم وصلى الله على مولانا محمد وآله الحمدُ للهِ ربِّ العالمينَ ، والصلاة والسلام على سيدنا ومولانا محمد خاتم النبيِّينَ وإمامِ المرسلين . اعلم : ما يجب معرفته على كلِّ مكلَّف ] : أَنَّهُ يجب على كلِّ مكلَّف أن يعرف ما يجب في حق مولانا جل وعزَّ ، وما يستحيل ، وما يجوزُ ، وكذا يجب عليه أن يعرف مثل ذلك في حقٌّ الرُّسُلِ عليهم الصلاة والسلام . [ حد الواجب والجائز والمستحيل ] وحقيقة الواجب : ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ ؛ إما بلا تأمل ويُسمَّى الضروري ؛ ككون الواحد نصف الاثنين مثلاً ، وإمَّا بعد التأمُّلِ ويُسمَّى النظري ؛ ككون الواحد نصف سدس الاثني عشر مثلاً . والمستحيلُ : ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل ثبوتُه ؛ إما بلا تأمل أيضاً ؛ ككونِ الواحدِ نصف الأربعةِ ، وإمَّا بعد التأمل ؛ ككون الواحدِ سدس الاثني عشر . والجائزُ : ما يصح في العقل ثبوته وعدمه ؛ إما بلا تأمُّل ؛ ككون الجسمِ أبيضَ مثلاً ، وإمَّا بعد التأمل ؛ كتمني الإنسان الموت مثلاً
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. And may Allah bless our master Muhammad and his family. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds, and blessings and peace be upon our master and lord Muhammad, the Seal of the Prophets and the Imam of the Messengers. Know: What is obligatory for every morally responsible person to know: that it is obligatory for every morally responsible person to know what is necessarily incumbent upon our Lord, Glorious and Exalted, what is impossible for Him, and what is possible for Him. Likewise, it is obligatory for him to know the like of that regarding the Messengers (peace be upon them). [Section: The Definition of the Obligatory, the Possible, and the Impossible] The reality of the obligatory: that which cannot be conceived by the intellect as non-existent; either without reflection, and it is called the necessary (ḍarūrī), such as the fact that one is half of two, for example; or after reflection, and it is called the theoretical (naẓarī), such as the fact that one is half of a sixth of twelve, for example. The impossible: that which cannot be conceived by the intellect as existent; either without reflection as well, such as the fact that one is half of four; or after reflection, such as the fact that one is a sixth of twelve. The possible: that whose existence and non-existence are both valid for the intellect; either without reflection, such as the fact that a body is white, for example; or after reflection, such as a person desiring death, for example.
Source pages p. 93

Commentary
This passage is a definition-and-classification passage. It begins by stating the obligatory knowledge for every mukallaf (morally responsible person): to know the three rational judgments (wājib, mustaḥīl, jā'iz) regarding Allah and the prophets. It then defines each judgment. For the obligatory (wājib), it is that whose non-existence cannot be conceived by sound intellect. This knowledge can be either immediate (ḍarūrī) without reflection, like knowing that one is half of two, or acquired through reflection (naẓarī), like knowing that one is half of a sixth of twelve. Similarly, the impossible (mustaḥīl) is that whose existence cannot be conceived, either immediately (e.g., one is half of four) or after reflection (e.g., one is a sixth of twelve). The possible (jā'iz) is that whose existence and non-existence are both intellectually conceivable, either without reflection (e.g., a body being white) or after reflection (e.g., a person wishing for death). The examples are simple arithmetic or everyday observations, illustrating that these categories are foundational for reasoning about God and prophets.

Key Terms
واجب
مستحيل
جائز
ضروري
نظري
مكلف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: All rational judgments are theoretical and require reflection.
Correction: The passage divides each judgment into necessary (known without reflection) and theoretical (known after reflection). For example, knowing that one is half of two is necessary, not theoretical.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the definition of the impossible (al-mustaḥīl)?
A) That whose non-existence cannot be conceived by the intellect.
B) That whose existence cannot be conceived by the intellect.
C) That which can be conceived as both existent and non-existent.
D) That which requires reflection to be known.

Key Takeaway
The three rational judgments — obligatory, impossible, and possible — are the basic categories for reasoning about Allah and the prophets, and each can be known either immediately or through reflection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you live as if Allah's existence is necessary (wājib) and inconceivable to be absent, or do your actions sometimes reflect a doubt that He is watching? What would change if you truly embodied that certainty today?
?
The possible (jā'iz) includes everything that could be, yet how often do you limit what Allah can do based on your own assumptions? Can you identify one area where you have implicitly declared something impossible for Him, and will you repent and expand your trust?
?
When you reflect on the difference between necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī) knowledge, which of your beliefs about Allah are merely inherited and not personally reasoned? Commit to studying one attribute until it becomes as certain as 'one is half of two'.

Definition of the Contingent (al-Ja'iz)
Sanusi defines the contingent and gives examples with and without reflection.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the contingent (al-ja'iz) in kalam, its two categories based on reflection, and the meaning of intellectual validity.

Core Idea
The contingent is what the intellect finds equally acceptable for existence or non-existence.

Before we can discuss what must be true about Allah, we need a clear vocabulary for classifying things according to reason. One of the most basic categories is the contingent — that which can either exist or not exist in the intellect. Sanusi defines it precisely and shows how it can be known with or without reflection.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice how Sanusi first defines 'reflection' with an example before defining the contingent.
The example of reflection (one being a sixth of twelve) is a mathematical relationship that requires thought.
2
Understand the definition of al-ja'iz
Focus on the core definition: what is valid in the intellect both its existence and non-existence. This is the key to understanding the contingent.
3
Distinguish between the two categories
Identify the two categories: without reflection (bila ta'mmul) and after reflection (ba'da al-ta'mmul). Examine the examples given for each.
The example of a body being white does not require deep thought, while wishing for death requires reflection.
4
Connect to broader kalam
Reflect on why this category is important: it sets the foundation for discussions about what is necessary and impossible for Allah and creation.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
التأمل ؛ ككون الواحدِ سدس الاثني عشر . والجائزُ : ما يصح في العقل ثبوته وعدمه ؛ إما بلا تأمُّل ؛ ككون الجسمِ أبيضَ مثلاً ، وإمَّا بعد التأمل ؛ كتمني الإنسان الموت مثلاً
Reflection: like one being a sixth of twelve. And the contingent is what is valid in the intellect its existence and non-existence; either without reflection, like a body being white for example, or after reflection, like a person wishing for death for example.
Source pages p. 94

Commentary
This passage is a definition and classification. It defines al-ja'iz (the contingent) as that whose existence and non-existence are both equally acceptable to the intellect. It then divides it into two categories: without reflection (bila ta'mmul), illustrated by the example of a body being white, and after reflection (ba'da al-ta'mmul), illustrated by a person wishing for death. The examples clarify the distinction between immediate and reasoned judgments.

Key Terms
الجائز
التأمل
بلا تأمل
بعد التأمل
الوجود
العدم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the contingent is what is possible in external reality, not about intellectual judgment.
Correction: The definition emphasizes intellectual validity (yashh fi al-'aql), meaning it is a rational category, not an empirical one.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what defines the contingent (al-ja'iz) in the intellect?
A) What is necessary in existence
B) What is impossible in existence
C) What is valid in the intellect both its existence and non-existence
D) What requires reflection to be known

Key Takeaway
The contingent is what the intellect judges as equally possible for existence and non-existence, known either immediately or after reflection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Sanusi gives 'a person wishing for death' as an example of a contingent matter that requires reflection. Have you ever wished for something that seemed appealing at first but, upon deeper reflection, you realized was not in your best interest? Why do you think the intellect needs to deliberate before accepting such a wish?
?
The contingent includes matters that are equally possible in the intellect. Think of one belief you hold confidently right now. Are you certain it is necessary, or could it actually be contingent—equally possible to be otherwise? How would acknowledging that contingency change your attachment to it?
?
When you consider your own future, how much of what you hope for or fear do you treat as if it were fixed? Does recognizing that many outcomes are intellectually contingent (both possible and impossible being absent) move you to rely more on the One who alone determines existence?

The Obligation to Seek Proof: Ruling on the Blind Follower
Sanusi states the ruling on one whose belief is correct but based on taqlid.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why a person whose belief is correct but based on mere blind following (taqlīd) must still seek rational proof to attain genuine knowledge, according to the stronger opinion.

Core Idea
Correct belief without proof is not enough; Allah requires reasoned certainty.

Sanusi addresses a critical question: Is it enough to hold correct beliefs if you simply inherited them from your family or community? The passage discusses the situation of a person whose beliefs happen to be true but were acquired through taqlīd, not through rational proof or necessity. The answer is significant for understanding what true faith requires.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Notice the heading and how the author first mentions disbelievers before giving the ruling.
2
Identify the key terms
Note the terms: taqlīd, burhān, maʿrifa. These are central to understanding the obligation.
3
Understand the ruling
The passage says there are multiple opinions, and the strongest is that the person must search for proof if they have the ability. Why might that be?
4
Reflect on the implication
Consider why inherited belief might be insufficient in the sight of Allah, especially for those capable of rational inquiry.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اليهود والنصارى وسائرِ الكَفَرَةِ بالكفريَّاتِ التي جزموا بها . . لم يكفِ ولم يُعذَرْ بهِ إجماعاً .
[ حكم المقلد لصحيح الاعتقادِ ]
ولو حصلَ منهُم جزمٌ مطابق لِمَا في نفس الأمر ، إلا أنه لم يكن عن ضرورة ولا برهان ، بل إنَّما كان عن تقليد . . ففي ذلك طرق وأقوال ، أصحها : أَنَّهُ يجب عليهِ البحثُ عن البرهان حتى تحصلَ لهُ المعرفة عنه ، مهما كانَتْ فيهِ قابليةٌ لفهم ذلكَ
The Jews, Christians, and all other disbelievers, by the acts of disbelief which they were certain of... it was not sufficient and they are not excused by it by consensus.
[The ruling on the blind follower who has correct belief]
If there occurred from them a certainty that corresponds to what is in reality, but it was not from necessity or proof, rather it was from blind following (taqlīd)... then concerning that there are ways and opinions, the most correct of which is that it is obligatory upon him to search for proof until knowledge of it becomes obtained by him, whenever he has the ability to understand that.
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage first notes that all disbelievers—Jews, Christians, and others—are not excused by their disbelief, a point the author uses to set the context. Then, under the heading 'The ruling on the blind follower (muqallid) who has correct belief', Sanusi addresses a scenario: a person holds a belief that happens to be true and corresponds to reality, but he arrived at it without necessity (ḍarūra) or proof (burhān), purely through taqlīd (uncritical acceptance of authority). The author states that there are multiple scholarly opinions on this situation. The strongest opinion (aṣaḥḥuhā) is that such a person is obligated to search for rational proof until he obtains genuine knowledge (maʿrifa) of that belief, provided he has the intellectual capacity (qābiliyya) to understand the proof. This ruling shows that sound belief based on taqlīd is insufficient for salvific knowledge; active inquiry is required.

Key Terms
المقلد
البرهان
المعرفة
تقليد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That simply holding correct beliefs without evidence is enough for salvation.
Correction: Sanusi shows that even correct belief from taqlid is insufficient; one must seek proof to attain genuine knowledge.

Checkpoint
According to the most correct opinion in this passage, what is the obligation of a person whose belief is correct but based on taqlīd?
A) He is excused and does not need to do anything.
B) He must search for proof if he has the ability to understand it.
C) He must repeat the Shahada.
D) He must follow a scholar.

Key Takeaway
A person with correct belief from blind following must still seek rational proof to be considered truly knowledgeable and excused before Allah.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever been content to believe something about Allah simply because you were raised that way, without ever thinking through the rational evidence? What might that mean for your accountability before Him?
?
If Sanusi says that even correct belief without proof is not enough, what does that require of you today? Where will you start to seek that proof?
?
The passage mentions the ability to understand as a condition. Can you honestly say you have used your God-given intellect to seek certainty in your beliefs? What is stopping you?

The Nature of Concomitance: Customary or Intellectual?
Sanusi presents the debate on whether concomitance is customary or intellectual and states his preference.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the difference between customary and intellectual concomitance in Ash'ari theology and why al-Ash'ari's view is considered more apparent.

Core Idea
Concomitance in creation is customary, not intellectually necessary, preserving divine power.

In Ash'ari theology, the relationship between cause and effect is a key topic. This passage presents a debate between two great scholars: al-Ash'ari and Imam al-Haramayn. Understanding their positions is essential for grasping how Ash'aris explain miracles and the contingency of natural regularities.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the two examples given: satiety with eating and accident with body.
2
Identify the two positions
The passage presents two positions: al-Ash'ari says concomitance is customary (lāzim ʿādatan), Imam al-Haramayn says it is intellectual (lāzim ʿaqlan). Note how each defines the possibility of non-occurrence.
Customary means non-occurrence is possible by divine power.
3
Understand the implications
Ask yourself: What difference does it make if concomitance is customary versus intellectual? Think about miracles: if intellectual, no miracles possible; if customary, miracles are possible as exceptions.
This connects to the broader Ash'ari emphasis on God's power.
4
Note Sanusi's preference
Sanusi says 'the most apparent' (al-aẓhar) is al-Ash'ari's view. Reflect on why this view aligns with Ash'ari theology: it avoids necessitarianism and upholds divine omnipotence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
كالشبع مع الأكل، أو لازم عقلاً؛ كالعَرَضِ معَ الجِرْم مثلاً؟ فقال الشيخ الأشعري رضي الله عنه : هو لازمٌ عادةً، فيصح التخلف. وقال إمام الحرمين : هو لازم عقلاً، فلا يصح التخلُّفُ. والأظهرُ : ما قاله الأشعري، والله تعالى أعلم.
[Something] like satiety with eating, or intellectually necessary, like an accident with a body for example? So Shaykh al-Ash'ari, may Allah be pleased with him, said: It is necessary by custom, so non-occurrence is possible. And Imam al-Haramayn said: It is intellectually necessary, so non-occurrence is not possible. And the most apparent is what al-Ash'ari said, and Allah Almighty knows best.
Source pages p. 131

Commentary
This passage presents a debate about the nature of concomitance (luzūm) between two things. The author poses the example of whether the concomitant is like satiety following eating (customary) or like an accident necessarily inhering in a body (intellectual). Al-Ash'ari holds that such concomitance is customary, meaning that it is possible for the concomitant to be absent (the cause can occur without the effect by divine power). Imam al-Haramayn (Juwaynī) holds it is intellectual, meaning it is impossible for the effect to be absent when the cause is present. The author (Sanusi) declares al-Ash'ari's view to be the most apparent (al-aẓhar). This distinction has implications for kalam arguments, especially regarding causality and miracles.

Key Terms
لازم
عادةً
عقلاً
تخلُّف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Customary concomitance means the connection is weak or unreliable.
Correction: In Ash'ari theology, customary concomitance is consistent and reliable in normal circumstances, but it is not intellectually necessary—meaning God can intervene to break the pattern.

Checkpoint
According to al-Ash'ari, the connection between a cause and its effect is:
A) Intellectually necessary, so the effect cannot be absent.
B) Customary, so the effect can be absent by divine power.
C) Neither necessary nor customary.
D) Only intellectually necessary for some causes.

Key Takeaway
In Ash'ari thought, the cause-effect relationship is habitual, not necessary, preserving God's absolute power.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you observe the constant patterns in your life—like food causing satiety—do you truly attribute that regularity to Allah's custom, or do you unconsciously treat it as a self-sufficient law? What would change in your reliance if you genuinely believed that Allah could break that pattern at any moment?
?
If you claim to believe that all causes are merely habits sustained by Allah, then why do you still fear created things as if they have independent power? Identify one fear of a created thing (e.g., illness, poverty, rejection) that you will actively combat this week by reminding yourself that its effect is only by habit, not necessity.
?
The debate between customary and intellectual concomitance is not just academic; your conviction about it shapes whether you expect miracles in your life. Do you live as if Allah can intervene suddenly and break the pattern, or have you become so comfortable with the routine that you have stopped expecting the extraordinary?

The Definition of Wājib and Its Two Types
Sanusi defines wājib and divides it into two types.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of wājib (necessary) in rational judgment and how it is divided into immediate (ḍarūrī) and speculative (naẓarī) knowledge.

Core Idea
Necessary knowledge is either immediate or reflective.

Sanusi begins by establishing what 'necessary' means in rational theology. Before discussing God's attributes, we need a precise definition of necessity as a category of rational judgment. This passage lays out the basic division between self-evident truths and truths that require reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of wājib
Read the Arabic definition carefully: 'that whose non-existence is inconceivable in the mind'. Notice the term 'wājib' here is a rational category, not a legal one.
This sets the stage for all future discussions.
2
Identify the two divisions
The passage divides wājib into immediate (ḍarūrī) and speculative (naẓarī). Pay attention to the examples: one being half of two (immediate) versus one being half of one-sixth of twelve (speculative).
3
Understand the difference
Ask yourself: what makes something immediate vs. speculative? The key is whether reflection is needed. Immediate knowledge is self-evident; speculative knowledge requires a process of reasoning.
This distinction is foundational for kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وحقيقة الواجب : ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ؛ إما بلا تأمل ويُسمَّى الضروري؛ ككون الواحد نصف الاثنين مثلاً، وإمَّا بعد التأمُّلِ ويُسمَّى النظري؛ ككون الواحدِ نصفَ سُدُسِ الاثنيْ عَشَرَ مثلاً. ولمَّا قدَّمَ الحكم بوجوب معرفةِ المكلَّف شرعاً لِمَا يجب عقلاً وما يستحيلُ عقلاً وما يجوز عقلاً في حق الله تعالى، وفي حقِّ رسلِهِ عليهم الصلاة والسلام (1)، وكانَ الحكم على شيء أو بشيء موقوفاً على تصوُّرِ معناهما (2) .. تَعَيَّنَ على كلِّ مُكلَّفٍ أن يعرف معنى الحكم العقلي وأقسامه ومعانيها؛ ليعرف بذلك معنى وجوبِ ما يجبُ مِنَ الكمالاتِ لمولانا تبارك وتعالى، ومعنى استحالة ما يُنزَّهُ عنهُ تعالى، ومعنى جواز ما يجوزُ في حَقَّهِ تعالى، ويعرف بذلك ما تتعلق بهِ. (1) قال العلامة البيلي في « تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى » ( ق ٦ ) : ( المراد بالرسل هنا : مطلق الأنبياء، فهو من ذكر الخاص وإرادة العام، أو أنه جارٍ على القول بمرادفة الرسول للنبي وإن كان ذلك غير مشهور، أو أنه خص الرسل لأشرفيَّتهم، أو لأن بعض الصفات كالتبليغ خاص بهم ). (2) يحتمل رجوع ضمير التثنية للحكم والمحكوم عليه، ويحتمل رجوعه للمحكوم عليه والمحكوم به، ومعرفة معنى الحكم تستلزم معرفة معنى المحكوم به والمحكوم عليه. انتهى من هامش ( د ).
And the reality of the necessary (wājib) is that whose non-existence is inconceivable in the mind; either without reflection, and it is called immediate (ḍarūrī), like one being half of two, for example; or after reflection, and it is called speculative (naẓarī), like one being half of one-sixth of twelve, for example. And since He [the author] has previously stated the ruling that it is legally obligatory for the responsible person to know what is intellectually necessary, what is intellectually impossible, and what is intellectually possible concerning Allah Almighty and concerning His messengers, peace be upon them (1), and since a ruling (ḥukm) about something or concerning something depends on conceiving the meaning of both (2) ... it is incumbent upon every responsible person to know the meaning of the rational judgment (al-ḥukm al-ʿaqlī), its divisions, and their meanings, so that by that he may know the meaning of the necessity of what is necessary of perfections for our Lord, Blessed and Exalted, and the meaning of the impossibility of what He is exalted above, and the meaning of the possibility of what is possible concerning Him, and by that he may know what relates to them. (1) The scholar al-Baylī said in his 'Taqyīdāt ʿalā Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā' (folio 6): 'What is meant by messengers here is absolutely prophets; so it is mentioning the specific while intending the general, or it is based on the view that "messenger" is synonymous with "prophet" even if that is not well-known, or he specified messengers due to their superiority, or because some attributes like conveying revelation are specific to them.' (2) It is possible that the dual pronoun refers to the ruling (ḥukm) and the thing ruled upon (al-maḥkūm ʿalayh), and it is possible that it refers to the thing ruled upon (al-maḥkūm ʿalayh) and the thing by which the ruling is made (al-maḥkūm bih). And knowing the meaning of the ruling necessitates knowing the meaning of the thing ruled upon and the thing by which the ruling is made. End from margin (d).
Source pages p. 132

Commentary
This passage begins by defining the term wājib (necessary) in the context of rational judgments: 'that whose non-existence is inconceivable in the intellect.' It then divides wājib into ḍarūrī (immediate/self-evident) and naẓarī (speculative/discursive). The author then explains why understanding the three rational judgments is necessary: because the obligation to know God's necessary, impossible, and possible attributes depends on understanding the meaning of 'rational judgment' and its divisions. The footnotes clarify the scope of 'messengers' (including prophets) and the dual pronoun in the phrase 'meaning of both'.

Key Terms
واجب
ضروري
نظري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The term 'wājib' here means legally obligatory, like prayer or fasting.
Correction: In this context, wājib means rationally necessary, i.e., something whose non-existence is inconceivable in the mind. This is a technical term in kalam, not a legal ruling.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is the defining characteristic of wājib?
A) It is known after reflection.
B) It is legally obligatory.
C) Its non-existence is inconceivable in the mind.
D) It is always immediate.

Key Takeaway
Wājib in kalam is rational necessity, divided into immediate (self-evident) and speculative (requiring reflection).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think of your belief in God, do you treat it as immediate (ḍarūrī) or speculative (naẓarī)? Have you ever reflected deeply on the rational proof for His existence, or do you rely solely on upbringing? If the latter, are you satisfied that your faith rests on unexamined assumptions?
?
Sanūsī says the non-existence of a necessary truth is inconceivable. Is there any belief about God that you hold so firmly that you cannot conceive its opposite? If that belief has not been tested by reflection, are you willing to risk that it might be false? What step will you take today to examine it?

Why Understanding Rational Judgments is Necessary
Sanusi argues that understanding rational judgments is obligatory for knowing God.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why every responsible person must know the meaning of rational judgment and its three divisions (necessary, impossible, possible) in order to properly know God's attributes.

Core Idea
Knowing rational judgments is the key to knowing God.

Having defined necessity, Sanusi now explains why this entire enterprise matters. The obligation to know God's attributes depends on first understanding the framework of rational judgment itself. This passage shows that one cannot know what is necessary, impossible, or possible for God without knowing what these terms mean in the first place.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage that begins with 'ولمَّا قدَّمَ الحكم بوجوب معرفةِ المكلَّف شرعاً...'. Notice the logical flow: since the obligation depends on understanding rational judgment, one must know it.
2
Identify the logical structure
The passage uses a 'since...therefore' structure: since the obligation to know God's necessary, impossible, and possible attributes depends on understanding rational judgment, it is incumbent to know what rational judgment is and its divisions.
This is a foundational argument for the study of kalam.
3
Connect to the three categories
The passage mentions the three rational judgments: necessary (wājib), impossible (mustaḥīl), and possible (jāʾiz). Understanding these is essential for knowing God's perfections, His transcendence, and what is possible for Him.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وحقيقة الواجب : ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ؛ إما بلا تأمل ويُسمَّى الضروري؛ ككون الواحد نصف الاثنين مثلاً، وإمَّا بعد التأمُّلِ ويُسمَّى النظري؛ ككون الواحدِ نصفَ سُدُسِ الاثنيْ عَشَرَ مثلاً. ولمَّا قدَّمَ الحكم بوجوب معرفةِ المكلَّف شرعاً لِمَا يجب عقلاً وما يستحيلُ عقلاً وما يجوز عقلاً في حق الله تعالى، وفي حقِّ رسلِهِ عليهم الصلاة والسلام (1)، وكانَ الحكم على شيء أو بشيء موقوفاً على تصوُّرِ معناهما (2) .. تَعَيَّنَ على كلِّ مُكلَّفٍ أن يعرف معنى الحكم العقلي وأقسامه ومعانيها؛ ليعرف بذلك معنى وجوبِ ما يجبُ مِنَ الكمالاتِ لمولانا تبارك وتعالى، ومعنى استحالة ما يُنزَّهُ عنهُ تعالى، ومعنى جواز ما يجوزُ في حَقَّهِ تعالى، ويعرف بذلك ما تتعلق بهِ. (1) قال العلامة البيلي في « تقييداته على شرح صغرى الصغرى » ( ق ٦ ) : ( المراد بالرسل هنا : مطلق الأنبياء، فهو من ذكر الخاص وإرادة العام، أو أنه جارٍ على القول بمرادفة الرسول للنبي وإن كان ذلك غير مشهور، أو أنه خص الرسل لأشرفيَّتهم، أو لأن بعض الصفات كالتبليغ خاص بهم ). (2) يحتمل رجوع ضمير التثنية للحكم والمحكوم عليه، ويحتمل رجوعه للمحكوم عليه والمحكوم به، ومعرفة معنى الحكم تستلزم معرفة معنى المحكوم به والمحكوم عليه. انتهى من هامش ( د ).
And the reality of the necessary (wājib) is that whose non-existence is inconceivable in the mind; either without reflection, and it is called immediate (ḍarūrī), like one being half of two, for example; or after reflection, and it is called speculative (naẓarī), like one being half of one-sixth of twelve, for example. And since He [the author] has previously stated the ruling that it is legally obligatory for the responsible person to know what is intellectually necessary, what is intellectually impossible, and what is intellectually possible concerning Allah Almighty and concerning His messengers, peace be upon them (1), and since a ruling (ḥukm) about something or concerning something depends on conceiving the meaning of both (2) ... it is incumbent upon every responsible person to know the meaning of the rational judgment (al-ḥukm al-ʿaqlī), its divisions, and their meanings, so that by that he may know the meaning of the necessity of what is necessary of perfections for our Lord, Blessed and Exalted, and the meaning of the impossibility of what He is exalted above, and the meaning of the possibility of what is possible concerning Him, and by that he may know what relates to them. (1) The scholar al-Baylī said in his 'Taqyīdāt ʿalā Sharḥ Ṣughrā al-Ṣughrā' (folio 6): 'What is meant by messengers here is absolutely prophets; so it is mentioning the specific while intending the general, or it is based on the view that "messenger" is synonymous with "prophet" even if that is not well-known, or he specified messengers due to their superiority, or because some attributes like conveying revelation are specific to them.' (2) It is possible that the dual pronoun refers to the ruling (ḥukm) and the thing ruled upon (al-maḥkūm ʿalayh), and it is possible that it refers to the thing ruled upon (al-maḥkūm ʿalayh) and the thing by which the ruling is made (al-maḥkūm bih). And knowing the meaning of the ruling necessitates knowing the meaning of the thing ruled upon and the thing by which the ruling is made. End from margin (d).
Source pages p. 132

Commentary
This passage begins by defining the term wājib (necessary) in the context of rational judgments: 'that whose non-existence is inconceivable in the intellect.' It then divides wājib into ḍarūrī (immediate/self-evident) and naẓarī (speculative/discursive). The author then explains why understanding the three rational judgments is necessary: because the obligation to know God's necessary, impossible, and possible attributes depends on understanding the meaning of 'rational judgment' and its divisions. The footnotes clarify the scope of 'messengers' (including prophets) and the dual pronoun in the phrase 'meaning of both'.

Misconception: One can know God's attributes without studying rational judgments.
Correction: Sanusi argues that the very act of knowing God's attributes (necessary, impossible, possible) presupposes understanding the categories of rational judgment. Without this framework, one cannot properly assign attributes to God.

Checkpoint

A) Because it is a legal obligation separate from theology.
B) Because a ruling about something depends on understanding the meaning of both.
C) Because rational judgment is the only source of knowledge.

Key Takeaway
Knowledge of God's attributes requires prior understanding of the three rational judgments: necessary, impossible, and possible.

?
You may pray and worship, but do you truly understand the intellectual foundation of your beliefs? Will you commit today to studying the three rational categories as a way to deepen your knowledge of God, not just as an academic exercise but as a matter of your soul?

Modes of Acquisition and the Threefold Division of Rational Judgment
Sanusi explains the acquisition of rational judgment and its threefold division into wajib, mustahil, and ja'iz.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how rational judgments are acquired either necessarily or through reflection, and how they are classified into three fundamental categories that underpin all kalam reasoning.

Core Idea
Rational judgments are either immediate or reflective, and always belong to one of three categories: wajib, mustahil, or ja'iz.

Sanusi begins by explaining how Allah creates rational judgments in the heart—either immediately through necessity or upon reflection. He then presents the threefold division that structures all theological reasoning. This passage lays the groundwork for understanding necessity, impossibility, and possibility in divine matters.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the translation. Let Sanusi's own phrasing introduce the concepts.
Notice the shift from acquisition (how judgments appear) to classification (what judgments are).
2
Identify the two modes of acquisition
Find where Sanusi describes how rational judgments are created: some necessarily (without reflection) and some upon reflection. Also note the mention of Allah's custom.
This counters the misconception that all rational judgment requires reflection.
3
List the three rational categories
Sanusi explicitly names three: wujub, istihala, and jawaz. Write them down with their definitions.
The word 'wajib' in kalam means logically necessary, not religious obligation.
4
Understand the rationale for the three
Read the passage's explanation: if the intellect accepts both existence and non-existence, it is ja'iz; if not, and existence is more preponderant, it is wajib; otherwise istihala.
This is a logical partition of all possibilities.
5
Reflect on why this matters
Consider how these categories apply to statements about Allah. For example, existence of Allah is wajib, non-existence is mustahil, and creating the world is ja'iz.
This classification is the bedrock of kalam arguments.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثم هذا الحكم العقلي وإنْ عَرِيَ عن القيدين فقد أجرى تبارك وتعالى العادة بأن يخلق بعض أنواعِهِ في القلبِ ضرورياً بلا تأمل ويخلق بعض أنواعِهِ عندَ النظرِ والتأمل والعلوم الحادثةُ كلُّها وإنْ كانَتْ حاصلة بمحض خلْقِ اللهِ تعالى يصح أن يخلقها في القلوب ابتداءً بلا واسطة تجربة ولا بعْثِ رسول ولا نظر ولا فكرة وقد أجرى سبحانه بمحضِ اختيارِهِ العادة في خلقها على هذا التقسيم . وأما أقسام الحكم العقلي : فهي ثلاثة : الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز . ووجه الحصر فيها : أنَّ كلَّ ما يَحكُمُ بهِ العقلُ إنْ كانَ يقبلُ الثبوت والانتفاء معاً فهو الجواز وإِنْ كانَ لا يقبل الأمرين معاً فهو إما أن يكون ثبوته أرجح من عدمه فالوجوب وإلا فالاستحالة . (وهما : التكرر والتجربة ، وواسطة التعليم . انتهى من هامش ( د ))
Then this rational judgment, even though it is free from the two restrictions, the Blessed and Exalted has made it His custom to create some of its types in the heart necessarily without reflection and to create some of its types upon reflection and contemplation. All originated sciences, even though they are obtained solely by the creation of Allah the Exalted, it is valid that He creates them in the hearts initially without a means of experience, nor sending of a messenger, nor reflection, nor thought, but He, glorified, by His mere choice has made the custom in their creation upon this division. As for the divisions of rational judgment, they are three: obligation (wujub), impossibility (istihala), and permissibility (jawaz). The reason for the restriction to them is that everything the intellect judges: if it accepts both existence and non-existence simultaneously, then it is permissibility; and if it does not accept both simultaneously, then either its existence is more preponderant than its non-existence, then it is obligation, otherwise it is impossibility. (These two are: repetition and experience, and the means of teaching. End from margin (d).)
Source pages p. 134

Commentary
This passage first discusses how rational judgments are acquired. Although they are free from custom and revelation, Allah's custom is to create some types necessarily in the heart without any reflection, and other types only upon reflection and contemplation. All originated knowledge is created by Allah directly; He could create it initially without any means, but His custom is to do so through this division. Then the passage presents the threefold division of rational judgment itself: wujub (obligation/necessity), istihala (impossibility), and jawaz (permissibility). The reason for limiting to three is that everything the intellect judges either accepts both existence and non-existence (that is jawaz) or does not accept both. In the latter case, if existence is more preponderant than non-existence, it is wujub; if non-existence is more preponderant, it is istihala. This is the foundational classification in kalam for understanding divine attributes and actions.

Key Terms
ضروري
نظري
الوجوب
الاستحالة
الجواز
الحصر

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think rational judgment always requires reflection.
Correction: Sanusi clearly states that some rational judgments are created necessarily in the heart without any reflection, while others come through reflection.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the criterion for a rational judgment to be classified as 'obligation' (wajib)?
A) The intellect accepts both existence and non-existence equally.
B) The intellect negates both existence and non-existence.
C) The intellect judges existence to be more preponderant than non-existence.
D) The intellect relies on custom.

Key Takeaway
Rational judgments are either necessary or acquired through reflection, and they are always classified as wajib, mustahil, or ja'iz.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hold a belief about God, do you know whether it is a necessary judgment (immediate) or one that requires reflection? Which type shapes most of your faith? Commit to examining one belief you have never questioned and determine its basis.
?
The passage explains that Allah could create knowledge directly but chooses to use means. Are you relying on His custom of using means by actively studying? Or do you expect knowledge to appear without effort? What step will you take today to pursue reflective understanding of your creed?
?
The division into wajib, mustahil, and ja'iz applies to everything you believe about Allah. Is there a belief you treat as merely ja'iz (possible) when it should be wajib (necessary) for you? For example, that He alone controls all outcomes. Write down one such belief and how you will strengthen it in your heart.

Rational Judgments

The Three Rational Judgments and Their Subdivisions
Sanusi enumerates the three rational judgments and explains their logical criterion and subdivision.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the classification of rational judgments into necessity, impossibility, and possibility, and how each of these is further divided into necessary and theoretical.

Core Idea
All rational judgments fall into three categories based on affirmation and negation.

Sanusi begins by establishing the foundational categories of rational judgment. Before we can reason about Allah's attributes, we need a clear understanding of the three possible judgments the mind can make: necessity, impossibility, and possibility. He then shows that each of these can be known either directly or through reflection.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through.
Notice the concise style of Sanusi.
2
Identify the three judgments and their criterion
Find the three rational judgments and understand the logical rule that distinguishes them: possibility admits both affirmation and negation, necessity admits only affirmation, impossibility admits only negation.
This is the core of the classification.
3
Understand the further subdivision
The passage states that each judgment is also either necessary (perceived without reflection) or theoretical (requires reflection). See how this division applies to each of the three types.
This subdivision is important for later proofs.
4
Reflect on the significance
Ask yourself why this classification is essential for kalam. How does it help in discussing Allah's attributes? The commentary will help clarify.
This connects to broader theological reasoning.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وجه حصر الحكم العقلي في الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز وأما أقسام الحكم العقلي فهي ثلاثة الوجوب والاستحالة والجواز. ووجه الحصر فيها أن كل ما يحكم به العقل إن كان يقبل الثبوت والانتفاء معاً فهو الجواز وإن كان لا يقبل الأمرين معاً فإن كان يقبل الثبوت فقط دون الانتفاء فهو الوجوب وإن كان يقبل الانتفاء فقط دون الثبوت فهو الاستحالة. ولما كان الحكم العقلي ينقسم إلى قسمين ضروري وهو ما يدرك بلا تأمل ونظري وهو ما لا يدرك إلا بعد التأمل لزم أن كل واحد من أقسامه ينقسم كذلك إلى ضروري ونظري.
The reason for the restriction of rational judgment to necessity, impossibility, and possibility. As for the divisions of rational judgment, they are three: necessity, impossibility, and possibility. And the reason for the restriction to them is that everything which the mind judges: if it admits affirmation and negation together, then it is possibility; and if it does not admit the two together, then if it admits affirmation only without negation, it is necessity; and if it admits negation only without affirmation, it is impossibility. And since rational judgment is divided into two types: necessary, which is perceived without reflection, and theoretical, which is not perceived except after reflection, it follows that each of its divisions is likewise divided into necessary and theoretical.
Source pages p. 134

Commentary
This passage enumerates the three rational judgments: necessity (wujūb), impossibility (istiḥālah), and possibility (jawāz). The author explains the criterion: if a thing can be both affirmed and negated, it is possible; if it can only be affirmed, it is necessary; if only negated, it is impossible. Then he notes that just as rational judgment in general is either necessary (ḍarūrī) or theoretical (naẓarī), each of these three divisions also has necessary and theoretical subtypes.

Key Terms
وجوب
استحالة
جواز
ضروري
نظري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: People may think possibility (jawaz) means something is uncertain or probabilistic in a subjective sense.
Correction: Possibility here means that the mind can conceive both existence and non-existence without contradiction; it does not imply uncertainty or lack of knowledge.

Checkpoint
What is the criterion for a rational judgment to be classified as 'possible' (al-jawaz)?
A) It can only be affirmed
B) It can only be negated
C) It admits both affirmation and negation
D) It requires reflection

Key Takeaway
Rational judgments are either necessary, impossible, or possible, and each can be known directly or through reasoning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you make a judgment about something in your life, do you ever reflect on whether it is truly necessary, impossible, or merely possible? How often do you treat a possible thing as necessary, causing anxiety, or an impossible thing as possible, causing false hope? In what area of your life are you holding onto a belief that is actually only possible but you treat it as necessary? Will you pause today to re-evaluate your assumptions?
?
The passage says rational judgments can be divided into necessary (without reflection) and theoretical (with reflection). How many of your deeply held beliefs have you actually reflected on, vs. simply absorbed? Take one belief you hold about God or yourself: is it something you know necessarily, or have you merely assumed it? What would happen if you examined it carefully?
?
Consider a claim you strongly believe: e.g., 'I will succeed if I try hard.' Is this judgment necessary, impossible, or possible? If you treat it as necessary, you may be ignoring the role of God's will. If you treat it as impossible, you may be despairing. How can the correct classification of this judgment change your attitude and your du'a?

The Rational Judgments of Obligation and Impossibility and Their Division
Sanusi defines al-wujūb and al-istiḥālah and divides each into ḍarūrī and naẓarī.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definitions of wujub (obligation) and istihalah (impossibility), and how each rational judgment is divided into necessary (daruri) and theoretical (nazari) knowledge.

Core Idea
Every rational judgment in kalam is either necessary or theoretical — and this applies to obligation, impossibility, and possibility alike.

Sanusi begins by defining two of the three rational judgments: obligation (wujub) and impossibility (istihalah). He then explains that every rational judgment, including these, can be known either immediately (daruri) or through reflection (nazari). This division is foundational for understanding how we acquire theological knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic carefully, then its translation. Notice the precise wording about affirmation and denial.
2
Identify the definitions
Find how Sanusi defines wujub and istihalah. How are they opposites?
3
Understand the division
The passage then states that each rational judgment is divided into daruri and nazari. Ask yourself: what makes something known without reflection?
4
Connect to the bigger picture
Realize that this classification applies to all three judgments, including possibility (jawaz), even though only two are defined here.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإنْ كانَ يقبلُ الثبوت فقط دونَ الانتفاء .. فهو الوجوبُ ، وإنْ كانَ يقبل الانتفاء فقط دون الثبوت . . فهو الاستحالة
ولمَّا كانَ الحكم العقلي ينقسم إلى قسمين : ضروري ؛ وهو ما يُدرَكُ بلا تأمل ، ونظري ؛ وهو ما لا يُدرَكُ إلا بعد التأمل .. لزمَ أَنَّ كلَّ واحدٍ مِنْ أقسامِهِ ينقسمُ كذلك إلى ضروري ونظري .
If it accepts affirmation only without denial, then it is obligation (al-wujūb). And if it accepts denial only without affirmation, then it is impossibility (al-istiḥālah). And since the rational judgment is divided into two parts: necessary (ḍarūrī) – which is perceived without reflection – and theoretical (naẓarī) – which is not perceived except after reflection – it follows that each one of its divisions is also divided into necessary and theoretical.
Source pages p. 135

Commentary
This passage defines two of the three rational judgments (al-wujūb and al-istiḥālah) and then explains that each rational judgment – whether obligation, impossibility, or possibility – is further divided into two types: necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). The necessary is known without any reflection, while the theoretical requires reflection. The author states that this division applies to each of the three judgments, meaning that there is a necessary obligation and a theoretical obligation, likewise for impossibility and possibility.

Key Terms
الوجوب
الاستحالة
الضروري
النظري
الحكم العقلي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Wujub is always known necessarily without reflection.
Correction: No, wujub can be either daruri or nazari; some obligations are known only through reflection.

Checkpoint
How many types of rational judgment are mentioned in this passage, and into which two categories is each divided?
A) Three types; divided into ضروري and نظري
B) Two types; divided into ثبوت and انتفاء
C) Three types; divided into وجود and عدم
D) Two types; divided into واجب and جائز

Key Takeaway
The rational judgments of obligation and impossibility are defined by their relation to affirmation and denial, and each judgment—including possibility—is further divided into necessary and theoretical knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This lesson distinguishes beliefs you know without reflection (ḍarūrī) from those requiring study (naẓarī). Consider your faith in God: do you believe because you have never questioned it, or because you have actively reflected? If it is only ḍarūrī without reflection, does your faith remain stable when doubts arise? Commit today to turning at least one of your theoretical beliefs into a well-reflected certainty.
?
The passage defines obligation (wujūb) as that which can only be affirmed. Think of an obligation in your life—like prayer or honesty—that you affirm verbally but sometimes fail to act upon. Why do you deny it in action while affirming it with your tongue? What would it take for you to live in complete alignment with what you know to be wajib?
?
Your knowledge is divided into necessary (immediate) and theoretical (acquired). Which aspects of your religion do you rely on without reflection? Are there areas where you have settled for a surface-level 'necessary' belief when deep reflection is required? Identify one such area and plan to study it until it becomes theoretical certainty.
