Rational Judgments
intermediate
6 min
Division of al-Wajib into Necessary and Theoretical
Sanusi explains the division and a theological debate

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the division of al-wajib into necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī) is according to God's habitual custom, and that there is a theological debate about whether all knowledge could be theoretical.

Core Idea
The division of obligatory knowledge is by divine custom, not by absolute necessity.

Sanusi now turns to the classification of what is rationally necessary (al-wājib). He explains that some rational truths are known immediately (ḍarūrī) and others require reflection (naẓarī), but this distinction is not absolute—it operates according to God's usual practice. He then introduces a debate about whether it is possible for all knowledge to be theoretical, a question that touches on the nature of the human mind itself.

Guided Path
1
Understand the division's basis
Read the passage and notice that the division of al-wajib into necessary and theoretical is said to be 'according to God's custom' (عادة الله). Ask yourself: Why is this qualification important?
This means the division is not inherent but habitual.
2
Recognize the consensus on God's power
Sanusi notes that it is permissible by consensus for God to make all knowledge necessary. This shows that the division is not absolute; God could change it.
This reinforces the idea that the division is by custom.
3
Examine the debated opposite scenario
The disagreement is about the reverse: could all sciences be only theoretical? The argument against this is that it would contradict the existence of the mind, which is either identical to or necessarily accompanied by necessary knowledge.
The passage is cut off but the core argument is presented.
4
Reflect on the incomplete argument
The passage ends mid-sentence. Consider what the full argument might be: if the mind itself is constituted by necessary knowledge, then denying all necessary knowledge would deny the existence of the mind.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقوله : ( إمَّا بلا تأمل ... ) إلى آخره ؛ يعني أنَّ الواجب ينقسم إلى ضروريّ ونظري بحسَبِ مُجرى عادةِ الله تعالى ، وإلا فيجوز بإجماع أن يُصير سبحانه جميع العلوم ضرورية يلجأ العقل إلى تيقنها ، وتُخلَقُ فيه بلا تأمل أصلاً ، كما يصح في العقل أن يجعلَ سبحانه جميع حركاتِنا اضطراريةً ، لا نجد عادةً تيشر تركها ، وإِنَّما وقع الخلافُ في العلوم في عكس ما سبق ؛ وهو : هل يصح أن تكونَ العلوم كلُّها نظريةً للعقل ، ولا يُعرَفَ منها شيء بالضرورة ، أو لا يصح ذلك ؛ لمنافاتِهِ وجودَ العقل ، بناءً على أنَّه نفس العلوم الضرورية ، أو ملزوم لها ، فالجمع بينَ وجودِ العقل وبين نفي كلِّ علم
And his saying: 'Either without reflection...' to the end means that the Necessary divides into necessary and theoretical according to the course of God's custom. Otherwise, it is permissible by consensus that He, glorified, makes all sciences necessary, such that the mind resorts to certainty of them and they are created in it without any reflection, just as it is valid in reason that He, glorified, makes all our movements involuntary, so that we do not ordinarily find ease in refraining from them. The disagreement only occurred in the sciences concerning the opposite of what preceded; namely: is it valid for all sciences to be theoretical for the mind, and nothing known by necessity, or is that not valid because it contradicts the existence of the mind, based on it being the same as the necessary sciences or their concomitant? So the combination between the existence of the mind and the negation of every science [is problematic].
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This passage explains the division of al-wājib into necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). The author clarifies that this division is by God's custom (عادة الله), meaning it is not inherent but habitual. It is possible for God to make all knowledge necessary and immediate. He then raises a debate: whether it is possible for all knowledge to be theoretical, or if that would contradict the existence of the mind itself (since the mind might be constituted by necessary knowledge or be a concomitant of it). The passage is incomplete but presents the core of the discussion.

Key Terms
الضروري
النظري
عادة الله
العلوم
الاضطرارية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the division of al-wajib into necessary and theoretical is absolute and cannot be otherwise.
Correction: The author clarifies that this division is according to God's custom, and God could make all knowledge necessary or all theoretical, but the existence of the mind requires at least some necessary knowledge.

Checkpoint
What does the author say about the division of al-wajib into necessary and theoretical?
A) It is an essential division
B) It is according to God's custom and could be otherwise
C) It is only theoretical
D) It is only necessary

Key Takeaway
The distinction between necessary and theoretical knowledge in rational theology is based on God's habitual custom, not on an inherent necessity; God could make all knowledge immediate or all knowledge reflective, but the existence of the human mind seems to require at least some necessary knowledge.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of one belief you hold about your Creator. Do you hold it with immediate, unshakeable certainty, or do you rely on arguments and proofs? What would change in your daily life if that belief were as certain as the fact that you exist right now?
?
If your mind cannot function without some necessary knowledge, then denying all immediate truths about God is self-defeating. In what areas of your faith have you allowed doubt to masquerade as intellectual humility? What necessary truth are you avoiding acknowledging?
?
The author says the mind itself is built on necessary sciences. Look at your own life: what truth do you know so deeply that you cannot escape it, yet you act as if it were a theoretical option? What would it cost you to live in light of that truth today?

The Ranking of Impossible Before Possible
Sanusi justifies the order of impossible before possible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi presents the impossible before the possible in the classification, based on the principles of closeness to the necessary and simplicity versus composition.

Core Idea
The impossible precedes the possible because it is simpler and directly opposes the necessary.

In the previous lesson, Sanusi introduced the three rational judgments: necessary, impossible, and possible. Now he explains the order in which they are presented. You might wonder why the impossible is discussed before the possible, but as you will see, there is a logical and pedagogical reason rooted in the structure of each category.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic text once to get a sense of Sanusi's concise style. Then read the English translation to understand the argument.
Notice the key terms: واجب, مستحيل, جائز, بسيط, مركب.
2
Understand the first reason
The first reason given is that the impossible is closer to the necessary because it is its opposite. Reflect on what 'closer' means in this context: opposites are directly related.
3
Analyze the second reason
The second reason is about simplicity versus composition. The necessary and impossible are simple (each has only one attribute), while the possible is composite (combining affirmation and negation). The simple is prior to the composite.
Think about why simplicity implies priority in a classification.
4
Connect to the overall classification
Now you can see that the order is not arbitrary. The impossible is placed before the possible because it is simpler and more directly related to the necessary. This helps in understanding the structure of rational judgments.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقدمنا المستحيل على الجائز : لأنَّهُ أقرب إلى الواجب ؛ إذ هو مقابله ، وأيضاً : فالجائز شبهُ مُركَّب ممَّا ثبت للواجب مِنَ الثبوتِ ، وما ثبت للمستحيل مِنَ النفي ، والواجب والمستحيل شبه بسيطين ، إذ لم يثبت لكلِّ واحدٍ منهما إلا أحد الأمرين ، ولا شك أن رتبة البسيط أحقُّ أن تكون قبل المركَّبِ
And we presented the impossible before the possible because it is closer to the necessary, since it is its opposite. Also, the possible is like a composite of what is affirmed of the necessary in terms of affirmation and what is affirmed of the impossible in terms of negation, while the necessary and the impossible are like simples, since only one of the two matters is affirmed for each. And there is no doubt that the rank of the simple is more deserving to be before the composite.
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This passage explains why the author placed the category of 'impossible' before 'possible' in the previous classification. The reason is that the impossible is closer to the necessary (wājib) because it is its direct opposite. Furthermore, the possible (jāʼiz) is like a composite of two aspects: from the necessary it takes affirmation (thubūt), and from the impossible it takes negation (nafy). In contrast, the necessary and the impossible are each simple (basīṭ), since only one aspect is affirmed for each—affirmation for the necessary, negation for the impossible. Since the simple is prior to the composite in rank, the impossible (which shares simplicity with the necessary) is placed before the possible (which is composite).

Key Terms
واجب (wājib)
مستحيل (mustaḥīl)
جائز (jāʼiz)
بسيط (basīṭ)
مركب (murakkab)
ثبوت (thubūt)
نفي (nafy)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
Why is the impossible presented before the possible in the classification?
A) Because the impossible is more important.
B) Because the impossible is closer to the necessary and is simpler.
C) Because the possible is derived from the impossible.
D) Because the author made an error.

Key Takeaway
The impossible precedes the possible because it is simple and directly opposite to the necessary, while the possible is composite.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about God, do you find it easier to start with what He is (affirmation) or what He is not (negation)? This lesson argues that negation logically precedes affirmation. Are you willing to first accept your limitations in knowing God before you build certain knowledge?
?
Your faith is a mix of certainty (affirmation) and doubt (negation). The possible (jāʼiz) is composite like that. But the simple—pure affirmation or pure negation—is prior. Is there an area of your faith where you long for the simplicity of pure certainty, and what would it cost you to seek that simplicity?
?
The impossible is placed first because it is simple and opposite to the necessary. In your life, are you ready to confront the 'impossible'—the things you cannot know or control—before assuming possibilities? What would it look like to order your spiritual priorities by starting with what is logically prior, even if it is uncomfortable?

Can All Sciences Be Theoretical? The Dispute and Preponderant View
Dispute on the possibility of all sciences being theoretical, with preponderant view and evidence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the dispute over whether all sciences can be theoretical and why the preponderant view holds that it is possible, supported by evidence from rational deniers and an example of a theoretical necessary judgment.

Core Idea
The intellect does not require necessary sciences to exist, so all sciences can be theoretical.

This passage addresses a question that is the reverse of the previous discussion: instead of asking whether all sciences can be necessary, it asks whether they can all be theoretical. The author argues that the preponderant view is that it is possible, and he provides both logical evidence and empirical evidence from the Samaniyya and Sophists. The lesson will walk through this argument and its implications for understanding the relationship between intellect, necessity, and theoretical reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Understand the dispute
Read the opening lines where the two views are presented: either all sciences can be theoretical or not. Notice that this is the opposite of the earlier question about all sciences being necessary.
2
Follow the preponderant view
The author states that the apparent (ẓāhir) view is the first opinion—that it is valid for all sciences to be theoretical. He supports this by defining the intellect as acceptance of knowledge or its opposites, not as knowledge itself or its necessary concomitant.
This definition refutes the objection that denying all necessary sciences would negate the intellect.
3
Examine the evidence
First, the existence of the Samaniyya (who deny non-sensible knowledge) and the Sophists (who deny all knowledge) shows that such people are still considered rational by the imams. Second, the example of 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve' illustrates how a theoretical necessary judgment requires two premises: one necessary and one theoretical.
The example shows that theoretical reasoning builds on necessary premises, so all sciences could theoretically be traced back to necessary foundations.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنَّما وقع الخلافُ في العلوم في عكس ما سبق ؛ وهو : هل يصح أن تكونَ العلوم كلُّها نظريةً للعقل ، ولا يُعرَفَ منها شيء بالضرورة ، أو لا يصح ذلك ؛ لمنافاتِهِ وجودَ العقل ، بناءً على أنَّه نفس العلوم الضرورية ، أو ملزوم لها ، فالجمع بينَ وجودِ العقل وبين نفي كلِّ علم ضروري . . جمع بين متنافيين عادةً . والظاهرُ : القولُ الأولُ ؛ بناءً على أنَّ العقل قبول القلبِ للعلم أو أضدادِهِ الخاصَّةِ ؛ كالظنّ والشكّ والوهم والجهل المركَّب ، وليس نفس العلم ولا ملزوماً له ، ويدلُّ على ذلكَ : وجودُ السُّمَنِيَّةِ المنكرينَ لِمَا عدا المحسوساتِ مِنَ العلوم ضروريةً كانَتْ أو نظرية ووجودُ السُّوفَسْطائية المنكرين لجميعِ العلوم؛ ضروريها ونظريها ، محسوسها وغير محسوسها ، وهم مِنَ العقلاء ؛ بدليل تعرُّض الأئمةِ لبدعتِهم ، والتحيل في مناظرتهم لدفعها . وتمثيلنا للواجب النظري بكون الواحدِ نصفَ سُدُسِ الاثني عشر . . جلي ; فإنَّ هذا الحكم إنَّما يحصل للعقل بعد استحضار مقدمتين : إحداهما ـ وهي الصغرى - : ضرورية ; وهي قولنا : الواحد نصفُ الاثنين . والأخرى : نظريةٌ ; وهي قولنا : ونصفُ الاثنين نصفُ سُدس الاثني عشرَ ; لأنَّها موقوفةٌ على معرفة كونِ الاثنينِ سُدُسَ الاثني عشرَ بقسمتها إلى ستة أقسام متساوية ، وأن الاثنين أحد أقسامها الستة المتساوية . فإذا استحضر العقلُ بالفكرةِ الدليل المركَّبَ مِنْ هاتين المقدمتين ؛
And the disagreement occurred concerning the sciences in the opposite of what preceded: namely, whether it is valid that all sciences are theoretical for the intellect, and nothing of them is known by necessity, or that is not valid because it conflicts with the existence of the intellect, based on the fact that it is the same as the necessary sciences or a necessary concomitant of them, so combining the existence of the intellect with the negation of every necessary science is, in the usual course, combining two contradictories. And the apparent (view) is the first opinion, based on the fact that the intellect is the heart's acceptance of knowledge or its specific opposites—like conjecture, doubt, illusion, and compound ignorance—and it is neither the same as knowledge nor a necessary concomitant of it. And evidence for that is the existence of the Samaniyya who deny any knowledge besides the sensible, whether necessary or theoretical, and the existence of the Sophists who deny all sciences, both necessary and theoretical, sensible and non-sensible, and they are among the rational people; evidence being the imams' engagement with their innovation and their contrivance in debating them to repel it. And our example of the theoretical necessary is that one is half of one-sixth of twelve—clear; for this judgment is obtained by the intellect only after adducing two premises: one of them—which is the minor—is necessary, namely our statement: one is half of two; and the other is theoretical, namely our statement: half of two is half of one-sixth of twelve; because it depends on knowing that two is one-sixth of twelve by dividing it into six equal parts, and that two is one of its six equal parts. So when the intellect with reflection adduces the proof composed of these two premises;
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This passage addresses a dispute that is the opposite of the earlier point: instead of whether all sciences can be necessary, it asks whether all sciences can be theoretical (naẓarī), with none known by necessity. One side argues this is impossible because it contradicts the existence of the intellect, which itself is either identical to necessary sciences or a necessary concomitant of them—so denying all necessary sciences would entail the absence of intellect. However, the author states that the apparent (ẓāhir) view is the first opinion (i.e., that it is possible for all sciences to be theoretical? Wait careful: The text says 'والظاهر: القول الأول'—but which is 'the first'? The two sides: first is that all sciences can be theoretical? Actually the passage says 'الخلاف في عكس ما سبق ; وهو: هل يصح أن تكون العلوم كلها نظرية... أو لا يصح' So the first opinion is that it is valid, the second is that it is not valid. Then it says 'والظاهر: القول الأول' meaning the apparent (or preponderant) view is the first—that it is valid for all sciences to be theoretical. But then it gives evidence that refutes the objection by defining the intellect as acceptance (qabūl) of knowledge or its opposites, not as knowledge itself or its necessary concomitant. This supports the validity of the first opinion, because if the intellect were not identical to necessary sciences, then denying all necessary sciences does not negate the intellect. Evidence from the existence of the Samaniyya (who deny non-sensible knowledge) and the Sophists (who deny all knowledge) confirms that such people are still considered rational (ʿuqalāʾ) by the imams, who debate them. Thus, it is possible for all sciences to be theoretical, at least in principle. Finally, the author gives an example of a theoretical necessary (wājib naẓarī): the proposition 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve'. This requires two premises: a necessary one ('one is half of two') and a theoretical one ('half of two is half of one-sixth of twelve'), which itself depends on understanding that two is one-sixth of twelve. The intellect, through reflection (fikra), combines these premises to reach the conclusion. This illustrates how a theoretical necessary differs from a necessary-by-itself.

Key Terms
نظري (naẓarī)
ضروري (ḍarūrī)
عقل (ʿaql)
سمينية (Samaniyya)
سوفسطائية (Sūfisṭāʾiyya)
مقدمة (muqaddima)
صغرى (ṣughrā)
فكرة (fikra)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If all sciences were theoretical, the intellect would be impossible because it requires necessary sciences.
Correction: The intellect is defined as acceptance of knowledge or its opposites, not as the necessary sciences themselves. Denying all necessary sciences does not negate the intellect, as evidenced by the Samaniyya and Sophists who are still considered rational.

Checkpoint
What is the 'apparent' (al-ẓāhir) view regarding whether all sciences can be theoretical?
A) It is impossible because it negates the intellect.
B) It is possible, as evidenced by the existence of Samaniyya and Sophists who are still rational.
C) It is impossible because necessary sciences are the only true knowledge.
D) It is possible only for sensory knowledge.

Key Takeaway
The preponderant view is that all sciences can be theoretical, supported by the definition of the intellect as acceptance of knowledge and by the existence of rational deniers like the Samaniyya and Sophists.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage defines your intellect not by what you know, but by your capacity to accept truth. In what specific area of your life are you currently denying a truth that your intellect could accept? What would it cost you to accept it, and are you willing to pay that price today?
?
The author argues that even the most basic knowledge (like 'one is half of two') is actually theoretical for someone who hasn't yet reasoned through it. What is one core belief or conviction you hold that you have never critically examined? Will you set aside time this week to test its foundations?
?
The Samaniyya and Sophists were considered rational despite denying all sciences. This means even profound error can coexist with intellect. How does this change the way you view someone whose beliefs you consider completely wrong? What does it demand of you in how you engage with that person from today?

Example of the Necessary Theoretical Judgment
Sanusi illustrates the necessary theoretical judgment with an arithmetic example.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how a necessary theoretical judgment is formed from a combination of necessary and theoretical premises, using the example of 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve'.

Core Idea
A theoretical judgment can be certain if built from necessary premises.

Sanusi gives a concrete arithmetic example to show how a judgment that is not immediately known can still be necessary. By breaking down the reasoning into two premises, he demonstrates the structure of theoretical knowledge. This passage clarifies that 'theoretical' does not mean uncertain, but rather that it requires reflection to reach certainty.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the two premises
Read the Arabic passage carefully, then its translation. Note the two premises the author lists: one necessary and one theoretical.
The necessary premise is 'one is half of two'. The theoretical premise is 'half of two is half of one-sixth of twelve'.
2
Observe why the second premise is theoretical
The author explains that the second premise depends on knowing that two is one-sixth of twelve, which itself requires a mental operation. This shows that theoretical premises are not self-evident but require further reasoning.
3
Trace how the intellect reaches the conclusion
See how the intellect combines both premises through reflection (fikra) to form a compound proof (dalīl murakkab). The proof yields the conclusion: 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve'.
Observe that the conclusion is the same as the original judgment, now proven.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وتمثيلنا للواجب النظري بكون الواحدِ نصفَ سُدُسِ الاثني عشر . . جلي ؛ فإنَّ هذا الحكم إنَّما يحصل للعقل بعد استحضار مقدمتين : إحداهما ـ وهي الصغرى - : ضرورية ؛ وهي قولنا : الواحد نصفُ الاثنين والأخرى : نظريةٌ ؛ وهي قولنا : ونصفُ الاثنين نصفُ سُدس الاثني عشرَ ؛ لأنَّها موقوفةٌ على معرفة كونِ الاثنينِ سُدُسَ الاثني عشرَ بقسمتها إلى ستة أقسام متساوية ، وأن الاثنين أحد أقسامها الستة المتساوية فإذا استحضر العقلُ بالفكرةِ الدليل المركَّبَ مِنْ هاتين المقدمتين ؛ وهو أنَّ الواحد نصفُ الاثنين ، ونصف الاثنين نصفُ سُدُس الاثني عشرَ ؛ لأنَّ الاثنينِ سُدُسُ أقسامها الستة المتساوية . . علم حينئذٍ نتيجةَ هذا الدليل ؛ وهو : الواحد نصفُ سُدُس الاثني عشر ، وقس على هذا ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق .
And our example of the necessary theoretical [judgment] as 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve' is clear. For this judgment is only obtained by the intellect after bringing to mind two premises: one of them—the minor—is necessary, which is our saying: 'one is half of two'; and the other is theoretical, which is our saying: 'and half of two is half of one-sixth of twelve', because it depends on knowing that two is one-sixth of twelve by dividing it into six equal parts, and that two is one of its six equal parts. So when the intellect, through reflection, brings to mind the compound proof from these two premises, that is: one is half of two, and half of two is half of one-sixth of twelve, because two is one-sixth of its six equal parts, then it knows the result of this proof, which is: one is half of one-sixth of twelve. And analogize to this. And by Allah the Exalted is success.
Source pages p. 137

Commentary
This passage provides a concrete example of a necessary theoretical judgment (al-wājib al-naẓarī). The author chooses the proposition 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve' as a clear example. He explains that this judgment is not immediate but requires the intellect to bring to mind two premises (muqaddimatān). The first premise (the minor, al-ṣughrā) is necessary (ḍarūriyya): 'one is half of two'. The second premise is theoretical (naẓariyya): 'half of two is half of one-sixth of twelve'. The second premise is theoretical because it depends on knowing that two is one-sixth of twelve, which itself requires a mental operation: dividing twelve into six equal parts and recognizing that two constitutes one of those parts. When the intellect (al-ʿaql) combines these two premises through reflection (fikra), it produces a compound proof (dalīl murakkab) whose conclusion is the original judgment: 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve'. This illustrates how theoretical knowledge arises from a combination of necessary and theoretical premises. The author then says 'and analogize to this' (wa qis ʿalā hādhā), indicating that all necessary theoretical judgments follow this pattern.

Key Terms
الواجب النظري
المقدمة
الصغرى
الضرورية
النظرية
الفكرة
الدليل المركب
النتيجة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Theoretical judgments are always uncertain or doubtful.
Correction: A theoretical judgment can be necessary (certain) if it is based on a valid argument from necessary premises, as in the example.

Checkpoint
In the example, which premise is theoretical (naẓariyya)?
A) 'One is half of two'
B) 'Half of two is half of one-sixth of twelve'
C) 'One is half of one-sixth of twelve'
D) 'Two is one-sixth of twelve'

Key Takeaway
A necessary theoretical judgment is certain knowledge obtained by combining a necessary premise with a theoretical premise through reflection.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Sanusi shows that even a seemingly simple mathematical truth requires reflection to be certain. Think of a core belief you hold about your faith. Have you ever truly reflected on the premises that lead to that certainty, or do you simply assume it? Will you take time to examine the 'theoretical premises' of your own convictions?
?
The proof for 'one is half of one-sixth of twelve' depends on breaking twelve into equal parts. When you face doubts about theological truths, do you break them down into smaller, self-evident premises? What necessary premise might you be neglecting in your own reasoning? Commit to identifying one necessary truth that anchors your faith today.

4 min
Definition of the Impossible and Its Divisions
Sanusi defines the impossible and divides it into necessary and theoretical with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the impossible (mustahil) and its two divisions: necessary impossible and theoretical impossible, with examples.

Core Idea
The impossible is not just what doesn't exist—it's what reason cannot conceive.

Sanusi now turns to the first of the three rational judgments: the impossible. He defines it precisely and immediately distinguishes between two types: one whose impossibility is obvious to everyone without reflection, and another that requires a moment of thought. The examples he gives from basic arithmetic make the distinction concrete.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of the impossible
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how Sanusi defines the impossible as that whose existence is not conceivable in the intellect.
The definition is crucial for understanding the rest of kalam.
2
Identify the two divisions
The passage divides the impossible into necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). Note the key difference: one requires no reflection, the other does.
The division is based on whether reflection is needed to perceive the impossibility.
3
Examine the necessary impossible example
The example 'one is half of four' is used. The author explains that everyone knows immediately that half of four is two, so the impossibility of one being half is instantly clear.
4
Examine the theoretical impossible example
The example 'one is one-sixth of twelve' is given. Common people may not know the sixth of twelve immediately, so they need to reflect to realize the impossibility.
This example shows how ignorance can make the impossibility seem non-obvious at first.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والمستحيلُ : ما لا يتصوَّرُ في العقل ثبوتُهُ ؛ إِمَّا بلا تأمل أيضاً ؛ ككونِ الواحدِ نصف الأربعةِ ، وإمَّا بعد التأمل ككون الواحدِ سُدُسَ الاثْنَيْ عشر . أمَّا تمثيلنا للمستحيل الضروري بكون الواحد نصف الأربعة : فظاهر للعام والخاص ؛ لأنَّهُ لمَّا عُرِفَ بالضرورة للجميع أن نصفَها اثنانِ . . لزمَ أَن يُعرَفَ بالضرورة انتفاء النصفية عن كل ما سواهما مِنْ واحد أو غيره وأما تمثيلنا للمستحيل النظري بكون الواحدِ سُدُس الاثني عشر : فهو باعتبار العوام ؛ لأنَّهم قد يجهلون قبلَ التأمُّلِ أَنَّ سُدُسَها اثنانِ أو غيرهما ، فلا يعرفون ابتداءً استحالة كونِ الواحدِ سُدساً منها ، بل حتى يعرفوا أنَّ سُدُسَ الاثني عشر هو القسمُ الواحدُ مِنْ أقسامها الستة المتساوية ، والواحد ليس كذلكَ ، وإنَّما هو قسم مِنْ أقسامها الاثني
And the impossible is that whose existence is not conceivable in the intellect; either without reflection also, like 'one is half of four', or after reflection, like 'one is one-sixth of twelve'. As for our example of the necessary impossible as 'one is half of four': it is apparent to the common and the elite, because when it is known by necessity by all that its half is two, it necessarily follows that it is known by necessity that the property of being half is negated from everything other than them, whether one or something else. And as for our example of the theoretical impossible as 'one is one-sixth of twelve': it is with respect to the common people, because they may be ignorant before reflection that its one-sixth is two or other than it, so they do not know initially the impossibility of 'one being a sixth of it', until they know that one-sixth of twelve is the single part from its six equal parts, and one is not like that, but rather it is a part of its twelve parts.
Source pages p. 138

Commentary
This passage defines the impossible (al-mustaḥīl) and divides it into necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). The impossible is defined as that whose existence (thubūt) is not conceivable (lā yataṣawwar) in the intellect. This inconceivability can be immediate (without reflection, bilā taʾammul) or after reflection (baʿda al-taʾammul). The example of the necessary impossible is 'one is half of four'. The author explains that this is obvious to everyone because it is known necessarily that the half of four is two, so it is necessarily known that half cannot apply to anything other than two, including one. The example of the theoretical impossible is 'one is one-sixth of twelve'. This is theoretical because for common people, they may not know that one-sixth of twelve is two, so they do not immediately realize the impossibility of one being a sixth. They need to reflect and first know that one-sixth of twelve is one part of six equal parts, and since one is a different part (out of twelve parts), it cannot be that one is a sixth. Thus, the impossibility becomes known after reflection. The passage ends abruptly (cut off at page break), but it completes the explanation of the theoretical example.

Key Terms
المستحيل
الضروري
النظري
التأمل
تصور
ثبوت

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The impossible is simply that which does not exist.
Correction: The author specifies the impossible as that which cannot be conceived to exist in the intellect, not merely that which does not exist.

Checkpoint
According to the author, what is an example of the theoretical impossible (al-mustaḥīl al-naẓarī)?
A) One is half of four
B) One is one-sixth of twelve
C) Two is one-sixth of twelve
D) One is half of two

Key Takeaway
The impossible (mustaḥīl) is what the intellect cannot conceive to exist, and it is divided into necessary (immediately known) and theoretical (known after reflection).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever dismissed a belief about Allah or the unseen as 'impossible' without honestly examining whether your reason had truly reflected on it? Would Sanusi say that such a judgment is the mark of a necessary or theoretical impossible in your heart?
?
Which of your core Islamic beliefs do you hold with 'necessary' certainty—known immediately in your intellect—and which remain 'theoretical', accepted but not yet fully reflected upon? How would your faith change if you committed to pondering the latter until they became necessary?
?
If the impossible is defined as 'that which cannot be conceived in the intellect,' then what impossible propositions about your own life (e.g., that Allah is unjust, that deeds are wasted) have you perhaps allowed to linger in your mind without confronting them with genuine reflection?

The Impossible: Necessary and Theoretical
Sanusi defines the impossible and distinguishes necessary from theoretical.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rational distinction between necessary impossible and theoretical impossible, and how reflection affects our knowledge of impossibility.

Core Idea
Impossibility is either immediately obvious or only clear after reflection.

Imam Sanusi begins by defining the impossible (al-mustahil) as that which cannot be conceived to exist. He then divides it into two types: necessary and theoretical, using clear numerical examples. This passage establishes a foundational concept in Islamic theology: rational judgments are not all of the same kind—some require no thought, others demand careful reflection. Let's examine his definition and examples closely.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
First, read the Arabic text once without analyzing. Then read the English translation. Let the author's structure become familiar before interpreting.
Notice that the passage starts with a definition then gives examples.
2
Identify the definition of impossible
Find the sentence where Sanusi defines what 'impossible' means. Understand that it relates to what the mind cannot conceive as existing.
3
Distinguish the two types
Look for the two categories: necessary impossible and theoretical impossible. Note the condition that distinguishes them: whether reflection is needed or not.
The key phrase is 'without reflection' vs 'after reflection'.
4
Understand the examples
Examine the arithmetic examples: one being half of four (necessary) and one being one-sixth of twelve (theoretical). Reflect on why the first is immediately known and the second requires thought for common people.
Also consider the author's note about mathematicians—it shows that knowledge level can shift the category for the individual.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
تعريف المستحيل الضروري والنظري ]
والمستحيلُ : ما لا يتصوَّرُ في العقل ثبوتُهُ ؛ إِمَّا بلا تأمل أيضاً ؛ ككونِ الواحدِ نصف الأربعةِ ، وإمَّا بعد التأمل
ككون الواحدِ سُدُسَ الاثْنَيْ عشر
؛
أمَّا تمثيلنا للمستحيل الضروري بكون الواحد نصف الأربعة : فظاهر للعام والخاص ؛ لأنَّهُ لمَّا عُرِفَ بالضرورة للجميع أن نصفَها اثنانِ . . لزمَ أَن يُعرَفَ بالضرورة انتفاء النصفية عن كل ما سواهما مِنْ واحد أو غيره
وأما تمثيلنا للمستحيل النظري بكون الواحدِ سُدُس الاثني عشر : فهو باعتبار العوام ؛ لأنَّهم قد يجهلون قبلَ التأمُّلِ أَنَّ سُدُسَها اثنانِ أو غيرهما ، فلا يعرفون ابتداءً استحالة كونِ الواحدِ سُدساً منها ، بل حتى يعرفوا أنَّ سُدُسَ الاثني عشر هو القسمُ الواحدُ مِنْ أقسامها الستة المتساوية ، والواحد ليس كذلكَ ، وإنَّما هو قسم مِنْ أقسامها الاثني عشر المتساوية ، وأمَّا بالنسبة إلى أهل الحساب فمعرفة استحالة كون الواحدِ سُدُسَ الاثني عشر ضروريةٌ ، والخَطْبُ في ذلك سهل ، ومقصودنا التقريبُ بالمثل ، والاعتراض على المثل ليسَ مِنْ أبِ المحققين ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
Definition of the necessary and theoretical impossible. The impossible: what cannot be conceived in the mind as existent; either without reflection also, like the one being half of four, or after reflection, like the one being one-sixth of twelve. As for our example for the necessary impossible by the one being half of four: it is apparent to the common and the elite; because when it is known by necessity for all that its half is two, it necessarily follows that the negation of halfness is known by necessity for everything other than them, whether one or other. And as for our example for the theoretical impossible by the one being one-sixth of twelve: it is with consideration of the common people; because they may be ignorant before reflection that its sixth is two or other than it, so they do not initially know the impossibility of the one being a sixth of it, but rather until they know that the sixth of twelve is the one part from its six equal parts, and the one is not like that, but rather it is a part from its twelve equal parts. And as for the people of calculation, the knowledge of the impossibility of the one being one-sixth of twelve is necessary. And the matter in that is easy, and our intention is approximation by example, and objecting to the example is not from the way of the investigators. And with Allah Almighty is success.
Source pages p. 138

Commentary
This passage defines the concept of the impossible (al-mustahil) and divides it into necessary (daruri) and theoretical (nazari). The impossible is that whose existence cannot be conceived in the mind. The necessary impossible is known without any reflection, such as the proposition that one is half of four—every rational person immediately knows that half of four is two, so one cannot be half of four. The theoretical impossible requires reflection, for example, that one is one-sixth of twelve. A common person might not realize this immediately; they need to think that one-sixth of twelve is two, and since one is not two, it is impossible. However, for mathematicians, this impossibility is also necessary because they already know the relationship. The author emphasizes that examples are for approximation, and objecting to the example itself is not a valid criticism.

Key Terms
المستحيل
المستحيل الضروري
المستحيل النظري
العقل
ثبوت
تأمل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think theoretical impossible is not truly impossible or is uncertain.
Correction: It is truly impossible, but requires reflection to realize; both types are equally impossible in reality.

Checkpoint
What distinguishes necessary impossible from theoretical impossible?
A) Necessary impossible is known without reflection, theoretical requires reflection.
B) Necessary impossible is about numbers, theoretical is about objects.
C) Necessary impossible is more difficult to conceive.
D) Theoretical impossible is not actually impossible.

Key Takeaway
The impossible is divided into two types: necessary (immediately known) and theoretical (known after reflection), but both are equally impossible in reality.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Are you willing to admit that some truths about Allah and His religion are immediately obvious, while others require patient reflection—and have you neglected to reflect on the latter, remaining in a state of ignorance that could be removed?
?
The theoretical impossible is still absolutely impossible; it only takes effort to see it. What false belief about your own soul, your purpose, or your Lord have you held onto simply because you never took the time to think deeply? Will you set aside time today to examine it?
?
The passage notes that for experts, what is theoretical for others becomes necessary. In your faith, are there areas where you rely on hearsay rather than seeking certain knowledge through reflection? Will you commit to becoming, like the 'people of calculation,' one who knows the truth by direct insight?

The Possible: Necessary and Theoretical
Sanusi defines the possible and distinguishes necessary from theoretical with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the possible in kalam and the distinction between necessary and theoretical possible, as illustrated by the examples of a white body and wishing for death.

Core Idea
The possible is what the mind can equally conceive as existing or not.

Sanusi now defines one of the three rational judgments—the possible (al-ja'iz)—and divides it into two types: necessary and theoretical. He uses concrete examples to show how some possibilities are known without reflection, while others require careful thought. This passage grounds the student in the basic vocabulary of possibility before moving to more complex theological arguments.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition of the possible
Read the Arabic definition: 'والجائز : ما يصح في العقل ثبوته وعدمه'. Note that it is defined by the mind's ability to conceive both existence and non-existence equally.
This is the foundational concept for the rest of the lesson.
2
Identify the two subtypes
The passage then divides the possible into two: necessary (without reflection) and theoretical (after reflection). Look for the phrases 'بلا تأمل' and 'بعد التأمل'.
The examples clarify each subtype.
3
Understand the reasoning for necessary possible
Sanusi argues that since we necessarily know the specific (e.g., existence of whiteness) by observation, the more general (the very possibility) is even more necessarily known. Follow the logical chain: 'فإذا كان الأخص ضرورياً للعقل .. فأحرى أن يكون الأعم ضرورياً.'
This is a key argument in kalam methodology.
4
Examine the theoretical possible example
Read the discussion of wishing for death. Notice how the author describes the initial assumption of impossibility and then the realization of possibility after reflection on calamities.
This shows that some possibilities require mental effort.
5
Reflect on the importance
Consider why this distinction matters for kalam: it allows us to classify statements about Allah and the world correctly, and it trains the mind to distinguish between immediate and reflective knowledge.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
تعريف الجائز الضروري والنظري ]
والجائز : ما يصح في العقل ثبوته وعدمه ؛ إما بلا تأمل ؛ ككون الجسمِ أبيضَ مثلاً ، وإِمَّا بعد التأمل ؛ كتمني الإنسان الموت مثلاً
لا شكٍّ أنَّ وجودَ البياض وعدمه للأجسام قد عرفه العقل ضرورةً بالمشاهدة ، وصحَّةُ وجودِ الشيء وعدمِهِ أعمُّ مِنْ وجودِهِ وعدمِهِ ، فإذا
كان الأخص ضرورياً للعقل .. فأحرى أن يكون الأعم ضرورياً. وأما الحكم على تمني الموتِ بالجواز النظري : فظاهر ، لكن في
حق أهل العافية الذين لم يذوقوا المصائب التي هي أشدُّ مِنَ الموتِ ويُستسهَلُ الموتُ ويُتمنَّى عندَها ، ولا خالطوا مَنْ وقع في ذلكَ ، ولا عرفوا المِحَنَ بالفكرة والتوهم ، فهؤلاء يَتوهَّمونَ ابتداءً أَنَّهُ محال أنْ يَتمنّى العاقل الموتَ لنفسه ، فإذا فكَّروا في المِحَنِ.. عرفوا أنَّ أشدُّ مِنَ الموتِ ، فحينئذ يحكمون بأنَّ تمنّي العاقل الموتَ هناك ما هو
Definition of the necessary and theoretical possible. The possible: what is valid in the mind its existence and non-existence; either without reflection, like the body being white for example, or after reflection, like a person wishing for death for example. There is no doubt that the existence and non-existence of whiteness in bodies the mind knows necessarily by observation, and the validity of existence and non-existence of a thing is more general than its existence and non-existence. So if the more specific is necessary for the mind, then the more general is more fitting to be necessary. And as for the judgment on wishing for death as theoretical possible: it is apparent, but with respect to healthy people who have not tasted calamities that are more severe than death and for which death is considered easy and is wished for at such times, and who have not mixed with those who experienced that, and have not known afflictions by thought and imagination. These initially imagine that it is impossible for a rational person to wish for death for himself. Then when they think about afflictions, they know that there is what is more severe than death. Then at that time they judge that the rational person's wishing for death there is something that is...
Source pages p. 139

Commentary
This passage defines the possible (al-ja'iz) and divides it into necessary and theoretical. The possible is that which the mind can deem feasible equally for existence and non-existence. The necessary possible is known without reflection, such as a body being white or not—this is witnessed directly. The theoretical possible requires reflection, such as a healthy person wishing for death. At first glance, one might think it impossible for a sane person to wish for death, but after considering extreme calamities worse than death, one realizes that under such circumstances, wishing for death is possible. The author argues that since the specific (e.g., whiteness existing or not) is known necessarily by observation, the more general (validity of existence and non-existence) is even more necessarily known.

Key Terms
الجائز
الجائز الضروري
الجائز النظري
صحة الثبوت والعدم
المشاهدة
تمني الموت

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: If something is possible, it must be observed empirically.
Correction: Some possibilities are known necessarily by observation, but others require reflection and reasoning.

Checkpoint
Why is wishing for death considered a theoretical possible?
A) Because it is impossible for a sane person.
B) Because it requires reflection on extreme suffering to recognize its possibility.
C) Because it is known by sense perception.
D) Because death is always wished for.

Key Takeaway
The possible (al-ja'iz) is defined by the mind's equal ability to conceive existence and non-existence, and it is divided into necessary (known without reflection) and theoretical (known after reflection).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When have you been quick to judge something as impossible in your life without stopping to reflect on the deeper realities? How can you train yourself to pause and use reflection before making a judgment about what is possible or not?
?
If the mind can conceive equally of existence and non-existence for the possible, what does that tell you about your own ability to change your perspective? Are there beliefs you hold that you have not truly examined by reflecting on both sides?
?
The healthy person initially cannot imagine wishing for death, but after reflecting on calamities, it becomes possible. What calamities in the lives of others have you not truly empathized with, and how might that empathy change what you consider possible for a person to feel?

The Rational Impossibility of Eternal Bodies and Attributes
Sanusi proves impossibility and derives two doctrinal conclusions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi argues that no body or attribute can be eternal and how that leads to affirming God's existence and the world's creation.

Core Idea
An eternal body would require a preponderator for its specific qualities, leading to a contradiction.

Sanusi presents a powerful rational argument that nothing in the universe except God can be eternal. He shows that if a body or attribute were eternal, its specific characteristics would have to be preponderant over alternatives without any cause — which is impossible. This leads to two key conclusions: the necessity of a Creator and the createdness of everything else.

Guided Path
1
Read the source passage carefully
Start by reading the Arabic passage and its translation. Pay attention to the logical flow: impossibility, absurdity, then the positive conclusions.
The argument is tightly structured — each part builds on the previous.
2
Understand the impossibility argument
Identify why an eternal body is impossible. Sanusi says it would involve a preponderance without a preponderator, which is contradictory.
Note the terms: 'jā’iz' (permissible), 'murajjiḥ' (preponderator), and the combination of equality and preponderance.
3
Identify the two conclusions
After establishing impossibility, Sanusi states that all worlds indicate two things: God's necessary existence and the temporality (creation) of all bodies and attributes.
These two conclusions are the main takeaways.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإذاً ؛ يستحيل عقلاً أن يكونَ جِرْمٌ مِنَ الأجرام أو صفةٌ مِنْ صفاتِهِ قديماً لم يسبق وجودَهُ عدمٌ ؛ لِمَا يلزمُ عليهِ مِنْ ترجُحِ وجودِ المقدارِ المخصوص الجائز على عدمِهِ المساوي له في الجواز ، وترجُحِ وجودِ صفتِهِ المخصوصة الجائزة على مقابلها بلا مُرجّح ، وذلك جمع بينَ متنافيين ؛ وهما الاستواء والرجحان ، وذلك لا يُعقَلُ . العوالم كلُّها بحدوثها ناطقة بوجوب وجودِهِ تعالى . فإذاً ؛ قد دلَّ كلُّ ما سوى مولانا تبارك وتعالى مِنْ جهةٍ مقدارِهِ المخصوص وصفتِهِ المخصوصة . . على أمرين : أحدهما : وجوبُ وجودِ المولى تبارك وتعالى ؛ ليرجح بإرادتِهِ مقدارَ كلَّ جِرْمٍ وصفته المخصوصينِ بهِ على مقابلهما ، ويُوجِدَ ما شاءَ مِنْ ذلكَ على وَفْقِ إرادتِهِ . الثاني : الحدوث لكلِّ جِرْمِ وصفاتِهِ ؛ لِمَا ثبتَ مِنْ طريقِ الجواز وجوب افتقارها للفاعل ؛ لأنَّ القديم لا يكون إلا واجباً غنياً عن الفاعل .
Thus, it is rationally impossible that any body among bodies or any attribute among its attributes be eternal, not preceded by non-existence; because from that would follow the preponderance of the existence of the specific permissible magnitude over its non-existence which is equal to it in permissibility, and the preponderance of the existence of its specific permissible attribute over its opposite without a preponderator, and that is combining two mutually exclusive things: equality and preponderance, and that is unintelligible. All worlds, by their temporality, speak of the necessity of His existence, exalted. Thus, everything other than our Lord, Blessed and Exalted, in terms of its specific magnitude and its specific attribute, indicates two things: First: the necessity of the existence of the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, so that by His will He may preponderate the magnitude of each body and its specific attribute over their opposites, and create whatever He wills of that in accordance with His will. Second: the temporality (ḥudūth) of every body and its attributes; because it has been established through the way of permissibility (jawāz) the necessity of their need for an agent (fā‘il); because the eternal (qadīm) is only necessary and independent of an agent.
Source pages p. 143

Commentary
This passage is a proof (dalīl) that no body or attribute can be eternal. Sanusi begins by stating the rational impossibility directly. He argues that if a body were eternal (not preceded by non-existence), then its specific magnitude and specific attribute would be preponderant over their opposites without a preponderator (murajjiḥ). Since the magnitude is permissible (jā’iz) – meaning it could have been otherwise – its existence over non-existence requires a preponderator. The same for the attribute. The lack of a preponderator would lead to the absurdity of combining equality (al-istiwā’) and preponderance (al-tarjīḥ) – that is, the two possibilities are equal, yet one is preponderant. This is unintelligible. Then he declares that all worlds, by their temporality, testify to the necessity of God’s existence. Thus, everything other than God indicates two things: 1) the necessity of God’s existence, because He preponderates by His will the specific magnitudes and attributes over their opposites, and creates them as He wills; 2) the temporality of every body and attribute, because they are permissible (jā’iz) and hence need an agent, while the eternal is self-sufficient. So the proof establishes both the existence of a preponderating agent (God) and the createdness of the world.

Key Terms
يستحيل
قديم
حادث
مقدار
صفة
جواز
ترجح
مرجح
إرادة
فاعل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think the proof assumes what it tries to prove, but it only assumes that magnitudes/attributes are permissible (contingent).
Correction: The proof does not assume the conclusion; it only posits that a body's quantity and attribute are permissible and thus require a preponderator.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why is it impossible for a body to be eternal?
A) Because all bodies eventually perish.
B) Because its specific magnitude and attribute would require a preponderator, lacking which leads to contradiction.
C) Because the Quran says so.
D) Because the body would need to be created.

Key Takeaway
The impossibility of an eternal body or attribute leads to affirming God as the necessary Preponderator and the createdness of all else.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If every detail of your body—your height, your abilities, your very existence—is contingent and requires God's will to preponderate it over its absence, do you truly live as though you are constantly dependent on Him? What would change in your daily routine if you woke up each moment aware that God is preponderating your existence against non-existence?
?
The passage says all worlds speak of God's necessity. Does your own body speak to you of God? When you look in the mirror, do you see a self-sufficient being or a constant miracle of divine preservation? How will you respond the next time you are tempted to feel self-sufficient?
?
The logical contradiction in the proof shows that without God, even the simplest feature of the world would be absurd. How does this rational demand for a Preponderator confront your moments of doubt or forgetfulness of God? Will you let this proof anchor your certainty when your emotions waver?

Rational Judgments
beginner
5 min
Proof of Temporality of Accidents and Bodies
Sanusi defines accidents and bodies, then proves their temporality through change.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi proves the temporality of accidents by direct observation and of bodies by their necessary connection to accidents.

Core Idea
Accidents are observed to change; bodies are temporal because they cannot exist without accidents.

This lesson introduces the foundational proof for the temporality of all contingent things. Sanusi begins by defining accidents and bodies, then demonstrates that accidents are always subject to change, which proves they are created. Since bodies always accompany accidents, they too must be temporally originated. This argument is central to establishing that the universe is not eternal.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once to get the flow, then the translation. Note the distinction between accidents (direct observation) and bodies (necessary concomitance).
The bracketed part is a heading summarizing the argument.
2
Distinguish observation from concomitance
Notice that accidents are proven temporal 'bil-mushahada' (by observation), while bodies are proven 'bil-mulazama' (by necessary concomitance) because they cannot exist without accidents.
3
Analyze the proof based on change
The proof rests on change (taghayyur). Accidents are never free from change, either actual or potential. Change necessitates temporality and dependence on an agent, and contradicts eternity.
Eternal things are necessary and do not accept change.
4
Reflect on the implication for bodies
Since bodies are always accompanied by accidents, and accidents are temporal, bodies must also be temporal. This completes the proof that all contingent things are originated.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ الصفات الأعراض حادثة بالمشاهدة ، والأجرام حادثة بالملازمة للأعراض ] قوله : ( ودليل حدوثها : لزومُها لِمَا يَفْتَقِرُ إلى المُخصص ) يعني : أن الحوادث تنقسم إلى : أجرام ، وأعراض ؛ وهي التي تتصف بها الأجرام ، ولا شك أن الأعراض لا يفارقها التغيرُ حصولاً ، أو قبولاً إن قدرنا بقاءَها ، والتغيرُ يستلزم الحدوث والافتقار إلى الفاعل ، وينافي القِدَمَ ؛ إذ القديم لا يكون إلا واجباً ، فلا يقبلُ التغير
[Attributes: Accidents are temporally originated by observation, and bodies are temporally originated by necessary concomitance with accidents.] His statement: 'And the proof of their temporality: their being inseparable from what is in need of a determiner' means: contingent things are divided into bodies and accidents; and accidents are what bodies are qualified by, and there is no doubt that accidents are never free from change, either in actual occurrence or in receptivity if we suppose their permanence. Change necessitates temporality and need for an agent, and contradicts eternity; for the eternal is only necessary, so it does not accept change.
Source pages p. 146

Commentary
This passage begins the section on attributes (al-sifat). It provides a definition and proof for the temporality (huduth) of accidents and bodies. The author states that accidents are observed to be temporally originated (haditha bil-mushahada), and bodies are temporally originated by necessary concomitance with accidents (bil-mulazama lil-a‘rad). The proof is that accidents are inseparable from change—either actual change or potential change if they could persist. Change necessitates temporality and dependence on an agent, and it is incompatible with eternity, because the eternal is necessary and does not accept change. Since bodies are the subjects of accidents, they must also be temporal.

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that bodies could be eternal while accidents change, allowing an eternal universe with changing properties.
Correction: Bodies are inseparable from accidents. Since accidents are temporal and change, bodies must also be temporal. An eternal body would be unchanging, but bodies continuously possess changing accidents.

Checkpoint
How do we know that bodies are temporally originated?
A) They are observed to be temporal directly.
B) They are inseparable from accidents, which are temporal.
C) They are necessarily eternal.
D) They change by themselves.

Key Takeaway
The temporality of accidents is observed directly, while the temporality of bodies is proven by their inseparable connection to changing accidents.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If every accident in your life—your health, mood, circumstances—is observed to change, does that not prove your absolute need for a Creator at every moment? How will you consciously acknowledge that dependence today?
?
The proof shows that because you are always changing, you cannot be self-sufficient. What is one area of your life where you still act as if you are independent of God, and what specific step will you take to surrender it to Him?
?
You have just learned that the eternal is necessary and does not change. How does this truth expose the futility of clinging to anything in this changing world? What must you transfer your hope to instead?

The Impossibility of Preponderance Without a Preponderant
Sanusi states the impossibility of preponderance without a preponderant in specific determinations.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why every specific determination of a contingent thing (time, place, direction, magnitude, quality) requires a preponderant cause.

Core Idea
Nothing in creation is arbitrary; every specific feature demands a preponderant.

Sanusi now presents a crucial argument: the principle of preponderance. He concisely lists several aspects of contingent things—their time, place, direction, size, and quality—and asserts that without a preponderant, their specific determination is impossible. This argument is foundational in kalam for proving the need for a voluntary agent (Allah) behind the precise details of the universe.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the original Arabic and its literal translation. Notice the concise style and the three main clauses.
The passage begins with 'and acceptance without a preponderant' referring to the existence itself.
2
Identify the specific aspects listed
List the five aspects: acceptance (existence), specific time, specific place, specific direction, specific magnitude, and specific quality (the last two only if bodies).
Each aspect is paired with its opposite to highlight the need for a preponderant.
3
Understand the logical principle
For each aspect, if it could have been otherwise, something must tip the balance—that something is the preponderant (muarjiḥ). Without it, the specific determination is impossible.
This shows that contingency is not enough; a cause is needed for actualization.
4
Reflect on the theological implication
This chain of preponderants cannot go on infinitely; it must end with a first preponderant, which is Allah. Thus, this argument supports the existence of a wise and powerful Creator.
Avoid the misconception that this leads to an infinite regress.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والقبول بلا مُرجّح ، وكذلك يستحيل ترجُحُ زمن وجودها المخصوص ومكانها المخصوص وجهتها المخصوصة على ما يقابلها بلا مُرجّح وكذلكَ يستحيلُ ترجُحُ مقدارها المخصوص وصفتها المخصوصة إن كانَتْ أجراماً على ما يقابلها مِنْ غيرِ مُرجح موجود .
And acceptance without a preponderant, and likewise it is impossible for the preponderance of the specific time of its existence, its specific place, its specific direction over what opposes them without a preponderant, and likewise it is impossible for the preponderance of its specific magnitude and its specific quality, if they are bodies, over what opposes them without an existing preponderant.
Source pages p. 146

Commentary
This is a proof (burhan) of the necessity of a specific determiner (mukhaṣṣiṣ) for contingent things. The author lists several aspects: time, place, direction, magnitude, and quality. For each, if a thing has a specific attribute, the possibility of it being otherwise means a preponderant (muarjiḥ) is needed to tip the balance. Without it, the specific determination is impossible. This is a key argument in kalam for the existence of a voluntary agent.

Key Terms
ترجح
مرجح
مخصص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: This argument implies an infinite chain of preponderants.
Correction: No, the argument only proves that each specific determination requires a preponderant. The chain stops at a first preponderant, which is uncaused and identified as Allah.

Checkpoint
Which of the following is mentioned as requiring a preponderant?
A) Time only
B) Place and direction only
C) Magnitude and quality only
D) All of the above

Key Takeaway
Every specific determination of a contingent thing—including its existence, time, place, direction, size, and quality—depends on a preponderant cause.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Consider the specific moment of your birth, the place, the family you were born into. This lesson teaches that every detail was determined by a preponderant. Is there any detail of your existence you can honestly attribute to chance? What does that mean for your relationship with the One who chose it?
?
You have a specific body with its own size and qualities. Are you grateful to the Preponderant for making you exactly as you are, or do you resent His determination? Can you list at least two specific physical attributes that you have and thank Allah for them?
?
If every specific aspect of your life—time, place, direction, size, quality—requires a preponderant, then what do you say about your choices? When you sin, do you think it's a product of chance? Or does this lesson show you that even your actions are determined by a preponderant? How does this affect your view of accountability?

The Proof of Origination: Change and Specificity
Sanusi explains the proof of origination via dependence on a determiner.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the constant change in accidents and the specific determination of bodies prove that all contingent things are originated and depend on a Creator.

Core Idea
Change and specificity demand a Creator.

The proof from origination is a cornerstone of Islamic theology. In this passage, Sanusi explains that both accidents (qualities) and bodies (substances) bear signs of being created: accidents are always changing, and bodies have specific sizes and places. Both depend on a determiner. This concise argument shows that the entire world is originated.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Identify the two categories
The passage divides contingent things into bodies and accidents. Find how each is defined and how the argument for origination is applied to each.
Accidents are qualities of bodies; bodies are substances.
3
Understand the proof for accidents
Sanusi argues that change never leaves accidents, whether actual or potential. Change entails origination and dependence on an agent, and contradicts pre-eternity.
Pre-eternal must be necessary and unchanging.
4
Understand the proof for bodies
Bodies are always accompanied by changing attributes (which need an agent) and specific magnitudes/places (which need a specific determiner). Thus bodies also depend on an agent.
Specific determination implies a determiner.
5
Conclude the argument
The conclusion is that all worlds (all contingent things) are not free from what makes them need an agent, so all are originated.
This is a universal proof for the creation of the universe.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قوله : ( ودليل حدوثها : لزومُها لِمَا يَفْتَقِرُ إلى المُخصص ) يعني : أن الحوادث تنقسم إلى : أجرام ، وأعراض ؛ وهي التي تتصف بها الأجرام ، ولا شك أن الأعراض لا يفارقها التغيرُ حصولاً ، أو قبولاً إن قدرنا بقاءَها ، والتغيرُ يستلزم الحدوث والافتقار إلى الفاعل ، وينافي القِدَمَ ؛ إذ القديم لا يكون إلا واجباً ، فلا يقبلُ التغير. وأما الأجرامُ : فملازمة للصفاتِ المتغيرة المفتقرة إلى الفاعل وملازمة للمقادير والأمكنة المخصوصة المفتقرة إلى المخصص . فإذاً ؛ جميعُ العوالم لا تنفتُ عمَّا يُحوِجُها إلى الفاعل ، فتكونُ كلُّها حادثة.
His saying: 'And the proof of their origination: their dependence on what requires a specific determiner' means: that contingent things are divided into bodies and accidents; and accidents are those with which bodies are qualified, and there is no doubt that change does not leave accidents, whether in actuality or in potentiality if we suppose their permanence, and change entails origination and dependence on an agent, and contradicts pre-eternity; because the pre-eternal is nothing but necessary, so it does not accept change. As for bodies: they are always with changing attributes that depend on an agent, and always with specific magnitudes and places that depend on a specific determiner. Therefore, all worlds are not free from what makes them need an agent, so all of them are originated.
Source pages p. 146

Commentary
This is an explanatory passage (sharh) of a proof for the origination (huduth) of accidents and bodies. The author explains that contingent things (al-hawadith) are of two kinds: bodies (ajram) and accidents (a'rad). Accidents are the qualities of bodies, and they are constantly changing. Change (taghayyur) necessarily implies origination (huduth) and dependence on an agent (fa'il), and it is incompatible with pre-eternity (qidam) because the pre-eternal must be necessary and unchanging. Bodies, on the other hand, are always accompanied by changing accidents (which depend on an agent) and also by specific magnitudes and places (which require a specific determiner). Therefore, everything that exists (the entire world) is dependent on an agent and thus originated. This is a classic proof from change and specific determination to the creation of the universe.

Key Terms
حدوث
أجرام
أعراض
تغير
فاعل
قديم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: This proof relies on the impossibility of an infinite regress.
Correction: The proof actually relies on the nature of change and specificity, not on infinite regress.

Checkpoint
What is the proof of origination for accidents according to the passage?
A) They are inseparable from bodies
B) They are accompanied by change
C) They are created by God
D) They are pre-eternal

Key Takeaway
All contingent things (accidents and bodies) bear signs of being created through change and specificity, proving they need a Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When was the last time you experienced a sudden change in your circumstances—good or bad? Did you recognize in that moment that change itself is a sign that you are not eternal and that you depend on a Creator who does not change? How will you train your heart to see the hand of the Agent behind every shift in your life?
?
Look around you right now. Every object has a specific size, shape, and location. This lesson teaches that these specific determinations demand a specific Determiner. Have you ever thanked the Determiner for placing you exactly where you are? What would it mean for you to live today with the conviction that nothing in your life is random, but every detail is chosen by the Creator?
?
Accidents like your thoughts and feelings are constantly changing. This constant flux is a reminder that you are contingent and needy. Are you honestly comfortable with your dependence on God, or do you try to ignore it? What practical step can you take tomorrow to acknowledge your need for the Creator in a specific area where you typically rely on yourself?

Proving God's Eternity and Perpetual Existence from the Contingency of Bodies
Sanusi proves the necessity of God's qidam and baqa by demonstrating the contingency of all bodies.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi proves that God must be eternal and perpetual (qidam and baqa) by first demonstrating the contingency of all bodies and then showing that the alternative leads to impossible circularity or infinite regress.

Core Idea
The impossibility of infinite regress forces us to conclude that God is eternal and without end.

In this passage, Sanusi presents a classic Ash'ari cosmological argument. He begins by arguing that all bodies—the entire physical universe—are contingent because they depend on changing attributes and specific quantities/places. Since they are contingent, they require a maker. He then shows that the Maker Himself cannot be contingent, otherwise we would fall into an infinite regress. Therefore, the Maker must be eternal and perpetual. Let's examine this argument step by step.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the concise wording; each phrase carries a lot of meaning.
2
Understand the contingency argument for bodies
Sanusi says bodies are inseparable from changing attributes and specific quantities/places. These need a maker and a particularizer. Therefore all worlds are contingent (haditha). Make sure you see the logical chain.
This is the first premise: all bodies are contingent.
3
Grasp the reductio ad absurdum for God's contingency
Sanusi argues that if God were not eternal and perpetual, He would need a maker and thus be contingent. This leads to either circularity or infinite regress, both impossible. Therefore God must be eternal and perpetual.
The impossibility of regress is the key move.
4
Learn the definitions of qidam and baqa
Sanusi defines qidam as not being susceptible to non-existence in pre-eternity (al-azal), and baqa' as not being susceptible to non-existence in perpetuity (ma la yazal).
These definitions are given after the proof.
5
Reflect on the role of circularity and infinite regress
Ask yourself why circularity and infinite regress are impossible. This is a foundational principle in kalam: they are intellectually absurd.
This connects to the broader theme of tawhid.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما الأجرامُ : فملازمة للصفاتِ المتغيرة المفتقرة إلى الفاعل وملازمة للمقادير والأمكنة المخصوصة المفتقرة إلى المخصص . فإذاً ؛ جميعُ العوالم لا تنفك عما يحوجها إلى الفاعل ، فتكونُ كلُّها حادثة. ويجبُ له تعالى القِدَمُ والبقاء ، وإلا كان محتاجاً إلى الفاعل ، فيكون حادثاً ، فيجبُ لَهُ مِنَ العجزِ ما وجبَ لسائرِ الحوادثِ ، بل يكونُ حينَئِذٍ وجوده مستحيلاً ؛ لِمَا يلزم على تقديرِ حدوثِهِ مِنَ الدور أو التسلسل المستحيلين. بطلان الدور والتسلسل دليل على قدمِهِ تعالى ثم على بقائه. يجب له تبارك وتعالى أن يكون غير قابل للعدم في الأزل ؛ وهو معنى القِدَمِ (۱) ، ولا فيما لا يزالُ ؛ وهو معنى البقاء (۲) ؛
And as for bodies: they are inseparable from changing attributes that need a maker, and inseparable from specific quantities and places that need a particularizer. So then, all worlds are not free from what makes them need the maker, so all of them are contingent. And it is necessary for Him, exalted, eternity and perpetual existence; otherwise He would be in need of a maker, thus He would be contingent, and then there would follow for Him from incapacity what followed for other contingents; rather, at that time His existence would be impossible, due to what would be entailed upon the supposition of His contingency of circularity or infinite regress, which are impossible. The impossibility of circularity and infinite regress is a proof for His eternity, then for His perpetual existence. He, blessed and exalted, must be not susceptible to non-existence in pre-eternity—that is the meaning of qidam (1)—and not in perpetuity—that is the meaning of baqa' (2).
Source pages p. 147

Commentary
This passage presents a cosmological argument for God's eternity (qidam) and perpetual existence (baqa'). First, Sanusi argues for the contingency of all bodies (ajrām). He states that bodies are necessarily (ملازمة) associated with two kinds of changing properties: (1) attributes that are subject to change, which require a maker (fāʿil), and (2) specific quantities and places, which require a particularizer (mukhaṣṣiṣ). Since bodies cannot be free of these properties, they are in need of an external cause, and therefore all of them are contingent (ḥāditha). The argument is a classic kalam proof from contingency to a necessary existent. Then Sanusi argues that God must possess the attributes of eternity and perpetual existence. If He were not eternal and perpetual, He would also be in need of a maker and thus be contingent. This would imply that He shares the same incapacity as other contingent beings, and even more: His existence would be impossible because postulating His contingency would lead to either circular reasoning (dawr) or infinite regress (tasalsul), both of which are impossible. Therefore, the impossibility of circularity and regress directly proves that God is eternal and perpetual. Finally, Sanusi defines qidam as not being susceptible to non-existence in pre-eternity (al-azal), meaning that God's existence has no beginning, and baqa' as not being susceptible to non-existence in perpetuity (mā lā yazāl), meaning that God's existence has no end. The passage follows the standard Ashʿarī method of establishing God's attributes via rational proof from the contingency of the world.

Key Terms
أجرام
صفات متغيرة
فاعل
مخصص
حادثة
قدم
بقاء
دور
تسلسل
أزل
لا يزال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The argument is circular because it assumes God's existence to prove His attributes.
Correction: Sanusi does not assume God's existence; he proves the necessity of qidam and baqa from the impossibility of regress, which is a logical consequence of contingency.

Checkpoint
What does Sanusi prove by the impossibility of circularity and infinite regress?
A) The contingency of bodies
B) The necessity of God's qidam and baqa
C) The definition of qidam
D) The existence of a particularizer

Key Takeaway
God's eternity and perpetual existence are necessary rational consequences of the impossibility of infinite regress.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
All bodies, including your own, are contingent and depend on a Maker every moment. How often do you live as though your existence is self-sufficient? What would change if you truly internalized that every breath is sustained by the Eternal?
?
The impossibility of circularity means there can be no infinite chain of causes—there must be a First Cause. Have you ever tried to find ultimate meaning or security in something finite? How does that lead to a dead end, just like circular reasoning?
?
God is not susceptible to non-existence in pre-eternity or perpetuity. You, however, are destined for non-existence after a short life. Are you preparing for that end in a way that connects you to the One who has no end?
