Rational Judgments
intermediate
7 min
Impossibility of an Infinite Chain of Created Powers
Sanusi proves that created power and will cannot exist due to infinite regress or circularity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why created (contingent) powers and wills cannot be the cause of other contingent events, due to the impossibility of infinite regress or circularity.

Core Idea
Contingent powers cannot cause other contingent powers without leading to impossibility.

Sanusi presents a powerful argument against the idea that created powers and wills can be self-sufficient. He shows that any attempt to explain contingent events through a chain of created powers leads to either circularity or infinite regress, both of which are rationally impossible. This forces us to recognize that the true cause must be a necessary being.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the logical structure: an assumption, two cases, and a conclusion.
The passage uses a conditional structure with 'if...then'.
2
Analyze the two cases
Identify the two scenarios Sanusi presents: when the chain of created powers stops (circularity) and when it continues infinitely (infinite regress). Ask yourself why both are considered impossible.
Remember that circularity means the first depends on itself, regress means no ultimate cause.
3
Understand the conclusion
Since the assumption (that every created power requires another) leads to impossibility, Sanusi concludes that created powers cannot be the cause of contingent events. Therefore, the cause must be necessarily existent.
This argument is a proof by contradiction, a common method in kalam.
4
Reflect on the broader implication
Consider why this argument is important for Islamic theology: it demonstrates that only a necessary being (Allah) can be the ultimate source of creation.
This connects to the concept of wajib al-wujud (necessary existence).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
على قدرة وإرادة أُخريين، ثمَّ هلم جرّاً، فإن وقف العدد لزم الدورُ وإن لم يقف أبداً لزم التسلسلُ، وكلاهما مستحيل، وملزوم المستحيل مستحيل، فيكون وجود القدرة والإرادة الحادثتين مستحيلاً، كيف وكلُّ حادثٍ تَوقَّفَ وجوده عليهما؟! فلزمَ ألا يتأتّى بهما الإحداث والتخصيص حتى تكونا واجبتي الوجودِ
upon another power and will, and so on ad infinitum. If the number stops, circularity (dawr) follows, and if it never stops, infinite regress (tasalsul) follows, and both are impossible. The necessary of the impossible is impossible, so the existence of created power and will is impossible. How, while every contingent event's existence depends on them?! So it necessarily follows that bringing about and specification cannot be achieved through them unless they are necessarily existent.
Source pages p. 169

Commentary
This passage is a proof by contradiction against the possibility of created (contingent) powers and wills being the cause of other contingent events. Sanusi assumes a chain: every created power and will requires another created power and will to bring it into existence. He then considers two cases: either the chain stops at some finite number, which would mean the first in the chain is self-caused (circularity, dawr), or it continues infinitely (tasalsul). Both are impossible. Since the premise leads to the impossible, the assumption that such created powers and wills exist is false. Therefore, the power and will that cause contingent events must be necessarily existent (wajib al-wujud), i.e., uncreated and divine.

Key Terms
قدرة (qudra)
إرادة (irada)
حادث (hadith)
تسلسل (tasalsul)
دور (dawr)
واجب الوجود (wajib al-wujud)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that the argument only applies to physical causation, not to attributes like power and will.
Correction: Sanusi explicitly extends the argument to the very attributes of power and will, showing that contingent powers cannot be self-sufficient even in their own existence.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, if the chain of created powers stops, what follows?
A) Circularity (dawr)
B) Infinite regress (tasalsul)
C) Both are impossible
D) The chain is valid

Key Takeaway
Created powers and wills cannot be the cause of other contingent events because any chain of contingents leads to rational impossibility; the ultimate cause must be necessarily existent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
This argument proves that no created power or will can be self-sufficient. When you rely on your own planning and will to achieve your goals, do you acknowledge that even that ability is a gift from the Necessarily Existent? How often do you pause to thank the Creator for every moment of existence and choice?
?
The chain of causes must stop at Allah. Think of a recent decision you made. Can you trace back the desires and abilities that led to that decision? Do you see that at every step you are dependent on Allah's creation and permission? How does this realisation change the way you approach your next decision?
?
If every created will depends on another cause, then your free will is not independent but is constantly sustained by Allah. Does this comfort you or trouble you? What would change in your life if you truly believed that your will is not your own, but a gift that could be taken away at any moment?

Rational Judgments
beginner
5 min
Rational Proof Necessary for Core Divine Attributes
Sanusi defines the first category of beliefs requiring rational proof and explains the circularity that would result from using scriptural proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why certain beliefs about Allah, such as His existence and power, can only be known through rational proof and cannot be proven by scripture alone.

Core Idea
Rational proof is the foundation; scripture depends on it, not the other way around.

Sanusi begins by distinguishing beliefs that require rational proof. These are the truths upon which the very credibility of miracles rests. If you tried to prove these by scripture, you would end up in circular reasoning. This lesson unpacks that crucial distinction.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage for the first category
Read the Arabic and translation for the first category. Notice that it includes specific attributes like existence, power, will, knowledge, and life.
2
Understand the circularity argument
Follow the logic: scripture requires a truthful messenger, which requires a miracle, which requires these attributes. Thus using scripture would be circular.
Circular reasoning is invalid in logic.
3
Reflect on why this matters
Think about how this establishes the need for rational theology (kalam) as a foundation for faith.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اعلم : أنَّ عقائد الإيمانِ تنقسم إلى ثلاثة أقسام : الأول : ما لا يصح أن يُعلَمَ إلا بالدليل العقلي ؛ وهو كلُّ ما تتوقَّفُ عليه دلالة المعجزة ؛ كوجودِهِ تعالى وقدرته وإرادته وعلمه وحياته ؛ فإِنَّهُ لَوِ اسْتُدِلَّ على هذا القسم بالدليل الشرعي وهو مُتوقفٌ على صدقِ الرسولِ المُتوقِّفِ على دلالة المعجزة لزم الدور كل القسم الثاني : ما يصح أن يُستدل عليه بالدليل الشرعي ؛ وهو ما لا تتوقَّفُ عليهِ دلالة المعجزة ؛ كالسمع والبصر والكلام ، والبعث وأحوال الآخرة جملة وتفصيلاً . الثالث : ما اختُلِفَ فيهِ بالتردُّدِ فيهِ : هل هو مِنَ القسم الأولِ ، أو مِنَ القسم الثاني ؛ كالوحدانية ؛ فإنَّهُ اختُلِفَ فيها : هل يكفي فيها الدليل السمعي ؛ بناءً على عدم توقفِ دلالة المعجزة عليها في علم الناظرِ وإِنْ تَوقَّفَ وجودُ المعجزة عليها في نفس الأمر ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الفعل مع وجودِ الشريكِ ، أو لا بدَّ فيها مِنَ الدليل العقلي ؛ نظراً إلى توقف دلالةِ المعجزة على صحة وجودِ المعجزة المتوقفة على الوحدانيةِ ؛ لأنَّ المعجزة فعل ، والفعلُ يستحيل وجوده على تقدير الاثنينيَّة في الألوهية ، والمتوقفُ على المتوقفِ على الشيء متوقِّفٌ على ذلك الشيء
Know that the beliefs of faith are divided into three sections: First: that which is not valid to be known except by rational proof; and it is everything upon which the indication of a miracle depends; such as His existence, power, will, knowledge, and life; For if one were to argue for this section by scriptural proof, which depends on the truthfulness of the messenger, which depends on the indication of a miracle, then circularity would necessarily occur. all Second: that which is valid to be argued for by scriptural proof; and it is that upon which the indication of a miracle does not depend; such as hearing, sight, speech, resurrection, and the states of the afterlife in general and in detail. Third: that concerning which there is disagreement, with hesitation: whether it belongs to the first section or the second section; such as oneness (al-waḥdāniyya); for there is disagreement concerning it: Is auditory proof sufficient for it, based on the non-dependence of the indication of a miracle upon it in the knowledge of the observer, even if the existence of a miracle depends on it in reality, due to the impossibility of an act existing with the existence of a partner; or must there be rational proof for it, considering the dependence of the indication of a miracle on the correctness of the existence of the miracle, which depends on oneness? Because the miracle is an act, and an act is impossible in existence on the assumption of duality in divinity, and that which depends on that which depends on a thing is itself dependent on that thing.
Source pages p. 174

Commentary
This passage is a classification of beliefs (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) into three divisions based on the type of evidence needed to establish them. The author begins by stating the division and then explains each category. The first category comprises beliefs that can only be known through rational proof (dalīl ʿaqlī), specifically those truths upon which the very indication of a miracle (dalālat al-muʿjiza) depends. Examples are God's existence, power, will, knowledge, and life. The reason is that if one tried to prove these using scriptural proof (dalīl sharʿī), which depends on the messenger's truthfulness, which itself depends on a miracle, there would be circularity (dawr). The second category consists of beliefs that can be known through scriptural proof, namely those not needed for the indication of a miracle, such as God's hearing, sight, speech, resurrection, and details of the afterlife. The third category is a disputed one, such as God's oneness (al-waḥdāniyya): some say auditory proof suffices because the miracle's indication does not depend on it in the observer's knowledge, while others require rational proof because the miracle's existence depends on oneness in reality—an act cannot occur with a partner in divinity. The passage concludes with the logical principle that what depends on what depends on a thing is itself dependent on that thing, reinforcing the need for rational proof for oneness according to the second opinion.

Key Terms
دليل عقلي
دليل شرعي
دلالة المعجزة
الدور

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that all religious beliefs can be proven by scripture alone.
Correction: This passage shows that core beliefs like God's existence cannot be proven by scripture without circularity; they require rational proof.

Checkpoint
Why does the first category of beliefs require rational proof rather than scriptural proof?
A) Because scriptural proof is weaker.
B) Because using scriptural proof would lead to circularity.
C) Because these beliefs are not mentioned in scripture.
D) Because they are sensory beliefs.

Key Takeaway
Core divine attributes must be established by rational proof to avoid circular reasoning.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you try to convince yourself or others that Allah exists, do you instinctively reach for a verse from the Quran or a rational argument? What does this lesson reveal about the sufficiency of that instinct, and are you willing to change your approach?
?
Have you ever held a belief about Allah's power or knowledge purely because you heard it in a sermon, without personally verifying its rational foundation? Would your faith survive if you had no rational proof?
?
This passage claims that rational proof for God's attributes is not optional but necessary to avoid logical flaws. Does this make you uncomfortable, as if reason is being elevated above revelation? How can you reconcile the necessity of reason with your trust in scripture?

Scriptural Proof Sufficient for Derived Matters
Sanusi defines the second category of beliefs that are validly proven by scriptural proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what kinds of beliefs can be proven by scripture alone and why they do not require rational proof.

Core Idea
Some beliefs about God and the afterlife can be known through scripture without needing rational proof.

After establishing what requires rational proof, Sanusi now turns to the second category: beliefs that can be proven by scripture. These are matters that do not affect the credibility of miracles. This lesson explains that category and its examples.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage for the second category
Read the Arabic and translation for the second category. Note the examples: hearing, sight, speech, resurrection, and details of the afterlife.
2
Understand the criterion
The key is that these matters do not serve as prerequisites for the indication of a miracle. So scripture is sufficient.
They can still be known rationally, but it's not necessary.
3
Contrast with the first category
Compare this to the first category to see the difference in epistemological status.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اعلم : أنَّ عقائد الإيمانِ تنقسم إلى ثلاثة أقسام : الأول : ما لا يصح أن يُعلَمَ إلا بالدليل العقلي ؛ وهو كلُّ ما تتوقَّفُ عليه دلالة المعجزة ؛ كوجودِهِ تعالى وقدرته وإرادته وعلمه وحياته ؛ فإِنَّهُ لَوِ اسْتُدِلَّ على هذا القسم بالدليل الشرعي وهو مُتوقفٌ على صدقِ الرسولِ المُتوقِّفِ على دلالة المعجزة لزم الدور كل القسم الثاني : ما يصح أن يُستدل عليه بالدليل الشرعي ؛ وهو ما لا تتوقَّفُ عليهِ دلالة المعجزة ؛ كالسمع والبصر والكلام ، والبعث وأحوال الآخرة جملة وتفصيلاً . الثالث : ما اختُلِفَ فيهِ بالتردُّدِ فيهِ : هل هو مِنَ القسم الأولِ ، أو مِنَ القسم الثاني ؛ كالوحدانية ؛ فإنَّهُ اختُلِفَ فيها : هل يكفي فيها الدليل السمعي ؛ بناءً على عدم توقفِ دلالة المعجزة عليها في علم الناظرِ وإِنْ تَوقَّفَ وجودُ المعجزة عليها في نفس الأمر ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الفعل مع وجودِ الشريكِ ، أو لا بدَّ فيها مِنَ الدليل العقلي ؛ نظراً إلى توقف دلالةِ المعجزة على صحة وجودِ المعجزة المتوقفة على الوحدانيةِ ؛ لأنَّ المعجزة فعل ، والفعلُ يستحيل وجوده على تقدير الاثنينيَّة في الألوهية ، والمتوقفُ على المتوقفِ على الشيء متوقِّفٌ على ذلك الشيء
Know that the beliefs of faith are divided into three sections: First: that which is not valid to be known except by rational proof; and it is everything upon which the indication of a miracle depends; such as His existence, power, will, knowledge, and life; For if one were to argue for this section by scriptural proof, which depends on the truthfulness of the messenger, which depends on the indication of a miracle, then circularity would necessarily occur. all Second: that which is valid to be argued for by scriptural proof; and it is that upon which the indication of a miracle does not depend; such as hearing, sight, speech, resurrection, and the states of the afterlife in general and in detail. Third: that concerning which there is disagreement, with hesitation: whether it belongs to the first section or the second section; such as oneness (al-waḥdāniyya); for there is disagreement concerning it: Is auditory proof sufficient for it, based on the non-dependence of the indication of a miracle upon it in the knowledge of the observer, even if the existence of a miracle depends on it in reality, due to the impossibility of an act existing with the existence of a partner; or must there be rational proof for it, considering the dependence of the indication of a miracle on the correctness of the existence of the miracle, which depends on oneness? Because the miracle is an act, and an act is impossible in existence on the assumption of duality in divinity, and that which depends on that which depends on a thing is itself dependent on that thing.
Source pages p. 174

Commentary
This passage is a classification of beliefs (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) into three divisions based on the type of evidence needed to establish them. The author begins by stating the division and then explains each category. The first category comprises beliefs that can only be known through rational proof (dalīl ʿaqlī), specifically those truths upon which the very indication of a miracle (dalālat al-muʿjiza) depends. Examples are God's existence, power, will, knowledge, and life. The reason is that if one tried to prove these using scriptural proof (dalīl sharʿī), which depends on the messenger's truthfulness, which itself depends on a miracle, there would be circularity (dawr). The second category consists of beliefs that can be known through scriptural proof, namely those not needed for the indication of a miracle, such as God's hearing, sight, speech, resurrection, and details of the afterlife. The third category is a disputed one, such as God's oneness (al-waḥdāniyya): some say auditory proof suffices because the miracle's indication does not depend on it in the observer's knowledge, while others require rational proof because the miracle's existence depends on oneness in reality—an act cannot occur with a partner in divinity. The passage concludes with the logical principle that what depends on what depends on a thing is itself dependent on that thing, reinforcing the need for rational proof for oneness according to the second opinion.

Key Terms

دلالة المعجزة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that all beliefs about God require rational proof.
Correction: This passage shows that many beliefs, such as God's hearing and sight, can be taken from scripture.

Checkpoint
What is the criterion for a belief to be in the second category (provable by scripture)?
A) It must be mentioned in the Qur'an.
B) It must not be a prerequisite for the indication of a miracle.
C) It must be about the afterlife.
D) It must be rationally unknowable.

Key Takeaway
Beliefs that do not affect the miracle's indication can be proven by scripture alone.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you come across an attribute of God like hearing or sight in scripture, do you find yourself needing to rationalize it before accepting it, or can you submit to the text? What does your response reveal about your reliance on reason versus revelation?
?
Is there a specific detail about the afterlife (e.g., the nature of paradise or hell) that you struggle to accept? Are you demanding a rational explanation for it, or can you entrust it to the authority of scripture? What would it take for you to fully embrace that belief?
?
The lesson teaches that certain beliefs can be accepted on scriptural proof alone because they do not affect the logical foundation of miracles. Does your heart rest in that confidence, or do you feel the need to verify everything intellectually? What might be holding you back from trusting revelation more deeply?

The Disputed Case of Divine Oneness
Sanusi presents the disputed case of divine oneness and the two opinions on the type of evidence required.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why divine oneness (tawhid) is a disputed case and the arguments for requiring rational proof versus scriptural proof.

Core Idea
Scholars disagree whether oneness requires rational proof or can be known by scripture.

The third category is a disputed one: divine oneness. Some argue that scripture is sufficient because the miracle's indication does not depend on oneness in the observer's knowledge. Others insist on rational proof because in reality, miracles depend on oneness. This lesson examines both sides.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage for the third category
Read the Arabic and translation for the disputed case of oneness. Note the two opinions and the reasoning given.
2
Analyze the first opinion
The first opinion says scriptural proof suffices because the miracle's indication does not depend on oneness in the observer's knowledge.
This is a subjective epistemological stance.
3
Analyze the second opinion
The second opinion requires rational proof because the miracle's existence in reality depends on oneness. The logical principle: what depends on what depends on a thing is itself dependent.
This is an objective ontological stance.
4
Reflect on the significance
Understand why this disagreement matters for the foundations of theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اعلم : أنَّ عقائد الإيمانِ تنقسم إلى ثلاثة أقسام : الأول : ما لا يصح أن يُعلَمَ إلا بالدليل العقلي ؛ وهو كلُّ ما تتوقَّفُ عليه دلالة المعجزة ؛ كوجودِهِ تعالى وقدرته وإرادته وعلمه وحياته ؛ فإِنَّهُ لَوِ اسْتُدِلَّ على هذا القسم بالدليل الشرعي وهو مُتوقفٌ على صدقِ الرسولِ المُتوقِّفِ على دلالة المعجزة لزم الدور كل القسم الثاني : ما يصح أن يُستدل عليه بالدليل الشرعي ؛ وهو ما لا تتوقَّفُ عليهِ دلالة المعجزة ؛ كالسمع والبصر والكلام ، والبعث وأحوال الآخرة جملة وتفصيلاً . الثالث : ما اختُلِفَ فيهِ بالتردُّدِ فيهِ : هل هو مِنَ القسم الأولِ ، أو مِنَ القسم الثاني ؛ كالوحدانية ؛ فإنَّهُ اختُلِفَ فيها : هل يكفي فيها الدليل السمعي ؛ بناءً على عدم توقفِ دلالة المعجزة عليها في علم الناظرِ وإِنْ تَوقَّفَ وجودُ المعجزة عليها في نفس الأمر ؛ لاستحالة وجودِ الفعل مع وجودِ الشريكِ ، أو لا بدَّ فيها مِنَ الدليل العقلي ؛ نظراً إلى توقف دلالةِ المعجزة على صحة وجودِ المعجزة المتوقفة على الوحدانيةِ ؛ لأنَّ المعجزة فعل ، والفعلُ يستحيل وجوده على تقدير الاثنينيَّة في الألوهية ، والمتوقفُ على المتوقفِ على الشيء متوقِّفٌ على ذلك الشيء
Know that the beliefs of faith are divided into three sections: First: that which is not valid to be known except by rational proof; and it is everything upon which the indication of a miracle depends; such as His existence, power, will, knowledge, and life; For if one were to argue for this section by scriptural proof, which depends on the truthfulness of the messenger, which depends on the indication of a miracle, then circularity would necessarily occur. all Second: that which is valid to be argued for by scriptural proof; and it is that upon which the indication of a miracle does not depend; such as hearing, sight, speech, resurrection, and the states of the afterlife in general and in detail. Third: that concerning which there is disagreement, with hesitation: whether it belongs to the first section or the second section; such as oneness (al-waḥdāniyya); for there is disagreement concerning it: Is auditory proof sufficient for it, based on the non-dependence of the indication of a miracle upon it in the knowledge of the observer, even if the existence of a miracle depends on it in reality, due to the impossibility of an act existing with the existence of a partner; or must there be rational proof for it, considering the dependence of the indication of a miracle on the correctness of the existence of the miracle, which depends on oneness? Because the miracle is an act, and an act is impossible in existence on the assumption of duality in divinity, and that which depends on that which depends on a thing is itself dependent on that thing.
Source pages p. 174

Commentary
This passage is a classification of beliefs (ʿaqāʾid al-īmān) into three divisions based on the type of evidence needed to establish them. The author begins by stating the division and then explains each category. The first category comprises beliefs that can only be known through rational proof (dalīl ʿaqlī), specifically those truths upon which the very indication of a miracle (dalālat al-muʿjiza) depends. Examples are God's existence, power, will, knowledge, and life. The reason is that if one tried to prove these using scriptural proof (dalīl sharʿī), which depends on the messenger's truthfulness, which itself depends on a miracle, there would be circularity (dawr). The second category consists of beliefs that can be known through scriptural proof, namely those not needed for the indication of a miracle, such as God's hearing, sight, speech, resurrection, and details of the afterlife. The third category is a disputed one, such as God's oneness (al-waḥdāniyya): some say auditory proof suffices because the miracle's indication does not depend on it in the observer's knowledge, while others require rational proof because the miracle's existence depends on oneness in reality—an act cannot occur with a partner in divinity. The passage concludes with the logical principle that what depends on what depends on a thing is itself dependent on that thing, reinforcing the need for rational proof for oneness according to the second opinion.

Key Terms

دليل سمعي
الدور

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that oneness is clearly in the first or second category.
Correction: This passage shows there is legitimate disagreement among scholars.

Checkpoint
According to the second opinion on divine oneness, why is rational proof necessary?
A) Because scripture does not mention oneness.
B) Because the indication of a miracle depends on the miracle's existence, which depends on oneness.
C) Because oneness is a sensory belief.
D) Because the observer cannot know oneness from scripture.

Key Takeaway
Divine oneness is a disputed case; the stronger argument requires rational proof because miracles logically depend on it.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly own your belief in the oneness of Allah as a rational conviction, or have you merely inherited it without thinking through the logical impossibility of partners, as this lesson forces you to? What step will you take today to make your tawḥīd intellectually unshakable?
?
When you look at the dependency chain—miracles depend on oneness, prophecy depends on miracles, and your knowledge of revelation depends on prophecy—are you standing on a house of cards, or have you seated your faith on the rational proof this passage describes? If you cannot explain this chain, what will you do to remedy that starting this week?
?
The passage presents two opinions: one holds that scripture alone suffices for oneness, the other insists on rational proof. Which opinion do you instinctively lean toward, and why? If you find yourself in the first camp, are you willing to follow the stronger argument even if it feels harder? What does this mean for how you study and defend your creed?

Rational Judgments
intermediate
6 min
The Permissible for God: Actions, Not Essence
Sanusi defines the permissible for God and distinguishes attributes of action from essence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what it means for something to be 'permissible' (ja'iz) with respect to God, and why permissibility applies only to His attributes of action, not to His essence or essential attributes.

Core Idea
God's permissibility is about what He can do, not what He is.

In this passage, Sanusi defines a key term in Islamic theology: al-ja'iz (the permissible) as it relates to God. He clarifies that while God's power and will encompass all possible things, permissibility does not touch His essence or essential attributes, which are necessarily existent. Instead, it refers to the actualization of contingent things through His power and will. This distinction is foundational for understanding divine attributes.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition and examples
Read the Arabic passage and translation to grasp the definition of al-ja'iz and the examples Sanusi provides (creating vision, hearing, reward, prophets).
2
Identify the core distinction
Notice the explicit statement that permissibility applies only to attributes of action, not to essence or essential attributes. Underline the reasoning: essence is necessary, so no permissibility.
3
Understand the 'actualized attachment'
Sanusi says permissibility refers to the actualized attachment (al-ta'alluq al-tanzizi) of God's power and will, which is not pre-eternal but occurs when contingent things come into existence. Reflect on why this matters.
4
Connect to earlier knowledge
The conclusion builds on earlier lessons about God's power and will being general to all possibles and His oneness. Recall those to see how every possible is permissible by God's power and will.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الجائز في حقه تعالى
وأما الجائز في حقِّهِ تعالى : ففعل كل ممكن أو تركه ، صلاحاً كان أو ضدَّهُ ؛ لِمَا عُرِفَ قبلُ مِنْ وجوب عموم قدرته وإرادته تعالى لجميع الممكناتِ ، ويدخل في ذلكَ : جواز خلْقِ الله تعالى الرؤية لذاتِهِ العليَّةِ ، والسمع لكلامِهِ القديم ، والثوابَ في دار النعيم ، والبعثَ لرسله الأكرمين ، صلواتُ اللهِ وسلامه عليهم أجمعينَ
[ الجائزُ مِنْ صفاتِ الأفعالِ ، لا مِنْ صفاتِ الذاتِ ] لا شكّ أنَّ الجواز لا يَتطرَّقُ للذاتِ العليَّةِ ، ولا لشيءٍ مِنْ صفاتها المرفّعةِ ؛ لوجوب الوجودِ لجميع ذلك ، وإِنَّما مرجع الجواز للتعلُّقِ التنجيزي لقدرته تعالى وإرادته ، وهذا التعلُّقُ ليس بقديم ، ومرجعُهُ
إلى صدور الكائناتِ عن قدرتِهِ تعالى وإرادتِهِ ، ولما عرفت فيما سبق عموم تعلَّقِ قدرتِهِ تعالى وإرادتِهِ لجميع الممكنات ، وعرفت وجوب وحدانيته تبارك وتعالى .. عرفت أنَّ كلَّ ممكن فهو جائز أنْ يكونَ
بقدرة الله تعالى وإرادته .
The permissible regarding Him, Exalted be He. As for the permissible regarding Him, Exalted be He: it is doing every possible thing or leaving it, whether it is good or its opposite; because it was known previously from the necessity of the generality of His power and His will, Exalted be He, for all possible things. And this includes: the permissibility of God, Exalted be He, creating vision of His lofty Essence, and hearing of His pre-eternal Speech, and reward in the abode of bliss, and the sending of His most honored messengers—may the blessings of God and His peace be upon them all. [The permissible is from the attributes of actions, not from the attributes of essence.] There is no doubt that permissibility does not apply to the lofty Essence, nor to any of its exalted attributes, due to the necessity of existence for all of that. Rather, the reference of permissibility is to the actualized (tanzīzī) attachment of His power and His will, and this attachment is not pre-eternal, and its reference is to the coming into existence of contingent things from His power and His will. And when you have known from what preceded the generality of the attachment of His power and His will to all possible things, and you have known the necessity of His Oneness, Blessed and Exalted be He, you know that every possible thing is permissible to be by the power of God and His will.
Source pages p. 182

Commentary
This passage defines the concept of 'al-ja'iz' (the permissible) with respect to God. It begins by stating that the permissible for God is doing or leaving any possible thing, whether it is considered good or its opposite (i.e., what may appear as evil). This is because God's power and will encompass all possible things, as established earlier. The author then gives examples: God may create vision of His essence, hearing of His pre-eternal speech, reward in paradise, and the sending of prophets. The passage then clarifies a crucial distinction: permissibility applies to God's attributes of action (ṣifāt al-afʿāl), not to His essence or attributes of essence (ṣifāt al-dhāt). The essence and essential attributes are necessary existent, so no permissibility attaches to them. The permissibility refers to the 'actualized attachment' (al-taʿalluq al-tajammurī?) of God's power and will, which is not pre-eternal but occurs when contingent things come into existence. The argument concludes by recalling that since God's power and will are general to all possibles, and God is necessarily one, every possible thing is permissible to occur by God's power and will.

Key Terms
الجائز
الصفات الأفعال
الصفات الذات
التعلق التنجيزي
الممكنات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that 'permissible' for God implies uncertainty or possibility in God's essence or essential attributes.
Correction: The passage clarifies that permissibility only pertains to God's attributes of action (what He does), not to His essence or essential attributes, which are necessarily existent.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, to what does the concept of 'al-ja'iz' (the permissible) apply with respect to God?
A) To God's essence and all attributes
B) Only to God's attributes of action, not to His essence or essential attributes
C) Only to things God does, not to things He leaves
D) To all possible things equally

Key Takeaway
The permissible for God pertains solely to His attributes of action—what He may do—never to His essence or essential attributes, which are necessarily existent.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When facing a difficult situation whose wisdom you cannot grasp, do you ever feel that God should have acted differently? How does the lesson's teaching that the permissible covers both good and its opposite (صلاحاً كان أو ضدَّهُ) challenge your assumption that God's actions must align with your sense of goodness? Will you now actively remind yourself that God's action is always permissible because of His absolute power and will, even when it hurts?
?
The lesson distinguishes between God's essence and His actions. Have you ever unknowingly projected limitations onto God's essence by thinking that something is 'impossible' for Him to command or do because it seems illogical or contradictory? How does this lesson protect you from such thinking? Commit to memorizing the distinction: permissibility belongs to actions, not to essence—how will this change your prayer and reliance on God's unlimited freedom?
?
If you truly internalize that God's power and will are free, unconstrained by any external standard, what would you need to change in the way you view the world? When you see injustice, do you still believe that God's action is permissible even if you cannot see the wisdom? Can you find peace in the fact that God's essence is beyond your questioning, and His actions are always rooted in His perfect attributes? What one concrete situation will you apply this to today?

Rational Judgments
intermediate
5 min
God is Not Obligated to Observe What is Most Beneficial
Sanusi refutes those who claim God must observe what is most beneficial for creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's rational argument that God is not obligated to observe what is most beneficial for creation.

Core Idea
Obligating God to provide benefit would destroy the contingent nature of creation.

Some have claimed that God must always do what is best for His creatures. Sanusi firmly rejects this, arguing that it would contradict both rational necessity and observable reality. In this passage, he lays out a concise logical refutation that hinges on the distinction between the contingent and the obligatory.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the assertive opening and the logical flow.
The footnote is part of the passage and reinforces the main argument.
2
Identify the claim being refuted
Find the phrase that states what the misguided group claims. Notice how Sanusi labels them.
The claim is that God must observe the beneficial and most beneficial.
3
Understand the inversion argument
Sanusi argues that obligating God to provide benefit would change the nature of benefit from contingent to necessary. Why is that a problem?
Contingent things can either exist or not; making them necessary removes that possibility.
4
Connect to observed reality
Sanusi points out that corruption (the opposite of benefit) is observed in the world. How does this observation support his refutation?
If corruption exists, then benefit cannot be obligatory, because then corruption would be impossible.
5
Reflect on the footnote
The footnote summarizes the core point: the contingent cannot become necessary by itself. Why is this a deeper problem?
It shows that the claim would require a change in the intrinsic nature of things.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ لا يجب على الله تعالى مراعاة الصلاح والأصلح ] وليس فيه ما هو واجب عقلاً (۱) ؛ كالصلاح والأصلح كما قاله بعضُ مَنْ ضَلَّ ؛ لأنَّه يلزمُ عليهِ قلب حقيقة الصلاح والأصلح الجائزة بأن ترجع واجبةً ، وذلك يمنع وقوع ضدّها ؛ وهو الفساد ، كيف وهو موجود بالمشاهدة ؟! (۱) فالجائز لا ينفكُ في حقيقته عن الإمكان ، فكيف يصير واجباً بذاته ؟!
God is not obligated to observe what is beneficial and most beneficial. And there is nothing in it that is rationally obligatory (1), like the beneficial and most beneficial, as some who have gone astray said, because it would entail inverting the nature of the beneficial and most beneficial, which are contingent, to become obligatory, and that prevents the occurrence of its opposite, which is corruption, yet how can it be when it is observed? (1) The contingent does not, in its essence, separate from possibility, so how can it become necessary in itself?
Source pages p. 183

Commentary
This passage refutes the claim that God is obligated to observe what is most beneficial (الصلاح والأصلح) for His creation. Sanusi states that there is nothing in this that is rationally obligatory. He identifies the proponents of this view as those who have gone astray (بعض من ضل). The argument is that if something contingent (جائز) like benefit were to become obligatory, its nature would be inverted, turning from contingent to necessary. This would prevent the occurrence of its opposite, which is corruption (الفساد). But corruption is observable in the world, so the claim is false. The footnote adds that the contingent is inherently possible, so it cannot become necessary by itself.

Key Terms
الصلاح والأصلح
واجب عقلاً
جائز
قلب الحقيقة
فساد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God is obligated to always do what is most beneficial for His creation.
Correction: Sanusi argues that this would invert the nature of benefit from contingent to necessary, which contradicts observed reality where corruption exists.

Checkpoint
What does Sanusi say would happen if God were obligated to observe the most beneficial?
A) It would make corruption impossible
B) It would make God unjust
C) It would prove God's existence
D) It would nullify free will

Key Takeaway
Sanusi refutes the claim that God must observe the most beneficial, arguing that it would invert the contingent nature of benefit and contradict the observed presence of corruption.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you see corruption and suffering in the world, do you ever feel that God is not acting fairly? How does Sanusi’s argument expose that feeling as based on a false assumption about God’s obligations? Will you repent from that feeling and submit to God’s absolute freedom?
?
If you truly believe that God is not obligated to give you what is best for you, how does that change the way you face trials? Are you ready to accept whatever God decrees, even when it seems harmful, trusting that He is not bound by your notion of benefit? What specific trial in your life right now demands this surrender?
?
Contemplate the word ‘contingent’—benefit is possible, not necessary. How does this affect your dua? Do you ask God for specific outcomes expecting them to be best, or do you ask with a heart that acknowledges His absolute freedom to give or withhold? Will you commit today to making a dua that fully surrenders to His wisdom, without dictating the outcome?

Rational Judgments

Why God is Not Obligated to Reward the Obedient
Sanusi argues that God's reward of the obedient is rationally contingent.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why rewarding the obedient is a contingent (jāʾiz) matter for God and not a rational obligation.

Core Idea
No one has a claim over God; obedience itself is His creation.

Sanusi continues his discussion of rational judgments by addressing a common question: Does God owe rewards to those who obey Him? In this passage, he explains why reward is merely possible and never mandatory. The reasoning is rooted in God's perfection and the nature of human actions.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage carefully
Read the Arabic text once without analysing. Notice the key terms like 'إثابة', 'مطيع', 'حق', 'اكتساب', 'خلق'. Then read the literal translation to grasp the overall argument.
The passage is concise; each phrase carries weight.
2
Identify the three reasons
Sanusi provides three distinct reasons why reward is not obligatory. List them: (1) no one has a right over God, (2) God gains no benefit from obedience, (3) obedience is God's creation and the servant only acquires it.
These reasons are linked by 'إذ' and 'أيضاً'.
3
Reflect on the concept of acquisition (kash/iktisab)
Sanusi says the servant has only 'اكتساب' (acquisition) and 'اتصاف' (attribution) in obedience, with no real effect. Think about what this means for human responsibility and divine reward.
This relates to the Ashʿarī theory of acquisition.
4
Consider the common misconception
Many think God must reward good deeds because humans deserve it. Sanusi refutes this by showing no one has a right over God. Reflect on how this changes your understanding of grace and justice.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وكذا مِنَ الجائزاتِ : إثابةُ اللهِ تعالى المطيع ؛ إذ لا حق لأحدٍ عليه تعالى ؛ إذ لا نفعَ لهُ تعالى بطاعةِ أحدٍ ، وأيضاً : فالطاعةُ خَلْق له تبارك وتعالى ، وليس للعبدِ فيها إلا الاكتساب والاتصافُ ، ولا أثر لهُ فيها أصلاً .
And similarly among contingent matters is God's rewarding the obedient, for no one has a right over Him, as He gains no benefit from anyone's obedience. Also, obedience is His creation, and the servant has only acquisition and attribution, having no effect in it at all.
Source pages p. 184

Commentary
This passage explains that God's rewarding the obedient is a contingent (جائز) matter, not obligatory. Sanusi gives three reasons: first, no one has a right over God; second, God does not benefit from anyone's obedience; third, obedience itself is created by God (خلق له), and the servant only acquires it and is described by it, without any real effect. Thus, there is no claim on God.

Key Terms
إثابة
مطيع
حق
اكتساب
خلق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: God must reward good deeds because humans deserve it.
Correction: Sanusi shows that no one has a right over God, and obedience is created by God, so reward is purely contingent and not obligatory.

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi say God is not obligated to reward the obedient?
A) Because obedience has no value
B) Because no one has a right over God and obedience is His creation
C) Because the obedient are not truly obedient
D) Because God does not care about obedience

Key Takeaway
God's reward of the obedient is a contingent act of grace, not an obligatory response to human merit.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever find yourself feeling that your obedience to God gives you a 'right' to His reward? How does this passage dismantle that feeling? What would change in your worship if you internalized that your very obedience is His creation?
?
If you knew with certainty that no reward would come from your prayers or charity, would you still perform them? What does your honest answer reveal about the state of your heart?
?
Think of a time you felt entitled to something from God. How does Sanusi’s argument that obedience is merely acquired (and not your independent production) transform that entitlement into humble gratitude? Will you let that transformation begin today?

Rational Judgments
beginner
4 min
Reward of the Obedient is Not Obligatory but Permissible
Sanusi states that rewarding the obedient is not obligatory but permissible.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi holds that rewarding the obedient is not rationally obligatory upon God, but is among the permissible acts of divine grace.

Core Idea
God's reward is a gift, not a debt.

This lesson examines a foundational principle in Sunni theology: that no human act creates an obligation upon God. While we might assume that obedience deserves reward, Sanusi argues that reward is purely a matter of divine permission. The passage uses two rational arguments to show that reward cannot be obligatory.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Get familiar with Sanusi's concise statement and the two reasons. Notice the key Arabic terms: واجب, جائز, اكتساب, اتصاف.
The passage is short but dense.
2
Identify the two reasons
The passage gives two arguments: no one has a right against God, and obedience is God's creation (the servant has no causal effect). Understand each.
3
Reflect on the theological implication
Consider why this matters: it protects God's absolute sovereignty and emphasizes that reward is purely by grace, not obligation.
This connects to broader Ash'ari theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لا يجب على الله تعالى إثابةُ المطيع عقلاً. وكذا مِنَ الجائزاتِ: إثابةُ اللهِ تعالى المطيع؛ إذ لا حق لأحدٍ عليه تعالى؛ إذ لا نفع له تعالى بطاعة أحد، وأيضاً: فالطاعة خلق له تبارك وتعالى، وليس للعبد فيها إلا الاكتساب والاتصاف، ولا أثر له فيها أصلاً.
It is not obligatory upon God, exalted, to reward the obedient, rationally. And likewise among the permissible things: rewarding of God, exalted, for the obedient; for there is no right for anyone against Him, exalted; for there is no benefit for Him, exalted, in the obedience of anyone. Also: obedience is a creation of His, blessed and exalted, and the servant has in it only acquisition (iktisāb) and description (ittiṣāf), and he has no effect in it at all.
Source pages p. 184

Commentary
This passage makes a doctrinal statement: rewarding the obedient is not rationally obligatory upon God, but is among the permissible things (jā'izāt). The author gives two reasons. First, no one has any right against God, and God derives no benefit from anyone's obedience. Second, obedience is God's creation; the servant only acquires (iktisāb) and is described (ittiṣāf) by it, and has no causal effect in bringing it about. Therefore, reward is purely by God's grace, not by obligation.

Key Terms
جائز
واجب
عقل
اكتساب
اتصاف

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think that since God commands obedience, He is obligated to reward it.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that obedience is God's creation and the servant has no claim, so reward is not obligatory but a gracious gift.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why is rewarding the obedient not obligatory upon God?
A) Because God is merciful and reward is required by justice.
B) Because no one has a right against God and the servant has no causal effect in obedience.
C) Because the servant deserves reward through his own efforts.
D) Because reward is a necessary consequence of obedience.

Key Takeaway
God's reward for obedience is a gracious gift, not an obligatory due, because no creature has a claim on God and all actions are created by Him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you perform an act of obedience, do you secretly feel that God owes you something in return? How does the truth that even your obedience is His creation challenge that feeling? Will you let go of that sense of entitlement starting today?
?
If God's reward is purely a gift and not a wage, then every single moment of your worship is a favor from Him. Does the way you pray, give charity, or strive in His path reflect that gratitude, or do you still act as if you are doing Him a favor?
?
The servant has 'no effect' in obedience—only acquisition and description. Does this humble you to the point of breaking your pride? Can you truly accept that you bring nothing to God except what He first gives you, and still serve Him with joy for His sake alone?

Life of Imam al-Sanusi
beginner
4 min
Imam al-Sanusi: His Name, Lineage, and Epithets
Biography of Imam al-Sanusi with name, lineage, and epithets.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the full name, lineage, tribal affiliation, and key epithets of Imam al-Sanusi, including the meaning of 'al-Tawhidi'.

Core Idea
Imam al-Sanusi's epithets reveal his scholarly identity and deep roots in Sunni theology.

This lesson introduces the biography of Imam al-Sanusi, the great Ash'ari theologian. We will learn his full name, his tribal origin from the Sanusa tribe, and why he is called 'al-Tawhidi'. Understanding his background helps us appreciate his contributions to Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the biography passage
Read the Arabic passage once and its translation. Notice the long list of honorifics and nisbas.
2
Identify the nisbas
Find all the relative names (nisbas) in his name: al-Sanusi, al-Tilmisani, al-Maliki, al-Ash'ari, al-Tawhidi. Also note the additional ones from his handwriting.
Each nisba connects him to a tribe, place, school, or specialty.
3
Understand 'al-Sanusi' and 'al-Tawhidi'
Focus on the explanations given: 'al-Sanusi' is from the Sanusa tribe, and 'al-Tawhidi' means he is occupied with the science of tawhid.
This shows his tribal roots and his theological focus.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ترجمة الإمام السنوسي شيخ متكلمي عصره ومصره ، الإمام العلامة المشارك ، المحدّث المُقرِئ ، الفقيه الأصولي المحقق ، الصوفي التقي الورع ؛ أبو عبد الله محمد بن يوسف بن عمر بن شعيب السنوسي التلمساني المالكي الأشعري التوحيدي . والسنوسي : نسبة إلى سَنُوسة ؛ قبيلة من البرابرة في المغرب ، قال العلامة الزبيدي في " تاج العروس » : ( وإليهم نسب الولي الصالح أبو عبد الله محمد بن يوسف بن عمر بن شعيب السنوسي ؛ لأنه نزل عندهم ، وقيل : بل هو منهم ، وأمه شريفة حسنية ، كذا حققه سيدي محمد بن إبراهيم الملالي في « المواهب القدسية » ووجد بخطه على ( شرح الآجرومية ( له : « السنوسي العيسي الشريف القرشي القصَّار » ، قلت : العيسي : من بيت عيسى . والتوحيدي : نسبة للاشتغال بعلم التوحيد ، كذا ذكر الحفناوي
Translation of Imam al-Sanusi: the shaykh of the theologians of his time and his country, the imam, the all-knowing scholar, the hadith specialist, the reciter, the jurist, the fundamentalist investigator, the pious ascetic; Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf ibn Umar ibn Shu'ayb al-Sanusi al-Tilmisani al-Maliki al-Ash'ari al-Tawhidi. And al-Sanusi: a relative attribution to Sanusa, a tribe of the Berbers in the Maghreb. The scholar al-Zabidi said in 'Taj al-Arus': 'To them is attributed the righteous saint Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf ibn Umar ibn Shu'ayb al-Sanusi because he settled among them; and it is said he is from them. His mother is a sharifa Hasaniyya.' So verified by my master Muhammad ibn Ibrahim al-Mallali in 'al-Mawahib al-Qudsiyya'. And it was found in his handwriting on his commentary on al-Ajrumiyya: 'al-Sanusi al-Isawi al-Sharif al-Qurashi al-Qassar.' I say: al-Isawi: from the house of Isa. And al-Tawhidi: relative attribution to preoccupation with the science of tawhid; so mentioned by al-Hafnawi.
Source pages p. 11

Commentary
This passage is a biography (tarjama) of Imam al-Sanusi. It begins by listing his honorifics and his full name: Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf ibn Umar ibn Shu'ayb al-Sanusi, with the nisbas al-Tilmisani (from Tlemcen), al-Maliki (school), al-Ash'ari (theological school), and al-Tawhidi (due to his work in tawhid). It then explains the origin of 'al-Sanusi', attributing it to the Sanusa tribe. It cites al-Zabidi's 'Taj al-Arus' for two opinions: that al-Sanusi settled among them or that he was from them, and that his mother was a sharifa (descendant of the Prophet) from the Hasanid line. It also notes that al-Sanusi wrote in his hand on his Sharh al-Ajrumiyya the nisbas 'al-Isawi al-Sharif al-Qurashi al-Qassar', and the author comments that 'al-Isawi' means from the house of Isa. Finally, it explains that 'al-Tawhidi' refers to his occupation with the science of tawhid (Islamic theology), citing al-Hafnawi.

Key Terms
السنوسي
التوحيدي
تلمساني
مالكي
أشعري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think al-Sanusi was named 'al-Tawhidi' because he wrote a book titled 'al-Tawhid'.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that 'al-Tawhidi' is a relative attribution to his preoccupation with the science of tawhid, not to a book title.

Checkpoint
What is the reason for al-Sanusi's epithet 'al-Tawhidi' according to the passage?
A) He wrote a book named al-Tawhid
B) He was from the Tawhid tribe
C) He was preoccupied with the science of tawhid
D) He was a student of al-Tawhidi

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Sanusi's full name and epithets reflect his scholarly lineage, tribal roots, and dedication to the science of tawhid.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Imam al-Sanusi was called 'al-Tawhidi' because he was habitually engaged in the science of tawhid. If your daily conversations and thoughts were examined, what 'science' or preoccupation would they reveal you are actually devoted to? Are you comfortable with that label before Allah?
?
His lineage includes tribal, regional, and school affiliations. When you introduce yourself, which identities do you highlight, and do they reflect a genuine commitment to truth or just social pride?
?
The passage mentions that al-Sanusi settled among a tribe and became associated with them. In your community, are you known more for your outward belonging or for your inner devotion to knowledge and worship? What change would make your 'epithet' more truthful?

Life of Imam al-Sanusi
beginner
5 min
Imam al-Sanusi's Name and Nisbas
Biography introduces Imam al-Sanusi's full name and explains his nisbas.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Imam al-Sanusi's full name, epithets, and nisbas establish his scholarly identity and specialization in theology.

Core Idea
Imam al-Sanusi's nisbas reveal both his tribal roots and his theological specialization.

In this lesson, we meet Imam al-Sanusi through the classical biographical tradition. The passage introduces his full name, a series of honorific epithets, and explains two of his primary nisbas—al-Sanusi and al-Tawhidi. This establishes his scholarly credentials and lineage in the Sunni tradition.

Guided Path
1
Read the biography
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the long list of titles describing al-Sanusi's expertise.
The epithets cover hadith, fiqh, usul, tasawwuf, etc.
2
Analyze the epithets
Identify each epithet and consider what it tells us about his mastery of different Islamic sciences.
3
Explore 'al-Sanusi' nisba
The author explains it relates to the Sanusa Berber tribe. Note the citation of al-Zabidi and the alternate attribution from al-Mallali.
This nisba links him to a specific tribal lineage.
4
Explore 'al-Tawhidi' nisba
This nisba directly indicates his specialization in the science of tawhid (Islamic theology).
5
Reflect on the purpose
Consider how this biographical introduction sets the stage for a work on theology by establishing the author's authority.
The nisba al-Tawhidi is key—it signals his field.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ترجمة الإمام السنوسي
شيخ متكلمي عصره ومصره ، الإمام العلامة المشارك ، المحدّث المُقرِئ ، الفقيه الأصولي المحقق ، الصوفي التقي الورع ؛ أبو عبد الله محمد بن يوسف بن عمر بن شعيب السنوسي التلمساني المالكي الأشعري التوحيدي .
والسنوسي : نسبة إلى سَنُوسة ؛ قبيلة من البرابرة في المغرب ، قال العلامة الزبيدي في " تاج العروس » : ( وإليهم نسب الولي الصالح أبو عبد الله محمد بن يوسف بن عمر بن شعيب السنوسي ؛ لأنه نزل عندهم ، وقيل : بل هو منهم ، وأمه شريفة حسنية (١) ، كذا حققه سيدي محمد بن إبراهيم الملالي في « المواهب القدسية (۲) ووجد بخطه على ( شرح الآجرومية ( له : « السنوسي العيسي الشريف القرشي القصَّار » ، قلت : العيسي : من بيت عيسى ((٣)
والتوحيدي : نسبة للاشتغال بعلم التوحيد ، كذا ذكر الحفناوي هذه النسبة له في « تعريف الخلف » (۱)
Translation of Imam al-Sanusi. The master of the theologians of his time and his country, the imam, the all-knowing scholar, the hadith specialist, the reciter, the jurist, the usuli investigator, the Sufi, pious and ascetic; Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf ibn Umar ibn Shu'ayb al-Sanusi al-Tilmisani al-Maliki al-Ash'ari al-Tawhidi.
Al-Sanusi: a relative attribution to Sanusa, a Berber tribe in the Maghrib. Al-Zabidi said in Taj al-Arus: 'To them is attributed the righteous saint Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Yusuf ibn Umar ibn Shu'ayb al-Sanusi because he settled among them; it is said he is from them. His mother is a sharifa Hasanid (1), similarly verified by Sidi Muhammad ibn Ibrahim al-Mallali in al-Mawahib al-Qudsiyya (2), and found in his handwriting on his commentary on al-Ajrumiyya: 'al-Sanusi al-Isa al-Sharif al-Qurashi al-Qassar.' I say: al-Isa is from the house of Isa (3).
Al-Tawhidi: a relative attribution to occupying oneself with the science of tawhid. Thus al-Hafnawi mentioned this attribution for him in Ta'rif al-Khalaf (1).
Source pages p. 11

Commentary
This passage is a biographical introduction that first describes Imam al-Sanusi with several epithets highlighting his scholarship in various Islamic sciences (hadith, fiqh, usul, tasawwuf), then explains two of his key nisbas: 'al-Sanusi' and 'al-Tawhidi'. The author provides the origin of 'al-Sanusi' as a relation to the Sanusa tribe, citing al-Zabidi's Taj al-Arus, and adds that his mother was a sharifa from the Hasanids. Then he mentions a different reading from al-Mallali that adds 'al-Isa al-Sharif al-Qurashi al-Qassar', and the author comments that 'al-Isa' refers to the house of Isa. The second nisba 'al-Tawhidi' is attributed to his specialization in the science of tawhid (Islamic theology). The passage sets the stage for his scholarly identity by linking him to both a tribal lineage and a theological discipline.

Key Terms
سنوسي
توحيدي
ترجمة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The nisba 'al-Sanusi' refers to a place name or a Sufi order.
Correction: It is described as a relation to the Sanusa Berber tribe; al-Sanusi himself is an individual scholar, not a founder of a later order.

Checkpoint
What does the nisba 'al-Tawhidi' indicate?
A) He was from a town called Tawhid
B) He specialized in the science of tawhid
C) He was a follower of the Tawhid sect
D) He wrote a book on tawhid

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Sanusi's name and nisbas encode his tribal lineage and his dedication to the science of tawhid, establishing his authority as a theologian.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Look at the titles and nisbas in your own name. Which of them reflect a conscious choice of learning (like 'al-Tawhidi') and which are just inherited? If you had to add one nisba describing your actual specialization, what would it be — and does your life reflect that?
?
Imam al-Sanusi is called 'master of the theologians' and described with many sciences, yet his nisba 'al-Tawhidi' singles out tawhid. Do you treat the knowledge of Allah's attributes as the centerpiece of your practice, or do you let other sciences distract you from the most important subject?
?
The passage notes that al-Sanusi may be from the tribe or just settled among them. Are you known among others by your chosen commitments or only by your unearned affiliations? What would it take for your identity to be more like 'al-Tawhidi' — defined by your devotion to knowing Allah?

Life of Imam al-Sanusi

The Disputed Birth Year of Imam al-Sanusi
The biographer discusses conflicting reports on Sanusi's birth year and cites al-Tunbukti's account.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the nature of the disagreement among early biographers regarding Imam Sanusi's birth year and how a specific report from al-Tunbukti provides one possible calculation.

Core Idea
Sanusi's birth year is not definitively known; early sources conflict.

This passage from a biographical work on Imam al-Sanusi highlights the challenges of reconstructing the lives of early scholars. The author notes a disagreement over al-Sanusi's birth year and cites a report from al-Tunbukti that offers one calculation. As you read, consider how oral transmission and written notes shaped Islamic biographical literature.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice the structure: it opens with a statement of disagreement, then cites a source, then draws a conclusion.
The passage is short but contains a complete argument.
2
Identify the source of the report
Find where the biographer cites his authority. The report comes from al-Tunbukti's book 'Nayl al-Ibtihaj', which quotes al-Mallali's statement about the shaykh's age.
Notice the chain: al-Tunbukti saw a note from some scholars who asked al-Mallali.
3
Calculate the birth year
Using the report that Sanusi died at age 63, and knowing his death year (895 AH), calculate the birth year. The passage gives 832 AH. Verify the math: 895 - 63 = 832.
This shows how biographers deduced dates from age reports.
4
Reflect on the nature of the disagreement
Consider why there might be conflicting reports. The passage mentions 'lack of certainty' and 'confusion in transmission'. This reflects the limitations of oral history.
Think about how this affects our knowledge of early scholars' lives.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مولده ونشأته يرجع لعدم ذلك اختلف في سنة ولادة الإمام السنوسي ; وسبب نصه هو على ذلك واضطراب نقل تلميذه الملالي في ذلك أيضاً ، قال العلامة التُنْبُكْتي في نيل الابتهاج (۲) : ( رأيت مقيداً عن بعض العلماء : أنه سأل الملالي المذكور عن سنّ الشيخ ، فقال له : مات عن ثلاث وستين سنة )(٣) فعلى هذه الرواية : تكون السنة التي ولد فيها الإمام السنوسي هي ( ۱۳۲ هـ )
His birth and upbringing: Due to the lack thereof, there is disagreement about the year of birth of Imam al-Sanusi, and the reason for his [the biographer's] text being thus, and the confusion in the transmission of his student al-Mallali regarding that as well. Al-Tunbukti said in Nayl al-Ibtihaj (2): 'I saw a note from some scholars that he asked the aforementioned al-Mallali about the age of the Shaykh, and he said to him: He died at sixty-three years.' (3) So according to this narration, the year in which Imam al-Sanusi was born is 832 AH.
Source pages p. 12

Commentary
This passage discusses the disagreement among sources regarding the birth year of Imam al-Sanusi. It begins by stating the problem: there is a lack of certainty, leading to disagreement. It then provides a specific report from al-Tunbukti in his book Nayl al-Ibtihaj, quoting a note that al-Mallali was asked about the Shaykh's age and responded that he died at 63. From this, the biographer deduces that the birth year would be 832 AH (since he died in 895 AH). The passage highlights the reliance on oral transmission and the need to reconcile conflicting reports.

Key Terms
السنوسي
الملالي
التنبكتي
نيل الابتهاج

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Sanusi's birth year is definitively known and agreed upon by all early biographers.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that there is disagreement due to lack of certainty and conflicting reports, and it offers only one possible calculation based on a specific narration.

Checkpoint
According to al-Tunbukti's report, what did al-Mallali say about the age of Imam Sanusi at his death?
A) 55 years
B) 63 years
C) 56 years
D) 65 years

Key Takeaway
The birth year of Imam al-Sanusi is uncertain, with one report from al-Tunbukti suggesting 832 AH based on al-Mallali's statement that he died at age 63.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter conflicting reports about an important figure in Islamic history, how do you decide what to believe? Does this uncertainty unsettle your trust in the tradition?
?
This lesson shows that even a great scholar like Imam al-Sanusi may have a fuzzy birth year. Does that make you more or less comfortable with ambiguity in your own understanding of Islamic knowledge?
?
Imagine you are asked about a significant event in your own life but your memory is faulty. How does this passage challenge you to be humble about historical claims you take for granted?

Life of Imam al-Sanusi

Imam Sanusi's Pious Father and Family Background
Description of Sanusi's pious father, lineage, and character.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the pious family environment in which Imam al-Sanusi was raised, including his father's character, lineage, and occupation, and how this shaped his early education.

Core Idea
Imam Sanusi's upbringing in a house of virtue and knowledge laid the foundation for his later scholarship.

Imam al-Sanusi's early life is not just a biography; it reveals the spiritual and intellectual soil from which his great work grew. In this lesson, you will read the description of his father, Shaykh Yusuf Abu Ya'qub, and learn about the pious atmosphere that surrounded the young Sanusi. This background helps us appreciate the tradition from which the 'Umm al-Barahin emerged.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Start by reading the Arabic passage once without analyzing, then read the literal translation to get the overall picture of the father's background.
Notice the footnote indicators (1), (2), (3) which refer to additional details.
2
Examine the father's character description
Focus on al-Mallali's quote describing the father's virtues: righteousness, asceticism, humility, good character, and cheerful demeanor.
The father is portrayed as a model of piety and learning.
3
Understand the lineage detail
Identify the term 'al-Hasani' and note that Sanusi's Hasanid lineage comes from his paternal grandmother, not directly from Imam Hasan.
This clarifies a common misconception about his nisba.
4
Reflect on the father's occupation and spiritual state
The father taught Qur'an to children, and divine care accompanied him with miracles and insight. Consider how this environment influenced Sanusi's early education.
This shows the transmission of knowledge and spirituality in the family.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
نشأ الإمام السنوسي في بيت فضل وعلم ; فوالده هو الشيخ الصالح الزاهد الخاشع الأستاذ المحقق يوسف أبو يعقوب 1 ، وعنه أخذ الإمام مبادئ العلوم وكان ذلك في تلمسان حاضرة العلم يومها ، وطينة الأولياء والصالحين وجدة الإمام السنوسي لأبيه كانت حسنية النسب ، ولذا قد يلقب الإمام السنوسي بالحسني من جهة والدة أبيه (۲) ، وقال الشيخ الملالي في صفة والد الإمام : ( كان سيدي يوسف السنوسي رحمه الله تعالى ورضي عنه وعن شيخنا ابنه . . رجلاً صالحاً ورعاً ، خاشعاً الله تعالى ، زاهداً [في] الدنيا معرضاً عنها ، مقبلاً على طاعة الله تعالى ، سالم الصدر ، حسن الأخلاق ، ومبتسماً في وجه كل من لقاه ، حسن المعاشرة ، كريم الطبع )(٣) وكانت حرفة هذا الوالد المبارك هي إقراء القرآن للأولاد في المكتب ، ويظهر من كلام الشيخ الملالي أن عناية الله قد رافقته من طفولته ، فأجرى الله على يديه الكرامات وكشف عن بصيرته
Imam al-Sanusi grew up in a house of virtue and knowledge; his father is the righteous, ascetic, humble, investigating professor Yusuf Abu Ya'qub 1, and from him the Imam learned the principles of the sciences. That was in Tlemcen, the capital of knowledge at that time, and the clay of saints and righteous people. The grandmother of Imam al-Sanusi on his father's side was of Hasanid lineage, so the Imam is sometimes called al-Hasani on account of his paternal grandmother. (2) Al-Mallali said in describing the Imam's father: 'My master Yusuf al-Sanusi, may Allah have mercy on him and be pleased with him and with our shaykh his son… he was a righteous, devout man, humble before Allah, ascetic in the world, turning away from it, devoted to obedience to Allah, pure of heart, good in character, smiling at everyone he met, good in companionship, noble in nature.' (3) The occupation of this blessed father was teaching the Qur'an to children in the school. From al-Mallali's words, it appears that divine care accompanied him from his childhood, so Allah granted through him miracles and opened his insight.
Source pages p. 13

Commentary
This passage describes the family background of Imam al-Sanusi, emphasizing his upbringing in a household of virtue and knowledge. It mentions his father, Shaykh Yusuf Abu Ya'qub, a respected scholar and teacher, from whom Sanusi learned the basics of the sciences in Tlemcen, a city known for its learning and saints. The text also notes that Sanusi's paternal grandmother was of Hasanid descent (descendants of Imam Hasan), hence Sanusi is sometimes called 'al-Hasani'. A lengthy description from al-Mallali portrays the father as righteous, ascetic, humble, good-natured, and cheerful, devoted to worship. His profession was teaching Qur'an to children in a maktab. The passage further suggests that divine grace accompanied him, leading to miraculous occurrences and enlightenment. This biographical detail highlights the pious environment that shaped the young Sanusi.

Key Terms
يوسف أبو يعقوب
الحسني
الملالي
تلمسان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think that Sanusi's 'Husni' lineage indicates a direct patrilineal descent from Imam Hasan.
Correction: The passage clarifies that the lineage comes from his paternal grandmother, not a direct paternal line. Hence, 'al-Hasani' is a secondary nisba.

Checkpoint
What was the occupation of Imam Sanusi's father?
A) Judge (qadi)
B) Teaching Qur'an to children in a school
C) Sufi mystic
D) Merchant

Key Takeaway
Imam al-Sanusi was raised by a righteous, ascetic father who taught him the principles of science and provided a spiritually rich environment conducive to his future scholarly and spiritual achievements.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Your father or mentor: what is the single quality from Yusuf al-Sanusi's character that you feel most lacking in yourself? Would your family describe you as 'smiling at everyone you meet'? If not, what would it take for you to become that person starting today?
?
This passage shows that the prophet's grandson's lineage (Hasanid) was transmitted through a woman (grandmother). How often do you think of the women in your own lineage as carriers of your family's Islamic heritage? Write one concrete action you will take this week to honor a woman in your family who has passed down iman to you.
?
The father's occupation was teaching the Qur'an to children in a humble school. If you were to be completely honest with yourself, do you secretly seek fame or recognition in your Islamic work? In your next act of service, deliberately do it in a way that no one will know it was you. Will you?

Imam Sanusi's Teachers and Expertise in Qira'at
Listing Sanusi's teachers and his high standing in qira'at.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the scope of Imam Sanusi's traditional education and his particular distinction in Quranic recitations (qira'at).

Core Idea
Sanusi's scholarly authority was built on elite teachers and mastery of qira'at.

This brief passage gives us a window into Imam Sanusi's scholarly formation. It tells us that he studied under the leading scholars of his time and that he was especially distinguished in the field of Quranic recitations. Understanding this helps us appreciate the depth of his credentials before we examine his theological works.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice how concisely it presents two key facts: his teachers and his specialty.
2
Identify the two main points
The passage makes two distinct claims. First, that Sanusi studied under the great scholars of his time and received spiritual care. Second, that he had high standing in qira'at. Identify both.
This is a common structure in biographical notices.
3
Reflect on why this matters
Ask yourself: Why would a kalam theologian begin his biography by highlighting qira'at expertise? This shows his grounding in the Quranic sciences, which underpins his theological authority.
Consider how this connects to the broader aim of the coursebook.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
شيوخه · أخذ الإمام السنوسي العلم عن جلَّة علماء عصره ، ونالته عناية ولحظات الأولياء والصالحين · وكان للإمام السنوسي علو كعب في جمع القراءات
His shaykhs: Imam al-Sanusi acquired knowledge from the greats of the scholars of his time, and he received the care and glances of the saints and righteous. Imam al-Sanusi had high standing in the compilation of Quranic recitations.
Source pages p. 13

Commentary
This short passage, under the heading 'His Shaykhs', summarizes the breadth of Sanusi's education. It states that he studied under the leading scholars of his era and was also the object of attention from saints and righteous people, indicating both formal scholarship and spiritual care. It then highlights his particular distinction in the field of qira'at (Quranic recitations), noting that he had a high rank in 'jami' al-qira'at' (the compilation/knowledge of the various recitations). This points to his specialization in this discipline, which was a hallmark of his scholarly profile.

Key Terms
شيوخه
جمع القراءات
جلَّة علماء عصره

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Imam Sanusi taught Quranic recitations to many students.
Correction: The passage only states that he had high standing in the compilation of qira'at, not that he taught them.

Checkpoint
What does the passage highlight about Imam Sanusi's knowledge of qira'at?
A) He was a beginner in the field
B) He studied only with minor scholars
C) He had high standing in compiling qira'at
D) He taught qira'at to many students

Key Takeaway
Imam Sanusi's education combined learning from top scholars, spiritual care, and a special mastery of Quranic recitations.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have access to many teachers and resources today. Are you sincerely seeking knowledge from the 'greats' of your time, or are you settling for convenience? What step can you take this week to connect with a true scholar?
?
Sanusi benefited from the spiritual care of saints alongside formal study. Do you actively seek righteous company to bless your learning, or do you rely only on books? How can you invite barakah into your pursuit of knowledge?
?
Sanusi mastered qira'at, a demanding science. What one subject in Islam are you willing to master at that level, and what is stopping you from beginning today? Write down a concrete first step.
