Interrelations of the Three Wajib Attributes
Sanusi analyses the interrelations, overlaps, and distinctions of the three wajib attributes.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the three wajib attributes of messengers (truthfulness, trustworthiness, and delivery) relate to each other, what each uniquely adds, and what they share in common.

Core Idea
Each wajib attribute of messengers has a distinct function that the others do not cover.

Sanusi now deepens our understanding of the three essential attributes of messengers: truthfulness (ṣidq), trustworthiness (amānah), and delivery (tablīgh). Rather than treating them as separate items, he shows how they interlock and complement each other. This passage is a precise logical analysis that reveals the unique contribution of each attribute and the overlaps between them.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage closely
Read the Arabic passage once through, then the translation. Pay attention to the pattern of 'adds to... by preventing...'.
Notice that each attribute is defined both positively and negatively.
2
Identify the three attributes being compared
The passage compares truthfulness (ṣidq), trustworthiness (amānah), and delivery (tablīgh). Note which one is called 'the first obligatory attribute'.
3
Trace the specific additions for each attribute
Follow the text to see what ṣidq adds to amānah and to tablīgh; what amānah adds to ṣidq and to tablīgh; and what tablīgh adds to ṣidq and to amānah.
Keep a mental or written note of each addition.
4
Reflect on the concluding remark
After the detailed comparisons, Sanusi says you can derive what the three share, what two share without the third, and what each one adds over the sum of the other two. Try to infer those from the analysis.
This is the key takeaway – the attributes have both common ground and unique functions.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فالواجبُ الأَوَّلُ يزيدُ على الأمانة بمنع الكذب سهواً ، ويزيدُ على التبليغ بمنع الزيادة على ما أُمروا بتبليغه عمداً أو نسياناً ، وتزيد الأمانة على الصدق بمنع وقوع المخالفة في كذب اللسانِ ، وعلى التبليغ بمنع المخالفة في غيرِ التبليغ ، ويزيدُ التبليغ على الصدق بمنع ترْكِ شيء مما أُمروا بتبليغه عمداً أو نسياناً ، مع لزومِ الصدق فيما بلغوا مِنْ ذلك ، ويزيد على الأمانة بمنع تركِ شيء مما أُمروا بتبليغه نسياناً . ولا يخفى عليك بعد هذا ما تشترك فيه الثلاثةُ ، وما يشترك فيه اثنان منها ، دونَ الثالث ، وما يزيدُ به كلُّ واحد منها على مجموع الباقيين .
The first obligatory [attribute, i.e., al-ṣidq] adds to trustworthiness (al-amānah) by preventing [inadvertent] lying (al-kadhib sahwan), and adds to delivery (al-tablīgh) by preventing addition to what they were commanded to deliver, intentionally or forgetfully. Trustworthiness adds to truthfulness by preventing the occurrence of violation in the lying of the tongue, and adds to delivery by preventing violation in other than delivery. Delivery adds to truthfulness by preventing the omission of something they were commanded to deliver, intentionally or forgetfully, with the necessity of truthfulness in what they deliver from that, and adds to trustworthiness by preventing the omission of something they were commanded to deliver forgetfully. And it is not hidden from you after this what the three share in common, and what two of them share without the third, and what each one adds over the sum of the remaining two.
Source pages p. 98

Commentary
This passage provides a detailed comparative analysis of the three wajib attributes: truthfulness (ṣidq), trustworthiness (amānah), and delivery (tablīgh). The author systematically explains what each attribute adds beyond the others. The first attribute (ṣidq) adds to amānah by preventing inadvertent lying (kadhib sahwan), which amānah does not cover. It adds to tablīgh by preventing adding to the message. Amānah adds to ṣidq by preventing contradiction in the tongue's lie, and to tablīgh by preventing violation in actions other than delivery. Tablīgh adds to ṣidq by preventing omission (whether intentional or forgetful), and to amānah by preventing forgetful omission. At the end, the author notes that from this analysis one can derive the commonalities between the three, the commonalities between any two, and what each one uniquely adds beyond the sum of the other two.

Key Terms
الزيادة
الاشتراك
الكذب سهواً

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The three wajib attributes are identical in meaning.
Correction: Each attribute has a unique function that the others do not cover, as shown by the analysis.

Checkpoint
What does al-ṣidq (truthfulness) add that al-amānah (trustworthiness) does not cover?
A) Preventing intentional lies.
B) Preventing inadvertent lies (kadhib sahwan).
C) Preventing omission of the message.
D) Preventing addition to the message.

Key Takeaway
The three wajib attributes of messengers are distinct yet complementary, each covering a different potential flaw in prophetic communication.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you speak about your religion, do you guard against both intentional and inadvertent errors? How often do you correct a mistake you made unintentionally, and does your conscience consider that a breach of amānah?
?
In your daily interactions, do you act on the principle that truthfulness must be paired with trustworthiness and full delivery? Where have you omitted part of a message that should have been conveyed, and what did that reveal about your own character?
?
Which of the three attributes—ṣidq, amānah, tablīgh—is weakest in your own life right now? What specific step will you take today to strengthen it, knowing that the prophets embodied all three perfectly as a standard?

Impossible and Permissible Attributes for Messengers
Sanusi enumerates impossible and permissible attributes for messengers with safeguards.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the impossible and permissible attributes for messengers, including the safeguards that clarify the proper understanding of their human experiences.

Core Idea
Messengers are immune to lying and treachery but experience human needs like illness.

Sanusi completes his discussion of prophetic attributes by addressing what cannot be attributed to messengers (the opposites of the three wajib attributes) and what can: human accidents that do not diminish their rank. He then introduces three safeguards to prevent common theological errors, such as deifying Jesus or denying the compatibility of humanity with prophethood.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to understand the two categories and the safeguards.
Notice the concise style and technical terms like 'aḍdād' and 'iḥtirāzāt'.
2
Identify the two categories
Locate the sections on impossible (mustaḥīl) and permissible (jāʾiz) attributes. Note that impossible is defined as the opposites of the three wajib attributes.
The three wajib attributes are truthfulness, trustworthiness, and conveying the message.
3
Examine the evidence for permissible attributes
Sanusi gives evidence: observation (mushāhadah) of illness in prophets and hidden benefits. Reflect on why this empirical proof is sufficient.
This shows that kalam uses both rational and empirical evidence.
4
Study the three safeguards (iḥtirāzāt)
Each safeguard clarifies a term to prevent a specific doctrinal error. The first guards against Christian deification of Jesus, the second against the pre-Islamic denial of humanity in prophethood, and the third (partial) guards against attributing degrading traits.
The third safeguard is incomplete in the text but the purpose is clear from context.
5
Reflect on the theological significance
Consider how these safeguards establish a balanced view of prophets: fully human yet protected from sin and deficiency.
This balance is crucial for Islamic orthodoxy.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وأما المستحيل في حقهم عليهم الصلاة والسلام : فأضداد هذه الثلاثة وأما الجائز في حقهم عليهم الصلاة والسلام : فالأعراض البشريَّةُ التي لا تنافي علوّ رتبتهم ؛ كالمرضِ ونحوه ، بدليل مشاهدة ذلك فيهم ، وفي اتصافهم بها فوائد لا تخفى . فقولنا : ( الأعراضُ ( احتراز مِنْ مذهب النصارى في وصفهم عيسى عليه السلام بالصفة القديمة وقولنا : ( البشريَّةُ ( احتراز من اعتقادِ الجاهلية أنَّ البشريّة تنافي الرسالة وقولنا : ( التي لا تنافي علو رتبتهم ( احترازُ مِن اعتقادِ اليهود وكثيرٍ مِنْ جهلةِ المؤرّخين والمفسرين اتصاف
As for what is impossible (al-mustaḥīl) for them, upon them peace and blessings: they are the opposites (aḍdād) of these three [wajib attributes]. As for what is permissible (al-jāʾiz) for them, upon them peace and blessings: they are the human accidents (al-aʿrāḍ al-bashariyyah) that do not conflict with the loftiness of their rank, such as illness and the like, as evidenced by the observation of that in them, and in their being described with them there are benefits that are not hidden. Our statement 'the accidents' (al-aʿrāḍ) is a safeguard (iḥtirāz) against the doctrine of the Christians in their description of Jesus, peace be upon him, with the eternal attribute. And our statement 'the human' (al-bashariyyah) is a safeguard against the belief of the pre-Islamic ignorant that humanity contradicts messengerhood. And our statement 'that do not conflict with the loftiness of their rank' is a safeguard against the belief of the Jews and many ignorant historians and exegetes describing...
Source pages p. 98

Commentary
This passage covers the remaining categories: impossible (mustaḥīl) and permissible (jāʾiz) attributes for messengers. For impossible, it is the direct opposites (aḍdād) of the three wajib attributes: lying, treachery, and concealment. For permissible, it lists human accidents (aʿrāḍ bashariyyah) that do not contradict their high rank, like illness. The evidence is empirical observation of such states in prophets. Then the author provides three iḥtirāzāt (safeguards) to clarify the wording. 'Al-aʿrāḍ' (accidents) excludes the Christian belief that Jesus had an eternal attribute (divinity). 'Al-bashariyyah' excludes the pre-Islamic belief that humanity is incompatible with prophethood. 'Illā lan yuqāraba' excludes the Jewish and ignorant historians who attribute to prophets.

Key Terms
المستحيل
الجائز
الأعراض البشرية
احتراز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Prophets cannot experience any human limitations like illness.
Correction: Prophets can experience human accidents like illness as long as they do not conflict with their lofty rank.

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi specify 'al-bashariyyah' (human) in defining the permissible attributes?
A) To exclude Christians who attribute divinity to Jesus.
B) To reject the belief that humanity contradicts messengerhood.
C) To emphasize that prophets are fully human.
D) To limit permissible attributes to physical ones.

Key Takeaway
The permissible attributes for messengers are human accidents that do not contradict their rank, and the safeguards prevent extreme views on either side.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?

?

?
The passage guards against the Christian belief that Jesus had an eternal attribute. Is there any belief or feeling in your heart that elevates any prophet beyond the human station that Allah chose for them? How can you actively purify your aqeedah from such traces?

The Three Obligatory Attributes of Prophets
Sanusi enumerates the three obligatory attributes of prophets and their logical relations.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three attributes that are obligatory for prophets—truthfulness, trustworthiness, and conveyance—and how they interrelate.

Core Idea
Prophets must be truthful, trustworthy, and convey the message fully—even if forgetful.

Sanusi begins by establishing the core attributes that every prophet must possess. These attributes define their mission and ensure the integrity of revelation. In this passage, he enumerates three obligatory qualities and explains how they relate to one another.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage about the three attributes
Read the Arabic and translation to get a sense of the classification.
2
Identify each attribute
Find truthfulness, trustworthiness, and conveyance. Note the specific requirement for each.
Conveyance includes both deliberate and forgetful transmission.
3
Understand the interrelation
Reflect on what the three share, what two share without the third, and what each uniquely adds.
4
Apply the concept
Consider why this classification is important for understanding prophetic infallibility.
These attributes ensure the message is delivered reliably.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أُمروا بتبليغه عمداً أو نسياناً ، مع لزومِ الصدق فيما بلغوا مِنْ ذلك ، ويزيد على الأمانة بمنع تركِ شيء مما أُمروا بتبليغه نسياناً .
ولا يخفى عليك بعد هذا ما تشترك فيه الثلاثةُ ، وما يشترك فيه اثنان منها ، دونَ الثالث ، وما يزيدُ به كلُّ واحد منها على مجموع الباقيين .
وأما المستحيل في حقهم عليهم الصلاة والسلام :
فأضداد هذه الثلاثة
وأما الجائز في حقهم عليهم الصلاة والسلام : فالأعراض البشريَّةُ التي لا تنافي علوّ رتبتهم ؛ كالمرضِ ونحوه ، بدليل مشاهدة ذلك فيهم ، وفي اتصافهم بها فوائد لا تخفى .
فقولنا : ( الأعراضُ ( احتراز مِنْ مذهب النصارى في وصفهم عيسى عليه السلام بالصفة القديمة
وقولنا : ( البشريَّةُ ( احتراز من اعتقادِ الجاهلية أنَّ البشريّة تنافي الرسالة
وقولنا : ( التي لا تنافي علو رتبتهم ( احترازُ مِن اعتقادِ اليهود وكثيرٍ مِنْ جهلةِ المؤرّخين والمفسرين اتصاف الأنبياء عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام بنقيصة المعصية والمكروه ونحوهما
•
وبهذا تعرفُ أنَّ كلَّ ما أوهم في حقهم أو حق الملائكة نقصاً مِنَ الكتاب أو السنة .. وجب تأويله .
They were commanded to convey it deliberately or forgetfully, while truthfulness is necessary in what they conveyed from that, and it adds to trustworthiness by prohibiting leaving out anything they were commanded to convey forgetfully. And it is not hidden from you after this what the three share, and what two of them share without the third, and what each one of them adds over the sum of the remaining two. As for the impossible in their right (upon them peace and blessings): they are the opposites of these three. As for the permissible in their right (upon them peace and blessings): they are human accidents that do not contradict their high rank, like illness and the like, evidenced by observing that in them, and in their being characterized by them are benefits that are not hidden. Our saying: 'accidents' is a caveat against the doctrine of the Christians in their describing Jesus (peace be upon him) with the eternal attribute. Our saying: 'human' is a caveat against the belief of the ignorant that humanity contradicts prophethood. Our saying: 'that do not contradict their high rank' is a caveat against the belief of the Jews and many of the ignorant historians and exegetes that the prophets (upon them peace and blessings) are characterized by the deficiency of sin, the disliked, and the like. And by this you know that everything that implies deficiency in their right or the right of the angels from the Book or the Sunnah must be interpreted.
Source pages p. 98

Commentary
This passage is a classification and explanation of the attributes of prophets (anbiya) into three categories: obligatory (wajib), impossible (mustahil), and permissible (ja'iz). It begins by discussing the obligatory attributes, specifically focusing on three: truthfulness (sidq), trustworthiness (amana), and conveyance (tabligh). The text explains that prophets were commanded to convey the message whether deliberately or forgetfully—meaning they cannot omit anything even if they forget, which is an extension of trustworthiness. Truthfulness is necessary in what they convey. Then it notes that these three attributes share commonalities and differences, and each one adds something unique over the other two. Next, the impossible attributes are simply the opposites of these three (e.g., lying, betrayal, concealment of the message). The permissible attributes are human accidents (a'rad bashariyya) that do not contradict their high rank, such as illness, which is evidenced by observation. The text then provides three caveats (ihtirazat) to the definition: 'accidents' excludes the Christian belief that Jesus had an eternal attribute; 'human' excludes those who think humanity negates prophethood; 'that do not contradict their high rank' excludes those who attribute sin or disliked actions to prophets. Finally, it concludes that any apparent deficiency regarding prophets or angels in the Quran or Sunnah must be interpreted (ta'wil) to preserve their perfection.

Key Terms
الصدق
الأمانة
التبليغ

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what does the attribute of conveyance (tabligh) require of a prophet?
A) To convey only what they remember
B) To convey deliberately but not forgetfully
C) To convey deliberately or forgetfully without omitting anything
D) To convey only the easy parts

Key Takeaway
Prophets must possess truthfulness, trustworthiness, and fullness of conveyance, with each attribute reinforcing the others.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If prophets are commanded to convey even what they might forget, what does that say about your own responsibility to convey the truth of Islam completely, even when it's difficult? What part of the message do you avoid sharing because you think people won't accept it?
?
The passage links truthfulness to trustworthiness: being honest in what you say strengthens your reliability. In your daily interactions, where do you downplay or omit truths to make things easier for yourself? How does that undermine your trustworthiness in the eyes of others and of Allah?

What is Impossible and Permissible for Prophets
Sanusi classifies the impossible and permissible attributes of prophets with caveats and the rule of interpretation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what attributes are impossible for prophets (their opposites of the obligatory) and what is permissible for them (human accidents that do not contradict their rank), along with the principle that any apparent deficiency must be interpreted.

Core Idea
Prophets cannot have any deficiency, but they can have ordinary human conditions like illness, and any scriptural suggestion otherwise must be interpreted.

Having established the obligatory attributes, Sanusi now turns to what is impossible and permissible for prophets. The impossible attributes are simply the opposites of the obligatory ones. The permissible attributes are human experiences that do not lower their status. He also provides three important caveats to ensure correct understanding and concludes with a rule for interpreting apparent deficiencies in revelation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage on impossible and permissible attributes
Read the Arabic and translation to see the full classification.
2
Understand the impossible attributes
Note that the impossible are the direct opposites of the three obligatory attributes.
3
Analyze the permissible attributes
Identify the definition of permissible as human accidents that do not contradict high rank, and study the three caveats.
Each caveat refutes a specific false belief.
4
Learn the rule of ta'wil
Understand that any apparent deficiency in scripture must be interpreted to preserve the perfection of prophets and angels.
5
Reflect on the benefits
Consider why these caveats and the rule of interpretation are important for correct belief.

Source Text

Key Takeaway
Prophets are free from all deficiencies, and any scriptural hint otherwise requires interpretation to affirm their perfection.

?

?
The passage refutes the idea that being human contradicts being a prophet. Do you sometimes feel that your own human flaws and limitations prevent you from being close to God or fulfilling His purpose for you? How does this teaching challenge that feeling, and what will you change in your self-perception?
?
Sanusi calls for interpreting any apparent deficiency in prophets. Is there a belief or practice in your life that you cling to despite it conflicting with a higher truth you know? Are you willing to reinterpret that belief through the lens of this principle, even if it means changing your behavior or convictions?

The Obligation of Ta'wil for Apparent Deficiencies
Sanusi states the obligation of ta'wil for texts implying deficiency in prophets or angels.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the obligation of figurative interpretation (ta'wil) for any scriptural text that appears to imply a deficiency in prophets or angels.

Core Idea
Any text implying deficiency in prophets or angels must be interpreted, not rejected.

This lesson presents a crucial hermeneutical principle in Sunni theology: when a Qur'anic verse or hadith seems to attribute a deficiency to a prophet or an angel, it must be interpreted figuratively (ta'wil). This preserves both the authority of revelation and the doctrine of 'isma (impeccability). As you read Sanusi's words, notice how the principle flows from the established truth that prophets are free from sin and disliked things.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Pay attention to the flow from the initial statement about prophets to the rule of ta'wil.
The first phrase is a continuation from the previous page.
2
Identify the key claim
Find the sentence that states what must be done with any text that suggests a deficiency. Underline or note the phrase 'وجب تأويله'.
3
Connect to 'isma
Reflect on why ta'wil is necessary: because prophets and angels are impeccable ('isma). The passage begins by affirming they are free from sin and disliked things.
4
Distinguish ta'wil from rejection
Note that the rule requires interpretation, not rejection. This is a key difference: the text remains authoritative but is understood metaphorically.
A common mistake is to think such texts should be dismissed.
5
Review the closing doxology
The passage ends with praise of the Prophet, his family, companions, and the traditional formula. Reflect on the devotional tone as a fitting conclusion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الأنبياء عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام بنقيصة المعصية والمكروه ونحوهما • وبهذا تعرفُ أنَّ كلَّ ما أوهم في حقهم أو حق الملائكة نقصاً مِنَ الكتاب أو السنة .. وجب تأويله . وأفضلهم سيّدنا ومولانا محمَّدٌ صَلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ وعلى آلِهِ عدَدَ ما ذكرَهُ الذاكرون وغفل عن ذكره الغافلون ، ورضي الله تعالى عن أصحاب رسولِ اللهِ أجمعين ، وسلام على المرسلين ، والحمدُ للهِ ربِّ العالمينَ .
The prophets, upon them prayer and peace, with deficiency of sin and disliked things and their like. • And by this you know that everything that suggests in their right or the right of the angels deficiency from the Book or the Sunnah .. its interpretation (ta'wil) is obligatory. And the best of them is our master and patron Muhammad, may God bless him and his family, to the number of what the rememberers remember and the heedless neglect, and may God be pleased with all the companions of the Messenger of God, and peace upon the messengers, and praise be to God, Lord of the worlds.
Source pages p. 99

Commentary
This passage concludes a discussion on the impeccable status of prophets and angels. The first phrase (likely continuing from earlier) establishes that prophets are not associated with deficiency, sin, or disliked things. The author then draws a hermeneutical rule: any text from the Qur'an or Sunnah that appears to imply a deficiency (naqs) in prophets or angels must be interpreted figuratively (ta'wil). This principle safeguards the doctrine of 'isma (impeccability) while maintaining the authority of revelation. The passage ends with praise of the Prophet Muhammad, a prayer for him and his family, and a request for God's pleasure upon the Companions, followed by the traditional closing formula.

Key Terms
تأويل (ta'wil)
نقص (naqs)
عصمة ('isma)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that any text implying deficiency should be rejected as unreliable.
Correction: The author insists on interpretation (ta'wil) rather than rejection, preserving both the text's authority and the doctrine of 'isma.

Checkpoint
According to this passage, what must be done with anything from the Book or Sunnah that seems to imply a deficiency in prophets or angels?
A) It must be rejected as unreliable.
B) It must be interpreted figuratively (ta'wil).
C) It must be accepted literally.
D) It must be ignored.

Key Takeaway
When a scriptural text seems to imply a deficiency in a prophet or angel, it must be interpreted figuratively, not taken literally or rejected.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever come across a verse or hadith that made you question the perfection of a prophet? What was your immediate reaction? Now that you know ta'wil is obligatory, what will you do the next time that happens? Will you seek knowledge or rely on your own misunderstanding?
?
Think of a specific story about a prophet that has been used by critics to claim they sinned. By not learning the accepted ta'wil, are you leaving yourself exposed to doubt? What step will you take today to learn the correct interpretation from a reliable scholar?
?
The passage ends with praising the Prophet Muhammad as the best. Do you truly place him above all others in your heart? If your actions do not reflect this – for example, if you follow social media trends over his sunnah – what can you change starting now to honour his station?

Sanusi glorifies the Prophet Muhammad with a salutation and blessing.

Learning Objective

Core Idea
Sending peace and blessings upon the Prophet is an act of veneration and acknowledgment of his supreme rank.

This lesson begins with a short but profound salutation that sets the tone for the entire work. Before delving into theological arguments, Sanusi honors the Prophet Muhammad, acknowledging him as the seal of the prophets and the imam of the messengers. This greeting is not merely a formality; it establishes the Prophet's central role in Islamic belief and the reverence due to him.

Guided Path
1

These titles affirm finality and leadership.
3

The Prophet as the Supreme Door to Allah's Pleasure
Sanusi venerates the Prophet as the supreme means to Allah's pleasure and the purpose of creation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi positions the Prophet Muhammad as the supreme means to Allah's pleasure and the very purpose of creation.

Core Idea
The Prophet is not just a guide; he is the door and the reason for creation.

Sanusi opens this passage by declaring that the highest of all perfections is attaining Allah's pleasure and safety from His wrath. He then presents the Prophet Muhammad as the unique, incomparable door to this happiness in both worlds. The passage culminates in the profound statement that all creation—earthly and heavenly—was created for the Prophet's sake, and his intercession removes all calamities.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Pay attention to the rhetorical question near the end.
The passage uses a question to emphasize the Prophet's central role.
2
Identify the central claim
Find the sentence that states the highest perfection. Then find the sentence that calls the Prophet a 'great open door'.
The Prophet is described as incomparable.
3
Analyze the rhetorical question
The author asks 'How? And for his sake Allah created all perfections...' Understand that this answers why the Prophet is the supreme door.
This connects the Prophet to the purpose of creation.
4
Reflect on intercession
The passage concludes with the Prophet's intercession that removes calamities. Consider how intercession reinforces his role as the door.
Intercession is a key concept in Sunni theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
سطور في تعظيم وتوقير النبي عليه الصلاة والسلام والصلاة والسلام على سيدنا ومولانا محمد خاتم النبيين وإمام المرسلين . لا شكّ أنَّ أعلى الكمالاتِ الحادثة كلها وأدومها كمالُ الفوز برضا مولانا جل وعلا والسلامةِ مِنْ غضبِهِ ، وقد جعل الله سبحانَهُ بفضلِهِ نبيَّنا وسيدنا ومولانا محمَّداً صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ باباً عظيماً مفتوحاً لذلك في الدنيا والآخرة لا يقاربه باب ، ولا يستغني عن التعلُّق بأذيالِهِ والإيواء إلى عتبةِ حَرَمِهِ وبابِهِ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الأعداء أو الأحباب . كيفَ ومِنْ أَجْلِهِ  َخلَقَ اللهُ تعالى الكمال الدنيوي والأخروي والعلوي والسفلي ؟! وبشفاعته الكبرى في الآخرة وما بعدها مِنْ شفاعاتِهِ : تنقشع أنواع الكروب ، وترتفع بفضل الله سبحانه أسبابها ، وتتجلى شموس نِعَمِ
Lines on venerating and honoring the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him. May blessings and peace be upon our master and lord Muhammad, the Seal of the Prophets and the Imam of the Messengers. There is no doubt that the highest of all originated perfections and the most enduring is the perfection of attaining the pleasure of our Lord, Glory be to Him, and safety from His wrath. And Allah, Exalted, by His grace, has made our Prophet, our master, and our lord Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, a great open door to that in this world and the Hereafter, no door is comparable to it, and none, whether enemies or friends, can dispense with clinging to his arms and seeking refuge at the threshold of his sanctuary and his door. How? And for his sake Allah created all worldly and otherworldly, celestial and terrestrial perfections. And by his great intercession in the Hereafter and his subsequent intercessions, various kinds of calamities (kurab) are removed, and their causes are lifted by the grace of Allah, and the suns of blessings become manifest...
Source pages p. 108

Commentary
This passage is a veneration of the Prophet Muhammad. It begins with blessings on him, then states the highest perfection is gaining Allah's pleasure and safety from His wrath. Allah has made the Prophet a unique door to this. The passage then asks a rhetorical question: How is this? And answers: For his sake Allah created all perfections. Then it mentions his intercession. The argument establishes the central role of the Prophet in both worldly and eternal felicity.

Key Terms
رضا مولانا – pleasure of our Lord
باب – door
شفاعة – intercession
الكروب – calamities

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the Prophet is merely a guide or messenger with no special role beyond delivering the message.
Correction: This passage asserts that the Prophet is the very door to Allah's pleasure and the purpose for which all creation was made, making his role far more central.

Checkpoint

A) The Prophet's status.
B) Gaining Allah's pleasure and safety from His wrath.
C) Knowledge of the Hereafter.
D) Miracles.

Key Takeaway
The Prophet Muhammad is the unique means to Allah's pleasure and the purpose of all creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
If even 'enemies and friends' cannot dispense with the Prophet, what does that mean for your relationship with him? Are you treating him as a friend or as someone optional? What step will you take to become a true friend of the Prophet?

Obligation of Gratitude and Virtue of Salawat
Author states the obligation to thank Allah for the Prophet and the merit of sending blessings.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why thanking Allah for the Prophet and sending blessings upon him are among the most important acts of devotion.

Core Idea
Gratitude for the Prophet and sending salawat are the highest means to Allah's favor.

This passage from Sanusi's work highlights the profound obligation to thank Allah for the blessing of the Prophet Muhammad (saw) and the immense virtue of sending blessings upon him. It grounds this practice in both rational recognition of the Prophet's station and authentic hadith, even linking it to spiritual development. Let's explore these teachings.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Carefully read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Notice the author's tone and the key terms like 'tawassul' and 'salawat'.
The passage begins with a strong statement about obligation.
2
Identify the two main claims
Find where the author states the obligation of praising Allah for the Prophet and the virtue of sending blessings. Also note the proof from authentic hadith.
The conditional argument 'If you know... then you know' is important.
3
Reflect on the practical implication
Consider how the author uses the hadith and the Sufi saying to encourage frequent salawat. Ask yourself how this applies to your own spiritual practice.
The saying about those without teachers is especially striking.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
حمد الله تعالى على نعمة النبي عليه الصلاة والسلام من أوجب الواجبات، والتوسل به من أعلى الوسائل للفوز بعلي الدرجات. وإذا عرفت أن منزلة سيدنا ومولانا محمد صلى الله عليه وسلم عند مولانا جل وعلا بهذه المثابة.. عرفت أن حمده تعالى وشكره على إنعامه به على الخلق من أوجب الواجبات، وأن التوسل إليه تعالى بحب هذا السيد، والتعظيم له، وكثرة الصلاة والتسليم عليه.. من أعلى الوسائل للأمن من المخوفات، والفوز بعلي الدرجات. ولو لم يكن للصلاة عليه من الفضل العظيم إلا ما ورد في الصحاح: أن من صلى على سيدنا ومولانا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام مرة صلى الله عليه بها عشراً.. لكان كافياً، كيف وقد ورد في فضلها العظيم ما ألف أئمتنا فيه على الانفراد تأليف عديدة؟! وقد رأيت لبعض أئمة التصرف: أن من فقد شيوخ التربية.. فليكثر من الصلاة على النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم؛ فإنه يصل به إلى مقصده، ولعله أخذ ذلك من قوله صلى الله عليه وسلم لأبي هريرة رضي الله عنه عندما التزم أن يجعل جميع صلاته للنبي صلى الله عليه وسلم: (إذا تُكفى همك، ويغفر ذنبك).
Praising Allah Almighty for the blessing of the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, is one of the most obligatory duties, and seeking intercession through him is among the highest means for attaining lofty degrees. And if you know that the station of our master and lord Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, with our Lord, Glorified and Exalted, is of such magnitude... then you know that praising and thanking Him for His bestowal of him upon creation is one of the most obligatory duties, and that seeking intercession to Him through love of this master, veneration of him, and frequent prayer and salutation upon him... are among the highest means for safety from fears and for attaining lofty degrees. And if there were no great virtue for prayer upon him except what has been related in the authentic collections: that whoever prays upon our master and lord Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, once, Allah prays upon him ten times by it... that would be sufficient. How so, when there has been related concerning its great virtue what our imams have authored individually in numerous works?! And I have seen from some of the masters of spiritual discipline: that whoever lacks the teachers of spiritual training... then let him increase prayer upon the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him; for it will bring him to his goal. And perhaps he took that from his saying, peace and blessings be upon him, to Abu Hurairah, may Allah be pleased with him, when he committed to making all his prayers upon the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him: (Then your concern will be taken care of, and your sin will be forgiven).
Source pages p. 112

Commentary
This passage is a devotional exhortation and proof of the high station of the Prophet Muhammad (saw). The author begins by stating that praising Allah for the blessing of the Prophet is one of the most obligatory duties, and that seeking intercession (tawassul) through him is among the highest means to attain lofty degrees in the Hereafter. He then draws a conditional argument: if you truly understand the immense rank of the Prophet with Allah, then you will recognize the obligation of thanking Allah for sending him, and that the means to divine protection and high ranks are through love, veneration, and frequent prayers and greetings upon him. The author supports this with a hadith from the authentic collections: whoever sends blessings upon the Prophet once, Allah sends ten blessings upon him. Even this alone would be sufficient incentive. But further, the author notes that the scholars have written many works on the great virtue of salawat, implying even more merit. He then quotes a saying from some Sufi masters: if a person lacks a spiritual teacher (shaykh), they should increase salawat, for it will lead them to their goal. He suggests this is derived from the hadith where the Prophet told Abu Hurairah that if he makes all his prayer salawat upon the Prophet, his worldly concerns will be removed and his sins forgiven. Thus, the passage emphasizes the centrality of gratitude and devotion to the Prophet as a means to Allah's favor.

Key Terms
الصلاة على النبي
التوسل
حمد الله

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Thinking that tawassul (seeking intercession) is limited to asking directly, excluding love and veneration.
Correction: The passage includes love, veneration, and frequent salawat as part of tawassul, which are among the highest means.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what is one of the most obligatory duties?
A) Fasting in Ramadan
B) Praising Allah for the blessing of the Prophet
C) Performing Hajj
D) Giving charity

Key Takeaway
Thanking Allah for the Prophet and sending salawat are obligatory duties that bring immense reward and spiritual benefit.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believed that sending just one salawat brings ten mercies from Allah, would you let a day pass without sending hundreds? What is stopping you?
?
The passage says tawassul through love and veneration is among the highest means. Is your love for the Prophet evident in your daily actions? What one change would show that love more clearly to yourself and to others?
?
When you face a trial, do you immediately turn to salawat as your first means of relief? If not, why not? What would it take to make sending blessings upon the Prophet your instant refuge?

Why the Prophet is Called Sayyid Before Mawla
Sanusi explains why the Prophet is called sayyid before mawla in the creed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the logical and theological reason why the Prophet is described as 'sayyid' before 'mawlā' in the Islamic creed.

Core Idea
In times of need, refuge comes before help — so sayyid precedes mawlā.

In the traditional creedal formulations, the Prophet is given two titles: sayyid (master) and mawlā (protector). But they are not placed arbitrarily. Sanusi explains a deep reason grounded in human psychology and divine order: we first turn to him for refuge, then he helps. This short lesson unfolds that reasoning.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic text and its literal translation. Notice the key terms 'sayyid' and 'mawlā'.
2
Understand the two roles
Identify that sayyid is the one sought for refuge, mawlā is the helper. Note the logical order: seeking refuge precedes receiving help.
This distinction is central to the argument.
3
Apply to the Prophet's mission
See how the Prophet fulfills both roles: guiding in this world and interceding in the Hereafter.
The passage mentions his guidance and his praiseworthy station.
4
Reflect on the supplication
Conclude with the prayer asking for benefit from his station. This shows the practical aim of the creedal priority.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وإنما قدَّمنا في أصل العقيدة وصفه عليه الصلاة والسلام بالسيد على وصفه بالمولى ؛ لأنَّ السيّد هو الذي يُفزَعُ إليهِ في كلِّ مُهِمَّ والمولى هو الناصرُ ، ولا شكّ أنَّ الفزعَ في المُهِمَّ إلى السيّدِ يكونُ أوَّلاً ، ونصرتَهُ لِمَنْ فَزِعَ إليه في نيلِ مُهِمِّهِ تكون ثانياً بعد فزعِهِ إِليه ، ولا شكّ أنَّهُ عليهِ الصلاةُ والسلامُ مَفزَعُ الخلائق ، وناصرهم في الدنيا؛ بَيَّنَ لهم مِنْ طريقِ النجاة ، وعلَّمَهُم مِنْ أنواع الهداياتِ ، حتى تركهم على المَحَجَّةِ البيضاء التي لا غبار عليها، ومَفزَعُهُم وناصرُهُم في الآخرة؛ إذ له المقام المحمود هناك، والشفاعة المتكثرة المشفّعة، والمقالة المسموعة، والسؤالُ المُعْطَى، والجاه الأعظم، والمنزلة العليا، نسأل الله تعالى أن يَهَب لنا نصيباً وافراً مِنَ النفع بسيادتِهِ وجاهه الأعظم دنيا وأخرى
And we only gave precedence in the original creed (Aṣl al-ʿAqīdah) to describing him, peace and blessings be upon him, as 'sayyid' (master) over describing him as 'mawlā' (protector/patron) because the sayyid is the one to whom one flees for refuge in every important matter, and the mawlā is the helper. There is no doubt that fleeing in an important matter to the sayyid occurs first, and his help to the one who flees to him in attaining his important matter occurs second after his fleeing to him. And there is no doubt that he, peace and blessings be upon him, is the refuge of all creatures and their helper in this world: he showed them the path of salvation and taught them the types of guidance until he left them on the clear path that has no dust upon it. And he is their refuge and helper in the Hereafter: for he has the Praiseworthy Station (al-Maqām al-Maḥmūd) there, the abundant accepted intercession, the listened-to speech, the given request, the greatest rank, and the high station. We ask Allah the Exalted to grant us an abundant portion of benefit through his masterhood and his greatest rank, in this world and the Hereafter.
Source pages p. 118

Commentary
This passage explains why in the creed the Prophet is described as 'sayyid' (master) before 'mawlā' (helper). The reason is lexical and logical: the sayyid is the one to whom one turns for refuge in difficulties, and the mawlā is the one who then provides help. Since turning for refuge comes first, the title sayyid is mentioned first. The Prophet is both the refuge and the helper in this world and the Hereafter. In this world, he guided people; in the Hereafter, he will intercede. The passage concludes with a supplication for benefit from his station.

Key Terms
سيد
مولى
فزع
نصر
مقام محمود
شفاعة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That 'sayyid' and 'mawlā' are synonymous titles for the Prophet.
Correction: The passage clarifies that 'sayyid' refers to the one sought for refuge, while 'mawlā' is the helper, and the order reflects the logical priority of seeking refuge before receiving help.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why is the Prophet called 'sayyid' before 'mawlā'?
A) Because sayyid is a higher rank
B) Because seeking refuge with the sayyid comes first, then his help follows
C) Because mawlā only applies in the Hereafter
D) Because sayyid indicates his lineage

Key Takeaway
The order of the Prophet's titles in the creed reflects the logical sequence of seeking refuge first and receiving help second.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face an important matter in your life, do you first flee to the Prophet as your sayyid, seeking his intercession and example—or do you rely on your own planning before turning to him? What practical step can you take this week to make him your first refuge?
?
The Prophet is your helper (mawlā) after you flee to him. Are you truly allowing him to help you by following the guidance he taught, or do you selectively obey only what is convenient? Which command of his are you neglecting that, if obeyed, would bring you the help you desperately need?
?
Sanusi prays for an abundant portion of benefit through the Prophet's masterhood and rank. Honestly examine your heart: do you truly long for that benefit, or are you content with a distant admiration? What would change in your daily life if you genuinely expected to receive his help in every important matter?

The Meaning of 'Seal of the Prophets'
Sanusi defines the Seal of the Prophets and lists its implications.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the meaning of the title 'Seal of the Prophets' and its logical implications for the finality of prophethood and messengership, as well as the honors bestowed upon the Prophet Muhammad (PBUH).

Core Idea
The Seal of the Prophets closes prophethood and necessarily messengership, completing all excellences in one final law.

Sanusi opens a key doctrine: what does it mean that Muhammad (PBUH) is the 'Seal of the Prophets'? The phrase is more than a title; it establishes the finality of prophethood. From this, Sanusi draws a logical conclusion about messengers, and then highlights the unique honors given to the Prophet and his community.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition
Read the Arabic and translation to grasp the literal meaning of 'Seal of the Prophets': that he is the last of them, completing the number 124,000.
Notice the specific number mentioned.
2
Trace the logical implication
Identify why Sanusi concludes that no messenger can come after the Prophet. Focus on the relationship between 'prophet' (general) and 'messenger' (specific).
He uses the logical principle: negation of the general implies negation of the specific.
3
Explore the honors
List the four honors mentioned: completion of all previous excellences, eternal law with no abrogation, warning from past nations, and the community as witnesses.
These honors highlight the Prophet's unique rank and the status of his community.
4
Connect to Aqeedah
Reflect on why this belief is essential—it safeguards the finality of revelation and the completeness of Islam.
This is a core belief in Islamic theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومعنى ( خاتم النبيِّينَ ) : أَنَّهُ آخِرُهُم ، وبهِ كَمُلَ عددُهُمُ الذي هو مئة ألف وأربعة وعشرون ألفاً ، فلا نبي بعده ، ومِنْ لازمِهِ : أَنْ لا رسول بعدَهُ ؛ لأنَّ النبيَّ أعمُّ مِنَ الرسول على الصحيح ، ونفي الأعم يستلزم نفْيَ الأخص ؛ فكَمَّلَ سبحانَهُ لنبينا ومولانا محمد عليه الصلاةُ والسلام جميع المحاسن التي تَفَرَّقَتْ في الأنبياء والرسل قبلَهُ ، وشَرَّفَ شريعته السمحة ؛ بأن جعلَ أحكامها متصلة بالآخرة ، لا ناسخ لها ولا مُبدِّلَ لها ، وأطلعَ أُمَّتَهُ المشرّفة على مساوي الأممِ الذينَ خلوا وعلى العقوبات التي نزلَتْ بهِم ؛ ليعتبروا بذلك ويرتدعوا عن المعاصي ولا يغتروا بالمهلة ومتعة الدنيا كما اغتر بذلك الذينَ هلكوا قبلَهُم ، فجعلَهم مولانا بفضلِهِ معتبرين لا معتبراً بهم ، ومتَّعظينَ وشاهدين على غيرِهِم لا مشهوداً عليهم ، وأظهر سبحانه محاسنَهُم لِمَنْ مضى مِنَ الأمم وسترَ مَساوِيَهُم ، بل نَوَّهَ المولى [؟] لا مُتَعَظاً بهم
And the meaning of 'Seal of the Prophets' is that he is the last of them, and with him their number was completed, which is one hundred and twenty-four thousand. Thus there is no prophet after him, and from this it necessarily follows that there is no messenger after him, because the prophet is more general than the messenger according to the correct view, and the negation of the more general necessitates the negation of the more specific. So Allah, glorified be He, completed for our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, all the excellences that were scattered among the prophets and messengers before him, and He honored his tolerant law by making its rulings continuous until the Hereafter, with no abrogating and no changing for it. And He informed his honored community about the faults of the nations that passed away and about the punishments that befell them, so that they might take heed thereby and desist from sins and not be deceived by the respite and enjoyment of the world as those who perished before them were deceived. So He made them, by His grace, admonishers, not being admonished, and witnesses against others, not being witnessed against. And He, glorified be He, displayed their excellences to the nations that passed and concealed their faults; rather, the Master praised [?] they are not admonished.
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This passage defines the meaning of 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' (Seal of the Prophets). Sanusi begins by stating that it means he is the last of them, completing the number of prophets (124,000). Therefore no prophet comes after him, and necessarily no messenger either, because 'prophet' is more general than 'messenger' (every messenger is a prophet but not vice versa), and negating the general negates the specific. Then Sanusi enumerates the honors bestowed on the Prophet: all excellences of previous prophets were combined in him; his law is eternal with no abrogation; his community is informed of past nations' faults to take warning; they are made witnesses over others, not against them; and Allah displays their virtues while hiding their faults, praising them.

Key Terms
خاتم النبيين
الرسول
النبي
الأعم والأخص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'Seal of the Prophets' only denies new prophets, but allows new messengers.
Correction: Sanusi shows that because 'prophet' is more general than 'messenger', negating prophets necessarily negates messengers, so no messenger can come after him.

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi conclude that no messenger can come after the Prophet Muhammad (PBUH)?
A) Because the Quran explicitly states there is no messenger after him.
B) Because the number of messengers is also completed at 124,000.
C) Because 'prophet' is more general than 'messenger', and denying the general implies denying the specific.
D) Because the Prophet said he is the last of all messengers.

Key Takeaway
The Seal of the Prophets means Muhammad (PBUH) is the final prophet and messenger, completing all prophethood and bestowing unique honors upon him and his community.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believe that Muhammad (PBUH) is the final prophet and messenger, and that his law is perfect and eternal, what specific areas of your life are you still seeking guidance from sources other than the Shari'ah? How will you realign them this week?
?
The passage says Allah made this community admonishers, not being admonished. Are you living as an admonisher – actively learning from past nations' mistakes and warning others – or are you comfortable being admonished by the world's standards? What change do you need to make?
?
You have been given the complete and final law with no abrogation. Are you treating it as a living guide or as a historical artifact? What one ruling do you currently neglect that you will commit to implementing starting today?

Wisdom Behind the Death of the Prophet's Male Children
Sanusi explains the wisdom behind the early death of the Prophet's sons.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the divine wisdom behind the early death of the Prophet's male sons and how it relates to his being the Seal of the Prophets.

Core Idea
The Prophet's sons died young to preserve their honor, not as a punishment.

This lesson explores a question that may arise: why did all of the Prophet Muhammad's male children die in infancy? Sanusi provides a theological explanation rooted in the Prophet's unique status as the final messenger. The passage you will read connects this historical fact to a verse from the Qur'an, showing that it was a mercy and a protection of their rank.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing it, then read the translation. Let the author's own logic come through before you interpret.
Notice the conditional reasoning: 'if they had lived... then...'
2
Identify the key terms
Find the three key terms: حطيطة (lowliness), سن النبوءة (age of prophethood), and شبه العلة (quasi-cause). Understand their roles in the argument.
These terms are central to the argument.
3
Trace the argument structure
The passage has a conditional argument: if the sons had lived, they would be lower than other prophets' children; therefore they died. Then it cites the Quranic verse as evidence.
This is a rational justification based on divine wisdom.
4
Reflect on the common misunderstanding
Many think the deaths were a punishment. The passage corrects this: it was a mercy to avoid a greater loss of rank.
This ties back to the core idea.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولأجل أنه عليه الصلاة والسلام خاتم النبيين: مات أولاده الذكور كلهم صغارا، قبل أن يكونوا رجالا؛ لأنهم لو عاشوا حتى يبلغوا سن النبوءة ثم لم ينبؤوا كانوا في ذلك أحط رتبة من أولاد كثير من الرسل الذين خلوا؛ كإبراهيم ويعقوب وداود عليهم الصلاة والسلام، فلما ماتوا صغارا انتفت هذه الحطيطة، وإلى هذا أشار القرآن في قوله تعالى: {مَا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبَا أَحَدٍ مِنْ رِجَالِكُمْ وَلَكِنْ رَسُولَ اللهِ وَخَاتَمَ النَّبِيِّينَ} [الأحزاب: ٤٠]، فجعل سبحانه كونه عليه الصلاة والسلام خاتم النبيين شبه العلة لما نفاه من أبوته عليه الصلاة والسلام.
And because he, peace and blessing be upon him, is the Seal of the Prophets: all his male children died young, before they became men; because if they had lived until they reached the age of prophethood and then were not made prophets, they would have been in that case lower in rank than the children of many of the messengers who passed—like Abraham, Jacob, and David, peace be upon them. So when they died young, this lowliness was removed. And to this the Qur'an alludes in His saying, exalted: 'Muhammad is not the father of any of your men, but he is the Messenger of God and the Seal of the Prophets' (Q. 33:40). Thus He, glorified, made his being, peace and blessing be upon him, the Seal of the Prophets as if the cause for what He negated of his paternity, peace and blessing be upon him.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage gives a rational explanation for a particular historical fact: none of the Prophet Muhammad's male children survived to adulthood. The author presents this as a consequence of his being the Seal of the Prophets. The argument is: if any of the Prophet's sons had lived to adulthood, they would have reached the age at which prophethood is normally bestowed (around forty). But since no prophet comes after Muhammad, they could not become prophets. This would place them in a lower rank than the children of previous prophets who did become prophets (e.g., Isaac, Jacob, Solomon). To avoid this apparent inferiority, God decreed that all his sons die in infancy. Thus, the verse 'Muhammad is not the father of any of your men' is interpreted as implying that no son of his reached adulthood to be counted among men, and this is linked to his being the Seal. The author calls this a 'shibh al-illa' (quasi-cause) for the negation of paternity of adult men.

Key Terms
حطيطة
سن النبوءة
شبه العلة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The death of the Prophet's sons was a punishment or a sign of Allah's displeasure.
Correction: The passage explains it was a mercy to preserve their rank, not a punishment.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why did all of the Prophet's male children die young?
A) To punish the Prophet
B) To prevent them from becoming prophets
C) To avoid them being lower in rank than other prophets' children
D) To fulfill a prophecy

Key Takeaway
The early death of the Prophet's sons was an act of divine mercy to protect their rank, confirming his status as the Seal of the Prophets.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a loss that seems painful or inexplicable, do you instinctively question Allah's wisdom or mercy? How does knowing that the Prophet's own sons died young — not as punishment but to preserve their honor — challenge your reaction to your own trials? Will you commit to pausing before complaining and instead seeking the hidden mercy?
?
You claim to believe in the Seal of the Prophets, but do you truly live as if no new revelation or way of life can surpass Islam? Or do you secretly admire and adopt values from other worldviews as if they could offer something better? This lesson shows that even the Prophet's sons could not have a higher rank than Islam's finality. Does your heart accept this with contentment, or is there a residue of doubt?
?
If you were in the Prophet's place, would you have been able to trust Allah's wisdom in losing your children? The Prophet endured this with patience and submission. Are you willing to surrender your most cherished plans and attachments to Allah, trusting that He is protecting you from a greater lowliness? Name one attachment you need to release today.

Wisdom in the Early Death of the Prophet's Sons
Sanusi explains the wisdom behind the early death of the Prophet's male sons.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the divine wisdom behind the early death of the Prophet Muhammad's male children and how it preserves his unique rank as the Seal of the Prophets.

Core Idea
The Prophet's sons died young to protect them from being lower in rank than the sons of earlier prophets.

The early death of the Prophet's male children is often a source of confusion. In this passage, Sanusi clarifies that this was not a punishment but a profound mercy. By understanding the logic of prophethood and finality, we see how Allah preserved the Prophet's honor and the honor of his children. Let's explore this teaching.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
First, read the Arabic passage and its English translation carefully. Notice the logical flow and the key terms.
Pay attention to the Quranic citation.
2
Identify key terms and argument
Identify the key terms: خاتم النبيين (Seal of the Prophets), حطيطة (diminishment), شبه العلة (like a cause). Follow the argument: because Muhammad is the final prophet, if his sons lived to adulthood they would not be prophets, making them lower in rank than the sons of earlier prophets who became prophets.
The argument relies on the principle of finality of prophethood.
3
Reflect on the Quranic evidence
Examine how Sanusi uses Quran 33:40 to support his point. The verse negates the Prophet being the father of any 'men', linking it to his being the Seal of the Prophets.
This is an example of tafsir (Quranic interpretation) within kalam.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ولأجلِ أنَّهُ عليه الصلاةُ والسلام خاتم النبيِّينَ : مات أولاده الذكورُ كلُّهم صغاراً ، قبل أن يكونوا رجالاً ؛ لأنَّهُم لو عاشوا حتى يبلغوا سِنَّ النبوءة ثمَّ لم يُنبَّؤُوا .. كانوا في ذلكَ أحطّ رتبةٌ مِنْ أولادِ كثيرٍ مِنَ الرسل الذين خلوا ؛ كإبراهيم ويعقوب وداود عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، فلما ماتوا صغاراً انتفَتْ هذهِ الحَطِيطة ، وإلى هذا أشار القرآنُ في قولِهِ تعالى : ﴿ مَا كَانَ مُحَمَّدٌ أَبا أَحَدٍ مِّن رِّجَالِكُمْ وَلَكِن رَّسُولَ اللهِ وَخَاتَمَ النَّبِيِّنَ ﴾ [الأحزاب: ٤٠] ، فجعل سبحانَهُ كونه عليه الصلاة والسلام خاتم النبيِّينَ شِبْهَ العِلَّةِ لِمَا نفاهُ مِنْ أُبوّتِهِ عليه الصلاة والسلام للكبارِ الذينَ يُطلَقُ عليهِمُ اسم الرجال ، والنكته فيه ما سبق تقريره ، والله تعالى أعلم .
And because he, peace and blessings be upon him, is the Seal of the Prophets: all his male children died young, before they became men; because if they had lived until they reached the age of prophethood and then were not made prophets, they would be lower in rank than the children of many of the messengers who passed before, like Abraham, Jacob, and David, peace be upon them. So when they died young, this diminishment was removed. And to this the Quran pointed in His statement, Exalted is He: 'Muhammad is not the father of any of your men, but he is the Messenger of Allah and the Seal of the Prophets' [Al-Ahzab: 40]. So He, Glorified, made his being, peace and blessings be upon him, the Seal of the Prophets like a cause for what He negated of his fatherhood, peace and blessings be upon him, of grown men who are called men. And the subtlety in it is what was previously established. And Allah, Exalted and Glorified, knows best.
Source pages p. 120

Commentary
This passage explains the divine wisdom behind the early death of the Prophet Muhammad's male children. The author argues that since the Prophet is the Seal of the Prophets (خاتم النبيين), i.e., the last and final prophet, if his sons had lived to adulthood, they would not become prophets (as prophethood ended with him). They would then be of a lower rank than the sons of earlier prophets (like Abraham, Jacob, and David) who did become prophets. To avoid this diminishment (حطيطة) in comparison, Allah caused them to die young. The author cites Quran 33:40, which negates the Prophet being the father of any 'men' (i.e., adult males) and instead emphasizes his role as the Seal of the Prophets. This verse is presented as evidence that the Prophet's fatherhood of adult men is incompatible with his being the final prophet. The 'subtlety' (النكته) refers to this wise arrangement already discussed. The passage concludes with 'والله تعالى أعلم' (Allah knows best), a common expression of humility and acknowledgment that deeper wisdom may be beyond human grasp.

Key Terms
خاتم النبيين
حطيطة
شبه العلة
النكته

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think the early death of the Prophet's sons was a punishment or a flaw in his prophethood.
Correction: The passage shows it was a mercy to preserve their honor and protect them from rank diminishment, affirming the Prophet's unique status.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why did all of the Prophet's male children die young?
A) Because the Prophet was sinful and his offspring were cursed.
B) To prevent them from being lower in rank than the children of earlier prophets who became prophets.
C) Because they were sickly and the Prophet prayed for their death.
D) To fulfill a prophecy about the end of the world.

Key Takeaway
The early death of the Prophet's male children was a divine mercy to protect them from rank diminishment, affirming his unique status as the Seal of the Prophets.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you experience a loss that seems to lower your standing in the eyes of others, do you believe that Allah is protecting your rank in a way you cannot see? What would it take for you to trust His plan more than your own understanding?
?
The Prophet's sons died young so they would not be lower in rank. Are there things you cling to—status, relationships, achievements—that might actually be a source of spiritual diminishment? Will you surrender them for the sake of Allah's greater wisdom?
?
If you saw a righteous person suffering, would you assume it is a punishment? How does this lesson—that the Prophet's own sons died young as a mercy—challenge you to rethink your judgment of Allah's decrees? Will you commit to seeing His mercy even in hardship?

Muhammad: Leader of the Prophets and Best of Creation
Sanusi argues that Muhammad is the leader of all prophets and the best of creation using two proofs.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the evidence Sanusi provides for Prophet Muhammad's supreme rank among all prophets and creation, and the humility of other prophets in recognizing his station.

Core Idea
Muhammad is the foremost prophet, and all prophets will be under his banner.

This lesson examines Sanusi's argument for the unparalleled status of Prophet Muhammad. Drawing on a key hadith and the event of the Night Journey, Sanusi establishes that Muhammad is the leader of all prophets and the best of all creation. At the same time, he highlights the profound humility of the other prophets who honor this rank.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the flow: from the claim of Muhammad's pride to the two proofs, then the conclusion and the reflection on humility.
The passage combines doctrinal proof with moral lesson.
2
Identify the two proofs
Find the two specific pieces of evidence Sanusi uses to prove Muhammad's leadership: the hadith about the banner and the event of the Night Journey. Write them down.
These are physical and eschatological proofs.
3
Understand the conclusion
After presenting the proofs, Sanusi states that Muhammad is the best of creation and most honorable to Allah. Reflect on why this conclusion follows from the proofs.
The conclusion is explicit: 'أفضل المخلوقات وأكرمها على الله'.
4
Examine the secondary lesson on humility
Notice how Sanusi uses this topic to highlight the humility of other prophets. Compare their attitude to that of the angels prostrating to Adam. What does this teach about perfect character?
This shows that recognizing superiority does not diminish others.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
سيدنا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام هو فخر الأنبياء والمرسلين. وقوله : ( وإمام المرسلينَ ) أي : مُقدَّمِهم في جميعِ الكمالاتِ ومتبوعهم ، يَتعلَّقونَ بهِ في شدائدِ الآخرة وأهوالها المعضلاتِ ، وقد قالَ عليه الصلاةُ والسلامُ : آدَمُ فَمَنْ دُونَهُ تَحْتَ لِوَائِي يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ. وقد ثبت أيضاً : أنَّهُ تَقَدَّمَهُم وأَمَّهُم حِسّاً في ليلة الإسراء ، وذلك كلُّه دليل واضح على أنَّ هذا السيّد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أفضل المخلوقاتِ ، وأكرمُها على الله تبارك وتعالى ، وفيه أيضاً دليل على كمال تواضعِ رُسُلِ اللهِ عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام للمولى تبارك وتعالى و امتلاء صدورِهِم بهيبته ومحبَّتِهِ ، والتعظيمِ لِمَا عظَمَهُ ، والتشريفِ لِمَا شرَّفَهُ ؛ إذ لم يجعلوا عليهم الصلاة والسلامُ ما حَصَّهُمُ اللهُ تعالى بِهِ مِنْ عظيم فضلِهِ مانعاً مِنَ التواضعِ لِمَنْ آثَرَهُ اللهُ تعالى بمزيَّةٍ وحَصَّهُ بفضْلٍ على جميع العوالم ، وأخلاقُهُمُ الكريمةُ في هذا نظيرُ أخلاقِ الملائكةِ عليهم الصلاةُ والسلامُ في تواضعِهِم وسجودِهِم لآدمَ عليه الصلاةُ والسلامُ.
Our master Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, is the pride of the prophets and messengers. And his statement: 'And the Imam of the messengers' i.e., their foremost in all perfections and the one followed, they cling to him in the severe difficulties and terrible terrors of the Hereafter. And he, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'Adam and those beneath him shall be under my banner on the Day of Resurrection.' And it is also established that he preceded them and led them physically in the Night of the Ascension. And all of that is clear evidence that this master, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, is the best of creation and the most honorable to Allah, Blessed and Exalted. And in it also is evidence of the complete humility of the messengers of Allah, peace be upon them, to the Lord, Blessed and Exalted, and the filling of their hearts with His awe and love, and magnifying what He magnified, and honoring what He honored; for they, peace be upon them, did not make what Allah had granted them of His great favor an impediment to humility towards the one whom Allah favored with a distinction and singled out with a virtue over all the worlds. And their noble characters in this are similar to the characters of the angels, peace be upon them, in their humility and their prostration to Adam, peace be upon him.
Source pages p. 121

Commentary
This passage establishes the supreme rank of Prophet Muhammad among all prophets and creation. The author begins by calling him 'the pride of the prophets and messengers' and explains the title 'Imam of the messengers' (إمام المرسلين) as meaning that he is foremost (مقدم) in all perfections (الكمالات) and the one followed (متبوع). On the Day of Judgment, all prophets will gather under his banner, as stated in the hadith: 'Adam and those beneath him shall be under my banner.' Additionally, during the Night Journey (الإسراء), he physically led the prophets in prayer, indicating his leadership. These two proofs (the banner and the leading in prayer) are presented as clear evidence that Muhammad is the best (أفضل) of all created beings and the most honorable (أكرم) to Allah. The passage then draws a secondary lesson: the humility of the other prophets. Despite their high rank, they did not consider their own excellence as a barrier to honoring the Prophet Muhammad. This character mirrors that of the angels, who prostrated to Adam despite their own lofty station. Thus, the passage simultaneously proves the Prophet's supreme status and praises the humility of the other prophets and angels.

Key Terms
إمام المرسلين
الكمالات
تحت لوائي
ليلة الإسراء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The superiority of Prophet Muhammad diminishes the rank of other prophets.
Correction: The passage emphasizes that other prophets showed complete humility and recognition of his rank, mirroring the angels' prostration to Adam, which shows that honoring superiority does not diminish one's own dignity.

Checkpoint
What are the two pieces of evidence Sanusi gives for Prophet Muhammad being the leader of all prophets?
A) He was born without a father and performed the splitting of the moon.
B) He will have the banner of praise on Judgment Day and he led the prophets in prayer during the Night Journey.
C) He received the Quran and was the last prophet.
D) He had many wives and conquered Mecca.

Key Takeaway
The essential point is that Prophet Muhammad is the best of all creation, as proven by his role as leader of the prophets in both this life and the Hereafter.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you truly honor the Prophet as the best of creation, or have you allowed other loyalties—to yourself, your desires, or worldly figures—to compete with that? How can you realign your heart to give him the rank Allah has given him?
?
When someone else is honored above you, do you feel humility or resentment? This passage shows the prophets themselves humbled before Muhammad. Examine your own heart: is there envy when a brother or sister is praised? What action will you take today to cultivate genuine joy for others' excellence?
?
If on the Day of Judgment all prophets will cling to Muhammad, what about you? Are you building a connection with him now through following his Sunnah and loving him, or are you distant? What one change will you make this week to draw closer to him?
