The Consensus and the Great Intercession: Proofs of the Prophet's Supreme Rank
Sanusi combines consensus and the intercession narrative to argue for the Prophet's absolute superiority.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the consensus of the Muslim community and the narrative of the Great Intercession definitively establish the Prophet Muhammad's superiority over all creation.

Core Idea
Consensus and the Great Intercession together prove the Prophet's unmatched honor.

In this passage, Sanusi presents two intertwined arguments for the Prophet's supreme rank: the unanimous consensus of the community (ijma') and the dramatic event of the Great Intercession on the Day of Judgment. He calls upon the reader to reflect on God's special address to the Prophet in that terrifying scene as the clearest proof of his status. This lesson unpacks both the logical force of consensus and the theological weight of the intercession narrative.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without stopping, then read the translation. Note how the author moves from general claims to a specific, vivid narrative.
The opening lines may seem unrelated but set the stage for the Prophet's universal significance.
2
Identify the consensus claim
Find the sentence where Sanusi states that consensus (ijma') has been established on the Prophet's superiority. Note that he dismisses any dissent as innovation.
Consensus is a major source of authority in Islamic theology.
3
Examine the intercession narrative
Read the description of the Day of Judgment carefully. Notice who is afraid, who is not, and what the Prophet says and does.
The Prophet's confidence is highlighted: he says 'I am for it' without hesitation.
4
Reflect on God's address
Pay attention to the words God speaks to the Prophet: 'Raise your head... ask, you will be given; intercede, you will be granted intercession.' Ponder what this reveals about the Prophet's rank.
This direct and honorific address is unprecedented on that day.
5
Understand the conclusion
The author ends by asserting that no one is more honored by God than Muhammad. Connect this back to consensus and the intercession story as proof.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
جميع الأمم، وظهر عليها كلّها، وخلط بين أجناسها، وجعلها على اختلافِ أديانها واختلافِ لغاتِها جنساً واحداً، وعلى لغة واحدة؛ إذ كلُّهم يقرؤون القرآن بلغة العرب، وبها يُصلُّونَ... إلى غير ذلكَ، وكلُّهم يدينون بدين واحدٍ؛ وهو دين الإسلام. وبالجملة: فنصوص الكتب السابقة على ثبوتِ نبوَّةِ سيّدِنا ومولانا محمد صلى الله عليهِ وسلَّمَ وتعظيم شأنِهِ، وإيصاءات الأنبياء الماضينَ عليهِ، وإشادتهم ذكرَهُ، وتبشيراتُ الأحبار به .. لا تكاد تنحصر وثبوت رسالتِهِ وشرفه على كلّ ما خلق مولانا تبارك وتعالى.. أجلى مِنَ الشمس. [انعقاد الإجماع على أفضليته عليه الصلاة والسلام] وقد ثبت الإجماع على أفضليَّتِهِ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ على الخلقِ، وشواهد ذلكَ مِنَ الكتاب والسنَّةِ لا تكاد تنحصر، ولا يُلتفتُ إلى مَنِ ابتدع وحاولَ غير ذلكَ. ويكفيكَ في معرفة شرفِهِ وعُلُوّ منزلتِهِ عندَ اللهِ تعالى على المخلوقاتِ عموماً بلا استثناء .. ما أُجْمَعَ عليهِ مِنَ التقدُّم للشفاعةِ الكبرى في موطن الآخرةِ، وتنويهِ اللهِ تعالى هناكَ بِقَدْرِهِ والرَّفْعِ لمنزلتِهِ والإكرام له؛ حيث اجتمع الأوَّلون والآخرون وجميع الأنبياء والمرسلين والملائكة كلهم والمقرَّبينَ، وعم الخطب واشتدَّ الهول وكلٌّ مشغول بنفسِهِ، خائفٌ هائبٌ لجلالِ المولى العظيم، جاثِ على ركبتيه؛ لما يرى في ذلك اليومِ مِنَ الخطر والهول الجسيم، ولا يتجاسر أحدٌ في ذلك اليوم الهائل على مخاطبة المولى تبارك وتعالى في رفعِ شيء ممَّا نزلَ .. سوى عبدِهِ وخاتم رُسُلِهِ، وعروس مملكته وسرها وإكسيرها، وسيّدِ كلَّ ما خَلَقَ مولانا جل وعلا، صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ، فيقولُ عند ما ينتهي الناسُ إليهِ في طلب الشفاعة إلى المولى تبارك وتعالى: «أَنَا لَهَا»، لا يخافُ ولا يُهِمُّهُ أمر نفسِهِ، ولا يتتعتع، ويذهب حتى يسجد تحت العرش، فيقول المولى جلَّ وعلا: (أَرْفَعْ رَأْسَكَ يَا مُحَمَّدُ، وَقُلْ يُسْمَعْ لَكَ، وَسَلْ تُعْطَهْ وَاشْفَعْ تُشَفْعْ). فانظُرْ رحمَكَ اللهُ هذا الخطاب العزيز اللطيف الشريف لهُ عليهِ الصلاة والسلامُ مِنْ مولانا تبارك وتعالى في ذلك اليوم الهائل، الذي غضبَ فيه سبحانَهُ غضباً لم يغضب قبلَهُ مثلَهُ، ولا يغضب بعده مثلَهُ كيف وهو صريح بالمعنى بلا نزاع ولا ريب ولا احتمالٍ أَنَّهُ لا أكرمَ من نبينا وسيدنا ومولانا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ على اللهِ تبارك وتعالى؟! وفي الحديثِ: (أنَّه صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أَوَّلُ مَنْ يَفْرَعُ بَابَ الْجَنَّةِ، فَيَقُولُ رِضْوَانُ خَازِنُهَا عَلَيْهِ السَّلامُ: مَنْ ؟ فَيَقُولُ: مُحَمَّدٌ
All nations, and it appeared upon all of them, and mixed among their races, and made them, despite the difference of their religions and the difference of their languages, one race, and upon one language; since all of them recite the Qur'an in the language of the Arabs, and with it they pray... and so on, and all of them follow one religion; and it is the religion of Islam. In summary: the texts of the previous books regarding the establishment of the prophethood of our master and our lord Muhammad (peace and blessings be upon him) and the magnification of his status, and the bequests of the past prophets concerning him, and their praise of his mention, and the announcements of the rabbis about him... are almost limitless, and the establishment of his message and his honor over all that our lord, Blessed and Most High, created... is clearer than the sun. [The establishment of consensus on his superiority – peace and blessings be upon him] And consensus has been established on his superiority (peace and blessings be upon him) over creation, and the evidences of that from the Book and the Sunnah are almost limitless, and no attention is paid to whoever innovated and attempted otherwise. And it suffices you in knowing his honor and the highness of his station with God Most High over all creatures in general without exception... what has been unanimously agreed upon regarding his precedence for the great intercession in the abode of the Hereafter, and God Most High's declaration there of his worth, the raising of his station, and honoring him; where the first and the last gathered, and all the prophets, messengers, all the angels, and those brought near, and the calamity became widespread and the terror intensified, and everyone was preoccupied with himself, fearful, awestruck at the majesty of the great Lord, kneeling on his knees; because of what he sees on that day of tremendous danger and terror, and no one dares on that terrible day to address the Lord, Blessed and Most High, to remove anything that has descended... except His servant and the Seal of His Messengers, the bride of His kingdom, its secret and its elixir, the master of all that our Lord, Majestic and Exalted, created – peace and blessings be upon him – and he says when the people come to him seeking intercession to the Lord, Blessed and Most High: 'I am for it' – he does not fear, nor does the matter of his self concern him, nor does he stammer, and he goes until he prostrates beneath the Throne, and then the Lord, Majestic and Exalted, says: 'Raise your head, O Muhammad, and speak, you will be heard; ask, you will be given; intercede, you will be granted intercession.' So consider, may God have mercy on you, this dear, gentle, noble address to him (peace and blessings be upon him) from our Lord, Blessed and Most High, on that terrible day, on which He, glorified be He, became angry with an anger the like of which He has never been angry before, nor will He be angry with its like afterwards – how is it not clear in meaning, without dispute, doubt, or possibility, that there is none more honored than our Prophet, our master, our lord Muhammad (peace and blessings be upon him) with God, Blessed and Most High?! And in the hadith: 'He (peace and blessings be upon him) is the first to knock at the gate of Paradise, and Ridwan, its keeper (peace be upon him), says: Who? He says: Muhammad.'
Source pages p. 243

Commentary
This passage is a combined argument and narrative affirming the Prophet Muhammad's absolute superiority over all creation. The author begins by noting the unification of nations under Islam, then states that the previous scriptures' testimonies to the Prophet's prophethood and honor are countless and clearer than the sun. He then declares that consensus (ijma') has been established on the Prophet's superiority, with evidence from the Qur'an and Sunnah being abundant. To illustrate this, he presents the universally acknowledged event of the Great Intercession (al-shafa'a al-kubra) on the Day of Judgment. In that dreadful assembly of all creatures, only the Prophet dares to approach God for intercession, and he does so with confidence. God addresses him with honor and grants him intercession. The author calls the reader to reflect on this divine address as proof that no one is more honored by God than Muhammad. He adds a hadith that the Prophet will be the first to knock on the gate of Paradise. The overall argument is that consensus and the specific narrative of intercession leave no room for doubt about the Prophet's supreme rank.

Key Terms
الإجماع
الشفاعة الكبرى
العرش
الخطب
الملائكة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the Prophet's superiority is merely a matter of personal devotion, but the author grounds it in consensus and explicit divine action.
Correction: The intercession narrative shows God directly honoring the Prophet, and consensus confirms this universally.

Checkpoint
What is the main evidence the author uses to demonstrate the Prophet's superiority over all creation?
A) The number of his miracles
B) The consensus on his precedence for the great intercession
C) His wealth and lineage
D) The size of his community

Key Takeaway
The consensus of the community and the Great Intercession together provide undeniable evidence that the Prophet Muhammad is the most honored of all creation before God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you face a moment of fear or helplessness, do you turn to the Prophet Muhammad with the same confidence that he had on the Day of Judgment, saying 'I am for it'? Or do you rely solely on your own strength? What would change in your life if you truly believed that he is the most honored intercessor for you before God?
?
The author says that the Prophet's superiority is 'clearer than the sun' and established by consensus. Is this truth as clear to you in your daily actions and priorities? Do you live as though the Prophet is the most honorable being, or do your actions suggest otherwise? Identify one specific way you can honor him more starting today.
?
On that terrible Day, every prophet and angel is consumed by fear, but the Prophet Muhammad remains fearless. How does this scene challenge your own fears and anxieties about the Hereafter? Are you preparing for that Day by following his sunnah and seeking his intercession? What is one concrete step you will take to strengthen your connection to him?

The Prophet's Authority Over the Fire
Sanusi describes the Fire's approach, the necks, and the Prophet's intercession.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the Prophet's intercession on the Day of Judgment demonstrates his unique authority over the Fire, as depicted in the Salaf's narrative.

Core Idea
The Prophet commands the Fire, and it obeys.

This lesson presents a vivid description of the Fire's terror on the Day of Judgment and the Prophet's decisive role in restraining it. The narrative, while not a matter of creed per se, illustrates the honor granted to the Prophet by Allah, showing that even the Fire submits to his command.

Guided Path
1
Read the narrative of the Fire
Read the Arabic passage describing the Fire being driven, its approach, and the emergence of necks. Notice the dramatic details.
The lengths mentioned (500 years) are symbolic of immense scale.
2
Identify the Prophet's action
Find where the Prophet rises, rebukes the Fire, and commands it to retreat. Observe the divine response: the Fire hears a command to listen to him.
3
Reflect on the significance
Consider what this narrative teaches about the Prophet's rank. How does it relate to his intercession?
This authority is a sign of his honor and closeness to Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فَيَقُولُ رِضْوَانُ عَلَيْهِ السَّلامُ : بِكَ أُمِرْتُ أَلا أَفْتَحَ لِأَحَدٍ قَبْلَكَ » أو كما قال . ورُوِيَ ما معناه : أنَّ النارَ عندَ ما تسوقها الملائكة الموكَّلون بها بالسلاسل لتحيط بالخلْقِ في المحشرِ ، فاذا قَرُبَتْ منهم بنحو خمس مئة سنةٍ تَشْهَقُ شهيقاً عظيماً منكراً، وتتفلَّتُ منها الأعناق إلى المحشرِ ، طولُ العُنُقِ خمس مئة سنة ، لهُ فم وأسنانٌ مِنْ نارٍ ، فيصل العنق إلى المحشرِ ويَزْفِرُ عليهم ويَشْهَقُ عليهم شهيقاً منكراً لا يستطاع سماعُه ، ويملأُ عليهم الجوَّ ظلمةً وناراً زيادةً على ما هم فيهِ مِنَ الأهوالِ الجسيمةِ ، ويلتقط العنقُ الناسَ مِنَ الموقفِ ويبتلعُهُم في ذلكَ العنق الطويل إلى جوفه ! وحينئذ تجثو على الرُّكَب الملائكة المقرَّبون والأنبياء والرُّسُلُ على جميعهم م الصلاة والسلام ، فحينئذ ينهضُ إلى النارِ نبينا وسيدنا ومولانا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ، فيزجرُها عنِ الناسِ ، ويأمرُها بالتأخُرِ عنهم ، فتسمعُ النارُ حينئذ نداءً مِنْ قِبَلِ اللهِ تبارك وتعالى : اسمعي له وأطيعي . وقد رُوِيَ عنهُ أَنَّهُ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ قالَ : « أَنَا سَيْدُ وَلَدِ آدَمَ ، وأَنَا سَيْدُ النَّاسِ ، وآدَمُ فَمَنْ دُونَهُ تَحْتَ لِوَانِي يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ ، و« لَوْ كَانَ مُوسَى وَعِيسَى حَيَّيْنِ مَا وَسِعَهُمَا إِلا اتباعي وبالجملة : فثبوت شرفِهِ وأفضليَّتِهِ على جميع المخلوقاتِ يكادُ أنْ يكون معلوماً مِنَ الدين ضرورةً ؛ بحيثُ لا يُحتاجُ إلى سردِ دليل . وَلَيْسَ يَصِحُ فِي الْأَذْهَانِ شَيْءٌ مَتَى أَحْتَاجَ النَّهَارُ إِلَى دَلِيلِ
Ridwan, peace be upon him, says: 'I was commanded not to open for anyone before you' – or as he said. And it is related in meaning: that when the Fire is driven by the angels entrusted with it with chains to surround the creation in the gathering-place, and when it approaches them to about five hundred years, it gives a great, terrible gasp, and necks burst forth from it to the gathering-place, the length of the neck being five hundred years, having a mouth and teeth of fire. Then the neck reaches the gathering-place and blows upon them and gasps upon them a terrible gasp that cannot be heard, and fills the air upon them with darkness and fire in addition to the tremendous terrors they are in. And the neck snatches people from the standing-place and swallows them into its interior through that long neck. Then the near-stationed angels, the prophets, and the messengers – upon all of them be prayer and peace – fall on their knees. Then our Prophet, our Master, our Patron Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, rises up to the Fire, rebukes it for the people, and commands it to retreat from them. Then the Fire hears a call from Allah, Blessed and Exalted: 'Listen to him and obey.' And it has been related from him that he, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, said: 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and I am the master of mankind, and Adam and whoever is beneath him are under my banner on the Day of Resurrection.' And he said: 'If Moses and Jesus were alive, they would have no choice but to follow me.' In summary: the proof of his honor and his superiority over all creatures is almost known from religion by necessity, such that there is no need to adduce evidence. And nothing is correct in minds that needs the day for proof.
Source pages p. 245

Commentary
This passage is a combined narrative and doctrinal statement. It begins by reporting a saying of Ridwan, the guardian of Paradise, who states that he was commanded not to open Paradise for anyone before the Prophet – this indicates the Prophet's precedence even over the entry to Paradise. Then it relates a description of the Fire on the Day of Judgment. The angels drive the Fire with chains; when it comes within five hundred years' distance, it gives a terrible gasp. Necks emerge from the Fire, each five hundred years long, with mouths and teeth of fire. These necks reach the gathering place, blow and gasp terrifyingly, fill the air with darkness and fire, and snatch people, swallowing them. At that point, the angels, prophets, and messengers fall to their knees. Then the Prophet Muhammad (saw) rises, rebukes the Fire, and commands it to retreat. The Fire hears a divine command to listen to him and obey. This demonstrates the Prophet's authority over even the Fire. The passage then cites two hadith: one where he declares himself master of all descendants of Adam and master of mankind, with Adam and others under his banner; another where he says that if Moses and Jesus were alive, they would have to follow him – indicating his supremacy over all prophets. The author concludes that the Prophet's honor and superiority over all creatures is so evident that it is known from religion by necessity, requiring no proof. He quotes a line of poetry: 'Nothing is correct in minds that needs the day for proof,' meaning that self-evident truths need no demonstration.

Key Terms
النار
العنق
المحشر
يجثو على الركب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What happens when the Prophet commands the Fire to retreat?
A) The Fire ignores him
B) The Fire hears a divine command to obey him
C) The Fire becomes even more violent
D) The angels force the Fire back

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's authority over the Fire on the Day of Judgment is a unique honor that underscores his central role in intercession.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were standing in the gathering place and saw the Fire's necks snatching people around you, would you be among those who fall on their knees in terror, or would you have prepared an answer for your Lord? What specific action will you take today to ensure you are not among those snatched?
?
The Prophet will rebuke the Fire on your behalf. But if you ignore his message now, how can you expect his intercession then? What will you change starting this week to show gratitude for his role as intercessor?
?
Every soul snatched by the Fire in this narrative knew the truth but did not act. Are you acting on what you know? Name one habit or sin you will abandon today because of the reality of this scene.

The Prophet's Supreme Status Over All Creation
Sanusi cites hadith and concludes the Prophet's superiority is known by necessity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Prophet's superiority over all creation is known by necessity from religion, not requiring logical proof.

Core Idea
The Prophet's superiority is self-evident in Islam.

After narrating the Prophet's role over the Fire, Sanusi cites two powerful hadith that establish his supreme status: he is the master of all children of Adam, and even Moses and Jesus would have to follow him. He then concludes that this superiority is so clear that it is known from the religion by necessity, needing no evidence.

Guided Path
1
Read the hadith on being master
Read the hadith where the Prophet declares himself master of Adam's children and says Adam and others are under his banner.
This is a well-known hadith about his leadership on the Day of Resurrection.
2
Understand the statement about Moses and Jesus
Consider the implication: even the greatest prophets would have no choice but to follow him if alive.
This underscores his superiority over all prophets.
3
Grasp the conclusion about necessary knowledge
Read Sanusi's assertion that his superiority is 'almost known from religion by necessity'. Reflect on what 'necessity of religion' means.
Necessary knowledge is so obvious that no proof is required.

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think the Prophet's superiority is a matter of intellectual proof or comparative analysis.

A) It requires extensive evidence
B) It is a self-evident truth for Muslims
C) It is known only by scholars
D) It is a matter of personal opinion

The Prophet's supremacy over all creation is a foundational Islamic belief known by necessity, requiring no proof.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You have read that the Prophet's superiority is a self-evident truth. But do you live in a way that reflects this certainty? Where in your daily life do you fall short of honoring him as the master of all creation? Write one specific action you will take today to show your love for him.
?
The Prophet said that even Moses and Jesus must follow him. How does this challenge your respect for previous prophets? Humble yourself now: what is one way you can increase your following of the Prophet's sunnah starting today?
?
If this belief is known by necessity, why do you sometimes give more weight to worldly opinions than to the Prophet's teachings? What is the first step you can take to make his guidance the absolute measure of your life?

Proof of the Prophet's Superiority Over All Creation
Sanusi cites hadiths and a concluding statement proving the Prophet's superiority by necessity.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the Prophet Muhammad's superiority over all creation is established through explicit prophetic hadiths and is considered a necessary knowledge from the religion.

Core Idea
The Prophet's superiority is a self-evident truth of Islam.

Sanusi presents compelling evidence for the Prophet's unmatched status. He cites multiple authentic hadiths where the Prophet himself declares his mastery, and then argues that this truth is so evident within Islam that it requires no elaborate proof. This lesson explores those hadiths and the powerful conclusion that follows.

Guided Path
1
Read the hadiths carefully
List the four hadiths that the author quotes. Notice how each one asserts the Prophet's supremacy over different categories: children of Adam, mankind, Adam and others under his banner, and even Moses and Jesus would have to follow him.
These hadiths are the core textual evidence.
2
Understand the concluding statement
Pay attention to the author's conclusion: the Prophet's superiority is 'almost known from religion by necessity.' Reflect on what that means — that it is so clear it doesn't need detailed proof.
This is a key concept in kalam: knowledge by necessity.
3
Analyze the poetic line
Read the final line from al-Mutanabbi. It says that nothing is accepted in minds when daylight itself needs a proof. How does this analogy reinforce the author's argument?
The poet is comparing the Prophet's status to daylight — self-evident.
4
Test your understanding
Answer the checkpoint question to ensure you grasp the main point.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقد رُوِيَ عنهُ أَنَّهُ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ قالَ : « أَنَا سَيْدُ وَلَدِ آدَمَ (۱) ، وا أَنَا سَيْدُ النَّاسِ (۲) ، وا آدَمُ فَمَنْ دُونَهُ تَحْتَ لِوَانِي يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ (٣) ، و« لَوْ كَانَ مُوسَى وَعِيسَى حَيَّيْنِ مَا وَسِعَهُمَا إِلا اتباعي (٤) || وبالجملة : فثبوت شرفِهِ وأفضليَّتِهِ على جميع المخلوقاتِ يكادُ أنْ يكون معلوماً مِنَ الدين ضرورةً ؛ بحيثُ لا يُحتاجُ إلى سردِ دليل . وَلَيْسَ يَصِحُ فِي الْأَذْهَانِ شَيْءٌ مَتَى أَحْتَاجَ النَّهَارُ إِلَى دَلِيلِ(٥)
And it has been narrated from him that he ﷺ said: 'I am the master of the children of Adam (1), and I am the master of mankind (2), and Adam and whoever is beneath him will be under my banner on the Day of Resurrection (3), and 'If Moses and Jesus were alive, they would have no recourse but to follow me (4).' And in summary: the establishment of his honor and superiority over all creatures is almost known from religion by necessity; such that there is no need to list a proof. And nothing is correct in minds when the day needs a proof (5).
Source pages p. 246

Commentary
This passage is a proof of the Prophet Muhammad's superiority over all creation. It begins by citing four hadiths that directly assert his preeminence: he is the master of Adam's children, the master of mankind, and on the Day of Resurrection Adam and all others will be under his banner; moreover, if Moses and Jesus were alive, they would have no choice but to follow him. After these narrations, the author concludes that the Prophet's honor and superiority (sharaf and afdaliyya) are nearly known from the religion by necessity (ḍarūra), so that one does not need to produce a detailed proof. The passage ends with a poetic line from al-Mutanabbi, reinforcing the idea that just as daylight is self-evident and needs no proof, the Prophet's superiority is similarly evident. The author thus presents a concise argument: the Prophet's status is so manifest that it is a necessary knowledge derived directly from the religion.

Key Terms
أفضليَّة (afdaliyya)
سيد ولد آدم
ضرورة دينية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That the Prophet's superiority requires extensive rational proof
Correction: The author argues that it is known by necessity from religion, so it is self-evident and does not require elaborate proof.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, how is the Prophet's superiority known?
A) Through detailed rational proofs
B) Through consensus of scholars
C) From religion by necessity
D) Through imitation of ancestors

Key Takeaway
The Prophet Muhammad's superiority over all creation is so evident in Islam that it is considered necessary knowledge, supported by clear hadiths and confirmed by reason's own logic.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Prophet's superiority is as clear as daylight, why do I still hesitate to fully submit to his teachings in my daily life? Identify one specific area where you have not followed his example this week, and commit to a concrete change starting today.
?
The hadith says even Moses and Jesus would follow him if alive. Do I truly honor the Prophet above all others in my heart? Ask yourself: Is there anyone or anything I prioritize over his sunnah? Write a prayer asking Allah to increase your love and reverence for him.
?
The author says this truth is 'known from the religion by necessity.' If I claim to know it, why do I sometimes act as if my own opinions or desires are more important? What would change in your life today if you fully lived this belief?

Prophethood

Opinions on the Best Prophet After Muhammad
Sanusi quotes al-Taftazani listing five opinions on the best prophet after Muhammad.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the differing scholarly opinions on who is the best prophet after Muhammad and the reasons given for each.

Core Idea
Even after consensus on Muhammad's supremacy, scholars disagreed on the next best prophet.

This lesson presents a fascinating glimpse into classical Islamic theological debate. While there is unanimous agreement that Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is the best of all prophets and messengers, scholars held various views on who holds that rank after him. Al-Taftazani records five distinct opinions, each supported by a specific virtue or characteristic of the prophet in question. As you read the passage, notice both the diversity of opinion and the types of arguments used.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic and English carefully. Observe the structure: consensus statement, then five opinions introduced by 'قيل' (it was said).
Pay attention to the phrasing used for each opinion.
2
Identify each opinion and its reasoning
List the five prophets and the reason given for each. Consider why these specific virtues were highlighted.
3
Reflect on the significance
Think about why this disagreement matters for Islamic theology. Does it affect other beliefs?
The passage does not resolve the debate, only reports it.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
تنبیهان الأول في بيان الفاضل بعده عليه الصلاة والسلام ] قال التفتازاني في ( شرح المقاصدِ الدينية » لهُ بعدما ذكرَ الإجماع على أنَّهُ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أفضل الأنبياء والرُّسُل عليهم الصلاة والسلام : ( اختلفوا في الأفضل بعده : فقيل : آدم عليه السلام ؛ لكونه أبا البشر وقيل : نوح عليه السلام ؛ لطول عبادتِهِ ومجاهدته . وقيل : إبراهيم عليه السلام ؛ لزيادة توكَّلِهِ واصطفائِهِ وقيل : موسى عليهِ السلامُ ؛ لكونه كليم الله تعالى ونجيَّهُ • وقيل : عيسى عليهِ السلامُ ؛ لكونِهِ رُوحَ اللَّهِ تعالى وصفيَّهُ )(۱)
Two Notices: The First: On explaining the one who is best after him ﷺ. Al-Taftazani said in his 'Sharh al-Maqasid al-Diniyya' after mentioning the consensus that he ﷺ is the best of the prophets and messengers, peace be upon them: 'They differed on the best after him. So it was said: Adam, peace be upon him, because he is the father of mankind. And it was said: Noah, peace be upon him, because of the length of his worship and striving. And it was said: Abraham, peace be upon him, because of his increased trust and chosenness. And it was said: Moses, peace be upon him, because he is the speaker of Allah and His intimate. And it was said: Jesus, peace be upon him, because he is the spirit of Allah and His chosen one.'
Source pages p. 246

Commentary
This passage is the first of two 'notices' (tanbīh). It reports a citation from al-Taftazani's Sharh al-Maqasid al-Diniyya. After noting that there is consensus on the Prophet Muhammad being the best of all prophets and messengers, Taftazani records that scholars differed on who is the best (al-afḍal) after him. The passage enumerates five distinct opinions, each with a brief reason: (1) Adam, because he is the father of mankind; (2) Noah, because of his long worship and striving; (3) Abraham, because of his increased trust and being chosen; (4) Moses, because he is the speaker of Allah (kalīm Allāh) and His intimate; (5) Jesus, because he is the spirit of Allah (rūḥ Allāh) and His chosen one. The author does not endorse any of these views; he simply presents the disagreement.

Key Terms
الأفضل بعده (al-afḍal ba'dahu)
آدم
نوح
إبراهيم
موسى
عيسى

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: There is consensus on who is the best prophet after Muhammad.
Correction: In fact, scholars differed; five opinions are recorded in this passage.

Checkpoint
How many opinions are mentioned regarding who is the best prophet after Muhammad?
A) 3
B) 4
C) 5
D) 6

Key Takeaway
Scholars differed on who is the best prophet after Muhammad, with five main opinions each supported by a specific virtue.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The scholars differed on who is the best after Muhammad, each pointing to a different virtue. Which virtue do you value most in your relationship with Allah? Are you actually living by that virtue, or do you just admire it from afar? Make one concrete change today to embody that virtue.
?
When you hear someone confidently rank the prophets, do you feel the need to correct them? Or do you feel insecure because you don't know the evidence? This lesson shows that even great scholars differed. How does that free you from the burden of having to have all the answers? What step can you take to be more humble about matters where scholars disagree?

Prophethood

Superiority Among Prophets is by God's Decree Without Cause
Sanusi defines the nature of superiority among prophets and angels.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi insists that superiority among prophets is solely by God's decree and not due to any inherent cause in the prophets themselves.

Core Idea
Superiority among prophets is by God's sovereign will alone, not by merit.

Sanusi tackles a sensitive theological question: why are some prophets superior to others? He warns against attributing superiority to personal virtues, which would imply that God's choice is constrained. Instead, he grounds superiority purely in God's decree, using an analogy of a master and his slaves to illustrate divine sovereignty. As you read, notice how he carefully distinguishes God's transcendence from human limitations.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation carefully. Pay attention to the key terms: الأفضلية, العِلَّة المُوجِبَة.
The Arabic is dense; take your time.
2
Identify the main claim
Identify Sanusi's main claim: superiority is by God's decree, not by a necessitating cause.
3
Analyze the analogy
Examine the analogy of a master preferring one slave over another. What does this illustrate? How does Sanusi qualify it?
The analogy is approximate because human masters have motives.
4
Understand the rejection
Understand why Sanusi rejects the view that superiority is due to different attributes. Note his warning about poor manners.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الأفضلية التي ثبتَتْ بينَ الأنبياء والرُّسُلِ ، وفي معناهم الملائكة ، على جميعهم الصلاة والسلام ، قال : ( إِنَّما وقعَتِ الأفضلية بينهم بحكم الله تعالى بأفضليّةِ بعضهم على بعض ، لا مِنْ أجلِ عِلَّةٍ مُوجِبةٍ لذلكَ وُجِدَتْ في الفاضلِ وفُقِدَتْ مِنَ المفضولِ ، وللسيّدِ أَنْ يُفضّلَ بعض عبيده على بعض وإنْ كانَ كلُّ واحدٍ منهم كاملاً في نفسِهِ ، بالغاً مِنْ ذلكَ الغاية التي تليقُ بهِ ، مِنْ غيرِ أنْ يحمله على ذلك وصف يكون فيهم وذلكَ ممَّا يجب له بحقِّ سيادتِهِ ، والتمثيل بالسيد أمر تقريبي ؛ إذ لا يخلو مِنَ البواعثِ والأغراض ، والله تعالى مُنزَّه عن جميع ذلك . ثمَّ إِنَّ الله تعالى أعلم بما يقتضيه هذا الحكم بالأفضلية ، فهذا هو الذي يظهر لي في سبب وجود الأفضلية بين الأنبياء عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، ولا يتصوَّرُ عندي إنكار لذلك . وأمَّا أَنْ يُعتقَدَ في سبب وجود الأفضلية اتصافُ الفاضل بصفاتٍ هي مفقودة في المفضولِ ، أو أنَّ صفاتِ الفاضل ناقصةٌ وصفاتِ الأفضل كاملة .. فهو عندي تكلف وتعشف ، ولا يَسلَمُ مِنَ الوقوع في سوء الأدب .
The superiority that is established among the prophets and messengers, and in their meaning the angels, upon all of them blessings and peace, he said: 'Indeed, the superiority among them occurred by the decree of God Most High, by His preferring some of them over others, not because of a necessitating cause found in the superior and lacking in the inferior. And it is permissible for a master to prefer some of his slaves over others even if each of them is perfect in himself, having reached the utmost degree befitting him, without being impelled to that by a description that exists in them. And that is something obligatory for him by right of his mastery. And the analogy with the master is an approximation, for it is not free of motives and purposes, and God Most High is transcendent above all that.' Then indeed God Most High knows best what this judgment of superiority necessitates. This is what appears to me regarding the reason for the existence of superiority among the prophets, upon them blessings and peace, and I cannot conceive of denying that. As for the belief that the reason for the existence of superiority is the superior's being qualified by attributes that are lacking in the inferior, or that the attributes of the superior are deficient and the attributes of the more excellent are perfect... that is, in my view, affectation and strain, and it does not escape falling into poor manners.
Source pages p. 248

Commentary
This passage defines and explains the nature of superiority among prophets and angels. It begins by stating that superiority is by God's decree alone, not due to any necessitating cause (عِلَّة مُوجِبَة) inherent in the superior person. Sanusi then offers an analogy: a master may prefer one slave over another even if both are equally perfect, without any external reason—simply by right of his mastery. However, Sanusi notes that this analogy is approximate (تقريبي) because human masters have motives, whereas God is transcendent (مُنزَّه) above all such. He then concludes that the true reason for superiority is known only to God. Finally, he rejects the alternative view that superiority stems from the superior possessing additional or better attributes, calling such a view an affectation (تكلف) that leads to disrespect.

Key Terms
الأفضلية
العِلَّة المُوجِبَة
التقريب
المُنزَّه

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Superiority among prophets is due to their greater virtues or closer relationship with God.
Correction: Sanusi argues that superiority is purely by God's decree and not caused by any attribute in the superior person.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why does one prophet have superiority over another?
A) Because the superior prophet has more virtues.
B) Because God decrees it without a necessitating cause.
C) Because the superior prophet works harder.
D) Because the inferior prophet lacks certain attributes.

Key Takeaway
Superiority among prophets is a matter of God's sovereign decree, not human merit or attribute.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you look at the differences in people's abilities, status, or faith, do you secretly believe that these reflect something they 'deserve' based on their own efforts or qualities? How does this lesson challenge that assumption, and what would it mean for you to truly submit to God's absolute sovereignty without needing to understand the reason?
?
Are you willing to accept that God may prefer some of His servants over others—even those you consider less pious or less knowledgeable—without any cause you can perceive? What does your reaction to this reveal about your heart's attachment to your own understanding of justice?
?
Sanusi warns that attributing superiority to personal attributes leads to 'poor manners' towards God. Examine your own conversations about the Prophets or righteous people: do you ever unconsciously reduce their rank to their deeds or character? How can you revere them without imposing human frameworks on God's choices?

Critique of Improper Comparisons Among Prophets
Sanusi refutes improper comparisons and answers a defense.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's critique of comparing prophets in a way that implies belittlement, and his response to the defense of such comparisons.

Core Idea
Comparing prophets can inadvertently belittle them; we cannot presume to know what superiority requires.

Sanusi, a master of Ash'ari theology, turns his attention to a common scholarly practice that he finds burdensome: the tendency to compare prophets by highlighting the greatness of Prophet Muhammad at the expense of others. He gives a concrete example and then addresses the objection that such comparisons are justified by the Prophet's superiority. This passage challenges us to think carefully about how we honor all prophets.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic text once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Let Sanusi's tone and argument come through.
Notice that Sanusi says 'I have always found it burdensome'.
2
Identify the problematic method
Find the two examples Sanusi gives of how scholars compare prophets. Understand why he considers this method degrading.
The comparison of water gushing from a rock vs. from fingers is a key example.
3
Examine the objection and answer
Read the final part where someone argues that such comparisons are required by Prophet Muhammad's superiority. How does Sanusi respond?
Sanusi's answer is sharp: 'From where do you have that?'

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وما زِلْتُ قطُّ أستثقل ما تواطأ عليهِ الجَمَّاءُ الغفيرُ مِنَ العلماء والمحققين حيثُ يقولونَ : « إِنَّ فلاناً مِنَ الأنبياءِ حاله كذا ، وحالَ نبينا كذا ، وشتَانَ ما بين الحالين ) أو يقولون : « إِنْ كانَ اختص بكذا فعند نبيّنا ما هو أعظمُ مِنْ ذلكَ » ! كما قالوا في انفجارِ الماءِ مِنَ الحَجَرِ لموسى عليه السلام ، وانفجارِ الماءِ مِنْ بين أصابع نبينا محمَّدٍ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ، ولم يُفرِّقوا بينهما سوى أنَّ الحَجَرَ مألوفٌ منهُ انفجار الماء ، والأصابع لم يُؤلف منها ذلك ! حتى إنَّ بعض أهل العصرِ الذي يلي عصرنا نظم قصيدة طويلة مليحة استنبط فيها مِنْ أحوالِ نبينا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ومعجزاته ما وازنَ بهِ جميعَ معجزاتِ الأنبياء عليهم السلام وشريف أحوالهم ، وسلك مسلك ما ذكرناه مِنَ التباين بينَ قَدْرِ نبينا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ وغيرهِ مِنَ الأنبياء عليهم السلام ، وقد أحسنَ في ذلك وأساءَ ؛ أحسنَ مِنْ حيثُ ذلك الاستنباط ، وأساءَ لِمَا يُفهَمُ منهُ مِنَ الغضِّ والانحطاط (۱) . فإن قالوا : ذلكَ ممَّا تقتضيه أفضليَّةُ نبينا محمَّدٍ صَلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ . قلنا لهم : ومِنْ أين لكم ذلكَ ؟! والذي تقتضيه أفضليتُهُ لا نعرفُ (۱) لعله أراد الإمامَ الأديب عبد الرحيم البرعيَّ المتوفى سنة ( ٨٠٣هـ ) ، والإمام ابن عباد المتوفى سنة ( ٧٩٢ هـ ) ، وذلك في قصيدته التي في « ديوانه » ( ص ٢٤٢ ) والتي مطلعها : [ من الوافر] سمعتُ سويجعَ الأثلاتِ غنَّى على مطلولة العذبات رنا
I have always found it burdensome what the great multitude of scholars and investigators have agreed upon, where they say: 'Indeed, so-and-so of the prophets had such a state, and the state of our Prophet is such, and how far apart are the two states!' Or they say: 'If he was distinguished by such-and-such, then our Prophet has what is greater than that!' As they said regarding the gushing of water from the rock for Moses, peace be upon him, and the gushing of water from between the fingers of our Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, and they distinguished between them only by the fact that the rock is familiar for water gushing from it, while fingers are not familiar for that! Even some of the contemporaries of our age composed a long, beautiful poem in which he derived from the states of our Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, and his miracles, what he balanced with all the miracles of the prophets, peace be upon them, and their noble states, and he followed the path we mentioned of the difference between the rank of our Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, and other prophets, peace be upon them. He did well in that and did ill: he did well with respect to that derivation, and he did ill because of what is understood from it of disparagement and degradation. (1) If they say: 'That is required by the superiority of our Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace.' We say to them: 'From where do you have that? What his superiority requires, we do not know.' (1) Perhaps he meant the literary Imam Abd al-Rahim al-Bara'i, died 803 AH, and Imam Ibn Abbad, died 792 AH, in his poem in his 'Diwan' (p. 242) which begins: [al-Wafir] 'I heard the lamenter of the athilat sing...'
Source pages p. 248

Commentary
This passage presents Sanusi's critique of a common scholarly practice: comparing prophets in a way that diminishes the status of other prophets to elevate Prophet Muhammad. He finds this practice burdensome (أستثقل). He gives an example: the miracle of water gushing from a rock for Moses versus water from between the Prophet Muhammad's fingers. Scholars claim the latter is greater because fingers are not accustomed to water, while rocks are. Sanusi notes that a contemporary poet composed a poem balancing Prophet Muhammad's miracles against all other prophets' miracles. Sanusi judges that the poet did well in the derivation (استنباط) but ill because it implies belittlement (الغض والانحطاط) of other prophets. He then presents an objection: some might argue that such comparisons are required by Prophet Muhammad's superiority. Sanusi's answer is sharp: from where do you know that? The actual requirements of his superiority are unknown to us. This underscores that we cannot presume to know how superiority manifests.

Key Terms
الجَمَّاءُ الغفيرُ
الغض والانحطاط
الاستنباط
أفضلية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Comparing prophets by saying our Prophet's miracle is greater is always permissible and respectful.
Correction: Sanusi shows that such comparisons can imply belittlement of other prophets and that we do not know what superiority requires.

Checkpoint
What is Sanusi's response to the claim that comparing miracles is required by Prophet Muhammad's superiority?
A) He agrees that it is required.
B) He says it is permissible if done respectfully.
C) He says we do not know what his superiority requires.
D) He says it is forbidden.

Key Takeaway
Sanusi cautions against comparing prophets in a way that diminishes others, and reminds us that we do not know the exact requirements of Prophet Muhammad's superiority.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you speak about the miracles of other prophets, do you ever catch yourself mentally ranking them against Prophet Muhammad? Sanusi says we don't know what superiority requires. Can you admit that you might be overstepping your knowledge, and commit to simply praising each miracle as a sign of Allah's power without comparison?
?
Sanusi calls a well-intentioned poet's work 'ill' because it implied belittlement. Think of a time you praised Prophet Muhammad in a way that subtly diminished another prophet. Were you aware of the potential harm? Will you now be more cautious, even if your intention was love?
?
The phrase 'What his superiority requires, we do not know' is a direct challenge to your pride in belonging to this ummah. Are you willing to let go of the need to prove Muhammad's superiority through comparisons, and instead trust that Allah's favor is beyond your full comprehension? What would change in your daily speech if you took this seriously?

Prophethood

6 min
The Proper Source of the Prophet's Superiority
Sanusi criticizes a poet's comparison of miracles and answers an objection about the Prophet's superiority.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi criticizes the poet's comparison of miracles and how we should derive the Prophet's superiority exclusively from revelation, not from our own reasoning.

Core Idea
We know the Prophet's superiority only from his own report, not by comparing miracles.

Sanusi addresses a contemporary poet who composed a poem matching the miracles of all prophets with those of Prophet Muhammad. While praising the derivation, he warns that this approach risks belittling other prophets. He then responds to the objection that this is required by the Prophet's superiority, clarifying that we cannot know the details of that superiority by our own reasoning—only through what the Prophet himself informed us.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and identify the poet's action
Read the Arabic passage carefully. Notice that Sanusi first describes the poet's composition and gives a dual assessment: he did well in derivation but poorly in what is understood from it.
The poet attempted to match all miracles of previous prophets with those of Prophet Muhammad.
2
Examine the objection and Sanusi's response
The objector claims that such matching is required by the Prophet's superiority. Sanusi challenges: 'From where do you have that?' This is a crucial turning point.
3
Understand the epistemological basis
Sanusi explains that we do not know the details of the Prophet's superiority from ourselves. We only know it from his own reports, which are either general or specific statements like 'I have been given such and such.'
Our knowledge is limited to what the Prophet told us.
4
Grasp the consequence of overstepping
If we go beyond the revealed reports and try to deduce superiority by comparing miracles or attributes, we fall into ill-adab (bad manners) and an inextricable difficulty. The correct path is to confine ourselves to what the Prophet informed, as the imams did.
This is the key warning of the passage.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
حتى إنَّ بعض أهل العصرِ الذي يلي عصرنا نظم قصيدة طويلة مليحة استنبط فيها مِنْ أحوالِ نبينا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ومعجزاته ما وازنَ بهِ جميعَ معجزاتِ الأنبياء عليهم السلام وشريف أحوالهم ، وسلك مسلك ما ذكرناه مِنَ التباين بينَ قَدْرِ نبينا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ وغيرهِ مِنَ الأنبياء عليهم السلام ، وقد أحسنَ في ذلك وأساءَ ؛ أحسنَ مِنْ حيثُ ذلك الاستنباط ، وأساءَ لِمَا يُفهَمُ منهُ مِنَ الغضُ والانحطاط . فإن قالوا : ذلكَ ممَّا تقتضيه أفضليَّةُ نبينا محمَّدٍ صَلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ . قلنا لهم : ومِنْ أين لكم ذلكَ ؟! والذي تقتضيه أفضليتُهُ لا نعرفُ مِنْ تلقاءِ أنفسنا جُمَلَها ولا تفاصيلها ، وإنَّما نعرفُ ذلكَ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ عليهِ الصلاة والسلام ، ثمَّ إِنَّا لم نعرف مِنْ قِبَلِهِ إلا أموراً جُمْليَّةٌ لا يعلمُ حقائقها إلا مَنْ فَضَّلَهُ ، وأموراً تفصيليَّةً ربَّما نعلمها ؛ كقوله : أُعطِيتُ كذا وأُعطِيتُ كذا ، وفُضّلْتُ بكذا ، وما معناه هذا ، فإذا اعتقدنا أفضليَّتَهُ بإخبارِهِ إيَّانا بذلكَ ، ووقفنا على ما أخبرنا بهِ مِنْ بعض البعضِ مما يقتضيهِ حُكْمُ اللهِ تعالى لهُ بالأفضلية - ومَنْ لنا بالاطلاع على كُنْهِ ما يقتضيه ذلك الحكمُ منه ؟! - ثمَّ اقتصرنا على ذلك ، ولم نتجاوزه إلى أنْ نَتَعرَّضَ لالتماس ما يُوجِبُ وجودَ الأفضليَّةِ مِنْ قِبَلِ نظرنا إلى ما أُعطِيَ مِنَ الآياتِ العِظامِ ، وما طُبِعَ عليهِ مِنْ محامد الصفاتِ ، وما اتَّصفَ بهِ مِنْ محاسنِ الحالاتِ ، وما فَقَدَ غيرُهُ مِنَ الأنبياء عليهم الصلاة والسلامُ مِنْ بعض هذهِ الأشياء.. كنا في ذلك مصيبين ، سالمينَ مِنْ سوء الأدبِ معَ خواصّهِ وأحبَّائِهِ ، وإلا فإن سوء الأدب والوقوع في النشب لازم لنا لزوماً ضرورياً لا محيص عنه ، كما فعلهُ أئمتنا رضيَ اللهُ تعالى عنهم .
Until indeed some of the people of the age immediately following our age composed a long beautiful poem, in which he derived from the states of our Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, and his miracles, that which he matched with all the miracles of the prophets, peace be upon them, and their noble states. And he followed the path we mentioned of the disparity between the rank of our Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, and other prophets, peace be upon them. And he did well in that and did poorly: he did well in terms of that derivation, and did poorly because of what is understood from it of belittling and degradation. If they say: That is required by the superiority of our Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace. We say to them: From where do you have that? That which his superiority requires, we do not know in its totality nor its details from ourselves. Rather, we know that from him, peace and blessings be upon him. Then, we have not known from him except general matters, whose realities none know except the one whom he favored, and specific matters that we may know, such as his saying: 'I have been given such and such, and I have been given such and such, and I have been favored with such and such,' and what has this meaning. So if we believe in his superiority by his informing us of that, and we stop at what he informed us of, which is some of what the decree of God Most High entails for him of superiority—and who has for us the access to the inner reality of what that decree entails from him?—then we confine ourselves to that and do not go beyond it to undertaking to seek what necessitates the existence of superiority from the perspective of our looking at what he was given of great signs, and what he was naturally endowed with of praiseworthy attributes, and what he was characterized by of beautiful states, and what other prophets, peace and prayers be upon them, missed of some of these things... then in that we would be correct, safe from ill-adab (bad manners) with his elect and his beloved ones. Otherwise, ill-adab and falling into al-nashab (an inextricable difficulty) would be necessary for us by necessary necessity with no escape from it, as our imams, may God be pleased with them, did.
Source pages p. 249

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer argument combined with a critique of an improper method. The author first criticizes a contemporary poet who composed a poem matching all the miracles of previous prophets with those of Prophet Muhammad. The poet's method of deriving parallels is praised, but the implication of belittling previous prophets is condemned. This leads to a potential objection: someone might say that such matching is required by the Prophet's superiority. The author answers sharply: 'From where do you have that?' He explains that we do not know the details of what the Prophet's superiority entails from our own reasoning. We only know it from the Prophet himself, and even then only general matters or specific explicit statements. Therefore, we must confine ourselves to what he reported and not go beyond to speculate about why he is superior based on comparing miracles or attributes. Doing so would lead to 'ill-adab' (improper conduct) with the Prophet's loved ones (i.e., previous prophets) and falling into an inextricable difficulty. The author concludes that the correct path, as followed by the imams, is to avoid such comparisons and accept the superiority based solely on divine report.

Key Terms
أفضلية (superiority)
استنباط (derivation)
سوء الأدب (ill-adab)
نشب (inextricable difficulty)
إخبار (report/informing)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Prophet's superiority can be proven by logically comparing his miracles to those of other prophets.
Correction: Sanusi insists that we only know the Prophet's superiority from his own reported statements and divine decree, not from our own reasoning or comparison of miracles.

Checkpoint
According to the author, how do we know the Prophet's superiority?
A) By comparing his miracles to those of other prophets
B) From the Prophet's own reports and divine decree
C) By rational deduction from his attributes
D) Through the consensus of scholars

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's superiority is known only through divine report, not human reasoning, and overstepping this leads to ill-adab.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about the Prophet's superiority over other prophets, do you find yourself mentally comparing his miracles to theirs? What does that instinct reveal about your reliance on revelation versus your own reasoning? Will you commit to checking such thoughts and replacing them with what the Prophet himself has said?
?
Sanusi warns that overstepping leads to 'ill-adab' (bad manners) with the Prophet's loved ones—the other prophets. Have you ever, even unintentionally, spoken or thought about another prophet in a way that might belittle them in comparison to Muhammad? How can you cultivate deeper respect for them, starting today?
?
If the only way to know the Prophet's superiority is through his own report, are you content to limit your knowledge to what he revealed? Or do you feel a pull to 'prove' it through your own analysis? What would it cost you to surrender that need to prove and simply submit to his word? Ask yourself: is your faith in his superiority based on what he told you, or on what you can verify? And if it's the latter, how secure is that faith?

The Danger of Independent Reasoning About Prophetic Superiority
Sanusi contrasts proper reliance on prophetic report with dangerous independent reasoning.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why we must not independently reason about the reasons for a prophet's superiority but must rely on what the Prophet informed us.

Core Idea
Prophetic superiority is known only through divine report, not human reasoning.

Sanusi begins by clarifying the proper epistemological approach to the question of prophetic superiority. He warns that attempting to deduce the causes of a prophet's excellence through our own observation of his attributes leads to ill-adab. Instead, we must accept what the Prophet himself has told us and stop there.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how Sanusi presents two scenarios: one safe and one dangerous.
The passage is a single conditional argument.
2
Identify the two scenarios
Find the 'if' and 'otherwise' parts. What is the safe approach? What leads to ill-adab?
3
Reflect on the role of revelation
Consider why Sanusi insists that we cannot know superiority from ourselves. What does this teach about the limits of human reason in theology?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مِنْ تلقاءِ أنفسنا جُمَلَها ولا تفاصيلها ، وإنَّما نعرفُ ذلكَ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ عليهِ الصلاة والسلام ، ثمَّ إِنَّا لم نعرف مِنْ قِبَلِهِ إلا أموراً جُمْليَّةٌ لا يعلمُ حقائقها إلا مَنْ فَضَّلَهُ ، وأموراً تفصيليَّةً ربَّما نعلمها ؛ كقوله : أُعطِيتُ كذا وأُعطِيتُ كذا ، وفُضّلْتُ بكذا ، وما معناه هذا ، فإذا اعتقدنا أفضليَّتَهُ بإخبارِهِ إيَّانا بذلكَ ، ووقفنا على ما أخبرنا بهِ مِنْ بعض البعضِ مما يقتضيهِ حُكْمُ اللهِ تعالى لهُ بالأفضلية - ومَنْ لنا بالاطلاع على كُنْهِ ما يقتضيه ذلك الحكمُ منه ؟! - ثمَّ اقتصرنا على ذلك ، ولم نتجاوزه إلى أنْ نَتَعرَّضَ لالتماس ما يُوجِبُ وجودَ الأفضليَّةِ مِنْ قِبَلِ نظرنا إلى ما أُعطِيَ مِنَ الآياتِ العِظامِ ، وما طُبِعَ عليهِ مِنْ محامد الصفاتِ ، وما اتَّصفَ بهِ مِنْ محاسنِ الحالاتِ ، وما فَقَدَ غيرُهُ مِنَ الأنبياء عليهم الصلاة والسلامُ مِنْ بعض هذهِ الأشياء.. كنا في ذلك مصيبين ، سالمينَ مِنْ سوء الأدبِ معَ خواصّهِ وأحبَّائِهِ ، وإلا فإن سوء الأدب والوقوع في النشب لازم لنا لزوماً ضرورياً لا محيص عنه ، كما فعلهُ أئمتنا رضيَ اللهُ تعالى عنهم .
ولا أقولُ : إنَّهم في ذلكَ بمنزلةِ مَنْ هدم قصراً وبنى مصراً ، أو بنى قصراً وهدم مصراً ، ولكنَّهم بمنزلة مَنْ هدمَهُما جميعاً ؛ لأنَّ الأفضل لا يحبُّ أن يُفضَّلَ بشيءٍ لم يجعله مولاه سبباً في وجودِ أفضليَّتِهِ ، ولا يحبُّ أيضاً أنْ يُحَطَّ الفاضلُ عن مرتبتِهِ ، كما قالَ عليهِ الصلاة والسلام : « لا تُفَضِّلُوا بَيْنَ الْأَنْبِيَاءِ ، وَلَا تُخَيَّرُونِي عَلَى مُوسَى، وَلا يَقُولَنَّ أَحَدُكُمْ : أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ يُونُسَ بْن مَتَّى »، والمفضولُ إنَّما لا يحبُّ أنْ يُجعَلَ لمفضوليَّتِهِ عِلَّةٌ لم يجعلها مولاه ؛ وهو فقدُهُ ما اتّصفَ بهِ الأفضلُ ، ولا يحبُّ أيضاً أنْ يُفرَّقَ بينَهُ وبينَ الأفضل ، وهم جميعاً رُسُلُ اللهِ عزَّ وجلَّ ، وعدم محبَّةِ كلَّ واحدٍ منهم لهذا كلّهِ إنَّما هو لحقِّ الله تعالى ، لا لهم ، فآل سوء الأدب معهم إلى سوء الأدب مع الله تبارك وتعالى ، وهذا عظيم .
فهذا كلامٌ جرَّ إليهِ ما كنا بصددِهِ ؛ مِنْ بيانِ أَنَّ الأسماء التي سمَّى الله تعالى بها نبيَّه محمَّداً صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أو أحداً مِنْ أَنبيائِهِ ورُسُلِهِ عليهم السلام .. لا يُقالُ في بعضها : إِنَّهُ أَشرفُ مِنْ بعض ؛ مِنْ حيثُ تسمية الله تعالى بذلكَ ، وأمَّا مِنْ حيثُ تسمية غيرِهِ ، كما إذا سمَّى ذلك الشخص المختص نفسَه .. فلا ينبغي لهُ أَنْ يُسمِّيَ نفسَهُ إلا باسم العبد ، ولا يختارُ إلا ذلكَ ، كما قال صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : خُيِّرْتُ بَيْنَ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيَّاً مَلِكاً ، أَوْ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً .. فَأَخْتَرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً
From ourselves [we know] its generalities nor its details, but we know that only from him, peace and blessings be upon him. Then, we do not know from him except general matters whose realities none know except the One who favored him, and detailed matters which perhaps we know; such as his saying: 'I was given such and such, and I was given such and such, and I was favored with such and such,' and what has this meaning. So if we believe in his superiority by his informing us of that, and we stop at what he informed us of, from some of the things that the judgment of God Almighty necessitates for him of superiority — and who is there for us to be informed of the inner reality of what that judgment necessitates from him?! — then we restrict ourselves to that and do not go beyond it to seek what necessitates the existence of superiority by our own consideration of what he was given of great signs, what he was naturally disposed to of praiseworthy attributes, what he was characterized by of excellent states, and what other prophets, peace and blessings be upon them, lack of some of these things... we would be correct in that, safe from ill-adab (Sū' al-Adab) with his elite and his beloved ones. Otherwise, ill-adab and falling into difficulty would be necessarily incumbent upon us with no escape, as our imams, may God be pleased with them, did. And I do not say that they are in that like one who demolished a palace and built a city, or built a palace and demolished a city, but they are like one who demolished both of them; because the superior one does not like to be favored by something that his Lord did not make a cause for his superiority, nor does he like that the excellent one be lowered from his rank, as he, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'Do not give preference between the prophets, and do not prefer me over Moses, and let none of you say: I am better than Jonah son of Matta.' And the inferior one does not like that a cause be made for his inferiority that his Lord did not make; that is, his lacking what the superior one is characterized by, nor does he like that a distinction be made between him and the superior, and all of them are messengers of God Almighty. And the dislike of each one of them for all of this is only for the right of God Almighty, not for themselves. So ill-adab with them leads to ill-adab with God, Blessed and Most High, and this is serious. So this is speech to which what we were discussing led: to clarify that the names by which God Almighty named His Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, or any of His prophets and messengers, peace be upon them... it should not be said of some of them that it is more honorable than some, from the perspective of God's naming thereby. And as for the perspective of naming by other than Him, such as when that specific person names himself... then he should not name himself except with the name of servant ('abd), and he should not choose other than that, as he, may God bless him and grant him peace, said: 'I was given the choice between being a prophet-king or a prophet-servant, and I chose to be a prophet-servant.'
Source pages p. 250

Commentary
This passage is a theological and ethical argument about the proper attitude towards the superiority of prophets, particularly the Prophet Muhammad. The author, Sanusi, warns against attempting to deduce or rationalize the reasons for a prophet's superiority by examining his attributes or signs independently, as this leads to ill-adab (lack of proper respect) towards the prophets and ultimately towards God.

The author begins by stating that we cannot know from ourselves the comprehensive realities or details of prophetic superiority; we only know them from the Prophet himself. From him, we know general matters whose true nature only God knows, and some specific matters like his statements about what he was given. If we simply believe in his superiority based on his own report and restrict ourselves to that, without trying to seek out causes through our own observation of his signs, attributes, and states, we are correct and safe from ill-adab. Otherwise, ill-adab is inevitable.

Sanusi then uses a metaphor: those who engage in such independent reasoning are not like one who demolishes a palace to build a city or vice versa, but like one who demolishes both. The explanation is that the superior (al-afḍal) does not like to be favored by something that God did not make the cause of his superiority, nor does he like for the excellent one to be lowered in rank. The inferior (al-mafḍūl) also does not like for his inferiority to be attributed to a cause God did not make, nor does he like to be distinguished from the superior. All prophets are messengers of God, and their dislike of these things is for God's right, not for themselves. Thus, ill-adab with them becomes ill-adab with God.

Finally, Sanusi connects this to the issue of the names of prophets: the names by which God named them should not be compared in terms of which is more honorable, from the perspective of God's naming. As for when a person names himself, he should only choose the name 'servant' (ʿabd), as the Prophet chose to be a prophet-servant rather than a prophet-king.

Key Terms
أفضليَّة
سوء الأدب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One can independently determine the reason for a prophet's superiority through rational analysis of his attributes.
Correction: Sanusi argues this leads to ill-adab; we must rely solely on what the Prophet informed us.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what is the consequence of trying to deduce a prophet's superiority by examining his attributes without relying solely on revelation?
A) It leads to correct belief and deeper understanding.
B) It is permissible as long as one remains respectful.
C) It constitutes ill-adab with God and His prophets.
D) It is a commendable act of scholarly investigation.

Key Takeaway
Prophetic superiority must be affirmed solely through prophetic report, not through independent reasoning, to avoid ill-adab.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever caught yourself trying to 'figure out' why Allah chose certain prophets for specific honors, basing your reasoning on your own sense of justice or merit? How does Sanusi's warning about exceeding prophetic report challenge that tendency in your own heart? What will you do today to replace that habit with pure reliance on revelation?
?
If you truly believe that the inner reality of Allah's judgment is beyond your comprehension, what changes in your daily spiritual life would that conviction demand? Are you willing to stop analyzing Allah's choices and instead submit to them with complete adab? What is one specific area where you need to practice this submission starting now?
?
Reflect on a moment when you felt you understood the wisdom behind a prophetic distinction better than what was explicitly stated in the sources. How did that sense of 'knowing better' affect your heart's state before Allah and His Prophet? What step will you take this week to guard your tongue and heart from such ill-adab?

The Prohibition of Comparing Prophets and the Etiquette of Preference
Sanusi uses a metaphor and a hadith to explain the prohibition of comparing prophets.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the rationale behind the prohibition of comparing prophets and the metaphor Sanusi uses to explain the proper etiquette.

Core Idea
Comparing prophets in terms of rank is a form of ill-adab that displeases both the superior and the inferior.

Sanusi deepens the discussion by explaining why independent reasoning about superiority is not just a methodological error but a breach of adab. He uses a powerful metaphor of demolishing both a palace and a city, and cites a hadith prohibiting preference between prophets. This lesson unpacks the reasons behind these rulings.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Notice the metaphor and the hadith.
2
Analyze the metaphor
Why does Sanusi say that those who compare are like one who demolishes both a palace and a city? What does each represent?
The superior (al-afḍal) and the inferior (al-mafḍūl) both dislike certain things.
3
Understand the hadith
The Prophet forbade preferring between prophets. Why? What is the basis for this prohibition?
It is for God's right, not for the prophets themselves.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books

From ourselves [we know] its generalities nor its details, but we know that only from him, peace and blessings be upon him. Then, we do not know from him except general matters whose realities none know except the One who favored him, and detailed matters which perhaps we know; such as his saying: 'I was given such and such, and I was given such and such, and I was favored with such and such,' and what has this meaning. So if we believe in his superiority by his informing us of that, and we stop at what he informed us of, from some of the things that the judgment of God Almighty necessitates for him of superiority — and who is there for us to be informed of the inner reality of what that judgment necessitates from him?! — then we restrict ourselves to that and do not go beyond it to seek what necessitates the existence of superiority by our own consideration of what he was given of great signs, what he was naturally disposed to of praiseworthy attributes, what he was characterized by of excellent states, and what other prophets, peace and blessings be upon them, lack of some of these things... we would be correct in that, safe from ill-adab (Sū' al-Adab) with his elite and his beloved ones. Otherwise, ill-adab and falling into difficulty would be necessarily incumbent upon us with no escape, as our imams, may God be pleased with them, did. And I do not say that they are in that like one who demolished a palace and built a city, or built a palace and demolished a city, but they are like one who demolished both of them; because the superior one does not like to be favored by something that his Lord did not make a cause for his superiority, nor does he like that the excellent one be lowered from his rank, as he, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'Do not give preference between the prophets, and do not prefer me over Moses, and let none of you say: I am better than Jonah son of Matta.' And the inferior one does not like that a cause be made for his inferiority that his Lord did not make; that is, his lacking what the superior one is characterized by, nor does he like that a distinction be made between him and the superior, and all of them are messengers of God Almighty. And the dislike of each one of them for all of this is only for the right of God Almighty, not for themselves. And as for the perspective of naming by other than Him, such as when that specific person names himself... then he should not name himself except with the name of servant ('abd), and he should not choose other than that, as he, may God bless him and grant him peace, said: 'I was given the choice between being a prophet-king or a prophet-servant, and I chose to be a prophet-servant.'

Commentary
This passage is a theological and ethical argument about the proper attitude towards the superiority of prophets, particularly the Prophet Muhammad. The author, Sanusi, warns against attempting to deduce or rationalize the reasons for a prophet's superiority by examining his attributes or signs independently, as this leads to ill-adab (lack of proper respect) towards the prophets and ultimately towards God.

Otherwise, ill-adab is inevitable.

Sanusi then uses a metaphor: those who engage in such independent reasoning are not like one who demolishes a palace to build a city or vice versa, but like one who demolishes both. The explanation is that the superior (al-afḍal) does not like to be favored by something that God did not make the cause of his superiority, nor does he like for the excellent one to be lowered in rank. Key Terms
تفضيل الأنبياء
سوء الأدب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: It is permissible to compare prophets in terms of rank as long as one acknowledges the superiority of Muhammad.
Correction: Sanusi argues that any such comparison is a form of ill-adab that offends both the superior and inferior prophet. The Prophet himself forbade it.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why does the inferior prophet dislike being compared to the superior?
A) Because he is jealous.
B) Because a cause is made for his inferiority that God did not make.
C) Because he wants to be superior.
D) Because he dislikes the superior.

Key Takeaway
Comparing prophets is prohibited because it offends both the superior and inferior prophet, and ultimately leads to ill-adab with God.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Have you ever, even in your heart, compared a scholar or a righteous person to another in a way that made you belittle the other? How does this passage’s warning that ill-adab with the prophets leads to ill-adab with God expose the hidden arrogance in such comparisons? Will you repent and train your tongue and heart to honour every believer’s rank as decreed by Allah?
?
When you hear the Prophet ﷺ say, 'Do not prefer me over Moses,' does your ego feel a twinge of resistance or an urge to justify your own preference? What does that reaction reveal about your attachment to your own opinions over submission to Allah’s command? How will you actively silence that voice and obey literally?
?
If the prophets themselves (the superior and the inferior) dislike being compared for Allah’s right, not their own, then what right do you have to make such comparisons for your own satisfaction? Think of a specific recent conversation where you ranked people. How will you guard against this in your future speech?
