The Names of Prophets and Choosing the Name 'Servant'
Sanusi discusses the proper attitude towards the names of prophets and the Prophet's choice of being a servant.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that the names by which God named His prophets should not be compared in terms of honor, and that the best name for a person to choose is 'servant' ('abd).

Core Idea
God's naming of prophets is beyond comparison; the best personal name is 'servant'.

Sanusi concludes the discussion by addressing a related issue: the names of prophets. He states that we should not say that one name is more honorable than another from the perspective of God's naming. However, from the perspective of a person naming himself, the best name to choose is 'servant' ('abd), following the Prophet's own choice to be a prophet-servant rather than a prophet-king.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic and translation. Identify the two perspectives: God's naming and human naming.
2
Understand the rule about God's naming
Why should we not compare the names of prophets when God named them? What is the reasoning?
Sanusi implies it is part of the adab not to rank God's names.
3
Learn from the Prophet's choice
The Prophet chose to be a prophet-servant rather than a prophet-king. What does this teach about the virtue of being a servant of God?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مِنْ تلقاءِ أنفسنا جُمَلَها ولا تفاصيلها ، وإنَّما نعرفُ ذلكَ مِنْ قِبَلِهِ عليهِ الصلاة والسلام ، ثمَّ إِنَّا لم نعرف مِنْ قِبَلِهِ إلا أموراً جُمْليَّةٌ لا يعلمُ حقائقها إلا مَنْ فَضَّلَهُ ، وأموراً تفصيليَّةً ربَّما نعلمها ؛ كقوله : أُعطِيتُ كذا وأُعطِيتُ كذا ، وفُضّلْتُ بكذا ، وما معناه هذا ، فإذا اعتقدنا أفضليَّتَهُ بإخبارِهِ إيَّانا بذلكَ ، ووقفنا على ما أخبرنا بهِ مِنْ بعض البعضِ مما يقتضيهِ حُكْمُ اللهِ تعالى لهُ بالأفضلية - ومَنْ لنا بالاطلاع على كُنْهِ ما يقتضيه ذلك الحكمُ منه ؟! - ثمَّ اقتصرنا على ذلك ، ولم نتجاوزه إلى أنْ نَتَعرَّضَ لالتماس ما يُوجِبُ وجودَ الأفضليَّةِ مِنْ قِبَلِ نظرنا إلى ما أُعطِيَ مِنَ الآياتِ العِظامِ ، وما طُبِعَ عليهِ مِنْ محامد الصفاتِ ، وما اتَّصفَ بهِ مِنْ محاسنِ الحالاتِ ، وما فَقَدَ غيرُهُ مِنَ الأنبياء عليهم الصلاة والسلامُ مِنْ بعض هذهِ الأشياء.. كنا في ذلك مصيبين ، سالمينَ مِنْ سوء الأدبِ معَ خواصّهِ وأحبَّائِهِ ، وإلا فإن سوء الأدب والوقوع في النشب لازم لنا لزوماً ضرورياً لا محيص عنه ، كما فعلهُ أئمتنا رضيَ اللهُ تعالى عنهم .
ولا أقولُ : إنَّهم في ذلكَ بمنزلةِ مَنْ هدم قصراً وبنى مصراً ، أو بنى قصراً وهدم مصراً ، ولكنَّهم بمنزلة مَنْ هدمَهُما جميعاً ؛ لأنَّ الأفضل لا يحبُّ أن يُفضَّلَ بشيءٍ لم يجعله مولاه سبباً في وجودِ أفضليَّتِهِ ، ولا يحبُّ أيضاً أنْ يُحَطَّ الفاضلُ عن مرتبتِهِ ، كما قالَ عليهِ الصلاة والسلام : « لا تُفَضِّلُوا بَيْنَ الْأَنْبِيَاءِ ، وَلَا تُخَيَّرُونِي عَلَى مُوسَى، وَلا يَقُولَنَّ أَحَدُكُمْ : أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ يُونُسَ بْن مَتَّى »، والمفضولُ إنَّما لا يحبُّ أنْ يُجعَلَ لمفضوليَّتِهِ عِلَّةٌ لم يجعلها مولاه ؛ وهو فقدُهُ ما اتّصفَ بهِ الأفضلُ ، ولا يحبُّ أيضاً أنْ يُفرَّقَ بينَهُ وبينَ الأفضل ، وهم جميعاً رُسُلُ اللهِ عزَّ وجلَّ ، وعدم محبَّةِ كلَّ واحدٍ منهم لهذا كلّهِ إنَّما هو لحقِّ الله تعالى ، لا لهم ، فآل سوء الأدب معهم إلى سوء الأدب مع الله تبارك وتعالى ، وهذا عظيم .
فهذا كلامٌ جرَّ إليهِ ما كنا بصددِهِ ؛ مِنْ بيانِ أَنَّ الأسماء التي سمَّى الله تعالى بها نبيَّه محمَّداً صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أو أحداً مِنْ أَنبيائِهِ ورُسُلِهِ عليهم السلام .. لا يُقالُ في بعضها : إِنَّهُ أَشرفُ مِنْ بعض ؛ مِنْ حيثُ تسمية الله تعالى بذلكَ ، وأمَّا مِنْ حيثُ تسمية غيرِهِ ، كما إذا سمَّى ذلك الشخص المختص نفسَه .. فلا ينبغي لهُ أَنْ يُسمِّيَ نفسَهُ إلا باسم العبد ، ولا يختارُ إلا ذلكَ ، كما قال صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : خُيِّرْتُ بَيْنَ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيَّاً مَلِكاً ، أَوْ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً .. فَأَخْتَرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً
From ourselves [we know] its generalities nor its details, but we know that only from him, peace and blessings be upon him. Then, we do not know from him except general matters whose realities none know except the One who favored him, and detailed matters which perhaps we know; such as his saying: 'I was given such and such, and I was given such and such, and I was favored with such and such,' and what has this meaning. So if we believe in his superiority by his informing us of that, and we stop at what he informed us of, from some of the things that the judgment of God Almighty necessitates for him of superiority — and who is there for us to be informed of the inner reality of what that judgment necessitates from him?! — then we restrict ourselves to that and do not go beyond it to seek what necessitates the existence of superiority by our own consideration of what he was given of great signs, what he was naturally disposed to of praiseworthy attributes, what he was characterized by of excellent states, and what other prophets, peace and blessings be upon them, lack of some of these things... we would be correct in that, safe from ill-adab (Sū' al-Adab) with his elite and his beloved ones. Otherwise, ill-adab and falling into difficulty would be necessarily incumbent upon us with no escape, as our imams, may God be pleased with them, did. And I do not say that they are in that like one who demolished a palace and built a city, or built a palace and demolished a city, but they are like one who demolished both of them; because the superior one does not like to be favored by something that his Lord did not make a cause for his superiority, nor does he like that the excellent one be lowered from his rank, as he, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'Do not give preference between the prophets, and do not prefer me over Moses, and let none of you say: I am better than Jonah son of Matta.' And the inferior one does not like that a cause be made for his inferiority that his Lord did not make; that is, his lacking what the superior one is characterized by, nor does he like that a distinction be made between him and the superior, and all of them are messengers of God Almighty. And the dislike of each one of them for all of this is only for the right of God Almighty, not for themselves. So ill-adab with them leads to ill-adab with God, Blessed and Most High, and this is serious. So this is speech to which what we were discussing led: to clarify that the names by which God Almighty named His Prophet Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, or any of His prophets and messengers, peace be upon them... it should not be said of some of them that it is more honorable than some, from the perspective of God's naming thereby. And as for the perspective of naming by other than Him, such as when that specific person names himself... then he should not name himself except with the name of servant ('abd), and he should not choose other than that, as he, may God bless him and grant him peace, said: 'I was given the choice between being a prophet-king or a prophet-servant, and I chose to be a prophet-servant.'
Source pages p. 250

Commentary
This passage is a theological and ethical argument about the proper attitude towards the superiority of prophets, particularly the Prophet Muhammad. The author, Sanusi, warns against attempting to deduce or rationalize the reasons for a prophet's superiority by examining his attributes or signs independently, as this leads to ill-adab (lack of proper respect) towards the prophets and ultimately towards God.

The author begins by stating that we cannot know from ourselves the comprehensive realities or details of prophetic superiority; we only know them from the Prophet himself. From him, we know general matters whose true nature only God knows, and some specific matters like his statements about what he was given. If we simply believe in his superiority based on his own report and restrict ourselves to that, without trying to seek out causes through our own observation of his signs, attributes, and states, we are correct and safe from ill-adab. Otherwise, ill-adab is inevitable.

Sanusi then uses a metaphor: those who engage in such independent reasoning are not like one who demolishes a palace to build a city or vice versa, but like one who demolishes both. The explanation is that the superior (al-afḍal) does not like to be favored by something that God did not make the cause of his superiority, nor does he like for the excellent one to be lowered in rank. The inferior (al-mafḍūl) also does not like for his inferiority to be attributed to a cause God did not make, nor does he like to be distinguished from the superior. All prophets are messengers of God, and their dislike of these things is for God's right, not for themselves. Thus, ill-adab with them becomes ill-adab with God.

Finally, Sanusi connects this to the issue of the names of prophets: the names by which God named them should not be compared in terms of which is more honorable, from the perspective of God's naming. As for when a person names himself, he should only choose the name 'servant' (ʿabd), as the Prophet chose to be a prophet-servant rather than a prophet-king.

Key Terms
أسماء الأنبياء
عبد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some names of prophets are more honorable than others due to their meanings.
Correction: Sanusi says we should not compare the names from God's perspective. The honor is in God's naming, not in the name's inherent meaning.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, which name should a person choose for himself?
A) The most honorable name he can think of.
B) The name 'servant' ('abd).
C) A name that reflects prophecy.
D) The name of the Prophet Muhammad.

Key Takeaway
God's naming of prophets is beyond comparison, and the best personal name reflecting humility is 'servant' ('abd).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were given the choice between being a 'servant' or a 'king' in your life, which would you truly prefer? Look at your daily actions and choices—do they reflect the humility of the Prophet or the desire for status? Name one area where you can actively choose servitude over control.
?
The Prophet picked the name 'abd. What are the 'kingships' in your life—positions, possessions, or pride—that you need to abandon to fully embody servitude to God? Write one concrete step you will take this week to practice humility in a specific situation.
?
When you introduce yourself or think of your identity, what name feels most fitting? Are you comfortable being called a 'servant of God', or does that title feel difficult? What does that discomfort reveal about your heart, and how can you grow in embracing this title?

The Prohibition of Preferring Among Prophets and Its Reason
Sanusi explains the proper attitude of superior and inferior prophets regarding rank.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why both superior and inferior prophets do not desire personal preference, and how this reflects reverence for Allah's rights.

Core Idea
Respecting prophetic ranks means respecting Allah's wisdom, not personal status.

This passage from Sanusi explains a subtle but crucial point about the moral and theological basis for not comparing prophets. It is not merely about avoiding offense, but about recognizing that praising one over another based on worldly considerations contradicts the divine assignment of ranks. The Prophet's own hadith is cited to ground this prohibition.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation. Notice how Sanusi first discusses the superior prophet, then the inferior.
The hadith is quoted directly.
2
Identify the two attitudes
Identify what the superior does not love (being praised for non-divine causes) and what the inferior does not love (being considered lesser for a reason not given by Allah).
Both are based on Allah's decree.
3
Understand the underlying reason
Understand that both prophets' lack of love for these things is for Allah's sake, not their own. Disrespect towards prophets leads to disrespect towards Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الأفضل لا يحبُّ أن يُفضَّلَ بشيءٍ لم يجعله مولاه سبباً في وجودِ أفضليَّتِهِ ، ولا يحبُّ أيضاً أنْ يُحَطَّ الفاضلُ عن مرتبتِهِ ، كما قالَ عليهِ الصلاة والسلام : « لا تُفَضِّلُوا بَيْنَ الْأَنْبِيَاءِ ، وَلَا تُخَيَّرُونِي عَلَى مُوسَى، وَلا يَقُولَنَّ أَحَدُكُمْ : أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ يُونُسَ بْن مَتَّى . والمفضولُ إنَّما لا يحبُّ أنْ يُجعَلَ لمفضوليَّتِهِ عِلَّةٌ لم يجعلها مولاه ؛ وهو فقده ما اتّصفَ به الأفضلُ ، ولا يحبُّ أيضاً أنْ يُفرَّقَ بينَهُ وبينَ الأفضل ، وهم جميعاً رُسُلُ اللهِ عزَّ وجلَّ ، وعدم محبَّةِ كلَّ واحدٍ منهم لهذا كلِّهِ إنَّما هو لحقِّ الله تعالى ، لا لهم ، فآل سوء الأدب معهم إلى سوء الأدب مع الله تبارك وتعالى ، وهذا عظيم
The superior does not love to be given preference by something that his Lord did not make a cause for the existence of his superiority, nor does he love that the superior be brought down from his rank, as he, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'Do not give preference among the prophets, and do not make me better than Moses, and let none of you say: I am better than Jonah son of Amittai.' And the inferior only does not love that a cause be made for his inferiority that his Lord did not make; and that is his lack of what the superior is characterized by, nor does he love that a distinction be made between him and the superior, and all of them are messengers of Allah, Mighty and Majestic. And the lack of love of each one of them for all of this is only for the sake of Allah, not for themselves. So disrespect towards them leads to disrespect towards Allah, Blessed and Exalted, and this is grave.
Source pages p. 251

Commentary
This passage explains the proper attitude of the superior and the inferior among prophets regarding rank. Sanusi argues that the superior (al-afḍal) does not desire to be esteemed for any quality not granted by Allah as the cause of his superiority, nor does he want the superior to be lowered from his station. This is illustrated by the Prophet's hadith forbidding preference among prophets and commanding not to claim superiority over Moses or Jonah. Similarly, the inferior (al-mafḍūl) does not want his lesser rank to be attributed to a cause not ordained by Allah—namely, his lack of the superior's qualities—nor does he desire separation from the superior, since all are messengers of Allah. Their disapproval of such comparisons is solely out of reverence for Allah's rights, not for their own. Thus, disrespect towards prophets ultimately becomes disrespect towards Allah, which is a grave matter.

Key Terms
الأفضل
المفضول
لا تفضلوا بين الأنبياء

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that comparing prophets is a matter of personal opinion or that it shows respect to elevate one over another.
Correction: Sanusi shows that such comparisons violate the divine assignment of ranks and reflect disrespect towards Allah's wisdom.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why does the inferior prophet not love to be considered inferior for a cause not granted by Allah?
A) Because he is jealous of the superior prophet.
B) Because it would imply that Allah's decree is incomplete.
C) Because his inferiority is due to his own lack, not a cause Allah did not make.
D) Because all prophets are equal in rank.

Key Takeaway
Disrespect towards prophets ultimately becomes disrespect towards Allah, because their ranks are divinely ordained.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you hear someone casually say they prefer one prophet over another, do you challenge that statement, or do you remain silent? What does your silence say about your reverence for Allah's wisdom in assigning ranks? This week, commit to gently correcting such statements with the hadith from this lesson.
?
Have you ever internally ranked prophets based on your own understanding of their stories, feeling that one is 'greater' in your heart? How does Sanusi's argument show that such thinking is rooted in disrespect for Allah, not just ignorance? Repent of any such private thoughts and resolve to see all prophets as Allah sees them.
?
If someone mocked a prophet's shortcomings (e.g., Yunus's leaving his people), would you feel the gravity of that as an affront to Allah? Examine your own reaction: are you more concerned about the prophet's honor or about divine displeasure? Strengthen your intention to defend all prophets for Allah's sake alone.

The Honor of the Name 'Abd' and Why the Prophet Chose It
Sanusi explains the superiority of the name 'Abd' for prophets and the Prophet's choice.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the name 'Abd' (slave) is the most suitable name for prophets and why the Prophet Muhammad chose it over being a prophet-king.

Core Idea
The name 'Abd' signifies complete annihilation before Allah, making it the noblest name for a prophet.

Sanusi discusses the significance of the names Allah bestows upon His prophets, explaining that from Allah's perspective no name is superior. However, when a prophet names himself, the most appropriate title is 'Abd', as exemplified by the Prophet Muhammad's choice to be a prophet-slave. This lesson unpacks the reasoning behind this preference.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Understand the distinction
Identify the key distinction Sanusi makes between names from Allah's perspective and names from a human perspective. This is central to the argument.
3
Reflect on the Prophet's choice
Consider why the Prophet chose to be a 'prophet-slave' rather than a 'prophet-king'. What does this tell you about the value of servitude?
The hadith is the proof.
4
Consider the hypothetical
Sanusi says if there were a name implying even more annihilation, the Prophet would have chosen it. This reinforces the idea that 'Abd' is the best possible name.
The poet's line is used as illustration.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فهذا كلامٌ جرَّ إليهِ ما كنا بصددِهِ ؛ مِنْ بيانِ أَنَّ الأسماء التي سمَّى الله تعالى بها نبيَّه محمَّداً صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أو أحداً مِنْ أَنبيائِهِ ورُسُلِهِ عليهم السلام .. لا يُقالُ في بعضها : إِنَّهُ أَشرفُ مِنْ بعض ؛ مِنْ حيثُ تسمية الله تعالى بذلكَ ، وأمَّا مِنْ حيثُ تسمية غيرِهِ ، كما إذا سمَّى ذلك الشخص المختص نفسَه .. فلا ينبغي لهُ أَنْ يُسمِّيَ نفسَهُ إلا باسم العبد ، ولا يختارُ إلا ذلكَ ، كما قال صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : خُيِّرْتُ بَيْنَ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيَّاً مَلِكاً ، أَوْ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً .. فَأَخْتَرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً . ولو وجدَ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ اسماً يَتضمَّنُ مِنَ التلاشي والعدم أشدَّ ممَّا يتضمَّنهُ اسم العبد .. لتسمَّى به واختارَهُ ، ويكون اسمُ العبدِ مِنْ هذهِ الحيثيَّةِ أشرفَ أسمائِهِ ، كما قال الشاعر : لا تَدْعُنِي إِلا بِيَا عَبْدَهَا فَإِنَّهُ أَشْرَفُ أَسْمَائِي . ثم قال : ولا معنى عندي لقولِ مَنْ قالَ في قولِهِ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : « أَنَا سَيِّدُ وَلَدِ آدَمَ ، وَلا فَخْرَ » : « ولا فخر لي بالسيادة ، وإنَّما الفخر لي بالعبودية »؛ لأنَّ الفخر أمر مذموم مطلقاً ، وهو الذي نفاه صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ونزَّهَ نفسَهُ عنهُ ، فقال : « وَلا فَخْرَ » ، خافَ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أنْ ينسبَهُ بعضُ مَنْ سَمِعَ أَوَّلَ كَلامِهِ إِلَى أَنَّهُ افتخر ، فحفظ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ موضعَ الفتنةِ مِنْ قلوب السامعين فقالَ : « وَلا فَخْرَ » أي : إِنَّما أعلمتُكم بسيادتي ؛ لتعلموا بذلك منزلتي ومكانتي ، ولنقوم بواجب حقٌّ ربي ، ونعمل بأمره في التحدث بنعمه ، وإشهار أمرِها ، وإشادة ذكرها .
So this is speech that we were led to by what we were dealing with: from the explanation that the names by which Allah named His Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, or any of His prophets and messengers, peace be upon them, one does not say about some of them that it is nobler than another, from the perspective of Allah naming Himself by that. But from the perspective of naming by other than Him, as when that specific person names himself, it is not befitting for him to name himself except with the name 'slave' ('Abd), and he does not choose except that, as the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'I was given the choice between being a prophet-king or a prophet-slave, and I chose to be a prophet-slave.' And if the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, had found a name that implied annihilation and non-existence more severely than what the name 'slave' implies, he would have named himself with it and chosen it. And the name 'slave' from this perspective is the noblest of his names, as the poet said: 'Do not call me except as O slave of her, for it is the noblest of my names.' Then he said: I do not accept the saying of those who said about his, peace and blessings be upon him, statement: 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride' that is: 'There is no pride for me in mastery, but pride is for me in servitude.' Because pride is blameworthy absolutely, and it is that which the Prophet negated and purified himself from, so he said: 'and no pride.' He feared, peace and blessings be upon him, that some of those who heard the first part of his speech would attribute to him that he was boasting, so he guarded the place of temptation from the hearts of the listeners and said: 'and no pride,' meaning: 'I only informed you of my mastery so that you may know thereby my rank and station, and so that we may fulfill the due right of my Lord, and act by His command in speaking of His blessings, publicizing them, and elevating their mention.'
Source pages p. 251

Commentary
This passage is a combined explanation of two related points. First, Sanusi clarifies that the names given by Allah to prophets—such as 'Muhammad' or 'Ahmad'—cannot be compared for superiority from the divine perspective. But from the human perspective, when a prophet names himself, the most appropriate name is 'Abd' (slave/servant). This is proven by the Prophet's choice to be a prophet-slave rather than a prophet-king. Sanusi adds that if there were a name implying even greater selflessness and annihilation (talāshī wa 'adam), the Prophet would have chosen it, making 'Abd' the noblest of his names in this respect. He illustrates with a poetic reference. Second, Sanusi addresses an interpretation of the hadith 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride' (anā sayyidu waladi Ādama wa lā fakhra). Some claimed that 'no pride' means 'I have no pride in mastery, but pride in servitude'. Sanusi rejects this because pride (fakhr) is absolutely blameworthy. The Prophet's addition 'wa lā fakhra' was to prevent listeners from misunderstanding his declaration of mastery as boasting. The real purpose of announcing his rank is to make known his status so that people fulfill Allah's rights and speak of His blessings, not out of arrogance.

Key Terms
عبد
تلاشي
عدم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that certain names given by Allah to prophets are inherently superior to others.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that from the divine perspective, no name is nobler than another because they are all from Allah. Superiority only applies to human naming.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why did the Prophet choose to be a prophet-slave?
A) Because he preferred humility over royalty.
B) Because the name 'Abd' implies annihilation before Allah, and he wanted the name that most reflects that.
C) Because kingship is forbidden for prophets.
D) Because the angel instructed him to choose that.

Key Takeaway
The name 'Abd' is the most suitable for prophets because it signifies total servitude and annihilation before Allah, reflecting the highest degree of humility.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you were given a choice between being seen as a 'king' (powerful, self-sufficient) or a 'slave' (utterly dependent and humble) in your life, which would you truly prefer? Why does the Prophet's choice of 'slave' make you uncomfortable?
?
The Prophet would have chosen a name even more humiliating if it existed. What does that reveal about your own attachment to your titles, achievements, or reputation? Are you willing to be a 'mere slave' before Allah, or do you secretly want recognition?
?
Sanusi says 'Abd' is the noblest because it implies annihilation and non-existence. Do you live with the awareness that you are nothing without Allah? What would change in your daily actions if you truly internalized that you are only a slave (ʿabd)?

Prophethood
beginner
4 min
The Meaning of 'No Pride' in the Hadith of Mastery
Sanusi refutes a misinterpretation of the hadith 'I am the master... and no pride'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand Sanusi's refutation of the claim that the Prophet's 'no pride' refers to pride in servitude, and the correct purpose of his declaration of mastery.

Core Idea
Pride is always blameworthy; the Prophet's 'no pride' negates all pride, not just pride in mastery.

Sanusi addresses a common misinterpretation of the Prophet's statement 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride'. Some claimed that the negation applies only to mastery, leaving room for pride in servitude. Sanusi firmly rejects this, explaining that pride is inherently blameworthy and that the Prophet's words were meant to prevent any misunderstanding of boasting, while his true purpose was to guide believers in acknowledging Allah's blessings.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice that Sanusi uses a very compressed style here.
2
Understand the misinterpretation
Identify the incorrect interpretation that Sanusi is refuting: that 'no pride' applies only to mastery.
3
Grasp Sanusi's reasoning
Why does Sanusi reject that interpretation? Focus on his statement that pride is absolutely blameworthy.
4
Learn the true purpose
Sanusi explains why the Prophet declared his mastery and then added 'no pride'. What is the real purpose?
It is to inform of rank so that people fulfill Allah's rights.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فهذا كلامٌ جرَّ إليهِ ما كنا بصددِهِ ؛ مِنْ بيانِ أَنَّ الأسماء التي سمَّى الله تعالى بها نبيَّه محمَّداً صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أو أحداً مِنْ أَنبيائِهِ ورُسُلِهِ عليهم السلام .. لا يُقالُ في بعضها : إِنَّهُ أَشرفُ مِنْ بعض ؛ مِنْ حيثُ تسمية الله تعالى بذلكَ ، وأمَّا مِنْ حيثُ تسمية غيرِهِ ، كما إذا سمَّى ذلك الشخص المختص نفسَه .. فلا ينبغي لهُ أَنْ يُسمِّيَ نفسَهُ إلا باسم العبد ، ولا يختارُ إلا ذلكَ ، كما قال صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : خُيِّرْتُ بَيْنَ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيَّاً مَلِكاً ، أَوْ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً .. فَأَخْتَرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ نَبِيّاً عَبْداً . ولو وجدَ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ اسماً يَتضمَّنُ مِنَ التلاشي والعدم أشدَّ ممَّا يتضمَّنهُ اسم العبد .. لتسمَّى به واختارَهُ ، ويكون اسمُ العبدِ مِنْ هذهِ الحيثيَّةِ أشرفَ أسمائِهِ ، كما قال الشاعر : لا تَدْعُنِي إِلا بِيَا عَبْدَهَا فَإِنَّهُ أَشْرَفُ أَسْمَائِي . ثم قال : ولا معنى عندي لقولِ مَنْ قالَ في قولِهِ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : « أَنَا سَيِّدُ وَلَدِ آدَمَ ، وَلا فَخْرَ » : « ولا فخر لي بالسيادة ، وإنَّما الفخر لي بالعبودية »؛ لأنَّ الفخر أمر مذموم مطلقاً ، وهو الذي نفاه صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ونزَّهَ نفسَهُ عنهُ ، فقال : « وَلا فَخْرَ » ، خافَ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أنْ ينسبَهُ بعضُ مَنْ سَمِعَ أَوَّلَ كَلامِهِ إِلَى أَنَّهُ افتخر ، فحفظ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ موضعَ الفتنةِ مِنْ قلوب السامعين فقالَ : « وَلا فَخْرَ » أي : إِنَّما أعلمتُكم بسيادتي ؛ لتعلموا بذلك منزلتي ومكانتي ، ولنقوم بواجب حقٌّ ربي ، ونعمل بأمره في التحدث بنعمه ، وإشهار أمرِها ، وإشادة ذكرها .
So this is speech that we were led to by what we were dealing with: from the explanation that the names by which Allah named His Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, or any of His prophets and messengers, peace be upon them, one does not say about some of them that it is nobler than another, from the perspective of Allah naming Himself by that. But from the perspective of naming by other than Him, as when that specific person names himself, it is not befitting for him to name himself except with the name 'slave' ('Abd), and he does not choose except that, as the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, said: 'I was given the choice between being a prophet-king or a prophet-slave, and I chose to be a prophet-slave.' And if the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, had found a name that implied annihilation and non-existence more severely than what the name 'slave' implies, he would have named himself with it and chosen it. And the name 'slave' from this perspective is the noblest of his names, as the poet said: 'Do not call me except as O slave of her, for it is the noblest of my names.' Then he said: I do not accept the saying of those who said about his, peace and blessings be upon him, statement: 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride' that is: 'There is no pride for me in mastery, but pride is for me in servitude.' Because pride is blameworthy absolutely, and it is that which the Prophet negated and purified himself from, so he said: 'and no pride.' He feared, peace and blessings be upon him, that some of those who heard the first part of his speech would attribute to him that he was boasting, so he guarded the place of temptation from the hearts of the listeners and said: 'and no pride,' meaning: 'I only informed you of my mastery so that you may know thereby my rank and station, and so that we may fulfill the due right of my Lord, and act by His command in speaking of His blessings, publicizing them, and elevating their mention.'

Commentary
This passage is a combined explanation of two related points. First, Sanusi clarifies that the names given by Allah to prophets—such as 'Muhammad' or 'Ahmad'—cannot be compared for superiority from the divine perspective. But from the human perspective, when a prophet names himself, the most appropriate name is 'Abd' (slave/servant). This is proven by the Prophet's choice to be a prophet-slave rather than a prophet-king. Sanusi adds that if there were a name implying even greater selflessness and annihilation (talāshī wa 'adam), the Prophet would have chosen it, making 'Abd' the noblest of his names in this respect. He illustrates with a poetic reference. Second, Sanusi addresses an interpretation of the hadith 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride' (anā sayyidu waladi Ādama wa lā fakhra). Some claimed that 'no pride' means 'I have no pride in mastery, but pride in servitude'. Sanusi rejects this because pride (fakhr) is absolutely blameworthy. The Prophet's addition 'wa lā fakhra' was to prevent listeners from misunderstanding his declaration of mastery as boasting. The real purpose of announcing his rank is to make known his status so that people fulfill Allah's rights and speak of His blessings, not out of arrogance.

Key Terms
لا فخر
سيادة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Prophet meant 'I have no pride in mastery, but I have pride in servitude.'
Correction: Pride is always blameworthy; the Prophet negated all pride. The 'no pride' was to prevent misunderstanding; the real purpose was to guide people in acknowledging blessings.

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi reject the interpretation that 'wa lā fakhra' means 'I have no pride in mastery, but pride in servitude'?
A) Because servitude is not worthy of pride.
B) Because pride is absolutely blameworthy and the Prophet negated it entirely.
C) Because the Prophet only negated pride in mastery, not in servitude.
D) Because the hadith is about something else.

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's statement 'wa la fakhra' negates all pride; his declaration of mastery was to inform of his status so that people fulfill Allah's rights, not to boast.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you achieve something, do you ever feel a sense of pride? How does this lesson challenge your understanding of pride in your own life? What specific step can you take this week to avoid any form of pride, even in your acts of worship?
?
The Prophet declared his mastery not to boast but to fulfil Allah's command to speak of blessings. Think of one blessing you have received. Have you ever shared it with the intention of showing off? Without this lesson, how might you have justified it? What change will you make?
?
Consider a time when you were praised for something. Did any part of you enjoy that praise? According to this lesson, even feeling proud of your humility is blameworthy. How can you train your heart to seek only Allah's approval and not the admiration of others? Write one practical action.

Module 10 · Lesson 159

Prophethood
intermediate
8 min
The Prophet's Absolute Negation of Pride
Sanusi refutes the claim that the Prophet's pride is in servitude and affirms absolute negation.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Prophet's statement 'I do not boast' is an absolute negation of pride, not restricted to mastership, and how the author refutes the claim that his pride is in servitude.

Core Idea
The Prophet negated all pride, not just in mastership, making servitude an equally invalid basis for pride.

This lesson examines a key theological argument by Sanusi regarding the Prophet's humility. Some interpreters claimed that when the Prophet said 'I do not boast' about being the master of mankind, he meant that his pride is instead in his servitude to Allah. Sanusi refutes this by showing that pride is absolutely blameworthy and that the Prophet's negation applies to all honors alike. Through careful analysis of the hadith and logical reasoning, Sanusi demonstrates that attributing pride to any aspect of the Prophet's station is a misunderstanding.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Notice the author's style and the flow of his argument.
2
Identify the two interpretations
Identify the first interpretation: that the Prophet's pride is in servitude. Then see how Sanusi refutes it. Look for his logical points.
The author uses an objection-and-answer structure.
3
Reflect on the extended hadith
The author cites a longer hadith where the Prophet lists several honors each followed by 'wa la fakhra'. Reflect on how this supports the absolute negation.
This is the key evidence for Sanusi's position.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
عَبْداً ، ولو وجدَ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ اسماً يَتضمَّنُ مِنَ التلاشي والعدم أشدَّ ممَّا يتضمَّنهُ اسم العبد .. لتسمَّى به واختارَهُ ، ويكون اسمُ العبدِ مِنْ هذهِ الحيثيَّةِ أشرفَ أسمائِهِ ، كما قال الشاعر : [من السريع] لا تَدْعُنِي إِلا بِيَا عَبْدَهَا فَإِنَّهُ أَشْرَفُ أَسْمَائِي
ثم قال : ولا معنى عندي لقولِ مَنْ قالَ في قولِهِ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : « أَنَا سَيِّدُ وَلَدِ آدَمَ ، وَلا فَخْرَ » : « ولا فخر لي بالسيادة ، وإنَّما الفخر لي بالعبودية » ؛ لأنَّ الفخر أمر مذموم مطلقاً ، وهو الذي نفاه صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ ونزَّهَ نفسَهُ عنهُ ، فقال : « وَلا فَخْرَ » ، خافَ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ أنْ ينسبَهُ بعضُ مَنْ سَمِعَ أَوَّلَ كَلامِهِ إِلَى أَنَّهُ افتخر ، فحفظ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ موضعَ الفتنةِ مِنْ قلوب السامعين فقالَ : « وَلا فَخْرَ » أي : إِنَّما أعلمتُكم بسيادتي ؛ لتعلموا بذلك منزلتي ومكانتي ، ولنقوم بواجب حقٌّ ربي ، ونعمل بأمره في التحدث بنعمه ، وإشهار أمرِها ، وإشادة ذكرها .
ثم قال : وقولُ مَنْ قالَ في معنى الحديثِ : « إِنَّما الفخرُ لي بالعبودية » كلام لا أفهمُهُ ؛ لأنَّ العبودية نسبتها إليه وإلى غيره نسبةً واحدةً .
فإن قيل : إنَّما عنى بذلك العبودية التي هي حاله ومَقامُهُ .
قلنا : إنَّما يصح الفخرُ بها إن صح مِنْ حيثُ كونُها مِنَّةٌ مِنَ اللهِ تعالى عليه ، فإن صح الفخرُ بها مِنْ هذا الوجه.. فلِمَ لا يصح افتخاره بالسيادة ، وهي أيضاً مِنَّةٌ مِنَ اللهِ تعالى عليهِ ؟!
فالظاهرُ : أَنَّهُ عليهِ الصلاة والسلامُ نفى التفاخر النفي المطلق ولم يَخُصَّ ذلك بسيادة ولا غيرها ، كما قال صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : « أَنَا سَيْدُ وَلَدِ آدَمَ وَلا فَخْرَ ، وَأَنَا حَامِلُ لِوَاءِ الْحَمْدِ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَلا فَخْرَ، وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ مَنْ تَنْشَقُ عَنْهُ الْأَرْضُ وَلَا فَخْرَ ، وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ شَافِعِ وَأَوَّلُ مُشَفّع وَلا فَخْرَ، وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ مَنْ يُحَرِّكُ حِلَقَ الْجَنَّةِ فَأَدْخُلُهَا مَعَ فُقَرَاءِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلا فَخْرَ ، وَأَنَا أَكْرَمُ الأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ وَلا فَخْرَ »
فبانَ لكَ بهذا كلِّهِ : أنَّ إطلاق الأولوية والأشرفية في بعض الأسماء دونَ بعض مِنْ غيرِ نظر إلى ما ذكرناه مِنْ تسمية الله تعالى وتسمية غيرِه.. قصور في النظرِ
A servant, and had the Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, found a name that implies more from annihilation and non-existence than the name 'abd implies, he would have named himself with it and chosen it, and the name 'abd from this aspect is the most honorable of his names, as the poet said: [al-sari'] 'Call me only with O her servant, for it is the most honorable of my names.' Then he said: And I find no meaning in the saying of one who said regarding his statement, may Allah bless him and grant him peace: 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and I do not boast' (wa la fakhra): 'I have no pride in mastership, but rather pride in servitude'; because pride is absolutely blameworthy, and it is what the Prophet negated and declared himself innocent of, so he said, 'and I do not boast.' The Prophet, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, feared that some who heard the beginning of his speech would attribute to him that he was boasting, so he guarded the place of temptation from the hearts of the listeners and said, 'and I do not boast,' i.e., I only informed you of my mastership so that you may know my rank and status, and so that we fulfill the obligatory right of my Lord, and act by His command in speaking of His blessings, making them known, and exalting their mention. Then he said: And the saying of one who said regarding the meaning of the hadith: 'Pride belongs to me only in servitude' is speech I do not understand, because servitude relates to him and to others in a single relation. If it is said: He meant by that the servitude which is his state and station. We say: Pride in it would be valid only if it is sound from the aspect of it being a grace from Allah upon him. If pride in it is valid from this aspect, then why is his pride in mastership not valid, and it is also a grace from Allah upon him? The apparent meaning is that he, peace and blessings be upon him, negated boasting absolutely and did not specify that to mastership or anything else, as he said: 'I am the master of the children of Adam and I do not boast, and I am the bearer of the banner of praise on the Day of Resurrection and I do not boast, and I am the first from whom the earth will split and I do not boast, and I am the first intercessor and the first whose intercession will be accepted and I do not boast, and I am the first to knock on the rings of Paradise and enter it with the poor believers and I do not boast, and I am the most noble of the first and the last and I do not boast.' So it became clear to you by all this that applying priority and nobility to some names over others without considering what we mentioned concerning Allah's naming and the naming of others is a deficiency in consideration.
Source pages p. 252

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of a claim about the Prophet's words. The author argues against the interpretation that the Prophet's statement 'wa la fakhra' (I do not boast) means he has no pride in his mastership but has pride in his servitude. The author explains that pride (fakhr) is absolutely blameworthy, and the Prophet negated it entirely. The Prophet clarified 'wa la fakhra' to prevent listeners from thinking he was boasting about his status. Then the author refutes the specific claim: servitude is a common attribute between the Prophet and others, so it cannot be the basis for pride; if it is considered a grace from God, then mastership is also a grace, so why would pride in that be invalid? The author cites the extended hadith where the Prophet lists several of his honors, each followed by 'wa la fakhra,' showing the negation is absolute and not restricted to mastership. The conclusion is that giving priority to some names (like 'abd) over others (like sayyid) without considering the divine naming principle is a deficiency in reasoning.

Key Terms
عبودية
سيادة
فخر
تلاشي
عدم
اسم

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Prophet allowed pride in his servitude but not in his mastership.
Correction: The author shows that the negation of pride is absolute and applies to all honors, including servitude. The Prophet did not make an exception for servitude.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, why does the author reject the claim that the Prophet's pride is only in his servitude?
A) Because the Prophet never used the word 'fakhr' for himself.
B) Because servitude is a common attribute, and if it were a grace from God, then mastership is also a grace, so pride in either would be equally invalid.
C) Because the hadith specifically says the Prophet has no pride in anything.
D) Because the author prefers the name 'abd over sayyid.

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's negation of pride is absolute and applies to all his honors, including servitude, so no form of pride is permissible.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you ever feel proud of your humility or your service to Islam? How does the Prophet's absolute negation of pride—even in servitude—force you to examine the hidden arrogance in your own heart?
?
You claim to follow the Prophet, yet you have felt pride in your achievements, whether religious or worldly. What would it cost you to abandon that pride entirely and embrace true selflessness?
?
The Prophet refused to boast about even the greatest honors. Are you willing to let go of the pride you feel in your own status, reputation, or spiritual rank? What practical step can you take today to uproot that pride?

Prophethood
intermediate
8 min
The Prophet's Absolute Denial of Boasting
Sanusi refutes an interpretation that the Prophet's denial of pride refers only to servitude.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why Sanusi rejects the interpretation that the Prophet's denial of boasting refers only to servitude, and how this affirms the Prophet's absolute humility.

Core Idea
The Prophet denied all boasting absolutely, not just in particular honors.

Sanusi engages with a subtle misinterpretation of the Prophet's famous statement 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride.' Some claimed that the Prophet meant his only pride is in servitude. Sanusi finds this unintelligible and offers a logical refutation, supported by multiple hadiths where the Prophet enumerates his honors and repeatedly says 'no pride.' This lesson clarifies the correct understanding of prophetic humility.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage and its English translation. Pay attention to the flow of argument: Sanusi presents a misinterpretation, rejects it, answers an objection, and then provides evidence from hadiths.
2
Identify the two interpretations
Notice that Sanusi rejects the idea that the Prophet meant pride only in servitude. He then considers a refined version (special servitude) and refutes it by asking why pride in mastership would not be allowed if both are from God.
This shows a logical reasoning pattern common in kalam.
3
Analyze the evidence from hadiths
The Prophet mentions several honors—mastership, banner of praise, first to be resurrected, first intercessor, first to enter Paradise, most honorable—and after each says 'no pride.' This proves the denial is absolute.
The repetition emphasizes that no honor justifies boasting.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وقولُ مَنْ قالَ في معنى الحديثِ : « إِنَّما الفخرُ لي بالعبودية » كلام لا أفهمُهُ ؛ لأنَّ العبودية نسبتها إليه وإلى غيره نسبةً واحدةً .
فإن قيل : إنَّما عنى بذلك العبودية التي هي حاله ومَقامُهُ .
قلنا : إنَّما يصح الفخرُ بها إنْ صَحَّ مِنْ حيثُ كونُها مِنَّةٌ مِنَ اللهِ تعالى عليه ، فإن صح الفخرُ بها مِنْ هذا الوجه.. فلِمَ لا يصح افتخاره بالسيادة ، وهي أيضاً مِنَّةٌ مِنَ اللهِ تعالى عليهِ ؟!
فالظاهرُ : أَنَّهُ عليهِ الصلاة والسلامُ نفى التفاخر النفي المطلق ولم يَخُصَّ ذلك بسيادة ولا غيرها ، كما قال صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ : ( أَنَا سَيْدُ وَلَدِ آدَمَ وَلا فَخْرَ ، وَأَنَا حَامِلُ لِوَاءِ الْحَمْدِ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَلا فَخْرَ، وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ مَنْ تَنْشَقُ عَنْهُ الْأَرْضُ وَلَا فَخْرَ ، وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ شَافِعِ وَأَوَّلُ مُشَفّع وَلا فَخْرَ، وَأَنَا أَوَّلُ مَنْ يُحَرِّكُ حِلَقَ الْجَنَّةِ فَأَدْخُلُهَا مَعَ فُقَرَاءِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلا فَخْرَ ، وَأَنَا أَكْرَمُ الأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ وَلا فَخْرَ )
فبانَ لكَ بهذا كلّهِ : أنَّ إطلاق الأولوية والأشرفية في بعض الأسماء دونَ بعض مِنْ غيرِ نظر إلى ما ذكرناه مِنْ تسمية الله تعالى وتسمية غيرِه.. قصور في النظرِ ( انتهى بلفظه ، وقليل منه بالمعنى )
And the statement of the one who said about the meaning of the hadith: 'Pride is only for me in servitude' is speech I do not understand; because the relationship of servitude to him and to others is one relationship. If it is said: 'He meant thereby the servitude which is his state and station.' We say: 'Pride in it is only valid if it is correct from the aspect of it being a favor from God upon him. If pride in it is valid from this aspect, then why is his pride in mastership not valid, and it also is a favor from God upon him?' So the apparent meaning is that he, peace and blessings be upon him, denied boasting absolutely and did not single out mastership or anything else, as he said, peace and blessings be upon him: 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride; I am the bearer of the banner of praise on the Day of Resurrection, and no pride; I am the first from whom the earth will split open, and no pride; I am the first intercessor and the first whose intercession is accepted, and no pride; I am the first to move the rings of Paradise and enter it with the poor believers, and no pride; I am the most honorable of the first and the last, and no pride.' So it became clear to you by all this that applying priority and nobility to some names without consideration of what we mentioned of naming God and naming others is a shortcoming in insight. (Ended with his wording, and a little with meaning.)
Source pages p. 253

Commentary
This passage is a refutation of an interpretation of a hadith in which the Prophet is reported to have said 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and I am not boasting.' Some people claimed that the Prophet meant that his only pride is in his servitude to God. The author rejects this interpretation as unintelligible, because the attribute of servitude is relative and applies equally to the Prophet and to others. If it is argued that what is meant is a particular state of servitude that is unique to the Prophet, the author responds that if pride in that state is permissible because it is a grace from God, then pride in his mastership (sayyid) should also be permissible, as that too is a grace from God. The author then asserts that the apparent meaning of the hadith is that the Prophet denied all forms of boasting absolutely, not just in certain titles. This is supported by multiple hadiths where the Prophet enumerates his honors—being the master of all humans, the bearer of the banner of praise, the first to be resurrected, the first intercessor, the first to enter Paradise—and after each he says 'and no pride.' The author concludes that applying priority and honor to some names (like 'sayyid') without considering the distinction between divine names and human names is a deficiency in insight. The passage thus clarifies the correct understanding of the Prophet's humility and rejection of any form of self-praise.

Key Terms
العبودية
السيادة
الفخر
النفي المطلق

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The Prophet denied pride only in his servitude, meaning he was proud of his mastership.
Correction: Sanusi shows that the Prophet denied all boasting absolutely, including in his mastership and other honors, as demonstrated by the hadiths where he says 'no pride' after each honor.

Checkpoint
What is the main point the author makes about the Prophet's statement 'I am the master of the children of Adam, and no pride'?
A) The Prophet was proud of his servitude.
B) The Prophet denied all forms of boasting absolutely, not just in specific honors.
C) The Prophet only denied pride in his mastership, not in other honors.
D) The Prophet's statement is ambiguous and cannot be understood.

Key Takeaway
The Prophet's denial of boasting is absolute; no honor, including mastership, justifies pride.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you achieve something significant—whether in your career, education, or even religious practice—do you feel a sense of pride that separates you from others? How does the Prophet's absolute denial of pride challenge the way you view your own accomplishments? Will you take a concrete step this week to acknowledge a success without any trace of self-congratulation?
?
Think of a time you silently considered yourself 'better' than another believer because of your knowledge, piety, or status. How does Sanusi's argument that servitude is equal for all press on that feeling? Are you willing to ask God tonight to purify your heart from that hidden pride?
?
The Prophet mentioned his unique honors but immediately denied boasting about them. What honors do you hold onto—perhaps your lineage, your role in the community, or your sacrifices for Islam—that you secretly feel give you a right to be recognized? Will you surrender that right today and seek only God's pleasure?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
4 min
The Six Necessary Attributes of Allah
Sanusi lists the six necessary attributes of Allah, divided into two categories.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the six attributes that are necessarily true of Allah and their division into the psychological attribute and the attributes of negation.

Core Idea
Allah's necessary attributes define His exclusive perfection and transcendence.

Imam al-Sanusi begins his discussion of Allah's necessary attributes by listing them in a concise yet profound statement. These attributes are foundational to Islamic theology, establishing that Allah is necessarily existent, eternal, unlike creation, self-subsistent, and absolutely one. This lesson unpacks these key concepts.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage
Read the Arabic passage once to get the flow. Notice the author's name and salutation, then the list of attributes.
The attributes are presented in a list after 'اعلم' (know).
2
Identify the two categories
The passage divides the attributes into two types: the psychological attribute (الصفة النفسية) which is 'واجب الوجود' (necessary of existence), and the attributes of negation (صفات السلوب) which include five attributes. Find each one.
Psychological here means essential, not psychological as in modern usage.
3
Understand each attribute
For each attribute, consider its meaning and significance. The commentary explains that 'قائم بنفسه' means independent of place and specifier.
These attributes are all about what Allah is not (negations) except for the first which is positive.
4
Reflect on the order
Notice the logical order: existence, then eternity, then difference, then self-subsistence, then unity. Why might this order be chosen?

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مقدمة المؤلف
بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم وصلى الله على سيدنا ومولانا محمد وآله وصحبه وسلم
قال الشيخ الإمام العالم أبو عبد الله سيدي محمد بن يوسف السنوسي الحسني رحمه الله تعالى ورضي عنه ونفعنا به :
الحمد لله ، والصلاة والسلام على رسول الله
1 الصفات الواجبة له سبحانه ] [ الصفة النفسية ، وصفات السلوب ]
اعلم : أن مولانا جل وعز واجب الوجود ، والقدم والبقاء ، مخالف لخلقه ، قائم بنفسه ؛ أي : غني عن المحل والمخصص ، واحد في ذاته وصفاته وأفعاله
Introduction of the author. In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful, and may Allah bless our master and patron Muhammad and his family and companions and grant them peace. The Shaykh, the Imam, the scholar, Abu Abdullah, my master Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Sanusi al-Hasani, may Allah the Exalted have mercy on him and be pleased with him and benefit us through him, said: Praise be to Allah, and blessings and peace be upon the Messenger of Allah. 1. The attributes necessary for Him, Exalted is He: [The psychological attribute, and the attributes of negation]. Know that our Master, may He be glorified and exalted, is Necessary of Existence, Pre-eternal, Everlasting, Different from His creation, Self-subsistent; meaning: Independent of place and specifier, One in His essence, attributes, and actions.
Source pages p. 86

Commentary
This passage is an introductory definition of the necessary attributes (الصفات الواجبة) of Allah. The author, Imam al-Sanusi, begins with the basmala and praise, then states his topic. He lists six attributes that are obligatory for Allah, which are divided into two categories: the psychological attribute (الصفة النفسية) which is 'necessary of existence' (واجب الوجود), and the attributes of negation (صفات السلوب) which are: pre-eternity (القدم), everlastingness (البقاء), being different from creation (مخالف لخلقه), being self-subsistent (قائم بنفسه) which means independent of place and specifier, and oneness (واحد) in essence, attributes, and actions. The author explains that 'self-subsistent' (قائم بنفسه) means Allah is not in need of a locus or a specifying agent. This sets the stage for the detailed discussion of each attribute. The order reflects a logical progression: first establishing that Allah's existence is necessary, then that He is eternal and everlasting, then that He is unlike anything created, then that He is self-sufficient and not dependent on anything, and finally that He is one and unique in every respect. These are foundational to Islamic theology.

Key Terms
واجب الوجود
القدم
البقاء
مخالف لخلقه
قائم بنفسه
واحد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The necessary attributes are just poetic or rhetorical descriptions of Allah.
Correction: These attributes are serious theological categories that define what is rationally necessary for Allah, based on scriptural and rational proofs.

Checkpoint
Which attribute of Allah is described as 'غني عن المحل والمخصص' (independent of place and specifier)?
A) واجب الوجود
B) القدم
C) قائم بنفسه
D) واحد

Key Takeaway
The six necessary attributes of Allah are: necessary existence (psychological attribute), and pre-eternity, everlastingness, difference from creation, self-subsistence, and oneness (attributes of negation).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage teaches that Allah is قائم بنفسه—self-subsistent, independent of everything. Where in your life do you still rely on created things or people more than you rely on Allah? What would it mean for you today to truly live as if Allah alone is sufficient?
?
Allah is مخالف لخلقه, completely different from His creation. Have you ever imagined Allah with human-like qualities, even if unconsciously? How can you purify your understanding of Allah to reflect His absolute transcendence?
?
The attribute الواحد reminds us that Allah is one in essence, attributes, and actions—nothing shares in His oneness. Is there any area of your life where you give the love, fear, or hope that belongs only to Allah to something else? Will you repent from that today?

What Is Kalam?

The Meaning of ‘Ālam: From Language to Theology
Sanusi defines ‘ālam linguistically and theologically.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the term ‘ālam (world) is defined linguistically and how it was adopted by kalam theologians to refer to everything created.

Core Idea
The word ‘ālam points to creation’s distinction from the Creator.

Sanusi begins by unpacking the word ‘ālam (world) as it appears in the Quranic phrase “Lord of the worlds.” He traces its linguistic roots, explains its plural form, and shows how theologians repurposed it to refer to every originated thing. The key insight is that everything created bears signs that set it apart from its Creator.

Guided Path
1
Understand the linguistic origin of 'ālam
Read the explanation of the root tarbiya and the plural 'ālamūn. Notice how language evolves.
"Tarbiya" originally meant nurturing, then came to mean ownership.
2
Grasp the lexical definition of 'ālam
Sanusi defines 'ālam as every species or genus with a distinguishing mark. Internalize the examples (humans, birds, animals, etc.).
The distinguishing mark is what makes it known.
3
See why theologians repurposed the term
The mutakallimūn transferred 'ālam to every created thing because each creation has signs that distinguish it from the eternal Creator.
This prevents any confusion between Creator and creation.
4
Connect to the broader purpose
Reflect on how this definition lays the groundwork for proving that the world is originated (hādith) and thus points to a Creator.
This will be developed later.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الكلام على العالَم مِنْ حيث تسميته ودَلالته على الله تعالى ] رب العالمين . أصل التربية : نقلُ الشيء مِنْ أمرٍ إلى أمر حتى يصير إلى غاية أرادها المربي ، ثمَّ نُقِلَ إلى المالك والمصلح للزوم التربية لهما غالباً . والعالَمونَ : جمع سلامة للعالم على غير قياس ، والعالم في اللُّغةِ : كلُّ نوع أو جنس فيهِ علامة يمتاز بها عن سائر الأنواع والأجناس الحادثةِ ؛ فيُقالُ في الأنواع : عالم الإنسانِ ، وعالم الطيرِ ، وعالم الخيلِ ، ويُقالُ في الأجناس : عالم الحيوانِ ، وعالم الأجسام ، وعالم الناميات . ويحتمل أن تكونَ المناسبة في تسمية النوع والجنس بالعالم : أنَّ لهما مِنَ الفصولِ والخواص ما يُعلَمانِ بهِ. ونقله المتكلمون إلى كل حادث ، والمناسبة في هذه التسمية : أنَّ كل حادثٍ فيهِ علامات تُميَّزه عن موجدِهِ المولى القديم حتى لا يلتبس بهِ أصلاً، ولهذا ردَّ مولانا جل وعلا على الضَّالِّينَ الذين جعلوا له
Discourse on the world, regarding its naming and its indication of God Almighty. Lord of the worlds. The origin of 'tarbiya' is: transferring something from one state to another until it reaches the goal intended by the one who raises it. Then it was transferred to the owner and the reformer because raising is usually necessary for them. And 'al-ʿālamūn' is a sound masculine plural of 'al-ʿālam' irregularly. And 'al-ʿālam' in language is: every species or genus in which there is a sign by which it is distinguished from other originated species and genera. So it is said concerning species: the world of humans, the world of birds, the world of horses; and it is said concerning genera: the world of animals, the world of bodies, the world of growing things. And it is possible that the appropriateness in naming the species and genus as 'ʿālam' is that they have distinguishing features and properties by which they are known. And the mutakallimūn transferred it to every originated thing. And the appropriateness in this naming is that every originated thing has signs that distinguish it from its Maker, the Eternal Lord, so that it is never confused with Him. And for this reason our Lord, Mighty and Majestic, refuted the astray ones who made for Him...
Source pages p. 104

Commentary
This passage is a linguistic and technical analysis of the term 'ālam' (world). Sanusi begins with the Quranic phrase 'Lord of the worlds' and then explains the root 'tarbiya' (nurturing) and how it came to mean ownership and reform. Then he discusses the plural 'ālamūn' as an irregular sound masculine plural. In language, 'ālam' refers to any species or genus that has a distinguishing mark. He gives examples: species like humans, birds, horses; genera like animals, bodies, growing things. The appropriateness is that they are known by their differentiating features. Then the mutakallimūn (theologians) transferred the term to every originated thing (hādith), i.e., everything created. The appropriateness here is that every created thing has signs that distinguish it from the eternal Creator, preventing any confusion. Finally, he mentions that God refuted those who set up partners with Him, implying that the signs of creation point to the sole Creator.

Key Terms
العالم
العالَمون
حادث
المتكلمون
تربية

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some may think 'world' refers only to the earth.
Correction: In kalam, 'ālam encompasses all created species and genera, including humans, animals, bodies, etc., as Sanusi illustrates.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, why did the mutakallimūn use the term 'ālam' for every originated thing?
A) Because it is linguistically correct
B) Because every created thing has signs distinguishing it from God
C) Because it is in the Quran
D) Because of the plural form

Key Takeaway
The term ‘ālam in kalam refers to every created thing, each bearing signs that distinguish it from the eternal Creator.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Look at the 'signs' in your own body and life that prove you are a creature, not the Creator. How does this recognition change the way you plan your day?
?
If every created thing has signs that distinguish it from Allah, why do you still sometimes live as if you are independent of Him? What one habit most blurs that distinction for you?
?
The mutakallimūn stressed that confusion between Creator and creation is impossible, yet shirk persists. Where in your heart do you still mingle love for creation with love for the Creator? What will you remove today to purify that?

beginner
5 min
The Meaning of 'Seal of the Prophets'
Sanusi defines 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' and deduces its logical implication.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the literal meaning of 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' and its necessary implication that no messenger can come after Prophet Muhammad.

Core Idea
The Seal of Prophecy closes the door to all prophets and messengers.

Sanusi begins by defining the term 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' as the last of the prophets. He then derives an important logical consequence: since prophethood is a broader category than messengerhood, the end of prophethood necessarily means the end of messengerhood as well. This lesson unpacks that definition and the principle behind it.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation to grasp the literal meaning.
Notice the logical structure of definition then implication.
2
Identify the logical principle
The passage uses a logic rule: negation of the more general (prophet) implies negation of the more specific (messenger). Understand why this is valid.
This principle is central in Islamic theology and law.
3
Reflect on significance
Consider why this distinction matters: it settles the finality of prophethood and messengerhood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ومعنى خاتم النبيين أنه آخرهم، وبه كمل عددهم الذي هو مئة ألف وأربعة وعشرون ألفا، فلا نبي بعده، ومن لازمه: أن لا رسول بعده؛ لأن النبي أعم من الرسول على الصحيح، ونفي الأعم يستلزم نفي الأخص؛ فكمل سبحانه لنبينا ومولانا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام جميع المحاسن التي تفرقت في الأنبياء والرسل قبله، وشرع شريعته السمحة؛ بأن جعل أحكامها متصلة بالآخرة، لا ناسخ لها ولا مبدل لها، وأطلع أمته المشرّفة على مساوي الأمم الذين خلوا وعلى العقوبات التي نزلت بهم؛ ليعتبروا بذلك ويرتدعوا عن المعاصي ولا يغتروا بالمهلة ومتعة الدنيا كما اغتر بذلك الذين هلكوا قبلهم، فجعلهم مولانا بفضله معتبرين لا معتبرا بهم، ومتعظين وشاهدين على غيرهم لا مشهودا عليهم، وأظهر سبحانه محاسنهم لمن مضى من الأمم وستر مساوئهم، بل نوه المولى الكريم بقدرهم وقدر نبيهم سيدنا ومولانا محمد عليه الصلاة والسلام تنويها تمنى بسببه كليم الله تعالى موسى صلى الله عليه وسلم أن يكون من هذه الأمة. وبالجملة: فنعم مولانا الكريم جل وعلا ومواهبه الاختصاصية التي خص بها نبينا ومولانا محمدا صلى الله عليه وسلم دنيا وأخرى لا يمكن إحصاؤها، نسأله سبحانه أن يجعلنا من خيار أمته، الفائزين بشرف قربه ومتابعته، المتحصنين من كل محنة وهول وخوف دنيا وأخرى بحرم محبته وولايته.
And the meaning of 'Seal of the Prophets' is that he is the last of them, and with him their number, which is one hundred twenty-four thousand, was completed, so there is no prophet after him, and from its necessary implication: that there is no messenger after him; because 'prophet' is more general than 'messenger' according to the correct view, and negation of the more general necessitates negation of the more specific. So He, glorified, perfected for our Prophet and Master Muhammad, peace and blessing be upon him, all the excellences which were dispersed among the prophets and messengers before him, and He made his tolerant law such that its rulings are connected to the Hereafter, having no abrogator nor replacement, and He informed his honored community of the faults of the nations who passed and of the punishments which descended upon them, so that they might take heed thereby and desist from sins and not be deluded by respite and enjoyment of this world as those who perished before them were deluded by that. So He, our Master, by His grace, made them admonishers, not admonished, and heedful and witnesses over others, not witnessed against. And He, glorified, manifested their virtues to those who came before from the nations and concealed their faults. Rather, the Generous Master exalted their status and the status of their Prophet, our Master and Chief Muhammad, peace and blessing be upon him, an exaltation due to which the Speaker with God, Moses, peace be upon him, wished to be from this community. In summary: the favors of our Generous Master, may He be exalted, and His special gifts with which He distinguished our Prophet and Master Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, in this world and the next cannot be enumerated. We ask Him, glorified, to make us among the best of his community, the ones who succeed in the honor of nearness to Him and following him, who are fortified from every trial, terror, and fear in this world and the next by the sanctity of love for him and his allegiance.
Source pages p. 119

Commentary
This passage defines the term 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' (Seal of the Prophets) and explains its theological implications. The author begins by stating the literal meaning: that the Prophet Muhammad is the last prophet, and with him the total number of prophets (124,000) is completed. He then draws a necessary implication: since prophethood is a more general category than messengerhood, denying that any prophet comes after him also denies that any messenger comes after him. The argument relies on the principle that negating the broader category (prophets) necessarily negates the narrower (messengers). The author then highlights the perfection bestowed upon the Prophet Muhammad: all excellences that were scattered among previous prophets were combined in him. His law (shari'a) is made continuous until the Hereafter, without abrogation or replacement. The community of Muhammad is given a privileged role: they are witnesses over other nations, as indicated in the Quranic verse (2:143). They are admonishers, not admonished; witnesses, not witnessed against. God also showed the virtues of this community to previous nations while concealing their faults. In fact, Prophet Moses (as) wished to be part of this community due to its high status. The passage concludes with a general statement that the favors and special gifts bestowed upon Prophet Muhammad (s) are innumerable, followed by a supplication to be among the best of his community.

Key Terms
خاتم النبيين
الأعم
الأخص

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The term 'Seal of the Prophets' only means there are no more prophets.
Correction: It also implies there are no more messengers, because prophethood is broader than messengerhood.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what does 'Khatam an-Nabiyyin' mean?
A) The first prophet
B) The last prophet
C) The most virtuous prophet
D) The prophet of the end times

Key Takeaway
The Seal of the Prophets means Muhammad is the final prophet, and logically no messenger can come after him.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If you truly believe that Prophet Muhammad is the final messenger, what does that demand of you regarding the sources from which you seek spiritual guidance? Are there any modern ideologies, trends, or personalities that you have given authority over your life that contradict this finality? How will you realign your heart to accept only the Quran and Sunnah as the ultimate guidance?
?
The lesson shows a logical chain that denies any new prophet or messenger. How does this challenge any secret desire you might have for a 'shortcut' or a 'new revelation' that bypasses the difficult parts of following the Prophet's path? What specific step will you take this week to submit more fully to the completeness of his message?
?
Knowing that the number of prophets is closed at 124,000 and Muhammad is the last, what does that imply about how you view people who claim spiritual authority today? Are you willing to reject any practice or teaching that does not come directly from the Prophet's way, even if it feels appealing or convenient? Ask yourself: Is my faith in the finality of prophethood merely an intellectual belief, or does it actively shape my decisions?

The Perfection of the Prophet and the Distinction of His Ummah
Sanusi enumerates the special gifts to Muhammad and the role of his Ummah.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will appreciate the special excellences bestowed on Prophet Muhammad and the privileged role of his community as witnesses over other nations.

Core Idea
All prophetic excellences were gathered in Muhammad, and his Ummah is made a witness over all nations.

Sanusi continues by describing how Allah perfected the Prophet Muhammad by combining all the excellences that were scattered among previous prophets. He then highlights the unique features of Muhammad's Sharia, which remains unaltered until the Hereafter, and the elevated status of his Ummah as admonishers and witnesses. The lesson also includes the poignant mention of Prophet Moses' wish to be part of this community.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage about excellences and Sharia
Read the Arabic and translation focusing on the gathering of excellences and the nature of the Sharia.
Notice the description of the Sharia as tolerant and abiding.
2
Identify the privileges of the Ummah
The passage says the Ummah are admonishers, not admonished, and witnesses over others. Find these phrases.
This reflects Quran 2:143.
3
Reflect on the supplication
The lesson ends with a prayer asking to be among the best of this Ummah. Consider its significance.

What Is Kalam?
beginner

The Definition of Knowledge (Ma'rifa) Required by Shari'a
Sanusi defines ma'rifa and lists its three distinguishing conditions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the precise definition of ma'rifa as a certain judgment conforming to reality based on necessity or proof, and why it excludes conjecture, doubt, illusion, compound ignorance, and blind following.

Core Idea
True knowledge is certain, matches reality, and is proven — nothing less counts.

This passage lays the foundation for all Islamic theology by defining what counts as genuine knowledge. Sanusi sets a high bar: not every correct belief qualifies. Only a judgment that is certain, corresponds to reality, and is derived from direct or demonstrative evidence merits the name 'ma'rifa'. Understanding this definition is crucial before studying the attributes of Allah and the prophets.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and its translation
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the concise, definitional style.
2
Identify the three conditions for ma'rifa
The definition has three conditions: certainty (jazm), conformity (muṭābiq), and source (ḍarūra or burhān). Find each in the text.
These three conditions are the core of the definition.
3
Examine what each condition excludes
For each condition, the author specifies what it excludes: certainty excludes conjecture, doubt, and illusion; conformity excludes compound ignorance; source excludes blind following.
These exclusions clarify the boundaries of true knowledge.
4
Reflect on the significance for Islamic creed
Ask yourself why the shari'a requires such a rigorous standard. What does this say about the seriousness of theological knowledge?
This connects to the importance of learning aqeedah with evidence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
اعلم : أَنَّهُ يجب على كلِّ مُكلَّفٍ أَنْ يعرف ما يجب حق مولانا جل وعزَّ ، وما يستحيلُ ، وما يجوز ، وكذا يجب عليهِ أنْ يعرف مثلَ ذلكَ في حقِّ الرُّسُلِ عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام .
[ حقيقةُ المعرفة المطلوبة شرعاً ]
حقيقة المعرفةِ الحادثة : الجزم المطابق عن ضرورة أو برهان . فقولنا : ( الجزم ) احترازُ مِنَ الظَّنِّ ؛ وهو الاحتمال الراجح . وعن الشكّ ؛ وهو الاحتمال المساوي ، وعن الوهم ؛ وهو الاحتمال المرجوح . وقولنا : ( المطابقُ ) احترازُ مِنَ الجهل المركَبِ ؛ فإنَّهُ جزمٌ غيرُ مطابق لِمَا في نفْسِ الأمرِ ؛ كجزمٍ الفلاسفة بقِدَم الأفلاكِ ، وجزمِ اليهود والنصارى بسلامتِهِم مِنَ الخلودِ في النارِ يومَ القيامةِ . وقولنا : ( عن ضرورة أو برهان ) احتراز من جزمِ المقلد المطابقِ ؛ فإنَّهُ ليس بمعرفةٍ وإِنْ كانَ جزماً مطابقاً لِمَا في نفس الأمرِ ، ويُسمَّى في الاصطلاح : اعتقاداً
Know: It is obligatory upon every legally responsible person to know what is obligatory concerning our Master, Mighty and Majestic, and what is impossible, and what is permissible; and likewise it is obligatory upon him to know the like of that concerning the messengers, peace be upon them. [The reality of the knowledge required by the Law] The reality of originated knowledge (al-ma'rifa al-ḥāditha) is: certain judgment (al-jazm) conforming to reality (al-muṭābiq) arising from necessity (ḍarūra) or proof (burhān). Our saying: 'certain judgment' excludes conjecture (al-ẓann), which is a preponderant probability; and doubt (al-shakk), which is an equal probability; and illusion (al-wahm), which is a weak probability. And our saying: 'conforming to reality' excludes compound ignorance (al-jahl al-murakkab), for it is a judgment that does not conform to the actual state of affairs; like the philosophers' judgment of the eternity of the celestial spheres, and the Jews' and Christians' judgment of their safety from eternal punishment in the Fire on the Day of Judgment. And our saying: 'arising from necessity or proof' excludes the judgment of the blind follower (al-muqallid) that conforms to reality, for it is not knowledge, even if it is a correct judgment, and it is called in terminology: belief (iʿtiqād).
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage presents a definition of 'knowledge' (ma'rifa) as required by the Law (sharʿ). It begins by stating the obligation for every morally responsible person to know what is obligatory, impossible, and permissible concerning Allah and the messengers—a classification that will be detailed later. Then it turns to the definition of knowledge itself.

The definition: 'Originated knowledge (al-ma'rifa al-ḥāditha, i.e., human acquired knowledge) is a certain judgment conforming to reality arising from necessity or proof.' The author then explains each qualifier:

1. 'Certain judgment' (al-jazm): This excludes conjecture (ẓann, a preponderant but not certain probability), doubt (shakk, equal probability), and illusion (wahm, weak probability). Only a firm, decisive judgment qualifies as knowledge.

2. 'Conforming to reality' (al-muṭābiq): This excludes compound ignorance (jahl murakkab), where a person holds a firm judgment that does not match how things really are, like the philosophers' false certainty about the eternity of the heavens or the Jews' and Christians' false assurance of escaping hellfire.

3. 'Arising from necessity or proof' (ʿan ḍarūra aw burhān): This excludes the blind follower's (muqallid) judgment, even if it happens to be correct, because it is not based on a direct or demonstrative foundation. Such a judgment is called 'belief' (iʿtiqād) in technical terms, not true knowledge.

The passage thus carefully distinguishes genuine knowledge from mere opinion, ignorance, and uncritical acceptance, establishing a rigorous standard for the foundation of Islamic creed.

Key Terms
معرفة
جزم
ظن
شك
وهم
جهل مركب
تقليد
اعتقاد
ضرورة
برهان

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any correct belief is considered knowledge.
Correction: Sanusi shows that even a correct belief without proper evidence is only iʿtiqād, not ma'rifa.

Checkpoint
According to the definition, what is excluded by the condition 'conforming to reality' (al-muṭābiq)?
A) Conjecture (ẓann)
B) Compound ignorance (jahl murakkab)
C) Blind following (taqlīd)
D) Doubt (shakk)

Key Takeaway
True knowledge (ma'rifa) is a certain, correct judgment based on either direct evidence or demonstrative proof.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you think about the core beliefs of your faith, do you have the kind of certainty (jazm) Sanusi describes, or have you been relying on what you've been taught without personal verification? What specific belief will you examine today to move from taqlid to true ma'rifa?
?
Consider an area where you feel confident but your confidence might be jahl murakkab (like the philosophers in the text). How can you actively seek proof (burhan) to ensure your certainty conforms to reality?
?
Sanusi warns that even a correct belief without proof is not ma'rifa. If you were to die tonight, would the knowledge you have about Allah be enough to satisfy the obligation? Or are there gaps you've never examined? Commit to one step to address that gap this week.

What Is Kalam?
beginner
