The Obligatory Knowledge of Allah and the Messengers
Sanusi enumerates the three rational judgments that are obligatory to know.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the three rational judgments—obligatory, impossible, and permissible—that every legally responsible Muslim must know concerning Allah and the prophets.

Core Idea
Knowing what is necessary, impossible, and permissible about Allah is the foundation of theology.

Sanusi begins by stating the most basic obligation of every mukallaf: to know the three rational judgments about Allah and the prophets. This sets the scope of kalam—theological inquiry is not arbitrary but grounded in what reason demands.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage
Read the short passage once to get the overall meaning. Then read the translation to clarify any terms.
The Arabic is concise; the translation will help.
2
Identify the three judgments
The passage mentions three categories: what is obligatory (wajib), impossible (mustahil), and permissible (ja'iz) for Allah and the prophets. Notice that the same categories apply to both.
These three are the backbone of all kalam discussions.
3
Reflect on the obligation
Why is knowing these judgments obligatory? Because it defines the boundary of correct belief about Allah and His messengers. Without this, one cannot have sound theology.

Source Text

وعن ما تجب معرفته على كل مكلف في حق الله تعالى اعلم : أَنَّهُ يجب على كلِّ مُكلَّفٍ أَنْ يعرف ما يجب حق مولانا جل وعزَّ ، وما يستحيلُ ، وما يجوز ، وكذا يجب عليهِ أنْ يعرف مثلَ ذلكَ في حقِّ الرُّسُلِ عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام . [ حقيقةُ المعرفة المطلوبة شرعاً ] حقيقة المعرفةِ الحادثة : الجزم المطابق عن ضرورة أو برهان . فقولنا : ( الجزم ) احترازُ مِنَ الظَّنِّ ؛ وهو الاحتمال الراجح ، وعن الشكّ؛ وهو الاحتمال المساوي ، وعن الوهم ؛ وهو الاحتمال المرجوح . وقولنا : ( المطابقُ ( احترازُ مِنَ الجهل المركَبِ ؛ فإنَّهُ جزمٌ غيرُ مطابق لِمَا في نفْسِ الأمرِ ؛ كجزمٍ الفلاسفة بقِدَم الأفلاكِ ، وجزمِ اليهود والنصارى بسلامتِهِم مِنَ الخلودِ في النارِ يومَ القيامةِ . وقولنا : ( عن ضرورة أو برهان ( احتراز من جزمِ المقلد المطابقِ ؛ فإنَّهُ ليس بمعرفةٍ وإِنْ كانَ جزماً مطابقاً لِمَا في نفس الأمرِ ، ويُسمَّى في الاصطلاح : اعتقاداً . ومعنى ( الضرورة ) : إلجاء المولى سبحانه النفس لأن تجزم بأمرٍ جزماً مطابقاً بلا تأمل ، بحيثُ لو حاولت أن تدفع عن نفسها ذلكَ الجزم بتشكيك أو نحوه .. لم تقدر . ومثالُهُ : جزمنا بوجودِ أنفسِنا ، وبأنَّ الواحد مثلاً نصفُ الاثنينِ ونحو ذلك ممَّا هو كثير . ومعنى ( البرهان ( : الدليلُ المركَّبُ مِنْ مُقدِّماتٍ قطعيَّةٍ ضروريَّةٍ في نفسها ، أو منتهية في الاستدلال عليها إلى علوم ضروريَّة . ومثال ذلك : إذا قيلَ لنا : فلان اشترى هذه السلعة بربع عُشْرِ أربعين درهماً ؛ فجزمُنا بأنَّهُ اشتراها بدرهم واحدٍ ليس بضروري لنا ندركُه بلا تأمل (۱) في (و) : ( لأنا لا ندركه بلا تأمل ) . بل لا يحصلُ لنا الجزم العرفاني بذلكَ مِنْ غيرِ تقليد لأحدٍ حتى نختبر لأنفسنا فنقول : أقل عددٍ لهُ رُبع : أربعة ، وربعها : واحدٌ ، وهذهِ مُقدّمةٌ واحدةٌ ضرورية لا تفتقر إلى تأمل أعني : كونَ الواحدِ رُبِّعَ الأربعة ، لكن لا تكفينا هذهِ في معرفة ما اشترى به الإنسان تلك السلعة حتى نعرف معرفةً قطعيَّةً أن الأربعةَ عُشْرُ الأربعينَ ، وهذه المعرفة بهذه المقدمة ليسَتْ ضروريةً ، إلا أنَّها تنتهي إلى الضرورةِ ؛ فإِنَّكَ إِذا قسَّمتَ أربعينَ على عشرة أنصباءً متساوية .. خرج في كلِّ نصيب أربعة ، وكذا لو عددتَ في أصابعك أربعةً ثم أربعة وتجمعُ إلى أن تفرغ مِنْ أصابعك العشرة .
And regarding what is obligatory to know for every mukallaf concerning the rights of Allah the Exalted: Know that it is obligatory upon every mukallaf to know what is obligatory for our Master, Glorified and Exalted, and what is impossible, and what is permissible, and likewise it is obligatory upon him to know the like of that concerning the rights of the messengers, peace be upon them. [The reality of the knowledge required by the Law] The reality of originated knowledge is: decisive belief conforming [to reality] from necessity or proof. Our saying: (decisive belief) excludes conjecture, which is a preponderant probability, and doubt, which is an equal probability, and illusion, which is a weak probability. And our saying: (conforming) excludes compound ignorance; for it is a decisive belief not conforming to what is in the actual matter, like the philosophers' decisive belief in the eternity of the celestial spheres, and the Jews' and Christians' decisive belief in their safety from eternal punishment on the Day of Judgment. And our saying: (from necessity or proof) excludes the decisive belief of the imitator that is conforming; for it is not knowledge, even if it is a decisive belief conforming to what is in the actual matter, and it is called in terminology: belief. The meaning of (necessity): the Subduer, Exalted, compelling the soul to decide a matter with a conforming decisive belief without reflection, such that if it tried to repel that decisive belief from itself by doubt or the like, it could not. Its example: our decisive belief in the existence of ourselves, and that one, for instance, is half of two, and the like of that which is abundant. And the meaning of (proof): a composite proof from certain premises that are necessary in themselves, or that terminate in their inference to necessary sciences. And an example of that: if it is said to us: So-and-so bought this commodity for a quarter of a tenth of forty dirhams; then our decisive belief that he bought it for one dirham is not necessary for us, we perceive it without reflection (1) in (w): (because we do not perceive it without reflection). Rather, the customary decisive belief does not occur to us from that without imitation of anyone until we test for ourselves and say: the smallest number that has a quarter is four, and its quarter is one, and this is one necessary premise not requiring reflection, namely that one is a quarter of four, but this alone is not sufficient for us to know with what price that person bought the commodity until we know with certain knowledge that four is a tenth of forty, and this knowledge from this premise is not necessary, but it terminates in necessity; for if you divide forty into ten equal shares, each share comes out as four, and likewise if you count on your fingers four then four and add until you finish your ten fingers.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage defines what every legally responsible person (mukallaf) must know about Allah and the prophets, and then provides a precise definition of true knowledge (ma'rifa). The author begins by stating the obligation to know the three rational judgments (obligatory, impossible, permissible) concerning Allah and likewise concerning the messengers. He then defines 'originated knowledge' (al-ma'rifa al-haditha) as 'decisive belief conforming [to reality] from necessity or proof'. He unpacks each condition: First, 'decisive belief' (jazzm) excludes conjecture (zann), doubt (shakk), and illusion (wahm). Second, 'conforming' (mutabiq) excludes compound ignorance (al-jahl al-murakkab), which is a firm but false belief, giving examples from philosophers and previous religions. Third, the source must be 'necessity or proof' (darura or burhan), which excludes the decisive belief of the imitator (muqallid) even if it happens to be true; such a belief is merely called 'belief' (i'tiqad) in terminology, not knowledge. Then the author explains each source: 'necessity' is when Allah compels the soul to a certain belief without reflection, like self-awareness or basic mathematical truths. 'Proof' is a composite argument from certain premises that are either self-evident or ultimately reducible to necessary knowledge. He illustrates proof with a worked example: determining the price of a commodity requires combining necessary premises (e.g., a quarter of four is one) with non-necessary premises that nevertheless lead to necessity through repeated reasoning (e.g., four is a tenth of forty). The example shows how demonstration builds certainty from foundational necessary truths.

Key Terms
معرفة (knowledge)
مكلف (legally responsible)
واجب (obligatory)
مستحيل (impossible)
جائز (permissible)
رسل (messengers)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what three categories of judgment must a mukallaf know about Allah?
A) Wajib, mustahil, ja'iz
B) Wajib, sunnah, makruh
C) Tawhid, nubuwwa, ma'ad
D) Iman, islam, ihsan

Key Takeaway
Every mukallaf must know the three rational judgments (obligatory, impossible, permissible) regarding Allah and the prophets.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you actually know, with certainty, what is wajib, mustahil, and ja'iz regarding Allah? If not, are you content to remain in willful ignorance of the most fundamental obligation of faith?
?
The source says this knowledge is obligatory upon every mukallaf—including you. If you were to die tonight, could you stand before Allah and say you fulfilled this duty? What concrete step will you take today to close that gap?
?
You have likely learned many things about Islam that are not obligatory. But this—this is obligatory. Have you prioritized your learning correctly? Will you rearrange your studies to give this its due weight?

What Is Kalam?
intermediate
8 min
The Definition of True Knowledge and Its Conditions
Sanusi defines true knowledge and explains each condition with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the precise definition of true knowledge (ma'rifa) as decisive belief conforming to reality from necessity or proof, and appreciate how each condition excludes false claimants.

Core Idea
True knowledge requires certainty, truth, and a valid source—necessity or demonstration.

After establishing what must be known, Sanusi now defines what counts as true knowledge itself. This rigorous definition ensures that not every firm belief qualifies—only that which is certain, matches reality, and derives from necessity or proof. He then unpacks each term and gives examples.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition segment
Read the first sentence: 'al-jazzm al-mutabiq an darura aw burhan'. Then read the translation.
This is the core of the lesson.
2
Understand 'decisive belief' (jazzm)
Jazzm excludes zann (conjecture), shakk (doubt), and wahm (illusion). Only certainty counts as knowledge.
3
Understand 'conforming' (mutabiq)
Mutabiq excludes compound ignorance—a firm belief that is false. Example: the philosophers' belief in the eternity of the heavens.
Truth matters, not just confidence.
4
Understand 'from necessity or proof'
This excludes imitation (taqlid) even if the belief is true. Only necessary or demonstrated certainty is knowledge.
Necessity is involuntary; proof is reasoning from certain premises.
5
Examine the examples of necessity and proof
Necessity: self-awareness, basic arithmetic (one is half of two). Proof: the example of the price calculation shows how composite reasoning leads to certainty.
Notice that proof ultimately rests on necessary premises.

وعن ما تجب معرفته على كل مكلف في حق الله تعالى اعلم : أَنَّهُ يجب على كلِّ مُكلَّفٍ أَنْ يعرف ما يجب حق مولانا جل وعزَّ ، وما يستحيلُ ، وما يجوز ، وكذا يجب عليهِ أنْ يعرف مثلَ ذلكَ في حقِّ الرُّسُلِ عليهم الصلاةُ والسلام . [ حقيقةُ المعرفة المطلوبة شرعاً ] حقيقة المعرفةِ الحادثة : الجزم المطابق عن ضرورة أو برهان . فقولنا : ( الجزم ) احترازُ مِنَ الظَّنِّ ؛ وهو الاحتمال الراجح ، وعن الشكّ؛ وهو الاحتمال المساوي ، وعن الوهم ؛ وهو الاحتمال المرجوح . وقولنا : ( المطابقُ ( احترازُ مِنَ الجهل المركَبِ ؛ فإنَّهُ جزمٌ غيرُ مطابق لِمَا في نفْسِ الأمرِ ؛ كجزمٍ الفلاسفة بقِدَم الأفلاكِ ، وجزمِ اليهود والنصارى بسلامتِهِم مِنَ الخلودِ في النارِ يومَ القيامةِ . وقولنا : ( عن ضرورة أو برهان ( احتراز من جزمِ المقلد المطابقِ ؛ فإنَّهُ ليس بمعرفةٍ وإِنْ كانَ جزماً مطابقاً لِمَا في نفس الأمرِ ، ويُسمَّى في الاصطلاح : اعتقاداً . ومعنى ( الضرورة ) : إلجاء المولى سبحانه النفس لأن تجزم بأمرٍ جزماً مطابقاً بلا تأمل ، بحيثُ لو حاولت أن تدفع عن نفسها ذلكَ الجزم بتشكيك أو نحوه .. لم تقدر . ومثالُهُ : جزمنا بوجودِ أنفسِنا ، وبأنَّ الواحد مثلاً نصفُ الاثنينِ ونحو ذلك ممَّا هو كثير . ومعنى ( البرهان ( : الدليلُ المركَّبُ مِنْ مُقدِّماتٍ قطعيَّةٍ ضروريَّةٍ في نفسها ، أو منتهية في الاستدلال عليها إلى علوم ضروريَّة . ومثال ذلك : إذا قيلَ لنا : فلان اشترى هذه السلعة بربع عُشْرِ أربعين درهماً ؛ فجزمُنا بأنَّهُ اشتراها بدرهم واحدٍ ليس بضروري لنا ندركُه بلا تأمل (۱) في (و) : ( لأنا لا ندركه بلا تأمل ) . بل لا يحصلُ لنا الجزم العرفاني بذلكَ مِنْ غيرِ تقليد لأحدٍ حتى نختبر لأنفسنا فنقول : أقل عددٍ لهُ رُبع : أربعة ، وربعها : واحدٌ ، وهذهِ مُقدّمةٌ واحدةٌ ضرورية لا تفتقر إلى تأمل أعني : كونَ الواحدِ رُبِّعَ الأربعة ، لكن لا تكفينا هذهِ في معرفة ما اشترى به الإنسان تلك السلعة حتى نعرف معرفةً قطعيَّةً أن الأربعةَ عُشْرُ الأربعينَ ، وهذه المعرفة بهذه المقدمة ليسَتْ ضروريةً ، إلا أنَّها تنتهي إلى الضرورةِ ؛ فإِنَّكَ إِذا قسَّمتَ أربعينَ على عشرة أنصباءً متساوية .. خرج في كلِّ نصيب أربعة ، وكذا لو عددتَ في أصابعك أربعةً ثم أربعة وتجمعُ إلى أن تفرغ مِنْ أصابعك العشرة .
And regarding what is obligatory to know for every mukallaf concerning the rights of Allah the Exalted: Know that it is obligatory upon every mukallaf to know what is obligatory for our Master, Glorified and Exalted, and what is impossible, and what is permissible, and likewise it is obligatory upon him to know the like of that concerning the rights of the messengers, peace be upon them. [The reality of the knowledge required by the Law] The reality of originated knowledge is: decisive belief conforming [to reality] from necessity or proof. Our saying: (decisive belief) excludes conjecture, which is a preponderant probability, and doubt, which is an equal probability, and illusion, which is a weak probability. And our saying: (conforming) excludes compound ignorance; for it is a decisive belief not conforming to what is in the actual matter, like the philosophers' decisive belief in the eternity of the celestial spheres, and the Jews' and Christians' decisive belief in their safety from eternal punishment on the Day of Judgment. And our saying: (from necessity or proof) excludes the decisive belief of the imitator that is conforming; for it is not knowledge, even if it is a decisive belief conforming to what is in the actual matter, and it is called in terminology: belief. The meaning of (necessity): the Subduer, Exalted, compelling the soul to decide a matter with a conforming decisive belief without reflection, such that if it tried to repel that decisive belief from itself by doubt or the like, it could not. Its example: our decisive belief in the existence of ourselves, and that one, for instance, is half of two, and the like of that which is abundant. And the meaning of (proof): a composite proof from certain premises that are necessary in themselves, or that terminate in their inference to necessary sciences. And an example of that: if it is said to us: So-and-so bought this commodity for a quarter of a tenth of forty dirhams; then our decisive belief that he bought it for one dirham is not necessary for us, we perceive it without reflection (1) in (w): (because we do not perceive it without reflection). Rather, the customary decisive belief does not occur to us from that without imitation of anyone until we test for ourselves and say: the smallest number that has a quarter is four, and its quarter is one, and this is one necessary premise not requiring reflection, namely that one is a quarter of four, but this alone is not sufficient for us to know with what price that person bought the commodity until we know with certain knowledge that four is a tenth of forty, and this knowledge from this premise is not necessary, but it terminates in necessity; for if you divide forty into ten equal shares, each share comes out as four, and likewise if you count on your fingers four then four and add until you finish your ten fingers.
Source pages p. 126

Commentary
This passage defines what every legally responsible person (mukallaf) must know about Allah and the prophets, and then provides a precise definition of true knowledge (ma'rifa). The author begins by stating the obligation to know the three rational judgments (obligatory, impossible, permissible) concerning Allah and likewise concerning the messengers. He then defines 'originated knowledge' (al-ma'rifa al-haditha) as 'decisive belief conforming [to reality] from necessity or proof'. He unpacks each condition: First, 'decisive belief' (jazzm) excludes conjecture (zann), doubt (shakk), and illusion (wahm). Second, 'conforming' (mutabiq) excludes compound ignorance (al-jahl al-murakkab), which is a firm but false belief, giving examples from philosophers and previous religions. Third, the source must be 'necessity or proof' (darura or burhan), which excludes the decisive belief of the imitator (muqallid) even if it happens to be true; such a belief is merely called 'belief' (i'tiqad) in terminology, not knowledge. Then the author explains each source: 'necessity' is when Allah compels the soul to a certain belief without reflection, like self-awareness or basic mathematical truths. 'Proof' is a composite argument from certain premises that are either self-evident or ultimately reducible to necessary knowledge. He illustrates proof with a worked example: determining the price of a commodity requires combining necessary premises (e.g., a quarter of four is one) with non-necessary premises that nevertheless lead to necessity through repeated reasoning (e.g., four is a tenth of forty). The example shows how demonstration builds certainty from foundational necessary truths.

ضرورة (necessity)
برهان (proof)
تقليد (imitation)

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that any firm belief based on authority counts as knowledge.
Correction: The passage clarifies that even true imitation (taqlid) is not true knowledge unless it is from necessity or proof. Such belief is merely called 'i'tiqad' (belief), not ma'rifa.

Checkpoint
What does the term 'mutabiq' (conforming) in Sanusi's definition exclude?
A) Conjecture and doubt
B) Compound ignorance
C) Imitation
D) Illusion

Key Takeaway
True knowledge is a certain, true belief derived from necessity or proof—imitation, doubt, and false certainty do not qualify.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of one belief you hold about your faith that you have never critically examined. Are you willing to set aside your comfort and test it against the conditions of knowledge in this lesson? What will you do today to seek proof?
?
The passage says that even a true belief obtained from imitation is not knowledge. Where in your religious practice are you simply following what you were taught without personal certainty? Will you remain content with that, or will you pursue certainty even if it is uncomfortable?
?
If you were asked to prove the existence of God using only necessary premises, could you do it? What step will you take this week to learn how to build such a proof?

What Is Kalam?

Types of Mental Perception: Dhann, Wahm, Shakk, and Jahl Murakkab
Sanusi defines jazzm, dhann, wahm, shakk, and jahl murakkab with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definitions of dhann, wahm, shakk, and jahl murakkab, and why they fall short of certain knowledge in kalam.

Core Idea
Not all strong beliefs are knowledge; some are compound ignorance.

Sanusi introduces the categories of mental perception that fall short of certain knowledge. Before defining knowledge (ma'rifa), he distinguishes between conjecture, misgiving, doubt, and the specific error of compound ignorance. This passage establishes the boundaries of what counts as valid knowledge in kalam.

Guided Path
1
Read and Translate
Read the Arabic passage once without analysing. Then read the English translation to grasp the overall flow.
Pay attention to the technical terms used by Sanusi.
2
Identify Each Term
Go through the passage and identify the definitions of dhann, wahm, and shakk. Notice how they are based on the balance of probability.
Dhann is for the more likely, wahm for the less likely, shakk for equal probability.
3
Analyze the Example
Examine the example of jahl murakkab: someone believes a quarter of a tenth of forty is two. Understand why this is compound ignorance and how it arises from specious argument or blind imitation.
The person is ignorant and ignorant of their ignorance.
4
Connect to Kalam
Reflect on why these distinctions are important for kalam: only certain knowledge (ma'rifa) qualifies as valid, and these categories help identify what does not.
This sets the stage for the definition of knowledge to come.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
الراجح ظنّاً ، وإدراككُ المرجوح وَهْماً ولو تساوى عندَكَ احتمال صدقِهِ وكذبِهِ .. لكانَ إدراكُكَ لكُلٌّ مِنَ الاحتمالين المتساويين شكاً . ولو جزمت على سبيل الغلط ؛ إمَّا لوقوعِكَ في شبهةٍ ، أو لتقليدِكَ مَنْ وقع فيها ممَّنْ تثقُ بهِ في زعمِكَ بأَنَّ رُبُعَ عُشْرِ الأربعينَ اثنانِ لا واحدٌ . . لكان جزمُكَ هذا جهلاً مُركَباً ؛ لأنَّكَ جهلت ما في نفس الأمر ، وجهلت أنَّكَ جاهل بهِ ، ويُسمى أيضاً هذا الجزم في الاصطلاح : اعتقاداً فاسداً .
The more likely (is) dhann (conjecture), and your perception of the less likely is wahm (misgiving). If the probability of its truth and falsehood are equal in your view, then your perception of each of the two equal probabilities would be shakk (doubt). And if you hold a judgment erroneously, either because you fell into a specious argument or because you imitated someone who fell into it, whom you trust in your claim, that a quarter of a tenth of forty is two, not one—then this judgment of yours would be jahl murakkab (compound ignorance); because you are ignorant of what is actually the case, and ignorant that you are ignorant of it. This judgment is also termed in technical usage: fasid i'tiqad (defective belief).
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage defines the types of mental perception that are not certain knowledge. The author begins by describing dhann (conjecture) as the perception of the more likely of two possibilities, and wahm (misgiving) as the perception of the less likely. Then he defines shakk (doubt) as the state when both possibilities are equally likely in one's mind. Next, he gives an example of erroneous judgment (jahl murakkab): someone falsely believes that a quarter of a tenth of forty is two, not one, either due to a specious argument or blind imitation of a trusted person. This person is ignorant of the truth and also ignorant of his own ignorance, which is 'compound ignorance' (jahl murakkab). In technical kalam terminology, this state is called 'fasid i'tiqad' (defective belief). The passage sets up the standard for what counts as valid knowledge (ma'rifa) later.

Key Terms
الظن
الوهم
الشك
الجهل المركب
الاعتقاد الفاسد

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any strong belief qualifies as knowledge.
Correction: A strong but false belief is actually jahl murakkab (compound ignorance), not knowledge, because it is based on error and lacks true certainty.

Checkpoint
What is the term for the perception of two equal probabilities?
A) Dhann
B) Wahm
C) Shakk
D) Jahl murakkab

Key Takeaway
Valid knowledge requires certainty in truth, not just strong conviction; what appears certain may be compound ignorance.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Think of a belief you hold strongly but have never critically examined. Could it be that your certainty is actually compound ignorance? What would it take for you to test that belief against the source of truth?
?
The passage warns that imitating a trusted person can lead to jahl murakkab. Who in your life do you trust most about religious matters, and are you prepared to verify their claims? How will you start that verification today?
?
You often act on dhann (conjecture) as if it were certainty. Where in your daily life are you treating a mere probability as a sure thing, and what harm might that cause your faith or character? Commit to identifying one such area by the end of this day.

Definition of Rational Judgment and Its Distinction from Customary and Legal Judgment
Sanusi defines rational judgment and distinguishes it from customary and legal judgment with examples.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand what rational judgment is and how it differs from customary and legal judgments.

Core Idea
Rational judgment relies solely on the intellect, not on habit or revelation.

Before we dive into theological arguments, we must first understand the kind of judgment they rely on. Sanusi begins by clarifying what makes a judgment 'rational' as opposed to judgments based on custom or divine law. This distinction is crucial because it defines the scope of intellectual reasoning in Islamic theology.

Guided Path
1
Read the definition carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without stopping. Then read the translation. Notice the precise wording 'without depending on repetition or the placing of a placer'.
The definition is compact; every word matters.
2
Identify the two exclusions
The author excludes two types: customary judgment (based on repetition) and legal judgment (based on revelation). Find where each exclusion is stated.
3
Study the examples
For each type, Sanusi gives concrete examples. Understand why eating food warming the body is customary, and why bartering rules are legal. Then consider the examples of rational judgment itself (affirmation and negation).
These examples clarify the abstract definition.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
أما معنى الحكم العقلي : فهو إثباتُ أمرٍ أو نفيه مِنْ غيرِ توقف على تكرر ولا وضعِ واضع (۱) فقولُنا : ( مِنْ غير توقُّف على تكرر ) احتراز من الحكم العادي أي : الذي عُرِفَ مِنَ العادةِ فإِنَّ الإثبات فيه والنفيَ إِنَّما عُرِفا وحُكِمَ بهما بواسطة التكرُّرِ والتجربة كقولنا : أَكُلُ هذا الطعامِ يُسخّنُ البدنَ وأَكْلُ هذا لا يُسخّنُهُ وقولنا : ( ولا وضع واضع ) احتراز مِنَ الحكم الشرعي أي : الذي عُرِفَ مِنَ الشرع فإنَّ الإثبات أيضاً فيه والنفيَ إِنَّما عُرِفا وحُكِمَ بهما بواسطة وضع الشرع لذلك كقولنا : البر بالتمر يجوزُ فيهِ التفاضل والبُرُّ بالبر لا يجوز فيه التفاضل . ومثال الإثباتِ في الحكم العقلي : قولنا مثلاً : كلُّ موجود فهو إما قديم أو حادث فالحكم بإثباتِ أحد الأمرين لكل موجود يعرفه العقل بلا واسطة تكرُّرٍ وتجربة ولا واسطة تعليمِ شرع ووضعه وإنَّما حصلَ بمحض خلْقِ اللهِ تعالى له في القلبِ عارياً عن القيدين (1) ومثالُ النفْيِ : قولنا مثلاً : كل موجود لا يخلو عنِ القِدَمِ والحدوث معاً . (1) قوله : ( إثبات أمر ( يعني : لأمرٍ آخر كإثبات وجوب الوجود له تعالى في قولك : الله واجب الوجود وقوله : ( أو نفيه ) يعني : عن أمر آخر كنفي القدم عن العالم في قولك : ( العالم ليس بقديم ) وسيأتي تمثيل الإمام .
As for the meaning of rational judgment: it is affirming or negating something without depending on repetition or the placing of a placer (1). Our saying: 'without depending on repetition' is a restriction to exclude customary judgment, i.e., that which is known from custom; for affirmation and negation in it are known and judged by means of repetition and experience, as our saying: 'Eating this food warms the body, and eating this does not warm it.' And our saying: 'nor the placing of a placer' is a restriction to exclude legal judgment, i.e., that which is known from law; for affirmation and negation in it are known and judged by means of the law's placing of that, as our saying: 'Bartering wheat for dates, disparity is permissible, and bartering wheat for wheat, disparity is not permissible.' And an example of affirmation in rational judgment is our saying: 'Every existent is either eternal or originated'; the judgment affirming one of the two things for every existent is known by the intellect without means of repetition and experience nor means of revealed instruction and its placement; rather it occurs solely by Allah's creation of it in the heart, free from the two restrictions (1). And an example of negation is our saying: 'Every existent is not free from both eternity and origination together.' (1) His saying: 'affirming something' means for another thing, like affirming the necessity of existence for Him, may He be exalted, in your saying: 'God is necessary of existence'; and his saying: 'or negating it' means from another thing, like negating eternity from the world in your saying: 'The world is not eternal'; and the author's example will come.
Source pages p. 133

Commentary
This passage defines rational judgment and distinguishes it from two other types: customary judgment (al-hukm al-'adi) and legal judgment (al-hukm al-shar'i). The definition states that rational judgment is affirming or negating something without dependence on repetition (which characterizes customary judgments based on experience) or on the placing of a placer (which characterizes legal judgments based on revelation). The author gives examples for each exclusion: for customary, eating food warms the body; for legal, the permissibility of disparity in bartering wheat for dates but not wheat for wheat. Then he gives examples of affirmation and negation within rational judgment itself: affirming that every existent is either eternal or originated, and negating that any existent is free from both eternity and origination. These rational judgments are created by Allah directly in the heart without any external means. The footnote explains the terms 'affirming something' and 'negating it' with further examples.

Key Terms
الحكم العقلي
الحكم العادي
الحكم الشرعي
تكرر
وضع واضع
إثبات
نفي

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think rational judgment includes all knowledge derived from reason alone, but it specifically excludes knowledge from custom and revelation.
Correction: Rational judgment is pure intellectual affirmation or negation, independent of habit and revelation, as defined by Sanusi.

Checkpoint
What is the defining characteristic that distinguishes rational judgment from customary judgment?
A) It depends on repetition.
B) It depends on the placing of a placer.
C) It does not depend on repetition or the placing of a placer.
D) It is based on revelation.

Key Takeaway
Rational judgment is pure intellectual affirmation or negation, independent of habit and revelation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
How much of your certainty about God and the afterlife comes from habit—what you were raised with—rather than from your own intellect? Would you dare to examine your beliefs with pure reason, even if it unsettles you?
?
This lesson separates rational judgment from what is simply customary or legally commanded. When you obey a religious ruling, do you ever confuse it with what you think is rationally necessary? How can you check that your obedience is not just blind custom?
?
Imagine you had no revelation and no repeated experience—only your intellect. Which of your current beliefs would survive? That thought can be terrifying, but it forces you to ask: what do you actually know to be true by reason alone?

Definition of Rational Judgment and Its Types
Sanusi defines rational judgment and classifies it into necessary and theoretical.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Islamic theology defines rational judgment and distinguishes between necessary and theoretical judgments.

Core Idea
Rational judgment is directly created by Allah, either with or without reflection.

Sanusi begins by establishing the foundational concept of rational judgment in kalam. He explains that it is a direct mental judgment without empirical or revelational intermediaries, and it is created by Allah in the heart. He then distinguishes two types: necessary (without reflection) and theoretical (with reflection). This classification is crucial for understanding how theological knowledge is acquired.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis. Then read the translation. Notice the author's concise style and the key example of 'every existent is either eternal or originated.'
2
Identify the two types of rational judgment
The passage explicitly distinguishes between judgments that arise necessarily without reflection and those that come after reflection. Underline the key terms 'ضروري' and 'نظري'.
Necessary judgments are immediate; theoretical ones require thought.
3
Reflect on the role of divine creation
Sanusi emphasizes that all rational judgments are created directly by Allah. Consider why this point is important in Islamic theology—it removes any suggestion that human reason operates independently of Allah's will.
This counters the idea that rational knowledge can be acquired solely by human effort.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
إما قديم ، أو حادث ، فالحكم بإثباتِ أحد الأمرين لكل موجود يعرفه العقل بلا واسطة تكرُّرٍ وتجربة ، ولا واسطة تعليمِ شرع ووضعه ، وإنَّما حصلَ بمحض خلْقِ اللهِ تعالى له في القلبِ عارياً عن القيدين. ومثالُ النفْيِ : قولنا مثلاً : كلُّ موجود لا يخلو عنِ القِدَمِ والحدوث معاً . ثم هذا الحكم العقلي وإنْ عَرِيَ عن القيدين فقد أجرى تبارك وتعالى العادة بأن يخلق بعض أنواعِهِ في القلبِ ضرورياً بلا تأمل ويخلق بعض أنواعِهِ عندَ النظرِ والتأمل ، والعلوم الحادثةُ كلُّها وإنْ كانَتْ حاصلة بمحض خلْقِ اللهِ تعالى يصح أن يخلقها في القلوب ابتداءً بلا واسطة تجربة ، ولا بعْثِ رسول ، ولا نظر ولا فكرة ، وقد أجرى سبحانه بمحضِ اختيارِهِ العادة في خلقها على هذا التقسيم.
Either eternal or originated, so the judgment of affirming one of the two matters for every existent which the mind knows without the intermediary of repetition and experience, nor the intermediary of teaching of law and its establishment; rather it occurred by the pure creation of Allah Most High in the heart, free from the two constraints. And an example of negation: our saying for instance: every existent is not free from eternity and origination together. Then this rational judgment, even if it is free from the two constraints, the Blessed and Exalted has set the habit of creating some of its types in the heart necessarily without reflection, and creating some of its types upon consideration and reflection. And all originated knowledges, even if they occur by the pure creation of Allah Most High, it is sound that He creates them in the hearts initially without the intermediary of experience, nor sending a messenger, nor reflection nor thought. And He, Glorified, by His pure choice has set the habit of creating them according to this division.
Source pages p. 134

Commentary
This passage defines rational judgment (al-ḥukm al-ʿaqlī) and explains how it is acquired. The author states that rational judgment is a direct judgment of the mind, e.g., that every existent is either eternal or originated. This judgment occurs without any empirical or revelational intermediary; it is created by Allah directly in the heart. Then he distinguishes two types: necessary (ḍarūrī), which occurs without reflection, and theoretical (naẓarī), which occurs after reflection. All such knowledge is created by Allah, but He has set the habit that some come immediately and some through thought.

Key Terms
حكم عقلي
ضروري
نظري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think rational judgment always requires reasoning or empirical evidence.
Correction: The passage clarifies that rational judgment can be necessary and directly created by Allah without any reflection or intermediary.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, what are the two types of rational judgment?
A) Necessary and theoretical
B) Eternal and originated
C) Possible and impossible
D) Empirical and legal

Key Takeaway
Rational judgments are either necessary or theoretical, both directly created by Allah in the heart.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you rationally deduce something, do you pause to acknowledge that this knowledge is directly created by Allah in your heart, or do you attribute it solely to your own intellect? How would this shift in awareness change your humility before Allah?
?
You know with certainty that 'every existent is either eternal or originated' – where did that certainty come from? Did you reason to it, or was it placed in your heart? If it is a gift from Allah, how should that affect your gratitude and your reliance on Him for understanding?
?
Are there matters of faith that you have not yet reflected upon because you assume they require complex reasoning? Realize that even theoretical knowledge is granted by Allah after reflection. Will you commit to engaging in thoughtful reflection, trusting that Allah will create the correct understanding in your heart?

Definition and Division of the Necessary
Sanusi defines the necessary and divides it into necessary-by-itself (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī).

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of the necessary (wājib) and its division into necessary-by-itself (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī), and why this division depends on God's custom.

Core Idea
The necessary is what the intellect cannot deny; its division into two types is based on God's usual way.

Before we can discuss God's attributes, we need a precise vocabulary for what is rationally necessary. Sanusi begins by defining 'necessary' itself and then showing that it comes in two kinds: one we grasp immediately, and another that requires thought. This passage also reminds us that the division is not absolute—it is rooted in God's custom, which He could change if He wished.

Guided Path
1
Understand the definition
Read the definition: 'that whose non-existence is not conceivable in the intellect.' This means the mind cannot deny it. Notice that it covers both positive realities (like God's essence) and negative attributes (like His eternity).
The definition is about what the intellect can or cannot deny, not about physical possibility.
2
Examine the division
The passage then explains the phrase 'either without reflection...' to show that the necessary divides into two types: necessary-by-itself (ḍarūrī), known without reflection, and theoretical (naẓarī), which needs reflection. This division is according to God's custom.
The division is not intrinsic to the necessary itself but relates to how we come to know it.
3
Reflect on the divine custom
The passage adds that God could, by consensus, make all sciences necessary, so the division is contingent. Compare this with making all movements involuntary—it shows God's power over our cognitive and physical faculties.
This prevents us from thinking the division is metaphysically necessary.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
قوله : ( ما لا يُتصوَّرُ في العقل عدمُهُ ) يعني : لا يُدرَكُ في العقل نفيه ، سواء كانَتْ حقيقة ذلك الواجب وجودية؛ كذاتِ مولانا تبارك وتعالى، أو سلبيَّةً؛ كقِدَمِهِ جَلَّ وعلا. وقوله : ( إمَّا بلا تأمل ... ) إلى آخره؛ يعني أن الواجب ينقسم إلى ضروريّ ونظري بحسب مُجرى عادةِ الله تعالى، وإلا فيجوز بإجماع أن يُصير سبحانه جميع العلوم ضرورية يلجأ العقل إلى تيقنها، وتُخلَقُ فيه بلا تأمل أصلاً، كما يصح في العقل أن يجعلَ سبحانه جميع حركاتِنا اضطراريةً، لا نجد عادةً تيسير تركها
His saying: (That whose non-existence is not conceivable in the intellect) means: its negation is not perceived in the intellect, whether the reality of that necessary is existential—like the essence of our Lord, Blessed and Exalted—or negative—like His Eternity, Mighty and Majestic. And his saying: (Either without reflection ...) to its end means that the necessary divides into necessary-by-itself (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī) according to the usual course of God's custom; otherwise, it is permissible by consensus that He, exalted, could make all sciences necessary, such that the intellect arrives at certainty of them and they are created in it without any reflection, just as it is intellectually valid that He, exalted, could make all our movements involuntary, so that we do not normally find it easy to abandon them.
Source pages p. 136

Commentary
This passage defines and expands on the category of 'necessary' (wājib). First, it explains the phrase 'that whose non-existence is not conceivable in the intellect' as meaning that its negation cannot be mentally perceived. This applies whether the necessary is a positive reality—like the essence of God—or a negative attribute—like His eternity. Then it interprets the author's phrase 'either without reflection...' to indicate that the necessary divides into two types according to God's usual custom: necessary-by-itself (ḍarūrī) which is known without reflection, and theoretical (naẓarī) which requires reflection. However, it adds that it is possible by consensus that God could make all sciences necessary, creating them in the mind without reflection, similar to how He could make all human movements involuntary. This establishes the division while noting the contingency of the usual order on divine will.

Key Terms
واجب (wājib)
ضروري (ḍarūrī)
نظري (naẓarī)
وجودي (wujūdī)
سلبي (salabī)
عادة الله (ʿādat Allāh)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think that the division between necessary-by-itself and theoretical is a fixed, metaphysical division inherent in the things themselves.
Correction: The passage clarifies that this division is according to God's custom (ʿādat Allāh) and that God could, in principle, make all knowledge necessary by creating certainty without reflection.

Checkpoint
What does the author mean by 'that whose non-existence is not conceivable in the intellect'?
A) It means the thing is physically impossible.
B) It means the intellect cannot deny it.
C) It means the thing does not exist.
D) It means the thing is unknown.

Key Takeaway
The necessary is defined by the intellect's inability to deny it, and its division into immediate and reflective knowledge depends on God's customary operation, not an absolute nature.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God could make all knowledge necessary without any effort on your part, does your intellectual certainty stem from your own reasoning or from His sustaining grace? How does that affect your humility before Him?
?
You likely assume that some things are 'obvious'—that they require no proof. But the lesson says this division is by God's custom. What would change in your daily life if you truly believed that even your most basic certainties are gifts from Him, not intrinsic to reality?
?
The passage says it is 'intellectually valid' that God could make all our movements involuntary. Consider a sin you commit by choice. If God took away your ability to even think of sinning, you would be sinless. What does that tell you about your own will and your dependence on His permission to do good?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
5 min
Defining the Intellect as a Faculty of Acceptance
Sanusi defines the intellect with its specific opposites.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the specific definition of the intellect (al-ʿaql) as the heart's capacity to receive knowledge and its opposites, and why it is not knowledge itself.

Core Idea
The intellect is a receptacle, not the knowledge it holds.

Imam Sanusi begins by clarifying what the intellect truly is. He argues that the correct view defines the intellect as the heart's ability to accept knowledge and certain cognitive states, rather than being identical to that knowledge. This distinction is crucial for understanding how rational judgments are possible.

Guided Path
1
Read the Arabic passage and translation
Read the Arabic and English translation carefully to grasp the author's definition of the intellect.
Pay attention to the key terms like ʿaql, qabūl al-qalb, and the list of opposites.
2
Identify the key terms
List the specific opposites of knowledge mentioned: conjecture, doubt, illusion, and compound ignorance. Understand what each means in the kalam context.
These are cognitive states opposed to certain knowledge.
3
Note the negation
The author explicitly says the intellect is not knowledge itself nor a necessary consequence. Reflect on why this matters.
If intellect were knowledge, it could not receive its opposites.
4
Reflect on significance
Consider how this definition underpins the possibility of rational judgments—the intellect is a neutral faculty that can accept truth or falsehood.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والظاهرُ : القولُ الأولُ ؛ بناءً على أنَّ العقل قبول القلبِ للعلم أو أضدادِهِ الخاصَّةِ ؛ كالظنّ والشكّ والوهم والجهل المركَّب ، وليس نفس العلم ولا ملزوماً له ، ويدلُّ على ذلكَ : وجودُ السُّمَنِيَّةِ المنكرينَ لِمَا عدا المحسوساتِ مِنَ العلوم ضروريةً كانَتْ أو نظرية ووجودُ السُّوفَسْطائية المنكرين لجميعِ العلوم ؛ ضروريها ونظريها ، محسوسها وغير محسوسها ، وهم مِنَ العقلاء ؛ بدليل تعرُّض الأئمةِ لبدعتِهم ، والتحيل في مناظرتهم لدفعها
And the apparent [opinion] is the first statement; based on the fact that the intellect is the heart's acceptance of knowledge or its specific opposites, like conjecture, doubt, illusion, and compound ignorance, and it is not knowledge itself nor a necessary consequence of it. And that is indicated by the existence of the Sumaniyya who deny what is other than sensory things from among knowledges, whether necessary or theoretical, and the existence of the Sophists who deny all knowledges, both necessary and theoretical, sensory and non-sensory, and they are among the rational beings, as evidenced by the imams' engagement with their innovation and their resorting to stratagems in debating them to refute it.
Source pages p. 137

Commentary
This passage defines the intellect (al-ʿaql) and then provides a proof for the necessity and reality of rational judgments. It begins by stating that the correct opinion (al-ẓāhir) is the first one, which defines the intellect not as knowledge itself but as the heart's capacity to receive knowledge or its specific opposites (ẓann, shakk, wahm, jahl murakkab). This means the intellect is a faculty, not the knowledge itself. Then it offers a proof (dalīl) for the existence of necessary and theoretical judgments: the existence of the Sumaniyya (who deny non-sensory knowledge) and the Sophists (who deny all knowledge). These groups are rational beings (ʿuqalāʾ) because the imams engaged with their innovations and used dialectical strategies to refute them. If these groups could exist while denying all knowledge, it would imply that rational judgments are not necessary. But since they are considered rational and their views are debated, this shows that rational judgments are indeed real and necessary, as the imams treat them as erroneous but rational.

Key Terms
العقل
قبول القلب
الظن
الشك
الوهم
الجهل المركب

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think the intellect is identical to knowledge.
Correction: The author clarifies that the intellect is a faculty that accepts knowledge, not knowledge itself.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, the intellect (al-ʿaql) is:
A) Knowledge itself
B) The heart's acceptance of knowledge and its specific opposites
C) A necessary consequence of knowledge
D) Doubt

Key Takeaway
The intellect is a receptive faculty, not the knowledge it contains.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter new Islamic knowledge, do you approach it with humility, allowing your heart to accept it, or do you rely on your intellect as if it already contains the truth? What would it take for you to become a more receptive vessel?
?
The passage warns that the intellect can accept not just knowledge but also conjecture, doubt, illusion, and compound ignorance. Which of these false states might you be holding onto as if it were knowledge? Repent from it now.
?
If the intellect is not the knowledge itself but only its acceptor, then your worth is not in how much you know, but in how open your heart is to the truth. Are you more concerned with being right or with surrendering to Allah? Pray for a heart that accepts.

What Is Kalam?
intermediate
6 min
Proving Rational Judgments from the Existence of Denying Sects
Sanusi proves rational judgments via opposing sects.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the existence of the Sumaniyya and Sophists proves the reality of necessary and theoretical rational judgments.

Core Idea
Error presupposes truth; denying knowledge still uses reason.

After defining the intellect, Sanusi provides a proof (dalīl) for the existence and necessity of rational judgments. He points to the historical existence of two extreme sects—the Sumaniyya who deny non-sensory knowledge, and the Sophists who deny all knowledge—and argues that even they, being rational, inadvertently confirm the reality of rational judgments through the very engagement of scholars with their claims.

Guided Path
1
Read the proof passage
Read the Arabic and translation describing the Sumaniyya and Sophists as evidence.
2
Identify the two groups
Note the Sumaniyya deny all but sensory knowledge; the Sophists deny all knowledge.
3
Understand the logic
The proof works because these groups are considered rational beings (ʿuqalāʾ), as imams engaged with them. If they were not rational, their denial would be irrelevant. But they are rational, so their denial implies that what they deny (sensory and rational knowledge) must exist as objects of denial.
4
Reflect on the implication
This shows that necessary and theoretical judgments are real; otherwise, the imams would not have bothered to refute them.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والظاهرُ : القولُ الأولُ ؛ بناءً على أنَّ العقل قبول القلبِ للعلم أو أضدادِهِ الخاصَّةِ ؛ كالظنّ والشكّ والوهم والجهل المركَّب ، وليس نفس العلم ولا ملزوماً له ، ويدلُّ على ذلكَ : وجودُ السُّمَنِيَّةِ المنكرينَ لِمَا عدا المحسوساتِ مِنَ العلوم ضروريةً كانَتْ أو نظرية ووجودُ السُّوفَسْطائية المنكرين لجميعِ العلوم ؛ ضروريها ونظريها ، محسوسها وغير محسوسها ، وهم مِنَ العقلاء ؛ بدليل تعرُّض الأئمةِ لبدعتِهم ، والتحيل في مناظرتهم لدفعها
And the apparent [opinion] is the first statement; based on the fact that the intellect is the heart's acceptance of knowledge or its specific opposites, like conjecture, doubt, illusion, and compound ignorance, and it is not knowledge itself nor a necessary consequence of it. And that is indicated by the existence of the Sumaniyya who deny what is other than sensory things from among knowledges, whether necessary or theoretical, and the existence of the Sophists who deny all knowledges, both necessary and theoretical, sensory and non-sensory, and they are among the rational beings, as evidenced by the imams' engagement with their innovation and their resorting to stratagems in debating them to refute it.
Source pages p. 137

Commentary
This passage defines the intellect (al-ʿaql) and then provides a proof for the necessity and reality of rational judgments. It begins by stating that the correct opinion (al-ẓāhir) is the first one, which defines the intellect not as knowledge itself but as the heart's capacity to receive knowledge or its specific opposites (ẓann, shakk, wahm, jahl murakkab). This means the intellect is a faculty, not the knowledge itself. Then it offers a proof (dalīl) for the existence of necessary and theoretical judgments: the existence of the Sumaniyya (who deny non-sensory knowledge) and the Sophists (who deny all knowledge). These groups are rational beings (ʿuqalāʾ) because the imams engaged with their innovations and used dialectical strategies to refute them. If these groups could exist while denying all knowledge, it would imply that rational judgments are not necessary. But since they are considered rational and their views are debated, this shows that rational judgments are indeed real and necessary, as the imams treat them as erroneous but rational.

Key Terms
السمنية
السوفسطائية
الضروري
النظري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
What do the Sumaniyya deny?
A) Only sensory knowledge
B) All knowledge
C) Only theoretical knowledge
D) Knowledge of God

Key Takeaway
The existence of groups that deny knowledge actually confirms the reality of rational judgments.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you encounter a skeptic who denies core Islamic beliefs, do you see their denial as a threat or as an affirmation that those truths are real? How will you use this lesson to engage with them as rational beings rather than dismiss them?
?
Have you ever avoided a difficult conversation about faith because you assumed the other person was irrational? What if their rejection actually confirms the truth you hold? Will you seek out one such conversation this week?
?
The imams used stratagems to refute falsehood. Are you willing to invest time and effort to learn how to rationally defend your beliefs? What step will you take today to strengthen your understanding of the proofs of faith?

What Is Kalam?
beginner
6 min
The Contingent: Definition and Two Types of Knowledge
Sanusi defines the contingent and distinguishes necessary from theoretical knowledge of its possibility.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the definition of al-ja'iz (the contingent) and how its possibility can be known either necessarily or through reflection.

Core Idea
The contingent is what can be or not be, known either by necessity or by thought.

Sanusi begins by defining the fundamental concept of the contingent (al-ja'iz), which is the basis for all later theological reasoning. He then shows that the judgment of possibility can be immediate (necessary) or require reflection (theoretical). This lesson unpacks that distinction with concrete examples.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the literal translation. Let the author's own words introduce the concepts before you analyze.
Notice the concise definition at the start.
2
Identify the definition of al-ja'iz
Find the sentence that defines the contingent. Understand that it refers to what is rationally possible—neither necessary nor impossible.
3
Distinguish the two types of knowledge
The passage divides knowledge of possibility into two: necessary (ḍarūrī) without reflection, and theoretical (naẓarī) after reflection. The examples clarify each.
The whiteness of a body is known necessarily; wishing for death is known theoretically.
4
Analyze the reasoning for the theoretical example
Follow Sanusi's explanation: a healthy person initially thinks wishing for death is impossible, but after considering severe calamities, realizes it is possible. This shows theoretical judgment.
The reasoning hinges on the contrast between initial imagination and reflection.
5
Understand the exception for those with experience
Sanusi notes that for someone who has actually experienced such causes (fear, hope, yearning), the permissibility becomes necessary knowledge. Note how context changes the type.
This highlights that classification depends on the knower's state.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
تعريف الجائز الضروري والنظري
والجائز : ما يصح في العقل ثبوته وعدمه ؛ إما بلا تأمل ؛ ككون الجسمِ أبيضَ مثلاً ، وإِمَّا بعد التأمل ؛ كتمني الإنسان الموت مثلاً
لا شكٍّ أنَّ وجودَ البياض وعدمه للأجسام قد عرفه العقل ضرورةً بالمشاهدة ، وصحَّةُ وجودِ الشيء وعدمِهِ أعمُّ مِنْ وجودِهِ وعدمِهِ ، فإذا كان الأخص ضرورياً للعقل .. فأحرى أن يكون الأعم ضرورياً. وأما الحكم على تمني الموتِ بالجواز النظري : فظاهر ، لكن في حق أهل العافية الذين لم يذوقوا المصائب التي هي أشدُّ مِنَ الموتِ ويُستسهَلُ الموتُ ويُتمنَّى عندَها ، ولا خالطوا مَنْ وقع في ذلكَ ، ولا عرفوا المِحَنَ بالفكرة والتوهم ، فهؤلاء يَتوهَّمونَ ابتداءً أَنَّهُ محال أنْ يَتمنّى العاقل الموتَ لنفسه ، فإذا فكَّروا في المِحَنِ.. عرفوا أنَّ أشدُّ مِنَ الموتِ ، فحينئذ يحكمون بأنَّ تمنّي العاقل الموتَ لنفسه ليس بواجب ولا مستحيل [بل يصح وجودُهُ إِنْ خَافَ مِنَ المصائب ما هو أشدُّ منه ، أو اشتاق ، أو رجا شيئاً عظيماً لا يحصل له إلا به]
وأما معرفةُ جواز تمنِّيها في حقٌّ مَنِ اتَّصف بأسباب ذلك خوفاً أو رجاءً أو اشتياقاً : فهي ضرورية لا تحتاج إلى تأمل ، لكنَّ المثال المقصود منه التقريبُ ، فيصحُ التمثيل بما وُجِدَ على الجملة أو قُدِّرَ وجوده
(وأبيات الوزير المهلبي في هذا مشهورة ، وقد قال سيدنا لبيد العامري) من الكامل رضي الله عنه : ولقد سئمتُ مِنَ الحياة وطولها وسؤال هـذا الناس كيف لبيد وقال آخر : (ألا أيُّها الدهرُ الذي قد مَلِلْتُهُ سألتُكَ إلا ما مَلِلْتَ حياتي)
(روى البخاري عن سيدنا أبي سعيد الخدري رضي الله عنه : أن رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم جلس على المنبر فقال : ( إن عبداً خيَّرَهُ اللهُ بينَ أن يؤتيَهُ مِنْ زهرة الدنيا ما شاءَ وبين ما عنده ، فاختار ما عنده ) ، فبكى أبو بكر وقال : فديناك بآبائنا وأمهاتنا ، فعجبنا له ، وقال الناس : انظروا إلى هذا الشيخ ! يخبر رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم عن عبد خيره الله أن يؤتيه من زهرة الدنيا وبين ما عنده ، وهو يقول : فديناك بآبائنا وأمهاتنا ! فكان رسول الله صلى الله عليه وسلم هو المخيَّرَ ، وكان أبو بكر هو أعلمنا به)
Definition of the Contingent, Necessary and Theoretical. The contingent (al-ja'iz) is what is valid in the intellect that it exists or does not exist; either without reflection, like a body being white for example, or after reflection, like a person wishing for death for example. There is no doubt that the existence of whiteness and its non-existence for bodies has been known by the intellect necessarily through observation, and the validity of the existence of a thing and its non-existence is more general than its existence and non-existence. So if the more specific is necessary for the intellect, then it is even more fitting that the more general be necessary. As for the judgment on wishing for death as being theoretically contingent (jawāz naẓarī), it is apparent, but it applies to healthy people who have not tasted calamities that are more severe than death, such that death is deemed easy and wished for in those situations, and they have not mixed with those who have experienced that, nor known tribulations through thought and imagination. So these initially imagine that it is impossible for a rational person to wish for death for himself. But when they think about tribulations, they realize that there is something more severe than death. Then they judge that a rational person's wishing for death for himself is neither obligatory nor impossible, but rather its existence is valid if he fears from calamities what is more severe than it, or yearns for something great that cannot be attained except through it, or hopes for something great that cannot be attained except through it. And as for knowing the permissibility of wishing for it in the case of one who is characterized by the causes of that - fear, hope, or yearning - it is necessary, requiring no reflection. But the example is intended for approximation, so it is valid to give an example with something that exists in general or whose existence is supposed. (And the verses of al-Wazir al-Muhallabi on this are famous, and our master Labid al-'Amiri, may Allah be pleased with him, said: 'I have grown weary of life and its length, and people asking how Labid is.' And another said: 'O time! I have grown weary of you. I ask you only that you grow weary of my life.') (Al-Bukhari narrated from our master Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, may Allah be pleased with him: that the Messenger of Allah, peace be upon him, sat on the pulpit and said: 'Indeed, a servant whom Allah gave the choice between being given whatever he wants of the splendor of this world and that which is with Him, chose what is with Him.' So Abu Bakr wept and said: 'We would ransom you with our fathers and mothers!' We were amazed at him, and the people said: 'Look at this old man! The Messenger of Allah tells us about a servant whom Allah gave the choice between being given from the splendor of this world and what is with Him, and he says: We would ransom you with our fathers and mothers!' The Messenger of Allah was the one given the choice, and Abu Bakr was the most knowledgeable of us about him.)
Source pages p. 139

Commentary
This passage defines al-ja'iz (the contingent or possible) and distinguishes two types of knowledge regarding its possibility: necessary (ḍarūrī) and theoretical (naẓarī). The author begins with the definition: al-ja'iz is what is valid in the intellect that it exists or does not exist. This is the standard definition in kalām. He then explains that this judgment of possibility can be made either without reflection (bila ta'mul) – like the whiteness of a body, which is known necessarily through observation – or after reflection (ba'da al-ta'mul), like wishing for death. The author argues that the more general validity of existence and non-existence (the possibility itself) is even more certainly necessary than the specific observed case (e.g., whiteness). Then he turns to the example of wishing for death. For healthy people who have not suffered severe calamities, the possibility of a rational person wishing for death is a theoretical judgment: they initially think it impossible, but after considering that there are calamities worse than death, they realize it is possible (not obligatory or impossible). However, for one who has experienced such causes (fear, hope, yearning), the permissibility is known necessarily, without reflection. The author then cites poetry from Labid and another poet expressing weariness with life, and the famous hadith about Abu Bakr understanding that the Prophet was given the choice between worldly splendor and what is with Allah, and chose the latter – illustrating that wishing for death (or preferring the afterlife) can be permissible. The overall purpose is to clarify that the classification of a judgment as necessary or theoretical can depend on the observer's knowledge state.

Key Terms
الجائز (al-ja'iz)
الضروري (al-ḍarūrī)
النظري (al-naẓarī)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Every judgment of possibility (al-ja'iz) requires reflection.
Correction: The author shows that some judgments of possibility, like the whiteness of a body, are known necessarily without reflection.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, how does a healthy person who has never experienced severe calamities initially view the possibility of a rational person wishing for death?
A) As necessary
B) As impossible
C) As theoretically possible
D) As obligatory

Key Takeaway
The contingent is what can exist or not, and its possibility can be known either necessarily (by direct observation) or theoretically (by reasoning).

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
The passage shows that some truths are known necessarily without reflection, like the possibility of a body being white. Are there truths about your own soul—your sins, your weaknesses—that you have been avoiding seeing directly because seeing would demand immediate repentance? What self-deception are you clinging to that you actually know is false?
?
The author says that those who have not tasted severe calamities initially think a rational person would never wish for death. But when they reflect on greater tribulations, they realize it is possible. Have you ever truly pondered what trials could make you wish for death? What does that reflection reveal about your current faith and reliance on Allah? Are you prepared for a test that might make you long for what you now consider unbearable?
?
For those who have experienced fear, hope, or yearning, the permissibility of wishing for death becomes necessary, known without thinking. Your intellectual beliefs about Allah—His mercy, His justice—are they merely theoretical concepts you have studied, or have they become necessary convictions through lived experience? What must change in your life so that your faith moves from theoretical to necessary, from reflection to direct knowledge?

Why the Phrase Attaches to 'Know' Not 'Must'
Sanusi explains why the phrase about events depends is attached to 'know' not 'must'.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the phrase 'for the dependence of events' is grammatically attached to 'know' (اعلم) rather than to 'must' (يجب) in the creed, and the theological reason behind it.

Core Idea
The phrase explains our knowledge of God's necessity, not God's necessity itself.

Sanusi addresses a subtle grammatical point in the creed that has deep theological implications. The phrase 'for the dependence of events on His existence' might seem to explain why God's existence is necessary, but attaching it to 'must' would imply that God's necessity depends on events. Sanusi clarifies that it is attached to 'know', making the events a cause of our knowledge, not of God's necessity.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without trying to analyse it. Then read the translation. Let the author's own rhythm come through before you interpret.
Notice the key terms: تعلق, المجرور, اللام, اعلم, يجب, etc.
2
Identify the two possible attachments
The passage presents two possible attachments for the prepositional phrase: to 'know' (اعلم) or to 'must' (يجب). Find where Sanusi discusses each and the consequence of the wrong attachment.
3
Understand the theological problem
Ask yourself: What would happen if the phrase attached to 'must'? Sanusi says it would imply that God's necessity is established after events or caused by them, which contradicts God's eternal necessity.
This is the key theological reason for the correct attachment.
4
See how the correct attachment preserves divine attributes
Attaching to 'know' makes the existence of events a customary cause (سبب عادة) for our knowledge, not a cause of God's necessity itself. This preserves God's eternal, uncaused necessity.
Review the distinction between علة (true cause) and سبب عادة (customary cause).

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقولنا في أصل العقيدة : ( لتوقف وجودِ الحوادثِ على وجودِهِ تعالى ) يتعلق المجرور باللام ؛ وهو (توقف) بـ (اعلم ) ، لا بقولنا : ( يجب لمولانا جل وعزَّ الوجودُ ) لِمَا يلزمُ عليهِ أَنْ يكونَ وجوب الوجودِ له تبارك وتعالى إنَّما ثبت له بعد وجودِ الحوادثِ وتوقُفِ وجودِها على وجودِهِ جلَّ وعلا ، أو يكون وجوب الوجودِ له تعالى معلولاً لوجودِ الحوادثِ ، كيف ووجوب الوجودِ لمولانا تبارك وتعالى قديم قبل وجودِ الحوادثِ ، غيرُ مُعلَّل بوجودها ؟! نعم ؛ وجودُ الحوادث سبب عادةً في علمنا بوجودِهِ تعالى ، فلذلك وجب تعليق هذا المجرور باللام في قولنا : ( اعلم ) لا بالمضارع في قولنا : ( يجب ) أي : اعلم وجوب الوجودِ لمولانا جلَّ وعزَّ مِنْ أجلِ معرفتِكَ بتوقفِ وجودِ الحوادث على وجودِهِ تعالى ؛ لاستحالة ترجح وجودِها الجائز على عدمها المساوي له في الجواز.
Our statement in the foundation of the creed: 'for the dependence of the existence of events on His existence, exalted is He' — the word with the preposition (laam) is attached, and it is 'dependence', to 'know', not to our statement: 'existence is necessary for our Master, majestic and mighty is He.' Because what would follow from it is that the necessity of existence for Him, blessed and exalted, is only established after the existence of events and their dependence on His existence, or that the necessity of existence for Him is an effect of the existence of events. But the necessity of existence for our Master, blessed and exalted, is eternal before the existence of events, not caused by their existence! Yes, the existence of events is a customary cause of our knowledge of His existence. Therefore, it is necessary to attach this word with the preposition (laam) in our statement: 'know', not to the verb in our statement: 'must'. That is: Know the necessity of existence for our Master, glorious and mighty, because of your knowledge of the dependence of the existence of events on His existence; due to the impossibility of the preponderance of their possible existence over its equal non-existence.
Source pages p. 145

Commentary
This passage is a grammatical and theological clarification about a phrase in the creed: 'for the dependence of events on His existence'. Sanusi explains that the prepositional phrase ('for the dependence') is attached to the verb 'know' (اعلم) not to 'must' (يجب). If it were attached to 'must', it would imply that God's necessary existence is contingent upon the existence of events or caused by them, which is false because God's necessity is eternal. Rather, events cause our knowledge of God's existence. So the phrase is saying: 'Know the necessity of existence for God because of your knowledge that events depend on Him.' This is a standard Ash'ari argument: possible existence of the world requires a preponderator, and the impossibility of preponderance without a cause leads to the knowledge of God.

Key Terms
تعلق
المجرور
اللام
اعلم
يجب
وجوب الوجود
قديم
علة
سبب عادة
جواز

Key Terms from Source Passage

Checkpoint
Why does Sanusi attach the prepositional phrase to 'know' (اعلم) rather than to 'must' (يجب)?
A) Because the language requires it for correct grammar
B) To avoid implying that God's necessity depends on events
C) Because 'must' is not used in creedal statements
D) To emphasize that God's existence is not known without events

Key Takeaway
The phrase 'for the dependence' is attached to 'know' to indicate that events cause our knowledge of God's necessity, not that God's necessity depends on events.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If God's necessity is eternal and uncaused, independent of every event, why does your heart often feel that His existence or favor depends on your circumstances? When you face hardship, where in yourself do you see the temptation to doubt that He is still absolutely necessary? Today, how will you root your certainty not in your situation but in the eternal truth that God is necessary regardless of all events?
?
Sanusi says events are a 'customary cause' for our knowledge of God. When you see the changes and happenings in your life, do you actually let them point you to the unchanging Necessary Being, or do you get absorbed in the events themselves? What is one specific event from yesterday that should have reminded you of God's necessity—and why did you miss it? Starting now, how can you train your mind to see every event as a sign of the Eternal?
?
The passage warns against the error that God's necessity is caused by creation. Is there any way you have been acting as though your faith or relationship with God is a result of what He does for you, rather than simply because He is God? If you lost everything, would you still affirm His necessity with the same certainty? Reflect on one attachment in your life that you have made a condition for your trust in God, and ask Him to free you from it.

What Is Kalam?
beginner

The Creedal Formula and Self-Subsistence
Sanusi explains how the creedal formula interprets the two parts of self-subsistence.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how the creedal formula 'essence described by attributes, independent of a locus, and the Agent' interprets the divine self-subsistence, and why each part is necessary.

Core Idea
The creedal formula unpacks self-subsistence into two parts to prevent misunderstanding.

In this passage, Sanusi explains the meaning behind the standard creedal formula used to affirm divine self-subsistence. He shows how each phrase prevents a specific theological error. The passage clarifies why 'independent of a locus' is added and how 'the Agent' addresses a potential misconception.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage and its translation carefully. Notice how Sanusi breaks the formula into two parts.
2
Identify the two parts
Identify the two parts of the formula: first part describing essence and attributes, second part about 'the Agent'.
The first part is 'essence described by attributes, independent of a locus'. The second is 'the Agent'.
3
Understand the role of each part
Understand that the first part prevents God being an attribute, and the second prevents resemblance to creation.
4
Reflect on the emphasis
Reflect on why 'independent of a locus' is added for emphasis, not as a new attribute.
Every essence described by attributes is already independent of a locus.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فقولنا في أصل العقيدة : ( أي : ذاتاً موصوفاً بالصفاتِ ، غنياً عن المحل ) هو تفسير للجزء الأولِ مِنَ القيام بالنفس ، وهو الذي منع كونه صفةٌ ، وزيادتنا الوصف بـ ( غنيّاً عن المحل ) بعد قولنا : ( موصوفاً بالصفاتِ ( للتأكيد ، وإلا فكلُّ ذاتِ موصوفة بالصفات هي غنية عن المحل ؛ أي : عن ذاتِ تقوم بها ، وقولنا : ( والفاعل ) هو تفسير للجزء الثاني مِنَ القيامِ بالنفس ، وهو الذي منع توهم كونِ ذاتِهِ تعالى تشبه شيئاً مِنَ الذواتِ و الصفات
So our saying in the foundational creed: (i.e., an essence described by attributes, independent of a locus) is an interpretation of the first part of self-subsistence, and it is that which prevented it from being an attribute. And our addition of the description 'independent of a locus' after our saying 'described by attributes' is for emphasis; otherwise every essence described by attributes is independent of a locus, i.e., of an essence in which it subsists. And our saying 'and the Agent' is an interpretation of the second part of self-subsistence, and it is that which prevented the illusion that His essence (Exalted) resembles any of the essences and attributes.
Source pages p. 157

Commentary
This passage explains the creedal formulation found in 'the foundational creed' (asl al-aqida). The formula 'an essence described by attributes, independent of a locus' interprets the first part of self-subsistence, which prevents God from being an attribute. The addition 'independent of a locus' is not to introduce a new attribute but to emphasize that being described by attributes already implies independence from a locus. The second part of self-subsistence is interpreted by the phrase 'and the Agent' (al-fa'il), which prevents the illusion that God's essence resembles any contingent essences or attributes.

Key Terms
أصل العقيدة
القول
الفاعل
القيام بالنفس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think 'independent of a locus' is a separate divine attribute.
Correction: Sanusi explicitly says it is added for emphasis, because being described by attributes already implies independence.

Checkpoint
Why is 'independent of a locus' added to the creedal formula?
A) to introduce a new divine attribute
B) to emphasize that being described by attributes already implies independence
C) to distinguish God from the world
D) to show that God is a body

Key Takeaway
The creedal formula explains self-subsistence in two parts: the first emphasizes God's independence from a locus, and the second affirms His agency to prevent any resemblance to creation.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you say 'Allah is Independent,' do you ever stop to think that this means He is not like anything you can imagine? How does that realization confront the way you visualize God in your prayers? What will you do today to purify your understanding?
?
The creedal formula guards against the illusion that God resembles creation. In what specific ways might you be unconsciously comparing God to yourself or to others? What step can you take right now to correct that in your heart?
?
Sanusi insists that 'independent of a locus' is mere emphasis, not a separate attribute. How does your own desire for 'extra' descriptions of God reveal a lack of trust in the sufficiency of what He has revealed? What will you change about your study of His names after this lesson?
