The Prophet as the Gate of Divine Mercy and Knowledge
Sanusi praises the Prophet as the gate to divine mercy and knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) is described as the great gate to divine mercy and how his intercession and mission bring about spiritual transformation.

Core Idea
The Prophet is the unique gate through which all divine mercy and knowledge flow.

This passage from Sanusi's introduction is a powerful devotional exordium that establishes the Prophet Muhammad's central role in the cosmos. Before delving into theological arguments, Sanusi paints a vivid picture of the Prophet as the indispensable conduit of God's mercy, knowledge, and spiritual light. As you read, notice how every blessing—from the removal of ignorance to the flourishing of saints—is traced back to him.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the English translation. Let the author's devotional tone wash over you before you start dissecting.
Pay attention to the imagery: gate, coattails, suns, clouds.
2
Identify the Prophet's role as a gate
Notice how Sanusi explicitly calls the Prophet a 'great open gate' that no other gate can match. Ask: What does this gate lead to? Who needs it?
The gate is to divine mercy and safety from wrath.
3
Examine the effects of his intercession
The passage lists several effects of the Prophet's intercession: dispelling distress, opening gates, spreading joy. List these effects in your own words.
Intercession is mentioned specifically for the Hereafter.
4
Reflect on the abundance of saints and knowledge
Sanusi concludes by noting that through the Prophet, treasures of divine knowledge and ranks of saints appear. Consider how this connects to the purpose of the book (which is kalam).
This shows the Prophet as the source of spiritual authority.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
مولانا جل وعلا والسلامةِ مِنْ غضبِهِ ، وقد جعل الله سبحانَهُ بفضلِهِ نبيَّنا وسيدنا ومولانا محمَّداً صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ باباً عظيماً مفتوحاً لذلك في الدنيا والآخرة لا يقاربه باب ، ولا يستغني عن التعلُّق بأذيالِهِ والإيواء إلى عتبةِ حَرَمِهِ وبابِهِ أَحَدٌ مِنَ الأعداء أو الأحباب . كيفَ ومِنْ أَجْلِهِ  َخلَقَ اللهُ تعالى الكمال الدنيوي والأخروي والعلوي والسفلي ؟! وبشفاعته الكبرى في الآخرة وما بعدها مِنْ شفاعاتِهِ : تنقشع أنواع الكروب ، وترتفع بفضل الله سبحانه أسبابها ، وتتجلى شموس نِعَمِ مولانا جل وعلا على كافَّةِ المؤمنينَ ، وتنفتح أبوابها التي لم يتجاسر أحدٌ مِنْ أهل الكمالاتِ على طلب فتحها ، وتنتشر بعنايته العظمى التي تَفضَّلَ بها المولى تبارك وتعالى على أهل الإيمانِ بهِ .. أنواع السرور وتنكشفُ عن الظواهر والبواطن أجناس الغموم وأنواع الشرورِ . وببركة مبعثِهِ الشريف وطلوع طلعتِهِ البهية السعيدة على الأرضِ انكشفت ظلماتُ الكفر والجهالات التي عمَّتْ وانتشرَتْ ، وتمكَّنَتْ غاية التمكُن في جميع الآفاقِ والقلوبِ ، وتشعشعَتْ أنوار الإيمان بالله تعالى وبرسله وكتبه وملائكتِهِ ، وانقلَعَتْ بفضل الله تعالى سحائب رانِ الجهلِ وغُمَّةُ السيئاتِ والذنوبِ ، وأفاض سبحانه رحمته على الخلقِ ، وأخرجَ لهم على يد مصطفاه سيّدِنا ومولانا محمَّدٍ صلى الله عليهِ وسلَّمَ ذخائر المعارفِ الربَّانية ، ونفائس الحكم والعلوم اللدنية ، وحلاهم بجواهرِ الأسرارِ التي خَبَأَها لهم في خزائنِ الغيوبِ ، حتى كثُرَ منهم في كل جيل الأقطابُ والأوتاد والنقباء والأخيار والأبدال وعجَّتِ الأرضُ ؛ سهلها وجبلها ، برُّها وبحرها .. بتوحيد المولى
Our Master, may He be exalted and glorified, and safety from His wrath. And Allah, glory be to Him, by His grace made our Prophet, our Master, and our Liege Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, a great open gate for that in this world and the Hereafter, no gate approaches it, and none among enemies or loved ones is independent of clinging to his coattails and seeking refuge at the threshold of his sanctuary and his gate. How and for his sake did Allah, exalted be He, create perfections—worldly and otherworldly, celestial and terrestrial? And by his great intercession in the Hereafter and his intercessions beyond it, types of distress are dispelled, and by the grace of Allah, glory be to Him, their causes are removed, and the suns of the blessings of our Master, may He be exalted and glorified, shine upon all the believers, and their gates which none among the people of perfections had dared to ask to open are opened, and by His great care, with which the Patron, blessed and exalted be He, has favored the people of faith in Him, types of joy spread, and from the outer and inner [realms] kinds of sorrows and types of evils are uncovered. And by the blessing of his honorable mission and the rising of his beautiful, auspicious countenance upon the earth, the darknesses of unbelief and ignorances that had covered and spread, and had become extremely entrenched in all horizons and hearts, were removed, and the lights of faith in Allah, exalted be He, and in His messengers, His books, and His angels radiated, and by the grace of Allah, exalted be He, the clouds of the rust of ignorance and the gloom of evil deeds and sins were cleared away. And He, glory be to Him, poured His mercy upon creation, and brought forth for them at the hand of His chosen one, our Master and Liege Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace, the treasures of divine knowledges, and the precious gems of wisdom and inspired sciences, and adorned them with the jewels of mysteries which He had hidden for them in the treasuries of the unseen, until there abounded among them in every generation the spiritual poles, the pegs, the leaders, the righteous, and the substitutes, and the earth—its plains and its mountains, its land and its sea—teemed with the declaration of the oneness of the Patron.
Source pages p. 109

Commentary
This passage is a devotional exordium praising the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) and his unparalleled role in the cosmic order. The author begins by stating that God has made the Prophet a great open gate (bāb ʿaẓīm) to His mercy and safety from His wrath, in this world and the Hereafter—a gate that no other gate can match. He asserts that no one, whether enemy or friend, can dispense with clinging to the Prophet's coattails and seeking refuge at his threshold. He then asks rhetorically: How and for the Prophet's sake did God create all perfections—worldly and otherworldly, celestial and terrestrial? This implies that the Prophet is the final cause or pinnacle of creation. Next, the author describes the effects of the Prophet's great intercession (shafāʿa) in the Hereafter: it dispels distress, removes its causes, and causes the suns of divine blessings to shine upon believers. It opens gates that even the spiritually accomplished dared not ask to open. The author then links the Prophet's mission (mabʿath) to the removal of spiritual darkness: the darknesses of unbelief and ignorance that had pervaded all horizons and hearts were dispelled, and the lights of faith radiated. The clouds of rust of ignorance and gloom of sins were cleared. God poured mercy upon creation and brought forth through the Prophet treasures of divine knowledge (maʿārif rabbāniyya) and precious gems of wisdom and inspired sciences (ʿulūm laduniyya). He adorned believers with jewels of mysteries hidden in treasuries of the unseen. As a result, in every generation there have been numerous spiritual elites: the aqṭāb (poles), awtād (pegs), nuqabāʾ (leaders), akhyār (righteous), abdāl (substitutes)—terms referring to ranks of saints and spiritual authorities. Finally, the entire earth—plains, mountains, land, and sea—teemed with the declaration of God's oneness (tawḥīd al-mawlā). The passage thus sets the stage for a work of kalam by emphasizing the Prophet's centrality as the conduit of divine knowledge and the key to all spiritual blessings.

Key Terms
باب
شفاعة
معارف ربانية
علوم لدنية
أقطاب
أوتاد
نقباء
أخيار
أبدال

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think this praise is mere exaggeration or emotional devotion without theological purpose.
Correction: Sanusi uses this praise to establish the Prophet as the necessary source of divine knowledge, which grounds the authority of revelation in kalam.

Checkpoint
According to the passage, what does the Prophet's intercession dispel in the Hereafter?
A) Darkness of unbelief
B) Types of distress
C) Gossip and slander
D) Poverty and sickness

Key Takeaway
The Prophet Muhammad is the divinely appointed gate through which all mercy, knowledge, and spiritual blessings are accessed.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
If the Prophet is the only gate to divine mercy and knowledge, what does that mean for your daily efforts to seek knowledge and closeness to Allah? Do you approach the Prophet's teachings as a living gate or as historical information? How will you consciously pass through that gate today?
?
Consider the darkness of ignorance that the Prophet's mission dispelled. What areas of your life are still darkened by ignorance or neglect of the Prophet's guidance? What is one concrete step you can take this week to let his light into that area?
?
The passage says all perfections were created for the sake of the Prophet. Does your gratitude and love for him reflect that reality? How can you increase your connection to him as the gate of mercy, not just in words but in action?

6 min
The Virtue of Humility: Lessons from Iblis and Musa (AS)
Sanusi exhorts humility using Iblis's fall and Musa's humility towards al-Khidr.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the Islamic imperative of humility towards Allah and His favored servants, and the grave danger of arrogance as exemplified by Iblis and the humility of Prophet Musa towards al-Khidr.

Core Idea
Arrogance caused Iblis' fall; humility elevated Musa.

Humility (tawāḍuʿ) is a foundational virtue in Islam, required of every believer towards Allah and towards those whom Allah has honored. This passage from Sanusi’s work contrasts the destructive arrogance of Iblis with the profound humility of Prophet Musa (Moses) when seeking knowledge from al-Khidr. By examining these two examples, the author teaches us that no personal merit justifies pride, and that true greatness lies in recognizing the superiority of others when Allah has favored them.

Guided Path
1
Reflect on the Opening
Read the opening lines praising humility. Notice that Sanusi frames humility as compliance with Allah's command and as emulation of His magnifying and honoring.
The opening sets the tone: humility is an act of worship.
2
Analyze the Example of Iblis
Understand why Iblis fell: his arrogance when ordered to prostrate to Adam. He saw himself as superior, forgetting that any merit is purely from Allah. This illustrates the danger of pride.
Iblis's objection was based on ignorance and a sense of entitlement.
3
Study the Example of Musa and al-Khidr
Examine how Musa (AS) humbled himself: he traveled to al-Khidr, asked politely using a question form, accepted harshness, and pledged obedience. This is a model for seeking knowledge.
Musa's humility was remarkable because he was a great prophet not compelled to seek that knowledge.
4
Draw the Lesson
Connect the two examples: Humility preserves Allah's favor, while arrogance leads to loss. Apply this to your own life: never let Allah's gifts make you arrogant.
The passage ends with a prayer for protection from Iblis's fate.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
والسلام ؛ امتثالاً لأمر مولانا جل وعلا، وتعظيماً لما عظم ، وتكريماً لِمَا كرَّمَ ، وحبّاً لِمَا أَحبَّ .
وأين هذه الأخلاق الكريمة الزكيَّةُ مِنْ خُلُقٍ إبليس الأحمق المحروم ؛ حيث أمره المولى العظيم مع الملائكة الكرام بالسجود لآدم فاستكبر ، ورأى لنفسِهِ الدنيَّةِ شُفوفاً على مَنْ فَضَّلَهُ المولى تبارك وتعالى (١) ، وأدركَهُ الزَّهْوُ والإعجاب بما ليسَ لهُ ولا يستحقُهُ ؟! وإنَّما هو محض فضلِ مِنَ المولى الكريم تبارك وتعالى ، وأخذ بجهله وقلة عقله وعدم حيائِهِ وسابقِ شقائِهِ يعترِضُ على مَنْ لا شريكَ لهُ في مُلْكِهِ ولا في حُكْمِهِ ، يحكم بما يشاءُ ، ويَخُصُّ مَنْ يَشَاءُ بما يشاءُ ، لا اعتراض عليه ، ولا سؤال لأحدٍ عليه ، وهو الحكيم المحمود على كلِّ حالٍ . ويجب على كلِّ مؤمن : أن يقتفي آثار الطاهرين المطهَّرينَ مِنْ كُلِّ حمق ودنس ؛ مِنْ رُسُلِ اللهِ تعالى وملائكتِهِ الكرام صلَّى الله وسلَّمَ على جميعهم ؛ فيتواضع الله تعالى ، ويُعلِّمَ كُلَّ مَنْ رأَى مِنَ المولى العظيمِ إيثاراً له وتفضيلاً بخاصَّةٍ ؛ مِنْ علم أو عبادة أو خُلُقٍ جميل ، ولا يجعل ما خَصَّهُ هو به مولانا جلَّ وعلا مِنْ فضل مانعاً مِنَ التواضع لذوي الفضل ، والتعظيم لجنابِهِمُ الرفيع عندَ اللهِ تعالى ، فيَهلِكَ ويُسلَبَ مِنْ فضلِهِ ومِنْ كلّ خيرٍ ، كما هلك بذلك قدوتُه إبليس اللعين ، عافانا الله تعالى إلى المماتِ ممَّا ابْتُلِيَ  ِبهِ ، بجاه نبيه وأشرف خلقِهِ سيدنا ومولانا محمد صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ .
التواضعُ لَمَنْ عظَمَهُ اللهُ تعالى ]
ولينظر العاقل إلى ما فعله كليمُ اللهِ تعالى صلواتُ اللهِ وسلامه عليهِ معَ الخضر عليه السلام ؛ عندما سمعَ مِنَ المولى العظيم تبارك وتعالى أنَّهُ خَصَّهُ بعلمٍ مِنْ لدنْهُ ؛ مِنْ إتعابِ نفسه الشريفة بالسفر إليه حتى لقيَهُ ، ثم تواضعَ له في الكلام ، والتمس منه أن يُعلِّمَهُ بصيغة الاستفهام ، لا بصيغة الأمرِ المستعملة في الإيجاب والاستعلاء ، فقالَ عليه الصلاة والسلامُ : هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلَى أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا [الكهف : ٦٦] ، فالتمس منه بطريقِ الأدب في العبارة أن يكون تابعاً لهُ متعلماً منه ، ثمَّ لمَّا قابلَهُ الخضرُ عليه السلام بأن أغلظَ لَهُ في القولِ ؛ إذ وصفه بعدم استطاعةِ الصبرِ معَهُ .. جاوبه هو عليه الصلاةُ والسلامُ بتواضع ولين ، والتزمَ لهُ أنْ يطيعه في كل ما يأمرُهُ بهِ ، كما هو شأنُ العبدِ معَ سيْدِهِ ؛ فقال عليه الصلاة والسلام : ( سَتَجِدُنِي إِن شَاءَ اللهُ صَابِرًا وَلَا أَعْصِي لَكَ أَمْرًا ﴾ [الكهف : ٦٩]
هذا التواضع وقعَ مِنْ هذا السيّدِ فِي عِلْمٍ لم يُضطَرَّ إِليهِ في ظاهرِهِ ولا في باطنِهِ ، وله الفضل العظيم والرتبةُ الفائقة من اصطفاء مولانا جلَّ وعزَّ له على الناس برسالتِهِ ، ومناجاتِهِ لهُ بلا واسطةٍ بكلامِهِ القديم
And peace; in compliance with the command of our Master, Glorified and Exalted, and magnifying what He magnified, and honoring what He honored, and loving what He loved. And where are these noble and pure morals compared to the character of Iblis, the foolish and deprived, when the Great Master commanded him along with the noble angels to prostrate to Adam, and he was arrogant, and saw for his lowly self a superiority over the one whom the Master, Blessed and Exalted, had favored (1), and pride and self-admiration overtook him for what was not his and he did not deserve?! And it is merely pure favor from the Generous Master, Blessed and Exalted, and he took, in his ignorance, lack of intellect, shamelessness, and previous misery, to objecting to the One who has no partner in His kingdom nor in His judgment, who judges as He wills, and singles out whom He wills with what He wills, no objection upon Him, nor question from anyone upon Him, and He is the Wise, the Praised in all circumstances. And it is obligatory upon every believer to follow the traces of the pure, the purified from every foolishness and filth, from the messengers of Allah Almighty and His noble angels, may Allah bless and grant peace to all of them; so that he humbles himself to Allah Almighty, and teaches everyone who sees from the Great Master a preference and distinction for him with a special quality, be it knowledge, worship, or beautiful character, and does not make what our Master, Glorified and Exalted, has singled him out with of favor a preventer from humility toward those of merit, and magnifying their lofty station before Allah Almighty, lest he perish and be stripped of His favor and all good, just as his role model, the accursed Iblis, perished from that. May Allah Almighty preserve us until death from what afflicted him, by the rank of His Prophet and the most honorable of His creation, our Master and Protector Muhammad, may Allah bless him and grant him peace. [Humility toward those whom Allah Almighty has magnified] And let the intelligent person look at what the interlocutor of Allah Almighty (Moses), peace and blessings be upon him, did with al-Khidr, peace be upon him, when he heard from the Great Master, Blessed and Exalted, that He had singled him out with knowledge from His own presence: he tired his noble self by traveling to him until he met him, then he humbled himself to him in speech, and asked him to teach him in the form of a question, not in the form of a command used in obligation and superiority, so he, peace and blessings be upon him, said: "Shall I follow you so that you teach me something of that which you have been taught of sound judgment?" [Al-Kahf: 66]. So he asked him, in the manner of politeness in expression, to be a follower of his, learning from him. Then when al-Khidr, peace be upon him, responded to him by being harsh in speech, as he described him as unable to be patient with him... he, peace and blessings be upon him, answered him with humility and gentleness, and committed to him that he would obey him in everything he commands him, just as is the manner of a slave with his master; so he, peace and blessings be upon him, said: "You will find me, if Allah wills, patient, and I will not disobey you in any matter" [Al-Kahf: 69]. This humility occurred from this master (Moses) concerning knowledge which he was not compelled to seek either externally or internally, and he has great virtue and an exalted rank from the selection of our Master, Glorified and Exalted, for him over people through His message and His speaking to him without intermediary with His eternal speech.
Source pages p. 122

Commentary
This passage is a moral exhortation on humility (tawāḍuʿ), using the contrast between Iblis and the prophets, especially Moses (Musa) and al-Khidr. It is not a doctrinal definition but a hortatory piece from a Sunni theological work. The author begins by praising humility as obedience to Allah and then condemns Iblis's arrogance: when commanded to prostrate to Adam, Iblis considered himself superior, even though any merit is purely from Allah. His objection to Allah's command stemmed from ignorance and folly. The author then states that every believer must follow the prophets and angels in humility, and not allow Allah's favors to make them arrogant. The story of Musa and al-Khidr is presented as a prime example: when Musa learned that al-Khidr had special knowledge, he traveled to him and asked to learn using a polite request (question form), not a command. When al-Khidr spoke harshly, Musa responded with gentleness and pledged obedience. The author notes that Musa's humility was even more remarkable because he was a great prophet and not compelled to seek that knowledge. The passage concludes by acknowledging Musa's exalted rank as one spoken to by Allah directly.

Key Terms
التواضع
الاستكبار
إبليس
موسى عليه السلام
الخضر عليه السلام

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some might think that seeking knowledge from a lesser-known teacher diminishes one's own status.
Correction: The story of Musa and al-Khidr shows the opposite: even the greatest prophet humbly sought knowledge from someone whom Allah had favored with special knowledge. True honor lies in humility.

Checkpoint
What form of request did Musa use when asking al-Khidr to teach him?
A) A command
B) A question (استفهام)
C) A silent gesture
D) A written request

Key Takeaway
Humility is essential for believers; arrogance leads to loss, as seen in Iblis, while humility brings blessing, as seen in Musa.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Is there a specific person or group you secretly feel superior to because of your knowledge, worship, or status? How does Iblis' fall warn you that this feeling could strip you of Allah's favor? What will you do this week to actively humble yourself before them?
?
Musa traveled far and humbled himself to learn from al-Khidr. What is one area of your life where pride has prevented you from seeking guidance or correction? Will you reach out to someone today to learn, even if it means swallowing your pride?
?
Objecting to Allah's decree is a form of arrogance. What is a situation you have been complaining about in your heart? How can you instead embrace it with the humility of Musa, trusting that Allah's judgment is wise?

Ruling on Wishing for Death
Sanusi clarifies the ruling on wishing for death and its conditions.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the ruling on wishing for death and the specific conditions that make it permissible.

Core Idea
Wishing for death is permissible under specific conditions, not always forbidden.

Sanusi addresses a common concern: is it ever allowed to wish for death? He clarifies that it is neither obligatory nor impossible, but permissible when motivated by fear of greater harm, longing for Allah, or hope for immense reward. This passage equips you to distinguish between prohibited despair and legitimate spiritual desire.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once and then the translation. Notice the concise wording and the footnote markers referring to additional sources.
The passage is dense; read slowly to grasp the legal nuance.
2
Identify the three conditions
Look for the three reasons Sanusi gives for the permissibility of wishing for death: fear of worse calamity, yearning, and hope for a great reward that only comes through death.
Each condition is introduced by "aw" (or), showing alternatives.
3
Understand the self-evident nature
Sanusi says that knowing the permissibility for someone with these causes is "daruriyya" (self-evident). Reflect on why this is obvious to the one experiencing such feelings.
This underscores that no deep reasoning is needed once the motives are genuine.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
لنفسه ليس بواجب ولا مستحيل (١) ، بل يصح وجودُهُ إِنْ خَافَ مِنَ المصائب ما هو أشدُّ منه ، أو اشتاق ، أو رجا شيئاً عظيماً لا يحصل له إلا به (٢) وأما معرفةُ جواز تمنِّيها في حقٌّ مَنِ اتَّصف بأسباب ذلك خوفاً أو رجاءً أو اشتياقاً : فهي ضرورية لا تحتاج إلى تأمل ، لكنَّ المثال المقصود منه التقريبُ ، فيصحُ التمثيل بما وُجِدَ على الجملة أو قُدِّرَ وجوده ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
For oneself, it is not obligatory nor impossible (1), rather its existence is valid if one fears from calamities what is more severe, or yearns, or hopes for something great that cannot be attained except through it (2). As for knowing the permissibility of wishing for it concerning one who is characterized by the causes of that—fear, hope, or yearning—it is necessary (daruriyya) and does not require reflection, but the example intended is for approximation, so it is valid to give an example with what exists in general or what is imagined to exist. And with Allah Almighty is success.
Source pages p. 140

Commentary
This passage defines the ruling on wishing for death (tamannī al-mawt). The author states that it is neither obligatory (wajib) nor impossible (mustahil) in itself, but rather permissible (ja'iz) under specific conditions. He then gives three conditions: if one fears more severe calamities, or yearns for something (like meeting Allah), or hopes for a great reward that cannot be obtained except through death. The author then emphasizes that knowing the permissibility for someone who has these causes is self-evident (daruriyya) and does not require deep reflection. He notes that the example is only for approximation, so it is valid to illustrate with actual or hypothetical cases.

Key Terms
تمني الموت
واجب
مستحيل
جائز
ضروري

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Some think wishing for death is always forbidden based on hadith prohibiting it.
Correction: Sanusi clarifies that it is permissible under specific conditions (fear of greater harm, yearning, hope for great reward), so it is not universally forbidden.

Checkpoint
What is the ruling on wishing for death for oneself according to Sanusi?
A) Forbidden
B) Obligatory
C) Permissible under specific conditions
D) Recommended

Key Takeaway
Wishing for death is permissible when motivated by fear of greater calamity, longing for Allah, or hope for immense reward, and this knowledge is self-evident for those with such motives.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Of the three motives—fear of greater calamity, yearning for Allah, or hope for immense reward—which one resonates most with your current spiritual state? How does that affect your view of death?
?
Sanusi says that for someone genuinely experiencing those motives, the permissibility is self-evident. Do you find that to be true in your own heart? If not, what does that reveal about your iman and certainty regarding the afterlife?
?
Think of a trial you have faced. Did the thought of wishing for death cross your mind? Was it rooted in fear of more severe trials, or in longing for Allah? How would knowing this ruling have changed your perspective at that moment?

Concluding Supplication and Prayer for Acceptance
Sanusi concludes the book with a prayer and request for divine reward.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the author's final prayer and the theological virtues he seeks for his work.

Core Idea
The author humbly prays for his work to be sincere, beneficial, and a light on the Day of Judgment.

Sanusi concludes his commentary not with a doctrinal summary but with a heartfelt prayer. This final passage reveals the humility of the scholar asking for divine acceptance and intercession. It also reminds us that the ultimate purpose of knowledge is sincere devotion to Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the prayerful passage
Read the Arabic passage and its translation, noting the tone of humility and hope.
2
Identify the key requests
List what the author asks for: sincerity, benefit, light on Judgment Day, and intercession through the Prophet.
3
Reflect on the purpose
Consider why a scholar ends his work with prayer rather than a statement of accomplishment.
This reflects the Islamic view that all success is from Allah.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
وليكن هذا آخر ما قصدناهُ مِنْ هذا الشرح المباركِ إِنْ شاءَ الله ·
والحمد الله تعالى على ما منّ بهِ مِنْ بَدْءِ ذلك وإتمامِهِ ، نسألُهُ سبحانه أن يجعلَه خالصاً لوجهه ، نافعاً لنا ولكلِّ مَنِ اجتهد في تحصيله يوم لا ينفع مال ولا بنون ، وأن يجعلهُ نوراً يسعى بين أيدينا وأيديهم إلى جنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ ، معَ الآباءِ والأُمَّهاتِ ، والإخوة والذريَّةِ والأحبَّةِ ، مَنْ كانَ منهم في الماضي والحالِ ومَنْ سيكونُ ، بجاهِ نبيِّهِ وأشرف خلقِهِ سيّدنا ونبينا ومولانا محمَّدٍ صلَّى اللهُ عليهِ وسلَّمَ وعلى آله وصحبه ، صلاة وسلاماً نأمنُ بهما في كل موطنٍ يَخافُ فِيهِ أَمثالُنا أهل الجرائم المذنبون
And let this be the end of what we intended from this blessed commentary, God willing. And praise be to God Almighty for what He bestowed from its beginning and its completion. We ask Him, exalted, to make it sincere for His face, beneficial to us and to everyone who strives in its acquisition on a day when neither wealth nor children avail, and to make it a light proceeding before us and before them to the Gardens of Eden, with the fathers and mothers, brothers, offspring, and loved ones, whoever of them were in the past and present and whoever will be, by the standing of His Prophet and the most honorable of His creation, our master, our Prophet, our protector Muhammad, may God bless him and grant him peace, and upon his family and companions, a prayer and peace by which we are safe in every place where our likes, the people of crimes and sinners, fear.
Source pages p. 254

Commentary
This passage is the concluding prayer of the book. The author asks that the commentary be accepted by God, be sincere, beneficial, and a light on the Day of Judgment. He includes intercession through the Prophet Muhammad and a general prayer for all believers. It ends the work on a note of humility and hope for divine mercy.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

How will you ensure your knowledge becomes a light?

The prayer ends with 'أهل الجرائم المذنبون' — the author counts himself among sinners. Do you truly see yourself that way? If you did, how would your prayers for acceptance change tonight?

Faith and Disbelief
beginner

Certainty Alone Is Not Enough: Conjecture, Doubt, and False Certainty
Sanusi rules out conjecture, doubt, misgiving, and incorrect certainty.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that valid faith requires both certainty (jazzm) and correctness (muṭābaqa), and that conjecture, doubt, misgiving, or mistaken certainty are insufficient by scholarly consensus.

Core Idea
Certainty alone is not enough—it must be correct certainty.

Sanusi now addresses a crucial refinement: what kind of knowledge suffices for the obligations of faith? He rules out not only weak forms like conjecture and doubt but also confidently held false beliefs. This passage establishes that saving faith requires both the quality of certainty and the truth of its object.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Let the author's two-part structure become clear.
Notice the consensus language (bi-ijmāʿ) at the end of each part.
2
Identify the two scenarios ruled out
The passage first rules out conjecture, doubt, and misgiving. Then it rules out certainty that does not match reality. Underline both conditions.
The second scenario uses the example of disbelievers who are certain of falsehood.
3
Understand the consensus argument
Sanusi uses 'by consensus' (bi-ijmāʿ) to show that this ruling is not his personal opinion but the agreed position of the scholars.
Consensus gives the ruling authority beyond individual reasoning.
4
Reflect on the implication for faith
Ask yourself: Why is it not enough to just feel certain? What does this teach us about the nature of true faith?
Faith is not just psychological conviction; it must correspond to objective truth.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فلو لم يحصل للمكلَّف الجزم بهذهِ الثلاثة في حق الله تعالى وفي حق رسله عليهم الصلاة والسلام ، بل إنّما حصل له الظن أو الشك أو الوهم . . لم يكفِهِ ذلكَ بإجماع . ولو حصل له الجزمُ ، إلا أنَّهُ غير مطابقِ لِمَا في نفس الأمرِ ؛ كجزم اليهود والنصارى وسائرِ الكَفَرَةِ بالكفريَّاتِ التي جزموا بها . . لم يكفِ ولم يُعذَرْ بهِ إجماعاً .
If the legally responsible person does not obtain certainty (jazzm) concerning these three matters regarding God Most High and regarding His messengers, peace and blessings be upon them, but rather only obtains conjecture (dhann), doubt (shakk), or misgiving (wahm), that will not suffice him, by consensus. And if he obtains certainty, but it does not conform to what is actually the case—like the certainty of the Jews, Christians, and all the disbelievers in the disbeliefs they were certain about—that will not suffice him, nor will he be excused for it, by consensus.
Source pages p. 129

Commentary
This passage clarifies that mere certainty is not enough; it must be correct certainty. The author first states that if a person only has conjecture (dhann), doubt (shakk), or misgiving (wahm) regarding the required beliefs about God and His messengers, that is insufficient by scholarly consensus. Then he adds that even if they have certainty (jazzm), but it does not match reality—as in the example of Jews, Christians, and other disbelievers who hold false beliefs with certainty—such certainty is also insufficient and they are not excused, again by consensus. This underscores that valid faith requires both the quality of certainty and the correctness of its object.

Key Terms
الظن
الشك
الوهم
الجزم الغير مطابق
الكفريّات

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Any strong conviction is enough for salvation.
Correction: Sanusi shows that false certainty (like that of disbelievers) is not sufficient and not excused. True faith requires both certainty and correctness.

Checkpoint
What is the consensus ruling on one who has certainty but it does not match reality?
A) It suffices because certainty is the key
B) It does not suffice and he is not excused
C) It suffices if he is sincere
D) It depends on the person's effort

Key Takeaway
True saving faith requires certainty that conforms to reality; mere subjective conviction is not enough.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
You may be certain that you are on the right path in your beliefs—but is your certainty based on truth or on comfort? Have you ever considered that your strongest convictions might be false, like those of the disbelievers mentioned? This lesson demands that you test your certainty against objective truth. What are you doing to ensure your faith is not just sincere but correct?
?
When you learn about other religions, do you feel a sense of superiority because of your certainty? But this lesson warns that certainty alone is not enough. How can you be sure that your certainty is not like that of the Jews and Christians, who were certain but wrong? Have you humbly sought the truth with an open heart, or have you simply inherited your faith?
?
Think of an area in your life where you are merely 'convinced' but not certain—maybe a practice or belief you have doubts about. This lesson says conjecture, doubt, and misgiving are insufficient for faith. Are you willing to face your doubts and seek certainty that conforms to reality? What step will you take this week to strengthen your conviction with sound knowledge?

Faith and Disbelief
beginner

Is Knowledge Itself Faith or Its Prerequisite?
Sanusi contrasts two kalam positions on the relation between knowledge and faith.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two kalam views on whether knowledge of God and His messengers is identical to faith or merely a prerequisite that leads to inner assent.

Core Idea
Faith is not just knowledge; it is inner assent that follows knowledge.

In this passage, Sanusi addresses a key question in kalam: does the knowledge we gain through rational proofs about God and His messengers constitute faith itself, or is faith something else that comes after? The Ash'ari school equates knowledge with faith, while al-Bāqillānī distinguishes knowledge as a necessary condition for the inner speech of faith. Let's explore both positions.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the two positions presented.
2
Identify the two schools
Find the two views: the Ash'ari view (that knowledge is faith) and the Qadi's view (that knowledge is a necessary condition, and faith is inner speech).
The Qadi is Abu Bakr al-Baqillani.
3
Reflect on the linguistic argument
Notice that some imams preferred the Qadi's view because it aligns with the Arabic meaning of iman as 'assent'.
Think about why linguistic appropriateness matters in theology.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثمَّ المعرفة بهذهِ الثلاثةِ في حقٌّ الله تعالى وفي حقٌّ رسلِهِ عليهم الصلاة والسلام : هل هي نفس الإيمان الذي كُلفنا بهِ ؛ وهو مذهبُ الأشعري ، أو ملزومةٌ للإيمان، فيكون الإيمان هو حديث النفس التابع لتلك المعرفةِ ؛ وهو مذهب القاضي ، وصحَّحَهُ بعضُ الأئمةِ ؛ لأنَّهُ أنسبُ لمعنى الإيمانِ لغةً ، وبالله تعالى التوفيق
Then, the knowledge (maʿrifa) of these three things with respect to God Almighty and with respect to His messengers (peace and blessings be upon them): Is it the very faith (īmān) that we are obligated to have? — That is the school of al-Ashʿarī. Or is it a necessary condition (malzūma) for faith, so that faith is the inner speech (ḥadīth al-nafs) that follows that knowledge? That is the school of al-Qāḍī [Abū Bakr al-Bāqillānī], and some imams deemed it correct because it is more appropriate to the linguistic meaning of faith (īmān). And with God is success.
Source pages p. 131

Commentary
This passage raises the question of whether the knowledge (maʿrifa) of the three fundamental matters concerning God and His messengers—i.e., the knowledge that results from the rational proofs discussed earlier—is identical to the faith (īmān) that humans are commanded to have, or whether it is something separate that is a prerequisite. The text presents two views: 1) The school of al-Ashʿarī holds that this knowledge is itself faith; i.e., faith is essentially knowledge. 2) The school of al-Qāḍī Abū Bakr al-Bāqillānī holds that knowledge is a 'necessary condition' (malzūma) for faith, and that faith is actually 'inner speech' (ḥadīth al-nafs) which follows that knowledge. Some later imams preferred al-Qāḍī's view because it aligns better with the linguistic meaning of īmān as 'assent' or 'acquiescence' (taṣdīq). The author does not decisively choose but notes the latter was corrected by some. (A footnote on the page corrects 'malzūma' to 'lāzima' [necessary concomitant], indicating that knowledge is a concomitant of faith, not a cause.) Sanusi concludes with 'And with God is success', leaving the issue open but indicating the linguistic reasoning for the Qāḍī's view.

Key Terms
المعرفة (al-maʿrifa)
الإيمان (al-īmān)
حديث النفس (ḥadīth al-nafs)
ملزومة (malzūma) / لازمة (lāzima)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Faith is merely intellectual assent without any inner speech.
Correction: The discussion clarifies that even if faith is more than knowledge, it involves inner speech (ḥadīth al-nafs) which is an act of the heart.

Checkpoint
According to al-Qāḍī's school, what is the relationship between knowledge and faith?
A) Knowledge is identical to faith.
B) Knowledge is a necessary condition for faith, and faith is inner speech following knowledge.
C) Faith is knowledge plus action.
D) Knowledge and faith are unrelated.

Key Takeaway
The relationship between knowledge and faith is debated: for al-Ashʿarī they are identical, while for al-Bāqillānī knowledge is a prerequisite for the inner speech of faith.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you recite the shahada with your tongue, do you also feel an inner speech of assent in your heart, or have you reduced faith to mere knowledge and outward words? What would it take for your inner speech to align fully with your knowledge today?
?
If faith is truly inner assent that follows knowledge, then what are the signs that your knowledge has actually produced that assent? Is there a gap between what you know about Allah and what your heart affirms? Pray for inner certainty.
?
You know that Allah is your Lord and that you must obey Him—but knowing alone does not save you. Have you allowed that knowledge to transform into a living, inner commitment that changes how you live this day? If not, what one step will you take to move from knowledge to inner assent?

Faith and Disbelief
beginner
5 min
Knowledge and Faith: Identity or Concomitance?
Sanusi presents the debate on maʿrifa and īmān with a terminological correction.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the two main positions on the relationship between knowledge of God and faith, and the correct terminology regarding causality.

Core Idea
Knowledge of God is a necessary result of faith, not its cause.

The relationship between knowledge (maʿrifa) and faith (īmān) is a subtle but important point in Islamic theology. In this passage, Sanusi presents two competing views and then corrects a common terminological error. Understanding this distinction helps clarify the nature of faith and its prerequisites.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once, then the English translation. Notice the technical terms معرفة and إيمان.
The passage includes a footnote with a crucial correction.
2
Identify the two views
Locate al-Ash'ari's view (knowledge = faith) and al-Qāḍī's view (knowledge is the source, faith is inner speech). Ask yourself what difference this makes.
3
Understand the correction
Read the footnote carefully. It corrects the terminology: knowledge is a necessary concomitant (lāzima) of faith, not its source (malzūma). Reflect on the Quranic evidence given.
This corrects a logical misunderstanding.
4
Apply to the broader context
Consider how this relates to the definition of faith and its opposites. The passage also mentions general infirmities that negate perception.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
هل المعرفة بالمذكورات هي الإيمان نفسه أو لازمةٌ عنه؟ ثمَّ المعرفة بهذهِ الثلاثةِ في حقٌّ الله تعالى وفي حقٌّ رسلِهِ عليهم الصلاة والسلام: هل هي نفس الإيمان الذي كُلفنا بهِ؛ وهو مذهبُ الأشعري، أو ملزومةٌ للإيمان (1)، فيكون الإيمان هو حديث النفس التابع لتلك المعرفةِ؛ وهو مذهب القاضي، وصحَّحَهُ بعضُ الأئمةِ؛ لأنَّهُ أنسبُ لمعنى الإيمانِ لغةً، وبالله تعالى التوفيق. ويضادُّهُ، والآفة العامة: كالموت والإغماء والنوم والجنون؛ مما ينافي الإدراك للمطلوب. (1) عبَّرَ في (الوسطى) (ص150) بلفظ: (لازمة) لا (ملزومة)، وهو الصواب، وفي هامش (و): (صوابه: أو لازمة؛ إذ المعرفة المذكورة لازمة للإيمان، لا ملزومةٌ له؛ لما أنَّ الإيمان بمعنى التصديق والإذعان إذا وجد لزمته تلك المعرفةُ، ولا يلزم من حصول تلك المعرفة فقط الإيمان، ألا ترى إلى قوله تعالى: ﴿ وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنفُسُهُمْ ﴾ [النمل: 14]، فإن المعرفة وجدت فيهم ولم يلزمها الإيمان، ولو كانت المعرفة ملزومة . . لاستحال تخلفُ الإيمان حينئذ؛ لأن تخلف اللازم عن ملزومه محال عقلاً).
Is knowledge of the aforementioned [three judgments] itself faith or a concomitant of it? Then knowledge of these three concerning Allah Almighty and concerning His messengers, peace be upon them: is it the very faith with which we are charged? That is the doctrine of al-Ash'ari. Or is it that which has faith as a concomitant (malzūma) (1), so that faith is the inner speech following that knowledge? That is the doctrine of al-Qāḍī, and some imams deemed it correct because it is more suitable to the linguistic meaning of faith. And with Allah is success. And it is opposed by general infirmities such as death, unconsciousness, sleep, and madness, which negate perception of the required [knowledge]. (1) He expressed in al-Wusṭā (p. 150) with the term (lāzima) not (malzūma), and that is correct. And in the margin of (w): (Its correction is: or a concomitant (lāzima), for the aforementioned knowledge is a concomitant of faith, not that which has faith as a concomitant, because faith in the sense of affirmation (taṣdīq) and submission, when it exists, that knowledge necessarily accompanies it, but from the mere presence of that knowledge, faith does not necessarily follow. Do you not see the saying of the Exalted: 'And they rejected them, while their souls were convinced of them' [Quran 27:14]? For knowledge existed in them but faith did not follow it. If knowledge were that which has faith as a concomitant (malzūma), then faith would be impossible to be absent when it is present, because the absence of the concomitant (lāzim) from its source (malzūm) is intellectually impossible.)
Source pages p. 131

Commentary
This passage addresses the relationship between maʿrifa (knowledge of the three rational judgments concerning God and His messengers) and īmān (faith). Two views are presented: (1) Al-Ash'ari's view that this knowledge is itself the faith that is required. (2) Al-Qāḍī's view (probably al-Bāqillānī) that this knowledge is the source (malzūma) from which faith necessarily arises, with faith being the inner speech (ḥadīth al-nafs) that follows that knowledge. The author notes that some imams corrected this view as more linguistically appropriate. The footnotes correct the terminology: the knowledge is a concomitant (lāzima) of faith, not its source (malzūma). Proof: faith (as affirmation and submission) necessitates knowledge, but knowledge does not necessitate faith, as shown by the Quranic verse where the disbelievers had certain knowledge yet did not have faith. The passage also briefly mentions what opposes faith: general infirmities (death, unconsciousness, sleep, madness) that negate the perception of the required knowledge.

Key Terms
معرفة
إيمان
لازم
ملزوم
حديث النفس

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: That knowledge of God is the cause of faith, meaning if you have knowledge you must have faith.
Correction: Knowledge is a necessary concomitant of faith; faith is the cause, knowledge is the effect. The disbelievers in the Quran had knowledge but no faith.

Checkpoint
According to the corrected view in the footnote, what is the relationship between knowledge of God and faith?
A) Knowledge is the source (malzūma) and faith is its concomitant (lāzima).
B) Knowledge is the concomitant (lāzima) of faith.
C) Knowledge is identical to faith.
D) Knowledge and faith are unrelated.

Key Takeaway
Knowledge of God is a necessary concomitant of genuine faith, not its cause.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Do you sometimes assume that knowing about God is enough, while your heart remains unmoved? How does this lesson challenge that assumption and what will you do today to move from knowledge to true faith?
?
The Quran says the disbelievers 'knew' but rejected. Are there areas of your life where you know the truth but resist submitting to it? What is one truth you will actively submit to starting now?
?
When you recite the Shahada, is it merely intellectual assent or does it produce the inner speech (hadith al-nafs) of genuine faith? How can you ensure your faith is not just knowledge but a deep-seated conviction that changes your actions?

Causality and Created Things
beginner
4 min
Knowledge Is Created by God, Not by the Proof
Sanusi denies causality of proof and affirms divine creation of knowledge.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that in Ash'ari theology, knowledge obtained through demonstration is directly created by God, not caused by the proof or human effort, and you will be able to distinguish this view from the philosophical and Mu'tazilite positions.

Core Idea
God alone creates knowledge; demonstration is merely the occasion, not the cause.

Sanusi here addresses a crucial point: after we have used a proof to arrive at knowledge, we must not attribute that knowledge to the proof itself. Rather, we must affirm that God creates both the understanding of the proof and the understanding of its conclusion. This passage safeguards the principle of divine sovereignty over all acts of cognition, rejecting any notion of secondary causality in the realm of knowledge.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage and translation
Read the Arabic passage once without analysis, then the English translation. Let the author's words establish the framework.
2
Identify the two rejected theories
Look for the phrases 'لا بطريق التعليل' and 'لا بطريق التولد'. Understand how the philosophers and Mu'tazilites explain knowledge differently.
The philosophers attribute knowledge to causation (ta'lil), the Mu'tazilites to generation (tawallud) from human action.
3
Understand the positive Ash'ari position
Focus on the statement that knowledge comes 'بمحض خلق الله فضلاً منه' and that God creates both understanding of the evidence and the thing evidenced. No other agent shares in this.
The word 'فضل' emphasizes that knowledge is a grace from God, not earned.
4
Reflect on implications
Consider why this distinction matters: if God alone creates knowledge, then human reasoning is a context but not a cause, preserving divine omnipotence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثمَّ يجب عليهِ إذا حصلَتْ له تلك المعرفة بواسطة البرهان أن يقطع بأنَّ تلك المعرفة إنَّما حصلَتْ بمحض خلْقِ اللهِ فضلاً منه سبحانَهُ ولا أثر للبرهان ولا لفكرة المكلَّف وبحثِهِ في حصولها ؛ لا بطريقِ التعليل كما يقولُ الفلاسفة ، ولا بطريق التولد كما تقول المعتزلة ، وإنَّما المولى الكريم تبارك وتعالى هو الذي مَنَّ بفضلِهِ بِخَلْقِ فَهْمِ الدليل ، وخلْقِ فهم المدلول عليهِ إثرَهُ ، لا شريكَ لهُ في ذلكِ ألبتةَ
Then, when that knowledge has been obtained by him through the medium of demonstration, he must be certain that that knowledge was obtained only by the sheer creation of God, as a favor from Him—exalted is He—and there is no effect of the demonstration nor of the thought and investigation of the legally responsible person in its attainment; not by way of causation (ta'lil) as the philosophers say, nor by way of generation (tawallud) as the Mu'tazilites say. Rather, the generous Master, blessed and exalted, is the One who graciously bestowed by creating the understanding of the evidence and creating the understanding of the thing evidenced after it. He has no partner whatsoever in that.
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage specifies the theological framework for how knowledge (ma'rifa) is attained through demonstration. After one obtains knowledge through a proof, he must affirm that this knowledge was created directly by God as a grace (fadl), and that the demonstration itself, as well as the person's own thought and investigation, have no causal effect (athar) in producing that knowledge. The author explicitly rejects two alternative views: (1) the philosophers' theory of causation (ta'lil), where the proof causes knowledge; and (2) the Mu'tazilites' theory of generation (tawallud), where human action generates knowledge. Instead, the true position is that God alone creates both the understanding of the evidence (dalil) and the understanding of the thing evidenced (madlul) in the mind of the seeker, with no partner. This upholds absolute divine sovereignty over all acts of cognition.

Key Terms
البرهان
التعليل
التولد
الفلاسفة
المعتزلة

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The proof itself causes knowledge to arise in the mind.
Correction: The passage explicitly states that there is no effect (athar) of the demonstration; only God creates knowledge as a favor.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi, how does knowledge occur through a demonstration?
A) The demonstration causes the knowledge
B) Human effort generates the knowledge
C) God creates the knowledge after the demonstration as a favor
D) Both the proof and God share in causing it

Key Takeaway
Knowledge obtained through demonstration is a direct gift from God; neither the proof nor human effort has any causal role.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you finally grasp a difficult concept in your deen, do you ever silently congratulate your own intellect? How can you train your heart to immediately attribute that understanding to Allah's favor, as this passage demands?
?
If you truly believe that your own investigation and reasoning have no effect in producing knowledge, what practical change does that demand in your dua before studying? Will you begin to ask Allah to create understanding in you, rather than relying on your mental effort?
?
In which areas of your life do you subconsciously act like a philosopher or Mu'tazilite, thinking that your efforts cause results? How can you remind yourself daily that even the ability to reason is a gift created by Allah at every moment?

Knowledge Created by God, Not by Reasoning
Sanusi explains the divine creation of knowledge and rejects causal theories.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand that in Ash'ari theology, knowledge obtained through reasoning is directly created by God, with no causal role for the reasoning process itself.

Core Idea
Knowledge is God's direct creation, not caused by proof or thought.

Sanusi now clarifies the proper theological understanding of how knowledge arises when a person reasons. He insists that the proof and the person's effort have no effect; rather, God creates both the understanding of the proof and the conclusion. This passage rejects two false views: the philosophers' causation and the Mu'tazilites' generation.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then its translation. Notice the key negations: 'no effect of proof nor thought'.
2
Identify the two rejected theories
Find where Sanusi mentions the philosophers' causation (ta'lil) and the Mu'tazilites' generation (tawallud). Understand why both are wrong.
3
Grasp the positive Ash'ari position
Sanusi states that God creates the understanding of the proof and the understanding of the conclusion. This is the occasionalist doctrine.
4
Reflect on the implications
Consider how this affects the responsibility of the learner: reasoning is a necessary condition but not a cause.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
ثمَّ يجب عليهِ إذا حصلَتْ له تلك المعرفة بواسطة البرهان أن يقطع بأنَّ تلك المعرفة إنَّما حصلَتْ بمحض خلْقِ اللهِ فضلاً منه سبحانَهُ ولا أثر للبرهان ولا لفكرة المكلَّف وبحثِهِ في حصولها ؛ لا بطريقِ التعليل كما يقولُ الفلاسفة ، ولا بطريق التولد كما تقول المعتزلة ، وإنَّما المولى الكريم تبارك وتعالى هو الذي مَنَّ بفضلِهِ بِخَلْقِ فَهْمِ الدليل ، وخلْقِ فهم المدلول عليهِ إثرَهُ ، لا شريكَ لهُ في ذلكِ ألبتةَ
Then it is obligatory upon him, when that knowledge is obtained by him by means of proof, to be certain that that knowledge was obtained solely through the creation of God, as a grace from Him — exalted is He — and there is no effect of the proof nor of the thought and investigation of the responsible person in its occurrence; not by way of causation (taʿlīl) as the philosophers say, nor by way of generation (tawallud) as the Muʿtazila say. Rather, the Generous Lord — blessed and exalted is He — is the One who bestows by His grace the creation of understanding the proof, and the creation of understanding the thing proven (al-madlūl ʿalayhi) after it, with no partner at all in that.
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage continues the ruling on the muqallid and explains the proper theological account of how knowledge arises after reasoning. Sanusi states that once a person obtains knowledge through proof, he must be certain that the knowledge was created directly by God as a pure favor (faḍl), with no causal role played by the proof itself or by the person's own thinking and investigation. The author explicitly rejects two erroneous views: the philosophers' theory of causation (taʿlīl), which holds that the proof produces knowledge as a natural effect, and the Muʿtazilite theory of generation (tawallud), which holds that human acts generate other acts or effects. Instead, the only real agent is God, who creates both the understanding of the proof and the understanding of the thing proven as a consequence. This underscores Ashʿarī occasionalism: all effects are directly from God, and secondary causes are merely occasions.

Key Terms
التعليل (al-taʿlīl)
التولد (al-tawallud)
البرهان (al-burhān)
خلق (khalq)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Human reasoning directly causes knowledge to arise.
Correction: Sanusi teaches that reasoning has no causal effect; God creates knowledge directly.

Checkpoint

A) The proof itself
B) The person's effort
C) God's direct creation
D) Both proof and God's creation

Key Takeaway
Knowledge from reasoning is solely God's direct creation, with no causal effect from the reasoning process itself.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?

?

?
If you were absolutely certain that your ability to grasp proofs comes solely from God's grace and not from your own ability, would you still feel pride in your religious knowledge? What would happen to that pride if you took this lesson to heart today?

Knowledge After Proof: Customary or Rational Necessity?
Sanusi records the Ashʿarī and al-Juwaynī disagreement on the nature of knowledge after proof.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand the difference between customary necessity (lāzim ʿādatan) and rational necessity (lāzim ʿaqlan) in the context of knowledge after proof, and why al-Ash'ari's view is considered more apparent.

Core Idea
Al-Ash'ari: God's pattern is customary, not logically binding.

Sanusi now turns to a key debate among Ash'ari theologians: When God creates knowledge of a proof, does He necessarily create knowledge of the conclusion? This question touches on divine causality and whether God's actions are bound by rational necessity. In this passage, two positions are presented, and Sanusi gives his preferred view.

Guided Path
1
Read the passage carefully
Read the Arabic passage once without analyzing. Then read the translation. Notice the structure: a question, two opinions, and an author's preference.
2
Identify the two types of necessity
Look for the terms 'lāzim ʿādatan' (customarily necessary) and 'lāzim ʿaqlan' (rationally necessary). Understand the analogies: satiety after eating vs. accident with a body.
3
Note the condition of no impediment
The passage specifies 'without the occurrence of a specific or general impediment'. This sets the context for the disagreement.
4
Compare the two views
Al-Ash'ari says it is customarily necessary, so it is possible for knowledge to be delayed. Al-Juwaynī says it is rationally necessary, so it cannot be delayed. Contrast what each view implies about God's freedom.
5
Reflect on Sanusi's conclusion
Sanusi states that al-Ash'ari's view is 'aẓhar' (more apparent). Consider why he might favor the view that preserves God's customary pattern without logical compulsion.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
واختلف أئمَّتُنا : هل خَلْقُ اللهِ تعالى معرفة المدلولِ عقب خلقِهِ معرفة الدليل مِنْ غيرِ عُروض آفةٍ خاصَّةٍ ولا عامة . . لازم عادةً؛ كالشبع مع الأكل ، أو لازم عقلاً ؛ كالعَرَضِ معَ الجِرْم مثلاً ؟ فقال الشيخ الأشعري رضي الله عنه : هو لازمٌ عادةً ، فيصح التخلف. وقال إمام الحرمين : هو لازم عقلاً ، فلا يصح التخلُّفُ. والأظهرُ : ما قاله الأشعري ، والله تعالى أعلم .
And our imams differed: Is God's creation of knowledge of the thing proven (al-madlūl) after His creation of knowledge of the proof, without the occurrence of a specific impediment (āfa khāṣṣa) or a general impediment (āfa ʿāmma), a customary (ʿāda) necessary consequence, like satiety after eating, or a rational (ʿaqlan) necessary consequence, like an accident with a body, for example? So Shaykh al-Ashʿarī (may God be pleased with him) said: It is a customary necessary consequence, so it is possible for it to be delayed (takhalluf). And Imām al-Ḥaramayn [al-Juwaynī] said: It is a rational necessary consequence, so it is not possible for it to be delayed. And the more apparent is what al-Ashʿarī said, and God knows best.
Source pages p. 130

Commentary
This passage presents a theological disagreement among the Ashʿarī imams concerning the relationship between the creation of knowledge of the proof and the creation of knowledge of the thing proven. Specifically, after God creates understanding of the proof, does He then necessarily create knowledge of the conclusion? The author notes that this question applies when no impediment (āfa) is present, whether specific (like doubt or ignorance) or general (like death or unconsciousness). Two views are given: Al-Ashʿarī holds that the sequence is 'customarily necessary' (lāzim ʿādatan), meaning it is God's habitual pattern but He could, in principle, withhold the knowledge, as in the case of satiety normally following eating but sometimes not. Imām al-Ḥaramayn (al-Juwaynī) holds that it is 'rationally necessary' (lāzim ʿaqlan), meaning it is inconceivable for God to create knowledge of the proof without also creating knowledge of the conclusion, like an accident cannot exist without a body. Sanusi then states that the more apparent (aẓhar) opinion is that of al-Ashʿarī, thus favoring the occasionalist view that God's customary patterns are not logically binding.

Key Terms
لازم عادة (lāzim ʿādatan)
لازم عقلاً (lāzim ʿaqlan)
آفة خاصة (āfa khāṣṣa)
آفة عامة (āfa ʿāmma)

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: Customary necessity means it must happen.
Correction: Customary necessity allows for exceptions; it is God's habitual pattern but not logically binding.

Checkpoint
What is the difference between al-Ashʿarī's and al-Juwaynī's positions on the creation of knowledge after proof?
A) Al-Ashʿarī says it is rationally necessary; al-Juwaynī says customary.
B) Al-Ashʿarī says it is customary; al-Juwaynī says rationally necessary.
C) Both say it is rationally necessary.
D) Al-Ashʿarī says it is not necessary at all.

Key Takeaway
Al-Ash'ari's view that knowledge after proof is a customary necessity affirms God's freedom and that His patterns are not logically binding.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you reach a firm conclusion about God based on reasoning, do you treat that knowledge as something God is obliged to give you, or do you recognize it as a gift from His custom? How does that awareness affect your humility before Him?
?
Al-Ash'ari teaches that even after understanding a clear proof, God could withhold the knowledge of the conclusion. Does this possibility unsettle you? Are you relying on your own reasoning or on God's mercy for your certainty?
?
If every moment of understanding about God is a custom He maintains, have you ever thanked Him specifically for the very act of comprehension? Or do you assume it is your natural right? Consider praising Him for the light of guidance.

Causality and Created Things
intermediate

Sanusi's Refutation of Eternal World and Essential Causation
Sanusi refutes objections to divine agency.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand how Sanusi uses the diversity and temporal delay of the world to prove that it is created by a free agent, not by a necessary cause or nature.

Core Idea
Only a free agent can explain the world's variety and temporal beginning.

In this passage, Sanusi addresses two major objections against the creation of the world: that it could be eternal, or that it could be caused by a necessary essence or nature. He shows that these alternatives are impossible because they would require uniformity and simultaneity. The observable world, however, is full of diversity and temporal delay, which can only be explained by a free agent who chooses to create differently at different times.

Guided Path
1
Understand the objection
Read the opening objection carefully. Note that the objector suggests two alternatives to divine choice: the world could be eternal, or its preponderance could be by causation (ta‘līl) or nature (ṭab‘).
These are classical philosophical objections from those who deny a personal creator.
2
Analyze Sanusi's response
Sanusi argues that if the world were caused by a single cause or nature, its effects would be uniform and simultaneous. This is a logical necessity. Then he points to the observable diversity and temporal delay to contradict this.
Focus on the key terms: ‘illa (cause), ṭabī‘a (nature), athar (effect).
3
Connect to the conclusion
The conclusion is that all bodies must be created, because they all share the attribute of neediness for an agent. This refutes both the eternity of the world and natural/essential causation.
See how observation (mushāhada) is used as evidence.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
[ إبطال قدم العالم أو أن يكون مرجّح وجودِهِ طبعاً أو علةً ] فإن قلت : ما المانعُ مِنْ أن يكون ما سوى الله تعالى قديماً ، أو يكون الترجيح لوجودِ مقاديره وصفاته بطريق التعليل أو الطبع لا بطريق الاختيار ؟ فالجواب : أنَّهُ لو كانَ كذلكَ لَمَا اختلفَتْ مقاديره وصفاته ، ولَمَا تأخَّرَ منها شيءٌ عنِ الأَزلِ ؛ لأنَّ العِلَّةَ الواحدة والطبيعة الواحدة يستحيل اختلافُ آثارهما وتأخُرُ شيءٍ منها عن وجودهما ، والمشاهدة الضرورية تقضي بخلافِ ذلكَ ؛ فإنَّ اختلافها في مقاديرها وصفاتها كثير لا حصر لهُ ، وتأخُرَ جميعها عن الأزلِ معلوم على القطع ؛ لمشاهدة التأخُرِ في كثيرٍ مِنَ الأجرام وصفاتها اللازمة لها ، فوجبَ أَنْ يكون جميعها كذلك ؛ لوجوب استوائها في صفة الافتقار إلى الفاعل .
Refuting the eternity of the world or that its existence is preponderated by nature or essence. For if you say: What prevents that what is other than God be eternal, or that the preponderance of the existence of its magnitudes and attributes be by way of causation (ta‘līl) or nature (ṭab‘) rather than by way of choice (ikhtiyār)? The answer: That if it were so, its magnitudes and attributes would not differ, and nothing of them would be delayed from eternity; because a single cause (‘illa) and a single nature (ṭabī‘a) cannot have different effects nor can any effect be delayed from them. And necessary observation testifies to the contrary; for their differences in magnitudes and attributes are numerous beyond count, and the delay of all of them from eternity is known with certainty, because of observing delay in many bodies and their necessary attributes. So it is necessary that all of them are like that; due to their equal necessity in the attribute of neediness for an agent.
Source pages p. 143

Commentary
This passage is an objection-and-answer (su’āl wa-jawāb) refuting two possibilities: that the world could be eternal (qadīm) or that its preponderance could be by causation (‘illa) or nature (ṭab‘) rather than divine choice (ikhtiyār). Sanusi presents the objection: What prevents this? The answer is that if it were so, there would be no variation in magnitudes and attributes and no temporal delay from eternity, because a single cause or nature produces uniform and simultaneous effects. But observation (mushāhada) shows immense variation and temporal occurrence (ta’akhkhur), so the world cannot be caused by a single nature or essence. The delay of many bodies from eternity proves they are not produced by a necessary cause. Since all bodies share the property of being contingent (needing an agent), they must all be created. This refutes both the eternity of the world and natural/essential causation.

Key Terms
قدم
علة
طبع
اختيار
تعليل
أثر
مشاهدة
تأخر
افتقار

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: The world could be eternal or caused by a necessary essence/nature without a free agent.
Correction: Sanusi shows that necessity implies uniformity and simultaneity, contrary to observation, so a free agent is necessary.

Checkpoint
What is the main evidence Sanusi uses to refute the idea that the world is caused by a single nature or essence?
A) Philosophical arguments.
B) The observed diversity and temporal delay of bodies and attributes.
C) Consensus of scholars.
D) Divine revelation.

Key Takeaway
The observable diversity and temporal beginning of the world prove it is created by a free agent, not by a necessary cause.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
When you observe the incredible diversity in creation—in people, in circumstances, in your own life—do you truly believe that each detail is intentionally chosen by a free Creator, or is there a part of you that attributes things to blind fate or natural necessity? How does your daily satisfaction with God's decrees reveal the answer?
?
The argument shows that delay (ta'akhkhur) in creation is only possible if the Creator is a free agent. When you face a delay in your life—in marriage, in career, in relief—does your impatience reflect a belief that God should have acted instantly, as if He were a necessary cause? What does that reveal about your understanding of His free choice?
?
Sanusi says that if the world were caused by a single nature, all effects would be identical. Look at the people around you: are any two exactly alike? How does this undeniable diversity force you to confront the reality that your existence is not a product of impersonal forces but of a personal, choosing God? What would change in your relationship with Him if you truly lived this conviction?

Causality and Created Things
intermediate
6 min
Refutation of Natural Causality with Conditions
Sanusi rebuts the claim that natural causes need conditions and may be delayed.

Learning Objective
By the end of this lesson, you will understand why the delay of the world cannot be explained by natural causes that require conditions and the absence of obstacles, and how Sanusi demonstrates the impossibility of such a scenario.

Core Idea
If nature's effect depends on conditions and no obstacles, delay would be permanent, not temporary.

The objector tries to salvage natural causality by insisting that nature only produces its effect when conditions are present and obstacles are absent. This would allow for a delay in the world's creation. Sanusi responds by showing that if the obstacle or missing condition is eternal, it would never be removed, so the world would never come to exist. This refutation strengthens the argument that the world must be created directly by a free agent, Allah.

Guided Path
1
Read the objection carefully
Read the Arabic passage once, then the translation. Notice the objector's concession and their claim that nature's effect is conditional.
The objector concedes that delay disproves necessary cause but not natural cause.
2
Understand the objection's logic
The objector says: Nature only acts when conditions are met and obstacles removed. So the world's delay could be due to an eternal obstacle or missing condition.
3
Analyze Sanusi's first response: eternal obstacle
If an obstacle existed eternally, it would be eternal and cannot cease to exist. Therefore, it would permanently prevent the world from existing.
This leads to the conclusion that the world would never exist, which contradicts observed existence.
4
Analyze Sanusi's second response: missing condition
If a condition was absent eternally, it would never come to be because its existence would require removing an eternal obstacle or an infinite regress of conditions, both impossible.
5
Reflect on the conclusion
Since both possibilities are impossible, the delay cannot be explained by natural causes. Therefore, the world must be created by a free agent.
This refutes the objector's attempt to preserve natural causality.

Source Text
⚠ Extracted via Advanced OCR ∼97% accuracy, minor errors may remain — always verify against original Arabic books
فإن قيل : لا شك أن تأخُرَ الأجرام وصفاتِها عن الأزلِ يدلُّ قطعاً على أن إيجادها ليس على طريق التعليل ؛ إذ العلَّةُ العقلية يستحيلُ مفارقتها لمعلولها ، وأمَّا دلالة التأخُرِ على أنَّ الإيجاد ليس بطريقِ الطبع .. فقد لا يُسلَّمُ ؛ لِمَا تَقَرَّرَ أنَّ تأثير الطبيعة عند مَنْ يقولُ بها مِنَ المبتدعة ليس على طريق اللزوم بكلِّ حالٍ ، بل إنَّما يلزمها مطبوعها إذا توفّرَتِ الشرائط وانتفَتِ الموانع ، فعلى هذا تأخُرُ العوالم عنِ الأزلِ لوجودِ مانع منع منها في الأزلِ ، أو انتفاء شرط هناك.
فالجواب : أنَّهُ لو وُجِدَ مانع مِنْ وجود العوالم في الأزلِ .. لَمَا انتفى أبداً ؛ لأنَّ ما ثبت قدمُهُ استحالَ عدمُهُ ، فيلزم ألا يوجد شيءٌ مِنَ العوالم أبداً ، ولو انتفى شرط وجودِ العوالم في الأزلِ .. لَمَا وُجِدَ ذلك الشرط أبداً ، فلا يوجد أيضاً شيءٌ مِنَ العوالم أبداً ؛ لأنَّ وجودَ ذلك الشرط فيما لا يزالُ .. مُتوقِّفٌ على انتفاء مانع أزلي ، أو تسلسل شرائط إلى غير أول ، وكلاهما محال.
If it is said: There is no doubt that the delay of bodies and their attributes from eternity decisively indicates that their creation is not by way of cause; for a rational cause cannot be separated from its effect. But as for the delay indicating that creation is not by way of nature, it may not be granted; because it is established that the effect of nature according to those who affirm it among the innovators is not necessary in every case, but rather its impression follows it only when conditions are fulfilled and obstacles are removed. On this basis, the delay of the worlds from eternity is due to the existence of an obstacle preventing them in eternity, or the absence of a condition there.
The answer: That if there were an obstacle preventing the worlds from existing in eternity, it would never cease to exist; because whatever is proven eternal is impossible to cease. Then it would follow that nothing of the worlds would ever exist. And if the condition for the existence of the worlds was absent in eternity, that condition would never come to exist; so nothing of the worlds would ever exist either; because the existence of that condition in the future depends on the removal of an eternal obstacle or an infinite regress of conditions, both of which are impossible.
Source pages p. 144

Commentary
This passage contains a further objection and its refutation. The objector concedes that delay disproves necessary causal production (because a cause cannot be separated from its effect), but argues that it does not disprove natural production, because according to those who believe in natures (like some innovators), nature's effect is not always necessary; it only occurs when conditions are present and obstacles absent. So the delay of the worlds could be due to an obstacle in eternity or a missing condition. Sanusi answers: If there were an eternal obstacle, it would be eternal and thus cannot cease, so nothing would ever come into existence. If a condition was absent in eternity, that condition itself would never come to be (since its existence would require removing an eternal obstacle or an infinite regress of conditions, both impossible). Therefore, the delay cannot be explained by obstacles or conditions, so the world must be created by a free agent.

Key Terms
تأخر
علة عقلية
معلول
طبع
مبتدعة
شرائط
موانع
قدم
تسلسل

Key Terms from Source Passage

Misconception & Correction
Misconception: One might think obstacles or missing conditions could indefinitely delay a natural effect, but eventually the effect would occur when the obstacle is removed or condition appears.
Correction: Sanusi shows that if the obstacle or missing condition is eternal, it would be permanent and prevent the effect forever, so the world would never exist.

Checkpoint
According to Sanusi's answer, why cannot an eternal obstacle explain the delay of the world?
A) Because obstacles can be removed by another cause
B) Because what is eternal cannot cease to exist, so the obstacle would permanently prevent the world
C) Because obstacles only affect physical things, not eternal ones
D) Because the world is not subject to obstacles

Key Takeaway
The delay of the world cannot be explained by natural causes with conditions, because any eternal condition or obstacle would be permanent and prevent existence forever.

How This Lesson Applies — to MY Life?
?
Sanusi argues that any eternal obstacle would be permanent and prevent existence forever. Is there an eternal obstacle in your life—a doubt, a sin, a fear—that you have accepted as unchanging? What if that obstacle is not eternal but created by you, and God can remove it today if you turn to Him?
?
If you believe that God creates all things directly, does your heart truly rely on Him for every small outcome, or do you still trust in 'natural' processes as if they operate independently? Where in your life do you act as if conditions and obstacles are the real determinants?
?
Sanusi says that a missing condition in eternity would never come to exist by itself. Is there a condition you are waiting for before you fully commit to God—like perfect circumstances, more knowledge, or a sign? What if that condition never comes, and God is calling you to act now without it?

Miracles
